topics.title: Basic Object-Oriented Concepts
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Object-Oriented Concepts

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** is a programming paradigm built around the concept of **objects** — data structures that bundle together **attributes (state)** and **behaviors (methods)** — rather than relying solely on functions and logic. It is the dominant paradigm taught under the KTU 2024 syllabus to model real-world systems using **classes**, **objects**, and the four (or five) foundational principles: **Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, and Polymorphism**.

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Car Blueprint"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Think of OOP like designing a **Car**:
> - A **Class** is the *blueprint* of the car. It defines what every car will have (4 wheels, 1 engine, color, max speed) and what it can do (start, accelerate, brake).
> - An **Object** is the *actual car* built from that blueprint (e.g., your red Honda City parked outside).
> - **Encapsulation** is the *dashboard* — you press the brake pedal, but you don't need to know the hydraulic mechanism inside.
> - **Abstraction** is the *steering wheel interface* — you turn it, but you don't need to know about the rack-and-pinion gear below.
> - **Inheritance** is how a *Sports Car blueprint* inherits all features from the generic Car blueprint but adds turbo and spoiler.
> - **Polymorphism** is the *start button* — pressing it makes a petrol car roar and an electric car hum, but the button is the same.

### 1.3 Core Vocabulary of OOP (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Definition (KTU Board Standard) | Standard Unit/Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Object** | A runtime entity with state, behavior, and identity. | Instance of a class |
| **Class** | A user-defined blueprint/template describing objects. | Type definition |
| **State** | The current values of an object's attributes. | Member variables |
| **Behavior** | The actions an object can perform (methods). | Member functions |
| **Identity** | The unique address/reference of an object. | Memory pointer |
| **Message** | A request sent to an object to invoke a method. | Method call |
| **Interface** | A contract of methods a class must implement. | Abstract type |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Examiners frequently test the difference between **Object vs Class**, **Encapsulation vs Abstraction**, and **Compile-time vs Run-time Polymorphism**. Mastery of these distinctions guarantees marks in Part A.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Building Blocks — Step-by-Step Logic

#### 2.1.1 **Objects** — The Living Instances
- An object is a **runtime instantiation** of a class.
- Every object possesses three defining characteristics:
  1. **State** — represented by the values of its attributes (e.g., `color = "red"`, `speed = 120`).
  2. **Behavior** — represented by the methods it can execute (e.g., `accelerate()`, `brake()`).
  3. **Identity** — a unique memory address distinguishing it from all other objects.
- *Why?* Objects allow a program to mirror the real world, where each entity (car, bank account, student) is unique and reactive.

#### 2.1.2 **Classes** — The Blueprint
- A class is a **logical construct** defining the structure (attributes) and behavior (methods) its objects will share.
- A class itself consumes **no memory** for its data; only objects (instances) do.
- *How?* When `new ClassName()` is executed, memory is allocated on the heap, and the object is constructed.

#### 2.1.3 **Encapsulation** — Hiding the Wiring
- Bundling **data + methods** that operate on that data within a single unit (class) and **restricting direct external access** to internal state.
- Achieved through access modifiers: **`private`**, **`protected`**, **`public`**.
- *Why?* Prevents unintended interference and misuse, enabling modular, maintainable code.

#### 2.1.4 **Abstraction** — Showing Only Essentials
- Exposing only the **relevant features** of an object while hiding the complex implementation details.
- Achieved via **abstract classes** and **interfaces**.
- *Why?* Reduces complexity for the user of the system.

#### 2.1.5 **Inheritance** — Reuse Through Hierarchy
- A mechanism where a new class (subclass/derived) acquires properties and behaviors of an existing class (superclass/base).
- Promotes **code reuse** and establishes a natural **"is-a"** relationship.
- Types in KTU scope: **Single, Multilevel, Hierarchical, Multiple (via interfaces only)**.

#### 2.1.6 **Polymorphism** — One Interface, Many Forms
- The ability of a single interface to represent different underlying forms (data types or classes).
- Two flavors KTU tests:
  - **Compile-time (Static):** Method overloading.
  - **Run-time (Dynamic):** Method overriding via virtual functions.

#### 2.1.7 **Message Passing** — Communication Between Objects
- Objects interact by **sending messages** (calling methods on each other).
- The receiver object decides how to interpret and act on the message.

#### 2.1.8 **Dynamic Binding** (Late Binding)
- The method to be invoked is determined **at runtime** based on the actual object type, not the reference type.
- *Why?* Enables true polymorphism and flexible system extension.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Rule / Formula | KTU Exam Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Class Instantiation** | $\text{Memory} = \sum \text{sizeof(attributes of object)}$ | High |
| **Method Overloading** | $\text{Same name} \Rightarrow \text{Different parameter list}$ | High |
| **Method Overriding** | $\text{Same signature} \Rightarrow \text{Different implementation in subclass}$ | High |
| **Encapsulation Ratio** | $\text{Cohesion} \uparrow \;\; \text{Coupling} \downarrow$ | Medium |
| **Inheritance Depth** | $\text{Class Hierarchy} = \{C_0, C_1, C_2, \dots, C_n\}$ where $C_{i+1} \text{ extends } C_i$ | High |
| **Dynamic Dispatch** | $\text{Binding Time} = t_{\text{compile}}$ (static) or $t_{\text{run}}$ (dynamic) | High |
| **Abstract Class** | Cannot be instantiated; may contain abstract + concrete methods | High |
| **Interface** | Pure abstract; all methods abstract (pre-Java 8) | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering:** OOP underpins nearly all modern engineering software — from **CAD systems** (modeling parts as objects) and **embedded controllers** (sensor objects) to **web applications** (Spring/Django MVC) and **AI/ML frameworks** (TensorFlow's `tf.Module` class hierarchy).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: How Each Concept Solves a Software Problem

Let us formally derive how each OOP concept addresses a specific programming challenge. We treat each problem as a function mapping a *bad design* to a *good design*.

**Step 1 — The "No Class" Problem (Procedural Code)**

$$
\text{Code}_{\text{old}} = \text{set of global functions operating on global data}
$$

This leads to **data insecurity** and **spaghetti dependencies**. Every function can corrupt every variable.

$$
\therefore \text{Encapsulation} = f(\text{class}) : \text{data} \to \text{private} \;\;+\;\; \text{accessors}
$$

**Step 2 — Modeling Real-World Entities**

$$
\text{Object} = \{ \text{state}, \text{behavior}, \text{identity} \}
$$

$$
\text{State}(obj) = \{ a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n \} \quad \text{where } a_i \in \text{Attributes}
$$

$$
\text{Behavior}(obj) = \{ m_1, m_2, \dots, m_k \} \quad \text{where } m_j \in \text{Methods}
$$

$$
\text{Identity}(obj) = \text{MemoryAddress}(obj) \in \mathbb{Z}^+
$$

**Step 3 — Class as a Template**

$$
\text{Class} \; C = (\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{M}) \quad \text{where} \quad \mathcal{A} = \text{Attribute Set}, \;\; \mathcal{M} = \text{Method Set}
$$

For any object $o_i$ of class $C$:

$$
o_i \leftarrow \text{new} \; C \implies o_i.\mathcal{A} = \text{initialized values}
$$

**Step 4 — Inheritance Derivation**

Let $C_{\text{parent}}$ be a base class and $C_{\text{child}}$ a derived class:

$$
\mathcal{A}_{C_{\text{child}}} = \mathcal{A}_{C_{\text{parent}}} \cup \mathcal{A}_{\text{new}}
$$

$$
\mathcal{M}_{C_{\text{child}}} = \mathcal{M}_{C_{\text{parent}}} \cup \mathcal{M}_{\text{new}} \cup \mathcal{M}_{\text{overridden}}
$$

This proves that inheritance achieves **code reuse by set union** of properties.

**Step 5 — Polymorphism & Dynamic Binding**

Let reference variable $r$ be of type $C_{\text{parent}}$ but point to object of $C_{\text{child}}$:

$$
r : C_{\text{parent}} \implies r \leftarrow \text{new} \; C_{\text{child}}
$$

At compile time, the compiler binds the call `r.method()` to the signature in $C_{\text{parent}}$. At runtime:

$$
\text{Actual method invoked} = \begin{cases} \mathcal{M}_{C_{\text{parent}}}.method & \text{if not overridden} \\ \mathcal{M}_{C_{\text{child}}}.method & \text{if overridden} \end{cases}
$$

This is the **dynamic dispatch equation** — a favorite KTU 14-marker.

**Step 6 — Abstraction Contract**

An abstract class $A$ enforces a contract:

$$
A = \{ \mathcal{M}_{\text{abstract}}, \mathcal{M}_{\text{concrete}} \}
$$

$$
\forall \text{ concrete subclass } S : S \text{ must implement } \mathcal{M}_{\text{abstract}}(A)
$$

This guarantees that any object passed as $A$ will have all required behaviors — a pillar of **interface-driven design**.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Modeling a University System

| Concept | Application in "University System" |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Class** | `Student`, `Faculty`, `Course` |
| **Object** | `student101 = new Student("Rahul", 21)` |
| **Encapsulation** | `marks` is `private`; accessed via `getMarks()` |
| **Abstraction** | `Person` abstract class with abstract `getRole()` |
| **Inheritance** | `TeachingAssistant extends Student` (multilevel) |
| **Polymorphism** | `getRole()` overridden differently in `Student` and `Faculty` |
| **Message** | `course.enroll(student101)` — course receives a message |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: OOP Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING"] --> B["CLASS"]
    A --> C["OBJECT"]
    A --> D["FOUR PILLARS OF OOP"]
    A --> E["MESSAGE PASSING"]
    A --> F["DYNAMIC BINDING"]

    B --> B1["Blueprint"]
    B --> B2["Defines Attributes"]
    B --> B3["Defines Methods"]

    C --> C1["State"]
    C --> C2["Behavior"]
    C --> C3["Identity"]

    D --> P1["ENCAPSULATION"]
    D --> P2["ABSTRACTION"]
    D --> P3["INHERITANCE"]
    D --> P4["POLYMORPHISM"]

    P1 --> P1A["Data Hiding via private"]
    P1 --> P1B["Public Getters and Setters"]

    P2 --> P2A["Abstract Classes"]
    P2 --> P2B["Interfaces"]

    P3 --> P3A["Single Level"]
    P3 --> P3B["Multilevel"]
    P3 --> P3C["Hierarchical"]
    P3 --> P3D["Multiple via Interfaces"]

    P4 --> P4A["Compile Time Overloading"]
    P4 --> P4B["Run Time Overriding"]

    E --> E1["objectA dot method called by objectB"]
    F --> F1["Method resolved at runtime"]
```

### 4.2 Inheritance Hierarchy Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1["Vehicle Class"] --> V2["Car Class"]
    V2 --> V3["SportsCar Class"]
    V2 --> V4["ElectricCar Class"]
    V1 --> V5["Truck Class"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The above `Vehicle → Car → SportsCar` chain represents a **multilevel inheritance** path. The split into `SportsCar` and `ElectricCar` from `Car` is a **hierarchical inheritance** split. A child class inherits the public and protected members of its parent.

### 4.3 Polymorphism Dispatch Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Reference variable of type Parent"] --> Q["Compile time check"]
    Q --> S["Actual object of type Child"]
    S --> T["Runtime lookup in VTable"]
    T --> U["Invoke Child version of method"]
    U --> W["Output reflects Child behavior"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a **Class** and an **Object** with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Class | Object |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Blueprint/template | Instance of a class |
| Memory | No memory allocated at declaration | Memory allocated at runtime |
| Declaration | Declared once | Can be created many times |
| Example | `class Student { ... };` | `Student s1 = new Student();` |

> **Marking Note:** Award **1 mark** for each correct row up to 3. Example line is essential.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain **Encapsulation** with its two key benefits.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Encapsulation is the wrapping of data and methods into a single unit (class) and restricting direct access using access modifiers.
**Benefit 1:** *Data Hiding* — internal state is protected from unauthorized access.
**Benefit 2:** *Increased Flexibility* — implementation can change without affecting external code.
*(Bonus 1 mark)* Example: `private double balance; public double getBalance() { return balance; }`

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four pillars of OOP** with one real-world analogy for each. Compare **compile-time** and **run-time polymorphism**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a short note on **dynamic binding** and **message passing**, and explain why they are essential for object-oriented systems.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**The Four Pillars of OOP:**

1. **Encapsulation** — *Analogy: ATM Machine.* You interact with the screen and buttons; the internal cash-dispensing mechanism and account logic are hidden inside a sealed unit. You cannot directly tamper with the cash vault.

2. **Abstraction** — *Analogy: Driving a Car.* You use the steering wheel, accelerator, and brake. You do not need to understand combustion engine cycles or hydraulic brake fluid dynamics.

3. **Inheritance** — *Analogy: Family Tree.* A child inherits the eye color and last name from parents, but may also have unique traits.

4. **Polymorphism** — *Analogy: A Single Word, Many Meanings.* The word "run" means a sprint (athlete), operating (a program), or managing (a business) — context determines the meaning.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Compile-time Polymorphism | Run-time Polymorphism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mechanism | Method/Operator Overloading | Method Overriding |
| Binding | Early / Static | Late / Dynamic |
| Performance | Faster (no runtime lookup) | Slightly slower (vtable lookup) |
| Flexibility | Lower | Higher |
| Example | `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(double a, double b)` | `void draw()` overridden in `Circle`, `Square` |

*(Full 7 marks: 1 mark per pillar + analogy = 4 marks; 3 marks for the comparison table with at least 4 valid rows.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Dynamic Binding:**
Dynamic binding (or late binding) is the process by which the method to be invoked is resolved **at runtime** rather than at compile time, based on the **actual type of the object** rather than the type of the reference.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$
\text{Method Invoked} = \mathcal{M}[\text{ActualType}(o)][\text{methodName}]
$$

**Message Passing:**
Message passing is the mechanism by which objects communicate. An object `A` sends a message to object `B` by invoking one of `B`'s methods. The receiver `B` interprets and responds to the message.

**Why Essential:**
- **Flexibility:** New subclasses can be added without modifying existing code (Open/Closed Principle).
- **Extensibility:** Systems can grow by adding new object types that respond to existing messages in new ways.
- **Modularity:** Objects are decoupled — they interact only through messages, not internal data.
- **Runtime Decision-making:** Enables polymorphic behavior critical for frameworks and design patterns.

*(7 marks: 2 marks each for definitions of dynamic binding and message passing; 3 marks for the "Why Essential" elaboration.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain **different types of inheritance** supported in object-oriented programming. State the **"is-a" vs "has-a"** relationship.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **abstract class** and **interface**. When would you prefer one over the other?

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Types of Inheritance (with diagrams):**

1. **Single Inheritance** — One class inherits from one parent.
   `A → B`

2. **Multilevel Inheritance** — A chain of inheritance.
   `A → B → C`

3. **Hierarchical Inheritance** — Multiple children from one parent.
   `A → B`, `A → C`, `A → D`

4. **Multiple Inheritance** — One class inherits from multiple parents. *(Achieved via interfaces in Java/C# to avoid diamond problem.)*
   `A → C`, `B → C`

5. **Hybrid Inheritance** — Combination of two or more types.

**"is-a" vs "has-a":**

| Relationship | Meaning | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **is-a** | Inheritance (subclass is a specialized parent) | `Dog is-a Animal` |
| **has-a** | Composition/Aggregation (object contains another) | `Car has-a Engine` |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently confuse **Hierarchical** with **Multilevel**. *Hierarchical = siblings from one parent*; *Multilevel = grandparent–parent–child chain*. **Lose 1 mark** if mixed up.
> - Always specify that **Multiple Inheritance via classes** causes the **Diamond Problem**; hence Java uses interfaces. Examiners award marks for this nuance.
> - Forgetting to draw the diagram costs **2 marks** in 7-mark sub-questions.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Abstract Class vs Interface:**

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Methods | Can have both abstract & concrete | Pure abstract (pre-Java 8) |
| Variables | Can have instance variables | Only `public static final` constants |
| Inheritance | A class can extend only one | A class can implement many |
| Access Modifiers | Any modifier allowed | Methods are implicitly `public` |
| Constructor | Can have constructors | Cannot have constructors |
| Use Case | Shared code + contract | Pure contract definition |

**When to Prefer:**
- Prefer an **abstract class** when subclasses share a common base implementation and have some default behavior.
- Prefer an **interface** when you need a pure contract (e.g., `Serializable`, `Comparable`) or want multiple inheritance of type.

*(7 marks: 4 marks for the comparison table with 5+ valid rows; 3 marks for the "When to Prefer" explanation with a real example.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Basic OOP Concepts**

- **Object** = State + Behavior + Identity. Always list all three for full marks.
- **Class** is a blueprint; **Object** is a runtime instance.
- **Four Pillars:** Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism.
- **Encapsulation** = data hiding via `private` + public getters/setters.
- **Abstraction** = showing essentials, hiding details; achieved via abstract classes and interfaces.
- **Inheritance Types:** Single, Multilevel, Hierarchical, Multiple (via interfaces), Hybrid.
- **is-a** → Inheritance; **has-a** → Composition.
- **Polymorphism has two flavors:** Compile-time (overloading) and Run-time (overriding).
- **Dynamic Binding** = method resolved at runtime based on actual object type.
- **Message Passing** = objects communicate by calling each other's methods.
- **Abstract Class** can have constructors and concrete methods; **Interface** cannot (traditional).
- Java supports **single class inheritance** but **multiple interface implementation** to avoid the **Diamond Problem**.
- A class with at least one **abstract method** must be declared `abstract`.
- **Memory of a class declaration = 0 bytes**; memory is allocated **only when objects are created**.
- In KTU 14-mark answers, always include a **diagram** for inheritance questions (worth ~2 marks).
- A favorite trick question: *"Can you create an object of an abstract class?"* — **No**, unless it is an anonymous subclass or a concrete subclass implements all abstract methods.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Basic Object-Oriented Concepts**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme OEERT613 syllabus and OBE pattern.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Java
topics.content:
> **APJ ADJUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Java # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Java** is a high-level, strongly-typed, class-based, object-oriented programming language developed by *James Gosling* and his team at **Sun Microsystems** in **1991** (released in **1995**). It is formally defined in the *Java Language Specification (JLS)* as a general-purpose, concurrent, class-based, object-oriented language designed to have **few implementation dependencies** — famously summarized as the principle **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEERT613, Module 1):** > *"Java is an object-oriented, platform-independent, secure, robust, multithreaded, and architecture-neutral programming language that uses both compilation and interpretation to produce portable bytecode executed by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM)."* ## 1.2 The Three Editions of Java (Java Platform) | Edition | Full Form | Purpose | KTU Relevance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Java SE** | Java Standard Edition | Core API, desktop & console apps | **Primary focus of OEERT613** | | **Java EE** | Java Enterprise Edition | Web, enterprise, server-side apps | Mentioned in elective modules | | **Java ME** | Java Micro Edition | Embedded, mobile, IoT devices | Historical context only | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Recipe" Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy: Java as a "Universal Recipe Translator"** > > Imagine you are a chef who writes down recipes in a special universal language (English). Every kitchen in the world (Windows, Linux, Mac) has a **translator** (JVM) that converts your recipe into that kitchen's local language on the fly. > > - The **recipe** = Java source code (`.java` file) > - The **translator machine** = JVM > - The **universal intermediate language** = Bytecode (`.class` file) > - The **kitchen** = Operating System + Hardware > > You never rewrite the recipe. The translator does the heavy lifting everywhere. This is the essence of **platform independence**. ## 1.4 Evolution Timeline of Java - **1990** — *Green Project* initiated at Sun Microsystems - **1991** — James Gosling develops *Oak* (later renamed Java) - **1995** — Java 1.0 officially released; Netscape integrates Java - **1998** — JDK 1.2 released, introducing *Collections Framework* - **2000** — JDK 1.3 (HotSpot JVM) - **2004** — JDK 1.5 (Generics, Annotations) - **2014** — Java SE 8 (Lambda expressions) - **2017** — Java SE 9 (Module system) - **2018** — Java SE 10 (Local variable type inference) - **2021** — Java 17 (LTS release) - **2023** — Java 21 (LTS release) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference ## 2.1 The 12 Buzzwords / Features of Java (Direct KTU Exam Favourite) > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently asks: *"List and explain the features of Java"* (3–7 marks). Memorize the list with one-line crisp definitions. 1. **Simple** — Easy to learn; eliminates complex features like pointers and operator overloading. 2. **Object-Oriented** — Everything is an object modeled using OOP principles. 3. **Platform Independent** — `.class` bytecode runs on any OS with a JVM. 4. **Secured** — No explicit pointer, runs inside the JVM sandbox, bytecode verifier checks code. 5. **Robust** — Strong memory management (garbage collection), exception handling, type checking. 6. **Architecture Neutral** — Fixed size of primitive types (e.g., `int` is always **32 bits** regardless of OS). 7. **Portable** — Same bytecode runs unchanged on Windows, Linux, Mac. 8. **High-Performance** — Uses *Just-In-Time (JIT)* compiler for hot code paths. 9. **Multithreaded** — Built-in `Thread` class for concurrent execution. 10. **Interpreted** — Bytecode is interpreted line-by-line by the JVM. 11. **Dynamic** — Supports runtime class loading, reflection. 12. **Distributed** — Designed for network programming (RMI, sockets). ## 2.2 JDK vs JRE vs JVM — The Triad > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **#1 most asked concept** in KTU Module 1. Memorize the containment relationship. | Component | Full Form | Contains | Role | Installed Where | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **JDK** | Java Development Kit | JRE + `javac`, `javadoc`, `jar`, debugger | For *developers* to **write & compile** code | Developer's machine | | **JRE** | Java Runtime Environment | JVM + Core Libraries (rt.jar) | For *end users* to **run** programs | Client/Server machine | | **JVM** | Java Virtual Machine | ClassLoader, Bytecode Verifier, JIT, GC | Executes **bytecode** line by line | Abstract runtime engine | $$\text{JDK} \;=\; \text{JRE} \;+\; \text{Development Tools (javac, jdb, javadoc)}$$ $$\text{JRE} \;=\; \text{JVM} \;+\; \text{Library Classes (rt.jar, etc.)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Pitfall:** JVM is **platform-dependent** (different JVM for Windows, Linux, Mac), but the **bytecode** it executes is **platform-independent**. ## 2.3 Java Compilation & Execution Pipeline The complete flow from source code to execution is a **two-step process**: $$\text{Source (.java)} \xrightarrow{\text{javac compiler}} \text{Bytecode (.class)} \xrightarrow{\text{JVM interpreter/JIT}} \text{Native Machine Code}$$ | Stage | Tool/Engine | Input | Output | KTU Mnemonic | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1. Source Creation | Text Editor | — | `Hello.java` | **S**ource | | 2. Compilation | `javac` (JDK) | `Hello.java` | `Hello.class` | **C**ompile | | 3. Class Loading | ClassLoader (JVM) | `Hello.class` | Loaded memory area | **L**oad | | 4. Verification | Bytecode Verifier | Loaded class | Validated code | **V**erify | | 5. Execution | Interpreter + JIT | Verified bytecode | Native machine code | **E**xecute | **Mnemonic:** **S-C-L-V-E** — Source, Compile, Load, Verify, Execute. ## 2.4 Anatomy of a Java Program (KRTU Board Pattern) Every KTU Java program must follow this template: ```java // File: HelloWorld.java package com.ktu.oeert613; // optional, maps to folder import java.util.Scanner; // optional, brings in libraries public class HelloWorld { // class declaration (mandatory, public) public static void main(String[] args) { // entry point System.out.println("Hello KTU!"); // statement } } // class end ``` | Element | Purpose | KTU Marker Word | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `public` | Access modifier (visible to all) | visibility | | `class` | Declares a class (blueprint for objects) | OOP | | `static` | Belongs to class, not instance | keyword | | `void` | Returns nothing | return-type | | `main` | JVM looks for this method to start execution | entry-point | | `String[] args` | Command-line arguments array | CLI input | ## 2.5 Primitive Data Types (KRTU Favourite) | Type | Size (bits) | Range | Default | Wrapper Class | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `byte` | **8** | $-128$ to $127$ | 0 | `Byte` | | `short` | **16** | $-2^{15}$ to $2^{15}-1$ | 0 | `Short` | | `int` | **32** | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ | 0 | `Integer` | | `long` | **64** | $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ | 0L | `Long` | | `float` | **32** | IEEE 754 single | 0.0f | `Float` | | `double` | **64** | IEEE 754 double | 0.0d | `Double` | | `char` | **16** (Unicode) | `\u0000` to `\uFFFF` | `\u0000` | `Character` | | `boolean` | JVM-dependent (1 bit logical) | `true` or `false` | `false` | `Boolean` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Java, **all primitive sizes are FIXED** across platforms — this is what makes Java *architecture-neutral*. ## 2.6 Type Conversion & Casting - **Implicit (Widening):** `byte` $\rightarrow$ `short` $\rightarrow$ `int` $\rightarrow$ `long` $\rightarrow$ `float` $\rightarrow$ `double` (no data loss) - **Explicit (Narrowing):** `double` $\rightarrow$ `float` $\rightarrow$ `long` $\rightarrow$ `int` $\rightarrow$ `short` $\rightarrow$ `byte` (requires cast operator, possible data loss) # 3. Step-by-Step Execution Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough: What Happens When You Run a Java Program? **Step 1 — Source File Creation** The programmer writes plain text in a file named `Hello.java`. This file contains tokens (keywords, identifiers, operators) readable by humans. **Step 2 — Compilation by `javac`** The Java compiler (`javac.exe` on Windows, located in `JDK/bin/`) parses the source. It performs: 1. Lexical analysis (tokenization) 2. Syntax analysis (parse tree) 3. Semantic analysis (type checking) 4. Bytecode generation **Step 3 — Bytecode Production** The output is `Hello.class` — a binary file containing **bytecode** (instructions for the JVM, not for any physical CPU). Bytecode is verified to be a sequence of valid JVM opcodes. **Step 4 — Class Loading** The JVM's **ClassLoader Subsystem** loads `Hello.class` into memory using a parent-delegation model: $$\text{Bootstrap ClassLoader} \rightarrow \text{Extension ClassLoader} \rightarrow \text{Application ClassLoader} \rightarrow \text{User Class}$$ **Step 5 — Bytecode Verification** The **Bytecode Verifier** checks the loaded class for: - Valid stack operations (no underflow/overflow) - No illegal data type conversions - No access control violations - No jumps to invalid instructions **Step 6 — Execution by JVM** The **Execution Engine** has two parts: - **Interpreter** — reads bytecode one instruction at a time - **JIT Compiler** — detects "hot" methods (executed many times) and compiles them to native code, caching the result **Step 7 — Garbage Collection (Runtime)** The **Garbage Collector (GC)** automatically frees memory of objects no longer referenced. Roots: stack references, static fields, JNI references. ## 3.2 JVM Runtime Data Areas (Memory Architecture) Every JVM partitions memory into **5 logical regions**: | Area | Stores | Thread-Shared? | KTU Symbol | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Method (Class) Area** | Class metadata, static variables, constant pool, method bytecode | **Shared** (one per JVM) | Shared | | **Heap** | All objects, instance variables | **Shared** | Shared | | **Stack** | Method frames, local variables, partial results | **Private** (one per thread) | Private | | **PC Register** | Address of current JVM instruction | **Private** | Private | | **Native Method Stack** | C/C++ native method calls (JNI) | **Private** | Private | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mnemonic:** **M-H-S-P-N** → **M**ethod, **H**eap, **S**tack, **P**C, **N**ative. The first two are shared; the last three are per-thread. ## 3.3 Worked Example: Primitive Promotion in Expression **Problem:** Evaluate the following expression and identify the type of the result. ```java byte a = 10; byte b = 20; int c = a + b; ``` **Step-by-Step Explanation:** **Step 1:** `a` and `b` are declared as `byte` (8-bit signed). **Step 2:** According to JLS §5.6.2, *binary numeric promotion* is applied to operands of operators like `+`. **Step 3:** The `byte` operands are automatically **widened to `int`** before the addition. **Step 4:** The `+` operator returns an `int` result. **Step 5:** Assigning this `int` back to a `byte` would require an **explicit cast**; assigning to `int c` is **implicit widening** — safe and allowed. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Result type} &= \text{int} \\ \text{Value of } c &= 10 + 20 = 30 \\ \text{Memory size} &= 32 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Compile-Time vs Run-Time Errors | Error Type | Detected When | Examples | KTU Common Question | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Compile-Time (Checked)** | `javac` compilation | Syntax error, missing semicolon, type mismatch | "Why doesn't this code compile?" | | **Run-Time (Unchecked)** | During JVM execution | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | "Why does code compile but crash on run?" | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Java does NOT have a "linker" step like C/C++. The linking happens at **runtime** by the JVM. So if a class is missing at compile time, you may not get an error until execution. ## 3.5 Command-Line Compilation & Execution (Exam Practical) **Step 1:** Open terminal in folder containing `Hello.java`. **Step 2:** Compile: ```bash javac Hello.java ``` This produces `Hello.class` in the same directory. **Step 3:** Execute: ```bash java Hello ``` This invokes the JVM, loads `Hello.class`, and calls `main()`. **Step 4:** Pass command-line arguments: ```bash java Hello KTU 2024 ``` Inside `main`, `args[0] = "KTU"`, `args[1] = "2024"`. > [!NOTE] > **KRTU Mistake to Avoid:** Do **NOT** write `java Hello.class` — this causes `Error: Could not find or load main class Hello.class`. The JVM looks for the class *name*, not the file name. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram: JDK / JRE / JVM Containment ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph devBox[Developer Machine] direction TB jdkBox["JDK
Java Development Kit"] end subgraph jreBox[Inside JDK] direction TB jreInner["JRE
Java Runtime Environment"] devTools["Development Tools
javac, jdb, javadoc, jar"] end subgraph jvmBox[Inside JRE] direction TB jvmInner["JVM
Java Virtual Machine"] libClasses["Library Classes
rt.jar, java.lang, java.util"] end subgraph jvmInternals[JVM Internals] direction TB cl["ClassLoader
Subsystem"] bv["Bytecode
Verifier"] ee["Execution Engine
Interpreter + JIT"] gc["Garbage
Collector"] end jdkBox --> jreInner jdkBox --> devTools jreInner --> jvmInner jreInner --> libClasses jvmInner --> cl jvmInner --> bv jvmInner --> ee jvmInner --> gc style jdkBox fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style jreInner fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style jvmInner fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style cl fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style bv fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style ee fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style gc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style devTools fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 style libClasses fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Diagram: Java Program Execution Flow (SC-L-V-E Pipeline) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Programmer Writes
Hello.java Source Code] --> B[javac Compiler
in JDK] B --> C[Hello.class
Bytecode File] C --> D[JVM ClassLoader
Loads Class into Memory] D --> E[Bytecode Verifier
Checks Safety] E --> F[Execution Engine
Interpreter + JIT] F --> G[Native Machine Code
Runs on OS] H[Operating System
Windows / Linux / Mac] --> G style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000 style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000 style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style H fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Diagram: JVM Runtime Memory Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph jvmMem[JVM Memory] direction TB subgraph sharedArea[Thread-Shared Areas] direction TB methodArea["Method Area
Class metadata, static vars, constant pool"] heapArea["Heap Area
Objects, instance variables, GC managed"] end subgraph thread1[Thread 1] direction TB stack1["Stack
Method frames, local variables"] pc1["PC Register
Current instruction pointer"] nms1["Native Method Stack
JNI C/C++ calls"] end subgraph thread2[Thread 2] direction TB stack2["Stack
Method frames, local variables"] pc2["PC Register
Current instruction pointer"] nms2["Native Method Stack
JNI C/C++ calls"] end end style methodArea fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style heapArea fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style stack1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style pc1 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style nms1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 style stack2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style pc2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style nms2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Diagram: Class Loader Delegation Model ```mermaid flowchart TD user["User Class
Application ClassLoader"] --> ext["Extension ClassLoader
Loads jre/lib/ext"] ext --> boot["Bootstrap ClassLoader
Loads rt.jar core classes"] boot --> found{Class Found?} found -->|Yes| loadClass[Load and Return Class] found -->|No| back1[Return to Extension] back1 --> back2[Return to Application] back2 --> cnf[Throw ClassNotFoundException] style user fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style ext fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style boot fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style found fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000 style loadClass fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style cnf fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** ### **Q1.** Differentiate between JDK, JRE, and JVM. **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** | Feature | JDK | JRE | JVM | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Full Form** | Java Development Kit | Java Runtime Environment | Java Virtual Machine | | **Purpose** | Develop & compile Java programs | Run Java programs | Execute bytecode | | **Contains** | JRE + dev tools (`javac`) | JVM + library classes | Runtime engine | | **Audience** | Developers | End users | Abstract specification | | **Platform Dependent?** | Yes | Yes | Yes (different for each OS) | **Conclusion:** JDK is a superset of JRE, which in turn is a superset of JVM. A developer needs JDK; a user only needs JRE. --- > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** ### **Q2.** Explain any three features of Java with examples. **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** 1. **Platform Independent:** Java source is compiled to `.class` bytecode that runs on any OS with a compatible JVM. *Example:* The same `Hello.class` runs unchanged on Windows, Linux, and Mac. 2. **Object-Oriented:** Java organizes code around classes and objects, supporting encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction. *Example:* `class Student { ... }` defines a blueprint; `Student s = new Student();` creates an object. 3. **Secured:** Java runs code inside the JVM sandbox with no explicit pointer arithmetic. The Bytecode Verifier checks the loaded class for illegal operations before execution. *Example:* A Java applet (historical) could not access arbitrary memory addresses. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. Do **NOT** confuse JVM (platform-dependent) with bytecode (platform-independent). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this swap. > 2. In the features list, **all 12 buzzwords are expected** for full marks. Listing only 5–6 loses 2–3 marks. > 3. When explaining compilation, you **MUST** mention that `javac` produces `.class` bytecode, not `.exe`. Markers specifically look for this distinction. > 4. For programs, missing `class` keyword or wrong `main` signature results in **zero** for the code section. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Java Architecture & Execution* > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`** #### **(a) Explain the architecture of JVM with a neat block diagram. List the various memory areas allocated by the JVM. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is an abstract computing machine that enables a computer to run a Java program. It is the runtime engine of the Java Platform. **JVM Architecture consists of three main subsystems:** **1. ClassLoader Subsystem** - **Loading:** Reads `.class` files and stores binary data in method area. - **Linking:** Performs Verification, Preparation, and Resolution. - **Initialization:** Executes static initializers and static variable assignments. **2. Runtime Data Areas (Memory)** - **Method Area:** Stores class-level data such as static variables, constant pool, method bytecode. - **Heap Area:** Stores all objects and instance variables. Garbage collector manages it. - **Stack Area:** Per-thread memory storing method frames, local variables, partial results. - **PC Registers:** Per-thread pointer to the current JVM instruction. - **Native Method Stacks:** Per-thread memory for native (C/C++) method calls. **3. Execution Engine** - **Interpreter:** Reads and executes bytecode line by line. - **JIT Compiler:** Compiles "hot" methods to native code for performance. - **Garbage Collector:** Reclaims memory of unreferenced objects automatically. *Refer to Section 4.3 for the diagram.* **Memory Areas Allocated by JVM:** | Area | Shared/Private | | :--- | :--- | | Method Area | Shared | | Heap Area | Shared | | Stack Area | Private (per thread) | | PC Registers | Private (per thread) | | Native Method Stack | Private (per thread) | #### **(b) Describe the step-by-step execution of a Java program from source code to runtime output. Why is Java called platform-independent? [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Step-by-Step Execution:** **Step 1:** The programmer creates a source file `Hello.java` using any text editor. **Step 2:** The source is passed to the **Java compiler (`javac`)** present in the JDK. `javac` parses the file, performs lexical, syntactic, and semantic analysis, and generates an intermediate **bytecode** file `Hello.class`. **Step 3:** When the user runs `java Hello`, the **JVM ClassLoader** loads `Hello.class` into memory using the parent-delegation model. **Step 4:** The **Bytecode Verifier** checks the loaded class for safety — no stack overflow, no illegal casts, no invalid jumps. **Step 5:** The **Execution Engine** executes the bytecode. The interpreter handles instructions one at a time; the JIT compiler optimizes frequently-executed methods to native code. **Step 6:** The OS executes the resulting native machine instructions and prints the output. **Why Java is Platform-Independent:** Java is platform-independent because: 1. The `.class` **bytecode** is not tied to any specific operating system or hardware. 2. Any machine that has a **JVM** (platform-specific) can execute the same bytecode. 3. Therefore, developers **write code once** and **run it anywhere** (WORA) without recompilation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note:** It is the **JVM** that is platform-dependent, not the bytecode. The bytecode is the universal contract. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Features, Data Types & Program Structure* > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Supplementary Exam 2023]`** #### **(a) List and explain all the features of Java. Why is Java considered robust and secure? [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** Java is a feature-rich language. The following are the **12 official buzzwords/features**: 1. **Simple** — Removes complexities of C++ like pointers, operator overloading, and multiple inheritance. 2. **Object-Oriented** — Everything is an object; supports OOP principles. 3. **Platform Independent** — Bytecode runs on any OS with a JVM. 4. **Secured** — No pointers, runs inside JVM sandbox, bytecode verifier. 5. **Robust** — Strong type checking, exception handling, automatic garbage collection. 6. **Architecture Neutral** — Primitive sizes are fixed (e.g., `int` = 32 bits). 7. **Portable** — Same bytecode runs anywhere. 8. **Multithreaded** — Built-in `Thread` class for parallel execution. 9. **Interpreted** — Bytecode interpreted by JVM. 10. **High Performance** — JIT compiler accelerates hot code paths. 11. **Distributed** — Designed for network programming (RMI, sockets). 12. **Dynamic** — Supports runtime class loading and reflection. **Why Java is Robust:** - **Strong memory management** — No manual `malloc`/`free`; Garbage Collector automatically reclaims unused memory, preventing memory leaks. - **Exception handling** — Built-in `try-catch-finally` and checked exceptions force developers to handle errors. - **Strong type checking** — Compile-time type safety eliminates many runtime crashes. - **No pointers** — Eliminates direct memory corruption. **Why Java is Secure:** - Programs run inside the **JVM sandbox** with restricted access to system resources. - **Bytecode Verifier** validates every loaded class for illegal operations. - **Security Manager** and **ClassLoader** enforce access policies. - No pointer arithmetic prevents unauthorized memory access. - **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** is built into `java.security` package. #### **(b) Write a Java program to demonstrate primitive data types, type casting (implicit and explicit), and command-line argument handling. Explain each section. [7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** ```java // File: PrimitiveDemo.java public class PrimitiveDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // ---- Primitive Data Types ---- byte b = 100; short s = 20000; int i = 100000; long l = 9999999999L; float f = 3.14f; double d = 3.1415926535; char c = 'K'; boolean bool = true; System.out.println("byte b = " + b); System.out.println("short s = " + s); System.out.println("int i = " + i); System.out.println("long l = " + l); System.out.println("float f = " + f); System.out.println("double d = " + d); System.out.println("char c = " + c); System.out.println("boolean bool = " + bool); // ---- Implicit (Widening) Conversion ---- int num = 100; double wide = num; // int -> double, automatic System.out.println("Implicit (int to double): " + wide); // ---- Explicit (Narrowing) Conversion ---- double pi = 3.14159; int narrowed = (int) pi; // double -> int, manual cast System.out.println("Explicit (double to int): " + narrowed); // ---- Command-Line Arguments ---- System.out.println("Number of arguments: " + args.length); if (args.length > 0) { System.out.println("First argument: " + args[0]); } else { System.out.println("No arguments passed."); } } } ``` **Compilation & Execution:** ```bash javac PrimitiveDemo.java java PrimitiveDemo KTU 2024 ``` **Explanation of Each Section:** 1. **Class & Main Method:** `public class PrimitiveDemo` declares the class. `public static void main(String[] args)` is the JVM entry point. `public` allows the JVM to call it from outside; `static` lets it run without creating an object; `void` means it returns nothing. 2. **Primitive Declarations:** Each primitive variable is declared with an explicit type, demonstrating fixed sizes (8–64 bits) and proper suffixes (`L` for `long`, `f` for `float`). 3. **Implicit Conversion:** Assigning `int num` to `double wide` is automatic because `double` has a larger range. No data loss occurs. 4. **Explicit Conversion:** Assigning `double pi = 3.14159` to `int narrowed` requires the cast operator `(int)`. The fractional part `.14159` is **truncated**, leaving `3`. This may cause data loss. 5. **Command-Line Arguments:** The `args` array receives values passed on the command line. `args.length` gives the count, and `args[0]` accesses the first argument. If none are passed, the `else` block runs. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to Java** - **Java** = high-level, class-based, OOP language by *James Gosling* at Sun Microsystems (1991/1995). - **WORA** = Write Once, Run Anywhere — the cornerstone of platform independence. - **Three Editions:** Java **SE** (core), **EE** (enterprise), **ME** (micro). KTU focuses on **SE**. - **JDK** ⊃ **JRE** ⊃ **JVM** — strict containment hierarchy. - **JDK** = JRE + development tools like `javac`, `javadoc`, `jdb`, `jar`. - **JRE** = JVM + library classes (`rt.jar`, `java.lang`, `java.util`). - **JVM** = abstract runtime engine with 3 subsystems: ClassLoader, Runtime Data Areas, Execution Engine. - **JVM is platform-dependent; bytecode is platform-independent.** This is the most common KTU trick question. - **Compilation** = `javac Hello.java` → `Hello.class` (bytecode). - **Execution** = `java Hello` → JVM loads, verifies, and executes. - **SC-L-V-E** = Source → Compile → Load → Verify → Execute pipeline. - **5 Memory Areas in JVM:** Method (shared), Heap (shared), Stack (private), PC Register (private), Native Method Stack (private). - **8 Primitive Types:** `byte` (8), `short` (16), `int` (32), `long` (64), `float` (32), `double` (64), `char` (16 Unicode), `boolean` (1-bit logical). - **Primitive sizes are FIXED** in Java — this guarantees architecture neutrality. - **Implicit (widening)** = small → large type, automatic, no data loss. - **Explicit (narrowing)** = large → small type, requires cast, may lose data. - **Garbage Collection** is automatic in Java; no `free()` or `delete`. - **No pointers** in Java → robust and secure. - **`main` signature must be exactly:** `public static void main(String[] args)`. - **File name MUST match public class name**, e.g., `Hello.java` contains `public class Hello`. - **JIT Compiler** optimizes hot methods at runtime for high performance. - **Two-step translation** (compile + interpret) is what enables platform independence. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, **always include a diagram** (JVM architecture or execution flow) and **label all 12 features** if asked. Examiners reward structured answers with sub-headings and tables.

topics.title: Java Programming and Runtime Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Java Programming and Runtime Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Java?

**Java** is a high-level, strongly-typed, object-oriented, platform-independent, general-purpose programming language developed by **James Gosling** and his team at **Sun Microsystems** in **1991**, officially released in **1995**. It follows the philosophy of **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)**, meaning compiled Java code can execute on any operating system that supports the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) without recompilation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEERT613 - Module 1)**
> Java is the primary implementation language for studying object-oriented concepts. KTU expects you to master the *Java Runtime Environment (JRE)* and the *Java Development Kit (JDK)* — these are the *two most repeated 3-mark questions* in the past three University Exam cycles.

### Conceptual Analogy 💡

Imagine you wrote a letter in **English**. You want someone in **France, Japan, and Russia** to read it.
- **Without WORA**: You translate the letter into French, Japanese, and Russian separately (platform-dependent compilation).
- **With Java (WORA)**: You write the letter once in English, and a **Universal Translator (JVM)** is installed in each country. The translator converts it on-the-fly into the local language.

The letter = **Java source code (.java)**  
The translated letters = **Native machine code**  
The Universal Translator = **JVM installed on each device**

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of the Java Platform

| Component | Full Form | Role | Audience |
|-----------|-----------|------|----------|
| **JDK** | Java Development Kit | Develops & compiles Java programs | Programmers |
| **JRE** | Java Runtime Environment | Runs Java programs (contains JVM + libraries) | End users |
| **JVM** | Java Virtual Machine | Executes bytecode on the host machine | Internal engine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Relationship:**
> $$\text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM}$$
> If you install **JDK**, you automatically get **JRE** and **JVM**. If you only install **JRE**, you can *run* Java programs but cannot *compile* them.

## 1.3 Key Buzzwords of Java (KTU-Favorite Definitions)

1. **Simple** – No pointers, no multiple inheritance for classes, automatic garbage collection.
2. **Object-Oriented** – Everything is an object (except primitives), built around 4 pillars: *Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism*.
3. **Platform-Independent** – Compile once, run on any OS with a JVM.
4. **Secure** – Runs inside a sandbox; no explicit pointer manipulation.
5. **Robust** – Strong memory management, exception handling, type checking.
6. **Multithreaded** – Built-in support for concurrent execution.
7. **Architectural-Neutral** – Bytecode is independent of processor architecture.
8. **High Performance** – Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler boosts speed.
9. **Distributed** – Supports RMI and EJB for network programming.
10. **Dynamic** – Loads classes on demand at runtime.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 3-markers, memorize: *"Simple, Secure, Portable, Robust, Object-Oriented, Platform-Independent, Multithreaded, Architectural-Neutral."* Any 5 from this list fetch full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the Java Platform (Layered Model)

Java's power lies in its **two-step translation process** that decouples code from hardware.

### Step-by-Step Execution Pipeline

1. **Source Code Creation** — Programmer writes `MyProgram.java` in any text editor or IDE.
2. **Compilation** — The **Java Compiler (`javac.exe`)** translates `.java` into **bytecode** stored in `.class` file.
   - Bytecode is *not* native machine code; it is an **intermediate representation** (ISA for the JVM).
3. **Class Loading** — The **Class Loader Subsystem** loads `.class` files into JVM memory on demand.
4. **Bytecode Verification** — The **Bytecode Verifier** checks for illegal code, stack overflows, type mismatches (security gate).
5. **Execution** — The **Execution Engine** (Interpreter + JIT Compiler) translates bytecode into native machine instructions.
6. **Runtime Data Areas** — Memory is partitioned into Heap, Stack, Method Area, PC Registers, and Native Method Stacks.

## 2.2 Inside the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) — Detailed Subsystems

### A. Class Loader Subsystem

| Phase | Action |
|-------|--------|
| **Loading** | Reads `.class` file via Bootstrap → Extension → Application class loaders |
| **Linking** | Verifies bytecode, allocates static memory, resolves symbolic references |
| **Initialization** | Executes static initializers and static block statements |

### B. Runtime Data Areas (Memory Zones)

| Memory Zone | Purpose | Thread Scope |
|-------------|---------|--------------|
| **Method Area** | Stores class metadata, static variables, constant pool, method bytecode | Shared (per JVM) |
| **Heap Area** | Stores all objects and instance variables — *garbage collected* | Shared (per JVM) |
| **Stack Area** | Stores method frames, local variables, partial results | Per-thread (private) |
| **PC Registers** | Holds address of current executing JVM instruction | Per-thread |
| **Native Method Stack** | Holds native (C/C++) method calls | Per-thread |

### C. Execution Engine

- **Interpreter** — Reads bytecode line-by-line and executes. *Slow but quick startup.*
- **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler** — Compiles *hot* (frequently executed) bytecode into native code at runtime. *Faster execution after warm-up.*
- **Garbage Collector (GC)** — Automatically reclaims memory of unreferenced objects from the **Heap**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a *must-memorize* quick reference for KTU Module 1 derivations and viva questions.

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| Java Source File | `*.java` | Plain text |
| Java Compiled File | `*.class` (Bytecode) | Binary |
| Bytecode Instruction Size | 1 byte opcode + operands | Stack-based ISA |
| JDK = JRE + Development Tools | $\text{JDK} = \text{JRE} \cup \{\text{javac, jar, javadoc, jdb}\}$ | Tools for coders |
| JRE = JVM + Library Classes | $\text{JRE} = \text{JVM} \cup \text{rt.jar} \cup \text{Java API}$ | Runtime support |
| WORA Condition | $\text{Bytecode} \rightarrow \text{Any OS with compatible JVM}$ | Platform-independent |
| Class Loading Order | $\text{bootstrap} \rightarrow \text{extension} \rightarrow \text{system (application)}$ | Delegation model |
| Heap Memory Division (Old Gen) | $\text{Tenured Space} + \text{Permanent Space (pre-Java 8)}$ | Garbage collected |
| Stack Frame Content | $\text{Locals} + \text{Operand Stack} + \text{Frame Data}$ | Per method call |
| Compile Command | `javac FileName.java` | Produces `FileName.class` |
| Run Command | `java FileName` (no extension!) | Invokes JVM |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table, note the use of `\cup` to express *set union* (e.g., $\text{JDK} = \text{JRE} \cup \{\text{Development Tools}\}$). This mathematical notation is used because JDK and JRE are technically *sets of components*.

## 2.4 Why Java is Used in Real Engineering

- **Enterprise Web Apps** (Spring, Jakarta EE)
- **Android Mobile Development** (primary language for years)
- **Embedded Systems & IoT** (Java ME, JavaCard)
- **Scientific Computing** (Apache Spark, Hadoop)
- **Banking & Financial Systems** (high security, multi-threading)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Java Execution Flow (From Source to Native Code)

Let us mathematically trace a Java program's life cycle from creation to execution.

**Given:**
- Source file: `HelloWorld.java`
- Host platform: Any OS (say, Windows 11)

**Find:** Step-by-step transformation to native execution.

---

### Step 1 — Source Code Authoring

The programmer writes:

```java
public class HelloWorld {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello, KTU!");
    }
}
```

**Logical meaning:** Defines a class `HelloWorld` with a `main` method — the entry point.

---

### Step 2 — Compilation by `javac`

The command issued is:
```
javac HelloWorld.java
```

**Logical conversion:**
$$
\text{HelloWorld.java} \xrightarrow{\text{javac (JavaC Compiler)}} \text{HelloWorld.class}
$$

The output is **bytecode**, not machine code. Bytecode consists of instructions for an *abstract stack machine* (the JVM). A sample bytecode instruction set looks like:

$$
\text{getstatic} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{ldc "Hello, KTU!"} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{invokevirtual} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{return}
$$

**Reasoning:** Bytecode is a *uniform intermediate language*. By compiling to bytecode instead of native code, Java achieves platform independence.

---

### Step 3 — Class Loading

When the command `java HelloWorld` is executed, the **JVM's Class Loader Subsystem** is invoked.

$$
\text{Class Loader} \rightarrow \text{Loading} \rightarrow \text{Linking} \rightarrow \text{Initialization}
$$

The class is located via the **classpath**, then loaded into the **Method Area** of JVM memory.

---

### Step 4 — Bytecode Verification

The verifier checks four rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rule 1:} & \quad \text{No stack overflow/underflow} \\
\text{Rule 2:} & \quad \text{No illegal data type conversions} \\
\text{Rule 3:} & \quad \text{No illegal access to private members} \\
\text{Rule 4:} & \quad \text{No invalid jumps / branch targets}
\end{aligned}
$$

If any rule is violated, the JVM throws a `VerifyError` and refuses to execute.

---

### Step 5 — Execution Engine Translation

$$
\text{Bytecode} \xrightarrow{\text{Interpreter (initial)}} \text{Native Code (per line)} \quad \text{[Slow]}
$$

$$
\text{Bytecode} \xrightarrow{\text{JIT Compiler (for hot methods)}} \text{Native Code (cached)} \quad \text{[Fast}]
$$

The **JIT compiler** identifies methods that are called more than a threshold number of times (typically 10,000) and compiles them into native machine code, caching it in the **Code Cache**.

---

### Step 6 — Native Execution

$$
\text{Native Code} \rightarrow \text{CPU Registers} \rightarrow \text{Output: "Hello, KTU!"}
$$

The CPU executes the native instructions, and the output appears on the standard output stream (`stdout`).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table for KTU

> This comparison matrix is the **single most important table** for KTU Module 1. Memorize the contrasting pairs.

| Feature | C / C++ | Java |
|---------|---------|------|
| Platform Dependency | Platform-dependent (compiled to native code) | **Platform-independent (bytecode + JVM)** |
| Memory Management | Manual (`malloc`/`free` in C, `new`/`delete` in C++) | **Automatic garbage collection** |
| Pointers | Direct pointer arithmetic allowed | **No pointers exposed** (handled internally by JVM) |
| Multiple Inheritance | Supported (C++) | **Not supported for classes** (uses interfaces) |
| Compilation | Direct to native machine code | **Compiled to bytecode, then interpreted/JIT'd** |
| Run-Time Environment | OS + Compiler-specific runtime | **JRE with JVM** |
| Security | Low (buffer overflow risks) | **High (sandbox + bytecode verifier)** |
| Threading | OS-level (Pthreads / Win32) | **Built-in (`Thread` class, `synchronized` keyword)** |
| Preprocessor | `#include`, `#define` macros | **No preprocessor** (uses packages & imports) |
| Default Entry Point | `int main()` | `public static void main(String[] args)` |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** "**Java = C++ minus the dangerous parts**" — this single sentence summarizes why Java was designed the way it is.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Listing: Components Inside `rt.jar` (Java Standard Library)

The `rt.jar` (runtime archive) houses the pre-built Java API. It contains approximately **4,000+ classes** organized into packages.

| Package | Purpose | KTU-Relevant Classes |
|---------|---------|----------------------|
| `java.lang` | Core language (auto-imported) | `String`, `Object`, `System`, `Math`, `Thread` |
| `java.util` | Data structures & utilities | `ArrayList`, `HashMap`, `Scanner`, `Date` |
| `java.io` | Input/Output streams | `File`, `FileInputStream`, `BufferedReader` |
| `java.net` | Networking | `URL`, `Socket`, `ServerSocket` |
| `java.awt` / `javax.swing` | GUI programming | `JFrame`, `JButton`, `JPanel` |
| `java.sql` | Database connectivity | `Connection`, `Statement`, `ResultSet` |
| `java.applet` | Old browser applets | `Applet` (deprecated) |

> **Note for KTU:** Questions on `java.lang` package (especially `Object` class methods like `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()`) are extremely common in the **14-mark** section.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Java Execution Pipeline (End-to-End Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Programmer writes Source Code MyProgram.java] --> B[Step 2: Java Compiler javac compiles .java file]
    B --> C[Step 3: Bytecode generated MyProgram.class]
    C --> D[Step 4: Class Loader Subsystem loads .class into JVM]
    D --> E[Step 5: Bytecode Verifier checks safety rules]
    E --> F[Step 6: Execution Engine interprets or JIT compiles]
    F --> G[Step 7: Native Machine Code executed by CPU]
    G --> H[Step 8: Output displayed on stdout]

    subgraph sec1 [Compilation Phase - Runs Once]
        A
        B
        C
    end

    subgraph sec2 [Runtime Phase - Runs on Target Machine]
        D
        E
        F
        G
        H
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The first subgraph (Compilation) is JDK's responsibility. The second subgraph (Runtime) is JRE + JVM's responsibility. This visual separation is what gives Java its **WORA** superpower.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: JVM Internal Architecture (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cls [Class Loader Subsystem]
        CL1[Loading Bootstrap / Extension / Application Loaders]
        CL2[Linking Verify Prepare Resolve]
        CL3[Initialization Static Block Execution]
        CL1 --> CL2 --> CL3
    end

    subgraph mem [Runtime Data Areas]
        M1[Method Area Class Metadata Constant Pool]
        M2[Heap Area All Objects Instance Variables]
        M3[Stack Area Per Thread Method Frames]
        M4[PC Registers Instruction Address]
        M5[Native Method Stack C Cpp Methods]
    end

    subgraph eng [Execution Engine]
        E1[Interpreter Line by Line Execution]
        E2[JIT Compiler Hot Method Native Code Cache]
        E3[Garbage Collector Heap Reclamation]
    end

    subgraph jni [JNI Native Method Interface]
        J1[Native Libraries C Cpp]
    end

    CL3 --> mem
    M2 --> E3
    M3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E1 --> J1
    J1 --> J1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The four major JVM subsystems — Class Loader, Memory, Execution Engine, and JNI — work cooperatively. The **Heap** is where the **Garbage Collector** continuously operates in the background.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: JDK vs JRE vs JVM Containment Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph jdk [JDK Java Development Kit]
        jdk1[javac Compiler]
        jdk2[jar Archiver]
        jdk3[javadoc Doc Generator]
        jdk4[jdb Debugger]
        subgraph jre [JRE Java Runtime Environment]
            jre1[Class Loader]
            jre2[Bytecode Verifier]
            subgraph jvm [JVM Java Virtual Machine]
                jvm1[Execution Engine]
                jvm2[Interpreter]
                jvm3[JIT Compiler]
                jvm4[Garbage Collector]
            end
            jre3[Java API Libraries rt.jar]
        end
    end
```

> **Key takeaway:** A student who only wants to **run** Java programs needs only the JRE. A developer needs the full JDK. The JVM is the *engine* tucked inside the JRE.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Java Program Structure (Anatomy of a `.java` File)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Java Source File MyApp.java] --> B[Package Declaration optional]
    A --> C[Import Statements e.g. import java.util.Scanner]
    A --> D[Class Definition public class MyApp]
    D --> E[Class Body]
    E --> F[Instance Variables Attributes]
    E --> G[Static Variables Class Level]
    E --> H[Constructors Object Initialization]
    E --> I[Instance Methods Behaviors]
    E --> J[Static Methods Utility Functions]
    E --> K[Main Method Entry Point]
    K --> K1[public static void main String args]
```

> **Rule:** In Java, **everything** lives inside a class. There are no top-level functions or global variables (a key contrast with C/C++).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between JDK, JRE, and JVM.**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

- **JDK (Java Development Kit)** is a software bundle used by developers to **build, compile, and debug** Java applications. It contains the `javac` compiler, `javadoc`, `jdb` debugger, and the JRE.

- **JRE (Java Runtime Environment)** provides the **runtime environment** required to *run* Java programs. It contains the JVM and the standard library classes (`rt.jar`).

- **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** is an **abstract machine** that executes Java bytecode. It is platform-dependent and converts bytecode into native machine instructions for the host OS.

**Relationship:** $\text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM}$.

> *Distribution: 1 mark for each distinction + 1 mark for the hierarchical relationship.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the "Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA) feature of Java.**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

Java achieves platform independence through a **two-stage compilation process**:
1. The Java compiler (`javac`) converts source code (`.java`) into **intermediate bytecode** (`.class`).
2. This bytecode is **executed by the JVM** installed on the host machine, which translates it into native machine code.

Since bytecode is the *same* for all platforms, and *each platform has its own JVM*, the same compiled code runs everywhere without modification — hence "Write Once, Run Anywhere."

> *Distribution: 1 mark for two-stage process + 1 mark for JVM translation + 1 mark for conclusion.*

---

## 📝 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **ONE full question out of two choices**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

#### **(a)** Explain the architecture of the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) with a neat diagram. List its main subsystems. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** What is Bytecode? Explain the role of the JIT compiler in improving Java program performance. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks:

The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is a platform-dependent abstract computing engine that enables Java's platform independence. Its architecture consists of **three major subsystems**:

**1. Class Loader Subsystem (2 marks):**
- **Loading:** Reads `.class` files using Bootstrap, Extension, and Application class loaders following the **delegation model**.
- **Linking:** Verifies bytecode correctness, prepares static memory, resolves symbolic references.
- **Initialization:** Executes static initializers and `static` blocks in top-to-bottom order.

**2. Runtime Data Areas / Memory (2.5 marks):**
- **Method Area** — Stores class-level data (metadata, static variables, constant pool). Shared across threads.
- **Heap Area** — Stores all `new` objects. Garbage collected. Shared.
- **Stack Area** — Per-thread LIFO structure storing method frames, local variables, and operand stacks.
- **PC Registers** — Per-thread, holds address of the currently executing JVM instruction.
- **Native Method Stack** — Supports native (C/C++) method calls.

**3. Execution Engine (2.5 marks):**
- **Interpreter:** Reads and executes bytecode line-by-line. Fast startup, slow execution.
- **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler:** Compiles "hot" (frequently called) bytecode methods into native code, caches them in the **Code Cache**, dramatically improving performance.
- **Garbage Collector (GC):** Automatically reclaims heap memory of unreferenced objects, eliminating manual memory management.

> *(Refer to Diagram 4.2 in Section 4 for the architectural visual.)*

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks:

**Bytecode (3.5 marks):**
Bytecode is the **intermediate, platform-neutral instruction set** generated by the Java compiler (`javac`) when compiling a `.java` file. It is stored in `.class` files.

- Each bytecode instruction is a **1-byte opcode** followed by zero or more operands.
- It is designed for a **stack-based abstract machine** (the JVM).
- Bytecode is *not* directly understandable by the CPU; it must be interpreted or JIT-compiled by the JVM.
- This intermediate representation is the foundation of Java's **WORA** property.

**JIT Compiler (3.5 marks):**
The **Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler** is part of the JVM's execution engine. It works as follows:

1. Initially, the **interpreter** executes the bytecode line by line (slow but immediate).
2. The JIT profiler monitors method call frequency.
3. When a method's call count exceeds a threshold (e.g., 10,000 invocations), it is marked as a **"hot method"**.
4. The JIT compiler translates this hot bytecode into **optimized native machine code** and stores it in the **Code Cache**.
5. Subsequent invocations use the cached native code directly, bypassing the interpreter.

**Performance benefit:** Execution speed approaches that of compiled languages (C/C++) for compute-intensive code, while retaining Java's portability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice for Internal Selection]**

#### **(a)** List and explain any **five features** of Java that make it suitable for object-oriented programming. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the structure of a Java application with a class, instance variables, methods, and the `main` method. Explain each component. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks:

1. **Simple (1.4 marks):** Java eliminates complex C++ features like pointers, multiple inheritance, and operator overloading. It uses automatic garbage collection, so programmers don't manage memory manually.

2. **Object-Oriented (1.4 marks):** Java is built entirely around objects and classes. It supports all four OOP pillars — *Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism* — making it ideal for modular, reusable code.

3. **Platform-Independent (1.4 marks):** Java code compiles to bytecode, which runs on any OS with a compatible JVM. This is critical for cross-platform enterprise software.

4. **Robust (1.4 marks):** Strong type-checking, exception handling, and automatic memory management make Java programs reliable and crash-resistant.

5. **Multithreaded (1.4 marks):** Java has built-in support for concurrent execution using the `Thread` class and `synchronized` keyword, vital for responsive GUIs and high-performance servers.

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks:

```java
// File: StudentDemo.java
// Package declaration (optional)
package oert613.module1;

// Import statement (optional, for using Scanner)
import java.util.Scanner;

// Class definition
public class StudentDemo {
    // 1. Instance variables (attributes of a Student)
    private String name;
    private int rollNumber;

    // 2. Constructor (used to initialize objects)
    public StudentDemo(String name, int rollNumber) {
        this.name = name;
        this.rollNumber = rollNumber;
    }

    // 3. Instance method (behavior of a Student)
    public void displayInfo() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);
        System.out.println("Roll No: " + rollNumber);
    }

    // 4. Main method (entry point of the application)
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Creating an object of StudentDemo
        StudentDemo s1 = new StudentDemo("Ananya", 42);
        s1.displayInfo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of each component (component-wise marking):**

| Component | Line | Purpose |
|-----------|------|---------|
| `package` | `package oert613.module1;` | Organizes related classes into a namespace |
| `import` | `import java.util.Scanner;` | Brings in pre-built classes from other packages |
| `class` | `public class StudentDemo` | Blueprint/template for creating objects |
| Instance variables | `name`, `rollNumber` | State/attributes unique to each object |
| Constructor | `public StudentDemo(...)` | Initializes a newly created object |
| Instance method | `displayInfo()` | Defines behavior of the object |
| `main` method | `public static void main(...)` | JVM calls this first; entry point |

> *Mark split: 3.5 marks for correct code + 3.5 marks for proper explanation with the above table.*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in Module 1 questions:**
> 1. **Confusing JDK and JRE** — Writing *"JRE is used to compile"* → **-2 marks**. JRE *runs* programs; only the JDK *compiles* them.
> 2. **Saying "Java is platform-independent" without justification** → KTU expects you to mention **bytecode + JVM** explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting `static` in `main` method** — In code-writing questions, missing `public static void main` causes **-1 to -2 marks** depending on the examiner.
> 4. **No diagram in JVM question** — A 7-mark JVM question *without* a labeled diagram will rarely score above **4/7**. Always draw the three subsystems.
> 5. **Confusing Heap and Stack** — Heap stores **objects**; Stack stores **method frames and local variables**. Swap them and lose **2 marks**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1:**

- ✅ Java = **class-based, object-oriented, strongly-typed** language by **James Gosling (Sun Microsystems, 1995)**.
- ✅ **WORA** is achieved by compiling `.java` → `.class` (bytecode) → executed by **JVM** (platform-specific).
- ✅ Hierarchy: **JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM**. Only JDK has the compiler (`javac`).
- ✅ **Bytecode** is stack-based, 1-byte opcodes, stored in `.class` files. It is **not** machine code.
- ✅ **JVM subsystems**: Class Loader, Runtime Data Areas, Execution Engine, JNI.
- ✅ **Memory zones**: Method Area (class metadata), Heap (objects — GC'd), Stack (method frames, per-thread), PC Registers, Native Method Stack.
- ✅ **JIT compiler** converts hot methods to native code, cached in Code Cache — boosts performance.
- ✅ **Garbage Collection** runs on the **Heap** automatically; eliminates `malloc`/`free`.
- ✅ **No pointers, no multiple inheritance for classes, no preprocessor** — Java simplifies C++.
- ✅ **Java program structure**: package → import → class → variables → constructors → methods → `main`.
- ✅ `main` method signature (MUST memorize): `public static void main(String[] args)`.
- ✅ Compiling: `javac FileName.java` | Running: `java FileName` (no `.class` extension).
- ✅ The **five most-tested buzzwords**: *Simple, Secure, Portable, Robust, Multithreaded*.
- ✅ JVM is **platform-dependent**; bytecode is **platform-independent** — this is the precise KTU phrasing.
- ✅ `rt.jar` (replaced by modules in Java 9+) contains `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, etc.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development Platforms - Java Virtual Machine (JVM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Development Platforms - Java Virtual Machine (JVM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is an abstract computing machine (a runtime engine) that enables a computer to run a Java program. It is the component of the Java Runtime Environment (JRE) responsible for loading, verifying, linking, initializing, and executing Java bytecode. The JVM is the cornerstone of Java's famous **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** paradigm, since the same compiled bytecode can be executed on any platform that hosts a conformant JVM implementation.

The JVM is formally specified by the **Java Language Specification (JLS)** and the **Java Virtual Machine Specification (JVMS)** published by Oracle. It is:

- **Platform-dependent** at the implementation level (a Windows JVM differs from a Linux JVM).
- **Platform-independent** at the bytecode level (the same `.class` file runs on all of them).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The JVM does **not** understand Java source code (`.java` files). It only understands **bytecode** (`.class` files). The Java compiler (`javac`) performs the translation from source to bytecode, after which the JVM takes over.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of the JVM as a **universal power adapter for electronics**:

- Imagine you travel from India to the USA. Your Indian laptop charger (3-pin plug) does not fit into a US wall socket (2-pin).
- You buy a **universal travel adapter** — it accepts any plug shape on one side and delivers a standardized current on the other.
- The **Java bytecode** is like your charger's plug (standardized, fixed shape).
- The **physical machine (Windows/Mac/Linux)** is the wall socket (different shapes in every country).
- The **JVM** is the universal adapter — it presents a uniform interface to the bytecode and translates it into the host machine's native instructions.
