Without the JVM, Java bytecode would be like a charger with the "wrong plug" for every device. With the JVM, **one bytecode runs on every device that has the adapter installed**.

## 1.3 Core Properties of the JVM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The JVM is characterized by three primary properties, all of which are **frequently asked in KTU exams**:

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Stack-based** | The JVM uses a **stack-based execution model** (operand stack), unlike register-based architectures (e.g., x86, ARM). |
| **Spec-defined** | The behavior is dictated by the JVMS document, not a single implementation. Vendors (Oracle, OpenJDK, Azul, IBM Semeru) provide conforming implementations. |
| **Garbage-collected** | The JVM automatically manages memory deallocation through a built-in **Garbage Collector (GC)** — the programmer does not `free()` memory manually. |

## 1.4 JVM, JRE, and JDK — The Relationship

These three acronyms are routinely confused. KTU examiners love testing this distinction.

| Component | Full Form | Role | Contains |
|---|---|---|---|
| **JVM** | Java Virtual Machine | Runtime engine that executes bytecode | Execution engine, memory areas, class loader, GC |
| **JRE** | Java Runtime Environment | Provides libraries + JVM to **run** Java programs | JVM + Core libraries (rt.jar / java.base module) |
| **JDK** | Java Development Kit | Provides tools to **develop + run** Java programs | JRE + Compiler (`javac`) + Debugger + Tools |

A useful rule of thumb:

$$ \text{JDK} = \text{JRE} + \text{Development Tools} $$

$$ \text{JRE} = \text{JVM} + \text{Java Standard Library} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory aid:** *JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM* (each is a superset of the next).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Execution Model

Java's execution follows a strict three-stage pipeline:

1. **Compile Time** — The Java source file (`MyApp.java`) is compiled by `javac` into bytecode (`MyApp.class`). Bytecode is a low-level, platform-neutral instruction set for the JVM. Each `.class` file contains:
   - **Magic Number** (`0xCAFEBABE`) — 4 bytes identifying it as a valid class file.
   - **Constant Pool** — symbolic references to fields, methods, and string literals.
   - **Method Bytecode** — actual JVM instructions for each method.
   - **Access Flags, this/super class, interfaces, fields, methods, attributes.**

2. **Class Loading & Verification** — When the program is launched, the **Class Loader Subsystem** reads `.class` files, parses them, performs **bytecode verification** (ensuring the code does not violate JVM safety rules, e.g., no stack overflows, no illegal type casts), and prepares the runtime data structures.

3. **Execution** — The **Execution Engine** interprets the bytecode line by line. Hot (frequently-executed) methods are further compiled to native machine code by the **Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler** for performance.

## 2.2 JVM Architecture — The Core Subsystems

The JVM is conventionally divided into **three major subsystems**:

### A. Class Loader Subsystem

Responsible for loading, linking, and initializing classes. It uses a **delegation hierarchy**:

- **Bootstrap ClassLoader** — loads core Java classes from `rt.jar` (or `java.base` module in Java 9+). Written in native C/C++.
- **Platform (Extension) ClassLoader** — loads classes from the extension directory (`java.ext.dirs` or extension modules).
- **Application (System) ClassLoader** — loads classes from the application's classpath (`-cp` / `CLASSPATH`).
- **User-defined ClassLoaders** — programmers may write custom loaders (e.g., for hot deployment, web containers, modular apps).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delegation Model:** When a class loader receives a load request, it first **delegates to its parent** before attempting to load the class itself. This prevents malicious code from overriding core classes like `java.lang.String`.

### B. Runtime Data Areas (Memory)

The JVM partitions memory into five logical regions:

| Memory Area | Purpose | Thread Scope | Key Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Method Area** | Stores class metadata, static variables, constant pool, method bytecode, JIT-compiled native code | Shared across threads | Also called *Metaspace* (Java 8+; replaced *PermGen*). |
| **Heap Area** | Stores all **objects** and instance variables | Shared across threads | Garbage-collected. Divided into *Young* (Eden + 2 Survivors) and *Old* (Tenured) generations. |
| **Stack Area** | One **JVM Stack per thread**, holding *Stack Frames* for active methods | Per-thread | Stores local variables, operand stack, frame data. |
| **PC Register** | Holds address of the currently executing JVM instruction | Per-thread | One PC per thread; native methods set its value to `undefined`. |
| **Native Method Stack** | Holds native (C/C++) method call state for JNI calls | Per-thread | Used when Java invokes platform-specific code via JNI. |

### C. Execution Engine

The brain of the JVM. It has three components:

1. **Interpreter** — Reads bytecode instructions one by one and dispatches them. **Slow** because every call re-translates bytecode to native.
2. **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler** — Detects *hot methods* (executed many times — threshold typically `10000` invocations) and compiles them to native machine code. The compiled code is cached in the **Code Cache** within the Method Area. **Two tiers exist:**
   - **C1 (Client) Compiler** — quick compile, basic optimizations. Used for moderately-hot code.
   - **C2 (Server) Compiler** — slower compile, aggressive optimizations (inlining, loop unrolling, escape analysis). Used for very-hot code.
   - In **Java 10+**, **Graal** replaced C2 as the default top-tier JIT compiler.
3. **Garbage Collector (GC)** — Automatically reclaims heap memory occupied by unreferenced objects. Common algorithms:
   - **Serial GC** — single-threaded, suitable for small applications.
   - **Parallel GC** — multi-threaded, throughput-oriented (default in Java 8).
   - **G1 GC (Garbage-First)** — default since Java 9; divides heap into regions, prioritizes regions with most garbage.
   - **ZGC, Shenandoah** — ultra-low-pause concurrent collectors for large heaps.

### D. Supporting Components

- **JNI (Java Native Interface)** — interface for calling native C/C++ libraries.
- **Native Method Libraries** — platform-specific `.dll`/`.so`/`.dylib` files providing native method implementations.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **cheat-sheet** for last-minute KTU revision. Memorize every row.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Source to Bytecode | `javac MyApp.java` $\rightarrow$ `MyApp.class` | Compilation step |
| Bytecode Magic | `\text{Magic Number} = 0xCAFEBABE` | Identifies a valid class file |
| Stack frame formula | `\text{Frame Size} = \text{Locals} + \text{Operand Stack Cap} + \text{Frame Data}` | Memory required per method invocation |
| JIT threshold | `\text{Invocation count} \geq 10000` (tunable via `-XX:CompileThreshold`) | Triggers JIT compilation |
| Heap generation ratio (default) | `\text{Young} : \text{Old} = 1 : 2` (configurable via `-Xmn`, `-XX:NewRatio`) | Default heap division |
| Object eligibility for GC | No **strong reference** from a GC root | Reachability rule |
| Class loading delegation | `\text{Child loader} \rightarrow \text{Parent loader} \rightarrow \text{Parent's parent} \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow \text{Bootstrap}` | Order of class lookup |
| WORA property | `\text{Same bytecode} + \text{Platform-specific JVM} = \text{Cross-platform execution}` | Core Java advantage |
| JDK relation | `\text{JDK} = \text{JRE} + \text{Compiler + Tools}` | Tool hierarchy |
| JRE relation | `\text{JRE} = \text{JVM} + \text{Runtime Libraries}` | Runtime hierarchy |
| Stack-based execution | Each JVM instruction pops operands from stack, computes, pushes result | No explicit registers in bytecode |
| Type safety check | `\text{Bytecode Verifier} \rightarrow \text{validates type correctness before execution}` | Security layer |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The JVM is not just an academic concept. It is the foundation of **massive production systems**:

- **Big Data:** Apache Hadoop, Apache Spark, Apache Flink, Kafka all run on the JVM for performance + portability.
- **Enterprise Backend:** Spring Boot, Jakarta EE servers (Tomcat, WildFly), microservices.
- **Android (historically):** Android's Dalvik/ART VM is a JVM-derivative. Modern Android also uses the standard OpenJDK for some components.
- **Financial Systems:** Banks use the JVM for high-frequency trading because of predictable GC behavior and JIT optimization.
- **Kotlin, Scala, Groovy, Clojure:** These languages all compile to **JVM bytecode** and run on the standard JVM — proving the JVM is a **polyglot platform**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The JVM's design has influenced **other runtimes**: the **CLR (Common Language Runtime)** of Microsoft's .NET, **BEAM** (Erlang's VM), and the **V8 JavaScript engine** all borrowed architectural ideas from the JVM.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **mechanical execution flow** of a Java program and derives the memory layout step-by-step. KTU frequently asks students to trace the execution path, calculate memory usage, or explain a class-loading scenario.

## 3.1 Detailed Execution Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

Consider the following canonical Java program (used conceptually — not for compilation here):

```
File: Hello.java
    public class Hello { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Hi"); } }
```

### Stage 1 — Compilation (Compile Time)

**Step 1.1:** The Java source file `Hello.java` is passed to `javac` (the Java compiler bundled with the JDK).

**Step 1.2:** `javac` performs lexical analysis (tokenization), parsing (builds an Abstract Syntax Tree), semantic analysis (type checking), and finally emits **bytecode**.

**Step 1.3:** The output is `Hello.class`, a binary file structured as:

$$
\text{Hello.class} = \text{Magic} + \text{Minor Ver} + \text{Major Ver} + \text{CP} + \text{Access Flags} + \text{this/super} + \text{Interfaces} + \text{Fields} + \text{Methods} + \text{Attributes}
$$

**Step 1.4:** At this point, the `.class` file is **platform-neutral**. The same file can be copied to a Windows PC, a Linux server, or a macOS laptop — it will not be re-compiled.

### Stage 2 — Class Loading & Verification (Load Time)

**Step 2.1:** The user executes `java Hello`. This launches the `java` launcher, which creates a **JVM instance** and initializes the bootstrap class loader.

**Step 2.2:** The JVM reads `Hello.class`. The **class loader subsystem** performs three actions:

- **Loading** — reads the binary `.class` file and creates a `java.lang.Class<Hello>` object in the heap.
- **Linking** — combines the just-loaded class with the runtime state:
  - *Verify* — bytecode verifier checks instructions for legality (e.g., no jump to invalid offsets, no stack underflow, valid type transitions).
  - *Prepare* — allocates memory for class-level static variables and initializes them to default values (e.g., `int` $\rightarrow$ `0`, `boolean` $\rightarrow$ `false`).
  - *Resolve* — replaces symbolic references in the constant pool with direct references (e.g., resolving `System.out` to an actual memory address).
- **Initialization** — runs the class's static initializers and static variable assignments (the `<clinit>` method), top-to-bottom in source order.

**Step 2.3:** For the `main` method invocation, the JVM also loads `java.lang.String`, `java.lang.System`, `java.io.PrintStream`, and any other transitively required classes (lazy loading — only as needed).

### Stage 3 — Execution (Run Time)

**Step 3.1:** A new **JVM thread** (the main thread) is created. Each thread gets its own **JVM Stack** and **PC Register**.

**Step 3.2:** A **Stack Frame** is pushed onto the main thread's stack for the `main` method. The frame contains:

- **Local Variable Array** — slots for parameters `args` and any local variables.
- **Operand Stack** — a LIFO workspace for the JVM instructions.
- **Frame Data** — reference to the constant pool of the current class, return address, and exception table.

**Step 3.3:** The **interpreter** begins executing bytecode instructions. Consider the conceptual `println` sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instruction sequence for } & \texttt{System.out.println("Hi")} : \\
&\text{1. } \texttt{getstatic} \quad \rightarrow \text{push } \texttt{System.out} \text{ reference onto operand stack} \\
&\text{2. } \texttt{ldc} \quad \rightarrow \text{push String constant "Hi" from constant pool onto operand stack} \\
&\text{3. } \texttt{invokevirtual} \quad \rightarrow \text{invoke } \texttt{PrintStream.println(String)} \text{ method} \\
&\text{4. } \texttt{return} \quad \rightarrow \text{pop frame, return from main}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.4:** The interpreter's overhead is significant for hot methods. The **JIT compiler** tracks invocation counts. When a method's count exceeds the threshold, JIT compiles it to native code and stores it in the **Code Cache** of the Method Area. From that point on, the JVM directly executes native code for that method — **bypassing the interpreter**.

**Step 3.5:** When the program terminates, the JVM unloads classes, stops threads, shuts down the class loaders, and exits.

## 3.2 Memory Layout Derivation — Worked Example

Suppose a thread invokes a method `compute(int n)` which declares:

- `int a, b, c;` $\rightarrow$ 3 local variables
- `long timestamp;` $\rightarrow$ 2 slots (longs occupy 2 slots in the local variable array)
- `Object obj = new Object();` $\rightarrow$ 1 reference slot
- `int[] arr = new int[10];` $\rightarrow$ 1 reference slot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** In the JVM local variable array, `long` and `double` consume **2 consecutive slots**; all other types (`int`, `float`, `reference`, etc.) consume **1 slot each**. The `this` reference occupies slot 0 for instance methods (or slot 0 is the first parameter for `static` methods).

### Derivation:

**Step A — Count the slots for `compute` (assume instance method):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slot 0} &= \texttt{this} \quad (\text{1 slot, instance method}) \\
\text{Slot 1} &= n \quad (\text{int} \rightarrow \text{1 slot}) \\
\text{Slot 2,3} &= a, b \quad (\text{2 int slots}) \\
\text{Slot 4} &= c \quad (\text{1 slot}) \\
\text{Slot 5,6} &= \text{timestamp} \quad (\text{long} \rightarrow \text{2 slots}) \\
\text{Slot 7} &= obj \quad (\text{reference} \rightarrow \text{1 slot}) \\
\text{Slot 8} &= arr \quad (\text{reference} \rightarrow \text{1 slot})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Total local variable slots:**

$$
\text{Total Locals} = 9 \text{ slots}
$$

**Step C — Operand stack capacity** (must be sufficient for the deepest expression evaluation). Suppose the deepest expression uses 3 intermediate values simultaneously:

$$
\text{Operand Stack Capacity} = 3 \text{ slots}
$$

**Step D — Frame data** (constant pool reference, return address, exception table):

$$
\text{Frame Data} \approx 64 \text{ bytes (implementation-dependent)}
$$

**Step E — Total frame size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frame Size} &= \text{Locals} + \text{Operand Stack} + \text{Frame Data} \\
&= 9 \times 4 \text{ bytes} + 3 \times 4 \text{ bytes} + 64 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(32-bit slot width)} \\
&= 36 + 12 + 64 \\
&= 112 \text{ bytes per frame}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Each invocation of `compute` allocates **112 bytes** on the JVM stack. Recursive calls multiply this by the recursion depth. This is why deep recursion causes `StackOverflowError` — the stack has a finite size (configurable via `-Xss`, default `512 KB` to `1 MB` per thread).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Performance Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks: *"Why is the JVM faster than pure interpretation?"* Use this table in your answer.

| Execution Mode | Translation Time | Execution Speed | Memory Cost | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure Interpretation** | None (per call) | **Slow** (10x–100x slower than native) | Low | Cold code, rarely-executed methods |
| **JIT Compilation (C1)** | Small upfront | **Moderate** (2x–3x slower than C++ native) | Code cache size | Warm code |
| **JIT Compilation (C2)** | Larger upfront | **Near-native** (often within 10–20% of C++ native) | Larger code cache | Hot code (server-class workloads) |
| **AOT Compilation (GraalVM Native Image)** | One-time ahead-of-time | **Fast startup + low memory** | Trade-off: longer build, no runtime profiling | Microservices, CLI tools |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **JIT compiler's** magic lies in **runtime profiling**: the JVM sees which branches are taken, which types are used, and which methods are hot — information that a static C++ compiler does not have. This enables optimizations like **aggressive inlining** of small methods and **escape analysis** (stack-allocating objects that don't escape a method).

## 3.4 Reachability Analysis for Garbage Collection

The GC uses a **mark-and-sweep** algorithm (or its variants). An object is eligible for collection if it is **not reachable** from any **GC root**.

$$
\text{Garbage} = \{\, o \in \text{Heap} \;:\; \nexists \, \text{path from any GC root to } o \,\}
$$

GC roots include:
- Local variables and parameters on any thread's **JVM Stack**.
- **Active threads** themselves.
- **Static fields** of loaded classes.
- **JNI references** (native code holding Java references).
- Objects held by the JVM for synchronization monitors.

> [!NOTE]
> Strong, soft, weak, and phantom references influence when an object becomes garbage. Only **strong references** keep an object alive past GC root reachability.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 JVM Internal Architecture (High-Level Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the full JVM internal structure. The flow goes from `.class` file input (left) through the class loader, into the runtime data areas, and finally to the execution engine output (native OS).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classfile[".class Bytecode File"]
    classloader["Class Loader Subsystem"]
    bootstrap["Bootstrap Loader"]
    platform["Platform Loader"]
    app["Application Loader"]
    verifier["Bytecode Verifier"]
    linker["Linker (Prepare + Resolve)"]
    methodArea["Method Area (Metaspace)"]
    heap["Heap Area (Young + Old Gen)"]
    jvmStack["JVM Stack per Thread"]
    pcReg["PC Register per Thread"]
    nativeStack["Native Method Stack"]
    interpreter["Interpreter"]
    jit["JIT Compiler (C1 + C2)"]
    gc["Garbage Collector"]
    jni["JNI Interface"]
    nativeLibs["Native Method Libraries"]
    os["Host Operating System / Hardware"]

    classfile --> classloader
    classloader --> bootstrap
    classloader --> platform
    classloader --> app
    bootstrap --> verifier
    platform --> verifier
    app --> verifier
    verifier --> linker
    linker --> methodArea
    linker --> heap
    linker --> jvmStack
    linker --> pcReg
    linker --> nativeStack
    methodArea --> interpreter
    heap --> interpreter
    jvmStack --> interpreter
    pcReg --> interpreter
    interpreter --> jit
    jit --> gc
    gc --> jni
    jni --> nativeLibs
    nativeLibs --> os
```

## 4.2 Class Loading Delegation Flow

This diagram shows the **parent-delegation** model — when a class is requested, the loader first asks its parent before loading it itself.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Application requests class MyClass"])
    appLoader["Application ClassLoader.loadClass(MyClass)"]
    checkParent1{"Is parent null?"}
    platformLoader["Platform ClassLoader.loadClass(MyClass)"]
    checkParent2{"Is parent null?"}
    bootstrapLoader["Bootstrap ClassLoader.findClass(MyClass)"]
    foundBoot{"Found in bootstrap path?"}
    foundPlat{"Found in extension path?"}
    foundApp{"Found in classpath?"}
    loadApp["Load from classpath"]
    loadPlat["Load from extension"]
    loadBoot["Load from rt.jar / java.base"]
    fail(["Throw ClassNotFoundException"])

    start --> appLoader
    appLoader --> checkParent1
    checkParent1 -- "No (parent is Platform)" --> platformLoader
    checkParent1 -- "Yes (end of chain)" --> foundApp
    platformLoader --> checkParent2
    checkParent2 -- "No (parent is Bootstrap)" --> bootstrapLoader
    checkParent2 -- "Yes (end of chain)" --> foundPlat
    bootstrapLoader --> foundBoot
    foundBoot -- "No" --> foundPlat
    foundPlat -- "No" --> foundApp
    foundApp -- "Yes" --> loadApp
    foundApp -- "No" --> fail
    foundPlat -- "Yes" --> loadPlat
    foundBoot -- "Yes" --> loadBoot
```

## 4.3 Bytecode Execution — Stack-Based Model

This diagram shows how a simple arithmetic expression `a + b` is evaluated on the JVM's operand stack, illustrating the **stack-based** nature of JVM execution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["iload_0 (push local a onto stack)"]
    stack1["Operand Stack: [a]"]
    step2["iload_1 (push local b onto stack)"]
    stack2["Operand Stack: [a, b]"]
    step3["iadd (pop two, push sum)"]
    stack3["Operand Stack: [a+b]"]
    step4["istore_2 (pop result into local c)"]
    stack4["Operand Stack: [empty]"]

    step1 --> stack1
    stack1 --> step2
    step2 --> stack2
    stack2 --> step3
    step3 --> stack3
    stack3 --> step4
    step4 --> stack4
```

## 4.4 Heap Memory Generational Layout

The heap is logically divided into generations to optimize GC. Young-generation collections are **fast and frequent**; old-generation collections are **slower and less frequent**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    heap["JVM Heap"]
    young["Young Generation"]
    old["Old Generation (Tenured)"]
    eden["Eden Space"]
    s1["Survivor 0 (From)"]
    s2["Survivor 1 (To)"]
    minorGC["Minor GC (frequent, fast)"]
    majorGC["Major GC / Full GC (rare, slow)"]
    g1["G1 GC Regions (Java 9+ default)"]

    heap --> young
    heap --> old
    young --> eden
    young --> s1
    young --> s2
    eden -. "Object survives 15+ GC cycles" .-> old
    young -. "Triggers" .-> minorGC
    old -. "Triggers" .-> majorGC
    heap -. "Modern alternative" .-> g1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why generational?** Empirical observation: **most objects die young** (the "infant mortality" hypothesis). By collecting the young generation frequently with a fast GC and the old generation rarely, the JVM achieves high throughput with low average pause times.
