```java
        Student s2 = new Student(102, "Beena");   // Object 2
        
        s1.display();
        s2.display();
    }
}
```

**Memory Diagram:**

```
          STACK MEMORY                      HEAP MEMORY
        ┌─────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────────┐
        │  main() frame   │            │  Object @0xABC123    │
        │  s1 → 0xABC123  │───────────▶│  rollNo = 101        │
        │  s2 → 0xDEF456  │─────┐      │  name  = "Anand"     │
        └─────────────────┘     │      └──────────────────────┘
                                │      ┌──────────────────────┐
                                └─────▶│  Object @0xDEF456    │
                                       │  rollNo = 102        │
                                       │  name  = "Beena"     │
                                       └──────────────────────┘
```

#### **Part (b) — Stack vs. Heap Memory (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | **Stack Memory** | **Heap Memory** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Used for** | Method frames, local variables, references | Objects and instance variables |
| **Order** | LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) | No specific order (dynamic) |
| **Allocation** | Automatic (per method call) | Manual via `new` keyword |
| **Deallocation** | Automatic (frame pops on method exit) | Garbage collection (automatic but non-deterministic) |
| **Size** | Smaller, fixed (typically 512KB–1MB) | Larger, dynamic (can grow to GB) |
| **Thread safety** | Thread-local (each thread has its own) | Shared across threads (requires synchronization) |
| **Speed** | Faster access | Slower access |
| **Error** | `StackOverflowError` | `OutOfMemoryError` |

**Lifecycle Example:**
When `main()` is called, a new frame is pushed onto the stack. Inside, the reference `s1` is stored. The `new` operator allocates space on the heap, and the reference `s1` is assigned the heap address. When `main()` finishes, the stack frame is destroyed. The heap object becomes unreachable and is later reclaimed by the **Garbage Collector**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **2 marks deducted** if the student writes "objects are stored in the stack" — objects are ALWAYS in the heap.
> - **1 mark deducted** if the student forgets to mention that `s1` is a **reference**, not the object itself.
> - **2 marks deducted** if no diagram is provided — KTU mandates at least one visual for a 7-mark sub-question on memory.
> - Common mistake: confusing **declaration** (`Student s1;`) with **instantiation** (`new Student()`). Both steps are mandatory for object creation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam Final-Day Cram Sheet**

- ✅ A **class** is a **blueprint**; an **object** is a **runtime instance** of that blueprint.
- ✅ A Java program must have **at least one `public class`** whose name **matches the filename** (case-sensitive).
- ✅ The `new` keyword performs **three actions**: (1) heap allocation, (2) default initialization, (3) constructor invocation.
- ✅ Reference variables live on the **stack**; objects live on the **heap**.
- ✅ If you write **any** constructor explicitly, the compiler will **NOT** auto-generate a default no-arg constructor.
- ✅ `this` refers to the **current invoking object**; it **cannot** be used in `static` contexts.
- ✅ `this(args)` (constructor chaining) **must be the first statement** in a constructor body.
- ✅ Static members belong to the **class**, not to objects — one copy shared across all instances.
- ✅ Access modifier strictest → most lenient: **`private` < default < `protected` < `public`**.
- ✅ **Encapsulation** = `private` fields + `public` getters/setters + controlled validation.
- ✅ Method overloading = **same name, different parameter list** (compile-time polymorphism).
- ✅ Method overriding = **same name AND same parameter list** in a subclass (run-time polymorphism).
- ✅ Object initialization order: **static → instance fields → instance block → constructor**.
- ✅ Garbage collection reclaims heap memory **only** when an object is **unreachable** (no live references).
- ✅ Use `final` on a class to **prevent inheritance**; on a method to **prevent overriding**; on a variable to make it a **constant**.
- ✅ **Memory errors:** `StackOverflowError` (deep recursion) vs. `OutOfMemoryError` (heap exhaustion).
- ✅ **KTU's favorite 3-mark question:** *"Explain default constructor with example."*
- ✅ **KTU's favorite 14-mark question:** *"Explain class, object, memory allocation with diagram."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Declaring Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Declaring Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Object-Oriented Programming, particularly in **Java**, **Declaring an Object** is the act of creating a **reference variable** of a class type that will be used to point to (reference) an actual object instance created in memory. Declaring an object is fundamentally a **two-step process**: **(1) Declaration** of a reference variable (compile-time activity that creates a name in the stack), and **(2) Instantiation** using the `new` keyword, which dynamically allocates memory on the **heap** for the object and binds the reference to it.

The general syntax adopted by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus is:

```java
ClassName  objectReference;
objectReference = new ClassName(argument-list);
```

Or the combined, frequently preferred form:

```java
ClassName  objectReference = new ClassName(argument-list);
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:** Declaring a reference variable **does NOT** create an object. It only creates a holder capable of storing the memory address of an object. The object itself comes into existence only when `new` is invoked, which invokes the class **constructor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Value Principle:** When a reference variable is declared but not yet instantiated, it holds the Java default reference value of `null` (a literal denoting "no object assigned"). Accessing a method or field on a `null` reference throws a `java.lang.NullPointerException` — a frequent KTU viva and exam pitfall.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **parking spot** in a shopping mall with a **name plate** "Car-1":

- The **name plate** is like the **declaration** of a reference variable. It exists, it has a name, but no car is parked yet.
- **Driving a car into the spot** is like the **instantiation** with `new`. The car (object) physically occupies the memory (parking space).
- The **name plate pointing to the car** is the **reference variable being assigned** the memory address.

If the car is removed (object becomes unreachable / garbage collected), the name plate still exists but points to "no car" — this is precisely the `null` state.

> [!TIP]
> A reference variable in Java is **not** the object itself — it is a *handle* or *remote control* that lets you operate on the object located elsewhere in memory.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU 2024 Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Reference Variable** | A variable of class type that stores the address (reference) of an object |
| **Instantiation** | The act of creating a new object in heap memory using the `new` keyword |
| **Constructor** | A special method invoked by `new` to initialize the newly created object |
| **Heap Memory** | The runtime data area where all Java objects are physically stored |
| **Stack Memory** | The runtime area where local primitive variables and reference variables are stored |
| **Null Reference** | A reference variable that has been declared but not yet assigned an object |
| **Garbage Collection** | Automatic JVM process that reclaims memory of unreferenced objects |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Step Process of Object Declaration

#### Step 1 — Declaration of the Reference Variable
A reference variable is declared in the same way any other variable is declared: by specifying its **type** and **identifier**. At this stage, the JVM reserves a small slot in the **stack frame** of the current method, sized to hold a memory address (typically 4 or 8 bytes depending on JVM architecture — **32-bit** JVM uses 4 bytes, **64-bit** uses 8 bytes, but both are JVM-internal details, not part of the language spec).

The key fact is: **No object exists yet.** The reference simply has the value `null`.

#### Step 2 — Instantiation with the `new` Operator
The `new` operator performs three distinct internal actions in sequence:

1. **Memory Allocation:** The JVM dynamically allocates enough contiguous bytes on the **heap** to hold all instance variables of the class, plus header information (object header, class pointer, monitor info, etc., which is JVM-specific).
2. **Default Initialization:** All primitive instance fields are set to their Java default values (`int → 0`, `boolean → false`, `float → 0.0f`, etc.). All reference-type instance fields are set to `null`.
3. **Constructor Invocation:** The appropriate constructor is called to perform explicit user-defined initialization. The `new` operator then returns the **memory address** of the newly constructed object, and this address is stored into the reference variable.

### 2.2 Why the Distinction Matters (The "Why")

- **Compile-time vs. Runtime:** Declaration is a purely compile-time concept (the compiler checks type compatibility). Instantiation is a runtime event that consumes memory.
- **Static Typing:** Java is strongly and statically typed. Declaring `Box b;` informs the compiler "only `Box` objects (or subclasses) can be stored in `b`." This enables early type error detection — a cornerstone of the language's safety guarantees.
- **Memory Efficiency:** You can declare many references up-front without allocating heap memory, deferring costly object creation until genuinely needed.

### 2.3 Variations of Object Declaration Accepted by the KTU Syllabus

**Variation 1 — Declaration followed by Instantiation (separate statements):**

```java
Student s1;          // Step 1: declare reference
s1 = new Student();  // Step 2: instantiate and bind
```

**Variation 2 — Combined Declaration and Instantiation:**

```java
Student s1 = new Student();
```

**Variation 3 — Declaration of Multiple References to the SAME object:**

```java
Student s1 = new Student();
Student s2 = s1;        // s2 refers to the SAME object as s1
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** `s2 = s1` does **NOT** copy the object. Both `s1` and `s2` are two distinct reference variables (two remote controls) pointing to **one single object** in the heap.

**Variation 4 — Array of Objects:**

```java
Student[] batch = new Student[30];
// Above only creates the ARRAY container (a single object with 30 null slots).
// Individual Student objects must be instantiated separately:

for (int i = 0; i < 30; i++) {
    batch[i] = new Student();
}
```

**Variation 5 — Anonymous Object (no reference retained):**

```java
new Student().display();   // Object becomes eligible for GC immediately after display() returns
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The syllabus specifically asks students to identify *when* an object becomes eligible for **Garbage Collection**. An object is GC-eligible when **no live reference** points to it. The `new Student()` in Variation 5 has no reference retained, so it is GC-eligible on the very next line.

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Syntax Cheat Sheet

| # | Pattern | Code Form | Memory State After Execution |
|---|---------|-----------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Pure declaration | `Box b;` | Stack: `b → null` ; Heap: nothing new |
| 2 | Declaration + instantiation | `Box b = new Box();` | Stack: `b → 0xABC` ; Heap: 1 Box object |
| 3 | Aliasing (shared reference) | `Box b2 = b;` | Stack: `b → 0xABC`, `b2 → 0xABC` ; Heap: still 1 Box |
| 4 | Re-assignment (old object GC-eligible) | `b = new Box();` | Stack: `b → 0xDEF`, `b2 → 0xABC` ; Heap: 2 Boxes (0xABC GC-eligible if b2 also nulled) |
| 5 | Array of references | `Box[] arr = new Box[5];` | Stack: `arr → 0xFFF` ; Heap: 1 array containing 5 `null` slots |
| 6 | Array of populated objects | `arr[0] = new Box();` | Heap: array at 0xFFF with slot 0 pointing to new Box |

> **Notation used in the table:** `→` denotes "reference points to memory address." The hex addresses (e.g., 0xABC) are illustrative placeholders, not real Java outputs.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The object-declaration model is the foundation of:

- **Frameworks like Spring & Hibernate:** Beans are declared as references but instantiated by the Inversion-of-Control container.
- **Game Development:** A `Player` object is declared once and instantiated only when the player joins a session; the reference can be re-pointed to a new player object.
- **Database Connectivity:** A `Connection` object is declared, instantiated by `DriverManager.getConnection()`, and re-assigned across queries — a textbook example of Variation 4.
- **Android UI:** View objects are declared in code (or via XML) and inflated (`instantiated`) at runtime by the `LayoutInflater`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Memory-State Analysis: A Worked Example

Consider the following Java code segment which the KTU board frequently uses in Part B questions:

```java
class Box {
    int length, width;
    Box(int l, int w) {
        length = l;
        width  = w;
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b1;                         // Statement A
        b1 = new Box(10, 20);           // Statement B
        Box b2 = b1;                    // Statement C
        b1 = new Box(30, 40);           // Statement D
        b2 = null;                      // Statement E
    }
}
```

We will trace memory state **line by line**.

**After Statement A:** `Box b1;`

A reference slot named `b1` is created in the stack frame of `main()`. It has not been assigned any object, so its value is the default reference literal `null`.

$$
\text{Stack: } b1 \;=\; \texttt{null}
$$

**Heap:** empty (no Box objects yet).

**After Statement B:** `b1 = new Box(10, 20);`

The `new` operator:
1. Allocates heap memory for one `Box` object (assume the JVM assigns address `0xA100` for illustration).
2. Initializes `length = 0`, `width = 0` (Java defaults for `int`).
3. Invokes the constructor `Box(10, 20)`, which sets `length = 10`, `width = 20`.
4. Returns the address `0xA100`, which is stored in `b1`.

$$
\text{Stack: } b1 \;=\; \texttt{0xA100}
$$

$$
\text{Heap at 0xA100: } \text{Box}\{ \text{length}=10,\ \text{width}=20 \}
$$

**After Statement C:** `Box b2 = b1;`

A new reference `b2` is created in the stack and assigned the **same value** as `b1` (i.e., the address `0xA100`). No new object is created.

$$
\text{Stack: } b1 \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{0xA100}, \quad b2 \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{0xA100}
$$

$$
\text{Heap at 0xA100: } \text{Box}\{ \text{length}=10,\ \text{width}=20 \}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** After Statement C, modifying the object via `b1` will be visible via `b2` (and vice versa), because both references point to the **same** heap object. This is the **aliasing** phenomenon.

**After Statement D:** `b1 = new Box(30, 40);`

A *second* Box object is allocated (assume address `0xB200`), with `length=30, width=40`. The address `0xB200` is stored in `b1`, **overwriting** the previous value.

$$
\text{Stack: } b1 \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{0xB200}, \quad b2 \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{0xA100}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Heap at 0xA100: } & \text{Box}\{ \text{length}=10,\ \text{width}=20 \} \\
\text{Heap at 0xB200: } & \text{Box}\{ \text{length}=30,\ \text{width}=40 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The object at `0xA100` is still **alive**, because `b2` still points to it. It has **not** become garbage-collectible.

**After Statement E:** `b2 = null;`

The reference `b2` is set to the literal `null`, severing the link to address `0xA100`.

$$
\text{Stack: } b1 \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{0xB200}, \quad b2 \;=\; \texttt{null}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Heap at 0xA100: } & \text{Box}\{ \text{length}=10,\ \text{width}=20 \} \;\;\text{[GC-ELIGIBLE: no live reference]} \\
\text{Heap at 0xB200: } & \text{Box}\{ \text{length}=30,\ \text{width}=40 \}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The object at `0xA100` is now eligible for garbage collection**, because the only reference (`b2`) has been nullified. The JVM may reclaim this memory at any time, though the *exact* moment is non-deterministic.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — All Declaration Patterns Compared

| Pattern | Code | Stack Change | Heap Change | GC Effect | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Declare only | `Box b;` | Slot added, value `null` | None | None | High |
| Declare + Instantiate | `Box b = new Box();` | Slot added, holds address | One new object | None | Very High |
| Copy reference | `Box b2 = b1;` | New slot with same address | None | None | Very High |
| Re-assign reference | `b1 = new Box();` | Slot value overwritten | New object added | Old object may become eligible | Very High |
| Nullify reference | `b1 = null;` | Slot value set to `null` | None | Old object becomes eligible | High |
| Array declaration | `Box[] a = new Box[5];` | One slot for array ref | One array object (5 nulls) | None | Medium |
| Populate array | `a[0] = new Box();` | No change | Slot 0 of array gets address | None | Medium |
| Anonymous object | `new Box().display();` | No persistent change | One transient object | Eligible immediately | Medium |

### 3.3 Symbolic Derivation of GC Eligibility

We can formalize GC eligibility for any object $\mathcal{O}$ using the following Boolean condition:

$$
\text{GC-Eligible}(\mathcal{O}) \;=\; \left(\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}_{\{r_i \text{ points to } \mathcal{O}\}} \;=\; 0 \;\right)
$$

Where:

- $n$ is the total number of live reference variables in the program at the current instant.
- $r_i$ is the $i$-th reference variable.
- $\mathbb{1}_{\{\cdot\}}$ is the **indicator function** (returns $1$ if the condition inside braces is true, else $0$).

In plain words: an object is GC-eligible **if and only if the count of live references pointing to it is exactly zero**.

**Application to our worked example after Statement E:**

We have two references, $b_1$ and $b_2$.

- $b_1$ points to `0xB200` (the second Box).
- $b_2$ points to `null`.

For object $\mathcal{O}_1$ at `0xA100` (the first Box):

$$
\text{GC-Eligible}(\mathcal{O}_1) \;=\; \left(\; \mathbb{1}_{\{b_1 \to 0\text{xA100}\}} + \mathbb{1}_{\{b_2 \to 0\text{xA100}\}} \;=\; 0 \;\right)
$$

$$
\text{GC-Eligible}(\mathcal{O}_1) \;=\; \left(\; 0 + 0 \;=\; 0 \;\right) \;=\; \text{TRUE}
$$

For object $\mathcal{O}_2$ at `0xB200` (the second Box):

$$
\text{GC-Eligible}(\mathcal{O}_2) \;=\; \left(\; 1 + 0 \;=\; 0 \;\right) \;=\; \text{FALSE}
$$

Hence, $\mathcal{O}_1$ is eligible for garbage collection, while $\mathcal{O}_2$ is not — confirming our previous analysis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stack–Heap Interaction During Object Declaration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stackArea["STACK MEMORY - main method frame"]
        refB1["ref b1 : Box"]
        refB2["ref b2 : Box"]
    end

    subgraph heapArea["HEAP MEMORY - object storage"]
        objA100["Box Object 0xA100\nlength=10  width=20"]
        objB200["Box Object 0xB200\nlength=30  width=40"]
    end

    subgraph gcArea["GARBAGE COLLECTOR SUBSYSTEM"]
        gcWatch["Reachability Tracker"]
        gcReclaim["Reclaim Unreachable Objects"]
    end

    refB1 -- "points to" --> objB200
    refB2 -- "points to" --> objA100
    refB2 -. "formerly held" .-> objA100
    objA100 -. "eligible for reclamation" .-> gcWatch
    gcWatch --> gcReclaim
```

### 4.2 Lifecycle Flowchart: From Declaration to Garbage Collection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Declare Reference Variable\nClassName obj;"] --> stepB{"Is obj initialized?"}
    stepB -- "No" --> stateC["State: NULL REFERENCE\nStack slot = null"]
    stateC --> stepD["Invoke 'new' Operator"]
    stepD --> stepE["Allocate Heap Memory\nfor Object"]
    stepE --> stepF["Default Initialize\nAll Fields"]
    stepF --> stepG["Invoke Constructor"]
    stepG --> stepH["Return Memory Address\nto Reference"]
    stepB -- "Yes, with new" --> stepE
    stepH --> stepI["OBJECT IN USE\nStack points to Heap"]
    stepI --> stepJ{"Any live reference\nremaining?"}
    stepJ -- "Yes" --> stepI
    stepJ -- "No" --> stepK["OBJECT IS\nGARBAGE COLLECTIBLE"]
    stepK --> stepL["JVM GC Reclaims\nHeap Memory"]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Primitives vs. Object References

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph primCase["PRIMITIVE VARIABLE CASE"]
        pDecl["Declare: int x = 25;"]
        pMem["Stack: x = 25\nHeap: untouched"]
    end
    pDecl --> pMem

    subgraph refCase["OBJECT REFERENCE CASE"]
        rDecl["Declare: Box b = new Box();"]
        rStack["Stack: b = 0xA100"]
        rHeap["Heap at 0xA100:\nBox{ length=0, width=0 }"]
    end
    rDecl --> rStack
    rStack -- "stores address of" --> rHeap
```

### 4.4 Detailed Step-by-Step Block: Class Definition to Object Instantiation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph classDef["PHASE 1 - CLASS DEFINITION (compile-time)"]
        cd1["Define class Box"]
        cd2["Declare fields: int length, int width"]
        cd3["Define constructor: Box(int l, int w)"]
        cd4["Class loaded into Method Area"]
    end

    subgraph objDecl["PHASE 2 - OBJECT DECLARATION (runtime, main)"]
        od1["Box b1;"]
        od2["Allocate 4-8 bytes on stack"]
        od3["Initialize b1 = null"]
    end

    subgraph objInst["PHASE 3 - INSTANTIATION (runtime)"]
        oi1["b1 = new Box(10, 20)"]
        oi2["Allocate heap memory"]
        oi3["Default-initialize fields"]
        oi4["Call constructor Box(10, 20)"]
        oi5["Assign returned address to b1"]
    end

    cd1 --> cd2 --> cd3 --> cd4
    cd4 --> od1 --> od2 --> od3
    od3 --> oi1 --> oi2 --> oi3 --> oi4 --> oi5
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to draw a memory diagram, always draw **two separate rectangles** — one labeled STACK (left) and one labeled HEAP (right). Draw arrows from stack references to heap objects, never the reverse. Mark null references explicitly with the word "null" or a slash through the arrow.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between declaring an object and instantiating an object in Java. What is the state of a reference variable immediately after declaration but before instantiation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Declaration** of an object is the act of creating a reference variable of a class type, which merely reserves a name in the stack to hold a memory address. **Instantiation** is the act of using the `new` operator to actually allocate heap memory for the object and invoke its constructor. *(2 marks)*
- Immediately after declaration but before instantiation, the reference variable holds the default value `null`, meaning it does not yet point to any object. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is meant by "aliasing" in the context of object references? Give a one-line code example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Aliasing occurs when two or more reference variables point to the **same** object in memory, so changes made through one reference are visible through the others. *(2 marks)*

Example:

```java
Box b1 = new Box();
Box b2 = b1;   // b1 and b2 are aliases of the same object
```

*(1 mark for example)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram how objects are stored in memory when declared and instantiated in Java. Discuss the difference between **stack** and **heap** memory in this context. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following Java code. Trace the memory state (stack and heap) after each statement, and identify which object(s) become eligible for garbage collection by the end. *(7 marks)*

```java
class Car {
    String model;
    Car(String m) { model = m; }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Car c1 = new Car("Honda");
        Car c2 = new Car("Toyota");
        Car c3 = c1;
        c1 = new Car("BMW");
        c2 = c3;
        c3 = null;
    }
}
```

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Memory Model Explanation (7 marks):**

In Java, when we declare an object reference and instantiate an object, memory is used in two distinct areas:

1. **Stack Memory** — Stores local variables of methods, including reference variables (handles to objects). Each method call creates a stack frame; when the method returns, the frame is popped. *(2 marks)*
2. **Heap Memory** — Stores the actual objects created by the `new` operator. Objects persist on the heap until they become unreachable, after which the Garbage Collector reclaims their memory. *(2 marks)*

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
STACK (main frame)                 HEAP
+---------------+                 +-----------------------------+
|  c1 | 0xA100  | ------------->  | Car{ model = "Honda" }      |
|  c2 | 0xB200  | ------------->  | Car{ model = "Toyota" }     |
|  c3 |  0xA100 | ------------->  | Car{ model = "BMW" }        |
+---------------+                 +-----------------------------+
                                    0xA100        0xB200        0xC300
```

The arrows from stack references `c1`, `c2`, `c3` terminate at heap objects — this is the standard textbook depiction. *(Award 3 marks for the diagram if arrows, stack/heap labels, and object contents are all correctly shown.)*

---

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step Trace (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** `Car c1 = new Car("Honda");`

- Stack: `c1 → 0xA100`
- Heap at `0xA100`: `Car{ model = "Honda" }`

**Step 2:** `Car c2 = new Car("Toyota");`

- Stack: `c2 → 0xB200`
- Heap at `0xB200`: `Car{ model = "Toyota" }`

**Step 3:** `Car c3 = c1;`

- Stack: `c3 → 0xA100` (same as c1, no new heap object)
- Heap: still two objects (Honda at `0xA100`, Toyota at `0xB200`)

**Step 4:** `c1 = new Car("BMW");`

- A new object is allocated at, say, `0xC300` with `model = "BMW"`.
- Stack: `c1 → 0xC300` (overwritten)
- Heap at `0xC300`: `Car{ model = "BMW" }`
- The Honda object at `0xA100` is **still alive** because `c3` still points to it.

**Step 5:** `c2 = c3;`

- Stack: `c2 → 0xA100` (c2 now points to Honda, no new heap object)
- The Toyota object at `0xB200` is **still alive** (no live reference yet, but it is reachable from the stack's `c2`'s previous value? No — once `c2` is overwritten, `0xB200` loses its only reference). 
- Therefore the Toyota object at `0xB200` is now **eligible for garbage collection**.

**Step 6:** `c3 = null;`

- Stack: `c3 = null` (the link to `0xA100` is severed)
- The Honda object at `0xA100` now has **zero** live references (since `c1` points to `0xC300` and `c2` previously was overwritten in step 5 — wait, in step 5 `c2 → 0xA100`, so it still points to Honda).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction of Step 5/6 reasoning:** After Step 5, `c2` points to `0xA100` (Honda). So Honda is still alive. After Step 6, `c3 = null`, but `c2` still points to `0xA100`. Therefore **Honda at `0xA100` remains alive**.

**Final GC Status:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Object at 0xA100 (Honda):} & \quad \text{ALIVE} \quad (c2 \text{ still points here}) \\
\text{Object at 0xB200 (Toyota):} & \quad \text{GC-ELIGIBLE} \quad (\text{no live reference after Step 5}) \\
\text{Object at 0xC300 (BMW):}    & \quad \text{ALIVE} \quad (c1 \text{ still points here})
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for correct final state, 6 marks distributed across 6 trace steps)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Alternative Model]

**(a)** What is the role of the `new` operator in object instantiation? Describe the three internal actions performed by `new` in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the declaration, instantiation, and aliasing of objects. Show with comments how modifying the object through one reference is reflected when accessed through another. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Role of the `new` Operator (7 marks):**

The `new` operator is the **only** standard Java mechanism to create a new object on the heap. It performs three sequential internal actions: *(1 mark for listing them)*

1. **Memory Allocation on the Heap:** The JVM determines the size of the class's instance data (sum of sizes of all non-static fields, plus JVM-specific object header) and allocates a contiguous block of heap memory large enough to hold it. If sufficient memory is unavailable, an `OutOfMemoryError` is thrown. *(2 marks)*

2. **Default Initialization of Instance Fields:** The newly allocated memory is zero-initialized by the JVM. All `int` fields become `0`, `float` fields become `0.0f`, `boolean` fields become `false`, and all reference fields become `null`. This happens **before** the constructor body executes. *(2 marks)*

3. **Constructor Invocation and Address Return:** The JVM identifies the appropriate constructor (matching the argument list) and invokes it on the newly allocated object. The keyword `this` inside the constructor refers to this object. After the constructor returns, the `new` expression evaluates to the **memory address** of the constructed object, which is then assigned to the reference variable on the left-hand side of the assignment. *(2 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — Demonstration Program (7 marks):**

```java
class Account {
    String holder;
    double balance;

    Account(String h, double b) {
        holder  = h;
        balance = b;
    }

    void deposit(double amt) {
        balance = balance + amt;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Holder: " + holder + "  Balance: " + balance);
    }
}

public class AliasingDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Declaration + Instantiation
        Account a1 = new Account("Arun", 1000.0);

        // Step 2: Aliasing - a2 points to the SAME object as a1
        Account a2 = a1;

        System.out.println("Before deposit:");
        a1.display();   // Holder: Arun  Balance: 1000.0
        a2.display();   // Holder: Arun  Balance: 1000.0

        // Step 3: Modify through a2; change is visible via a1 (aliasing proof)
        a2.deposit(500.0);

        System.out.println("After deposit through a2:");
        a1.display();   // Holder: Arun  Balance: 1500.0   <-- visible via a1 too!
        a2.display();   // Holder: Arun  Balance: 1500.0
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Before deposit:
Holder: Arun  Balance: 1000.0
Holder: Arun  Balance: 1000.0
After deposit through a2:
Holder: Arun  Balance: 1500.0
Holder: Arun  Balance: 1500.0
```

**Mark Distribution for Part (b):**
- Class definition with fields and constructor: *(2 marks)*
- Correct declaration and instantiation in `main`: *(2 marks)*
- Aliasing demonstration (`a2 = a1`) with comment: *(1 mark)*
- Modifying via `a2` and showing effect via `a1`: *(1 mark)*
- Correct expected output: *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation for "Declaring Objects":**
>
> 1. **Conflating "declare" with "create":** Examiners *will* deduct marks if a student writes "`Box b = new Box();` creates an object" without first explaining that *only* `new` creates the object; the left-hand side merely creates a reference. Always state both halves.
> 2. **Forgetting the `null` state:** Students frequently skip mentioning the default `null` value of a freshly declared but uninstantiated reference. This is a 1–2 mark loss in Part A.
> 3. **Confusing aliasing with copying:** A common error is writing "`b2 = b1;` copies the object." It does **not** — it copies the **reference**. Use precise language.
> 4. **Memory-diagram errors:** Drawing the heap object on the stack, omitting the arrow, or failing to label stack/heap loses 2–3 marks in Part B.
> 5. **GC eligibility mistakes:** Marking an object as GC-eligible while a live reference still points to it (or vice versa) results in zero marks for the trace question. Always count the references pointing to each heap object at the final line.
> 6. **Confusing array-of-references with array-of-objects:** `new Box[5]` creates ONE array object with 5 null slots, not 5 Box objects. This is a frequently tested nuance.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Declaring Objects**
>
> - **Declaration** ≠ **Object Creation.** Declaration reserves a *reference*; the `new` operator *creates* the object.
> - The `new` operator performs three actions in order: **(1) allocate heap memory, (2) default-initialize fields, (3) invoke constructor and return the address.**
> - A **reference variable** stores the memory address of an object — it is *not* the object itself.
> - A reference variable that has been declared but not yet assigned any object holds the literal **`null`**.
> - **`null`** is a literal (lowercase) in Java — not a keyword, not an object, not zero. Dereferencing it throws `NullPointerException`.
> - **Aliasing** happens when multiple references point to the same object: `b2 = b1;` makes `b2` an alias of `b1`. There is still only **one** object.
> - **Garbage Collection eligibility rule:** an object is GC-eligible iff **zero** live references point to it. The moment of actual reclamation is JVM-internal and **non-deterministic**.
> - **Stack** holds local variables and reference variables; **Heap** holds actual objects. Arrows go *from* stack *to* heap in diagrams.
> - An **array of object references** (e.g., `new Box[5]`) creates only the array — each slot still requires its own `new Box()` to populate.
> - **Anonymous objects** (e.g., `new Box().display();`) have no persistent reference and become GC-eligible immediately after the expression evaluates.
> - **Java is pass-by-value, always** — for object references, the *value copied* is the address, not the object. This is a frequent viva question and a direct consequence of how objects are declared.
> - **Default field values** (assigned during `new`-step 2): `int → 0`, `long → 0L`, `float → 0.0f`, `double → 0.0d`, `boolean → false`, `char → '\u0000'`, any reference type → `null`.
> - **Constructor invocation** is mandatory via `new` (or via reflection / `clone()` / deserialization — beyond KTU Module 2 scope).
> - **Memory size note (informational only):** A reference occupies 4 bytes on a 32-bit JVM and 8 bytes on a 64-bit JVM with compressed-oops disabled; KTU does not test this but is useful for viva context.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Methods ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** with **Java**, a **method** is a named, reusable block of statements grouped together to perform a specific, well-defined task. A method is a **member of a class** (or an object) that encapsulates behaviour — it defines *what an object can do* rather than *what an object knows* (which is the role of instance variables). From the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEERT613 - Object Oriented Concepts)** perspective, methods are the **behavioural backbone** of any Java class. They enable: - **Modularity** — dividing a large problem into smaller, manageable logical units. - **Reusability** — writing code once and invoking it many times. - **Abstraction** — hiding the internal implementation behind a clean interface. - **Maintainability** — modifying behaviour in one place without disturbing callers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 scheme, "Methods" is the foundational topic of Module 2 and directly feeds into higher topics like *Constructors*, *Overloading*, *Recursion*, and *Arrays/String handling*. Examiners frequently test **method declaration syntax, parameter passing, return types, and stack-based execution** at the ESE (End Semester Examination). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Think of a **method** as a **specialised machine stationed inside a factory (the class)**. - The **factory** = the Class. - The **machine's name** = the Method name (e.g., `calculateTax`). - The **raw materials fed in** = the **Parameters/Arguments** (inputs). - The **finished product coming out** = the **Return value** (output). - The **machine's blueprint & internal gears** = the **Method body** (the actual logic). - The **"No-Product" label** = the `void` return type (machine just performs an action, e.g., a fan that spins but produces no tangible output). - **Switching ON the machine** = **Method invocation/call**. > **Real-world example:** An ATM (method `withdrawAmount`) takes a number (the parameter), checks your balance, dispenses cash, and returns a receipt (the return value) — or, in a `void` case, just prints "Transaction Successful" on the screen. ### 1.3 Why Methods Exist — The Three Pillars > [!NOTE] > **Without methods**, a Java program would be one giant `main` method — impossible to read, debug, or extend. The Three Pillars of Methods in OOP are: > > 1. **Encapsulation of Behaviour** — logic is bundled with the data it operates on. > 2. **Code Reusability (DRY Principle — Don't Repeat Yourself)** — one method, N invocations. > 3. **Interface-Implementation Separation** — callers use the method name/signature without needing to know the internal code. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Java Method — Structural Breakdown A Java method declaration has **six optional/mandatory components** arranged in a strict order. Below is the universal template used across the **KTU 2024 syllabus**: ```text [access_modifier] [static] [final] [abstract] return_type method_name(parameter_list) { // method body [return statement;] } ``` #### Component-wise Explanation | # | Component | Mandatory? | Purpose | Common Values | |---|-----------|------------|---------|---------------| | 1 | **Access Modifier** | Optional (defaults to package-private) | Visibility control | `public`, `private`, `protected` | | 2 | **`static` keyword** | Optional | Makes the method a *class method* (shared across all objects) | `static` or omitted | | 3 | **`final` keyword** | Optional | Prevents method overriding in subclasses | `final` or omitted | | 4 | **Return Type** | **Mandatory** | The data type of value the method sends back | `void`, `int`, `double`, `String`, etc. | | 5 | **Method Name** | **Mandatory** | Identifier following Java naming conventions | `camelCase` (e.g., `calculateArea`) | | 6 | **Parameter List** | Optional (can be empty `()`) | Inputs the method accepts | Comma-separated `type name` pairs | ### 2.2 The Method Signature — The Contract > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite:** The **method signature** in Java consists **ONLY** of the **method name** and the **parameter list** (number, type, and order of parameters). The return type is **NOT** part of the signature. > > Example: For `public int add(int a, double b)`, the signature is `add(int, double)`. This distinction is critical because: - The **Java compiler (javac)** uses the signature to resolve method calls. - Two methods can have the **same name but different signatures** → this enables **Method Overloading** (covered in Module 2 Part B). - The return type alone **cannot** differentiate two methods with the same name. ### 2.3 Categories of Methods (Classification) | Category | Description | Example | |----------|-------------|---------| | **Predefined / Library Methods** | Built into Java libraries | `Math.sqrt(25)`, `System.out.println()` | | **User-defined Methods** | Written by the programmer | `calculateGrade()` | | **Static Methods** | Belong to the class, called via class name | `Integer.parseInt("123")` | | **Instance Methods** | Belong to an object, called via object reference | `obj.display()` | | **Void Methods** | Perform an action, return nothing | `void printHello()` | | **Value-returning Methods** | Compute and return a value | `int sum(int a, int b)` | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Syntax Reference Sheet | Concept | Java Syntax Rule | Exam Tip | |---------|------------------|----------| | **Void method** | `void methodName() { }` | No `return value;` allowed — only `return;` for early exit. | | **Return statement** | `return expression;` | Expression type must match declared return type (or be assignable to it). | | **Parameter passing** | **Pass-by-value** (always) | A *copy* of the argument is passed; original variable is unchanged. | | **Local variables** | Declared inside method body | Scope = method block only; not initialised by default. | | **Method overloading** | Same name, different parameter lists | Differ by **type, number, or order** of parameters. | | **`main` method** | `public static void main(String[] args)` | Entry point of every standalone Java application. | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Methods are not merely an academic concept — they are the **primary abstraction mechanism** in production-grade software: - **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA):** Each REST API endpoint is essentially a "method" exposed over HTTP. - **Embedded Systems (Java ME):** A sensor-reading routine is a method called periodically. - **Game Development:** Methods model actions — `player.jump()`, `enemy.takeDamage(20)`. - **Banking Software:** `account.debit(amount)`, `account.credit(amount)` — methods ensure atomic, validated transactions. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** In a 10,000-line enterprise Java application, an average of **300–800 methods** exist. Each method typically performs **one well-defined task** (a principle called *Single Responsibility Principle* in SOLID design). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis, Execution Flow & Parameter Passing Derivations ### 3.1 Method Invocation — The Complete Execution Lifecycle (Step-by-Step) > **Scenario:** A program calls a method `add(int, int)` that returns the sum. ```java public class Calculator { static int add(int a, int b) { int sum = a + b; // Step 4: local variable 'sum' created return sum; // Step 5: return value pushed to caller } public static void main(String[] args) { int result = add(5, 3); // Step 1: method invocation System.out.println(result); // Step 6: uses returned value } } ``` #### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Execution Trace | Step | Action | JVM Memory State | |------|--------|------------------| | **1** | `main` method begins; JVM creates **stack frame** for `main`. | `main` frame pushed onto **Call Stack**. | | **2** | `add(5, 3)` is encountered → JVM creates a **new stack frame** for `add`. | `add` frame pushed **on top** of `main` frame. | | **3** | Parameters `a = 5`, `b = 3` are **copied** into `add`'s stack frame. | `add` frame: `{ a: 5, b: 3 }`. | | **4** | Local variable `sum` is declared and computed: `sum = 5 + 3 = 8`. | `add` frame: `{ a: 5, b: 3, sum: 8 }`. | | **5** | `return sum;` → value `8` is sent back to the caller's expression. | `add` frame is **popped & destroyed**. | | **6** | Back in `main`, `result = 8`. | `main` frame: `{ args: [...], result: 8 }`. | | **7** | `println(result)` prints `8`. | Output: `8`. | ### 3.2 Pass-by-Value — Mathematical Proof of Concept > **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Java is **strictly pass-by-value**. There is **no pass-by-reference** in Java, even for object references (the *reference itself* is passed by value). #### Worked Example (Primitive Parameter) ```java public static void modify(int x) { x = x + 10; // Local copy modified } public static void main(String[] args) { int num = 5; modify(num); System.out.println(num); // What is printed? } ``` **Trace:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: } & \text{In } main, \text{ memory: } num \rightarrow 5 \\ \text{Step 2: } & \text{modify(num) called} \Rightarrow \text{copy } x = 5 \text{ created in modify's frame} \\ \text{Step 3: } & x = x + 10 = 5 + 10 = 15 \quad (\text{only } x \text{ changes, NOT } num) \\ \text{Step 4: } & \text{modify frame destroyed; back in } main, num \text{ is still } 5. \\ \text{Output: } & 5 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The original `num` in `main` is **never touched**. Methods receive a *copy* of primitives. This is why primitive parameters cannot be used to "send back" multiple values to the caller. ### 3.3 Return Type Compatibility — The Compiler's Rule If a method declares `return_type T`, then **every** code path that reaches a `return` statement must return a value **assignable to T**. For a `void` method, the rules are: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Rule 1: } & \text{No } \texttt{return value;} \text{ allowed} \\ \text{Rule 2: } & \text{The statement } \texttt{return;} \text{ (bare) is allowed for early exit} \\ \text{Rule 3: } & \text{No value-producing expression can be the sole statement} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Example — Invalid `void` method (Compile-time error):** ```java void greet() { return "Hello"; // ERROR: void methods cannot return a value } ``` **Example — Valid `int` method:** ```java int square(int n) { return n * n; // OK: n*n is int, matches return type } ``` ### 3.4 Local Variable Scope — Block-Level Visibility A local variable is **born** at the point of declaration and **dies** at the closing brace of its enclosing block. $$ \text{Scope}(v) = \{ \text{statements between } \texttt{\{} \text{ and } \texttt{\}} \text{ containing the declaration of } v \} $$ **Visualisation using a code skeleton:** ```java void demo() { int x = 10; // x is alive from here if (x > 5) { int y = 20; // y is alive only inside this if-block System.out.println(x + y); } // y dies here // System.out.println(y); // COMPILE ERROR: y out of scope } // x dies here ``` --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Method Invocation Flow — Call Stack Visualisation The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the **JVM Call Stack** behaves during a method call. Each rectangle represents a **stack frame** pushed/popped during execution. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Stack Frame: main"] --> B["1. main starts
num = 5
result = ?"] B --> C["2. modify num invoked
Stack grows"] C --> D["Stack Frame: modify
x = 5 copy
Local to modify"] D --> E["3. x = x + 10
x becomes 15
num in main untouched"] E --> F["4. modify returns
Stack shrinks
modify frame destroyed"] F --> G["5. Back in main
num still equals 5"] G --> H["6. Println num
Output = 5"] H --> I["7. main ends
Stack empty"] ``` ### 4.2 Block Architecture of a Java Method Declaration The diagram below maps **every syntactic slot** of a Java method declaration and the corresponding role it plays in the KTU 2024 evaluation scheme. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["public"] --> M["Access Modifier Slot
public / private / protected"] B["static"] --> N["Static Keyword Slot
class-level vs object-level"] C["int"] --> O["Return Type Slot
data type returned"] D["calculate"] --> P["Method Name Slot
camelCase identifier"] E["int a, int b"] --> Q["Parameter List Slot
inputs, comma-separated"] F["{ ... }"] --> R["Method Body Slot
declarations + return"] M --> S["Method Signature
= Name + Parameter List"] N --> S O --> S P --> S Q --> S R --> T["Execution Engine
JVM stack frame"] S --> T ``` ### 4.3 Pass-by-Value vs Pass-by-Reference — Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Call["Method Invocation"] --> Branch{Argument Type?} Branch -- Primitive --> PBV["Pass-by-Value
Copy of value
Original unchanged"] Branch -- Object Reference --> PBR["Pass-by-Value of Reference
Reference copy passed
Object state CAN be modified
But reference cannot be redirected"] PBV --> Out1["Example: int, double, char"] PBR --> Out2["Example: String, Array, custom objects"] ``` ### 4.4 Method Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Root["Java Methods"] --> Pre["Predefined
Library Methods"] Root --> User["User-Defined Methods"] User --> StaticM["Static Methods
class-name dot method-name"] User --> InstanceM["Instance Methods
object-reference dot method-name"] User --> VoidM["Void Methods
no return value"] User --> ValueM["Value-Returning Methods
return type declared"] Pre --> Lib1["java.lang.Math"] Pre --> Lib2["java.util.Scanner"] Pre --> Lib3["java.io.PrintStream"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]** **Q1. Define a method in Java. List and explain any four components of a method declaration with an example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **method** in Java is a named block of statements within a class that performs a specific task and may return a value. The four key components of a method declaration are: 1. **Access Modifier** (`public`, `private`, `protected`) — controls visibility of the method. 2. **Return Type** (e.g., `int`, `void`, `double`) — specifies the type of value the method returns, or `void` for no return. 3. **Method Name** — a valid Java identifier following camelCase convention. 4. **Parameter List** — comma-separated list of typed inputs (can be empty). **Example:** ```java public int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } ``` Here, `public` is the access modifier, `int` is the return type, `add` is the method name, and `(int a, int b)` is the parameter list. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest, Module 2]** **Q2. What is a method signature? Why is the return type not included in the method signature in Java?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **method signature** uniquely identifies a method for the Java compiler. It consists of the **method name** and the **parameter list** (number, types, and order of parameters). The **return type is not part of the signature** because: - If return type were included, then two methods with the same name and parameters but different return types would be considered different, leading to ambiguity at the call site. - The compiler resolves a call using only the name and arguments, not the expected return type. **Example:** The signatures `add(int, int)` and `add(int, double)` are different, but `int add(int,int)` and `double add(int,int)` would conflict at the call site `add(2,3)`. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern with Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **ONE full question per module slot**. --- #### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the different categories of methods in Java with suitable examples. Differentiate between **static** and **instance** methods. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program to define a class `Circle` with an instance variable `radius`. Include a method `calculateArea()` that returns the area and a `void` method `displayDetails()` that prints the radius and area. Demonstrate the invocation of both methods from `main`. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A **(a) Categories of Methods & Static vs Instance (7 Marks)** Java methods can be broadly classified into: 1. **Predefined Methods** — built into the Java API. Example: `Math.pow(2,3)`, `String.length()`. 2. **User-defined Methods** — written by the programmer as per application logic. 3. **Static Methods** — declared with the `static` keyword; belong to the class, not to any object. 4. **Instance Methods** — belong to a specific object; require an object reference to be called. 5. **Void Methods** — perform an action but return no value. 6. **Value-Returning Methods** — return a value of a specified type. **Static vs Instance Methods — Comparison Table:** | Feature | Static Method | Instance Method | |---------|---------------|-----------------| | Keyword | Declared with `static` | Declared without `static` | | Belongs to | Class | Object (instance) | | Called via | `ClassName.method()` | `objectRef.method()` | | Can access `this`? | No | Yes | | Memory | One copy shared | One copy per object | | Example | `Math.sqrt(16)` | `sc.nextInt()` | **Example:** ```java class Demo { static void staticMethod() { // Static System.out.println("Static"); } void instanceMethod() { // Instance System.out.println("Instance"); } } // Invocation: Demo.staticMethod(); // Static call Demo obj = new Demo(); obj.instanceMethod(); // Instance call ``` --- **(b) Java Program — Circle Class (7 Marks)** ```java class Circle { double radius; // Instance variable // Parameterized constructor for initialization Circle(double r) { radius = r; } // Value-returning method double calculateArea() { return Math.PI * radius * radius; } // Void method void displayDetails() { double area = calculateArea(); // calling value-returning method System.out.println("Radius: " + radius); System.out.println("Area: " + area); } public static void main(String[] args) { Circle c = new Circle(5.0); // Object creation c.displayDetails(); // Calling void method } } ``` **Output:** ``` Radius: 5.0 Area: 78.53981633974483 ``` **Explanation of key points (for full marks):** - The `calculateArea()` method uses the formula $A = \pi r^2$ and returns a `double`. - The `displayDetails()` method is `void` and internally calls `calculateArea()` — demonstrating **method-to-method invocation**. - The `main` method instantiates `Circle` and invokes `displayDetails()` on the object reference `c`. --- #### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative > **[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Model 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain **pass-by-value** in Java with a suitable example involving a primitive parameter. Prove that the original variable is unaffected. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program with a method `findMax(int a, int b, int c)` that returns the largest of three integers. Include a `void` method `printResult(int value)` that displays the result. Call both from `main`. **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B **(a) Pass-by-Value in Java (7 Marks)** **Definition:** In Java, arguments are **always passed by value**. This means a *copy* of the actual argument's value is made in memory and passed to the parameter of the called method. Any modification to the parameter inside the method does **not** affect the original variable in the caller. **Example Program:** ```java public class PassByValueDemo { static void changeValue(int x) { x = 100; // Modifies only the local copy System.out.println("Inside method, x = " + x); } public static void main(String[] args) { int num = 10; changeValue(num); System.out.println("After method call, num = " + num); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Inside method, x = 100 After method call, num = 10 ``` **Trace (Mathematical Style):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Initial state: } & num = 10 \text{ in main's stack frame} \\ \text{After changeValue(10) call: } & x = 10 \text{ (copy) in changeValue's stack frame} \\ \text{After } x = 100 \text{ executes: } & x = 100 \text{ in changeValue's frame, but } num = 10 \text{ in main} \\ \text{After method returns: } & \text{changeValue frame destroyed; main continues with } num = 10. \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Important Nuance:** When an **object reference** is passed, the reference itself is copied (pass-by-value of the reference), but the object it points to is shared. Hence, **object state CAN be modified** through the method, but the caller's reference cannot be redirected to a new object. --- **(b) Java Program — findMax and printResult (7 Marks)** ```java public class MaxFinder { // Value-returning method static int findMax(int a, int b, int c) { int max = a; if (b > max) max = b; if (c > max) max = c; return max; } // Void method static void printResult(int value) { System.out.println("The maximum value is: " + value); } public static void main(String[] args) { int result = findMax(25, 47, 19); // Call value-returning method printResult(result); // Call void method } } ``` **Output:** ``` The maximum value is: 47 ``` **Explanation of key concepts (for full marks):** - `findMax()` is a **value-returning** static method that returns an `int`. - `printResult()` is a **void** static method that only displays the value. - `main` calls `findMax()`, captures the return value into `result`, then passes it to `printResult()`. - This program demonstrates **method composition** — one method's return value is used as another's argument. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the return statement** in value-returning methods → *"missing return statement"* compile error (loses 2–3 marks). > 2. **Confusing method signature with method declaration** — students often include the return type inside the signature. Examiners deduct marks for this. > 3. **Writing `return value;` inside a `void` method** → compile-time error; lose 1–2 marks. > 4. **Treating object parameters as "pass-by-reference"** — this is a common conceptual error. Always write *"pass-by-value of the reference"*. > 5. **Not mentioning method scope (local variable lifecycle)** in Part-A questions worth 3 marks → examiners expect at least one example. > 6. **Missing `static` keyword** when invoking methods like `Math.sqrt()` directly from `main` without object creation → confusion about static vs instance. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ A **method** is a named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task — it encapsulates **behaviour** in OOP. - ✅ The **method signature** = **method name + parameter list** (return type is **excluded**). - ✅ Java methods have **six declaration slots**: access modifier, static, final, return type, name, parameters, body. - ✅ `void` methods return **no value**; non-void methods **must** return a value of the declared type. - ✅ Java is **strictly pass-by-value** — primitives get a copy, object references get a copy of the reference. - ✅ **Local variables** declared inside a method are **not initialised by default** (unlike instance variables). - ✅ **Static methods** belong to the class; **instance methods** belong to objects. - ✅ Method invocation creates a **stack frame** in the JVM **Call Stack**; the frame is destroyed when the method returns. - ✅ A method **can call another method**, and a value-returning method's result can be passed directly as an argument. - ✅ Always include the `static` keyword for methods called directly from `main` without object creation. - ✅ Common exam question: *"Differentiate between static and instance methods"* — use a comparison table for full marks. - ✅ Common exam question: *"What is method overloading?"* — same name, different parameter list (previewed for Module 2 Part B). ---

topics.title: Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Constructors # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **CONSTRUCTOR — KTU 2024 OFFICIAL DEFINITION** > A **constructor** in Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a special instance member method of a class that is **automatically invoked** at the moment of object instantiation to initialize the object's state. It has the **exact same name as the class**, has **no return type** (not even `void`), and is executed exactly **once per object lifecycle** before any other method can be called on the object. In Java (the reference language for OEERT613), every class must have at least one constructor. If the programmer does not write one, the Java compiler automatically inserts a **public no-argument default constructor**. Constructors are the first line of defense against uninitialized memory states and the foundational mechanism that enforces the OOP principle of **encapsulation at the point of birth**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Birth Registration Counter" Imagine a **hospital maternity ward**: - A **baby (object)** is born the instant a **Birth Certificate Form (constructor)** is filed. - The form **must carry the hospital's letterhead (class name)** — no other letterhead is accepted. - The form has **no "return receipt" (no return type)** — you don't get anything back; the certificate *is* the deliverable. - The form **auto-activates** the moment a new life enters the registry — you never call a nurse and say *"please run the birth form now"*. - Different forms exist for different scenarios: **single-birth form, twin-birth form, adoption form (parameterized, copy, etc.)** — all are *forms of the same name* but with different fields (constructor overloading). - One form can **reference the previous form** (e.g., for filling parent details) — that is `this()` and `super()` chaining. Just as a Birth Certificate guarantees the baby has an **identity, name, and date of birth**, a constructor guarantees the object has a **valid initial state**. ## 1.3 Key Properties (Examinable Highlights) > [!IMPORTANT] > **THE 7 GOLDEN RULES OF CONSTRUCTORS (High-Yield for KTU 2-Mark Questions)** > 1. **Name Identity** — The constructor name must be **exactly identical** to the class name (case-sensitive). > 2. **No Return Type** — Not even `void`. Writing a return type converts it into a regular method. > 3. **Auto-Invocation** — Called by the `new` keyword (or by another constructor via `this()` / `super()`). > 4. **Implicit `return this`** — A constructor implicitly returns the current object reference; you cannot return any other value. > 5. **Cannot be `static`, `final`, or `abstract`** — Constructors belong to instances, not classes, and they must be overridable across inheritance hierarchies. > 6. **Cannot be Inherited** — Subclasses do not inherit constructors of the superclass (though they can invoke them via `super()`). > 7. **Overloadable** — A class may declare multiple constructors differing in parameter list (constructor overloading). ## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall) - **Default Constructor** (compiler-generated) → **zero parameters**, **public access modifier**, **empty body**. - **No-Arg Constructor** (programmer-written) → **zero parameters**, but programmer may set any access modifier and any body logic. - **Object Memory Footprint Formula** → `ObjectSize = ClassHeader + InstanceVariables + Padding`, where padding aligns to **8 bytes** on 64-bit JVMs. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Decomposition — The "Why" and "How" ### 2.1.1 Why Do We Need Constructors? 1. **State Initialization Guarantee** — Without constructors, instance variables would hold default values (`0`, `false`, `null`), often leading to `NullPointerException` and `ArithmeticException` at runtime. 2. **Encapsulation Enforcement** — Forces the caller to provide required parameters; rejects invalid object creation. 3. **Polymorphic Object Birth** — Different parameter sets yield different valid initial configurations (constructor overloading). 4. **Inheritance Chain Initialization** — `super()` ensures parent class state is initialized *before* child class state, preserving **IS-A** relationships. ### 2.1.2 How Does the JVM Execute a Constructor? (Step-by-Step) When the JVM encounters the statement `Student s = new Student("Rahul", 21);`, the following sequence fires: - **Step 1 — Class Loading Check** — ClassLoader verifies `Student.class` is loaded into the **Method Area** of the JVM heap memory. - **Step 2 — Memory Allocation** — The JVM allocates raw memory in the **Heap** of size `ClassHeader (12 bytes) + fields(...) + padding`. - **Step 3 — Zero-Initialization** — All instance variables are set to JVM defaults (`int → 0`, `boolean → false`, `reference → null`). - **Step 4 — Constructor Resolution** — The compiler binds the call to the matching constructor signature via **static binding** (compile-time resolution based on argument types). - **Step 5 — Explicit / Implicit `super()` Call** — The first statement of *every* constructor (unless you write `this()`) is either an explicit `super(args)` or an implicit `super()` (no-arg) call. - **Step 6 — Field Initialization & Constructor Body Execution** — Instance initializers and the constructor body execute **top-to-bottom**. - **Step 7 — Object Reference Return** — The fully constructed object reference is returned to the call site. ## 2.2 Types of Constructors — Classification Matrix | # | Type | Trigger | Parameter Count | Use Case | KTU Frequency | |---|------|---------|----------------|----------|---------------| | 1 | **Default Constructor** | Compiler auto-generates | **0** | When no constructor is written | ★★★ | | 2 | **No-Arg Constructor** | Programmer writes | **0** | Force initialization logic without parameters | ★★★ | | 3 | **Parameterized Constructor** | Programmer writes | **≥ 1** | Mandatory field initialization | ★★★★★ | | 4 | **Copy Constructor** | Programmer writes | **1 (same-class object)** | Cloning state from existing object | ★★★★ | | 5 | **Private Constructor** | Programmer writes | Any | Singleton / Utility / static-only class | ★★ | | 6 | **Chained Constructor** | Uses `this()` | Varies | Code reuse within same class | ★★★★ | ## 2.3 Constructor Overloading — The Polymorphism at Birth **Definition:** Declaring multiple constructors within the same class with **different parameter lists** (number, type, or order of parameters). **Why it's high-yield:** KTU frequently tests the binding rule: - `new Student()` → binds to `Student()` - `new Student("Rahul")` → binds to `Student(String)` - `new Student(21)` → binds to `Student(int)` - `new Student("Rahul", 21)` → binds to `Student(String, int)` > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Trick Alert:** If you write a parameterized constructor but **no no-arg constructor**, the compiler **stops** generating the default constructor. Code that does `new MyClass()` will **fail to compile** — a classic 2-mark question. ## 2.4 Constructor Chaining — `this()` and `super()` ### 2.4.1 `this()` Chaining (Within Same Class) - `this(args)` calls another constructor of the **same class**. - **MUST be the very first statement** in the calling constructor. - Cannot be used inside regular methods — only in constructors. - Prevents duplication of initialization code. ### 2.4.2 `super()` Chaining (Parent Class) - `super(args)` calls a constructor of the **immediate parent class**. - **MUST be the very first statement** in the calling constructor. - If not written, the compiler **automatically inserts `super();`** as the first line. - If parent has no no-arg constructor, the child **must** explicitly call `super(args)`. > [!WARNING] > **`this()` and `super()` cannot coexist** in the same constructor. They are mutually exclusive first-statements. This is one of the most-tested KTU 2-mark rules. ## 2.5 Constructor vs Method — The Definitive Comparison | Property | Constructor | Method | |----------|-------------|--------| | **Name** | Same as class | Any valid identifier | | **Return Type** | None (not even `void`) | Mandatory (or `void`) | | **Invocation** | Automatic (via `new` / `this` / `super`) | Manual (object.method()) | | **Inheritance** | Not inherited | Inherited | | **`static` modifier** | Not allowed | Allowed | | **`final` modifier** | Not allowed | Allowed | | **Purpose** | Initialize object | Define behavior | | **Execution Count** | Once per object | Multiple times per object | ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Rule / Formula | Application | |---|----------------|-------------| | F1 | `Constructor.name == Class.name` (case-sensitive) | Naming correctness check | | F2 | `Constructor.returnType == null` | No return type ever | | F3 | `this()` is **first statement** of constructor | Chaining within class | | F4 | `super()` is **first statement** of constructor | Chaining to parent | | F5 | `this()` AND `super()` → **Compile Error** | Mutual exclusivity | | F6 | If programmer writes *any* constructor → **Default constructor auto-generation stops** | Critical bug source | | F7 | `ObjectSize = 12 (header) + Σ field sizes + padding to 8-byte boundary` | JVM memory calculation | | F8 | **Default field values** : `int → 0`, `double → 0.0`, `boolean → false`, `reference → null` | Pre-constructor state | | F9 | **Cyclic constructor chaining** → StackOverflowError | Infinite recursion guard | | F10 | `super()` call depth = **inheritance hierarchy height** | Tracing initialization order | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **JDBC `DriverManager`** — Uses **private constructors** to prevent instantiation of utility classes. - **Spring Framework `@Autowired`** — Relies on **constructor injection** as the recommended dependency-injection pattern. - **Singleton Design Pattern** — Implemented via **private constructors** exposing only a static `getInstance()`. - **Java `String`, `Integer`, `LocalDate`** — Heavy use of **static factory methods over constructors** (Bloch's *Effective Java* Item 1). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Execution Traces & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Example 1 — Constructor Chaining with `this()` Consider the following **Java class** that demonstrates full constructor chaining: ```java class Student { private String name; private int age; private String course; private double cgpa; // Constructor 1: Full parameterized public Student(String name, int age, String course, double cgpa) { this.name = name; // (a) initialize name this.age = age; // (b) initialize age this.course = course; // (c) initialize course this.cgpa = cgpa; // (d) initialize cgpa System.out.println("Step D: Full constructor finished."); } // Constructor 2: 3-arg → chains to Constructor 1 public Student(String name, int age, String course) { this(name, age, course, 0.0); // first statement → calls Constructor 1 System.out.println("Step C: 3-arg constructor finished."); } // Constructor 3: 2-arg → chains to Constructor 2 public Student(String name, int age) { this(name, age, "B.Tech CSE"); // first statement → calls Constructor 2 System.out.println("Step B: 2-arg constructor finished."); } // Constructor 4: 1-arg → chains to Constructor 3 public Student(String name) { this(name, 18); // first statement → calls Constructor 3 System.out.println("Step A: 1-arg constructor finished."); } } ``` **Driver Code:** ```java public static void main(String[] args) { Student s = new Student("Rahul"); } ``` ### 3.1.1 Execution Trace (Step-by-Step Derivation) Let the entry point be the call `new Student("Rahul")`. The JVM resolves this to **Constructor 4** because the argument list `(String)` matches its signature. **Trace Block 1 — Constructor 4 (1-arg) Entry** - Line: `this(name, 18);` is encountered as the first statement. - Control transfers to **Constructor 3 (2-arg)** with arguments `("Rahul", 18)`. **Trace Block 2 — Constructor 3 (2-arg) Entry** - Line: `this(name, age, "B.Tech CSE");` is encountered as the first statement. - Control transfers to **Constructor 2 (3-arg)** with arguments `("Rahul", 18, "B.Tech CSE")`. **Trace Block 3 — Constructor 2 (3-arg) Entry** - Line: `this(name, age, course, 0.0);` is encountered as the first statement. - Control transfers to **Constructor 1 (4-arg)** with arguments `("Rahul", 18, "B.Tech CSE", 0.0)`. **Trace Block 4 — Constructor 1 (4-arg) — Terminal of Chain** - Field initializations execute top-to-bottom: `name = "Rahul"`, `age = 18`, `course = "B.Tech CSE"`, `cgpa = 0.0`. - Prints: `Step D: Full constructor finished.` - Returns to **Constructor 2**. **Trace Block 5 — Unwinding** - Constructor 2 continues after the `this(...)` line. - Prints: `Step C: 3-arg constructor finished.` - Returns to **Constructor 3**. **Trace Block 6 — Unwinding** - Constructor 3 continues. - Prints: `Step B: 2-arg constructor finished.` - Returns to **Constructor 4**. **Trace Block 7 — Unwinding** - Constructor 4 continues. - Prints: `Step A: 1-arg constructor finished.` - Returns to `main()`. **Final Output Sequence (in order of printing):** ``` Step D: Full constructor finished. Step C: 3-arg constructor finished. Step B: 2-arg constructor finished. Step A: 1-arg constructor finished. ``` **Mathematical Statement of the Chain Order:** $$ \text{ExecutionOrder} = \left[ C_4 \xrightarrow{this()} C_3 \xrightarrow{this()} C_2 \xrightarrow{this()} C_1 \right] \xrightarrow{\text{unwind}} C_1 \to C_2 \to C_3 \to C_4 $$ where $C_i$ denotes the $i$-th constructor in descending parameter count. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Example 2 — Copy Constructor (Deep State Clone) A **copy constructor** takes an object of the same class as its single parameter and produces a **field-by-field deep or shallow copy**. ```java class Address { String city; String state; public Address(String city, String state) { this.city = city; this.state = state; } // Copy constructor (deep) public Address(Address other) { this.city = other.city; this.state = other.state; // deep copy because String is immutable } } class Employee { int id; String name; Address address; // reference field // Parameterized constructor public Employee(int id, String name, Address address) { this.id = id; this.name = name; this.address = address; // (i) shallow assignment } // Copy constructor — creates a fully independent clone public Employee(Employee other) { this.id = other.id; // primitive copy this.name = other.name; // immutable safe this.address = new Address(other.address); // (ii) deep copy } } ``` **Shallow vs Deep Copy Derivation:** For a primitive field `x`, the copy operation is: $$ x_{\text{new}} = x_{\text{old}} \quad \text{(value semantics, no aliasing)} $$ For a reference field `ref` under **shallow copy**: $$ \text{ref}_{\text{new}} = \text{ref}_{\text{old}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{aliasing occurs} $$ For a reference field `ref` under **deep copy**: $$ \text{ref}_{\text{new}} = \text{new Type}(\text{ref}_{\text{old}}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{independent heap allocation} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Keyword:** Use the term *"field-by-field deep copy"* explicitly when describing copy constructors that allocate new memory for reference fields. Examiners award full marks only for this precise terminology. ## 3.3 Exhaustive Example 3 — Inheritance Constructor Chain via `super()` ```java class Person { String name; public Person() { System.out.println("Person default"); } public Person(String name) { this.name = name; System.out.println("Person: " + name); } } class Teacher extends Person { String subject; public Teacher() { super(); // explicit no-arg super call System.out.println("Teacher default"); } public Teacher(String name, String subject) { super(name); // explicit parameterized super call this.subject = subject; System.out.println("Teacher: " + subject); } } ``` **Execution Trace for `new Teacher("Anita", "Maths")`:** - **Step 1:** `main()` invokes `Teacher(String, String)`. - **Step 2:** First statement is `super(name)`. Control jumps to `Person(String)`. - **Step 3:** `Person(String)` runs: sets `this.name = "Anita"`, prints `Person: Anita`. - **Step 4:** Control returns to `Teacher(String, String)`. - **Step 5:** `this.subject = "Maths"` executes. - **Step 6:** Prints `Teacher: Maths`. **Output:** ``` Person: Anita Teacher: Maths ``` **Order Formula:** $$ \text{InitOrder} = \text{Parent Constructor} \;\to\; \text{Child Constructor} $$ **Mathematical Statement of Recursive Constructor Depth:** $$ \text{ChainDepth}(C_n) = 1 + \text{ChainDepth}(\text{superclass of } C_n) $$ $$ \text{ChainDepth}(\text{Object}) = 1 $$ $$ \therefore \quad \text{TotalConstructorCalls} = \text{InheritanceHierarchyHeight} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes & Error Conditions | # | Code Pattern | Compiler / Runtime Outcome | Mark Impact | |---|--------------|----------------------------|-------------| | 1 | `class A { A() { return 5; } }` | **Compile Error** — constructor cannot return a value | ⚠️ High | | 2 | `class A { void A() { } }` | **Compiles** but is a regular method, not a constructor | ⚠️ High | | 3 | `class A { A() { this(10); } A(int x) { this(); } }` | **Runtime StackOverflowError** — cyclic chaining | ⚠️ Critical | | 4 | Child constructor without `super()` when parent has no no-arg | **Compile Error** — implicit `super()` has no match | ⚠️ Critical | | 5 | `class A { A(String s) { } }` then `new A();` | **Compile Error** — default constructor not generated | ⚠️ High | | 6 | `static` / `final` / `abstract` constructor | **Compile Error** — illegal modifier | ⚠️ Medium | | 7 | `this()` not as first statement | **Compile Error** — "call to this must be first statement" | ⚠️ High | | 8 | `super()` in `main()` or static block | **Compile Error** — super outside constructor | ⚠️ Medium | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Constructor Execution Lifecycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Client Code: new ClassName args] --> B{Class Loaded in Method Area?} B -- No --> C[ClassLoader: Load .class file] C --> D B -- Yes --> D[Step 2: Allocate Heap Memory] D --> E[Step 3: Zero-Initialize All Fields] E --> F{Compiler-Inserted super call?} F -- Yes --> G[Step 4a: Execute Parent Constructor] F -- No --> H[Step 4b: Execute this chain] G --> I[Step 5: Execute Field Initializers] H --> I I --> J[Step 6: Execute Constructor Body] J --> K[Step 7: Implicitly return this] K --> L[Object Reference Returned to Client] ``` ## 4.2 Constructor Chaining Topology ```mermaid flowchart LR C1["Constructor 1
4 parameters"] --> C2["Constructor 2
3 parameters"] C2 --> C3["Constructor 3
2 parameters"] C3 --> C4["Constructor 4
1 parameter"] C4Entry["Client Call new Class arg"] --> C4 C1 --> Out["Object Fully Constructed"] C2 --> Out C3 --> Out C4 --> Out ``` **Reading the diagram:** Arrows show the **call direction** (top-down), and the **unwind** happens bottom-up — the deepest constructor (Constructor 1) finishes first. ## 4.3 Inheritance Constructor Flow — `super()` Propagation ```mermaid flowchart TD Main["main new Child args"] --> Child["Child Constructor"] Child -- super args --> Parent["Parent Constructor"] Parent -- super args --> Grand["Grandparent Constructor"] Grand -- super args --> ObjectRoot["Object Constructor"] ObjectRoot --> Grand Grand --> Parent Parent --> Child Child --> Main ``` **Reading the diagram:** Constructor calls propagate **upward** through the inheritance chain; execution **unwinds downward**, ensuring parent state is initialized before child state. ## 4.4 Constructor Type Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Root["Constructor Types"] --> Auto["Compiler Generated"] Root --> Manual["Programmer Defined"] Auto --> DefCon["Default Constructor
