public, no-arg, empty body"] Manual --> NoArg["No-Arg Constructor"] Manual --> Param["Parameterized Constructor"] Manual --> Copy["Copy Constructor"] Manual --> Private["Private Constructor"] NoArg --> ChainLink1["Used in this chaining"] Param --> ChainLink2["Used in super chaining"] Copy --> UseCase1["Cloning state"] Private --> UseCase2["Singleton pattern"] ``` ## 4.5 Constructor vs Method Decision Block ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Member of class"] --> Q1{"Name equals class name?"} Q1 -- No --> Method["Regular Method"] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Has return type declared?"} Q2 -- Yes --> Method Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Invoked via new or this or super?"} Q3 -- Yes --> Con["Constructor"] Q3 -- No --> Method ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between a constructor and a method in Java. List any four distinguishing properties.** **Model Answer (4 Key Properties — pick any 4):** | Property | Constructor | Method | |----------|-------------|--------| | **Name** | Same as class | Any identifier | | **Return type** | None | Mandatory | | **Invocation** | Automatic (`new` / `this` / `super`) | Manual (object.method()) | | **Inheritance** | Not inherited | Inherited | | **`static` allowed** | No | Yes | | **Execution** | Once per object | Multiple times | **Examiner's Note:** Students often forget "return type" or write "no return type" for methods. Be precise. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is a copy constructor? Write a Java code snippet demonstrating a copy constructor for a class `Book` with fields `title` (String) and `price` (double).** **Model Answer:** A **copy constructor** is a constructor that accepts an object of the same class as its parameter and creates a new object by copying the field values of the passed object. ```java class Book { String title; double price; // Parameterized constructor public Book(String title, double price) { this.title = title; this.price = price; } // Copy constructor public Book(Book other) { this.title = other.title; // field-by-field copy this.price = other.price; } public void display() { System.out.println(title + " - Rs." + price); } public static void main(String[] args) { Book b1 = new Book("Java Programming", 499.50); Book b2 = new Book(b1); // invoking copy constructor b1.display(); b2.display(); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Java Programming - Rs.499.5 Java Programming - Rs.499.5 ``` --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Forgetting to make `this()` / `super()` the **first statement** → **−2 marks** (compiler error) > - Writing `void` before constructor name → **−1 mark** (it becomes a method) > - Confusing **default constructor** (compiler-generated) with **no-arg constructor** (programmer-written) → **−1 mark** > - Missing `super()` in child class when parent lacks a no-arg constructor → **Compile-time failure in exam code** → **−3 marks** > - Not drawing inheritance diagram in super-related questions → **−2 marks** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the concept of constructor overloading in Java with a suitable example. Discuss how the compiler resolves which constructor to invoke. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is constructor chaining? Explain the use of `this()` and `super()` keywords with a Java program demonstrating both **same-class chaining** and **inheritance-based chaining**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — Constructor Overloading **Definition:** **Constructor overloading** is the technique of declaring **multiple constructors** within the same class with **different parameter lists** (varying number, type, or order of parameters). The compiler selects the appropriate constructor at **compile time** based on the arguments provided to the `new` keyword. **Java Code:** ```java class Box { double length, breadth, height; // No-arg constructor public Box() { this.length = this.breadth = this.height = 0; System.out.println("No-arg Box created"); } // 1-arg constructor (cube) public Box(double side) { this.length = this.breadth = this.height = side; System.out.println("Cube Box created with side " + side); } // 3-arg constructor public Box(double length, double breadth, double height) { this.length = length; this.breadth = breadth; this.height = height; System.out.println("Cuboid Box created"); } public double volume() { return length * breadth * height; } public static void main(String[] args) { Box b1 = new Box(); Box b2 = new Box(5.0); Box b3 = new Box(2.0, 3.0, 4.0); System.out.println("Volume b2 = " + b2.volume()); System.out.println("Volume b3 = " + b3.volume()); } } ``` **Compiler Resolution Mechanism:** The Java compiler uses **static binding** (compile-time binding) to match the call to a constructor signature. The matching is based on: 1. **Number of arguments** 2. **Type of arguments** 3. **Order of arguments** **Resolution Trace:** - `new Box()` → no arguments → matches `Box()` → binds to **No-arg constructor** - `new Box(5.0)` → one `double` argument → matches `Box(double)` → binds to **1-arg constructor** - `new Box(2.0, 3.0, 4.0)` → three `double` arguments → matches `Box(double, double, double)` → binds to **3-arg constructor** **Mathematical Statement:** $$ \text{Resolve}(C, \text{args}) = C_i \quad \text{where } \text{signature}(C_i) = \text{types}(\text{args}) $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) — Constructor Chaining **Definition:** **Constructor chaining** is the mechanism by which one constructor calls another constructor — either within the **same class** (using `this()`) or in the **parent class** (using `super()`) — to **reuse initialization code** and maintain a consistent initialization order. **Java Code Demonstrating Both:** ```java class Vehicle { String type; public Vehicle() { System.out.println("Vehicle default constructor"); } public Vehicle(String type) { this.type = type; System.out.println("Vehicle parameterized: " + type); } } class Car extends Vehicle { String model; int speed; // Same-class chaining using this() public Car() { this("Sedan", 120); // (i) chain to 2-arg System.out.println("Car default constructor"); } public Car(String model, int speed) { super("Four-Wheeler"); // (ii) chain to parent 1-arg this.model = model; this.speed = speed; System.out.println("Car parameterized: " + model); } public static void main(String[] args) { Car c = new Car(); } } ``` **Execution Trace for `new Car()`:** - **Step 1:** `main()` calls `Car()` (no-arg). - **Step 2:** First statement is `this("Sedan", 120)` → jumps to `Car(String, int)`. - **Step 3:** First statement of `Car(String, int)` is `super("Four-Wheeler")` → jumps to `Vehicle(String)`. - **Step 4:** `Vehicle(String)` executes: `this.type = "Four-Wheeler"`, prints `Vehicle parameterized: Four-Wheeler`. - **Step 5:** Returns to `Car(String, int)`: assigns `model = "Sedan"`, `speed = 120`, prints `Car parameterized: Sedan`. - **Step 6:** Returns to `Car()`: prints `Car default constructor`. **Output:** ``` Vehicle parameterized: Four-Wheeler Car parameterized: Sedan Car default constructor ``` **Order Formula:** $$ \text{ChainOrder} = \text{Vehicle(String)} \to \text{Car(String, int)} \to \text{Car()} $$ **Rules Highlighted:** 1. `this()` and `super()` **must be the first statement** of the constructor. 2. They are **mutually exclusive** — cannot coexist in the same constructor. 3. Cycles (e.g., `this()` calling itself indirectly) cause `StackOverflowError`. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the different types of constructors in Java. Write a program to demonstrate a **parameterized constructor** and a **copy constructor** for a class `Student` with fields `rollNo`, `name`, and `marks`. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are private constructors? Explain their role in implementing the **Singleton design pattern** with a complete Java program. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — Types of Constructors **The 5 Main Types:** 1. **Default Constructor** — Auto-generated by compiler when no constructor exists; public, no-arg, empty body. 2. **No-Arg Constructor** — Programmer-written, zero parameters. 3. **Parameterized Constructor** — Programmer-written, accepts one or more parameters. 4. **Copy Constructor** — Accepts one parameter of the same class type. 5. **Private Constructor** — Restricts external object instantiation. **Java Code for `Student` Class:** ```java class Student { int rollNo; String name; double marks; // (i) Parameterized constructor public Student(int rollNo, String name, double marks) { this.rollNo = rollNo; this.name = name; this.marks = marks; System.out.println("Parameterized constructor called"); } // (ii) Copy constructor public Student(Student s) { this.rollNo = s.rollNo; this.name = s.name; this.marks = s.marks; System.out.println("Copy constructor called"); } public void display() { System.out.println(rollNo + " | " + name + " | " + marks); } public static void main(String[] args) { Student s1 = new Student(101, "Arjun", 89.5); Student s2 = new Student(s1); // copy constructor invoked s1.display(); s2.display(); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Parameterized constructor called Copy constructor called 101 | Arjun | 89.5 101 | Arjun | 89.5 ``` **Mathematical Notation of Copy Operation:** $$ \forall f \in \text{fields}(S): \quad S_{\text{new}}.f \leftarrow S_{\text{old}}.f $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) — Private Constructors & Singleton **Definition:** A **private constructor** is a constructor declared with the `private` access modifier, **restricting object instantiation** from outside the class. It is primarily used in: - **Singleton Design Pattern** (only one object exists) - **Utility/Helper Classes** (e.g., `java.lang.Math`) - **Static Factory Method** implementations **Singleton Design Pattern with Private Constructor:** ```java class DatabaseConnection { // (1) Static reference to hold the single instance private static DatabaseConnection singleInstance = null; // (2) Some instance field for demonstration private String connectionString; // (3) Private constructor — prevents external instantiation private DatabaseConnection() { connectionString = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktu_db"; System.out.println("DatabaseConnection created (only once)"); } // (4) Public static factory method — global access point public static DatabaseConnection getInstance() { if (singleInstance == null) { singleInstance = new DatabaseConnection(); } return singleInstance; } public String getConnectionString() { return connectionString; } public static void main(String[] args) { // Cannot do: new DatabaseConnection(); // Compile Error DatabaseConnection db1 = DatabaseConnection.getInstance(); DatabaseConnection db2 = DatabaseConnection.getInstance(); System.out.println("db1 hashCode: " + db1.hashCode()); System.out.println("db2 hashCode: " + db2.hashCode()); System.out.println("Same instance? " + (db1 == db2)); System.out.println("URL: " + db1.getConnectionString()); } } ``` **Output:** ``` DatabaseConnection created (only once) db1 hashCode: 1234567 db2 hashCode: 1234567 Same instance? true URL: jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktu_db ``` **Mathematical Proof of Singleton Property:** $$ \forall t_1, t_2 \in \text{time}: \quad \text{id}(getInstance(t_1)) = \text{id}(getInstance(t_2)) $$ where $\text{id}(x)$ denotes the unique object identity (memory address) of object $x$. **Key Exam-Worthy Points:** 1. Private constructor **prevents** `new DatabaseConnection()` from any external class. 2. The static method `getInstance()` controls the single creation. 3. After the first call, `singleInstance != null` → subsequent calls return the **same** reference. 4. `db1 == db2` evaluates to `true` because both refer to the same heap object. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Print This Section Before Exam** - ✅ Constructor **name = class name** (case-sensitive, no exceptions). - ✅ Constructor has **NO return type** (writing `void` makes it a method). - ✅ Constructor is **auto-invoked** by `new`, `this()`, or `super()`. - ✅ **Default constructor** is auto-generated **only if no constructor is written**. - ✅ Writing **any** constructor disables compiler's default constructor. - ✅ **`this()`** chains within same class — must be **first statement**. - ✅ **`super()`** chains to parent class — must be **first statement**. - ✅ **`this()` and `super()` cannot coexist** in the same constructor. - ✅ If parent has **no no-arg constructor**, child must **explicitly call `super(args)`**. - ✅ **Constructor overloading** is resolved at **compile time** via static binding. - ✅ **Copy constructor** takes one parameter of the same class type. - ✅ **Private constructor** is used in **Singleton pattern** and utility classes. - ✅ Constructors are **not inherited**; they are not `static`, `final`, or `abstract`. - ✅ Cyclic `this()` chaining causes **StackOverflowError** at runtime. - ✅ **Execution order**: parent constructor → child constructor (top-down unwind). - ✅ Object memory: `12 (header) + fields + padding` to **8-byte alignment**. > **END OF MODULE 2 — CONSTRUCTORS NOTES** > *OEERT613 — Object Oriented Concepts | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: this Keyword
Method Overloading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method Overloading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method Overloading** is a core feature of Object-Oriented Programming that allows a class to declare **multiple methods with the exact same name** but with **different parameter lists** (also called *signatures*). The compiler differentiates between these methods at **compile time** based on the arguments passed, making it a classic example of **Compile-Time Polymorphism** (also known as *Static Binding* or *Early Binding*).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Java-based), method overloading is also referred to as **Static Polymorphism** because the decision of which method to invoke is made by the Java compiler during compilation, **not** at runtime by the JVM.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Serve" Function

Imagine a waiter in a restaurant who understands the word *"Serve"* in multiple ways:

- **Serve()** — Brings water to the table.
- **Serve(String item)** — Serves a single named dish.
- **Serve(String item, int quantity)** — Serves a specific quantity of a dish.
- **Serve(String item, String spiceLevel)** — Serves a dish with a custom spice level.

The waiter's instruction is the **same word ("Serve")**, but the *context* (what the customer says) determines which exact action is performed. This is exactly how method overloading works — the **method name stays the same**, but the **parameter list (signature)** tells the compiler which version to use.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Method Signature** | The combination of method name + parameter list (types, number, order). Return type is **NOT** part of the signature. |
| **Static Binding** | The linking of a method call to its definition at compile time. |
| **Compile-Time Polymorphism** | Polymorphism resolved by the compiler, not the JVM. |
| **Argument List** | The list of values passed during a method call. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Method Overloading is a guaranteed question topic in Module 2. It is frequently combined with *Constructors* and *Type Promotion* in Part B (14-mark) questions. Expect at least one 3-mark or 7-mark sub-part on this topic in every University Exam cycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Method Overloading

For the compiler to treat two methods as **overloaded**, their parameter lists must differ in **at least one** of the following three ways:

1. **Different Number of Parameters**
   `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(int a, int b, int c)`

2. **Different Data Types of Parameters**
   `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(double a, double b)`

3. **Different Order of Parameters** (when types differ)
   `display(String name, int age)` vs `display(int age, String name)`

## 2.2 Rules That Govern Method Overloading

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following rules are **exam-favorite** and must be memorized verbatim:

- ✅ **Valid overloading**: Changing the parameter list (number, type, or order).
- ❌ **Invalid overloading**: Changing **only** the return type.
- ❌ **Invalid overloading**: Changing **only** the access modifier (public → private).
- ❌ **Invalid overloading**: Changing **only** the exception list (throws clause).
- ✅ **Valid overloading**: Changing both parameter list **and** return type simultaneously.
- ✅ **Valid overloading**: A `static` method **can** be overloaded with a non-static method (and vice-versa), provided the parameter list differs.

## 2.3 Method Resolution & Type Promotion (Critical Concept)

When the exact argument type does not match any overloaded method, the Java compiler applies **automatic type promotion** following a strict hierarchy:

> **byte → short → int → long → float → double**
> **char → int** (direct promotion)

The compiler picks the method with the **smallest type that is wider than (or equal to)** the argument type, which prevents data loss.

If no exact match exists and the promotion leads to **two equally valid methods** (e.g., passing `null` to overloaded methods accepting two unrelated reference types), a **compile-time ambiguity error** is thrown.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule | Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Method Signature = | `name + parameter list` | High |
| Return Type in Signature? | **No** | Very High |
| Access Modifier in Signature? | **No** | High |
| Binding Type | **Static (Compile-Time)** | Very High |
| Polymorphism Category | **Ad-hoc Polymorphism** | Medium |
| Type Promotion Order | `byte → short → int → long → float → double` | Very High |
| Can `main` be overloaded? | **Yes** (but JVM calls only `public static void main(String[] args)`) | High |
| Overloading across Classes? | **No** (must be in the **same class**; across classes it becomes *overriding* if inherited) | High |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Method overloading is heavily used in:

- **Java Standard Library**: `System.out.println()` is overloaded for `int`, `double`, `String`, `char`, `boolean`, and `Object`.
- **Math Class**: `Math.max(int, int)`, `Math.max(double, double)`, `Math.max(long, long)`, `Math.max(float, float)`.
- **String Class**: `String.valueOf()` is overloaded for every primitive type.
- **Constructors**: Every class implicitly demonstrates overloading when multiple constructors are defined.

This improves **API readability**, **code maintainability**, and **intuitive usage** for end developers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1: Overloading by Number of Parameters

```java
class Calculator {
    // Version 1: Two parameters
    int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    // Version 2: Three parameters (Overloaded by NUMBER)
    int add(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Calculator calc = new Calculator();
        System.out.println(calc.add(10, 20));       // Calls Version 1 → Output: 30
        System.out.println(calc.add(10, 20, 30));    // Calls Version 2 → Output: 60
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Resolution Logic:**
1. The compiler sees `calc.add(10, 20)` — argument count is **2**.
2. It searches the `Calculator` class for an `add` method accepting **2 parameters**.
3. It finds the first method — **binds statically**.
4. For `calc.add(10, 20, 30)`, it finds the second method — **binds statically**.

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2: Overloading by Data Type

```java
class Printer {
    void show(int a) {
        System.out.println("Integer: " + a);
    }

    void show(double a) {
        System.out.println("Double: " + a);
    }

    void show(String a) {
        System.out.println("String: " + a);
    }
}
```

When `show('A')` is called (a `char`), the compiler promotes `char → int` and binds to `show(int)`. If only `show(double)` existed, it would still work via `char → int → long → float → double` promotion chain.

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3: Type Promotion and Ambiguity

```java
class AmbiguityDemo {
    void process(int a, double b) { System.out.println("int-double"); }
    void process(double a, int b)  { System.out.println("double-int"); }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        AmbiguityDemo obj = new AmbiguityDemo();
        obj.process(10, 20.5);   // ✅ Exact match → "int-double"
        obj.process(10.5, 20);   // ✅ Exact match → "double-int"
        // obj.process(10, 20);  // ❌ COMPILE ERROR: ambiguous (both int & double are valid)
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **The `process(10, 20)` call fails to compile.** Why? Both `10` and `20` are `int` literals. The compiler can promote the *second* `int` to `double` to match `process(int, double)`, OR promote the *first* `int` to `double` to match `process(double, int)`. Since both are equally valid, this is a **compile-time ambiguity error**. This is a classic KTU question!

## 3.4 Walkthrough 4: Invalid Overloading — Return Type Only

```java
class Invalid {
    int greet() { return 1; }
    // double greet() { return 1.0; }  // ❌ COMPILE ERROR: duplicate method greet()
}
```

**Why?** Two methods with the same name and parameter list but different return types are treated as **duplicate methods** by the compiler, because the signature does not include the return type. The compiler cannot distinguish them when a call like `greet();` is made (the return value is ignored).

## 3.5 Walkthrough 5: Overloading `main()` Method

```java
public class MainOverload {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Standard main() called by JVM");
        main("KTU");
        main(2024);
    }

    public static void main(String msg) {
        System.out.println("Overloaded main with String: " + msg);
    }

    public static void main(int year) {
        System.out.println("Overloaded main with int: " + year);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Standard main() called by JVM
Overloaded main with String: KTU
Overloaded main with int: 2024
```

The JVM **only** invokes `public static void main(String[] args)` as the entry point. All other `main()` versions must be called explicitly from within the program.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scenario | Overloads Successfully? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `sum(int, int)` and `sum(double, double)` | ✅ Yes | Different parameter types |
| `sum(int, int)` and `sum(int, int, int)` | ✅ Yes | Different parameter count |
| `sum(int, int)` and `sum(int, int)` | ❌ No | Duplicate declaration |
| `int sum(int, int)` and `double sum(int, int)` | ❌ No | Same signature, return type ignored |
| `void print(int)` and `static void print(double)` | ✅ Yes | Different signatures |
| `print(int)` and `private print(double)` | ✅ Yes | Different signatures (modifier ignored) |
| Inherited `display()` and child `display(String)` | ❌ No (it's **Overriding** if signatures match) | Different concept (different class) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Compile-Time Method Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program calls overloadedMethod arguments] --> B{Exact match in class?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Bind to exact-match method]
    B -- No --> D{Type promotion possible?}
    D -- Yes and unique --> E[Apply type promotion and bind]
    D -- No or multiple valid --> F{Autoboxing to wrapper class?}
    F -- Yes and unique --> G[Bind to wrapper-type method]
    F -- No or multiple valid --> H[Varargs method available?]
    H -- Yes --> I[Bind to varargs method]
    H -- No --> J[COMPILE-TIME ERROR: ambiguous]
    C --> K[Generate bytecode with direct call]
    E --> K
    G --> K
    I --> K
```

## 4.2 Class Architecture Showing Overloaded Methods

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Calculator {
        +int add(int a, int b)
        +int add(int a, int b, int c)
        +double add(double a, double b)
        +String add(String a, String b)
    }

    class Demo {
        +main(String[] args)
    }

    Demo ..> Calculator : creates object and calls add
```

## 4.3 Memory-Level Block Diagram of Binding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CompileTime[Compile Time - Static Binding]
        SRC[Source Code: calc.add 10, 20] --> CMP[Java Compiler]
        CMP -->|Resolves to| M1[Method 1: add int, int]
        CMP -->|Generates| BC[Bytecode with direct reference to Method 1]
    end

    subgraph RunTime[Run Time - JVM]
        BC --> JVM[JVM Executes Method 1 directly]
    end
```

## 4.4 Type Promotion Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[byte] --> B[short]
    B --> C[int]
    C --> D[long]
    D --> E[float]
    E --> F[double]
    G[char] --> C
    C --> H[Object]
    I[boolean] --> X[No Promotion Allowed]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define method overloading. List any two rules that must be followed while overloading methods in Java.**

**Model Answer:**

Method overloading is a feature in Java that allows a class to have multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists** (different number, type, or order of parameters). It is an example of **compile-time polymorphism** (static binding).

**Rules:**
1. Overloaded methods **must differ** in the parameter list — either in number, type, or order of arguments.
2. The **return type alone cannot** be used to differentiate overloaded methods; it must be accompanied by a different parameter list.

*(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for any two rules)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**Can the `main` method in Java be overloaded? Justify your answer with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

Yes, the `main` method in Java **can be overloaded** by defining additional methods named `main` with **different parameter lists**. However, the JVM always invokes the standard `public static void main(String[] args)` as the program's entry point. Other overloaded versions must be **explicitly called** from within the program.

**Example:**
```java
public static void main(String[] args) {  // Entry point
    main("Hello");
}
public static void main(String msg) {     // Overloaded
    System.out.println(msg);
}
```

*(2 marks for yes-with-justification, 1 mark for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each alternative typically has sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks. Below are two fully solved alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of method overloading in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the different ways in which methods can be overloaded. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Method overloading allows a class to declare multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists**. The compiler decides which method to invoke at **compile time** based on the arguments passed — hence it is called **static (compile-time) polymorphism**.

**Different ways to overload:**

1. **By changing the number of parameters:**
   ```java
   void display(int a) { }
   void display(int a, int b) { }
   ```

2. **By changing the data type of parameters:**
   ```java
   void display(int a) { }
   void display(double a) { }
   ```

3. **By changing the order of parameters:**
   ```java
   void display(String name, int age) { }
   void display(int age, String name) { }
   ```

**Complete Example Program:**
```java
class OverloadDemo {
    void show(int a) {
        System.out.println("int: " + a);
    }
    void show(double a) {
        System.out.println("double: " + a);
    }
    void show(int a, int b) {
        System.out.println("two ints: " + a + "," + b);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        OverloadDemo obj = new OverloadDemo();
        obj.show(5);          // calls show(int)
        obj.show(5.5);        // calls show(double)
        obj.show(5, 10);      // calls show(int, int)
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
int: 5
double: 5.5
two ints: 5,10
```

*(3 marks for concept, 2 marks for the three ways, 2 marks for example program and output)*

---

**(b)** What is type promotion in method overloading? Explain with an example what happens when no exact match is found for the arguments passed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Type Promotion** is the automatic conversion of a smaller data type to a larger data type by the Java compiler when resolving overloaded method calls, in order to find a compatible method signature.

**Promotion Hierarchy:**
$$ \text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double} $$
$$ \text{char} \rightarrow \text{int} $$

**Example Program:**
```java
class PromoDemo {
    void print(int a)    { System.out.println("int: " + a); }
    void print(double a) { System.out.println("double: " + a); }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        PromoDemo obj = new PromoDemo();
        obj.print(10);      // Exact match → int version
        obj.print(10.5);    // Exact match → double version
        obj.print('A');     // No exact match; char promoted to int
        obj.print(10L);     // No exact match; long promoted to float then double
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
int: 10
double: 10.5
int: 65          (char 'A' promoted to int → ASCII 65)
double: 10.0     (long promoted to double)
```

**What happens when no match is found at all?**
The compiler traverses the promotion chain. If still no match exists, a **compile-time error** is raised. If promotion leads to **two equally valid methods** (e.g., `process(int, double)` and `process(double, int)` both reachable for `process(10, 20)`), an **ambiguity error** occurs.

*(3 marks for promotion concept + hierarchy, 2 marks for example + output, 2 marks for ambiguity explanation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding. Can we achieve method overloading by changing only the return type? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---|---|---|
| **Class Scope** | Within the **same class** | Across **parent and child** classes |
| **Binding Time** | **Compile-time** (static) | **Run-time** (dynamic) |
| **Signature** | Must **differ** | Must be **exactly same** |
| **Return Type** | Can differ (if signature differs) | Must be **same** or *covariant* |
| **Inheritance Required?** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Polymorphism Type** | Static / Compile-time | Dynamic / Run-time |
| **Access Modifier** | No restrictions | Cannot be more restrictive |

**Can we overload by changing only the return type? — NO.**

```java
class Test {
    int show()    { return 1; }
    double show() { return 1.0; }  // ❌ Compile-time error
}
```

**Justification:** The Java compiler determines a method's identity using its **method signature**, which includes the method name and parameter list — but **NOT the return type**. Two methods with the same name and parameter list but different return types are treated as **duplicate methods**, causing a compile-time error. Additionally, even if the compiler allowed it, the call `show();` (where return value is ignored) would become **ambiguous**.

*(3 marks for differences table, 2 marks for "No" answer, 2 marks for justification with example)*

---

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a class `Shape` that overloads a method `area()` to compute the area of: (i) a square (side), (ii) a rectangle (length, breadth), and (iii) a circle (radius). Demonstrate the calls in `main()`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
class Shape {
    // Area of square
    double area(double side) {
        return side * side;
    }

    // Area of rectangle
    double area(double length, double breadth) {
        return length * breadth;
    }

    // Area of circle
    double area(int radius) {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s = new Shape();
        System.out.println("Square area: " + s.area(5.0));
        System.out.println("Rectangle area: " + s.area(4.0, 6.0));
        System.out.println("Circle area: " + s.area(7));
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Square area: 25.0
Rectangle area: 24.0
Circle area: 153.93804002589985
```

**Explanation:**
- The method `area()` is overloaded **three times**.
- `area(double)` is called for the square (single `double` argument).
- `area(double, double)` is called for the rectangle (two `double` arguments).
- `area(int)` is called for the circle (single `int` argument). Note that we deliberately used `int` to demonstrate **overloading by data type** as well.

*(2 marks for class declaration, 3 marks for all three overloaded methods, 1 mark for object creation, 1 mark for output)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Writing "return type can be different" as a rule of overloading** — Marks are deducted because the *signature* must differ; return type alone is **not** a signature component. Always clarify "return type may differ, but the parameter list **must** differ."
> 2. **Forgetting that `main()` overloads are valid** — Several students incorrectly claim `main` cannot be overloaded. State clearly that **only the standard `main` is the JVM entry point**, but other overloads are valid.
> 3. **Confusing overloading with overriding** — This is the **#1 confusion**. Overloading = same class, different signature, compile-time. Overriding = parent-child, same signature, run-time.
> 4. **Not showing the OUTPUT of the program** — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for **tracing and writing the output**. Always dry-run and display expected output.
> 5. **Ignoring type promotion** — When asked "what if no exact match exists?", failing to mention the **promotion chain** and **ambiguity case** loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Definition:** Method overloading = same method name, **different parameter list**, within the **same class**.
- 📌 **Binding Type:** **Compile-time** (Static Binding) — resolved by the compiler, not the JVM.
- 📌 **Polymorphism Category:** **Static Polymorphism** (also called *Ad-hoc Polymorphism*).
- 📌 **Three valid ways to overload:** Different **number**, different **type**, or different **order** of parameters.
- 📌 **Return type alone CANNOT** differentiate overloaded methods.
- 📌 **Access modifier and throws clause** are not part of the method signature for overloading.
- 📌 **Type Promotion Order:** `byte → short → int → long → float → double`; `char → int` directly.
- 📌 **Ambiguity Error:** Occurs when type promotion leads to **two equally valid** methods.
- 📌 **`main()` CAN be overloaded**, but JVM only calls `public static void main(String[] args)`.
- 📌 **Overloading ≠ Overriding:** Overloading is **within one class**; Overriding is **across parent-child classes**.
- 📌 **Real-world examples:** `System.out.println()`, `Math.max()`, `String.valueOf()` are all overloaded.
- 📌 **Constructor Overloading** follows the exact same rules as method overloading.
- 📌 **Exam Tip:** Always include a **complete program with output** for 7-mark questions — it secures easy traceability marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Objects as Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Using Objects as Parameters ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Using Objects as Parameters** is a mechanism in Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) where a method accepts one or more **objects** as its formal parameters, allowing the method body to access, manipulate, or invoke the state (attributes) and behavior (methods) of the passed objects. In Java, the reference (memory address) of the object is passed to the method, which means the method receives a **copy of the reference**, not a copy of the actual object. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OEERT613 – Object Oriented Concepts (Module 2: Java Fundamentals and Objects)**, this topic is positioned as a foundational stepping stone toward understanding **class interaction, encapsulation in distributed systems, and method-level data flow**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Remote Control of a Smart TV** > > Imagine a TV (the **object**) sitting in your living room. You hand the *remote control* to your friend. Your friend does not carry the TV in their hands — they only hold the *remote* that points to the TV. Whatever button they press, the TV responds because the remote is *linked* to it. If they change the channel or volume, the TV's actual state changes. > > In Java, when you "pass an object as a parameter," you are essentially handing over the *remote control* (the **reference**) to a method. The method operates on the *same TV* (the *same object in heap memory*) using that remote. ### 1.3 Why This Concept Matters in KTU | KTU Syllabus Keyword | Engineering Significance | |----------------------|--------------------------| | **Object Reference** | Memory address pointing to heap-allocated object | | **Formal Parameter** | Variable declared in method signature | | **Actual Parameter** | Real object reference passed during invocation | | **Pass-by-Value** | Java's universal rule — a copy of the reference is passed | | **Mutator Effect** | Changes to object state via reference are visible to the caller | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Rule (KTU Examiner's Favorite):** Java is **100% Pass-by-Value**. For primitives, the actual value is copied. For objects, the *reference value* (memory address) is copied. This subtle distinction is the most commonly tested point in Module 2. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mechanics of Passing Objects as Parameters When a method is invoked with an object as its argument, the following sequence occurs: - **Step 1 — Caller Allocates Object:** The calling code creates an object in the **heap memory** using the `new` keyword. A reference variable stores the memory address of that object. - **Step 2 — Reference is Copied:** During the method invocation, the JVM creates a **copy of the reference** and binds it to the formal parameter of the method. - **Step 3 — Method Body Operates on Heap Object:** The method uses the copied reference to access the same heap-allocated object. Any modification to *instance variables* of the object reflects globally. - **Step 4 — Reassignment Inside Method Does Not Affect Caller:** If the formal parameter is *reassigned* to a new object, the caller's original reference remains unchanged (because the original reference copy is unaffected by the local reassignment). ### 2.2 Three Canonical Scenarios Tested in KTU > [!NOTE] > **Scenario A — Modifying Object State (Mutator)** > > Method alters the instance variables of the passed object using setter logic. Changes are reflected in the caller's object. > [!NOTE] > **Scenario B — Reassigning the Reference Inside Method** > > Method assigns a new object to its formal parameter. The caller's reference is **NOT** affected. Original object remains untouched. > [!NOTE] > **Scenario C — Returning an Object** > > A method constructs a new object and returns its reference to the caller, enabling **object chaining** and fluent method designs. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formal Rule | KTU Memory Trick | |---|---------|-------------|------------------| | 1 | Primitive Parameter | `value` copied → local change invisible | "Numbers live in the box" | | 2 | Object Parameter | `reference` copied → same heap object | "Remote, not TV" | | 3 | State Modification | Visible to caller | "Pressing remote changes TV" | | 4 | Reference Reassignment | Invisible to caller | "Friend buys new TV, yours safe" | | 5 | Returning Object | Heap address returned | "Receipt of your new TV" | | 6 | `this` keyword | Refers to current calling object | "Self-address label" | | 7 | `final` parameter | Reference cannot be reassigned | "Glued remote" | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Enterprise Java (J2EE):** Service layer methods accept *DTO (Data Transfer Object)* instances to decouple layers. - **Android SDK:** `onCreate(Bundle savedInstanceState)` passes the state object as parameter. - **Database Connectivity (JDBC):** `PreparedStatement.setObject(int, Object)` accepts parameters as objects. - **Spring Framework:** Dependency injection passes entire beans (objects) as constructor parameters. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Trace — Object Parameter Passing (Mutator Case) Let us trace the canonical KTU exam problem: **a method receives an object and modifies its state.** Let the object instance be $O$ located at heap address $H_1$. The caller's reference is $R_c$ where $R_c = H_1$. $$ R_c \rightarrow H_1 \rightarrow O $$ **Step 1 — Method Invocation** The method signature is declared as: $$ \text{void updateObject}(\text{MyClass } p) $$ A new local reference $R_m$ is created, which is a **byte-for-byte copy** of $R_c$: $$ R_m = R_c = H_1 $$ **Step 2 — State Mutation Inside the Method** When the method executes `p.setValue(50)`, the JVM dereferences $R_m$ and reaches $H_1$, modifying the instance variable $v$ of object $O$. $$ O.v_{\text{old}} = 25 \quad\longrightarrow\quad O.v_{\text{new}} = 50 $$ **Step 3 — Verification After Method Return** After the method returns, the local reference $R_m$ is destroyed (popped from stack). However, the heap object at $H_1$ is unchanged in its location but reflects the new state. The caller's reference $R_c$ still points to $H_1$, so reading $R_c.v$ yields $50$. $$ R_c \rightarrow H_1 \rightarrow O.v = 50 \quad (\text{visible globally}) $$ ### 3.2 Analytical Trace — Reassignment Case (No Mutator Effect) Let us examine the case where the formal parameter is *reassigned* to a new object. **Step 1 — Initial State** $$ R_c \rightarrow H_1 \rightarrow O_1 $$ **Step 2 — Method Receives Reference Copy** $$ R_m = R_c = H_1 $$ **Step 3 — Reassignment Inside Method** The method executes `p = new MyClass(99)`. This allocates a new object $O_2$ at heap address $H_2$ and binds the local reference $R_m$ to $H_2$. $$ R_m = H_2 \rightarrow O_2 $$ **Step 4 — Caller's Reference Unaffected** The caller's reference $R_c$ still equals $H_1$, and $O_1$ is untouched. Only the local copy $R_m$ was redirected. $$ R_c \rightarrow H_1 \rightarrow O_1 \quad (\text{unchanged}) $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Trace — Returning an Object Consider a factory-style method: $$ \text{MyClass createObject}(\text{int } x) \rightarrow R_{\text{return}} $$ **Step 1 — Method Allocates Object** A new object $O_3$ is created at heap address $H_3$ with $O_3.x = x$. $$ O_3.x = x, \quad H_3 \rightarrow O_3 $$ **Step 2 — Reference Returned to Caller** The reference $H_3$ is copied into the caller's variable: $$ R_{\text{caller}} = H_3 \rightarrow O_3 $$ The heap object $O_3$ persists beyond the method's stack frame because at least one reference ($R_{\text{caller}}$) is alive. ### 3.4 Comprehensive Reference Behavior Matrix | Action Inside Method | Affects Caller's Object? | Affects Caller's Reference? | KTU Verdict | |----------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|-------------| | Modify instance variable via reference | **Yes** | No | Mutator — visible | | Call a method on the object | **Yes** | No | Mutator — visible | | Reassign parameter to new object | No | No | Safe — invisible | | Modify a primitive parameter | No | No | Isolated — invisible | | Modify a reference parameter's target via `final` reassignment | Compile error | Compile error | Blocked by `final` | | Return an object from method | N/A | N/A | New reference created in caller | ### 3.5 Worked-Out Java Program Trace Consider the following canonical KTU-style program: ```java class Box { int length, width; Box(int l, int w) { length = l; width = w; } int volume(Box b) { return b.length * b.width; } Box doubleBox(Box b) { Box temp = new Box(b.length * 2, b.width * 2); return temp; } } ``` **Trace for `volume(b2)`:** $$ \text{volume} = b.\text{length} \times b.\text{width} = 10 \times 5 = 50 $$ **Trace for `doubleBox(b1)`:** $$ b_{\text{new}}.\text{length} = 4 \times 2 = 8, \quad b_{\text{new}}.\text{width} = 3 \times 2 = 6 $$ The new `Box(8, 6)` reference is returned to the caller. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Memory Architecture — Object Parameter Passing (Mutator) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef heapStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef stackStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef methodStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 CallerStack["CALLER STACK FRAME
Reference R_c = H1"]:::stackStyle MethodStack["METHOD STACK FRAME
Formal Param p = H1 (copy)"]:::stackStyle HeapObj["HEAP MEMORY at H1
Object O
length = 10
width = 5"]:::heapStyle Mutator["p.setLength(50)
Mutator Operation"]:::methodStyle CallerStack -- "passes reference copy" --> MethodStack MethodStack -- "dereferences to H1" --> HeapObj MethodStack -- "executes" --> Mutator Mutator -- "modifies state at H1" --> HeapObj HeapObj -- "reflects new value
length = 50" --> CallerStack ``` ### 4.2 Memory Architecture — Reference Reassignment (No Effect) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef heapStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef stackStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef newHeapStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 CallerStack["CALLER
R_c = H1 (unchanged)"]:::stackStyle MethodStack["METHOD
p = H2 (reassigned)"]:::stackStyle OrigObj["ORIGINAL OBJECT at H1
length = 10
width = 5
(untouched)"]:::heapStyle NewObj["NEW OBJECT at H2
length = 99
width = 0
(only local visibility)"]:::newHeapStyle CallerStack -- "still points to" --> OrigObj MethodStack -- "now points to" --> NewObj ``` ### 4.3 Functional Flow — Object Parameter Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef defStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef actStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 A["STEP A
Object created in heap
via new keyword"]:::defStyle B["STEP B
Reference stored in
caller's variable"]:::actStyle C["STEP C
Method invoked
reference copied to formal param"]:::actStyle D["STEP D
Method operates on
same heap object"]:::actStyle E["STEP E
Modifications persist
after return"]:::outStyle F["STEP F
Local reference destroyed
heap object remains if referenced"]:::outStyle A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Method Interaction with Object Parameter ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef clsStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mthStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef objStyle fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 BoxClass["Box CLASS
int length, width
int volume(Box b)
Box doubleBox(Box b)"]:::clsStyle Method1["Method: volume
Input: Box reference
Output: int"]:::mthStyle Method2["Method: doubleBox
Input: Box reference
Output: Box reference"]:::mthStyle ObjInst["Object Instance b1
length=4, width=3
heap address H1"]:::objStyle BoxClass --> Method1 BoxClass --> Method2 Method1 -- "receives reference" --> ObjInst Method2 -- "receives + returns reference" --> ObjInst ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Explain with an example how objects are passed as parameters to methods in Java. State the underlying mechanism of parameter passing.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** In Java, when an object is passed as a parameter, the method receives a **copy of the object's reference**, not the object itself. The reference is a memory address pointing to the object located in the heap. **Example:** ```java class Student { String name; Student(String n) { name = n; } void update(Student s) { s.name = "Updated"; } } public class Test { public static void main(String[] args) { Student st = new Student("Original"); new Student("Dummy").update(st); System.out.println(st.name); // Output: Updated } } ``` Java uses **pass-by-value** universally. For object parameters, the *reference value* is copied. Hence, modifications to the object's instance variables through the formal parameter are reflected in the caller's object. However, reassigning the formal parameter to a new object does not affect the caller's original reference. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for code/example, 1 mark for pass-by-value rule)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between passing primitive type parameters and object type parameters in Java. Give one example each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Primitive Parameter | Object Parameter | |--------|--------------------|--------------------| | What is copied? | Actual value (literal) | Reference value (memory address) | | Effect of modification | Invisible to caller | Visible to caller (state changes) | | Memory location | Stack only | Stack + Heap | | Reassignment effect | No effect on caller | No effect on caller's reference | **Primitive Example:** ```java void change(int x) { x = 50; } int a = 10; change(a); // a remains 10 ``` **Object Example:** ```java void change(Box b) { b.length = 50; } Box b1 = new Box(10); change(b1); // b1.length becomes 50 ``` **(1 mark for table, 1 mark each for two examples)** --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme]* **A.** *(a)* Design a Java class `Rectangle` with attributes `length` and `width`. Write a method `enlarge(Rectangle r, int factor)` that doubles the dimensions of the passed rectangle object. Demonstrate the working of this method by passing an object from `main()` and printing the dimensions before and after enlargement. *(7 marks)* *(b)* Explain what happens when the formal parameter `r` is reassigned to a new `Rectangle` object inside the `enlarge` method. Will the original object in `main()` be affected? Justify your answer with a memory diagram description. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Code (4 marks) + Explanation (3 marks):** ```java class Rectangle { int length, width; Rectangle(int l, int w) { length = l; width = w; } void enlarge(Rectangle r, int factor) { r.length = r.length * factor; r.width = r.width * factor; } void display() { System.out.println("Length = " + length + ", Width = " + width); } } public class TestRectangle { public static void main(String[] args) { Rectangle rect = new Rectangle(4, 5); System.out.print("Before: "); rect.display(); rect.enlarge(rect, 2); System.out.print("After : "); rect.display(); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Before: Length = 4, Width = 5 After : Length = 8, Width = 10 ``` **Explanation:** The method `enlarge` receives a copy of the reference `rect`. It dereferences the copy to access the same heap object and multiplies its dimensions. Since the same heap object is modified, the changes are visible in `main()`. *(7 marks total)* --- **Part (b) — Reassignment Analysis (7 marks):** ```java void reassignDemo(Rectangle r) { r = new Rectangle(100, 200); // local reassignment } public static void main(String[] args) { Rectangle rect = new Rectangle(4, 5); reassignDemo(rect); rect.display(); // Output: Length = 4, Width = 5 } ``` **Memory Behavior:** - `rect` (in `main`'s stack) points to heap address $H_1$. - When `reassignDemo` is called, the formal parameter `r` receives a copy of the reference $H_1$. - The statement `r = new Rectangle(100, 200)` allocates a new object at heap address $H_2$ and makes the *local* `r` point to $H_2$. - The caller's `rect` still points to $H_1$ — the original object is **NOT affected**. **Justification:** Java is strictly pass-by-value. The reference variable is copied; the underlying object is not. Reassigning the local copy changes only the method's local view, not the caller's view. **(2 marks for code demonstration, 3 marks for memory-level explanation, 2 marks for pass-by-value justification)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]* **B.** *(a)* Write a Java program with a class `Circle` having a `radius` attribute. Implement two methods: (i) `compareCircles(Circle c1, Circle c2)` that returns the larger circle object, and (ii) `createCircle(double r)` that returns a newly created circle object. Demonstrate both methods from `main()`. *(7 marks)* *(b)* Discuss the significance of returning objects from methods with respect to the KTU Module 2 syllabus. Explain the role of the heap memory and garbage collector in this context. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Code (5 marks) + Output Discussion (2 marks):** ```java class Circle { double radius; Circle(double r) { radius = r; } Circle compareCircles(Circle c1, Circle c2) { if (c1.radius >= c2.radius) return c1; else return c2; } Circle createCircle(double r) { return new Circle(r); } void display() { System.out.println("Radius = " + radius); } } public class TestCircle { public static void main(String[] args) { Circle c1 = new Circle(5.0); Circle c2 = new Circle(7.5); Circle c3 = new Circle(0.0); Circle larger = c3.compareCircles(c1, c2); System.out.print("Larger circle: "); larger.display(); Circle newC = c3.createCircle(10.0); System.out.print("Newly created: "); newC.display(); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Larger circle: Radius = 7.5 Newly created: Radius = 10.0 ``` --- **Part (b) — Theoretical Discussion (7 marks):** **Significance of Returning Objects:** - Enables **factory method design pattern** where methods construct and return objects. - Allows **method chaining** and **fluent interfaces** in advanced Java (e.g., `StringBuilder.append()`). - Permits **conditional object selection** (as in `compareCircles`). - Decouples object creation from the calling code, enhancing modularity. **Heap Memory and Garbage Collector:** - Objects are always allocated in the **heap memory** of the JVM. - When a method returns an object reference, the heap object persists as long as at least one live reference points to it. - When all references are dropped, the object becomes eligible for **Garbage Collection (GC)**. The JVM's GC daemon reclaims the heap memory, preventing memory leaks. - In `createCircle`, the returned reference keeps the new `Circle` alive in heap; if the caller discards the reference, the object becomes GC-eligible. **(3 marks for significance, 2 marks for heap behavior, 2 marks for garbage collector explanation)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Mistake:** Writing "Java is pass-by-reference for objects." **Reality:** Java is 100% pass-by-value — only the *reference value* is copied. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this. > 2. **Mistake:** Forgetting to print the object state *after* method call, leading to incomplete output. Always show before/after. > 3. **Mistake:** Confusing reassignment of parameter with mutation of object. The former has NO external effect; the latter DOES. > 4. **Mistake:** Not mentioning the **heap** explicitly when explaining object storage. KTU examiners reward heap-stack distinction with bonus marks. > 5. **Mistake:** Writing return type as `void` for object-returning methods. Use the class name as the return type (e.g., `Circle compareCircles(...)`). --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Using objects as parameters means passing object references to methods; Java passes a *copy of the reference* (pass-by-value of reference). - **Memory Layout:** Objects live in the **heap**; references live in the **stack** frame of the calling method. - **Mutator Effect:** Modifying an object's instance variable through the formal parameter **IS** visible to the caller. - **Reassignment Effect:** Reassigning the formal parameter to a new object **IS NOT** visible to the caller. - **Returning Objects:** Methods can return references to newly created objects; the heap object persists as long as a reference exists. - **Garbage Collection:** Objects with no live references become eligible for GC. - **`this` Keyword:** Inside a method, `this` refers to the calling object — useful for disambiguating parameters from instance variables. - **`final` Parameter:** A `final` reference parameter cannot be reassigned but its object's state can still be mutated. - **KTU Hot Spots:** Pass-by-value rule, mutator vs reassignment distinction, returning objects, heap-stack memory model. - **Code Style Tip:** Always include `display()` or `toString()` in your class for cleaner output in exam answers.

topics.title: Returning Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Returning Objects # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Returning Objects** is a fundamental Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) mechanism in which a **method** (or **function**) is declared with a **user-defined class type** as its return type, allowing it to send back a fully constructed **object** (i.e., a reference / handle to an instance residing in the **heap memory**) to the **caller** upon method completion. In languages like **Java**, **C++**, and **C#**, the return statement is not limited to primitive data types (`int`, `float`, `char`, etc.). Any method can be designed to return: 1. A **reference** to a newly created object (allocated dynamically). 2. A **reference** to an existing object (e.g., one of its private fields, or a parameter that was passed in). 3. A **reference** to a *copy* of an object (created via copy constructor in C++, or via the `clone()` mechanism in Java). The receiving variable in the calling code is then bound to this returned object reference, allowing the caller to invoke instance methods on the returned object via the dot operator (`.`). ### 📐 Syntax Template ```java accessModifier ClassName methodName(parameterList) { // method body return objectReference; // returns an object of ClassName } ``` ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vending Machine" Model 🍫 Imagine a **vending machine** in a college canteen: - You insert **coins** (input parameters). - You press a **button** (method invocation: `vendingMachine.dispense(coin, buttonCode)`). - The machine **processes internally** and then **delivers a product** (a chocolate bar, a cold drink) into the collection tray. - The dispensed product is itself a **complex, packaged thing** — not a single coin, but a complete object with properties (brand, flavor, weight) and behaviors (it can be opened, consumed, etc.). > [!NOTE] > The vending machine is the **method**. The dispensed chocolate is the **returned object**. Just as the chocolate is fully formed *before* you pick it up, the returned object is fully constructed *before* the `return` statement hands over its reference to the caller. ### Why is this concept so important in OOP? | Reason | Explanation | |---|---| | **Encapsulation of Construction** | Complex objects can be built inside a method (e.g., a *factory method*) and handed to the caller as a finished product, hiding all internal construction steps. | | **Method Chaining** | Returning `this` (the current object) enables fluent APIs like `builder.setName("KTU").setAge(21).build()`. | | **Object Recycling / Pooling** | Pre-built object pools can lend out reusable objects by returning references, avoiding expensive re-creation. | | **Returning Aggregate Results** | A method can package multiple values into a single object (e.g., a `Result` object) and return that, instead of using arrays or out-parameters. | > [!TIP] > In the KTU 2024 syllabus, "Returning Objects" typically appears in **Module 2** alongside topics like constructors, `this` keyword, and parameterized methods. Mastering it is essential for understanding **Factory Design Patterns** and **Builder Design Patterns** which are often tested in Part B questions. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Forms of "Returning Objects" In OOP, when we say a method "returns an object," we are really returning a **reference (address)**, not the actual object data block. The object itself continues to live in the **heap**; the return statement simply transfers the *handle* to it. There are three distinct forms: ### 🔹 Form 1 — Returning a Newly Constructed Object (Most Common) A method creates a brand new object using the `new` keyword and returns its reference. ```java class Box { double width, height, depth; Box(double w, double h, double d) { width = w; height = h; depth = d; } // Factory method that constructs and returns a Box static Box createBox(double side) { return new Box(side, side, side); // returns a new cube } } ``` **Mechanism:** 1. The method's local stack frame is allocated. 2. `new Box(...)` allocates memory in the **heap** and the constructor initializes it. 3. The reference to that heap object is placed in the **return value slot** of the stack frame. 4. After the method exits, the local reference variable inside the method is destroyed, but the heap object survives because the caller now holds a reference. ### 🔹 Form 2 — Returning `this` (Method Chaining / Fluent API) A non-static method can return the current object using `return this;`. This is the foundation of **builder patterns** and **cascading method calls**. ```java class Student { String name; int age; Student setName(String n) { this.name = n; return this; } Student setAge(int a) { this.age = a; return this; } } // Caller: Student s = new Student().setName("Arjun").setAge(21); ``` ### 🔹 Form 3 — Returning a Passed-In Object (Reference Re-routing) A method can return one of its parameters or one of its instance fields, effectively exposing an internal reference to the outside world. ```java class Warehouse { private Item bestItem = new Item("Premium", 999); Item getBestItem() { // returns reference to internal field return bestItem; } } ``` > [!WARNING] > Returning internal field references **breaks encapsulation** if the returned object is mutable. Any change made by the caller is reflected in the original object. This is a classic KTU exam pitfall. ## 2.2 Memory Model — What Actually Happens? When a method returns an object, the **JVM / runtime** performs the following sequence: | Step | Stack (Method's Frame) | Heap (Object Pool) | Notes | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | Method invoked; parameters loaded | — | Stack frame pushed | | 2 | Local `new` reference variable created | Object allocated and initialized | `new` triggers heap allocation | | 3 | Constructor executes, fields populated | Fields stored in heap | Object becomes reachable | | 4 | `return` statement copies reference into return slot | Object address is now in two places | The local variable & the return slot both reference it | | 5 | Stack frame popped; local variable destroyed | Object **survives** (now referenced by caller) | Garbage Collector will *not* reclaim it | | 6 | Caller's variable assigned the returned reference | — | Caller now "owns" the handle | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The local variable inside the returning method is a *short-lived handle*. The *object itself* lives in the heap and is unaffected by the method's stack-frame destruction — a process known as the **"stack-discard / heap-survive"** invariant. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet | Aspect | Returning a Primitive | Returning an Object | |:---|:---|:---| | What is actually returned? | The **value** itself (a copy) | A **reference / address** (a copy of the handle) | | Default value of return type | `0`, `0.0`, `false`, `'\u0000'` | `null` | | Memory location of returned data | Stack / register | Heap | | Effect of modifying the return value at caller | No effect on the original | Mutates the **original** heap object (because reference is shared) | | Use of `static` modifier | Allowed | Allowed (e.g., static factory methods) | | Can be used in `return` of constructor? | N/A | Allowed via constructor chaining (`this(...)`) | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications - **GUI Frameworks (JavaFX, Swing, Android):** Factory methods like `Button.create()` return ready-to-use UI component objects. - **Database Connectivity (JDBC):** `DriverManager.getConnection(url)` returns a `Connection` object. - **Collections Framework:** `Collections.unmodifiableList(list)` returns a *wrapped* (defensive-copy) list object. - **Parsers & Compilers:** Lexers return `Token` objects; parsers return `ASTNode` trees. - **Game Development:** Object pools lend out `Bullet`, `Enemy`, or `Particle` objects, then take them back — implemented via "checkout / checkin" methods returning references. # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Trace ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Returning a Newly Constructed `Box` Object > **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Design a class `Box` with dimensions `width`, `height`, `depth`. Write a method `enlarge(double factor)` that **returns a new Box** whose dimensions are scaled by `factor`. Demonstrate its usage in `main()`. ### Complete Java Code (Traceable) ```java class Box { double width, height, depth; Box(double w, double h, double d) { width = w; height = h; depth = d; System.out.println(" [Constructor] Box created: " + w + " x " + h + " x " + d); } // Method that RETURNS a Box object Box enlarge(double factor) { System.out.println(" [enlarge] Called with factor = " + factor); Box scaled = new Box(width * factor, height * factor, depth * factor); System.out.println(" [enlarge] Returning new Box reference: " + scaled); return scaled; } void printBox() { System.out.println(" Box dimensions -> " + width + " x " + height + " x " + depth); } public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Step 1: Creating original Box b1"); Box b1 = new Box(2.0, 3.0, 4.0); System.out.println("Step 2: Calling b1.enlarge(2.0)"); Box b2 = b1.enlarge(2.0); System.out.println("Step 3: Calling b2.enlarge(3.0)"); Box b3 = b2.enlarge(3.0); System.out.println("Step 4: Final Box dimensions"); b1.printBox(); b2.printBox(); b3.printBox(); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Analytical Trace | Caller Line | What Happens in `enlarge()` | What is Returned | Net State After Call | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | `Box b1 = new Box(2,3,4)` | Constructor runs on heap object H1 | — | `b1` → H1 (2×3×4) | | `Box b2 = b1.enlarge(2.0)` | New heap object H2 (4×6×8) created inside method | Reference to H2 | `b2` → H2; H1 unchanged | | `Box b3 = b2.enlarge(3.0)` | New heap object H3 (12×18×24) created inside method | Reference to H3 | `b3` → H3; H1, H2 unchanged | ### Memory-State Diagram (Symbolic) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Stack (main)} &: \quad \text{b1} \to H_1, \;\; \text{b2} \to H_2, \;\; \text{b3} \to H_3 \\[4pt] \text{Heap} &: \quad H_1 = \{2, 3, 4\},\;\; H_2 = \{4, 6, 8\},\;\; H_3 = \{12, 18, 24\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Sample Output ``` Step 1: Creating original Box b1 [Constructor] Box created: 2.0 x 3.0 x 4.0 Step 2: Calling b1.enlarge(2.0) [enlarge] Called with factor = 2.0 [Constructor] Box created: 4.0 x 6.0 x 8.0 [enlarge] Returning new Box reference: Box@15db9742 Step 3: Calling b2.enlarge(3.0) [enlarge] Called with factor = 3.0 [Constructor] Box created: 12.0 x 18.0 x 24.0 [enlarge] Returning new Box reference: Box@6d06d69c Step 4: Final Box dimensions Box dimensions -> 2.0 x 3.0 x 4.0 Box dimensions -> 4.0 x 6.0 x 8.0 Box dimensions -> 12.0 x 18.0 x 24.0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Important Observation:** None of the original objects (H1, H2) were modified. Each call to `enlarge()` produced a **brand new heap object** and handed back its reference. This is the safest form of object returning. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Returning `this` for Method Chaining (Fluent Builder) > **Problem Statement:** Build a `Course` class that supports fluent configuration. Each setter returns `this`, allowing chained calls. ### Complete Java Code ```java class Course { String code; String title; int credits; Course setCode(String c) { this.code = c; return this; } Course setTitle(String t) { this.title = t; return this; } Course setCredits(int cr) { this.credits = cr; return this; } void display() { System.out.println("Course: " + code + " | " + title + " | Credits: " + credits); } public static void main(String[] args) { Course c = new Course() .setCode("OEERT613") .setTitle("Object Oriented Concepts") .setCredits(4); c.display(); } } ``` ### Analytical Trace | Step | Expression Evaluated | Object State After Step | Returned | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | `new Course()` | `{code=null, title=null, credits=0}` | reference `this` | | 2 | `.setCode("OEERT613")` | `{code="OEERT613", ...}` | `this` (same object) | | 3 | `.setTitle("Object Oriented Concepts")` | `{code="OEERT613", title="...", ...}` | `this` (same object) | | 4 | `.setCredits(4)` | `{code=..., title=..., credits=4}` | `this` (same object) | | 5 | `c.display()` | unchanged | prints details | ### Mathematical Representation of the Chain $$ \begin{aligned} c &= \texttt{new Course}() \\ &\quad .\texttt{setCode}(x_1) \\ &\quad .\texttt{setTitle}(x_2) \\ &\quad .\texttt{setCredits}(x_3) \end{aligned} $$ Since each method returns the same heap reference (call it $H_C$): $$ c = H_C, \quad \text{and} \quad H_C.\text{code} = x_1,\;\; H_C.\text{title} = x_2,\;\; H_C.\text{credits} = x_3 $$ ### Output ``` Course: OEERT613 | Object Oriented Concepts | Credits: 4 ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Can constructors return objects?", the precise answer is: *Java constructors **do not** have a return type, so they cannot formally "return" an object via the `return` keyword. However, the JVM **implicitly** returns `this` to the caller when a constructor finishes, which is why `new Course(...)` produces a reference that can be assigned to a variable.* --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Returning a Composite / Aggregate Object > **Problem Statement:** Create a `Statistics` class with a method that accepts an array and returns a `Result` object containing the sum, average, min, and max — all packaged into a single returned object. ### Complete Java Code ```java class Result { double sum, avg, min, max; Result(double s, double a, double mn, double mx) { sum = s; avg = a; min = mn; max = mx; } void display() { System.out.println("Sum = " + sum + ", Avg = " + avg + ", Min = " + min + ", Max = " + max); } } class Statistics { static Result compute(int[] arr) { if (arr.length == 0) return null; // guard double sum = 0, min = arr[0], max = arr[0]; for (int x : arr) { sum += x; if (x < min) min = x; if (x > max) max = x; } double avg = sum / arr.length; return new Result(sum, avg, min, max); // returns composite object } public static void main(String[] args) { int[] data = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}; Result r = Statistics.compute(data); r.display(); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Trace $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Input array} &= [10, 20, 30, 40, 50] \\[4pt] \text{Sum} &= \sum_{i=0}^{4} \text{arr}[i] = 10+20+30+40+50 = 150 \\[4pt] \text{Average} &= \frac{\text{Sum}}{N} = \frac{150}{5} = 30.0 \\[4pt] \text{Min} &= \min(\text{arr}) = 10 \\[4pt] \text{Max} &= \max(\text{arr}) = 50 \end{aligned} $$ ### Output ``` Sum = 150.0, Avg = 30.0, Min = 10.0, Max = 50.0 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why return an object instead of multiple values?** Because Java methods can return **only one** value. By bundling related values into a `Result` class, we return **one object** that conveniently carries many pieces of data — a foundational pattern in API design. # 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Lifecycle of a Returned Object The following Mermaid flowchart traces what happens *step by step* when a method is called and returns an object to its caller. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Caller invokes method
b2 = b1.enlarge 2.0"] --> B["Stack frame pushed
for enlarge method"] B --> C["Allocate new Box object
in heap memory H2"] C --> D["Constructor runs
initializes width height depth"] D --> E["Assign local ref scaled to H2
inside enlarge method"] E --> F["Execute return scaled
copy ref into return slot"] F --> G["Stack frame of enlarge popped
local scaled ref destroyed"] G --> H["Caller variable b2 receives ref
b2 now points to H2"] H --> I["Heap object H2 survives
reachable through b2"] styleA fill:#e3f2fd style I fill:#c8e6c9 style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2 style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00 style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00 style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00 style E fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00 style F fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b style G fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b style H fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Object Handoff Between Caller and Method ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Caller as Main Method participant Callee as enlarge Method participant Heap as Heap Memory Caller->>Callee: invoke enlarge 2.0 activate Callee Callee->>Heap: allocate new Box H2 Heap-->>Callee: return address of H2 Callee->>Callee: populate fields of H2 Callee->>Caller: return reference to H2 deactivate Callee Note over Caller,Heap: Caller now holds handle to H2
Heap object H2 remains alive Caller->>Heap: invoke printBox on H2 Heap-->>Caller: print dimensions 4x6x8 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reference vs Value Return ```mermaid block-beta columns 3 block:prim["PRIMITIVE RETURN"] P1["int x = 5"] P2["method returns 5"] P3["Caller gets COPY of 5"] end block:objref["OBJECT RETURN"] O1["Box b on heap H"] O2["method returns reference"] O3["Caller gets COPY of ADDRESS"] end block:effect["SIDE EFFECT OF MUTATION"] E1["int y = x: y is independent"] E2["Box c = b: c points to SAME H"] E3["c.width = 99 mutates original"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The left block shows that returning a primitive is a **value copy** — completely independent. The middle block shows that returning an object is a **reference copy** — the caller and the original both point to the *same* heap object. The right block illustrates the **danger**: mutating the returned reference mutates the original. This is why defensive copying is sometimes needed in professional Java/C++ code. ## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Object Reachability After Return ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Allocated : new keyword executed Allocated --> Constructed : constructor initializes fields Constructed --> Returned : return statement copies reference Returned --> Reachable : caller stores reference in variable Reachable --> Unreachable : caller drops reference null or out of scope Unreachable --> GarbageCollected : JVM Garbage Collector runs GarbageCollected --> [*] ``` # 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **What do you mean by "returning objects" in Java? How is it different from returning primitive data types?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Returning objects* refers to a method whose return type is a user-defined class; it hands back a **reference (address)** to an object that lives on the heap, instead of a raw value. The key difference is: 1. **Primitives** (`int`, `float`, etc.) — the *value itself* is copied into the caller's variable. 2. **Objects** — only the *reference* (memory address) is copied; both the original and the caller now point to the **same** heap object. 3. Modifying the returned object via the caller's reference will also mutate the original, whereas modifying a returned primitive has no such side effect. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for primitive distinction, 1 for side-effect distinction)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]** **Explain the role of the `return this` statement in object-oriented programming with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The statement `return this;` is used inside a non-static method to return a reference to the **current object** on which the method was invoked. This enables **method chaining** (also called the *fluent interface* pattern). For example: ```java class Point { int x, y; Point setX(int x) { this.x = x; return this; } Point setY(int y) { this.y = y; return this; } } // Caller: Point p = new Point().setX(5).setY(10); ``` Here each setter returns `this`, allowing multiple method calls in a single statement. *(3 marks: 1 for explanation, 1 for syntax, 1 for chained example)* --- ## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Design a Java class `Time` with three integer fields: `hours`, `minutes`, `seconds`. Provide a constructor to initialize them and a method `add(Time t)` that **returns a new `Time` object** representing the sum of the current time and the passed time. Demonstrate with a `main` method. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram what happens in memory (stack and heap) when a method returns an object. Discuss why the original object is not destroyed even after the returning method's stack frame is popped. #### Model Solution **(a) Complete Java Code (7 marks):** ```java class Time { int hours, minutes, seconds; Time(int h, int m, int s) { hours = h; minutes = m; seconds = s; } Time add(Time t) { int totalSec = this.seconds + t.seconds; int totalMin = this.minutes + t.minutes + totalSec / 60; int totalHr = this.hours + t.hours + totalMin / 60; return new Time(totalHr % 24, totalMin % 60, totalSec % 60); } void display() { System.out.printf("Time: %02d:%02d:%02d%n", hours, minutes, seconds); } public static void main(String[] args) { Time t1 = new Time(10, 45, 50);
