Time t2 = new Time(2, 20, 30); Time t3 = t1.add(t2); // t1.add returns a NEW Time object t1.display(); t2.display(); t3.display(); } } ``` **Step-by-step explanation:** - `t1 = (10:45:50)`, `t2 = (2:20:30)`. - `t1.add(t2)` computes `totalSec = 50+30 = 80`, `totalMin = 45+20 + 80/60 = 65+1 = 66`, `totalHr = 10+2 + 66/60 = 12+1 = 13`. - Result: `13:06:20` — returned as a new `Time` object. - Output: ``` Time: 10:45:50 Time: 02:20:30 Time: 13:06:20 ``` *(7 marks: 2 for class + constructor, 3 for `add` logic, 2 for `main` and output)* **(b) Memory Model Explanation (7 marks):** | Step | Stack (main + add) | Heap | Remarks | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | `main` frame created | `t1` → H1, `t2` → H2 | Two Time objects allocated | | 2 | `add` frame pushed, parameter `t` = ref to H2 | — | Method begins | | 3 | `new Time(...)` allocates H3, constructor runs | H3 holds `13:06:20` | Local `result` ref → H3 | | 4 | `return result;` | — | H3's address copied to return slot | | 5 | `add` frame popped; `result` ref destroyed | H3 **survives** (referenced by `t3`) | Caller assigns to `t3` | | 6 | `t3` now points to H3 in heap | — | Original `t1`, `t2` unchanged | > **Why does the object not get destroyed?** > Java uses a **Garbage Collector** that reclaims memory **only** when an object becomes **unreachable** — i.e., no live reference points to it. The `return` statement copies the reference into the caller's variable, so the object is *still reachable* and therefore *not eligible* for garbage collection. The destruction of the *stack frame* only removes the *local* handle, not the heap object itself. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for step trace, 2 for GC explanation)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 3, Module 2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Java class `Matrix` that internally stores a 2×2 integer array. Implement: - A constructor that initializes the matrix. - A method `transpose()` that **returns a new `Matrix` object** representing the transpose. - A method `add(Matrix m)` that **returns a new `Matrix` object** representing the sum of two matrices. - A `display()` method and a `main` to demonstrate both. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the three forms of returning objects: (i) returning a newly constructed object, (ii) returning `this`, (iii) returning a parameter or instance field. For each, provide one real-world use case. #### Model Solution **(a) Complete Java Code (7 marks):** ```java class Matrix { int[][] a = new int[2][2]; Matrix(int x00, int x01, int x10, int x11) { a[0][0] = x00; a[0][1] = x01; a[1][0] = x10; a[1][1] = x11; } Matrix transpose() { return new Matrix(a[0][0], a[1][0], a[0][1], a[1][1]); // returns new Matrix } Matrix add(Matrix m) { return new Matrix(a[0][0] + m.a[0][0], a[0][1] + m.a[0][1], a[1][0] + m.a[1][0], a[1][1] + m.a[1][1]); } void display() { System.out.println("| " + a[0][0] + " " + a[0][1] + " |"); System.out.println("| " + a[1][0] + " " + a[1][1] + " |"); } public static void main(String[] args) { Matrix m1 = new Matrix(1, 2, 3, 4); Matrix m2 = new Matrix(5, 6, 7, 8); System.out.println("Matrix 1:"); m1.display(); System.out.println("Matrix 2:"); m2.display(); System.out.println("Transpose of Matrix 1:"); m1.transpose().display(); System.out.println("Sum of Matrix 1 and Matrix 2:"); m1.add(m2).display(); } } ``` **Sample Output:** ``` Matrix 1: | 1 2 | | 3 4 | Matrix 2: | 5 6 | | 7 8 | Transpose of Matrix 1: | 1 3 | | 2 4 | Sum of Matrix 1 and Matrix 2: | 6 8 | | 10 12 | ``` *(7 marks: 1 for fields + constructor, 2 for transpose, 2 for add, 2 for main + output)* **(b) Comparison (7 marks):** | Form | Description | Use Case | Safety | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | (i) **Return newly constructed** | Method allocates a fresh object via `new` and returns its reference | Immutable wrappers, mathematical operations (e.g., `BigInteger.add`) | **Very safe** — caller cannot affect the original | | (ii) **Return `this`** | Method returns a reference to the *current* (calling) object | Fluent builders (`StringBuilder.append`), cascading setters | **Safe** — same object, but caller already had access | | (iii) **Return parameter / field** | Method exposes an internal reference to the outside | Getters in `Optional.orElse(value)`, pool leasing | **Risky** — caller can mutate internals; defensive copy recommended | > *(7 marks: 2 marks per form (1 for description, 1 for example), 1 mark for overall safety comparison)* # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Returning Objects** is a fundamental Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) mechanism in which a **method** (or **function**) is declared with a **user-defined class type** as its return type, allowing it to send back a fully constructed **object** (i.e., a reference / handle to an instance residing in the **heap memory**) to the **caller** upon method completion. In languages like **Java**, **C++**, and **C#**, the return statement is not limited to primitive data types (`int`, `float`, `char`, etc.). Any method can be designed to return: 1. A **reference** to a newly created object (allocated dynamically). 2. A **reference** to an existing object (e.g., one of its private fields, or a parameter that was passed in). 3. A **reference** to a *copy* of an object (created via copy constructor in C++, or via the `clone()` mechanism in Java). The receiving variable in the calling code is then bound to this returned object reference, allowing the caller to invoke instance methods on the returned object via the dot operator (`.`). ### 📐 Syntax Template ```java accessModifier ClassName methodName(parameterList) { // method body return objectReference; // returns an object of ClassName } ``` ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vending Machine" Model 🍫 Imagine a **vending machine** in a college canteen: - You insert **coins** (input parameters). - You press a **button** (method invocation: `vendingMachine.dispense(coin, buttonCode)`). - The machine **processes internally** and then **delivers a product** (a chocolate bar, a cold drink) into the collection tray. - The dispensed product is itself a **complex, packaged thing** — not a single coin, but a complete object with properties (brand, flavor, weight) and behaviors (it can be opened, consumed, etc.). > [!NOTE] > The vending machine is the **method**. The dispensed chocolate is the **returned object**. Just as the chocolate is fully formed *before* you pick it up, the returned object is fully constructed *before* the `return` statement hands over its reference to the caller. ### Why is this concept so important in OOP? | Reason | Explanation | |---|---| | **Encapsulation of Construction** | Complex objects can be built inside a method (e.g., a *factory method*) and handed to the caller as a finished product, hiding all internal construction steps. | | **Method Chaining** | Returning `this` (the current object) enables fluent APIs like `builder.setName("KTU").setAge(21).build()`. | | **Object Recycling / Pooling** | Pre-built object pools can lend out reusable objects by returning references, avoiding expensive re-creation. | | **Returning Aggregate Results** | A method can package multiple values into a single object (e.g., a `Result` object) and return that, instead of using arrays or out-parameters. | > [!TIP] > In the KTU 2024 syllabus, "Returning Objects" typically appears in **Module 2** alongside topics like constructors, `this` keyword, and parameterized methods. Mastering it is essential for understanding **Factory Design Patterns** and **Builder Design Patterns** which are often tested in Part B questions. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Forms of "Returning Objects" In OOP, when we say a method "returns an object," we are really returning a **reference (address)**, not the actual object data block. The object itself continues to live in the **heap**; the return statement simply transfers the *handle* to it. There are three distinct forms: ### 🔹 Form 1 — Returning a Newly Constructed Object (Most Common) A method creates a brand new object using the `new` keyword and returns its reference. ```java class Box { double width, height, depth; Box(double w, double h, double d) { width = w; height = h; depth = d; } // Factory method that constructs and returns a Box static Box createBox(double side) { return new Box(side, side, side); // returns a new cube } } ``` **Mechanism:** 1. The method's local stack frame is allocated. 2. `new Box(...)` allocates memory in the **heap** and the constructor initializes it. 3. The reference to that heap object is placed in the **return value slot** of the stack frame. 4. After the method exits, the local reference variable inside the method is destroyed, but the heap object survives because the caller now holds a reference. ### 🔹 Form 2 — Returning `this` (Method Chaining / Fluent API) A non-static method can return the current object using `return this;`. This is the foundation of **builder patterns** and **cascading method calls**. ```java class Student { String name; int age; Student setName(String n) { this.name = n; return this; } Student setAge(int a) { this.age = a; return this; } } // Caller: Student s = new Student().setName("Arjun").setAge(21); ``` ### 🔹 Form 3 — Returning a Passed-In Object (Reference Re-routing) A method can return one of its parameters or one of its instance fields, effectively exposing an internal reference to the outside world. ```java class Warehouse { private Item bestItem = new Item("Premium", 999); Item getBestItem() { // returns reference to internal field return bestItem; } } ``` > [!WARNING] > Returning internal field references **breaks encapsulation** if the returned object is mutable. Any change made by the caller is reflected in the original object. This is a classic KTU exam pitfall. ## 2.2 Memory Model — What Actually Happens? When a method returns an object, the **JVM / runtime** performs the following sequence: | Step | Stack (Method's Frame) | Heap (Object Pool) | Notes | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | Method invoked; parameters loaded | — | Stack frame pushed | | 2 | Local `new` reference variable created | Object allocated and initialized | `new` triggers heap allocation | | 3 | Constructor executes, fields populated | Fields stored in heap | Object becomes reachable | | 4 | `return` statement copies reference into return slot | Object address is now in two places | The local variable & the return slot both reference it | | 5 | Stack frame popped; local variable destroyed | Object **survives** (now referenced by caller) | Garbage Collector will *not* reclaim it | | 6 | Caller's variable assigned the returned reference | — | Caller now "owns" the handle | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The local variable inside the returning method is a *short-lived handle*. The *object itself* lives in the heap and is unaffected by the method's stack-frame destruction — a process known as the **"stack-discard / heap-survive"** invariant. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet | Aspect | Returning a Primitive | Returning an Object | |:---|:---|:---| | What is actually returned? | The **value** itself (a copy) | A **reference / address** (a copy of the handle) | | Default value of return type | `0`, `0.0`, `false`, `'\u0000'` | `null` | | Memory location of returned data | Stack / register | Heap | | Effect of modifying the return value at caller | No effect on the original | Mutates the **original** heap object (because reference is shared) | | Use of `static` modifier | Allowed | Allowed (e.g., static factory methods) | | Can be used in `return` of constructor? | N/A | Allowed via constructor chaining (`this(...)`) | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications - **GUI Frameworks (JavaFX, Swing, Android):** Factory methods like `Button.create()` return ready-to-use UI component objects. - **Database Connectivity (JDBC):** `DriverManager.getConnection(url)` returns a `Connection` object. - **Collections Framework:** `Collections.unmodifiableList(list)` returns a *wrapped* (defensive-copy) list object. - **Parsers & Compilers:** Lexers return `Token` objects; parsers return `ASTNode` trees. - **Game Development:** Object pools lend out `Bullet`, `Enemy`, or `Particle` objects, then take them back — implemented via "checkout / checkin" methods returning references. # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Trace ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Returning a Newly Constructed `Box` Object > **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Design a class `Box` with dimensions `width`, `height`, `depth`. Write a method `enlarge(double factor)` that **returns a new Box** whose dimensions are scaled by `factor`. Demonstrate its usage in `main()`. ### Complete Java Code (Traceable) ```java class Box { double width, height, depth; Box(double w, double h, double d) { width = w; height = h; depth = d; System.out.println(" [Constructor] Box created: " + w + " x " + h + " x " + d); } // Method that RETURNS a Box object Box enlarge(double factor) { System.out.println(" [enlarge] Called with factor = " + factor); Box scaled = new Box(width * factor, height * factor, depth * factor); System.out.println(" [enlarge] Returning new Box reference: " + scaled); return scaled; } void printBox() { System.out.println(" Box dimensions -> " + width + " x " + height + " x " + depth); } public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Step 1: Creating original Box b1"); Box b1 = new Box(2.0, 3.0, 4.0); System.out.println("Step 2: Calling b1.enlarge(2.0)"); Box b2 = b1.enlarge(2.0); System.out.println("Step 3: Calling b2.enlarge(3.0)"); Box b3 = b2.enlarge(3.0); System.out.println("Step 4: Final Box dimensions"); b1.printBox(); b2.printBox(); b3.printBox(); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Analytical Trace | Caller Line | What Happens in `enlarge()` | What is Returned | Net State After Call | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | `Box b1 = new Box(2,3,4)` | Constructor runs on heap object H1 | — | `b1` → H1 (2×3×4) | | `Box b2 = b1.enlarge(2.0)` | New heap object H2 (4×6×8) created inside method | Reference to H2 | `b2` → H2; H1 unchanged | | `Box b3 = b2.enlarge(3.0)` | New heap object H3 (12×18×24) created inside method | Reference to H3 | `b3` → H3; H1, H2 unchanged | ### Memory-State Diagram (Symbolic) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Stack (main)} &: \quad \text{b1} \to H_1, \;\; \text{b2} \to H_2, \;\; \text{b3} \to H_3 \\[4pt] \text{Heap} &: \quad H_1 = \{2, 3, 4\},\;\; H_2 = \{4, 6, 8\},\;\; H_3 = \{12, 18, 24\} \end{aligned} $$ ### Sample Output ``` Step 1: Creating original Box b1 [Constructor] Box created: 2.0 x 3.0 x 4.0 Step 2: Calling b1.enlarge(2.0) [enlarge] Called with factor = 2.0 [Constructor] Box created: 4.0 x 6.0 x 8.0 [enlarge] Returning new Box reference: Box@15db9742 Step 3: Calling b2.enlarge(3.0) [enlarge] Called with factor = 3.0 [Constructor] Box created: 12.0 x 18.0 x 24.0 [enlarge] Returning new Box reference: Box@6d06d69c Step 4: Final Box dimensions Box dimensions -> 2.0 x 3.0 x 4.0 Box dimensions -> 4.0 x 6.0 x 8.0 Box dimensions -> 12.0 x 18.0 x 24.0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Important Observation:** None of the original objects (H1, H2) were modified. Each call to `enlarge()` produced a **brand new heap object** and handed back its reference. This is the safest form of object returning. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Returning `this` for Method Chaining (Fluent Builder) > **Problem Statement:** Build a `Course` class that supports fluent configuration. Each setter returns `this`, allowing chained calls. ### Complete Java Code ```java class Course { String code; String title; int credits; Course setCode(String c) { this.code = c; return this; } Course setTitle(String t) { this.title = t; return this; } Course setCredits(int cr) { this.credits = cr; return this; } void display() { System.out.println("Course: " + code + " | " + title + " | Credits: " + credits); } public static void main(String[] args) { Course c = new Course() .setCode("OEERT613") .setTitle("Object Oriented Concepts") .setCredits(4); c.display(); } } ``` ### Analytical Trace | Step | Expression Evaluated | Object State After Step | Returned | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | `new Course()` | `{code=null, title=null, credits=0}` | reference `this` | | 2 | `.setCode("OEERT613")` | `{code="OEERT613", ...}` | `this` (same object) | | 3 | `.setTitle("Object Oriented Concepts")` | `{code="OEERT613", title="...", ...}` | `this` (same object) | | 4 | `.setCredits(4)` | `{code=..., title=..., credits=4}` | `this` (same object) | | 5 | `c.display()` | unchanged | prints details | ### Mathematical Representation of the Chain $$ \begin{aligned} c &= \texttt{new Course}() \\ &\quad .\texttt{setCode}(x_1) \\ &\quad .\texttt{setTitle}(x_2) \\ &\quad .\texttt{setCredits}(x_3) \end{aligned} $$ Since each method returns the same heap reference (call it $H_C$): $$ c = H_C, \quad \text{and} \quad H_C.\text{code} = x_1,\;\; H_C.\text{title} = x_2,\;\; H_C.\text{credits} = x_3 $$ ### Output ``` Course: OEERT613 | Object Oriented Concepts | Credits: 4 ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Can constructors return objects?", the precise answer is: *Java constructors **do not** have a return type, so they cannot formally "return" an object via the `return` keyword. However, the JVM **implicitly** returns `this` to the caller when a constructor finishes, which is why `new Course(...)` produces a reference that can be assigned to a variable.* --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Returning a Composite / Aggregate Object > **Problem Statement:** Create a `Statistics` class with a method that accepts an array and returns a `Result` object containing the sum, average, min, and max — all packaged into a single returned object. ### Complete Java Code ```java class Result { double sum, avg, min, max; Result(double s, double a, double mn, double mx) { sum = s; avg = a; min = mn; max = mx; } void display() { System.out.println("Sum = " + sum + ", Avg = " + avg + ", Min = " + min + ", Max = " + max); } } class Statistics { static Result compute(int[] arr) { if (arr.length == 0) return null; // guard double sum = 0, min = arr[0], max = arr[0]; for (int x : arr) { sum += x; if (x < min) min = x; if (x > max) max = x; } double avg = sum / arr.length; return new Result(sum, avg, min, max); // returns composite object } public static void main(String[] args) { int[] data = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}; Result r = Statistics.compute(data); r.display(); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Trace $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Input array} &= [10, 20, 30, 40, 50] \\[4pt] \text{Sum} &= \sum_{i=0}^{4} \text{arr}[i] = 10+20+30+40+50 = 150 \\[4pt] \text{Average} &= \frac{\text{Sum}}{N} = \frac{150}{5} = 30.0 \\[4pt] \text{Min} &= \min(\text{arr}) = 10 \\[4pt] \text{Max} &= \max(\text{arr}) = 50 \end{aligned} $$ ### Output ``` Sum = 150.0, Avg = 30.0, Min = 10.0, Max = 50.0 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why return an object instead of multiple values?** Because Java methods can return **only one** value. By bundling related values into a `Result` class, we return **one object** that conveniently carries many pieces of data — a foundational pattern in API design. # 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Lifecycle of a Returned Object The following Mermaid flowchart traces what happens *step by step* when a method is called and returns an object to its caller. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Caller invokes method
b2 = b1.enlarge 2.0"] --> B["Stack frame pushed
for enlarge method"] B --> C["Allocate new Box object
in heap memory H2"] C --> D["Constructor runs
initializes width height depth"] D --> E["Assign local ref scaled to H2
inside enlarge method"] E --> F["Execute return scaled
copy ref into return slot"] F --> G["Stack frame of enlarge popped
local scaled ref destroyed"] G --> H["Caller variable b2 receives ref
b2 now points to H2"] H --> I["Heap object H2 survives
reachable through b2"] style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2 style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00 style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00 style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00 style E fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00 style F fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b style G fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b style H fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Object Handoff Between Caller and Method ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Caller as Main Method participant Callee as enlarge Method participant Heap as Heap Memory Caller->>Callee: invoke enlarge 2.0 activate Callee Callee->>Heap: allocate new Box H2 Heap-->>Callee: return address of H2 Callee->>Callee: populate fields of H2 Callee->>Caller: return reference to H2 deactivate Callee Note over Caller,Heap: Caller now holds handle to H2
Heap object H2 remains alive Caller->>Heap: invoke printBox on H2 Heap-->>Caller: print dimensions 4x6x8 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reference vs Value Return ```mermaid block-beta columns 3 block:prim["PRIMITIVE RETURN"] P1["int x = 5"] P2["method returns 5"] P3["Caller gets COPY of 5"] end block:objref["OBJECT RETURN"] O1["Box b on heap H"] O2["method returns reference"] O3["Caller gets COPY of ADDRESS"] end block:effect["SIDE EFFECT OF MUTATION"] E1["int y = x: y is independent"] E2["Box c = b: c points to SAME H"] E3["c.width = 99 mutates original"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The left block shows that returning a primitive is a **value copy** — completely independent. The middle block shows that returning an object is a **reference copy** — the caller and the original both point to the *same* heap object. The right block illustrates the **danger**: mutating the returned reference mutates the original. This is why defensive copying is sometimes needed in professional Java/C++ code. ## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Object Reachability After Return ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Allocated : new keyword executed Allocated --> Constructed : constructor initializes fields Constructed --> Returned : return statement copies reference Returned --> Reachable : caller stores reference in variable Reachable --> Unreachable : caller drops reference null or out of scope Unreachable --> GarbageCollected : JVM Garbage Collector runs GarbageCollected --> [*] ``` # 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **What do you mean by "returning objects" in Java? How is it different from returning primitive data types?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Returning objects* refers to a method whose return type is a user-defined class; it hands back a **reference (address)** to an object that lives on the heap, instead of a raw value. The key differences are: 1. **Primitives** (`int`, `float`, etc.) — the *value itself* is copied into the caller's variable. 2. **Objects** — only the *reference* (memory address) is copied; both the original and the caller now point to the **same** heap object. 3. Modifying the returned object via the caller's reference will also mutate the original, whereas modifying a returned primitive has no such side effect. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for primitive distinction, 1 for side-effect distinction)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]** **Explain the role of the `return this` statement in object-oriented programming with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The statement `return this;` is used inside a non-static method to return a reference to the **current object** on which the method was invoked. This enables **method chaining** (also called the *fluent interface* pattern). For example: ```java class Point { int x, y; Point setX(int x) { this.x = x; return this; } Point setY(int y) { this.y = y; return this; } } // Caller: Point p = new Point().setX(5).setY(10); ``` Here each setter returns `this`, allowing multiple method calls in a single statement. *(3 marks: 1 for explanation, 1 for syntax, 1 for chained example)* --- ## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Design a Java class `Time` with three integer fields: `hours`, `minutes`, `seconds`. Provide a constructor to initialize them and a method `add(Time t)` that **returns a new `Time` object** representing the sum of the current time and the passed time. Demonstrate with a `main` method. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram what happens in memory (stack and heap) when a method returns an object. Discuss why the original object is not destroyed even after the returning method's stack frame is popped. #### Model Solution **(a) Complete Java Code (7 marks):** ```java class Time { int hours, minutes, seconds; Time(int h, int m, int s) { hours = h; minutes = m; seconds = s; } Time add(Time t) { int totalSec = this.seconds + t.seconds; int totalMin = this.minutes + t.minutes + totalSec / 60; int totalHr = this.hours + t.hours + totalMin / 60; return new Time(totalHr % 24, totalMin % 60, totalSec % 60); } void display() { System.out.printf("Time: %02d:%02d:%02d%n", hours, minutes, seconds); } public static void main(String[] args) { Time t1 = new Time(10, 45, 50); Time t2 = new Time(2, 20, 30); Time t3 = t1.add(t2); // t1.add returns a NEW Time object t1.display(); t2.display(); t3.display(); } } ``` **Step-by-step explanation:** - `t1 = (10:45:50)`, `t2 = (2:20:30)`. - `t1.add(t2)` computes `totalSec = 50+30 = 80`, `totalMin = 45+20 + 80/60 = 65+1 = 66`, `totalHr = 10+2 + 66/60 = 12+1 = 13`. - Result: `13:06:20` — returned as a new `Time` object. - Output: ``` Time: 10:45:50 Time: 02:20:30 Time: 13:06:20 ``` *(7 marks: 2 for class + constructor, 3 for `add` logic, 2 for `main` and output)* **(b) Memory Model Explanation (7 marks):** | Step | Stack (main + add) | Heap | Remarks | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | `main` frame created | `t1` → H1, `t2` → H2 | Two Time objects allocated | | 2 | `add` frame pushed, parameter `t` = ref to H2 | — | Method begins | | 3 | `new Time(...)` allocates H3, constructor runs | H3 holds `13:06:20` | Local `result` ref → H3 | | 4 | `return result;` | — | H3's address copied to return slot | | 5 | `add` frame popped; `result` ref destroyed | H3 **survives** (referenced by `t3`) | Caller assigns to `t3` | | 6 | `t3` now points to H3 in heap | — | Original `t1`, `t2` unchanged | > **Why does the object not get destroyed?** > Java uses a **Garbage Collector** that reclaims memory **only** when an object becomes **unreachable** — i.e., no live reference points to it. The `return` statement copies the reference into the caller's variable, so the object is *still reachable* and therefore *not eligible* for garbage collection. The destruction of the *stack frame* only removes the *local* handle, not the heap object itself. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for step trace, 2 for GC explanation)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 3, Module 2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Java class `Matrix` that internally stores a 2×2 integer array. Implement: - A constructor that initializes the matrix. - A method `transpose()` that **returns a new `Matrix` object** representing the transpose. - A method `add(Matrix m)` that **returns a new `Matrix` object** representing the sum of two matrices. - A `display()` method and a `main` to demonstrate both. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the three forms of returning objects: (i) returning a newly constructed object, (ii) returning `this`, (iii) returning a parameter or instance field. For each, provide one real-world use case. #### Model Solution **(a) Complete Java Code (7 marks):** ```java class Matrix { int[][] a = new int[2][2]; Matrix(int x00, int x01, int x10, int x11) { a[0][0] = x00; a[0][1] = x01; a[1][0] = x10; a[1][1] = x11; } Matrix transpose() { return new Matrix(a[0][0], a[1][0], a[0][1], a[1][1]); // returns new Matrix } Matrix add(Matrix m) { return new Matrix(a[0][0] + m.a[0][0], a[0][1] + m.a[0][1], a[1][0] + m.a[1][0], a[1][1] + m.a[1][1]); } void display() { System.out.println("| " + a[0][0] + " " + a[0][1] + " |"); System.out.println("| " + a[1][0] + " " + a[1][1] + " |"); } public static void main(String[] args) { Matrix m1 = new Matrix(1, 2, 3, 4); Matrix m2 = new Matrix(5, 6, 7, 8); System.out.println("Matrix 1:"); m1.display(); System.out.println("Matrix 2:"); m2.display(); System.out.println("Transpose of Matrix 1:"); m1.transpose().display(); System.out.println("Sum of Matrix 1 and Matrix 2:"); m1.add(m2).display(); } } ``` **Sample Output:** ``` Matrix 1: | 1 2 | | 3 4 | Matrix 2: | 5 6 | | 7 8 | Transpose of Matrix 1: | 1 3 | | 2 4 | Sum of Matrix 1 and Matrix 2: | 6 8 | | 10 12 | ``` *(7 marks: 1 for fields + constructor, 2 for transpose, 2 for add, 2 for main + output)* **(b) Comparison (7 marks):** | Form | Description | Use Case | Safety | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | (i) **Return newly constructed** | Method allocates a fresh object via `new` and returns its reference | Immutable wrappers, mathematical operations (e.g., `BigInteger.add`) | **Very safe** — caller cannot affect the original | | (ii) **Return `this`** | Method returns a reference to the *current* (calling) object | Fluent builders (`StringBuilder.append`), cascading setters | **Safe** — same object, but caller already had access | | (iii) **Return parameter / field** | Method exposes an internal reference to the outside | Getters in `Optional.orElse(value)`, pool leasing | **Risky** — caller can mutate internals; defensive copy recommended | > *(7 marks: 2 marks per form (1 for description, 1 for example), 1 mark for overall safety comparison)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "returning object" with "returning value"** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if a student writes that the *object itself* is returned (it is the *reference* that is returned, the object stays in heap). > 2. **Forgetting to mention the heap** — A method returning an object implies heap allocation. If the student says "the object is created on the stack," they lose marks. > 3. **Ignoring garbage collection** — When asked why the object survives method exit, the answer *must* mention the JVM Garbage Collector and reachability. A vague "because Java manages memory" answer is insufficient for full marks. > 4. **Confusing constructor return with method return** — Constructors do not have a declared return type; the implicit `return this` happens at the JVM level. Writing `return this;` inside a constructor is a **compilation error** in Java. > 5. **Method-chaining pitfall** — In a fluent chain like `new A().setX().setY()`, if any method in the chain forgets to `return this`, the chain breaks with a compile error. Examiners look for this in code-tracing questions. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A method's return type can be **any class type**, not just a primitive. - ✅ What is actually returned is a **reference (handle/address)**, never the object body itself. - ✅ The returned object **lives in the heap**; the local handle inside the returning method is destroyed when the stack frame pops. - ✅ **Three forms**: returning a new object, returning `this`, returning a parameter/field. - ✅ `return this;` enables **method chaining** and the **Builder / Fluent API** design pattern. - ✅ Modifying an object via the returned reference **mutates the original** — this is the aliasing problem. - ✅ Constructors in Java **implicitly** return `this`; you cannot write `return this;` explicitly inside a constructor. - ✅ Returning a *parameter* or *field* can break **encapsulation** if the object is mutable — use **defensive copying**. - ✅ The **Garbage Collector** keeps the object alive as long as at least one reference is reachable. - ✅ A method can return only **one value**; bundling values into a result-object is the standard workaround. - ✅ Always match the **declared return type** with the type of the object being returned — mismatches cause compile-time errors. - ✅ Be cautious with `null` returns — handle them in the caller to avoid `NullPointerException`. - ✅ Returning objects is foundational to **Factory**, **Builder**, **Prototype**, and **Singleton-with-clone** design patterns.

topics.title: Static Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Static Members # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), **static members** are class-level entities (variables, methods, blocks, and nested classes) that belong to the **class itself** rather than to any individual object (instance) of the class. In Java, they are declared using the keyword `static`. There is exactly **one shared copy** of a static member across all objects of the class, regardless of how many instances are created. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Static members form the bridge between **procedural** and **object-oriented** paradigms. A `static` member exists *before* any object of the class is constructed and survives *after* all objects are garbage-collected. The JVM stores them in the **Method Area (Class Area)** of the runtime data area, while instance variables live in the **Heap** along with their objects. The four flavours of static members recognised by the KTU 2024 OOP module are: | # | Static Member | Declared With | Belongs To | |---|---------------|---------------|------------| | 1 | Static Variable (Class Variable) | `static dataType name;` | Class | | 2 | Static Method (Class Method) | `static returnType method(){}` | Class | | 3 | Static Block (Static Initializer) | `static { ... }` | Class | | 4 | Static Nested Class | `static class Inner {}` | Outer Class | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Notice Board" Imagine a **college classroom**: - Each **student** is an *object* (instance) of the class `Student`. - Each student has **personal belongings** kept in their own *bag* → these are **instance variables** (every student has their own copy). - Now, on the **front wall of the classroom**, there is a single **Notice Board**. *Every* student in that class reads the *same* notice board. If a new notice is pinned, *all* students see it instantly. If a student erases something, *all* students lose it. - That Notice Board is the **static variable**. - The **class teacher** who manages the notice board without needing any particular student to be present is the **static method**. - The **rules** painted on the wall during the inauguration (executed once when the classroom is first built) form the **static block**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Static = **shared, single-copy, class-owned**. Instance = **unique, multi-copy, object-owned**. ## 1.3 Why KTU Emphasises This Topic Static members control **memory efficiency**, **global access patterns**, **utility design** (e.g., `Math.sqrt()`), and the **execution order** of a Java program. Several KTU questions on Module 2 test *exactly* these nuances — output prediction, access rules, and the lifetime of static entities. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Static Members The OOP design philosophy normally says *"encapsulate data inside objects."* But certain data and behaviour are **inherently collective** and do not depend on the state of any single object. Forcing such data to be stored per-object would: 1. **Waste memory** — duplicating the same constant for thousands of objects. 2. **Break consistency** — different copies could drift out of sync. 3. **Violate the "no global variables" OOP rule** — unless you give them a *class* home, not a *file* home. Hence, `static` was introduced: a **controlled, scoped global** that lives within a class boundary. ## 2.2 Classification & Rules of Static Members ### 2.2.1 Static Variables (Class Variables) - Declared with the keyword `static` inside a class but **outside** any method, constructor, or block. - A single copy is created when the class is **loaded** by the ClassLoader (typically on first active use). - Stored in the **Method Area / Class Area** of JVM memory. - Can be accessed using: `ClassName.variableName`, `objectName.variableName` (compiler warning, but legal). - Default values apply just like instance variables (`int → 0`, `boolean → false`, references → `null`). ### 2.2.2 Static Methods - Belong to the class; can be invoked **without creating any object** (`ClassName.methodName()`). - **Restrictions** (high-yield KTU point): > [!WARNING] > Inside a `static` method, you **cannot** use: > 1. `this` keyword (no current object) > 2. `super` keyword (no current object to look upward from) > 3. Instance variables directly > 4. Instance methods directly > > You **can** use: other static variables, other static methods, and you *can* create new objects and use their instance members. - A `static` method can be **overloaded** but **cannot be overridden** (it can be *hidden* — a frequent KTU trick question). ### 2.2.3 Static Blocks (Static Initializers) - Written as `static { /* code */ }` inside a class. - **Executed exactly once**, when the class is loaded — *before* `main()` and *before* any object is constructed. - If multiple static blocks exist, they execute **top-to-bottom** in source order. - Used to initialise complex static variables or load native libraries (e.g., `System.loadLibrary`). ### 2.2.4 Static Nested Class - Declared as `static class Inner` inside an outer class. - Unlike non-static inner classes, a static nested class **does not need an outer-class object** to be instantiated. - Can only access **static** members of the outer class directly. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorise the following table verbatim — these are the *exact rules* the KTU examiner tests. | Concept | Formula / Rule | Lifetime / Location | |---------|----------------|---------------------| | Number of static variable copies | **1 per class** (not per object) | Method Area / Class Area | | Number of instance variable copies | **1 per object** | Heap (inside object) | | Static variable access | `ClassName.var` (recommended) | Class-load → program-end | | Static method access | `ClassName.method()` | Class-load → program-end | | `this` / `super` in static method | **Not allowed** | N/A | | Static block execution | Once, at class-loading, top-to-bottom | Before `main` & before constructors | | Static method overriding | **Not allowed** (only *hiding*) | Compile-time polymorphism only | | `main()` method signature | `public static void main(String[] args)` | Entry point — must be static so JVM can call without object | | Static nested class needs outer object? | **No** | Independent lifetime | | `final static` variable | **Compile-time constant** | Inlined at compile time | | Order of execution (full program) | **Static block → main() → Instance block → Constructor** | Per class load & per object creation | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Use of Static | |--------|---------------| | **Utility Libraries** | `java.lang.Math.sqrt()`, `Integer.parseInt()` — all static so no object overhead | | **Constants** | `public static final double PI = 3.14159;` — single source of truth | | **Counters / Auto-IDs** | `private static int nextId = 1000;` — every object gets a unique running serial | | **Singleton Pattern** | `private static final INSTANCE = new Singleton();` — controlled global access | | **Entry Point** | `public static void main()` — JVM invokes class, not object | | **Caching / Registry** | `static Map registry = new HashMap<>();` | | **Native Library Loading** | `static { System.loadLibrary("opencv"); }` | # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs > Below are three fully-traced examples. Every line of output is justified with a *why*. ## 3.1 Walkthrough A — Shared Static Variable (Counter) ```java class Employee { int id; // instance static int count = 0; // shared Employee() { id = ++count; // pre-increment System.out.println("Employee created with id: " + id); } } ``` ### Trace **Step 1 — Class Load:** JVM loads `Employee`. The static variable `count` is allocated in the **Method Area** and initialised to its default value, then reassigned to the explicit value `0`. **Step 2 — `new Employee()` (1st object):** `id = ++count` → `count` becomes `1`, `id = 1`. Output line 1 printed. **Step 3 — `new Employee()` (2nd object):** `count` is **shared** → becomes `2`, `id = 2`. Output line 2 printed. **Step 4 — `new Employee()` (3rd object):** `count` becomes `3`, `id = 3`. Output line 3 printed. ### Final Output ```text Employee created with id: 1 Employee created with id: 2 Employee created with id: 3 ``` ### Math-style summary of state $$ \text{count}_{\text{shared}} = N \quad \text{after the } N\text{-th object} $$ $$ \text{id}_i = i, \quad \forall i \in \lbrace 1, 2, \dots, N \rbrace $$ > [!NOTE] > If `count` had been declared *without* `static`, every object would start from `1` and IDs would clash — defeating the purpose. ## 3.2 Walkthrough B — Execution Order (Static Block vs Instance Block vs Constructor) ```java class Demo { static { System.out.println("1 - Static Block"); } { System.out.println("3 - Instance Initializer Block"); } Demo() { System.out.println("4 - Constructor"); } } public class Test { static { System.out.println("2 - Test class Static Block"); } public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("--- main starts ---"); new Demo(); new Demo(); System.out.println("--- main ends ---"); } } ``` ### Trace Table | Order | Event | Output | |-------|-------|--------| | 1 | `Test` class loaded (triggered by `main` entry) | `1 - Static Block` | | 2 | `Test`'s own static block | `2 - Test class Static Block` | | 3 | `main()` begins | `--- main starts ---` | | 4 | `new Demo()` → `Demo` class loaded (first use) → static block | `1 - Static Block` *(appears again)* | | 5 | Instance block of `Demo` runs | `3 - Instance Initializer Block` | | 6 | `Demo` constructor runs | `4 - Constructor` | | 7 | Second `new Demo()` → class already loaded, no static block | *(skipped)* | | 8 | Instance block again | `3 - Instance Initializer Block` | | 9 | Constructor again | `4 - Constructor` | | 10 | `main` ends | `--- main ends ---` | ### Final Console Output ```text 1 - Static Block 2 - Test class Static Block --- main starts --- 1 - Static Block 3 - Instance Initializer Block 4 - Constructor 3 - Instance Initializer Block 4 - Constructor --- main ends --- ``` ### Key Rule Derivation $$ \text{Execution Order} \;=\; \text{Static (per class, once)} \;\succ\; \text{Instance (per object)} \;\succ\; \text{Constructor (per object)} $$ ## 3.3 Walkthrough C — Static Method Restrictions (Compilation Error Demo) ```java class Restrict { int instanceVar = 10; static int staticVar = 20; static void staticMethod() { System.out.println(staticVar); // OK // System.out.println(instanceVar); // ❌ Compile-time error // System.out.println(this.instanceVar);// ❌ 'this' not allowed // super.toString(); // ❌ 'super' not allowed Restrict r = new Restrict(); System.out.println(r.instanceVar); // OK via object } } ``` ### Why Each Line Fails or Succeeds - `staticVar` → belongs to the class, hence accessible from a static method. **Allowed.** - `instanceVar` → belongs to *an* object; static method does not know *which* object. **Compile error.** - `this` → refers to the current invoking object. In a static method, *no* current object is guaranteed. **Compile error.** - `super` → similarly requires a current object context. **Compile error.** - `r.instanceVar` → an explicit object reference is provided, so the JVM can resolve the instance member. **Allowed.** ### Compiler Error Message (verbatim from `javac`) ```text error: non-static variable instanceVar cannot be referenced from a static context ``` ## 3.4 Walkthrough D — Static Method *Hiding* vs *Overriding* ```java class Parent { static void show() { System.out.println("Parent static show"); } } class Child extends Parent { static void show() { System.out.println("Child static show"); } // hides, not overrides } ``` ### Compile-time binding (important) $$ \text{Which method runs} \;=\; f(\text{Reference Type},\; \text{not Object Type}) $$ If the call is `Parent p = new Child(); p.show();` → **"Parent static show"** is printed. This is the **opposite** of instance-method polymorphism, which is a classic KTU trick question. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 JVM Memory Layout — Static vs Instance ```mermaid graph TD subgraph JVM["JVM Runtime Data Areas"] subgraph MA["Method Area (Class Area)"] CL1["Class: Employee
static int count = 3
static methods, static blocks
class metadata"] end subgraph HP["Heap"] OBJ1["obj1
id = 1"] OBJ2["obj2
id = 2"] OBJ3["obj3
id = 3"] end subgraph ST["Stack"] F1["main() frame
references: e1, e2, e3"] end end OBJ1 -.points to.-> CL1 OBJ2 -.points to.-> CL1 OBJ3 -.points to.-> CL1 F1 --> OBJ1 F1 --> OBJ2 F1 --> OBJ3 style CL1 fill:#ffe7a0,stroke:#b58900 style OBJ1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style OBJ2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style OBJ3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style F1 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#1d6f1d ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The single yellow box in the *Method Area* is the **one-and-only** static `count`. All three blue objects in the *Heap* point to / share it. The green *Stack* frame holds only the local references, not the data itself. ## 4.2 Execution Order Flow (Class Load → main → Object Creation) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["JVM starts"] --> B["Load class containing main()"] B --> C["Execute static blocks of that class"] C --> D["Invoke main(String[] args)"] D --> E{"new SomeClass()?"} E -- "Yes, first time" --> F["Load SomeClass into Method Area"] F --> G["Execute static blocks of SomeClass"] G --> H["Allocate object in Heap"] H --> I["Execute instance initializer blocks"] I --> J["Execute constructor"] J --> K["Object ready"] E -- "Yes, class already loaded" --> H K --> D D --> L["main() returns → JVM exits"] style A fill:#f9d6d5 style L fill:#d5f9d6 style C fill:#fff3b0 style G fill:#fff3b0 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Yellow nodes are *static* (run **once per class**). The diamond checks whether the class has been loaded before. If yes, the static-block node is **bypassed**, which is why the second `new Demo()` in Walkthrough B did not re-print `"1 - Static Block"`. ## 4.3 Decision Map — "Can I use X inside a static method?" ```mermaid flowchart TD Q["Inside a static method,
can I use ___?"] Q --> A{"Is it another static
variable or method?"} A -- "Yes" --> A1["ALLOWED"] A -- "No" --> B{"Is it an instance
variable/method?"} B -- "Yes, but you have
an object reference 'r'" --> B1["ALLOWED via r.x"] B -- "Yes, accessed directly
(no object)" --> B2["COMPILE ERROR"] Q --> C{"Is it 'this' or 'super'?"} C -- "Yes" --> C1["COMPILE ERROR"] style A1 fill:#c8e6c9 style B1 fill:#c8e6c9 style B2 fill:#ffcdd2 style C1 fill:#ffcdd2 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Why is the `main()` method in Java declared as `static`?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `main()` method is declared `static` so that the **JVM can invoke it without creating an object** of the class. When the JVM starts program execution, no objects of the user's class exist yet. Since a static method belongs to the class (not to any object), the JVM can directly call `ClassName.main()` using the class loaded into the Method Area. If `main` were non-static, the JVM would have no way to construct an object to call it on, leading to a chicken-and-egg problem. Additionally, the signature must be `public static void main(String[] args)` exactly for the JVM to recognise it as the entry point. **(3 marks — full credit: 1 for "no object needed", 1 for "JVM entry point", 1 for exact signature mention.)** ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between static variables and instance variables.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Static Variable | Instance Variable | |--------|-----------------|-------------------| | Declaration | `static int x;` | `int x;` | | Copies | **One per class** | **One per object** | | Memory | Method / Class Area | Heap (inside object) | | Lifetime | Class load → program end | Object creation → garbage collection | | Access | `ClassName.x` (preferred) | `objectRef.x` | | Default value | Same as instance | Same | A static variable is **shared** across all objects; an instance variable is **unique** to each object. **(Full 3 marks for tabular comparison with at least 4 distinct points.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Q3 (A). `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]` **(a)** Explain static variables and static methods in Java with suitable examples. State any **four restrictions** on static methods. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program using a `static` variable to count the number of objects created for a class `Book`. Display the count after every object creation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks **Static Variable (3 marks):** A static variable is declared with the `static` keyword inside a class but outside any method. It is also called a *class variable*. Only **one copy** exists per class, allocated in the **Method Area** at class-loading time, and is shared by all objects. It can be accessed without an object using `ClassName.variableName`. ```java class College { static String collegeName = "KTU"; // single shared copy String studentName; // per-object } ``` **Static Method (3 marks):** A static method is declared with `static`. It is invoked using `ClassName.methodName()`. The most famous static method is `public static void main(String[] args)`. ```java class MathUtil { static int square(int n) { return n * n; } } // Call: MathUtil.square(5); // no object needed ``` **Four Restrictions on Static Methods (1 mark for the list):** 1. `this` keyword **cannot** be used. 2. `super` keyword **cannot** be used. 3. **Instance variables** cannot be accessed directly. 4. **Instance methods** cannot be called directly (must use an object reference). 5. *(Bonus — 5th rule)* They cannot be overridden, only **hidden**. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks ```java class Book { private static int count = 0; // shared counter [1 mark] private String title; Book(String title) { this.title = title; count++; // increment on every creation [2 marks] System.out.println("Book created: " + title + " | Total books so far: " + count); } public static int getCount() { // accessor [1 mark] return count; } public static void main(String[] args) { Book b1 = new Book("Java Basics"); Book b2 = new Book("OOP Concepts"); Book b3 = new Book("Data Structures"); System.out.println("Final count via getCount(): " + Book.getCount()); // class-level access [2 marks] } } ``` **Expected Output (1 mark):** ```text Book created: Java Basics | Total books so far: 1 Book created: OOP Concepts | Total books so far: 2 Book created: Data Structures| Total books so far: 3 Final count via getCount(): 3 ``` **Explanation (full marks if above is shown):** The variable `count` is declared `static`, so it is allocated **once** in the Method Area. Each constructor invocation increments the *same* variable, giving a running total. Access via `Book.getCount()` in `main()` demonstrates class-level access without needing an object. --- ### Q3 (B). `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 – Alternative Choice]` **(a)** What is a static block in Java? Explain its purpose and the rules governing its execution. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Predict and justify the output of the following Java program. Show the **exact order** in which each statement is printed. **(7 marks)** ```java class A { static { System.out.println("A-static"); } A() { System.out.println("A-ctor"); } } class B extends A { static { System.out.println("B-static"); } B() { System.out.println("B-ctor"); } } public class Q { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("main-start"); new B(); new B(); System.out.println("main-end"); } } ``` #### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks **Definition (2 marks):** A *static block* (also called a *static initializer*) is a block of code prefixed with the `static` keyword, written inside a class but outside any method or constructor. Its syntax is `static { /* statements */ }`. It is executed **exactly once** by the JVM when the class is loaded into memory — before any object of the class is constructed and before any static method (including `main`) of that class is invoked. **Purpose (3 marks):** 1. To initialise **complex static variables** that cannot be done in a single expression (e.g., populating a `static Map` from a file). 2. To **load native libraries** using `System.loadLibrary("name")` exactly once. 3. To perform **one-time setup** for a class — logging, registering drivers (`Class.forName("com.mysql.jdbc.Driver")`), or seeding a `Random` object used by all instances. 4. To enforce a *strict, deterministic* order of class-level initialisation. **Rules (2 marks):** - A class can have **multiple** static blocks; they execute **top-to-bottom** in source order. - Static blocks run **only when the class is actively referenced** for the first time (lazy class-loading). - They run **before** instance initializer blocks and constructors. - They **cannot** throw checked exceptions without being wrapped or declared. - They **cannot** use `this` or `super`. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks **Step-by-step Trace:** 1. JVM invokes `main` → loads class `Q` (no static block) → prints **"main-start"**. 2. `new B()` is encountered → JVM checks: is `B` loaded? **No** → loads `B`. 3. Before executing `B`'s body, JVM loads `B`'s superclass `A`. 4. **`A`'s static block** runs first → prints **"A-static"**. 5. **`B`'s static block** runs next → prints **"B-static"**. 6. Constructor of `B` is called. Before its body, `super()` chain is resolved: `A`'s constructor runs → prints **"A-ctor"**. 7. `B`'s constructor body runs → prints **"B-ctor"**. 8. Second `new B()`: both `A` and `B` are **already loaded** → static blocks **skipped**. Only constructors run. 9. **`A-ctor`** then **`B-ctor`** are printed. 10. **`main-end`** is printed. **Final Output (full 7 marks):** ```text main-start A-static B-static A-ctor B-ctor A-ctor B-ctor main-end ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently (a) forget that **parent's static block runs before child's**, (b) mistakenly print the second `new B()`'s static blocks again, and (c) confuse the order of *static* vs *constructor* in the inheritance chain. The correct mental model is: **Statics cascade from root to leaf, constructors cascade from root to leaf, but statics run only once per class.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision) - [x] **`static` = class-level**, not object-level. - [x] Static variable → **one copy** per class, stored in **Method Area**. - [x] Instance variable → **one copy per object**, stored in **Heap**. - [x] Access static members via `ClassName.member` (preferred) or `objectRef.member` (legal but discouraged). - [x] `this` and `super` are **forbidden** inside static methods. - [x] Instance variables and instance methods **cannot** be accessed directly from a static method — use an object reference. - [x] Static block runs **once** at class-loading, **top-to-bottom** if multiple, **before** instance initialisers and constructors. - [x] Execution order: **Static → Instance-block → Constructor**, repeated per object. - [x] Static methods are **not overridden**, they are **hidden** (compile-time binding). - [x] `static` nested class does **not** need an outer-class instance. - [x] `main()` is `static` so the JVM can call it without instantiating the class. - [x] `public static final` (in caps by convention) → true **compile-time constant**. - [x] Common use-cases: counters, constants, utility/helper classes, Singleton pattern, factory registration, driver loading. > **Final KTU Mantra for this topic:** *"Statics live in the class, instances live in the object; class loads once, objects come and go."*

topics.title: Final Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Final Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Variable (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> A **final variable** in Java is a variable whose value **cannot be changed** (re-assigned) once it has been initialized. The `final` keyword acts as a **constant modifier** that enforces immutability of the variable's binding (not necessarily the object's internal state). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OEERT613, final variables are classified into **final instance variables, final static variables, final local variables, final parameters, and blank final variables**, each governed by distinct initialization and scope rules.

### Categories of Final Variables in Java

| **Category** | **Declaration Site** | **Lifetime** | **Number of Copies** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Final Instance Variable | Inside class, outside method | Per Object | One per object |
| Final Static Variable | Inside class, with `static` | Per Class/JVM | One per class |
| Final Local Variable | Inside a method/block | Per method call | One per invocation |
| Final Parameter | In method signature | Per method call | One per call |
| Blank Final Variable | Declared but not initialized | Depends on type | Depends on type |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Engraved Stone Tablet" 🪨**
>
> Imagine you have a **stone tablet** where you carve a person's name. Once the name is carved:
> - You **cannot erase it** and carve a different name.
> - However, if the name refers to a *person*, the person themselves can still grow, change, or act differently — you just cannot change *who the tablet points to*.
>
> Similarly, a `final` variable in Java is like this engraved tablet:
> - 🔒 The **reference (binding)** is permanent — you cannot reassign the variable to a different value/object.
> - 🔓 But the **object's internal state** (its fields) may still be modified if they are not themselves `final`.

### Simple Intuitive Example

```java
final int MAX_SCORE = 100;   // MAX_SCORE is permanently 100
final StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("KTU");

// sb = new StringBuilder("APJ");   ❌ ERROR — cannot reassign reference
sb.append(" 2024");                  // ✅ OK — internal state of object changes
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of `final`**
> `final` makes the **variable's reference** immutable, **NOT the object** the reference points to. This subtle distinction is heavily tested in KTU university exams.

---

## 1.3 Why Final Variables? (Engineering Motivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why `final` exists in Java:**
> 1. **Constants** — Define mathematical/physical constants (e.g., `PI`, `MAX_LIMIT`).
> 2. **Security** — Prevent accidental modification of critical values (e.g., database URLs, keys).
> 3. **Thread Safety** — Immutable references are inherently thread-safe (no race conditions on the binding).
> 4. **Design Contracts** — Enforce API contracts where parameters must not be reassigned.
> 5. **Compiler Optimization** — JVM can inline final variables for performance.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Final Variables — Detailed Classification

### 🔹 2.1.1 Final Instance Variables

Declared inside a class but outside any method, constructor, or block. Each object has its **own copy**.

**Initialization Rules (KTU Board Standard):**
- Must be initialized **at the time of declaration**, OR
- Inside **every constructor** of the class (otherwise compile-time error).

```java
class Student {
    final int rollNo;        // blank final
    final String college = "KTU";   // initialized at declaration

    Student(int r) {
        rollNo = r;          // ✅ initialized in constructor
    }
}
```

### 🔹 2.1.2 Final Static Variables (Class Constants)

Declared with both `static` and `final` modifiers. Belong to the class, shared across all objects. Treated as **compile-time constants** if initialized with a constant expression.

**Initialization Rules:**
- Must be initialized **at the time of declaration**, OR
- Inside a **static initializer block**.

```java
class Constants {
    static final double PI = 3.14159;
    static final int MAX_USERS;

    static {
        MAX_USERS = 1000;
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> By KTU convention and Java Code Conventions, final static variables are named in **UPPER_SNAKE_CASE** (e.g., `MAX_VALUE`, `PI`).

### 🔹 2.1.3 Final Local Variables

Declared inside a method, constructor, or block. Their lifetime is limited to the block scope.

**Initialization Rules:**
- Can be declared **without initialization** (blank local final).
- Must be initialized **exactly once before use**.

```java
void calculate(int x) {
    final int y;        // blank final — legal
    y = x * 2;          // ✅ initialized once
    // y = 10;          ❌ ERROR — already assigned
    System.out.println(y);
}
```

### 🔹 2.1.4 Final Parameters

Method parameters declared with `final`. The method **cannot reassign** the parameter, but can still use it.

```java
void process(final int data) {
    // data = 20;        ❌ ERROR — cannot reassign final parameter
    System.out.println(data);
}
```

### 🔹 2.1.5 Blank Final Variables (Instance & Static)

A final variable **declared but not initialized** at declaration. Must be assigned exactly once later.

| **Type** | **Where Initialized** |
|---|---|
| Blank Final Instance Variable | In every constructor OR initializer block |
| Blank Final Static Variable | In static initializer block only |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** A blank final **instance** variable does NOT need to be the same value across all constructors — each constructor can assign its own value. But it must be assigned **exactly once** per constructor execution path.

### 🔹 2.1.6 Final Reference Variables

When a reference variable is final, you **cannot make it point to another object**, but the object it points to can be modified internally (if the class is mutable).

```java
final Student s = new Student(101);
s.rollNo = 202;          // ✅ Allowed if rollNo is not final
// s = new Student(999); ❌ ERROR — cannot reassign reference
```

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Reference Table for Final Variables (Examination-Ready)**

| **Rule #** | **Final Variable Type** | **Initialization Mandatory At** | **Reassignment Allowed?** | **Naming Convention** |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | Final Instance Variable | Declaration / Constructor / Instance Init Block | ❌ No | camelCase |
| 2 | Final Static Variable | Declaration / Static Init Block | ❌ No | **UPPER_SNAKE_CASE** |
| 3 | Final Local Variable | Before first use (in scope) | ❌ No | camelCase |
| 4 | Final Parameter | At method call (caller) | ❌ No | camelCase |
| 5 | Blank Final Instance | Each constructor (once) | ❌ No | camelCase |
| 6 | Blank Final Static | Static block (once) | ❌ No | **UPPER_SNAKE_CASE** |
| 7 | Final Reference | Declaration / Constructor | ❌ No (but object mutable) | camelCase |

### Key "Formula" for KTU Answers

$$
\text{Initialization Window} = 
\begin{cases} 
[\text{Declaration}, \text{Constructor Exit}] & \text{for blank final instance} \\
{[\text{Declaration}, \text{Static Init Block}]} & \text{for blank final static} \\
{[\text{Declaration}, \text{First Use}]} & \text{for blank final local} \\
\text{Caller} & \text{for final parameter}
\end{cases}
$$

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where final variables are used in industry:**
> - **Configuration Constants:** `static final String DB_URL = "jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/ktu";`
> - **Mathematical Constants:** `static final double PI`, `static final double E`
> - **API Tokens & Keys:** `static final String API_KEY`
> - **Immutability in Multithreading:** Reduces synchronization overhead.
> - **Effective Java (Item 17) — Joshua Bloch:** *"Minimize mutability"* — `final` is a key tool.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Code Analysis — Initialization Paths

### Demonstration 1: Final Instance Variable Initialization Path

```java
class Circle {
    final double radius;     // blank final instance variable
    final String unit = "cm"; // initialized at declaration

    // Constructor 1
    Circle(double r) {
        radius = r;          // ✅ initialized here
    }

    // Constructor 2 — would cause COMPILE ERROR
    // Circle() {
    //     // ❌ radius not initialized → compile error
    // }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:**

**Step 1:** When `new Circle(5.0)` is invoked, JVM allocates memory for the new object, including space for `radius` and `unit`.

**Step 2:** Field initializers and instance initializer blocks run **before** the constructor body.

**Step 3:** Since `unit = "cm"` is initialized at declaration, it is set first.

**Step 4:** Constructor body executes: `radius = r;` assigns `5.0` to `radius`.

**Step 5:** From this point, `radius` is **locked** — any further `radius = ...` causes a compile error.

**Step 6:** If a second constructor `Circle()` existed without assigning `radius`, the compiler detects an uninitialized blank final and rejects the code.

### Demonstration 2: Final Static Variable (Compile-Time Constant)

```java
class Config {
    static final int PORT = 8080;
    static final String APP_NAME = "KTU-OOP";
}
```

**Derivation Table:**

| **Step** | **Action** | **Memory Behavior** |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | Class `Config` loaded by ClassLoader | `PORT` and `APP_NAME` allocated in **Method Area** |
| 2 | `static final` initialized | Values stored permanently |
| 3 | All objects share same copy | Only one memory location |
| 4 | Inlined by compiler if primitive/String literal | Replaced with literal value in bytecode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a `static final` variable is initialized with a **constant expression** (literal or compile-time constant), the compiler **inlines** it — replacing references with the actual value in the `.class` file. This is why `static final int PORT = 8080;` is treated as a compile-time constant.

### Demonstration 3: Blank Final Local Variable Flow

```java
void demo(int input) {
    final int result;
    if (input > 0) {
        result = input * 2;       // ✅ assigned in if branch
    } else {
        result = 0;                // ✅ assigned in else branch
    }
    System.out.println(result);    // ✅ used after definite assignment
}
```

**Definite Assignment Analysis:**

The Java compiler uses a **definite assignment analysis** algorithm:

**Step 1:** The compiler tracks all possible execution paths from declaration to use.

**Step 2:** It checks that on **every path**, the variable is assigned **exactly once** before its first use.

**Step 3:** In the example above:
- **Path A** (input > 0): `result` assigned at line 4.
- **Path B** (input ≤ 0): `result` assigned at line 6.

**Step 4:** Both paths converge at `System.out.println(result)`, where `result` is definitely assigned.

**Step 5:** Compiler accepts the code.

> [!WARNING]
> **If you remove the `else` block**, the compiler reports:
> *"The local variable result may not have been initialized"* — because path B leaves it unassigned.

### Demonstration 4: Final Reference vs Final Object — The Critical Distinction

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;

class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();

        list.add("KTU");       // ✅ Allowed — modifying object
        list.add("OEERT613");  // ✅ Allowed

        System.out.println(list);   // [KTU, OEERT613]

        // list = new ArrayList<>();  ❌ Compile Error
    }
}
```

**Logical Derivation:**

**Step 1:** `final ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();`
- Creates a `ArrayList` object on the heap.
- Stores its **reference** in stack variable `list`.
- The `final` keyword locks the **reference (memory address)**, not the object.

**Step 2:** `list.add("KTU");`
- Calls a method on the **object** that `list` points to.
- Modifies the object's internal array — does NOT change the reference.
- ✅ Allowed.

**Step 3:** `list = new ArrayList<>();`
- Would attempt to change the reference stored in `list` to a new object.
- ❌ Compile-time error: *"cannot assign a value to final variable list"*.

### Demonstration 5: Final Parameter in Lambda / Inner Class

```java
interface Validator { boolean check(int x); }

class Checker {
    void test(final int threshold) {
        Validator v = (x) -> x > threshold;  // ✅ allowed
        // threshold = 10;                  ❌ not allowed
    }
}
```

**Why `final` is required for captured variables in lambdas:**
- Java captures local variables by **value** in lambda expressions.
- To prevent inconsistency, the captured variable must be effectively final.
- A variable is **effectively final** if it is not reassigned after declaration.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Behavior Comparison

| **Scenario** | **Without `final`** | **With `final`** |
|---|---|---|
| `int x = 5; x = 10;` | ✅ Compiles | ❌ Compile error |
| `static int y = 5;` in multiple objects | Each object gets its own copy | One shared copy |
| `final List l = new ArrayList(); l.add(1);` | Allowed | ✅ Allowed (object mutated) |
| `final List l = new ArrayList(); l = new LinkedList();` | Allowed | ❌ Compile error |
| Method parameter reassignment | `void m(int x) { x = 5; }` ✅ | `void m(final int x) { x = 5; }` ❌ |
| `final` variable in lambda | Captured by value (non-final → error) | Captured cleanly |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Memory Architecture of Final Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STACK["STACK MEMORY (Per Thread)"]
        L1["final int x = 10"]
        L2["final String s = 'KTU'"]
        L3["final double pi = 3.14"]
        L4["final ArrayList list"]
    end

    subgraph HEAP["HEAP MEMORY (Objects)"]
        H1["String Object: 'KTU'"]
        H2["ArrayList Object [ ]"]
        H3["Internal Object State"]
    end

    subgraph METHOD["METHOD AREA (Class Metadata)"]
        M1["static final int MAX = 100"]
        M2["static final String APP = 'OEERT'"]
        M3["Class Constant Pool"]
    end

    L2 -->|reference locked| H1
    L4 -->|reference locked| H2
    H2 -->|internal mutable state| H3
    L1 -.->|inlined by compiler| M3
    L3 -.->|compile-time constant| M3
    M1 --> M3
    M2 --> M3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Local final variables** live on the stack and their references cannot be redirected.
> - **Static final variables** live in the Method Area and are inlined as compile-time constants.
> - **The objects in the heap** remain mutable internally — only the references in stack/final are locked.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow for Final Variable Initialization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Variable declared with final keyword"] --> B{"Where is it declared?"}

    B -->|Inside class, not in method| C{"Is it static?"}
    B -->|Inside method or block| D["Final Local Variable"]
    B -->|Method signature| E["Final Parameter"]

    C -->|Yes - static final| F{"Initialized at declaration?"}
    C -->|No - instance final| G{"Initialized at declaration?"}

    F -->|Yes| F1["✅ Valid - Class Constant"]
    F -->|No| F2["Initialize in static block"]

    G -->|Yes| G1["✅ Valid - Object Field"]
    G -->|No| G2["Initialize in EVERY constructor"]

    D --> D1["Initialize before first use"]
    E --> E1["Initialized by caller at method call"]

    F2 --> Z["Compile-time check by compiler"]
    G2 --> Z
    D1 --> Z
    E1 --> Z
    F1 --> Z
    G1 --> Z

    Z -->|All paths assigned exactly once| Y["✅ Code Compiles"]
    Z -->|Any path unassigned or multiple assignment| X["❌ Compile Error"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Final Reference vs Object Mutability

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STACK2["STACK"]
        FREF["final List ref = 0xABC"]
    end

    subgraph HEAP2["HEAP"]
        OBJ["ArrayList Object @ 0xABC"]
        ARR["Internal array: [A, B, C]"]
    end

    FREF ==>|"LOCKED reference (cannot redirect)"| OBJ
    OBJ -->|"Mutable (methods can change)"| ARR

    OP1["ref.add('D')"] -->|"✅ Allowed - mutates object"| ARR
    OP2["ref = new ArrayList()"] -.->|"❌ Compile Error - cannot redirect"| FREF

    style FREF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style OBJ fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OP1 fill:#90EE90
    style OP2 fill:#FF6B6B
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define a final variable in Java. Can a final variable's value be changed after initialization? Justify with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **final variable** in Java is a variable declared using the `final` keyword whose value **cannot be changed (reassigned)** once initialized. It enforces immutability of the **reference/binding**, not necessarily the object state.

**Example:**
```java
final int MAX = 100;
// MAX = 200;   // ❌ Compile-time error
```

If `MAX` is a reference to a mutable object (e.g., `ArrayList`), the object's internal state can be modified, but the reference itself cannot be redirected to another object. **(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 justification + 1 example)**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is a blank final variable? Where can it be initialized? Give a Java example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **blank final variable** is a `final` variable that is **declared but not initialized** at the point of declaration. It must be assigned **exactly once** before use.

**Initialization locations:**
1. **Blank final instance variable** → inside every constructor or instance initializer block.
2. **Blank final static variable** → inside a static initializer block.

**Example:**
```java
class Test {
    final int x;          // blank final instance
    static final int Y;   // blank final static

    Test(int v) { x = v; }

    static { Y = 50; }
}
```

**(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 location + 1 example)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of final variables in Java with suitable examples. Discuss the rules for initializing each type.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Write a Java program that demonstrates a final reference variable pointing to a mutable object. Show that the reference cannot be changed but the object can be modified.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

The different types of final variables in Java are:

**1. Final Instance Variable (2 marks)**
A final variable declared inside a class but outside any method/block. Each object has its own copy. It must be initialized at declaration, in every constructor, or in an instance initializer block.

```java
class Employee {
    final int empId;
    final String company = "KTU";

    Employee(int id) {
        empId = id;
    }
}
```

**2. Final Static Variable (2 marks)**
Declared with both `static` and `final`. One copy per class, shared by all objects. Initialized at declaration or in a static block. Conventionally named in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE.

```java
class Constants {
    static final double PI = 3.14159;
    static final int MAX;
    static { MAX = 100; }
}
```

**3. Final Local Variable (1.5 marks)**
Declared inside a method. Must be initialized before first use, exactly once.

```java
void m() {
    final int x;
    x = 10;
    // x = 20;  ❌ error
}
```

**4. Final Parameter (1.5 marks)**
Method parameters marked final cannot be reassigned inside the method.

```java
void show(final int n) {
    // n = 5;  ❌ error
}
```

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;

public class FinalReferenceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Declaring a final reference variable
        final ArrayList<String> ktuSubjects = new ArrayList<>();

        // ✅ Modifying the object — allowed
        ktuSubjects.add("Object Oriented Concepts");
        ktuSubjects.add("Data Structures");
        ktuSubjects.add("Operating Systems");

        System.out.println("Initial list: " + ktuSubjects);

        // ✅ Mutating internal state — allowed
        ktuSubjects.set(0, "OEERT613");
        System.out.println("After modification: " + ktuSubjects);

        // ❌ Reassigning reference — COMPILE ERROR
        // ktuSubjects = new ArrayList<>();  // Error
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Initial list: [Object Oriented Concepts, Data Structures, Operating Systems]
After modification: [OEERT613, Data Structures, Operating Systems]
```

**Explanation (3 marks):** The `final` keyword applied to `ktuSubjects` locks the **reference** to the `ArrayList` object. We can mutate the internal state of the list (add, set, remove), but we cannot make `ktuSubjects` point to a new `ArrayList` object. This demonstrates that `final` enforces **reference immutability, not object immutability**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the rules and restrictions associated with final variables in Java? Explain with a program that throws a compile-time error when rules are violated.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between final, finally, and finalize in Java. Why is the term "final variable" considered an oxymoron in some contexts?

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

**Rules and Restrictions (4 marks):**

1. A final variable **cannot be reassigned** after initialization.
2. A blank final instance variable must be initialized in **every constructor**.
3. A blank final static variable must be initialized in a **static block**.
4. A final local variable must be initialized before **first use**.
5. A final parameter cannot be modified inside the method.
6. A final reference cannot point to a different object, but the object itself can be mutated.

**Program demonstrating compile-time errors (3 marks):**

```java
public class FinalRules {
    final int x;             // blank final instance
    final static int Y;      // blank final static

    FinalRules() {
        x = 10;              // ✅ OK
    }

    FinalRules(int v) {
        // ❌ ERROR: variable x might not have been initialized
        // if you don't assign x here
    }

    static {
        Y = 100;             // ✅ static initialization
    }

    void m(final int p) {
        // p = 20;           // ❌ ERROR: cannot assign to final parameter

        final int local;
        // System.out.println(local);  // ❌ ERROR: might not be initialized
        local = 50;
        // local = 60;       // ❌ ERROR: already assigned
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        final String s = "KTU";
        // s = "APJ";         // ❌ ERROR: cannot reassign
    }
}
```
