#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **final** | **finally** | **finalize()** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Keyword | Block | Method |
| **Used For** | Variables, methods, classes | Exception handling cleanup | Garbage collection cleanup |
| **Execution** | Compile-time enforcement | Always runs (even on exception) | Called by GC before object destruction |
| **Java Version** | Since 1.0 | Since 1.0 | Since 1.0 (deprecated in 9, 18) |
| **Example** | `final int x = 5;` | `try {...} finally {...}` | `protected void finalize() {...}` |

**Why "Final Variable" is an Oxymoron (2 marks):**

The term "final variable" is considered contradictory because:
- A **variable**, by definition, suggests a value that can **vary** (change).
- A **final** element is one that **cannot** be changed.

Hence, a "final variable" is essentially a **constant** — the variable's binding is permanent, making it conceptually closer to a constant than a true variable. Java uses this terminology to maintain backward compatibility with the original language design, where all named storage units are called "variables" regardless of mutability.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing reference final vs object final** — Most common error. Students write *"final means the object cannot be changed"* — this is **WRONG**. The object can be mutated; only the reference is locked. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting "exactly once" rule** — A blank final must be assigned **exactly once**, not "at least once". Re-assignment is a compile error.
> 3. **Missing static block for blank final static** — A blank final static variable can ONLY be initialized in a static block, NOT in a constructor.
> 4. **Not following naming convention** — KTU expects `static final` variables in `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` (e.g., `MAX_VALUE`). Lowercase loses a mark.
> 5. **Mixing up `final`, `finally`, `finalize`** — These three are completely different concepts. Confusing them is a guaranteed mark loss in Part A.
> 6. **Missing "effectively final" concept** — In lambda expressions, captured variables must be effectively final (no reassignment). Missing this loses 2–3 marks in Part B.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Final Variables**

- 🔹 **`final` keyword** → makes a variable's **reference** immutable (not the object).
- 🔹 **Five types:** final instance, final static, final local, final parameter, blank final.
- 🔹 **Final Instance Variable** → initialized at declaration, in constructor, or in instance block.
- 🔹 **Final Static Variable** → initialized at declaration or in static block; named in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE.
- 🔹 **Final Local Variable** → must be initialized before first use, exactly once.
- 🔹 **Final Parameter** → cannot be reassigned inside the method.
- 🔹 **Blank Final** → declared but not initialized; assigned exactly once later.
- 🔹 **Final Reference** → cannot point to another object, but the object itself is mutable.
- 🔹 **Compile-time constant** → `static final` primitives/String literals are inlined by the compiler.
- 🔹 **Effectively final** → required for variables captured in lambdas/anonymous classes.
- 🔹 **Difference from const** → Java has no `const` keyword; `final` plays the role of `const`.
- 🔹 **Immutability trio** → `final` + private fields + no setters = true immutable class (e.g., `String`).
- 🔹 **Exam trap** → `final` ≠ `immutable object`. Final locks the reference; immutability requires more design.
- 🔹 **Common KTU keywords to avoid mixing:** `final` vs `finally` vs `finalize` — all three are distinct.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inner Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inner Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Inner Class (Nested Class):** An *inner class* (or *nested class*) is a class that is declared as a **member** of another class or interface, or defined within the body of a *block of executable code* (such as a method, constructor, or initialization block). Inner classes are a strict subset of *nested classes* and exist logically *inside* the lexical scope of their enclosing class.

In the **Java Language Specification (JLS)**, an inner class is a **non-static nested class** — i.e., it has an implicit reference to an instance of its enclosing class. However, in the broader KTU 2024 syllabus, the term *"inner classes"* loosely encompasses **all four varieties** of nested classes:

1. **Member Inner Class** (non-static nested class)
2. **Static Nested Class**
3. **Local Inner Class** (defined inside a method/block)
4. **Anonymous Inner Class** (declared and instantiated in a single expression)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Russian Doll" Model:** Imagine a Matryoshka (Russian nesting) doll. The outer doll contains a smaller doll, which in turn contains an even smaller one. An *inner class* is exactly that: a *complete, fully-functional class living **inside** another class*. The inner doll can *see and use* everything inside its outer doll, but the outer doll's existence is *independent* of the inner one.

- The **enclosing (outer) class** = the bigger doll that acts as the *logical environment* and provides its members (variables/methods) to the inner class.
- The **inner class** = the smaller doll, which has *direct access* to the private/protected members of the outer doll, and can be instantiated *only through* an instance of the outer doll (for non-static member inner classes).

## 1.3 Why Inner Classes Exist — Core Motivation

- **Logical Grouping:** Two classes that are *logically tied together* (e.g., a `Car` and its `Engine`) can be grouped into a single source file, improving code organization.
- **Increased Encapsulation:** An inner class can be marked `private` so it is hidden from the outside world — only the outer class can instantiate it.
- **Direct Access to Outer's Private Members:** Inner classes can read/write the outer class's private fields without violating encapsulation (the compiler generates synthetic accessor methods).
- **Concise Event Handling:** Used heavily in GUI programming (e.g., `ActionListener` implementations).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Java Compiler Standards

> [!TIP]
> **Compiled Class File Naming Standard (`.class` artifact):**
> The Java compiler emits **a separate `.class` file** for every inner class, following the convention:
> $$\texttt{OuterClassName\\$InnerClassName.class}$$
> For anonymous classes, the compiler auto-generates numeric suffixes:
> $$\texttt{OuterClassName\\$1.class,\ \ OuterClassName\\$2.class,\ \dots}$$
> **Standard size limit** — Java imposes *no specific byte-size limit* on inner classes, but the JVM constant pool entry count per class must not exceed **65,536** (the `$` character itself is reserved in compiler-generated names).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Taxonomic Categories of Inner Classes

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to distinguish between the four varieties with **scope**, **modifiers**, and **access rules** clearly in mind.

### 2.1.1 Member Inner Class (Non-Static Nested Class)

- Declared as a **member** of the outer class, at the **class-body level** (not inside any method).
- **Implicitly bound** to an instance of the outer class → cannot exist without an outer object.
- Has *direct access* to **all members** (private, protected, public) of the outer class.
- **Modifiers allowed:** `public`, `protected`, `private`, `package-private`, `abstract`, `final`.
- **Access rule:** Inside the inner class, use `OuterClassName.this.fieldName` to disambiguate shadowed members.

```java
class Outer {
    private int data = 10;
    class Inner {
        void show() { System.out.println(data); }  // direct access to private
    }
}
```

### 2.1.2 Static Nested Class

- Declared with the `static` modifier at the *class-body level*.
- **NOT** logically tied to any outer instance — cannot access non-static members directly.
- Can only access *static* members of the outer class (or use an explicit outer instance).
- **Instantiation:** `Outer.StaticNested obj = new Outer.StaticNested();`  (no outer object required).

```java
class Outer {
    static int data = 10;
    static class Nested {
        void show() { System.out.println(data); }
    }
}
```

### 2.1.3 Local Inner Class

- Declared **inside a method, constructor, or initialization block** (`{ }`).
- **Scope** is restricted to the *block* in which it is declared.
- Can access `final` or *effectively final* local variables of the enclosing block (Java 8+ rule).
- Can also access all members of the enclosing outer class.

```java
class Outer {
    void display() {
        final int x = 5;
        class Local { void print() { System.out.println(x); } }
        new Local().print();
    }
}
```

### 2.1.4 Anonymous Inner Class

- A *class declared and instantiated* in a single expression — **no name**.
- Used to provide a one-off implementation of an interface or extension of a class.
- Compiled into a `.class` file with synthetic names like `Outer$1.class`.
- **Cannot declare constructors**; uses an *instance initializer block* instead.

```java
interface Greet { void hello(); }
class Demo {
    void useGreet() {
        Greet g = new Greet() {           // anonymous class begins
            public void hello() { System.out.println("Hi"); }
        };                                 // anonymous class ends
        g.hello();
    }
}
```

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Rule

| Rule | Reason | Engineering Justification |
|------|--------|--------------------------|
| Inner class can access private members of outer | Compiler generates synthetic *access-bridge* methods | Preserves encapsulation while enabling tight coupling |
| Local inner class needs *effectively final* variables | Captured variables become fields of an inner copy | Avoids inconsistent-state bugs after the enclosing method exits |
| Anonymous class has no constructor | It has no name to reference from `super()` | Forces inline, lightweight, one-shot implementations |
| Static nested class cannot access non-static outer members | No enclosing instance reference exists | Restores the *class-level* (not object-level) scope of `static` |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it represents 70% of Part-A marks on Inner Classes.**

| Property | Member Inner | Static Nested | Local Inner | Anonymous Inner |
|----------|--------------|---------------|-------------|-----------------|
| Declared at class-body level? | **Yes** | **Yes** | No (inside method) | No (expression) |
| Needs outer instance? | **Yes** | **No** | **Yes** (for non-static outer) | **Yes** (if extending non-static inner) |
| Can access outer non-static members? | **Yes** | No (needs `outerObj.field`) | **Yes** | **Yes** (via enclosing instance) |
| Can access outer `private`? | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Allowed modifiers | `private`, `protected`, `final`, `abstract` | `private`, `protected`, `final` | *None* (effectively final locals) | *None* |
| Can declare constructors? | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No** (uses initializer block) |
| `.class` file produced | `Outer$Inner.class` | `Outer$Nested.class` | `Outer$1Local.class` (numeric) | `Outer$1.class`, `Outer$2.class` |
| Extending/Implementing | Class or Interface | Class or Interface | Class or Interface | **Exactly one** (interface OR class) |
| Compilation unit | Member of outer file | Member of outer file | Inside method | Single expression statement |

## 2.4 The "Effectively Final" Rule (Java 8+)

> [!NOTE]
> A local variable is *effectively final* if its value is **never modified** after initialization (including no reassignment in lambdas / inner classes). The compiler treats it as `final` for the purpose of capture. The relationship is:
> $$\text{Captured Local Variable} \longrightarrow \text{Synthetic Field of Inner Class Copy}$$
> The reference is preserved at the moment of capture; subsequent changes to the original local would break consistency — hence the *effectively final* restriction.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GUI Frameworks (Swing/JavaFX):** `ActionListener` and `MouseAdapter` are typically implemented as anonymous inner classes.
- **Data Structures (Java Collections):** `LinkedList$Node`, `HashMap$Entry` are *static nested classes* — they are tightly bound to their outer container's logic but have no need for outer instance state.
- **Design Patterns:** *Iterator* design pattern uses inner classes to traverse collection internals (e.g., `ArrayList$Itr`).
- **Builder Pattern (Effective Java, Item 2):** Often uses static nested `Builder` classes for fluent construction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walk-Through — Member Inner Class Instantiation

Below is the **complete logical derivation** of how a member inner class is created, compiled, and accessed at runtime. Each step maps to a JVM-level concept.

### Step 1 — Source Code Declaration
The programmer writes a class `University` containing a non-static inner class `Department`:

```java
class University {
    private String name = "KTU Kerala";
    class Department {
        void display() {
            System.out.println("University: " + name);
        }
    }
}
```

### Step 2 — Implicit Outer-Reference Injection
The Java compiler detects that `Department` is a *non-static* member class and **injects a synthetic field** holding a reference to the enclosing `University` instance. After compilation, the bytecode for `Department` is conceptually transformed as:

```java
class Department {
    final University this$0;          // synthetic field
    void display() {
        System.out.println("University: " + this$0.name);
    }
}
```

> **Logic behind the injection:** A non-static member inner class can only be created *in the context of* an outer object. The compiler therefore forces the outer reference to be present in every inner instance.

### Step 3 — Outer-Reference Initialization via `this$0`
The compiler generates a **synthetic constructor** for `Department` that accepts the outer instance as a parameter:

$$
\texttt{Department(University outer)} \equiv \texttt{this.this\$0 = outer;}
$$

### Step 4 — User-Level Instantiation Syntax
The client code must always use an *outer instance* to create the inner instance. There are **two equivalent syntaxes**:

```java
University u = new University();
University.Department d1 = u.new Department();          // explicit form
University.Department d2 = new University().new Department(); // chained form
```

### Step 5 — Method Dispatch
When `d1.display()` is invoked, the JVM:
1. Locates the synthetic field `this$0` (the outer `University` reference).
2. Reads the private field `name` of `University` through a synthetic accessor method (`access$000`).
3. Prints `"University: KTU Kerala"`.

> [!TIP]
> The full call resolution is:
> $$\texttt{d1.display()} \longrightarrow \texttt{d1.this\$0.name} \longrightarrow \texttt{u.name} \longrightarrow \texttt{"KTU Kerala"}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Compilation Output Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> The table below traces each inner class type through the **declaration → compilation → instantiation** pipeline. This matrix is a frequent 14-mark question stem on KTU ESE.

| Inner Class Type | Declaration Site | Compiled `.class` | Instantiation Syntax (outer = `O`, inner = `I`) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Member Inner | Class body (non-static) | `O$I.class` | `O obj = new O(); O.I inner = obj.new I();` |
| Static Nested | Class body (`static` keyword) | `O$I.class` | `O.I inner = new O.I();`  (no outer object needed) |
| Local Inner | Inside a method or block | `O$1Local.class` | Created via `new Local()` *inside the method only* |
| Anonymous Inner | Expression returning supertype | `O$1.class`, `O$2.class` | `SuperType obj = new SuperType() { ... };` |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Code Trace — Local Inner Class with Captured Variable

```java
class Outer {
    void compute(final int n) {
        final int multiplier = 3;            // effectively final
        class LocalCalc {
            int square(int x) { return x * x; }
            int computeResult() {
                int squared = square(n);
                return squared * multiplier;
            }
        }
        LocalCalc lc = new LocalCalc();
        System.out.println(lc.computeResult());
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Outer().compute(4);
    }
}
```

### Execution Trace (Line-by-Line Reasoning)

1. `main` calls `new Outer().compute(4)`. JVM allocates a new `Outer` instance and pushes `n = 4` onto the operand stack.
2. Inside `compute`, `multiplier = 3` is allocated on the local variable table as an *effectively final* local.
3. The class `LocalCalc` is *declared*; it is **not** loaded into the JVM until first referenced.
4. `new LocalCalc()` is executed → triggers lazy class loading of `Outer$1LocalCalc.class`.
5. The constructor of `LocalCalc` (compiler-generated) **copies** the value of `multiplier` (which is `3`) into a synthetic field of the inner instance.
6. `lc.computeResult()` is called → executes `square(n) = 16`, then returns $16 \times 3 = 48$.
7. The result `48` is printed to the console.

$$
\texttt{Result} = (n^2) \times \texttt{multiplier} = 4^2 \times 3 = 16 \times 3 = 48
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the programmer had later written `multiplier = 5;` before `new LocalCalc()`, the compiler would throw:
> **"Local variable multiplier defined in an enclosing scope must be final or effectively final"**

## 3.4 Detailed Comparison — Member vs. Static Nested

| Aspect | Member Inner | Static Nested |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| Implicit `this$0` field? | **Yes** | No |
| Can be instantiated from a `static` context (like `main`) directly? | **No** — needs outer object | **Yes** |
| Memory per inner instance | Outer ref + inner fields | Inner fields only |
| `Outer.this` access? | **Yes** | Compile error |
| Most common use | Iterator implementations (needs outer state) | Builder / Entry / Key-Value pairs (no outer state needed) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Nested Classes (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    RootA["Nested Classes in Java"] --> RootB["Inner Non-Static Classes"]
    RootA --> RootC["Static Nested Classes"]

    RootB --> NodeM1["Member Inner Class"]
    RootB --> NodeM2["Local Inner Class"]
    RootB --> NodeM3["Anonymous Inner Class"]

    NodeM1 --> NodeM1a["Declared at class body level"]
    NodeM1 --> NodeM1b["Has implicit this dollar zero reference"]
    NodeM1 --> NodeM1c["Needs outer instance to instantiate"]

    NodeM2 --> NodeM2a["Declared inside method or block"]
    NodeM2 --> NodeM2b["Scope limited to enclosing block"]
    NodeM2 --> NodeM2c["Can capture final locals"]

    NodeM3 --> NodeM3a["Declared and instantiated together"]
    NodeM3 --> NodeM3b["No class name assigned"]
    NodeM3 --> NodeM3c["Cannot have explicit constructor"]

    RootC --> NodeS1["Static Nested Class"]
    NodeS1 --> NodeS1a["Has static keyword"]
    NodeS1 --> NodeS1b["No outer reference required"]
    NodeS1 --> NodeS1c["Can access only static outer members"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inner Class Compilation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SrcFile["Source File Outer dot java"] --> CompProc["Java Compiler javac"]
    CompProc --> OutCls["Outer dot class"]
    CompProc --> InnerCls1["Outer dollar Inner dot class"]
    CompProc --> InnerCls2["Outer dollar 1 dot class Anonymous"]
    CompProc --> InnerCls3["Outer dollar 1Local dot class LocalInner"]

    OutCls --> JvmLoad["JVM Class Loader"]
    InnerCls1 --> JvmLoad
    InnerCls2 --> JvmLoad
    InnerCls3 --> JvmLoad

    JvmLoad --> RunCtx["Runtime Memory Heap"]
    RunCtx --> OuterObj["Outer Instance"]
    OuterObj --> Synthetic["Synthetic Field this dollar zero"]
    Synthetic --> InnerObj["Inner Instance"]
    InnerObj --> AccessOuter["Access Outer Private Members via Synthetic Accessor"]
```

## 4.3 Access-Modifier Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QStart["Need an inner class"] --> Q1{"Does it need outer instance state?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2["Use Member Inner Class"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3["Use Static Nested Class"]
    Q2 --> Q4{"Defined inside a method?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5["Use Local Inner Class"]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6["Use Member Inner Class"]
    Q5 --> Q7{"Need a name?"}
    Q6 --> Q7
    Q7 -- No --> Q8["Use Anonymous Inner Class"]
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q9["Use Local or Member Class as decided"]
```

## 4.4 Memory-Layout Schematic (Member Inner)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HeapObj["Heap Memory for Outer Instance"]
        OuterData["Outer Fields name data"]
    end
    subgraph HeapInner["Heap Memory for Inner Instance"]
        InnerData["Inner Fields value"]
        SynRef["Synthetic Reference to Outer this dollar zero"]
    end
    HeapObj --> SynRef
    HeapInner --> OuterData
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q1. Define an inner class in Java. Differentiate between a *member inner class* and a *static nested class*.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

An *inner class* is a class declared as a member of another class, providing a logically-grouped helper type that can access all members (including private ones) of its enclosing class.

| Feature | Member Inner Class | Static Nested Class |
|---|---|---|
| `static` keyword? | Not used | **Required** |
| Needs outer instance? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Can access non-static outer members? | **Yes** | **No** (requires explicit object) |
| Inner class type | Truly *inner* | Just *nested* (not strictly *inner* per JLS) |

---

**Q2. What is an *anonymous inner class*? State one limitation.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

An *anonymous inner class* is a class that is **declared and instantiated** in a single expression without being assigned a name. It is used to provide a one-off implementation of an interface or extension of a class.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation:** An anonymous inner class **cannot declare its own constructors**, because the class has no name to reference in a `super()` call. It can only use *instance initializer blocks* to perform initialization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, Module 2, with Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the four types of inner classes in Java with suitable code examples.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

The four types of inner classes supported in Java are:

**1. Member Inner Class (Non-Static)**
Declared at the class-body level without the `static` keyword. It has an implicit reference to its outer instance.

```java
class CPU {
    private double price;
    CPU(double p) { this.price = p; }
    class Processor {
        private int cores = 8;
        void display() {
            System.out.println("CPU Price: " + price);
            System.out.println("Cores: " + cores);
        }
    }
}
```

**2. Static Nested Class**
Declared at the class-body level with the `static` keyword. It does **not** have an outer reference.

```java
class CPU {
    static class Cache {
        int size = 12;
        void show() { System.out.println("Cache: " + size + "MB"); }
    }
}
```

**3. Local Inner Class**
Declared *inside a method*; its scope is restricted to that method.

```java
class Demo {
    void run() {
        int limit = 100;
        class Validator {
            boolean check(int x) { return x <= limit; }
        }
        System.out.println(new Validator().check(50));
    }
}
```

**4. Anonymous Inner Class**
Declared and instantiated together as an expression; no class name.

```java
abstract class Shape { abstract void draw(); }
class App {
    void paint() {
        Shape s = new Shape() {
            void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing circle"); }
        };
        s.draw();
    }
}
```

**(b) Discuss the restrictions and rules that apply to local inner classes in Java. Write a Java program demonstrating the use of a local inner class that accesses both an outer-class field and a final local variable.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Rules for Local Inner Classes:**

1. **Scope:** Restricted to the block in which they are defined (method, constructor, or initializer).
2. **Access to enclosing class:** They can access all members of the enclosing class, including private members.
3. **Local variable capture:** They can capture local variables of the enclosing block **only if those variables are `final` or *effectively final*** (Java 8+).
4. **Modifiers:** Local classes cannot be declared `public`, `protected`, `private`, or `static` — they are scoped at the block level.
5. **Inheritance:** Local classes can extend a class or implement an interface (only one, like any class).
6. **Instantiation:** They can be instantiated **only within the enclosing block** — outside the block, the class is unreachable.

**Program Demonstrating Local Inner Class:**

```java
class OuterDemo {
    private String greeting = "Hello";

    void displayMessage(final String user) {
        // 'user' is final; 'greeting' is an outer field
        class LocalGreeter {
            void greet() {
                System.out.println(greeting + ", " + user + "!");
            }
        }
        LocalGreeter lg = new LocalGreeter();
        lg.greet();
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new OuterDemo().displayMessage("Aparna");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Hello, Aparna!
```

**Explanation:**
- `greeting` is a *private outer field*, accessible directly inside the local class.
- `user` is a *final parameter* of the enclosing method — captured into the local class's synthetic copy.
- The local class `LocalGreeter` is declared, instantiated, and used **all within** `displayMessage`. After the method returns, the class symbol is out of scope.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) With a neat diagram and Java code, explain how a member inner class accesses the private members of its enclosing class. Explain the role of the synthetic `this$0` reference.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

A *member inner class* is logically associated with an instance of its enclosing outer class. The Java compiler enforces this association by automatically inserting a **synthetic field named `this$0`** of type *OuterClass* into every non-static inner class.

**Diagram:**

```
    +----------------------+         +------------------------+
    |   Outer Instance     |         |   Inner Instance       |
    |----------------------|         |------------------------|
    | private data = 10    |  <----  |  this$0 ---> (Outer*)  |
    |                      |  refers |  ownField = 5          |
    +----------------------+  back   +------------------------+
```

**Java Program:**

```java
class Outer {
    private int data = 10;
    private String label = "KTU";

    class Inner {
        private int ownField = 5;

        void display() {
            // Direct access to outer's private members
            System.out.println("Data: " + data);
            System.out.println("Label: " + label);
            // Disambiguating shadowed names
            System.out.println("Own: " + this.ownField);
            System.out.println("Outer label: " + Outer.this.label);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Outer outer = new Outer();
        Outer.Inner inner = outer.new Inner();
        inner.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Data: 10
Label: KTU
Own: 5
Outer label: KTU
```

**Role of the Synthetic `this$0` Reference:**

1. **Auto-Injection:** The compiler adds a package-private synthetic field `this$0` of type `Outer` to every non-static inner class.
2. **Outer-Reference Storage:** This field stores the reference to the enclosing outer instance that "owns" this inner object.
3. **Access Enablement:** All accesses to outer's private members from inside the inner class are rewritten by the compiler as accesses through `this$0`, e.g., `data` becomes `this$0.data`.
4. **Disambiguation:** The qualified syntax `Outer.this.fieldName` resolves to the same `this$0.fieldName` in the bytecode.
5. **No Manual Access:** Programmers cannot directly assign to `this$0` — it is read-only after construction.

---

**(b) Compare and contrast *member inner classes* and *anonymous inner classes*. Provide a Java example where an anonymous inner class is used to handle a button-click event in a Swing GUI.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Member Inner Class | Anonymous Inner Class |
|---|---|---|
| Name | Has a class name | **No class name** |
| Declaration | Separate `class Inner { }` block | Inline within an expression |
| Constructors | **Can declare** | **Cannot declare** |
| Number of instances | Multiple per outer instance | Usually one (or few) inline uses |
| Extensibility | Can extend *and* implement (one each) | Can extend *exactly one* class OR implement *exactly one* interface |
| Readability | Slightly verbose but well-organized | Very concise but harder to read in complex forms |
| Use-case | Reusable helper class tied to outer | One-off event handlers / callbacks |

**Java Example — Anonymous Inner Class with Swing:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class ButtonDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("KTU Inner Class Demo");
        JButton btn = new JButton("Click Me");

        // Anonymous inner class implementing ActionListener
        btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Button clicked! Welcome to KTU.");
            }
        });

        frame.add(btn);
        frame.setSize(300, 200);
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- The expression `new ActionListener() { ... }` creates an **anonymous class** that implements the `ActionListener` interface.
- The compiler emits the bytecode file `ButtonDemo$1.class` for this anonymous class.
- It is instantiated and passed to `addActionListener` *in a single step* — no name, no separate constructor.
- This is the canonical use of anonymous inner classes for **event-driven GUI programming** in Java.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing "inner" with "nested":** Per JLS, *only* non-static nested classes are technically "inner". Students who call a `static` nested class an "inner class" lose 1 mark. Use the term **"static nested class"** unless it is non-static.
> 2. **Forgetting `outerObj.new`:** Instantiating a member inner class with `new Inner()` from a `static` context (like `main`) will throw a **compile error**. The correct syntax is `outerObj.new Inner()`.
> 3. **Overlooking effectively-final rule:** Modifying a captured local variable inside a local inner class is a common error that KTU examiners specifically test.
> 4. **Missing `Outer.this`:** When a field name is shadowed, students often write `this.field` (which refers to the inner class field) instead of `Outer.this.field`.
> 5. **Anonymous class constructor:** Students incorrectly write constructors for anonymous classes — these are syntactically impossible.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- ✅ Inner classes are classes defined **inside** another class — they are compiled into **separate `.class` files** named `Outer$Inner.class`.
- ✅ **Four types:** Member Inner, Static Nested, Local Inner, Anonymous Inner.
- ✅ **Member Inner** has implicit `this$0` reference → needs outer instance → cannot exist in `static` context alone.
- ✅ **Static Nested** has no outer reference → can be instantiated directly: `new Outer.Nested()`.
- ✅ **Local Inner** is declared inside a method → scope is the enclosing block → captures *effectively final* locals.
- ✅ **Anonymous Inner** has no name and no constructor → used heavily for callbacks and event listeners.
- ✅ All non-static inner classes can access **private** members of the outer class (via compiler-generated synthetic accessors).
- ✅ Inside a non-static inner class, use **`OuterClassName.this`** to disambiguate shadowed fields.
- ✅ The **`.class` file naming** convention is `Outer$Inner.class` for named inner classes, and `Outer$1.class`, `Outer$2.class`, ... for anonymous and local inner classes.
- ✅ Inner classes are extensively used in **design patterns** (Iterator, Builder, Observer) and **GUI event handling** (Swing, JavaFX, Android).
- ✅ The **"effectively final"** rule (Java 8+) allows lambdas and inner classes to capture local variables without explicit `final` declaration, provided the variable is never reassigned.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inheritance - Super Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inheritance - Super Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Super Class (KTU 2024 Official Terminology):** A **Super Class** in Object-Oriented Programming is the class from which features (attributes and methods) are inherited. It is also called the **Parent Class**, **Base Class**, or **General Class**. The class that inherits from a Super Class is called a **Sub Class** (Child/Derived/Specialized Class). In Java, the `extends` keyword establishes this `IS-A` relationship.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEERT613), the Super Class concept is foundational to **inheritance**, one of the four pillars of OOP (along with Encapsulation, Polymorphism, and Abstraction). It enables **code reuse**, **method overriding**, and forms the basis for **dynamic polymorphism (runtime dispatch)**.

### Mathematical Representation of the Inheritance Relationship

If $C_{parent}$ denotes the Super Class and $C_{child}$ denotes the Sub Class, then inheritance establishes the following formal relationship:

$$C_{child} \xrightarrow{\text{extends}} C_{parent}$$

This implies that $C_{child}$ **inherits all accessible members** of $C_{parent}$ (subject to access modifiers), and additionally, $C_{child}$ may:
- **Add** new fields and methods
- **Override** existing methods
- **Specialize** behavior

The cardinality is typically **single inheritance in Java** (one direct parent), but can be chained:

$$C_1 \rightarrow C_2 \rightarrow C_3 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow C_n$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Tree Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Think of inheritance like a **family tree** or a **biological classification system**.

Imagine a **biological taxonomy**:

| Level | Example | In OOP Terminology |
|:------|:--------|:-------------------|
| Kingdom | Animal | `Object` (Java's universal Super Class) |
| Phylum | Mammal | Abstract Super Class |
| Class | Canine | Concrete Super Class |
| Species | Dog | Sub Class |

A **Dog** (Sub Class) inherits properties from **Canine** (Super Class) — like having four legs, fur, and the ability to bark. But a Dog *specializes* this by having a breed name and a specific size. The Dog does not need to re-declare "has four legs" because that trait was already defined in the Super Class.

### Why Do We Need Super Classes? (The "Why" Behind the Concept)

> [!TIP]
> **Three Engineering Motivations for Super Classes:**
> 1. **DRY Principle (Don't Repeat Yourself):** Avoid duplicating common fields/methods across multiple related classes.
> 2. **Polymorphic Behavior:** Enable a single interface (Super Class type) to represent multiple underlying forms (Sub Class objects).
> 3. **Logical Hierarchy:** Model real-world taxonomical relationships (Vehicle → Car, Truck; Employee → Manager, Engineer).

## 1.3 The `super` Keyword — A Direct Reference to the Super Class

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** In Java, `super` is a **reserved keyword** that acts as a reference variable referring to the **immediate parent class object**. It is the bridge that allows a Sub Class to explicitly access the members of its Super Class.

The `super` keyword has **three primary uses** in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **`super.variableName`** — Accesses the Super Class's hidden/overridden data members.
2. **`super.methodName()`** — Invokes the Super Class's overridden method.
3. **`super()` or `super(arguments)`** — Invokes the Super Class's constructor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Super Class Member Access

When a Sub Class is created, the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) constructs a **complete object** that contains:
- All fields declared in the Sub Class
- All fields inherited from the Super Class
- All methods of both the Sub Class and the Super Class

The object is therefore a **fusion** of parent and child definitions. The `super` reference provides controlled access to the "parent portion" of this composite object.

### 2.1.1 Access Rules Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **what a Sub Class can access** from its Super Class under different access modifiers. This is a frequently tested concept in KTU examinations.

| Super Class Member | `private` | `default` (package) | `protected` | `public` |
|:-------------------|:---------:|:-------------------:|:-----------:|:--------:|
| **Same Package** (Sub Class) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Different Package** (Sub Class via inheritance) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Different Package** (non-Sub Class) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |

**Key Insight:** The `protected` modifier is specifically designed for inheritance scenarios — it allows Sub Classes (even in different packages) to access the member, while still hiding it from unrelated classes.

## 2.2 Constructor Chaining — The Most Critical Super Class Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Java Constructors:** When a Sub Class object is instantiated, the **Super Class constructor is ALWAYS executed first**, before the Sub Class constructor body runs. This is enforced by the compiler via an implicit `super()` call.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Constructor Invocation

1. When `new SubClass()` is called, the JVM begins executing the Sub Class constructor.
2. As the **first statement** (either explicitly written or implicitly inserted), the compiler ensures a call to a Super Class constructor.
3. The chain continues **upward** through the inheritance hierarchy until the `Object` class constructor (the ultimate Super Class in Java) is reached.
4. After `Object`'s constructor finishes, control returns downward, executing each constructor in reverse order.

### The Two Forms of Super Constructor Calls

| Syntax | Meaning | When Used |
|:-------|:--------|:----------|
| `super()` | Calls the **no-argument** constructor of the Super Class | When the Super Class has a default constructor or one with no args |
| `super(args)` | Calls the **parameterized** constructor matching the argument list | When the Super Class has no default constructor, or specific initialization is required |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** If the Super Class has **only parameterized constructors** (i.e., no default constructor is explicitly defined), then the Sub Class constructor **MUST** explicitly call `super(args)`. Otherwise, the compiler will throw an error because it cannot insert an implicit `super()` that matches a non-existent no-arg constructor.

## 2.3 Method Overriding and `super.methodName()`

When a Sub Class defines a method with the **same signature** as a method in the Super Class, it is said to **override** the method. The `super` keyword allows the Sub Class to call the **overridden version** from the parent — effectively extending the parent's behavior rather than completely replacing it.

### The Template Method Pattern (Conceptual Formula)

$$\text{SubClass.method()} = \text{SuperClass.method()} + \text{Additional Sub Class Logic}$$

This is expressed in code as:

```java
@Override
public void display() {
    super.display();        // Call parent's version
    // Additional Sub Class-specific code here
}
```

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table serves as your **rapid revision reference** for all Super Class-related syntax. Memorize this for guaranteed marks in Part A questions.

| Concept | Java Syntax | Purpose | KTU Exam Frequency |
|:--------|:------------|:--------|:------------------:|
| Defining inheritance | `class Sub extends Super` | Establish IS-A relationship | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Access Super Class field | `super.fieldName` | Access hidden/overridden variable | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Call Super Class method | `super.methodName()` | Invoke overridden method | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Call Super Class constructor (no-arg) | `super();` | Implicit/explicit default constructor call | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Call Super Class constructor (parameterized) | `super(param1, param2);` | Pass initialization data to parent | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Prevent inheritance | `final class SuperClass` | Make Super Class un-extendable | ⭐⭐ |
| Prevent method overriding | `final void method()` | Lock method implementation | ⭐⭐ |
| Block overriding (advanced) | `super.methodName()` inside `final` | Not applicable (final blocks inheritance) | ⭐ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Super Class pattern is not merely an academic exercise — it powers numerous production-grade systems:

- **GUI Frameworks (JavaFX, Swing):** A base `Component` class serves as the Super Class for `Button`, `TextField`, `Label`, etc.
- **Exception Handling:** `Exception` is the Super Class for `IOException`, `NullPointerException`, etc.
- **Collections Framework:** `AbstractList` is the Super Class for `ArrayList` and `LinkedList`.
- **Enterprise Applications (Spring, Hibernate):** Entity hierarchies use Super Classes for shared audit fields (`createdDate`, `modifiedBy`).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Construction Analysis: Object Creation Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation traces the **complete execution path** when a Sub Class object is instantiated. This is a common 7-mark question in KTU examinations.

### 3.1.1 The Class Hierarchy Setup

Let us define a three-level inheritance chain as our analytical model:

$$\text{Level 0: } Object \rightarrow \text{Level 1: } Parent \rightarrow \text{Level 2: } Child$$

The corresponding Java code is:

```java
// Level 0 — Implicit Super Class (Java's root)
class Object { /* Provided by JDK */ }

// Level 1 — User-defined Super Class
class Parent {
    int x;
    
    Parent() {
        System.out.println("Parent default constructor");
        this.x = 100;
    }
    
    Parent(int x) {
        System.out.println("Parent parameterized constructor");
        this.x = x;
    }
    
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Parent's display: x = " + x);
    }
}

// Level 2 — Sub Class inheriting from Parent
class Child extends Parent {
    int y;
    
    Child() {
        super();        // Explicit call to Parent's no-arg constructor
        System.out.println("Child default constructor");
        this.y = 200;
    }
    
    Child(int x, int y) {
        super(x);       // Explicit call to Parent's parameterized constructor
        System.out.println("Child parameterized constructor");
        this.y = y;
    }
    
    @Override
    void display() {
        super.display();   // Invoke Parent's overridden method
        System.out.println("Child's display: y = " + y);
    }
}
```

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Execution Trace

Now let us derive what happens when we execute the statement:

```java
Child obj = new Child(50, 75);
```

**Step 1 — JVM Enters the `Child(int, int)` Constructor**

The JVM allocates memory for the `Child` object. This memory contains space for:
- Inherited fields from `Parent` (i.e., `x`)
- Fields declared in `Child` (i.e., `y`)
- Method tables for both `Child` and `Parent`

**Step 2 — Execution of the First Statement: `super(x);`**

The call `super(50)` is dispatched to the `Parent(int x)` constructor.

**Step 3 — Execution of `Parent(int x)` Constructor**

Inside `Parent(int x)`:
- The compiler inserts an **implicit** call: `super();` which invokes the `Object()` constructor (Java's root class).
- The `Object()` constructor executes (typically a no-op or minimal native initialization).
- Control returns to `Parent(int x)`.
- `this.x = x;` assigns the value $50$ to the field `x`.
- The statement `"Parent parameterized constructor"` is printed.
- `Parent(int x)` completes and returns.

**Step 4 — Return to `Child(int, int)` Constructor**

Control resumes at the line following `super(x);`:
- The statement `"Child parameterized constructor"` is printed.
- `this.y = y;` assigns the value $75$ to the field `y`.
- `Child(int, int)` completes.

**Step 5 — Object Reference Assignment**

The fully constructed `Child` object is assigned to the reference variable `obj`.

**Step 6 — Invoking `obj.display()`**

When `obj.display()` is called:
- The JVM looks up `display()` in `Child`'s method table first (dynamic dispatch).
- `Child`'s `display()` executes.
- `super.display();` invokes `Parent`'s version, printing `"Parent's display: x = 50"`.
- Control returns to `Child`'s `display()`, printing `"Child's display: y = 75"`.

### 3.1.3 Final Output

The complete console output is:

```
Parent parameterized constructor
Child parameterized constructor
Parent's display: x = 50
Child's display: y = 75
```

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Variable Resolution with `super`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Variable Shadowing Problem:** When a Sub Class declares a variable with the **same name** as one in the Super Class, the Sub Class's variable **shadows** (hides) the Super Class's variable. To access the hidden parent variable, you MUST use `super`.

### 3.2.1 Mathematical Model of Name Resolution

Let us define a variable resolution function $R$ that maps a name reference to the correct memory location:

$$R(\text{name}, \text{context}) = \begin{cases} \text{Sub Class scope} & \text{if name exists in Sub Class} \\ \text{Super Class scope} & \text{if accessed via } \texttt{super.name} \\ \text{Compilation Error} & \text{if name not found} \end{cases}$$

### 3.2.2 Worked Example

```java
class SuperClass {
    int value = 10;
    String name = "Parent";
}

class SubClass extends SuperClass {
    int value = 20;           // Hides SuperClass.value
    String label = "Child";
    
    void printValues() {
        System.out.println(value);        // Prints 20 (Sub Class's value)
        System.out.println(this.value);   // Prints 20 (same as above)
        System.out.println(super.value);  // Prints 10 (Super Class's value)
        System.out.println(name);         // Prints "Parent" (inherited, no shadowing)
        System.out.println(label);        // Prints "Child" (Sub Class only)
    }
}
```

**Derivation of Output:**

| Statement | Resolution Path | Value | Reasoning |
|:----------|:----------------|:------|:----------|
| `value` | $R(\text{value}, \text{local}) \rightarrow \text{Sub Class}$ | $20$ | Local scope takes priority |
| `this.value` | $R(\text{value}, \text{this}) \rightarrow \text{Sub Class}$ | $20$ | `this` refers to current object (Sub Class portion) |
| `super.value` | $R(\text{value}, \text{super}) \rightarrow \text{Super Class}$ | $10$ | Explicit override of resolution to parent |
| `name` | $R(\text{name}, \text{local}) \rightarrow \text{Super Class (inherited)}$ | `"Parent"` | No shadowing; inherited directly |
| `label` | $R(\text{label}, \text{local}) \rightarrow \text{Sub Class}$ | `"Child"` | Only exists in Sub Class |

## 3.3 The "Constructor Must Be First Statement" Rule — Formal Proof

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** In Java, a call to `super()` or `super(args)` (if explicitly written) MUST be the **first statement** in a Sub Class constructor.

**Proof by Contradiction (Conceptual):**

Assume `super()` is NOT the first statement:

$$ \begin{aligned}
&\text{Child()} \begin{cases}
\texttt{this.y = 200;} & \text{--- (A) Sets child's field} \\
\texttt{super();} & \text{--- (B) Calls parent's constructor} \\
\end{cases}
\end{aligned} $$

At point (A), the parent object is **not yet fully initialized**. The child's code might attempt to use inherited fields (e.g., `this.x`), which would contain **default/garbage values**, leading to undefined behavior. Therefore, Java enforces that the parent must be **completely constructed** before the child's body executes, making `super()` mandatory as the first statement.

**Compiler Enforcement:** If you place `super()` after any other statement, the Java compiler produces the error:

```
error: call to super must be first statement in constructor
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inheritance Hierarchy Diagram (Class Tree)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates a multi-level Super Class-Sub Class hierarchy, showing the **flow of inheritance** and member inheritance:

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeObj["OBJECT"] --> nodeA["Vehicle SuperClass"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["Car SubClass"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Truck SubClass"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["SportsCar SubClass"]
    
    nodeObjDesc["Root Class of Java"] -.-> nodeObj
    nodeADesc["Fields: speed, fuelType"] -.-> nodeA
    nodeBDesc["Fields: modelName, numDoors"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeCDesc["Fields: cargoCapacity, axles"] -.-> nodeC
    nodeDDesc["Fields: maxSpeed, turbo"] -.-> nodeD
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**

- `OBJECT` is the **ultimate Super Class** in Java (every class implicitly extends `Object`).
- `Vehicle` is a **user-defined Super Class** inheriting implicitly from `Object`.
- `Car` and `Truck` are **Sub Classes** of `Vehicle` (single-level inheritance).
- `SportsCar` is a **Sub-Sub Class** of `Car` (multi-level inheritance chain: `Object → Vehicle → Car → SportsCar`).

## 4.2 Memory Layout and `super` Reference Visualization

The following block diagram shows how a Sub Class object is laid out in memory and how the `super` reference navigates between the two portions:

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph objMemory["Child Object in Heap Memory"]
        direction TB
        parentPortion["PARENT PORTION (accessed via super)"]
        childPortion["CHILD PORTION (accessed via this)"]
        
        parentPortion --> parentField1["int x = 50"]
        parentPortion --> parentMethod["void display()"]
        childPortion --> childField1["int y = 75"]
        childPortion --> childMethod["void display() OVERRIDDEN"]
    end
    
    superRef["super reference"] -.-> parentPortion
    thisRef["this reference"] -.-> childPortion
    
    note1["super.display() calls parent's version"] -.-> parentPortion
    note2["this.display() calls child's overridden version"] -.-> childPortion
```

## 4.3 Constructor Chaining Execution Flow (Block Diagram)

This diagram traces the call stack during object creation, showing the **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** nature of constructor execution:

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["new Child arg1 arg2"] --> step1["Step 1: Child constructor entered"]
    step1 --> step2["Step 2: Implicit/Explicit super call"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3: Parent constructor entered"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4: Implicit super call to Object"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5: Object constructor executed"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 6: Parent constructor body runs"]
    step6 --> step7["Step 7: Return to Child"]
    step7 --> step8["Step 8: Child constructor body runs"]
    step8 --> finish["Step 9: Object fully constructed"]
    
    step5 -.->|"Stack unwind begins"| step6
    step6 -.->|"Stack unwind continues"| step8
```

**Reading the Flow:**
- **Downward arrows (1→5):** Call stack grows as we move UP the inheritance hierarchy.
- **Dashed upward arrows (5→9):** Call stack unwinds as each constructor body finishes, moving DOWN the hierarchy.

## 4.4 Access Modifier Decision Flowchart

This flowchart helps determine whether a Sub Class can access a Super Class member based on access modifiers and package relationships:

```mermaid
graph TD
    startQ{"Is the member private?"} -->|Yes| denyA["ACCESS DENIED"]
    startQ -->|No| pkgQ{"Same package?"}
    
    pkgQ -->|Yes| checkDefault{"Is it default?"}
    pkgQ -->|No| checkProtected{"Is it protected?"}
    
    checkDefault -->|Yes| allowA["ACCESS GRANTED"]
    checkDefault -->|No| allowB["ACCESS GRANTED (public)"]
    
    checkProtected -->|Yes| allowC["ACCESS GRANTED (only for Sub Class)"]
    checkProtected -->|No| allowD["ACCESS GRANTED (public)"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a Super Class. How is it related to a Sub Class in Java? Provide a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Super Class** (also called a Base Class or Parent Class) is a class whose properties and methods are inherited by another class. The class that inherits these features is called a **Sub Class** (or Derived Class). The relationship is established in Java using the `extends` keyword, forming an **IS-A** relationship.

For example, if `Animal` is the Super Class and `Dog` extends `Animal`, then `Dog` IS-A `Animal` and inherits all accessible members of `Animal` while potentially adding or overriding behaviors.

```java
class Animal {
    void eat() { System.out.println("Eating..."); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    void bark() { System.out.println("Barking..."); }
}
```

### Question 2: Use of `super` Keyword `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three uses of the `super` keyword in Java with appropriate examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `super` keyword in Java is used in the following three ways:

1. **Accessing Super Class's hidden data members:**
   ```java
   super.variableName;
   ```

2. **Invoking Super Class's overridden methods:**
   ```java
   super.methodName();
   ```

3. **Invoking Super Class's constructor (explicitly):**
   ```java
   super(arguments);
   ```

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of a Super Class in Java with a suitable example. Discuss how the `super` keyword is used to access the members of the Super Class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate constructor chaining between a Super Class and a Sub Class. Show the output and explain the execution flow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Super Class and `super` Keyword

A **Super Class** in Java is the class from which another class inherits features. It is declared using the `extends` keyword by the Sub Class. The `super` keyword serves as a reference to the immediate parent class object and has three uses:

**Use 1: Accessing hidden variables**

```java
class Super {
    int num = 100;
}
class Sub extends Super {
    int num = 200;
    void show() {
        System.out.println("Sub Class num: " + num);     // 200
        System.out.println("Super Class num: " + super.num); // 100
    }
}
```

**Use 2: Calling overridden methods**

```java
class Super {
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Super Class display");
    }
}
class Sub extends Super {
    @Override
    void display() {
        super.display();  // Calls parent version
        System.out.println("Sub Class display");
    }
}
```

**Use 3: Invoking Super Class constructor**

```java
class Sub extends Super {
    Sub() {
        super();  // Calls Super()
        // Sub class initialization
    }
}
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Constructor Chaining Program

**Complete Java Program:**

```java
class Person {
    String name;
    int age;
    
    Person() {
        this("Unknown", 0);
        System.out.println("Person default constructor");
    }
    
    Person(String name, int age) {
        this.name = name;
        this.age = age;
        System.out.println("Person parameterized: " + name + ", " + age);
    }
}

class Student extends Person {
    int rollNo;
    String course;
    
    Student() {
        super();
        System.out.println("Student default constructor");
    }
    
    Student(String name, int age, int rollNo, String course) {
        super(name, age);
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.course = course;
        System.out.println("Student parameterized: " + rollNo + ", " + course);
    }
    
    void display() {
        super.toString();
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Age: " + age);
        System.out.println("Roll: " + rollNo + ", Course: " + course);
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student("Alice", 20, 101, "Computer Science");
        s1.display();
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Person parameterized: Alice, 20
Student parameterized: 101, Computer Science
Name: Alice, Age: 20
Roll: 101, Course: Computer Science
```

**Execution Flow Explanation:**

1. `new Student("Alice", 20, 101, "Computer Science")` invokes the parameterized `Student` constructor.
2. The first statement `super(name, age)` calls `Person(String, int)`.
3. `Person(String, int)` sets `name` and `age`, then prints its message.
4. Control returns to `Student`, which sets `rollNo` and `course`, then prints its message.
5. `s1.display()` prints all inherited and Sub Class fields.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Differentiate between `this` and `super` keywords in Java with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain method overriding in the context of inheritance. Write a Java program where a Sub Class overrides a method of its Super Class and uses `super` to call the parent's version. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Difference Between `this` and `super`

| Feature | `this` keyword | `super` keyword |
|:--------|:---------------|:----------------|
| **Refers to** | Current object of the class | Immediate parent class object |
| **Accesses** | Current class's members | Parent class's hidden/overridden members |
| **Constructor call** | `this()` calls another constructor in the **same class** | `super()` calls a constructor of the **parent class** |
| **Position in constructor** | Must be the **first statement** | Must be the **first statement** |
| **Mutual exclusivity** | Cannot use both `this()` and `super()` in the same constructor | Same restriction applies |
| **Scope** | Limited to the current class hierarchy level | Reaches one level up to the parent |

**Code Example Demonstrating Both:**

```java
class Box {
    int length;
    
    Box(int length) {
        this.length = length;  // 'this' refers to current object's field
    }
}

class ColoredBox extends Box {
    String color;
    
    ColoredBox(int length, String color) {
        super(length);          // 'super' calls parent constructor
        this.color = color;     // 'this' refers to current object's field
    }
    
    void printInfo() {
        System.out.println("Length: " + this.length);
        System.out.println("Length via super: " + super.length);
        System.out.println("Color: " + this.color);
    }
}
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Method Overriding with `super`

**Concept:** Method overriding occurs when a Sub Class provides a **specific implementation** of a method already defined in its Super Class. The `@Override` annotation (optional but recommended) signals compiler intent. Using `super.methodName()` allows the Sub Class to **extend** rather than **replace** the parent's behavior.

**Java Program:**

```java
class Account {
    double balance;
    
    Account(double balance) {
        this.balance = balance;
    }
    
    double calculateInterest() {
        return balance * 0.04;  // 4% standard interest
    }
    
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Balance: ₹" + balance);
        System.out.println("Standard Interest: ₹" + calculateInterest());
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    SavingsAccount(double balance) {
        super(balance);
    }
    
    @Override
    double calculateInterest() {
        double standardInterest = super.calculateInterest();
        double bonus = balance * 0.01;  // Additional 1% for savings
        return standardInterest + bonus;
    }
    
    @Override
    void display() {
        super.display();
        System.out.println("Total Savings Interest: ₹" + calculateInterest());
    }
}

public class BankDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SavingsAccount sa = new SavingsAccount(100000);
        sa.display();
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Balance: ₹100000.0
Standard Interest: ₹4000.0
Total Savings Interest: ₹5000.0
```

**Explanation of `super` Usage in Overriding:**

- `super.calculateInterest()` in `SavingsAccount` calls the **parent's** interest calculation method.
- The Sub Class then **adds** its own bonus logic on top, achieving a **5% total interest** instead of the parent's 4%.
- `super.display()` in `SavingsAccount.display()` invokes the parent's `display()` first, which prints the standard information, then the Sub Class appends the savings-specific output.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Placing `super()` after other statements** — The compiler will reject this. `super()` MUST be the first statement in a Sub Class constructor. (Penalty: 2 marks)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting `super()` when Super Class lacks a default constructor** — If `Parent` has only `Parent(int x)`, then `Child` MUST call `super(x)` explicitly. An implicit `super()` call will cause a compilation error. (Penalty: 2-3 marks)
> 
> 3. **Confusing `super.variableName` with `this.variableName`** — `super` accesses the **parent's** variable (useful when shadowed), while `this` accesses the **current** class's variable. (Penalty: 1-2 marks)
> 
> 4. **Not mentioning the implicit `super()` call** — Even if you don't write `super()`, the compiler inserts it. Examiners expect you to mention this implicit behavior. (Penalty: 1 mark)
> 
> 5. **Writing `super.super()`** — Java does NOT support multi-level super constructor calls directly. You can only call **one level up** using `super()`. (Penalty: 2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before Every Exam:**

- **Definition:** A Super Class is the **parent class** in an inheritance relationship; the Sub Class is the **child class** that inherits from it.
- **Keyword:** Inheritance is established in Java using the `extends` keyword (e.g., `class Sub extends Super`).
- **Three Uses of `super`:**
  - `super.field` → Access parent's hidden variable
  - `super.method()` → Call parent's overridden method
  - `super(args)` → Invoke parent's constructor
- **Constructor Rule:** `super()` is **always called** (implicitly or explicitly) as the **first statement** in a Sub Class constructor — before the Sub Class body executes.
- **Object Class:** Every class in Java implicitly extends `java.lang.Object`, making it the **ultimate Super Class** of the entire Java type system.
- **Single Inheritance:** Java supports only **single inheritance** for classes (one direct parent), but allows **multi-level** chains (A → B → C).
- **Access Modifiers & Inheritance:** `private` members are **NOT** inherited; `protected` and `public` members ARE inherited across packages.
- **Variable Shadowing:** If a Sub Class declares a variable with the same name as the Super Class, the Sub Class's variable **hides** the parent's. Use `super.variableName` to access the parent's version.
- **Method Overriding & `super`:** Use `super.methodName()` inside an overridden method to call the parent's version, enabling **behavior extension** (Template Method Pattern).
- **Compilation vs Runtime:** `super` calls are **resolved at compile time** (static binding), unlike polymorphic method calls which are resolved at **runtime** (dynamic binding).
- **No Multiple Inheritance:** A Java class can extend only ONE Super Class directly (use **interfaces** for multiple inheritance of type).

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> In Java, the **`super`** keyword is a reference variable that is used to refer to the **immediate parent class object**. It serves three primary purposes in Object-Oriented Programming: (1) invoking the **parent class instance variables**, (2) invoking the **parent class methods** (both overridden and hidden), and (3) invoking the **parent class constructor** from within a child class constructor. It is the direct counterpart to the `this` keyword, but `this` refers to the *current* object, whereas `super` refers to the *parent* object.

The `super` keyword is an **implicit reference** automatically created by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) for every instance method and constructor. Unlike `this`, `super` can never be assigned a new value; its meaning is always statically bound to the direct superclass at compile time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Family Tree**
> Think of `super` as a **telephone line to your father**. In a family, a child inherits traits (variables), skills (methods), and a family name (constructor) from the father. But sometimes, the child wants to:
> - **Access the father's original property** (e.g., a hidden locker) that the child also has a copy of → `super.variable`
> - **Call the father's original recipe** (overridden by the child) → `super.method()`
> - **Initialize the father's part of the house** before adding the child's own rooms → `super(...)` constructor call

Just as you cannot change who your father is, `super` is **statically resolved at compile time** and always points to the **immediate parent class**, not grandparents or further up the chain (unless chained through another `super` call inside the parent class).

## 1.3 Three Uses at a Glance

| Use Case | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Parent Variable** | `super.variableName` | Access hidden parent class field |
| **Parent Method** | `super.methodName()` | Invoke overridden parent method |
| **Parent Constructor** | `super(args)` | Initialize parent class portion of object |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:**
> The `super` keyword belongs to the family of *reference keywords* alongside `this`. Both are **non-static** and **cannot be used inside a `static` method, block, or static variable initializer**. This is a frequently tested KTU 2-mark question.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Use Case 1 — `super` to Access Parent Class Variables

When a child class declares a variable with the **same name** as one in the parent class, the child's variable **hides** the parent's variable. To explicitly access the parent's hidden variable, `super.variableName` is used.

### Operational Logic Steps:
1. **Declaration Conflict** → Child class declares a field `int value` when parent already has `int value`.
2. **Name Hiding** → The child's `value` shadows the parent's within the child's scope.
3. **Disambiguation Required** → Using `value` alone refers to the child's copy.
4. **`super.value` Resolves** → The compiler walks up the inheritance chain to the immediate parent and fetches that class's `value`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this needed?**
> Without `super`, polymorphic field access in Java is resolved at **compile time** based on the *reference type*, not the *object type*. This is different from method overriding (which is resolved at runtime). The `super` keyword forces the lookup to start at the parent, bypassing the child's hiding.

## 2.2 Use Case 2 — `super` to Invoke Parent Class Methods

When a child class **overrides** a parent class method, calling the method name directly invokes the child's version. To invoke the parent's overridden version, `super.methodName()` is used. This is commonly seen in:
- **Extending functionality** (child calls parent logic, then adds its own).
- **Specialized constructors** that need to reuse base behavior.

### Operational Logic Steps:
1. **Method Overriding Occurs** → Child class redefines a method inherited from parent.
2. **Direct Call is Polymorphic** → `obj.display()` calls the *overriding* (child) version based on object type.
3. **`super.display()` Forces Parent Call** → Bypasses dynamic dispatch, directly invoking the parent's implementation.

## 2.3 Use Case 3 — `super()` to Invoke Parent Class Constructors

`super()` is used to call a **constructor of the immediate parent class**. It must be the **very first statement** inside a child class constructor. If not written explicitly, the compiler **automatically inserts a no-argument `super()` call**.

### Operational Logic Steps:
1. **Object Construction Begins** → `new ChildClass()` is invoked.
2. **Parent Portion Must Be Initialized** → The parent's data members need to be set up first.
3. **`super(args)` is Called** → Matches a parent constructor based on argument types.
4. **Implicit Default** → If no `super()` is written, the compiler inserts `super();` (no-arg parent constructor).
5. **Constructor Chaining** → If the parent's constructor also needs to call *its* parent, that happens through *its* implicit or explicit `super()`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Constructor Calls:**
> - `super()` **must be the first line** in a constructor. Any code before it causes a **compile-time error**.
> - If the parent class has **only parameterized constructors** and the child doesn't explicitly call `super(args)`, the compiler tries to insert `super();` which **fails to compile** because no matching no-arg parent constructor exists.
> - `super()` and `this()` **cannot both be used** in the same constructor, because both demand the first-line position.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Property | `this` | `super` |
|---|---|---|
| **Refers to** | Current object | Immediate parent class object |
| **Variable Access** | Current class fields | Parent class hidden fields |
| **Method Access** | Current class methods (explicit) | Overridden parent methods |
| **Constructor Call** | `this(args)` — another constructor in same class | `super(args)` — parent class constructor |
| **Position Rule** | First line in constructor (if used) | First line in constructor (if used) |
| **Static Context** | ❌ Not allowed | ❌ Not allowed |
| **Reassignment** | ❌ Cannot assign to `this` | ❌ Cannot assign to `super` |
| **Static Binding** | Dynamic (object-level) | Static (class-level, compile-time) |
| **Inheritance Required** | Not required | Required (must have a parent) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The `super` keyword is foundational in **framework design**, particularly in:
- **Java Collections Framework** — `ArrayList` extends `AbstractList`, which extends `AbstractCollection`. The `super()` chain ensures that each level's internal state is correctly initialized.
- **Swing / JavaFX GUI toolkits** — Custom components extend base classes like `JFrame` or `Application`, calling `super(title)` to set the window title inherited from the parent.
- **Spring Framework Beans** — Custom beans extend infrastructure classes, and the framework relies on the constructor chain to inject dependencies correctly.
- **Android Activity Lifecycle** — `Activity` subclasses call `super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)` to ensure the framework's state restoration logic runs before the developer's custom logic.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough 1 — Variable Hiding Resolution

Consider the following class hierarchy that we will trace through **step by step**.

```java
// PARENT CLASS
class Vehicle {
    int speed = 60;   // parent variable
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle speed: " + speed);
    }
}

// CHILD CLASS
class Car extends Vehicle {
    int speed = 120;  // hides parent's speed
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Car speed: " + speed);          // child value
        System.out.println("Vehicle speed: " + super.speed); // parent value
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation of `new Car().display()`:

**Step 1 — Object Creation:**
The expression `new Car()` triggers the `Car` constructor. The JVM allocates memory for **both** the inherited `Vehicle` portion (containing `speed = 60`) and the `Car` portion (containing `speed = 120`).

**Step 2 — Method Invocation:**
The call `display()` is dispatched to `Car`'s version (dynamic dispatch based on object type `Car`).

**Step 3 — First `println` Statement:**
The unqualified identifier `speed` is resolved using the **reference type's** scope chain. Since we are inside `Car`, the compiler finds `speed` in `Car` first.

$$
\text{output}_1 = \text{"Car speed: "} \;+\; 120 \;=\; \text{"Car speed: 120"}
$$

**Step 4 — Second `println` Statement:**
The expression `super.speed` forces the compiler to begin variable lookup at `Vehicle`. The compiler walks the inheritance chain upwards starting from the **parent class**.

$$
\text{output}_2 = \text{"Vehicle speed: "} \;+\; 60 \;=\; \text{"Vehicle speed: 60"}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Consolidated Output:**
$$
\text{Final Output} = \begin{cases} \text{"Car speed: 120"} \\ \text{"Vehicle speed: 60"} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Compiler Logic Behind `super.speed`:**
> The Java Language Specification (JLS) §15.11.2 defines `super.speed` as a *Primary* expression that is restricted to access non-static members of the superclass. The lookup table used by the compiler for `super.speed` is:
> 1. Search the parent class `Vehicle` for a field named `speed`.
> 2. If found, bind to it.
> 3. If not found in `Vehicle`, continue to `Vehicle`'s parent (grandparent of `Car`).
> `super` **never** searches the current class first — it is a one-way upward tunnel.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough 2 — Overridden Method Invocation via `super`

```java
class Animal {
    void sound() {
        System.out.println("Animal makes a sound");
    }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    void sound() {
        super.sound(); // call parent first
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation of `new Dog().sound()`:

**Step 1 — Dynamic Dispatch:**
The JVM sees an object of type `Dog`. The method `sound()` is **virtual** (instance methods are virtual by default in Java). The vtable lookup resolves to `Dog.sound()`.

**Step 2 — Entering `Dog.sound()`:**
The first executable statement is `super.sound()`. This generates an `invokespecial` JVM bytecode instruction, which **bypasses the vtable** and directly calls `Animal.sound()`.

**Step 3 — Executing `Animal.sound()`:**
Prints `"Animal makes a sound"`. Control returns to the statement *after* `super.sound()` in `Dog.sound()`.

**Step 4 — Continuing in `Dog.sound()`:**
Prints `"Dog barks"`.

**Step 5 — Final Output:**

$$
\text{Final Output} = \begin{cases} \text{"Animal makes a sound"} \\ \text{"Dog barks"} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bytecode Insight — `invokespecial` vs `invokevirtual`:**
> - `invokevirtual` is used for normal polymorphic method calls → respects overriding.
> - `invokespecial` is used for `super.method()`, private methods, and constructors → **bypasses** overriding.
> This is why `super.sound()` reliably calls the *parent's* version regardless of how many levels deep the inheritance chain extends.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough 3 — Constructor Chaining via `super(args)`

```java
class Person {
    String name;
    Person(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        System.out.println("Person constructor: " + name);
    }
}

class Employee extends Person {
    int id;
    Employee(String name, int id) {
        super(name);          // MUST be first line
        this.id = id;
        System.out.println("Employee constructor: " + id);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation of `new Employee("Alice", 101)`:

**Step 1 — Constructor Call:**
The `new Employee("Alice", 101)` expression invokes the `Employee(String, int)` constructor.

**Step 2 — First Statement is `super(name)`:**
The compiler generates an `invokespecial Person.<init>(String)` bytecode instruction **before any other logic** in `Employee`'s constructor body executes.

**Step 3 — `Person` Constructor Executes:**
Inside `Person(String name)`, `this.name = name` assigns `"Alice"` to the parent's `name` field. Prints `"Person constructor: Alice"`.

**Step 4 — Control Returns to `Employee`:**
The line `this.id = id` assigns `101` to the child's `id` field. Prints `"Employee constructor: 101"`.

**Step 5 — Final Output Sequence (chronological):**

$$
\text{Output Sequence} = \begin{cases} \text{"Person constructor: Alice"} \\ \text{"Employee constructor: 101"} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Construction Order Invariant:**
> In Java, an object's construction always proceeds **from the root of the inheritance hierarchy down to the most derived class**. The JVM guarantees that for an object of class $C_n$ in a chain $C_1 \to C_2 \to \dots \to C_n$, the constructors execute in the order $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_n$. This is enforceable because `super()` (implicit or explicit) is always the first statement in every constructor.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for `super` Usage

| Scenario | Code | Resolves To | JVM Instruction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access hidden parent field | `super.speed` | `Vehicle.speed` | `getfield` (with owner = `Vehicle`) |
| Call overridden parent method | `super.sound()` | `Animal.sound()` | `invokespecial` |
| Call parent constructor (no-arg) | `super();` | `Person()` (if exists) | `invokespecial <init>` |
| Call parent constructor (parameterized) | `super("Alice");` | `Person(String)` | `invokespecial <init>(Ljava/lang/String;)` |
| No `super()` written | *(implicit)* | `super();` (no-arg parent) | Auto-inserted `invokespecial <init>` |
| Parent has no no-arg constructor | *(implicit)* | ❌ **Compile error** | — |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: What is the `super` keyword in Java? List its uses.**

**Model Answer:**

The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable that refers to the **immediate parent class object**. It is implicitly provided by the JVM for every non-static method and constructor.
