Whenever you override `equals()`, you **must** also override `hashCode()` to maintain the contract.

### Formal Java Implementation:

```java
@Override
public boolean equals(Object obj) {
    // Step 1: Self-reference check
    if (this == obj) return true;
    
    // Step 2: Null and type check
    if (obj == null || getClass() != obj.getClass()) return false;
    
    // Step 3: Type cast
    Student other = (Student) obj;
    
    // Step 4: Field comparison
    return id == other.id && name.equals(other.name);
}

@Override
public int hashCode() {
    return Objects.hash(id, name);
}
```

## 3.2 Derivation: Mathematical Formulation of `hashCode()` Contract

Given two objects `a` and `b`:

$$
\text{If } a.\text{equals}(b) = \text{true, then } a.\text{hashCode}() = b.\text{hashCode}()
$$

**Logical Equivalence (Truth Table):**

| $a.\text{equals}(b)$ | $a.\text{hashCode}() = b.\text{hashCode}()$ | **Allowed?** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `true` | `true` | ✅ **Required** |
| `true` | `false` | ❌ **Contract Violation** |
| `false` | `true` | ✅ **Allowed (Hash Collision)** |
| `false` | `false` | ✅ **Allowed** |

**Mathematical Justification:**

Let $E(a,b) = 1$ if $a.\text{equals}(b)$ is `true`, else $0$.
Let $H(a) = a.\text{hashCode}()$.

The contract states:

$$
E(a,b) = 1 \implies H(a) = H(b)
$$

The contrapositive is:

$$
H(a) \neq H(b) \implies E(a,b) = 0
$$

**Interpretation:** Two **unequal hash codes** MUST mean the objects are unequal. However, two objects can have **equal hash codes** but still be **unequal** — this is called a **hash collision** and is handled by `HashMap` via separate chaining.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Construction of `toString()` Format

For a class `Employee` with fields `int id`, `String name`, `double salary`:

$$
\text{toString()} = \text{"Employee\{id="} \oplus \text{id} \oplus \text{", name='"} \oplus \text{name} \oplus \text{"', salary="} \oplus \text{salary} \oplus \text{"\}"}
$$

Where $\oplus$ denotes **string concatenation**. The result is a single, well-formatted `String` object.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Override What

| **Scenario** | **Override `toString()`?** | **Override `equals()`?** | **Override `hashCode()`?** |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Value-like class (e.g., `Date`, `Money`) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Entity class with DB ID (`Student`, `Order`) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (by ID) | ✅ Yes |
| Singleton / Utility class | ⚠️ Optional | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Class used in `HashSet` / `HashMap` keys | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ **Mandatory** |
| Class used in `ArrayList` only | ✅ Yes | ✅ Optional | ❌ No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: Define the `Object` class in Java. Why is it called the superclass of all classes?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `Object` class, defined in the `java.lang` package, is the **root of the Java class hierarchy**. It is the **superclass of every class** in Java — whether predefined (like `String`, `Integer`) or user-defined (like `Student`, `Employee`).

It is called the superclass of all classes because **Java does not support multiple inheritance** of classes. To ensure every class shares a **common set of fundamental methods** (like `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()`), Java forces single inheritance from a single root — the `Object` class. The Java compiler **implicitly adds `extends Object`** to any class that does not explicitly extend another class. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2: List any three methods of the `Object` class and state their purpose.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **`toString()`** — Returns a string representation of the object. Default returns `ClassName@hashcode`. Should be overridden for meaningful output. **(1 mark)**

2. **`equals(Object obj)`** — Compares two objects for equality. Default uses `==` (reference comparison). Should be overridden for **content-based equality**. **(1 mark)**

3. **`hashCode()`** — Returns an integer hash value used by hash-based collections like `HashMap` and `HashSet`. The contract states: if `a.equals(b)` is true, then `a.hashCode() == b.hashCode()` must be true. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **one full question** (with sub-parts) per module slot. The following two are **internal choice alternatives**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a) Explain the `equals()` method of the `Object` class in detail. Why is it necessary to override it? Write a Java program to demonstrate overriding of `equals()` for a `Student` class with fields `rollNo` (int) and `name` (String). (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition & Default Behavior:** The `equals()` method is defined in `Object` as:
```java
public boolean equals(Object obj) {
    return (this == obj);
}
```
It uses the `==` operator, which performs **reference comparison** — checks if both references point to the **same memory location**, not whether contents are logically the same. **(1 mark)**

**Why Override is Necessary:**
Consider two `Student` objects with the **same `rollNo` and `name`**. By default, `s1.equals(s2)` returns `false` because they are different objects in memory. For **logical/content equality**, we must override `equals()`. This is critical for using objects in `ArrayList.contains()`, `HashSet`, and `HashMap`. **(2 marks)**

**Java Program:**

```java
class Student {
    int rollNo;
    String name;
    
    Student(int rollNo, String name) {
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.name = name;
    }
    
    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object obj) {
        if (this == obj) return true;              // self check
        if (obj == null || getClass() != obj.getClass()) return false;
        Student s = (Student) obj;
        return rollNo == s.rollNo && name.equals(s.name);
    }
    
    @Override
    public int hashCode() {
        return Objects.hash(rollNo, name);
    }
    
    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Student{rollNo=" + rollNo + ", name='" + name + "'}";
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student(101, "Arjun");
        Student s2 = new Student(101, "Arjun");
        System.out.println(s1.equals(s2));        // true (overridden)
        System.out.println(s1 == s2);             // false (different refs)
    }
}
```
**(4 marks for code — 1 mark for class structure, 1 mark for equals override, 1 mark for hashCode, 1 mark for output explanation)**

---

**b) Discuss the `toString()` method of the `Object` class. What is the default implementation? Demonstrate overriding it with an example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The `toString()` method returns a `String` representation of the object. It is defined in `Object` as:
```java
public String toString() {
    return getClass().getName() + "@" + Integer.toHexString(hashCode());
}
```
The default output looks like `Student@15db9742` — **not human-readable**. **(1 mark)**

**Purpose of Overriding:**
- Enables **meaningful debugging** output.
- Used by **logging frameworks** (Log4j, SLF4J).
- Used by **System.out.println()** implicitly when printing objects.
- Used by **IDE debuggers** (Eclipse, IntelliJ). **(2 marks)**

**Example Program:**

```java
class Book {
    String title;
    String author;
    double price;
    
    Book(String title, String author, double price) {
        this.title = title;
        this.author = author;
        this.price = price;
    }
    
    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return "Book{title='" + title + "', author='" + author + 
               "', price=" + price + "}";
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Book b = new Book("Java Complete Reference", "Herbert Schildt", 750.50);
        System.out.println(b);   // Calls b.toString() implicitly
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Book{title='Java Complete Reference', author='Herbert Schildt', price=750.5}` **(4 marks for code and explanation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a) Explain the contract between `equals()` and `hashCode()` methods. What happens if this contract is violated? Write Java code to demonstrate a correct implementation of both. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The Contract:** When two objects are equal according to the `equals()` method, they **must** have the same hash code. Formally:

$$
\forall a, b : a.\text{equals}(b) = \text{true} \implies a.\text{hashCode}() = b.\text{hashCode}()
$$

Conversely, if hash codes are equal, objects **may or may not** be equal (this is called a **hash collision**). **(2 marks)**

**Consequences of Violation:**
- If `equals()` is overridden but `hashCode()` is **not**, then two "equal" objects can land in **different buckets** of a `HashMap`/`HashSet`.
- The object **cannot be retrieved** from the collection, even though `equals()` confirms it exists.
- Example: `map.get(key)` returns `null` even when `key.equals(storedKey)` is `true`. **(2 marks)**

**Correct Implementation:**

```java
import java.util.Objects;

class Employee {
    int empId;
    String name;
    
    Employee(int empId, String name) {
        this.empId = empId;
        this.name = name;
    }
    
    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object o) {
        if (this == o) return true;
        if (!(o instanceof Employee)) return false;
        Employee e = (Employee) o;
        return empId == e.empId && name.equals(e.name);
    }
    
    @Override
    public int hashCode() {
        return Objects.hash(empId, name);
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e1 = new Employee(1, "Rahul");
        Employee e2 = new Employee(1, "Rahul");
        System.out.println(e1.equals(e2));         // true
        System.out.println(e1.hashCode() == e2.hashCode());  // true
    }
}
```
**(3 marks for code with explanation)**

---

**b) Explain the `getClass()` method and the concept of reflection in Java. How does it relate to the `Object` class? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The `getClass()` method is declared as:
```java
public final Class<?> getClass()
```
It returns the **runtime class** of the object as an instance of `java.lang.Class`. The keyword `final` means it **cannot be overridden**. **(1 mark)**

**How It Works:** When the JVM loads a `.class` file, it creates a `Class<?>` object representing that type. Every object carries a hidden reference to this `Class` object, accessible via `getClass()`. **(1 mark)**

**Uses:**
- **Runtime Type Identification:** `if (obj.getClass() == Student.class)`
- **Reflection:** `obj.getClass().getDeclaredMethods()` lists all methods.
- **Dynamic Instantiation:** `obj.getClass().newInstance()` (deprecated; use `getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance()`).
- **Class Name Retrieval:** `obj.getClass().getName()` returns fully qualified name. **(2 marks)**

**Relation to `Object` Class:** Since every class inherits from `Object`, every object in Java has `getClass()` available. This is the **foundation of Java Reflection API** (`java.lang.reflect` package). It enables frameworks like **Spring, Hibernate, and JUnit** to inspect and manipulate objects at runtime. **(1 mark)**

**Example Program:**

```java
class Vehicle {}
class Car extends Vehicle {}

public class ReflectionDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Vehicle v = new Car();
        System.out.println(v.getClass().getName());   // Car (runtime type)
        System.out.println(v instanceof Vehicle);      // true
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Car` and `true` — demonstrating that `getClass()` returns the **actual subclass** type, not the reference type. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 🔴 **Pitfall 1:** Overriding `equals()` but **forgetting `hashCode()`** — leads to collection bugs. **Loss: 2-3 marks.**
> 
> 🔴 **Pitfall 2:** Using `instanceof` in `equals()` instead of `getClass()` comparison — breaks symmetry contract when subclasses are involved. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 
> 🔴 **Pitfall 3:** Not including `@Override` annotation — does not cause logical error but examiners **deduct 0.5 mark** for not following best practice.
> 
> 🔴 **Pitfall 4:** Confusing `==` (reference comparison) with `.equals()` (content comparison) in answers. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
> 
> 🔴 **Pitfall 5:** Writing `toString()` without mentioning the **default implementation** — examiners expect both default and overridden behavior. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 
> 🔴 **Pitfall 6:** Not mentioning that `getClass()` is `final` and cannot be overridden — frequently missed point. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **`Object` is the root of Java's class hierarchy** — every class implicitly extends it.
- ✅ The class is in the **`java.lang` package** — imported automatically.
- ✅ **`Object` has 11 methods**: `toString`, `getClass`, `equals`, `hashCode`, `clone`, `wait` (×3), `notify`, `notifyAll`, `finalize`.
- ✅ **`getClass()`, `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` are `final`** — cannot be overridden.
- ✅ **`equals()` default uses `==`** — reference comparison, not content.
- ✅ **Override `equals()` → MUST override `hashCode()`** — contract enforcement.
- ✅ **`toString()` default returns `ClassName@hexHashCode`** — rarely useful, always override.
- ✅ **`clone()` requires implementing `Cloneable` interface** — else throws `CloneNotSupportedException`.
- ✅ **`clone()` performs shallow copy by default** — for deep copy, override and clone mutable fields too.
- ✅ **`finalize()` is deprecated since Java 9** — use `try-with-resources` or `AutoCloseable` instead.
- ✅ **`wait()` releases the monitor lock**; `sleep()` does NOT.
- ✅ **`Objects.hash(...)`** utility (from `java.util.Objects`) is the modern way to compute `hashCode()`.
- ✅ **Reflexive, Symmetric, Transitive, Consistent, Non-null** — the 5 properties of `equals()` contract.
- ✅ **Hash collision is allowed**; unequal hash codes for equal objects is **NOT allowed**.
- ✅ **Inheritance from `Object` enables polymorphism** — `Object obj = new Student();` is valid.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Classes and Methods
Using final with Inheritance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Using final with Inheritance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **`final` keyword** in Object-Oriented Programming (primarily Java) is a non-access modifier that, when applied within the context of **inheritance**, restricts the redefinition, extension, or mutation of class members and the class itself. It enforces a strict *terminal* point in the inheritance hierarchy, ensuring design integrity, immutability, and predictable behavior.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEERT613) syllabus, `final` can be used in **three distinct inheritance-related contexts**:

| Context | Syntax | Effect on Inheritance |
|---|---|---|
| Final Class | `final class ClassName` | Class **cannot be inherited** (subclassed) |
| Final Method | `final returnType method()` | Method **cannot be overridden** in child class |
| Final Variable | `final type varName` | Variable behaves as a **constant** (cannot be reassigned) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The `final` keyword is a fundamental tool for enforcing the **Liskov Substitution Principle** boundaries and creating **immutable classes** in Object-Oriented design. It is one of the most frequently asked concepts in KTU module-end evaluations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **royal decree sealed with a wax seal** in a monarchy:

- A **Final Class** is like a **sealed family crest** — no other family lineage is permitted to adopt or extend this family's identity. Once a class is marked `final`, the "bloodline" of inheritance is permanently closed.

- A **Final Method** is like a **non-negotiable rule of a royal house** — every descendant (subclass) must obey this rule exactly as written. They may add their own customs (new methods), but they **cannot alter** this inherited law.

- A **Final Variable** is like a **carved stone tablet** — its inscription is permanent. No one in the family line may change what is written upon it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** In real-world engineering, the `final` keyword acts as a **security guard** at the gates of your class. It prevents accidental or malicious overriding of critical business logic — for instance, you wouldn't want a subclass to redefine how a banking transaction is validated or how a security check is performed.

## 1.3 Standard Java Syntax Patterns (KTU Board Standard)

```java
// 1. Final Class - inheritance blocked
final class ImmutableValue {
    // ...
}

// 2. Final Method - overriding blocked
class Parent {
    final void display() {
        System.out.println("Cannot override me!");
    }
}

// 3. Final Variable - reassignment blocked
class Constants {
    final int MAX_LIMIT = 100;
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** A `final` reference variable means the *reference* cannot change — but the *object it points to* can still be modified internally (unless the object is also of an immutable class). This is a classic KTU examiner trap question.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Final Classes — Blocking Inheritance Entirely

When a class is declared `final`, the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) **rejects any `extends` clause** attempting to inherit from it at compile time. This produces a compile-time error:

```
cannot inherit from final class
```

**Engineering Justification:**
- **Security:** Prevents tampering with sensitive classes (e.g., `String` in Java is `final` to protect its internal character array from malicious modification).
- **Immutability:** Combined with private fields, it produces truly immutable objects — essential for thread safety in multi-threaded applications.
- **Performance:** The JVM can perform certain optimizations on final classes because it knows the class structure will never change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples of `final` classes in Java:** `java.lang.String`, `java.lang.Math`, `java.lang.Integer` (wrapper classes for primitives), `java.lang.System`.

### 2.1.2 Final Methods — Selective Overriding Prevention

A `final` method can be inherited normally by subclasses, but **cannot be redefined (overridden)**. The child class can still:
- Use the method as-is
- Overload it (different parameters)
- Hide it using static context (different concept)

**Engineering Justification:**
- **Template Method Pattern:** Critical steps in an algorithm are marked `final` so subclasses cannot break the algorithm's flow, but they can extend specific hooks.
- **API Stability:** When you distribute a library, marking methods `final` guarantees the behavior of those methods won't change due to user subclassing.

### 2.1.3 Final Variables — Compile-Time Constants

Three sub-cases exist:

1. **Final instance variable:** Must be assigned exactly once, either at declaration, in an initializer block, or in every constructor.
2. **Final static variable:** Must be assigned exactly once, either at declaration or in a static initializer block. Often used to define `public static final` constants.
3. **Final local variable:** Must be assigned exactly once before use, in the method body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blank Final Variable:** A `final` variable that is **not initialized at the time of declaration** is called a "blank final variable." It must be initialized in the constructor (for instance) or static block (for static). This is a common 3-mark KTU question.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Declaration | Inheritable? | Overridable? | Reassignable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Final Class** | `final class A` | **No** | N/A | N/A |
| **Final Method** | `final void m()` | **Yes** | **No** | N/A |
| **Final Variable** | `final int x = 5` | **Yes** (if accessible) | N/A | **No** |
| **Final Parameter** | `void m(final int x)` | N/A | N/A | **No** (within method) |
| **Final + Static** | `public static final double PI = 3.14` | **Yes** | N/A | **No** |
| **Private Method** | `private void m()` | **Not visible** | **Cannot override** | N/A |
| **Static Method** | `static void m()` | **Yes** | **Cannot override** (can hide) | N/A |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The `final` keyword is heavily used in:
> - **Banking software** — to prevent subclasses from altering transaction validation rules.
> - **Cryptographic libraries** — to ensure hashing algorithms cannot be tampered with.
> - **Game engines** — to mark core game-loop methods as `final` so modders cannot break the engine.
> - **Android SDK** — `Activity` lifecycle methods are designed to be overridden, but helper utility classes are `final`.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough: Final Class (Compile-Time Error Demonstration)

### Code Example 1 — The Wrong Way

```java
// File: FinalClassDemo.java

// Step 1: Declare a final class
final class SecureVault {
    private String password = "Admin@123";
    
    public void showPassword() {
        System.out.println("Password: " + password);
    }
}

// Step 2: Attempt to inherit from it
class HackerVault extends SecureVault {  // COMPILE-TIME ERROR HERE
    public void crack() {
        // malicious logic
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step Analysis:**

| Line / Step | What Happens | Compiler Response |
|---|---|---|
| `final class SecureVault` | JVM marks the class as terminal in its symbol table | Class sealed permanently |
| `class HackerVault extends SecureVault` | Compiler checks inheritance chain | **ERROR: cannot inherit from final SecureVault** |
| Compilation halts | `.class` file is NOT generated | Program cannot run |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:** The error occurs at **compile time**, not runtime. This is part of Java's *fail-fast* design philosophy — catch errors as early as possible.

## 3.2 Detailed Walkthrough: Final Method (Overriding Prevention)

### Code Example 2 — The Wrong Way

```java
class Vehicle {
    final void startEngine() {
        System.out.println("Engine started safely with safety checks.");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    // Attempting to override
    void startEngine() {  // COMPILE-TIME ERROR
        System.out.println("Skipping safety checks.");
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step Analysis:**

| Step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parent defines `final void startEngine()` | Method becomes "locked" in the inheritance chain |
| 2 | Child class attempts to redefine it with the **same signature** | Compiler detects override attempt |
| 3 | Compiler emits error | **ERROR: startEngine() in Car cannot override startEngine() in Vehicle; overridden method is final** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice that the method **can still be inherited** (a `Car` object can call `startEngine()`), it just **cannot be redefined** with different behavior. This is the power of selective restriction.

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough: Final Variable (Constant Enforcement)

### Code Example 3 — Demonstrating Blank Final Variable

```java
class Student {
    // Step 1: Blank final instance variable (not initialized here)
    final String rollNumber;
    final String name;
    
    // Step 2: Blank final MUST be initialized in constructor
    Student(String rollNumber, String name) {
        this.rollNumber = rollNumber;   // First and only assignment — OK
        this.name = name;              // First and only assignment — OK
    }
    
    void tryToChange() {
        // rollNumber = "NEW";  // COMPILE-TIME ERROR — cannot assign again
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step Logical Deduction:**

$$
\text{Assignment Count}(\text{final var}) = 1 \text{ (exactly)}
$$

$$
\text{If Assignment Count} > 1 \implies \text{Compile Error: "cannot assign a value to final variable"}
$$

$$
\text{If Assignment Count} = 0 \text{ at end of constructor} \implies \text{Compile Error: "variable might not have been initialized"}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | `final` Class | `final` Method | `final` Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Prevent inheritance | Prevent overriding | Prevent reassignment |
| **Compile-time or Runtime?** | Compile-time | Compile-time | Compile-time |
| **Can be inherited?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (value is copied) |
| **Can be overridden?** | N/A | ❌ No | N/A |
| **Can be overloaded?** | N/A | ✅ Yes | N/A |
| **Memory allocation** | Once (in heap) | Once per class | Once per instance (or once if static) |
| **JVM Optimization** | Method inlining | Method inlining | Constant folding |
| **Used in** | `String`, `Math` | Template Method Pattern | `public static final` constants |

## 3.5 Common Use Case: Immutable Class Design

A complete, production-quality immutable class combines **all three** `final` usages:

```java
// KTU Standard Immutable Class Example
final class Employee {                          // 1. Final class — no inheritance
    private final String name;                  // 2. Final field — no reassignment
    private final int id;
    
    public Employee(String name, int id) {      // 3. Constructor-based initialization
        this.name = name;
        this.id = id;
    }
    
    // Only getters, NO setters
    public String getName() { return name; }
    public int getId() { return id; }
}
```

**Why is this "immutable"?**
- Cannot be subclassed to add mutators (final class).
- Fields cannot be reassigned (final fields).
- No setters exist (design choice).
- **Result:** Once constructed, the object state can never change — thread-safe by default.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Final Keyword Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply final keyword to?] --> B{What element?}
    
    B -->|Class| C[final class MyClass]
    C --> C1[Effect: Cannot be inherited]
    C1 --> C2[Compiler Error on extends]
    C2 --> C3[Example: String, Math]
    
    B -->|Method| D[final void myMethod]
    D --> D1[Effect: Cannot be overridden]
    D1 --> D2[Compiler Error on override]
    D2 --> D3[Can still be inherited and called]
    
    B -->|Variable| E[final type myVar]
    E --> E1{Where declared?}
    E1 -->|Instance field| F1[Init in constructor or initializer block]
    E1 -->|Static field| F2[Init in static block or at declaration]
    E1 -->|Local variable| F3[Init before first use]
    F1 --> G[Reassignment: COMPILE ERROR]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#581c87,stroke:#d8b4fe,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Inheritance Restriction Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph NormalInheritance["NORMAL INHERITANCE (Without Final)"]
        P1[Parent Class] -->|extends| C1[Child Class]
        C1 -->|overrides| M1[Method Redefinition Allowed]
    end
    
    subgraph BlockedInheritance["BLOCKED INHERITANCE (With Final)"]
        P2[final Parent Class] -.->|X BLOCKED| C2[Child Class]
        P3[Parent Class] -->|extends| C3[Child Class]
        C3 -.->|X BLOCKED override| M2[final Method]
    end
    
    style P2 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    style C2 fill:#374151,stroke:#9ca3af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style P3 fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style C3 fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style M2 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Architecture Flow: Immutable Object Lifecycle with Final

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Developer writes final class Employee] --> B[Declare private final fields]
    B --> C[Initialize fields only via constructor]
    C --> D[Provide getters ONLY - no setters]
    D --> E[Compile-time: Java verifies all 3 final rules]
    E --> F[Object created in HEAP memory]
    F --> G{Any attempt to modify?}
    G -->|Inherit| H[BLOCKED - final class]
    G -->|Reassign field| I[BLOCKED - final field]
    G -->|Override behavior| J[BLOCKED - no setters exist]
    H --> K[Immutable Object - Thread Safe]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#14532d,stroke:#86efac,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#fca5a5,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the use of the `final` keyword with classes in Java. Why is the `String` class declared as `final`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `final` keyword, when applied to a class, **prevents the class from being inherited** by any other class. Once a class is declared `final`, no other class can use the `extends` keyword with it, and any attempt to do so results in a **compile-time error**.

**Syntax:**
```java
final class SecureClass {
    // class body
}
```

**Why `String` is `final`:** The `String` class in Java is declared as `final` for the following critical reasons:

1. **Security:** If `String` were inheritable, a malicious subclass could override methods like `equals()` or `hashCode()` to break the string comparison logic used throughout Java (e.g., in network connections, file paths, class loading).

2. **Immutability Guarantee:** `final` ensures that no subclass can introduce mutable behavior, preserving the **immutability** of strings — a foundation of Java's memory model and String Constant Pool optimization.

3. **Thread Safety:** Immutable objects are inherently thread-safe, which is essential since strings are used in concurrent environments.

4. **Performance:** The JVM performs **string interning** optimizations assuming strings are immutable. Without `final`, this optimization would be unsafe.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for syntax, 1 mark for String explanation)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between a `final` method and a `private` method in Java with respect to inheritance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `final` Method | `private` Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Inheritance** | **Can be inherited** by subclasses | **Cannot be inherited** (not visible to subclasses) |
| **Overriding** | **Cannot be overridden** | **Cannot be overridden** (because not inherited) |
| **Accessibility** | Accessible to subclasses | Not accessible to subclasses |
| **Compile-time check** | Explicitly checked for override attempts | Implicitly blocked due to access restriction |
| **Use case** | Template Method Pattern — enforce algorithm steps | Implementation hiding / encapsulation |

**Key Difference:** A `final` method *can* be inherited and called by the subclass, but its implementation cannot be changed. A `private` method *cannot even be seen* by the subclass, so inheritance and overriding are both impossible.

*(Award 1.5 marks for table, 1.5 marks for the key distinguishing statement)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format:** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Explain the different uses of the `final` keyword in Java with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `final` keyword in Java is a non-access modifier that can be applied to **three contexts**:

**1. Final Variable (2.5 marks):**
A `final` variable becomes a **constant** — its value cannot be changed after initialization.
```java
final int MAX_VALUE = 100;
// MAX_VALUE = 200; // ERROR: cannot assign a value to final variable MAX_VALUE
```
- If declared but not initialized, it is called a **blank final variable** and must be initialized in the constructor (for instance variables) or static block (for static variables).

**2. Final Method (2.5 marks):**
A `final` method **cannot be overridden** by any subclass, though it can be inherited.
```java
class Parent {
    final void display() { System.out.println("Parent"); }
}
class Child extends Parent {
    // void display() { } // ERROR: cannot override the final method
}
```

**3. Final Class (2 marks):**
A `final` class **cannot be inherited** at all.
```java
final class Constants { /* ... */ }
// class MathHelper extends Constants { } // ERROR
```

---

**(b) Write a Java program to demonstrate the creation of an immutable class using the `final` keyword. Explain the design principles involved. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
// ImmutableEmployee.java
final class ImmutableEmployee {           // 1. Final class - prevents inheritance
    private final String name;            // 2. Final & private fields
    private final int id;
    private final java.util.Date joiningDate;
    
    public ImmutableEmployee(String name, int id, java.util.Date joiningDate) {
        this.name = name;
        this.id = id;
        // 3. Defensive copy for mutable parameter
        this.joiningDate = new java.util.Date(joiningDate.getTime());
    }
    
    public String getName() { return name; }
    public int getId() { return id; }
    
    // 4. Defensive copy on getter
    public java.util.Date getJoiningDate() {
        return new java.util.Date(joiningDate.getTime());
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        java.util.Date d = new java.util.Date();
        ImmutableEmployee emp = new ImmutableEmployee("Arun", 101, d);
        
        System.out.println("Name: " + emp.getName());
        System.out.println("ID: " + emp.getId());
        System.out.println("Joining Date: " + emp.getJoiningDate());
        
        // emp.getJoiningDate().setYear(2025); // Won't affect original
    }
}
```

**Design Principles Explained:**

1. **Final Class:** Prevents subclassing that could introduce mutability. (1.5 marks)
2. **Final and Private Fields:** Ensures fields cannot be reassigned or directly accessed. (1.5 marks)
3. **No Setter Methods:** Eliminates external modification. (1 mark)
4. **Defensive Copies:** For mutable fields (like `Date`), both the constructor and getter create copies to prevent the original internal state from being modified through external references. (2 marks)
5. **Constructor Initialization:** All fields initialized only once through the constructor. (1 mark)

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) What is a blank final variable in Java? Explain with an example. How is it different from a regular final variable? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **blank final variable** is a `final` variable that is **declared but not initialized at the time of declaration**. It must be assigned a value exactly once, and this assignment is permitted only in specific places depending on whether the variable is an instance or static variable.

**Example Program:**

```java
class BankAccount {
    // Blank final instance variable
    final String accountHolder;
    final String accountNumber;
    final double interestRate;
    
    // Regular final variable (initialized at declaration)
    final String bankName = "KTU Bank";
    
    // Constructor: blank final variables MUST be initialized here
    BankAccount(String holder, String number, double rate) {
        accountHolder = holder;     // Initialization of blank final
        accountNumber = number;     // Initialization of blank final
        interestRate = rate;        // Initialization of blank final
    }
    
    void display() {
        // interestRate = 5.0; // ERROR: cannot reassign final
        System.out.println(accountHolder + " | " + accountNumber + " | " + interestRate);
    }
}
```

**Difference Table (2.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Regular Final Variable | Blank Final Variable |
|---|---|---|
| **Initialization** | Done at the time of declaration | Done later (in constructor / static block) |
| **Readability** | Value visible immediately in code | Value assigned dynamically, often constructor parameters |
| **Flexibility** | Static value | Can differ per object (instance) or per class loading (static) |
| **Use case** | Universal constants | Per-object constants whose value comes from constructor args |

**Key Points:**
- A blank final instance variable **must be initialized in every constructor** of the class. (1.5 marks)
- A blank final static variable **must be initialized in a static initializer block**. (1 mark)
- Once initialized, a blank final variable behaves identically to a regular final variable — no reassignment allowed. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Discuss the advantages and limitations of using the `final` keyword in inheritance. Provide a real-world scenario where final classes are preferred. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages (3.5 marks):**

1. **Security:** Prevents malicious or accidental overriding of critical business logic. For example, a banking system can mark the `validateTransaction()` method as `final` to ensure no subclass can disable fraud checks.

2. **Immutability:** When combined with private fields, `final` creates truly immutable objects that are inherently thread-safe — critical in multi-threaded server applications.

3. **Performance Optimization:** The JVM can perform **method inlining** for final methods because it knows the method dispatch will not change at runtime. This results in faster execution.

4. **API Stability:** Library developers can mark classes/methods as `final` to guarantee that distributed libraries will not behave differently due to user-side inheritance tricks.

5. **Design Intent Communication:** It clearly signals to other developers that "this is a terminal implementation — do not extend or modify."

**Limitations (2 marks):**

1. **Reduced Extensibility:** Once a class is `final`, you cannot use polymorphism through inheritance on it, limiting flexibility in framework design.

2. **Harder to Mock in Testing:** Unit testing frameworks often need to create mock subclasses; `final` classes make this impossible without tools like Mockito's `mockito-inline`.

3. **Inflexibility in Frameworks:** Some design patterns (like Strategy or Template Method) become harder to implement when key classes are sealed.

**Real-World Scenario: Java's `String` Class (1.5 marks):**

The `String` class is declared `final` because:
- Strings are used as **keys in HashMap/HashSet** — if mutable, the hashcode could change, breaking hash-based data structures.
- Strings are used in **class loading** (e.g., `Class.forName("com.example.Foo")`) — mutability would be a massive security risk.
- String constants in the **String Constant Pool** rely on immutability for memory optimization.

**Sample Code Illustration:**
```java
final class SecurityToken {
    private final String tokenId;
    private final long expiryTime;
    
    public SecurityToken(String id, long expiry) {
        this.tokenId = id;
        this.expiryTime = expiry;
    }
    
    public boolean isValid() {
        return System.currentTimeMillis() < expiryTime;
    }
}
// No subclass can override isValid() to bypass expiry — security enforced.
```

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating `final` with `finally` and `finalize`:** These three are completely different. `final` is a keyword, `finally` is a block for cleanup, and `finalize()` is a deprecated GC method. Writing "finally" instead of "final" in your answer will fetch **zero marks**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting that `final` reference ≠ immutable object:** Many students incorrectly write that a `final` array's contents cannot change. Wrong — only the reference is locked. Elements can still be modified: `final int[] arr = {1,2,3}; arr[0] = 99;` is **legal**.
> 
> 3. **Confusing "cannot be inherited" with "cannot be used":** A `final` class cannot be inherited, but its objects can still be instantiated and used normally. Don't write "final class cannot be used" — that's a **major mark-deduction error**.
> 
> 4. **Missing the "exactly once" rule:** A `final` variable must be assigned **exactly once**. Students often write "at least once" or "only once at declaration" — both are wrong. Blank final variables are the exception, requiring initialization in the constructor.
> 
> 5. **Thinking `private` methods are also `final` by default:** While true that private methods cannot be overridden, this is because they are not inherited — **not** because of a `final` designation. In your answer, distinguish clearly between the two reasons.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets — Memorize These:**

- ✅ `final` is a **non-access modifier** in Java used to restrict modification.
- ✅ A `final` **class** cannot be inherited (cannot use `extends`).
- ✅ A `final` **method** can be inherited but cannot be overridden.
- ✅ A `final` **variable** must be assigned exactly once and cannot be reassigned.
- ✅ A **blank final variable** is declared without initialization and assigned in the constructor (instance) or static block (static).
- ✅ `final` reference variable ≠ immutable object (object's internal state can still mutate).
- ✅ `final` and `static` together create a **compile-time constant** (e.g., `public static final double PI = 3.14159`).
- ✅ `String`, `Math`, and all wrapper classes (`Integer`, `Double`, etc.) in Java are `final`.
- ✅ `final` enables **JVM optimizations** like method inlining and constant folding.
- ✅ Use `final` for: **immutability, security, API stability, performance, design clarity**.
- ✅ The error from misusing `final` is a **compile-time error** (fail-fast principle).
- ✅ `final` is **NOT** the same as `finally` (exception block) or `finalize()` (GC method).
- ✅ `private` methods *cannot* be overridden, but this is due to access restriction, not `final`.
- ✅ `final` does **NOT** affect method **overloading** — only **overriding**.
- ✅ All `final` rules are **enforced at compile time** by the Java compiler (`javac`).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packages and Interfaces - Defining Package
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Packages and Interfaces - Defining Package # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)** > A **Package** in Java is a **namespace** that organizes a set of related classes, interfaces, enumerations, and sub-packages. It acts as a **container** or a **logical folder** in the Java runtime environment that provides: > 1. **Name-Space Management** — preventing class name conflicts (two classes with the same name can exist in different packages). > 2. **Access Protection** — controlling visibility via access modifiers (`public`, `protected`, `default`, `private`). > 3. **Encapsulation at a Higher Level** — grouping functionally or logically related types together. > 4. **Reusability & Maintainability** — facilitating modular software design. The **Java Platform Package Specification** (per JLS §7.1) defines a package as: > *"A package is a grouping of related types providing access protection and name-space management."* A package is **declared** using the `package` keyword as the **first statement** in a Java source file (excluding comments and blank lines). ```java package university.department.cse; public class Student { ... } ``` ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: The Office Filing System** > > Imagine a **large multinational company** with thousands of employees. If everyone's name and personal file were dumped into a single global drawer called "Employees", finding anything would be a nightmare. To solve this, the company uses **departments**: > - `Accounts` department > - `HumanResources` department > - `Engineering` department > > Now, two employees named "John" can exist — one in Accounts, one in Engineering — identified uniquely as `Accounts.John` and `Engineering.John`. The departments also enforce **clearance rules** (an intern in HR cannot open confidential salary files from Accounts). > > A **Java Package** is exactly this departmental system: > - The **package name** is the department name. > - The **classes inside** are the employees. > - The **access modifiers** are the security clearance levels. > - The **fully qualified class name** is `Department.Employee` (e.g., `java.util.ArrayList`). ## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standards | Metric | Standard Value | Description | |---|---|---| | **Default Java API Package** | `java.lang` | Auto-imported into every Java program | | **Package Naming Convention** | Reverse-DNS (e.g., `com.ktu.cse`) | Globally unique identifier | | **Lowercase Convention** | All lowercase | Mandated by Java Code Conventions | | **Compilation Unit Limit** | **One** `public` class per `.java` file | Enforced by the JLS | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — How a Package is Created > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU board expects a **3-step mental model** for any "Defining a Package" question. Memorize this. ### Step 1: The `package` Declaration Statement - The **first non-comment, non-blank statement** in the source file. - Syntax: `package ;` - A source file can declare **only one** package. - If omitted, the class belongs to the **unnamed/default package** (a "nameless" workspace, discouraged in production). ### Step 2: Directory Structure Mapping - The Java compiler (`javac`) and runtime (`java`) **map package names to directory paths** (on the filesystem). - The dot `.` in the package name translates to a folder separator (`/` on Unix, `\` on Windows). $$ \text{Package: } \texttt{com.ktu.cse.oop} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Path: } \texttt{com/ktu/cse/oop/} $$ ### Step 3: Compilation & Execution Cycle 1. Save the file `MyClass.java` inside the folder `com/ktu/cse/oop/`. 2. Compile: `javac com/ktu/cse/oop/MyClass.java` → produces `MyClass.class`. 3. Execute: `java com.ktu.cse.oop.MyClass`. ## 2.2 Types of Packages in Java | Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Built-in (Java API) Packages** | Pre-defined by JDK/JRE | `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.net` | | **User-defined Packages** | Created by the programmer for project organization | `com.ktu.banking.accounts` | | **Default (Unnamed) Package** | No `package` statement; classes placed in a "root" directory | (No name) — **not recommended for projects** | ## 2.3 Package Naming Conventions (Industry-Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Sun/Oracle Java Code Conventions Mandate:** > - Package names are written in **all lowercase** to avoid conflict with class names. > - Companies use their **reversed Internet domain name** to ensure global uniqueness. > - Example: A company with domain `ktu.ac.in` creating a CSE project package would use `in.ac.ktu.cse.`. $$ \text{Fully Qualified Name (FQN)} = \text{Package Name} \; . \; \text{Class Name} $$ ## 2.4 The CLASSPATH Variable — The Engine's Map > [!IMPORTANT] > The **CLASSPATH** is an environment variable that tells the JVM and `javac` **where to look for user-defined packages**. | Environment | Purpose | |---|---| | `javac` (Compiler) | Searches CLASSPATH to **find referenced classes** during compilation | | `java` (JVM) | Searches CLASSPATH to **load classes** at runtime | - Default value: Current directory (`.`) - Can be set using: `set CLASSPATH=.;C:\myclasses` (Windows) or `export CLASSPATH=.:/home/myclasses` (Linux) - From Java 6+, you can use the **`-cp`** flag: `java -cp .;C:\myclasses MyProgram` ## 2.5 Access Protection in Packages > [!NOTE] > The four standard Java access modifiers interact with packages as follows: | Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclass (Different Pkg) | Other Packages | |---|---|---|---|---| | `private` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | **default** (no modifier) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | | `protected` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | | `public` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | **Key Insight for KTU**: The **default** (package-private) access is the *only* modifier that grants visibility to the entire package but nowhere else. This is the package-level encapsulation mechanism. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Enterprise Applications**: Microservices and large-scale Java EE / Spring Boot apps use package layering like `com.company.app.controller`, `com.company.app.service`, `com.company.app.repository`. - **Library Development**: Apache, Google Guava, and the JDK itself rely on packages to ship millions of lines of code without naming collisions. - **Team Collaboration**: Multiple developers can work on `com.teamA.utils` and `com.teamB.utils` simultaneously without interference. - **Code Maintainability**: Refactoring a module becomes "move this folder" rather than "rewrite this code". # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: From Source Code to a Compiled Package > [!NOTE] > The KTU board often asks: *"Explain the procedure to create and use a user-defined package with a sample program."* Below is the **canonical 5-step derivation** that scores full marks. ### Step 1 — Project Directory Layout Create the package folder structure matching the FQN. Suppose the fully qualified name is `com.ktu.cse.oop`. ``` ProjectRoot/ └── com/ └── ktu/ └── cse/ └── oop/ ├── Calculator.java └── CalculatorDemo.java ``` ### Step 2 — Define the Package Member Class The class `Calculator` resides in `com.ktu.cse.oop`. Save the following as `Calculator.java`: ```java package com.ktu.cse.oop; public class Calculator { public int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } public int multiply(int a, int b) { return a * b; } } ``` **Logic behind each line:** - `package com.ktu.cse.oop;` — The **mandatory** first statement. Tells the compiler the class belongs to this namespace. - `public class Calculator` — Must be `public` to be accessible from outside the package. **One** `public` class per file is enforced. ### Step 3 — Define the Driver Class (Same Package or Different) Save as `CalculatorDemo.java` in the **same** package directory: ```java package com.ktu.cse.oop; public class CalculatorDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Calculator calc = new Calculator(); System.out.println("Sum = " + calc.add(10, 20)); System.out.println("Product = " + calc.multiply(10, 20)); } } ``` **Logic:** Since both classes are in the **same package**, no `import` statement is needed. The `Calculator` class is directly visible to `CalculatorDemo`. ### Step 4 — Compilation Logic Open a terminal inside `ProjectRoot` and execute: ```bash javac -d . com/ktu/cse/oop/Calculator.java javac -d . com/ktu/cse/oop/CalculatorDemo.java ``` The `-d .` flag instructs `javac` to **create the package directory structure under the current directory** based on the declared package name. **Resulting folder structure post-compilation:** ``` ProjectRoot/ └── com/ └── ktu/ └── cse/ └── oop/ ├── Calculator.class └── CalculatorDemo.class ``` ### Step 5 — Execution Logic Run from the `ProjectRoot`: ```bash java com.ktu.cse.oop.CalculatorDemo ``` **Output:** ``` Sum = 30 Product = 200 ``` The JVM uses the **fully qualified class name** to locate the `.class` file by converting dots to slashes and searching the **CLASSPATH**. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: The 3 Access Scenarios > [!NOTE] > A frequently tested KTU question: *"Demonstrate accessing a class from the same package, a sub-package, and a different package."* ### Scenario A — Same Package Access (No Import Needed) **File:** `com/ktu/cse/oop/Helper.java` ```java package com.ktu.cse.oop; public class Helper { public void display() { System.out.println("Helper from com.ktu.cse.oop"); } } ``` **File:** `com/ktu/cse/oop/Main.java` ```java package com.ktu.cse.oop; public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { Helper h = new Helper(); // direct access, no import h.display(); } } ``` **Reasoning:** Classes within the same package share an implicit visibility — Java's compiler automatically resolves the reference. ### Scenario B — Sub-Package Access (Import Required) A sub-package is **logically distinct** from its parent, even though it shares a name prefix. Sub-packages do **not** inherit access to each other's package-private members. **File:** `com/ktu/cse/oop/advanced/AdvancedHelper.java` ```java package com.ktu.cse.oop.advanced; import com.ktu.cse.oop.Helper; // explicit import required public class AdvancedHelper { public void invoke() { Helper h = new Helper(); h.display(); } } ``` **Reasoning:** `com.ktu.cse.oop.advanced` is a **separate namespace**. Java does **not** treat it as nested. The `import` statement is mandatory to bring `Helper` into the local namespace. ### Scenario C — Wildcard & Fully Qualified Import **File:** `com/ktu/cse/oop/Main2.java` ```java package com.ktu.cse.oop; import com.ktu.cse.oop.advanced.*; // wildcard import public class Main2 { public static void main(String[] args) { AdvancedHelper ah = new AdvancedHelper(); ah.invoke(); } } ``` **Reasoning:** The `*` wildcard imports **all public classes** from `com.ktu.cse.oop.advanced`. It does **not** recursively import sub-sub-packages. ## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Why Default Package is Discouraged The **unnamed package** (no `package` statement) has the following KTU-validated disadvantages: 1. **Cannot be imported** — Classes from the unnamed package are inaccessible to any named package. 2. **No encapsulation boundary** — All `default`-access classes become globally visible within the unnamed-package directory. 3. **Compilation tooling fails** — Modern build systems (Maven, Gradle) require a named package declaration. 4. **Breaks modularity** — Violates the OOP principle of grouping by functionality. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** If asked *"What happens if you omit the package statement?"*, your answer must include the term **"default package"** and state that it is **invisible to any named package**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Package Creation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Decide Package Name
e.g. com.ktu.cse.oop] --> B[Step 2: Create Directory Structure
com/ktu/cse/oop/] B --> C[Step 3: Write Source Files
with package statement as line 1] C --> D[Step 4: Compile with javac -d .
generates .class files] D --> E[Step 5: Set CLASSPATH
or use -cp flag] E --> F[Step 6: Run with java FQN
java com.ktu.cse.oop.Main] F --> G[Program Output] ``` ## 4.2 Package Hierarchy Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[Java Platform] JROOT[java] JROOT --> JLANG[java.lang
auto-imported] JROOT --> JUTIL[java.util] JROOT --> JIO[java.io] JROOT --> JNET[java.net] JROOT --> JSQL[java.sql] USER[User Defined] USER --> COM[com] COM --> KTU[ktu] KTU --> CSE[cse] CSE --> OOP[oop] CSE --> DS[datastructures] OOP --> CALC[Calculator.class] OOP --> HELPER[Helper.class] DS --> LINKED[LinkedList.class] ``` ## 4.3 Access Control Flow Within a Package ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Class A in pkg X] -->|public| B[Class B in pkg X] A -->|default| C[Same Package Members] A -.->|not visible by default| D[Class D in pkg Y] A -.->|protected + inheritance| E[Subclass in pkg Y] D -->|requires import| A style D stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style E stroke-dasharray: 5 5 ``` ## 4.4 Compilation Pipeline (javac → java) ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Source File
MyClass.java] -->|javac -d| BYTECODE[Bytecode
MyClass.class] BYTECODE -->|placed in| PKG[Package Directory
matching FQN] PKG -->|java FQN| JVM[JVM ClassLoader] CLASSPATH[CLASSPATH / -cp] -->|search path| JVM JVM --> OUTPUT[Program Execution] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a package in Java. What is the significance of the `package` statement?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A package in Java is a namespace that organizes a set of related classes and interfaces, preventing name conflicts and providing access control. The `package` statement, which must be the first statement in a Java source file, declares the namespace to which the class belongs. It maps the class to a directory structure on the filesystem using a fully qualified name (e.g., `com.ktu.cse.oop`). **(3 marks)** ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the role of the CLASSPATH environment variable in Java?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The CLASSPATH tells the Java compiler (`javac`) and the Java Virtual Machine (`java`) **where to find user-defined packages and classes** on the filesystem. It is a sequence of directories and JAR files searched in order. By default, it includes the current directory (`.`). If the CLASSPATH is not set correctly, the program fails with a `ClassNotFoundException` or `NoClassDefFoundError` at runtime. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain the concept of packages in Java. Discuss the advantages of using packages with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program to create a user-defined package named `com.ktu.geometry` containing a class `Circle` with a method `area()` that returns the area of the circle. Demonstrate how to access this class from another package using the `import` statement. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Packages: Concept & Advantages **(7 marks)** **Concept (3 marks):** A package is a **namespace** in Java used to group related classes, interfaces, and sub-packages. Declared using the `package` keyword as the first statement in a source file, it maps a class to a directory structure on the filesystem. Java provides built-in packages like `java.lang`, `java.util`, and `java.io`, and allows programmers to define their own. **Advantages (4 marks):** 1. **Name-Space Management** — Two classes with the same name can exist in different packages (e.g., `java.util.Date` and `java.sql.Date`). 2. **Access Protection** — Default and `protected` access modifiers work at the package level. 3. **Logical Grouping** — Related classes are kept together (e.g., all data structures in `java.util`). 4. **Reusability** — Packages can be distributed as JARs and reused across projects. 5. **Maintainability** — Modular structure simplifies debugging and refactoring. #### Part (b) — Program: `com.ktu.geometry.Circle` **(7 marks)** **Step 1: Folder Structure** ``` Project/ └── com/ └── ktu/ └── geometry/ ├── Circle.java └── TestCircle.java ``` **Step 2: `Circle.java` (in `com/ktu/geometry/`)** ```java package com.ktu.geometry; public class Circle { private double radius; public Circle(double radius) { this.radius = radius; } public double area() { return Math.PI * radius * radius; } public double getRadius() { return radius; } } ``` **Step 3: `TestCircle.java` (in a different folder, e.g., `Project/test/`)** ```java package test; import com.ktu.geometry.Circle; // explicit import public class TestCircle { public static void main(String[] args) { Circle c = new Circle(7.0); System.out.println("Radius = " + c.getRadius()); System.out.println("Area = " + c.area()); } } ``` **Step 4: Compilation & Execution** ```bash javac -d . com/ktu/geometry/Circle.java javac -d . test/TestCircle.java java test.TestCircle ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Radius = 7.0 Area = 153.93804002589985 ``` --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the directory structure mapping of a Java package. How does the `-d` flag affect compilation? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between accessing a class from the **same package**, a **sub-package**, and a **different package** with code examples. What is the role of the wildcard `*` import? **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Directory Mapping & `-d` Flag **(7 marks)** **Directory Mapping (4 marks):** Java's package name maps directly to a directory path. Each dot (`.`) in the package name becomes a folder separator. For the package `com.ktu.cse.oop`, the path is `com/ktu/cse/oop/`. ``` C:\Project\ └── com\ └── ktu\ └── cse\ └── oop\ ├── Student.java └── Student.class (after compilation) ``` The source file `Student.java` is saved in this directory, and after compilation, `Student.class` is also placed here. **The `-d` Flag (3 marks):** The `-d` flag in `javac` specifies the **destination directory** for compiled `.class` files. When you compile from a parent directory using `javac -d . ...`, the compiler **automatically creates the package folder hierarchy** under `.` and places each `.class` file in its corresponding subdirectory. Without `-d`, the `.class` files are placed in the same directory as the source. ```bash javac -d . com/ktu/cse/oop/Student.java ``` This command ensures that the compiled class is stored at `./com/ktu/cse/oop/Student.class`, preserving the package structure. #### Part (b) — Access Scenarios & Wildcard Import **(7 marks)** **Same Package Access (2 marks):** Classes in the same package have implicit access to each other. No `import` is needed. ```java package com.ktu.cse.oop; public class A { public void msg() { System.out.println("From A"); } } package com.ktu.cse.oop; public class B { public static void main(String[] args) { A obj = new A(); // no import needed obj.msg(); } } ``` **Sub-Package Access (2 marks):** Sub-packages are **separate namespaces** and require an explicit `import` statement. ```java package com.ktu.cse.oop.advanced; import com.ktu.cse.oop.A; // mandatory public class C { public static void main(String[] args) { A obj = new A(); obj.msg(); } } ``` **Different (Unrelated) Package Access (2 marks):** A fully qualified name can be used **without** an `import` statement, though importing is preferred for readability. ```java package com.example.app; public class D { public static void main(String[] args) { com.ktu.cse.oop.A obj = new com.ktu.cse.oop.A(); obj.msg(); } } ``` **Wildcard `*` Import (1 mark):** The statement `import com.ktu.cse.oop.*;` imports **all public classes** from `com.ktu.cse.oop`. It does **not** recurse into sub-packages. It reduces code clutter when many classes from one package are used. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting `package` as line 1**: Students often place imports or comments above `package`. *Remember:* `package` must be the **first non-comment, non-blank statement**, but comments above are allowed — imports are **not**. > 2. **Confusing sub-packages with nested access**: A frequent error is assuming `com.ktu.cse.oop.advanced` can access package-private members of `com.ktu.cse.oop`. **It cannot** — sub-packages are independent namespaces. > 3. **Misplacing `.class` files**: Forgetting the `-d .` flag or compiling from the wrong directory leads to `NoClassDefFoundError`. Always compile from the **package root** directory. > 4. **Capitalization errors**: Package names must be **lowercase**. `Com.Ktu` is a *different* package from `com.ktu`. This is a 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation. > 5. **Forgetting the FQN in `java` command**: Running `java CalculatorDemo` instead of `java com.ktu.cse.oop.CalculatorDemo` is a common mistake. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Defining a Package"** > > - 🔑 A **package** = **namespace + directory** in Java. > - 🔑 Declared using `package ;` as the **first statement** of a source file. > - 🔑 Package name ↔ Directory path: dots (`.`) become folder separators. > - 🔑 One **public** class per `.java` file (enforced by JLS). > - 🔑 **CLASSPATH** tells the compiler/JVM where to find user-defined packages. > - 🔑 The `javac -d` flag auto-creates the package folder hierarchy. > - 🔑 **Built-in packages**: `java.lang` (auto-imported), `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.net`, `java.sql`. > - 🔑 **Default package** (no `package` statement) cannot be imported by named packages. > - 🔑 **Access modifiers** interact with packages: `default` = same package only; `protected` = package + subclasses. > - 🔑 **Sub-packages are independent** — they do not inherit package-private access from parents. > - 🔑 **Wildcard import** `pkg.*` imports all public classes of `pkg` (not recursive). > - 🔑 **Naming convention**: all lowercase, reverse-DNS for global uniqueness (e.g., `in.ac.ktu.cse`). > - 🔑 Run with: `java ` from the package root. > - 🔑 Common compile flag: `javac -d . ` to preserve package structure.

topics.title: CLASSPATH
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CLASSPATH

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CLASSPATH** is an **environment variable** (and a command-line argument) used by the **Java Compiler (`javac`)** and the **Java Virtual Machine (`java` / `java -jar`)** to locate user-defined classes, interfaces, compiled `.class` files, and packages on the local file system during the **compilation** and **runtime** phases of a Java program.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **OEERT613 (Object Oriented Concepts)**, CLASSPATH is the **search path** that supplements the Java runtime's **Bootstrap Class Path** (which contains core Java API classes such as `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`) with the locations of **third-party libraries** and **application-specific compiled classes**.

The **CLASSPATH environment variable** can point to:
1. **Directories** containing `.class` files (root of a package structure).
2. **JAR (Java ARchive) files** — compressed archives of `.class` files and resources.
3. **ZIP files** — used similarly to JAR files (legacy support).

The default value of CLASSPATH is a single dot: **"."** which represents the **current working directory**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of CLASSPATH as a LIBRARIAN'S DIRECTORY CARD SYSTEM.**

Imagine you walk into a massive, multi-building library (the JVM) and ask the librarian, *"Please give me the book titled `MyCalculator.class`."* The librarian cannot search every shelf in every building — that would take forever. Instead, the librarian checks a **registered list of buildings, floors, and shelves** that are known to contain the books you might request.

That registered list is the **CLASSPATH**.

- The **dot (`.`)** means "look at the desk I am standing at right now" (current directory).
- A **directory path** like `C:\MyProject\bin` means "there is a known shelf in this building — check it first."
- A **JAR file** like `C:\libs\mysql-connector.jar` means "there is a sealed box of books — open the box and search inside."

If the librarian (JVM) cannot find the requested book (`class`) in **any** of the listed locations, it throws the infamous:

```
java.lang.NoClassDefFoundError
```

or during compilation:

```
error: cannot find symbol
```

So in the simplest terms:
- **PATH** tells the operating system *where to find programs to run* (e.g., `javac.exe`).
- **CLASSPATH** tells the JVM *where to find Java classes to load* (e.g., `MyCalculator.class`).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Highlighted)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **CLASSPATH** | Search path for Java classes |
| **`.`** (dot) | Default current working directory |
| **`.class` file** | Compiled Java bytecode file |
| **JAR** | Java ARchive — packaged set of `.class` files |
| **`java.lang.NoClassDefFoundError`** | Runtime error if class missing on CLASSPATH |
| **`ClassNotFoundException`** | Thrown when `Class.forName()` cannot locate a class |
| **Bootstrap Class Path** | Built-in JDK class locations (e.g., `rt.jar`) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 Why CLASSPATH is Required — The Operational Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Java programs are distributed as **bytecode (`.class` files)** that must be located by the JVM. Unlike C/C++ where the linker resolves symbols at link time, Java resolves classes **lazily, on-demand, at runtime** (dynamic loading). This mechanism is governed by the **ClassLoader subsystem**.

When a Java program references another class (e.g., `MyHelper obj = new MyHelper();`), the following sequence occurs:

1. The **JVM's ClassLoader** is invoked.
2. The ClassLoader checks the **Bootstrap ClassLoader** → loads core Java API classes (`java.lang.*`, `java.util.*`) from `rt.jar` / `jrt-fs`.
3. If not found → checks the **Extension ClassLoader** → loads from `<JAVA_HOME>/lib/ext`.
4. If not found → checks the **System/Application ClassLoader** → searches the **CLASSPATH** entries.
5. If still not found → `ClassNotFoundException` is thrown.

**CLASSPATH is the contract between the user application and the System ClassLoader.**

---

## 2.2 The CLASSPATH Search Order (KTU High-Yield)

The JVM searches class locations in this **strict order**:

| Priority | ClassLoader | Source | Configured By |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bootstrap ClassLoader** | `rt.jar`, `jrt-fs`, core API | JVM internals (immutable) |
| 2 | **Platform ClassLoader** (Java 9+) | `java.sql`, `javax.*` extension modules | JVM internals |
| 3 | **System/Application ClassLoader** | **CLASSPATH entries** | User / environment / `-cp` flag |
| 4 | **User ClassLoader** (custom) | Programmatic (`URLClassLoader`) | Application code |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU exam:** The Bootstrap ClassLoader is **always searched first**. You **cannot** override core classes (e.g., `java.lang.String`) using CLASSPATH — this is a deliberate security feature of the JVM.

---

## 2.3 Syntax for Setting CLASSPATH

### A. As an Environment Variable (Windows — Permanent)

```bat
set CLASSPATH=.;C:\myproject\bin;C:\libs\mysql.jar
```

### B. As an Environment Variable (Linux / macOS)

```bash
export CLASSPATH=.:/home/user/myproject/bin:/opt/libs/mysql.jar
```

### C. Command-Line Flag (Transient — **Preferred by KTU**)

```bash
# Compile
javac -cp ".;C:\libs\*" MyProgram.java

# Run
java -cp ".;C:\libs\*" MyProgram
```

The `-cp` (or `-classpath`) flag **overrides** the `CLASSPATH` environment variable for that single invocation. The dot (`.`) explicitly includes the current directory.

---

## 2.4 The Wildcard `*` Notation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When CLASSPATH points to a **directory**, you can use `*` to include **all JAR files** inside that directory (but NOT subdirectories — Java does not recurse).

| Syntax | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `C:\libs\*` | Includes **every `.jar` file** inside `C:\libs\` |
| `C:\libs\*;C:\more\*` | Multiple wildcard directories (separated by `;` on Windows, `:` on Linux) |
| `C:\libs\*\*` | **INVALID** — no recursive wildcard in standard Java CLASSPATH |
| `*.jar` alone | **INVALID** — must include the parent directory path |

---

## 2.5 PATH vs CLASSPATH (The Classic KTU 2-Mark Question)

> [!NOTE]
> This distinction is **extremely high-yield** in KTU exams. It is asked almost every semester.

| Feature | **PATH** | **CLASSPATH** |
|---|---|---|
| Used by | **Operating System** | **JVM / Java Compiler** |
| Purpose | Locates **executable programs** (e.g., `javac.exe`, `java.exe`) | Locates **`.class` files** and **JAR archives** |
| Default value | OS-defined (e.g., `/usr/bin`, `C:\Windows\System32`) | **`.`** (current directory) |
| Includes | Directories only | Directories **and** JAR/ZIP files |
| Separator (Windows) | `;` | `;` |
| Separator (Linux/macOS) | `:` | `:` |
| Example | `C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-21\bin` | `.;C:\myproject\bin;C:\libs\*` |
| Affects | Running executables | Running Java programs |

---

## 2.6 CLASSPATH and Packages — The Critical Relationship

When a Java class belongs to a **package** (e.g., `package com.ktu.student;`), the compiled `.class` file is stored at:

```
<CLASSPATH_DIRECTORY>/com/ktu/student/MyClass.class
```

So if your class is `com.ktu.student.MyClass` and the `.class` file is at:

```
C:\myproject\bin\com\ktu\student\MyClass.class
```

Then your CLASSPATH **must include**:

```
C:\myproject\bin
```

…**NOT** `C:\myproject\bin\com`. This is a **very common KTU exam pitfall**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often include the package directory in CLASSPATH. This is **incorrect**. CLASSPATH must point to the **root** of the package hierarchy, and the JVM reconstructs the full package path by concatenating the package name (with `.` converted to `/`).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `.` | Current directory (default) | `CLASSPATH=.` |
| `;` (Windows) / `:` (Unix) | Path separator | `.;C:\libs\*` |
| `-cp` | Command-line CLASSPATH override | `java -cp . MyApp` |
| `-classpath` | Long-form of `-cp` | `java -classpath . MyApp` |
| `*` | All JARs in a directory | `-cp "C:\libs\*"` |
| JAR file | Direct class archive reference | `-cp "C:\libs\util.jar"` |
| `-jar` | Execute a JAR file (overrides CLASSPATH) | `java -jar app.jar` |
| `-Xbootclasspath` | Override Bootstrap ClassPath (advanced) | `java -Xbootclasspath/p:custom.jar Main` |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real-world software engineering:

1. **Enterprise Java (Spring, Hibernate)** — JAR files are placed in a `lib/` folder and referenced via CLASSPATH or build tools (Maven/Gradle, which manage CLASSPATH automatically).
2. **JDBC Drivers** — Database driver JARs (e.g., `mysql-connector-java-8.x.jar`) **must be on CLASSPATH** to connect to databases.
3. **Android Development** — Uses its own class-loading via the Dalvik/ART runtime, but the underlying principle mirrors CLASSPATH.
4. **Big Data (Hadoop, Spark)** — Custom JARs are submitted with `--jars` (Spark) or `-libjars` (Hadoop).
5. **Web Containers (Tomcat, Jetty)** — Each web application has its own CLASSPATH (the `WEB-INF/classes` and `WEB-INF/lib` directories).

---
