e \in \texttt{ArithmeticException} \subseteq \texttt{RuntimeException} \subseteq \texttt{Exception} \subseteq \texttt{Throwable}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Behavior Under All Scenarios

Let us derive the output for **five canonical scenarios** that KTU frequently tests. Let $T$ = try block, $C$ = catch block, $F$ = finally block, $R$ = return statement.

$$
\text{Execution Output} = f(T, C, F, R) = \begin{cases}
T \rightarrow F & \text{if } T \text{ completes normally, no } R \\
T \rightarrow F \rightarrow \text{return} & \text{if } R \in T \\
T \rightarrow \text{exception} \rightarrow C \rightarrow F & \text{if } C \text{ matches} \\
T \rightarrow \text{exception} \rightarrow F \rightarrow \text{propagate} & \text{if no } C \text{ matches} \\
F \rightarrow \text{return} & \text{if } R \in F
\end{cases}
$$

### Scenario-by-Scenario Truth Table

| Scenario | Code in `try` | Has `catch`? | `finally` executes? | Final outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | Normal (no exception) | Yes | **Yes** | Normal return |
| S2 | Throws matched exception | Yes | **Yes** | Catch handles, normal return |
| S3 | Throws unmatched exception | No | **Yes** | Exception propagates AFTER `finally` |
| S4 | `return` statement | Yes | **Yes (before return)** | `finally` runs, then return occurs |
| S5 | `System.exit(0)` | Yes | **No (skipped!)** | JVM terminates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight — The "Return vs Finally" Question:** This is the **#1 most-asked trap question** in KTU 2024 Scheme exams. The rule is: if `try` has a `return`, the JVM **suspends** the return, executes `finally`, and **then** completes the return. This guarantees that cleanup happens even during a return.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Analytical Example — Output Prediction

Given the following code, predict the output:

```
public class Test {
    static int compute() {
        try {
            return 10;
        } catch (Exception e) {
            return 20;
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Cleanup done");
        }
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println(compute());
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

- **Step 1:** `main()` calls `compute()`.
- **Step 2:** `compute()` enters `try`, evaluates `10` as return value, **stores it in a temporary location** (it is NOT returned yet).
- **Step 3:** No exception, so `catch` is skipped.
- **Step 4:** `finally` executes, printing "Cleanup done".
- **Step 5:** The stored return value `10` is finally returned to `main()`.
- **Step 6:** `main()` prints `10`.

**Final Output:**

```
Cleanup done
10
```

This exact problem pattern is worth **7 marks** standalone in a KTU 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Exception Propagation with `throws`

The following block-level flow diagram shows how an exception travels up the call stack when intermediate methods use `throws` for delegation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["MAIN method"] --> B1["method1 with try catch finally"]
    B1 --> C1["method2 with throws"]
    C1 --> D1["method3 with throws"]
    D1 --> E1["Risky code: division by zero"]
    E1 --> F1["ArithmeticException object created"]
    F1 --> G1["JVM throws to method3"]
    G1 --> H1["method3 has no catch, uses throws to delegate"]
    H1 --> I1["JVM throws to method2"]
    I1 --> J1["method2 has no catch, uses throws to delegate"]
    J1 --> K1["JVM throws to method1"]
    K1 --> L1{"catch block matches ArithmeticException"}
    L1 -- YES --> M1["Catch handles the exception"]
    M1 --> N1["Finally block executes ALWAYS"]
    L1 -- NO --> O1["Finally executes, then exception propagates further"]
    N1 --> P1["method1 returns normally to main"]
    O1 --> P1
    P1 --> Q1["Program continues in main"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — `finally` Execution State Machine

This state machine illustrates all possible states a `try-catch-finally` construct can transition through.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State1: Enter try block
    State1: Try block executing
    State1 --> State2: Code runs normally
    State1 --> State3: Exception thrown
    State1 --> State4: return encountered
    State2: Try completed normally
    State3: Exception in try
    State4: Return in try
    State2 --> State5: Skip catch go to finally
    State3 --> State6: catch matches
    State3 --> State7: catch does not match
    State4 --> State5: Suspend return go to finally
    State5: Finally block executing
    State6: Catch handling exception
    State6 --> State5: After catch go to finally
    State7: Exception uncaught
    State7 --> State5: Before propagation go to finally
    State5 --> State8: Exit normally
    State5 --> State9: Exit via return in finally
    State5 --> State10: Re-throw new exception from finally
    State8 --> [*]
    State9 --> [*]
    State10 --> [*]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Comparison of `throw` vs `throws`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["throw keyword"] --> A1["Used INSIDE method body"]
    A1 --> A2["Manually triggers ONE exception object"]
    A2 --> A3["Example: throw new IOException"]
    B["throws keyword"] --> B1["Used in method SIGNATURE"]
    B1 --> B2["Declares LIST of possible exceptions"]
    B2 --> B3["Example: void read throws IOException"]
    A -.VS.- B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight for KTU Students:** Think of `throws` as a **"warning label"** on a package, and `throw` as the **"actual alarm bell"** that rings when the package breaks. The warning label (throws) tells you what *might* happen; the alarm bell (throw) tells you what *is* happening.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between `throw` and `throws` keywords in Java with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `throw` | `throws` |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Used to **manually throw** an exception | Used to **declare** an exception |
| **Position** | Inside **method body** | In **method signature** |
| **Count** | Throws **one** exception at a time | Can declare **multiple** exceptions |
| **Syntax Example** | `throw new ArithmeticException("Div by zero");` | `void read() throws IOException` |
| **Checked exception?** | Cannot throw checked exception without `throws` | Can declare checked exceptions |

**(3 Marks: 1 for definition of each + 1 for example/syntax comparison)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the purpose of the `finally` block in Java? Is it always executed?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `finally` block in Java is used to execute **important cleanup code** such as closing files, releasing database connections, or unlocking resources. It is **always executed** regardless of whether:
- The `try` block completes normally (1 mark)
- An exception occurs and is caught (1 mark)
- An exception occurs and is NOT caught (1 mark)

**Exception:** `finally` does NOT execute only when `System.exit()` is called or when the JVM crashes. *(Optional extra point for full credit.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the `throws` keyword in Java with an example program. Discuss the rules that must be followed when using `throws`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating the use of the `finally` block. Explain its execution behavior in the case where:
   (i) An exception occurs in the `try` block and is caught.
   (ii) The `try` block executes a `return` statement.
   **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Definition:** The `throws` keyword in Java is used in a method's signature to declare that the method **may generate one or more checked exceptions** that it does not handle internally. It delegates the exception-handling responsibility to the calling method. **(1 Mark)**

**Example Program:** **(3 Marks)**

```
import java.io.IOException;
public class ThrowsDemo {
    static void readFile() throws IOException {
        throw new IOException("File not found");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            readFile();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Rules for `throws`:** **(3 Marks)**
1. Used only in method signature, not in method body.
2. Multiple checked exceptions can be declared using commas: `throws IOException, SQLException`.
3. Unchecked exceptions (`RuntimeException` subclasses) do NOT require `throws` declaration.
4. If a parent class method declares `throws IOException`, an overriding child method **cannot** declare a broader exception like `Exception`.
5. Constructors and static methods can also use `throws`.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Program demonstrating `finally`:** **(3 Marks)**

```
public class FinallyDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Case 1: Exception caught
        try {
            int x = 10 / 0;
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Finally block ran (Case 1)");
        }

        // Case 2: return in try
        System.out.println("Result: " + testReturn());
    }

    static int testReturn() {
        try {
            return 100;
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Finally block ran (Case 2 - even with return)");
        }
    }
}
```

**Execution Explanation:** **(4 Marks)**

**Case (i) — Exception caught:** The `try` block throws `ArithmeticException`. The `catch` block catches it and prints the message. The `finally` block then runs and prints "Finally block ran (Case 1)". Output: `Caught: / by zero` followed by `Finally block ran (Case 1)`.

**Case (ii) — Return in try:** When the `try` block executes `return 100`, the JVM **temporarily stores** the return value, executes the `finally` block (printing "Finally block ran (Case 2 - even with return)"), and **then** returns `100` to the caller. This proves `finally` runs *before* the return is completed.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** What is exception propagation in Java? Explain with an example how `throws` enables exception propagation across multiple methods. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java. Write a program that demonstrates the use of `try`, `catch`, `finally`, and `throws` together. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Exception Propagation:** Exception propagation is the process by which an exception **travels up the call stack** from the method where it occurred to the method that handles it. If no method in the chain handles the exception, it propagates to the JVM, which terminates the program. **(2 Marks)**

**Example:** **(3 Marks)**

```
public class PropagationDemo {
    static void methodC() throws ArithmeticException {
        int a = 5 / 0;   // Exception generated here
    }
    static void methodB() throws ArithmeticException {
        methodC();       // Not handled, uses throws
    }
    static void methodA() {
        try {
            methodB();
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught at top: " + e);
        }
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        methodA();
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** The exception originates in `methodC`, propagates to `methodB` (which uses `throws ArithmeticException` to delegate it), and finally reaches `methodA` where it is caught. This shows the **call stack unwinding** mechanism enabled by `throws`. **(2 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions:** **(3 Marks)**

| Feature | Checked Exceptions | Unchecked Exceptions |
|---|---|---|
| **Compiler check** | Yes, checked at compile time | No, checked at runtime |
| **Parent class** | `Exception` (excluding RuntimeException) | `RuntimeException` and `Error` |
| **Mandatory `throws`?** | Yes, if not caught | No |
| **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` |
| **Recovery** | Usually recoverable | Often programming bugs |

**Combined Program using `try-catch-finally-throws`:** **(4 Marks)**

```
import java.io.IOException;
public class CombinedDemo {
    static void riskyOp() throws IOException {
        throw new IOException("Simulated I/O failure");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            riskyOp();
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Exception caught: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Finally executed for cleanup");
        }
        System.out.println("Program ended normally");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Exception caught: Simulated I/O failure
Finally executed for cleanup
Program ended normally
```

This program demonstrates all four concepts in a single flow: `throws` declares the exception in `riskyOp()`, `try` encloses the risky call, `catch` handles the checked `IOException`, and `finally` runs the cleanup code.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Before Writing Exam!)**
>
> 1. **Confusing `throw` and `throws`** — Costs 1–2 marks per occurrence. Remember: `throw` is the *action*, `throws` is the *declaration*.
> 2. **Forgetting that `finally` overrides `catch` return** — If both `catch` and `finally` have `return`, the `finally` return wins. Many students write the wrong output.
> 3. **Claiming `finally` ALWAYS runs** — It does NOT run if `System.exit()` is called. The phrase "always" can cost you a mark if you don't qualify it.
> 4. **Missing semicolon after `throws` clause** — The `throws` clause ends with a method body's opening brace `{`, not a semicolon. A misplaced semicolon is a syntax error.
> 5. **Declaring `throws RuntimeException` unnecessarily** — Unchecked exceptions do NOT need `throws`. Declaring them shows a conceptual gap to the examiner.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ `throws` is a **compile-time declaration** keyword placed in a method's signature to delegate checked exception handling to the caller.
- ✅ `finally` is a **runtime cleanup block** that is guaranteed to execute under virtually all exit conditions from a `try` (normal, exceptional, or `return`).
- ✅ A `try` block must be followed by **either** a `catch` block, **or** a `finally` block, **or both** (but at least one is mandatory).
- ✅ The **"Handle or Declare" rule** mandates that checked exceptions must be either caught or declared via `throws`.
- ✅ `finally` runs **even if a `return` statement is in `try` or `catch`** — this is the most heavily tested behavior.
- ✅ The **one exception** to `finally`'s guarantee is `System.exit(0)` or a JVM crash.
- ✅ `throw` (singular, body) and `throws` (plural, signature) are **two distinct keywords** — never use them interchangeably.
- ✅ Unchecked exceptions (`RuntimeException` subclasses) do **NOT** require `throws` declaration.
- ✅ Overriding methods **cannot throw broader checked exceptions** than their parent.
- ✅ `finally` is the historical foundation of **resource management** in Java (modern `try-with-resources` builds on it).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Java Library - String Handling - String Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Java Library - String Handling - String Constructors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — STRING HANDLING: STRING CONSTRUCTORS

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly maps to **CO3 — Apply Java standard library classes for effective string manipulation and event handling**. It is a high-weightage topic, frequently appearing as a 7-mark sub-question in Part B of the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java Standard Edition (Java SE) platform, the `java.lang.String` class represents an **immutable sequence of Unicode characters stored internally as a `char[]` array (in JDK 8 and earlier) or a `byte[]` array backed by a `byte coder` flag (Compact Strings optimization introduced in JDK 9)**. A **String Constructor** is a special member method of the `String` class whose explicit purpose is to **instantiate a new `String` object** by initializing its internal character/byte backing array from a specified source — such as a character array, byte array, another String, a StringBuffer, a StringBuilder, or a code-point array.

The `String` class is declared as:

```java
public final class String
    implements java.io.Serializable, Comparable<String>, CharSequence
```

The keyword `final` signifies that **no subclass can extend `String`**, thereby guaranteeing immutability at the language level. All constructors of `String` ultimately allocate and initialize the internal character array (or byte array in Compact Strings).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Highlight:** Always begin your answer with the line — *"The `String` class belongs to `java.lang` package and is implicitly imported into every Java program. It is declared `final` and therefore cannot be subclassed."* Examiners award 1 mark just for this opening line.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Glass Box" Metaphor

Imagine you own a **sealed, transparent glass box** with a label pasted on it. You can place a message inside it, but once the box is sealed, **you cannot change the contents inside**. If you want a modified message, you must build a **brand new glass box** with the new content.

- The **glass box** = a `String` object.
- The **label** = the reference variable (e.g., `String name`).
- The **message inside** = the character sequence.
- The **seal** = the `final` keyword + immutability contract.
- **Constructors** are the *only authorized factory assembly lines* that build these sealed boxes from raw materials (raw char arrays, byte arrays, other boxes, or builders).

This analogy immediately clarifies two KTU-favourite conceptual questions:
1. *"Why are Strings immutable?"* → Because the box is sealed at the factory.
2. *"What happens when you reassign a String?"* → You are replacing the *label*, not modifying the contents of the original box.

---

## 1.3 The Java String Class — Quick Facts Table

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Package** | `java.lang` |
| **Class modifier** | `public final` |
| **Implements** | `Serializable`, `Comparable<String>`, `CharSequence` |
| **Internal storage (JDK 8-)** | `private final char[] value;` |
| **Internal storage (JDK 9+)** | `private final byte[] value; private final byte coder;` |
| **Object count after creation** | Exactly **1** new String object (unless String Pool interning is triggered) |
| **Default import** | Automatic (no `import` statement needed) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — THE STRING CONSTRUCTOR FAMILY

> [!NOTE]
> The `String` class provides **15 constructors in total** in modern Java (JDK 17 / 21 LTS). Below is the exhaustive KTU-board-relevant taxonomy. Of these, **8 are considered "core"** and are routinely asked in university exams.

## 2.1 Complete Constructor Catalogue

### 🔹 Category A: Default & Copy Constructors

| # | Constructor Signature | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `public String()` | Creates an **empty** String object (`""`) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | `public String(String original)` | Creates a **copy** of the given String literal/obj | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### 🔹 Category B: Character Array Constructors

| # | Constructor Signature | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | `public String(char[] value)` | Allocates a new String representing the entire char array | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | `public String(char[] value, int offset, int count)` | Allocates a new String from a **subarray** of chars | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | `public String(int[] codePoints, int offset, int count)` | Allocates from Unicode **code-point** subarray (supports supplementary characters) | ⭐⭐⭐ |

### 🔹 Category C: Byte Array Constructors (Charset-based decoding)

| # | Constructor Signature | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | `public String(byte[] bytes)` | Decodes bytes using the **platform default charset** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | `public String(byte[] bytes, String charsetName)` | Decodes using a **named charset** (UTF-8, ISO-8859-1, …) — deprecated since JDK 1.1 for throwing checked UnsupportedEncodingException | ⭐⭐ |
| 8 | `public String(byte[] bytes, Charset charset)` | Decodes using a **Charset object** (preferred over #7) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | `public String(byte[] bytes, int offset, int length)` | Decodes a **subrange** using platform default charset | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | `public String(byte[] bytes, int offset, int length, String charsetName)` | Subrange with named charset — deprecated | ⭐⭐ |
| 11 | `public String(byte[] bytes, int offset, int length, Charset charset)` | Subrange with Charset object — **preferred** for I/O streams | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### 🔹 Category D: StringBuilder / StringBuffer Constructors

| # | Constructor Signature | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 12 | `public String(StringBuffer buffer)` | Synchronized (`StringBuffer` content snapshot) | ⭐⭐ |
| 13 | `public String(StringBuilder builder)` | Non-synchronized (`StringBuilder` content snapshot) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### 🔹 Category E: Internal / Deprecated Constructors

| # | Constructor Signature | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 14 | `String(char[] value, boolean share)` | **Package-private**, avoids defensive array copy — used by JVM internals | ⭐ |
| 15 | `String(AbstractStringBuilder, …)` | Internal helper — **not public** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Charset Pitfall (Frequently Asked):** `new String(byte[])` uses the **JVM's default platform charset** (typically UTF-8 on Linux/Mac, Cp1252 on Windows). This makes code **non-portable**. Always prefer `new String(byte[], StandardCharsets.UTF_8)` in production. KTU may ask this as a 3-mark "What is the disadvantage?" question.

---

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics — How Each Constructor Works Internally

### 🔹 Mechanism #1: `String()` — Empty Initialization

- Internally invokes `this.value = "".value;` (or equivalent in JDK 9+ Compact Strings).
- Creates an object whose `length()` returns **0**.
- This is **NOT** equivalent to `String s = null;` — `null` is a reference with no object; `new String()` is a real, valid object containing zero characters.

### 🔹 Mechanism #2: `String(String original)` — Defensive Copy

The compiler/JVM performs a **defensive copy** of the original's internal character array. This is **critical for immutability** — if the original's backing array were shared, the new String could be mutated through the original.

Internal simplified flow:

```java
public String(String original) {
    this.value = original.value;
    this.coder = original.coder;
    this.hash = original.hash;
}
```

Wait — actually JDK shares the array reference here for efficiency, but the `final` modifier on `value` plus the immutable contract guarantees safety. No public mutator exists.

### 🔹 Mechanism #3: `String(char[] value)` — Full Array Initialization

```java
public String(char[] value) {
    this(value, 0, value.length, null);
}
```

It calls the 3-argument constructor with `offset=0` and `count=value.length`.

### 🔹 Mechanism #4: `String(char[] value, int offset, int count)` — Subarray Initialization

This is one of the **most examination-relevant constructors** because it has **3 boundary validation checks** that KTU loves to test:

1. `offset >= 0` check (throws `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` if false)
2. `count >= 0` check (throws `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` if false)
3. `offset + count <= value.length` check (throws `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` if false)

Internal simplified flow:

```java
public String(char[] value, int offset, int count) {
    if (offset < 0) throw new StringIndexOutOfBoundsException(offset);
    if (count < 0) throw new StringIndexOutOfBoundsException(count);
    if (offset > value.length - count)
        throw new StringIndexOutOfBoundsException(offset + count);
    // defensive copy of subarray
    this.value = Arrays.copyOfRange(value, offset, offset + count);
}
```

### 🔹 Mechanism #5: `String(byte[] bytes, Charset charset)` — Charset-Based Decoding

The byte array is decoded into characters using the supplied `Charset`. This is the **safest, modern, non-deprecated** form. The internal call is:

```java
public String(byte[] bytes, Charset charset) {
    this(bytes, 0, bytes.length, charset);
}
```

### 🔹 Mechanism #6: `String(StringBuilder builder)` and `String(StringBuffer buffer)`

Both perform a **synchronized snapshot copy** of the builder/buffer's internal character array. Since JDK 1.5, `StringBuilder.toString()` itself uses this constructor for efficiency — completing a tight feedback loop.

```java
// Inside StringBuilder.toString():
@Override
public String toString() {
    // Create a new String without copying the buffer (JDK optimization)
    return new String(this, null);
}
```

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Constructor Selection Guide

| **If your source data is…** | **Use this constructor** | **Why** |
|---|---|---|
| Nothing (default empty) | `new String()` | Creates empty String, not `null` |
| Another String | `new String(String original)` | Explicit defensive copy (rare in practice — usually literals used directly) |
| A full `char[]` | `new String(char[] value)` | Decouples array from String — modifying the array later does NOT affect the String |
| Part of a `char[]` | `new String(char[] value, int offset, int count)` | Avoids manual array copying |
| A full `byte[]` (known charset) | `new String(byte[] bytes, Charset)` | Portable, explicit decoding |
| A full `byte[]` (unknown charset) | `new String(byte[] bytes)` | Uses platform default — risky |
| A `StringBuilder` | `new String(StringBuilder)` | Efficient immutable snapshot |
| A `StringBuffer` | `new String(StringBuffer)` | Safe snapshot of synchronized buffer |
| Unicode supplementary chars | `new String(int[] codePoints, int offset, int count)` | Handles code points beyond BMP (> U+FFFF) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional Java engineering, the **byte-array constructors** are absolutely critical for:

1. **Network I/O** — `Socket.getInputStream().readAllBytes()` returns `byte[]`; you must wrap it in `new String(bytes, StandardCharsets.UTF_8)`.
2. **File I/O** — `Files.readAllBytes(Paths.get("file.txt"))` returns `byte[]`.
3. **Database CLOB/BLOB handling** — JDBC drivers return byte arrays.
4. **Cryptography / Encoding pipelines** — Converting between `byte[]` (machine format) and `String` (human format) is the bridge of all secure data handling.
5. **HTTP server responses** — `HttpExchange.sendResponseHeaders(...)` followed by byte-array body construction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** A 14-mark Part B question may ask: *"Explain the byte-array constructors of String. Why is `new String(byte[])` considered non-portable? Demonstrate with a program."* — Always illustrate with both `StandardCharsets.UTF_8` and the platform default in your code example.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> Below are **four exhaustive worked derivations** corresponding to the most examination-relevant constructors. Every line of logic is explicitly stated, satisfying KTU's "Show every step" valuation standard.

---

## 3.1 Derivation A — Constructing a String from a Sub-range of a `char[]`

**Problem:** Given `char[] data = {'J', 'A', 'V', 'A', 'P', 'O', 'I', 'N', 'T'};`, construct the String `"AVAP"` using `String(char[], int, int)` constructor.

### Step 1 — Identify Source Array Properties

```
data = ['J', 'A', 'V', 'A', 'P', 'O', 'I', 'N', 'T']
index:   0     1     2     3     4     5     6     7     8
length(data) = 9
```

### Step 2 — Identify Target Subarray Indices

Target = `"AVAP"` → characters at indices `1, 2, 3, 4`.

```
offset = 1   (starting index)
count  = 4   (number of characters to copy)
```

### Step 3 — Validate Pre-conditions (Exception Safety)

The constructor enforces three boundary checks:

$$
\text{Check 1: } offset \geq 0 \implies 1 \geq 0 \quad ✓
$$

$$
\text{Check 2: } count \geq 0 \implies 4 \geq 0 \quad ✓
$$

$$
\text{Check 3: } offset + count \leq length(data) \implies 1 + 4 \leq 9 \implies 5 \leq 9 \quad ✓
$$

All three checks pass → **no `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException`** is thrown.

### Step 4 — Compute Defensive Copy

The JVM invokes `Arrays.copyOfRange(data, offset, offset + count)`:

$$
\text{copyOfRange}(data, 1, 5) = [data[1], data[2], data[3], data[4]] = [\, \text{'A'}, \text{'V'}, \text{'A'}, \text{'P'} \,]
$$

### Step 5 — Wrap as Immutable String

```java
char[] data = {'J','A','V','A','P','O','I','N','T'};
String result = new String(data, 1, 4);
System.out.println(result);   // Output: AVAP
```

### Step 6 — Final Output

$$
\boxed{result = \texttt{"AVAP"}, \quad length(result) = 4}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Constructing a String from a `byte[]` Using UTF-8 Charset

**Problem:** Given the byte array `byte[] b = {(byte)72, (byte)101, (byte)108, (byte)108, (byte)111};`, decode it using UTF-8 and then demonstrate the same with platform default. Explain the output difference (if any).

### Step 1 — Convert Decimal to Binary

$$
\begin{aligned}
72_{10}  &= 01001000_2 \\
101_{10} &= 01100101_2 \\
108_{10} &= 01101100_2 \\
111_{10} &= 01101111_2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — UTF-8 Decoding (ASCII subset — all bytes < 128)

UTF-8 maps single bytes in `[0, 127]` directly to the same Unicode code point.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b[0] = 72  &\rightarrow U+0048 \rightarrow \text{'H'} \\
b[1] = 101 &\rightarrow U+0065 \rightarrow \text{'e'} \\
b[2] = 108 &\rightarrow U+006C \rightarrow \text{'l'} \\
b[3] = 108 &\rightarrow U+006C \rightarrow \text{'l'} \\
b[4] = 111 &\rightarrow U+006F \rightarrow \text{'o'} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Construct the String

```java
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets;

byte[] b = {72, 101, 108, 108, 111};

String s1 = new String(b);                        // Platform default
String s2 = new String(b, StandardCharsets.UTF_8); // Explicit UTF-8

System.out.println(s1);   // Output: Hello
System.out.println(s2);   // Output: Hello
```

### Step 4 — Output Analysis

For this **ASCII-only** example, both constructors produce **identical** output: `"Hello"` (length 5).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The outputs will DIVERGE the moment a byte value ≥ 128 appears. For example, byte `0xC3` followed by `0xA9` is the UTF-8 encoding of `"é"`, but in Cp1252 (Windows default), it decodes as `"Ã©"`. This is the **#1 portability bug** in legacy Java code.

### Step 5 — General Formula for Byte-Length to Char-Length

$$
L_{chars} = \sum_{i=1}^{L_{bytes}} \text{bytes\_per\_codepoint}(b_i)
$$

Where `bytes_per_codepoint ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4}` depending on the leading byte's high bits under UTF-8.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Exception Triggering Analysis for `String(char[], int, int)`

**Problem:** Determine the outcome of `new String(new char[5], 3, 5)`.

### Step 1 — Establish Parameters

```
value.length = 5
offset       = 3
count        = 5
```

### Step 2 — Apply the Three Boundary Checks

$$
\text{Check 1: } offset \geq 0 \implies 3 \geq 0 \quad ✓
$$

$$
\text{Check 2: } count \geq 0 \implies 5 \geq 0 \quad ✓
$$

$$
\text{Check 3: } offset + count \leq value.length \implies 3 + 5 \leq 5 \implies 8 \leq 5 \quad ✗
$$

### Step 3 — Exception Resolution

The third check **fails** → JVM throws:

$$
\boxed{\texttt{java.lang.StringIndexOutOfBoundsException: \texttt{begin 3, end 8, length 5}}}
$$

The constructor **never reaches** the array-copy phase.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Unicode Code-Point Constructor for Supplementary Characters

**Problem:** Construct a String from the code-point array `[0x1F600, 0x1F4A9]` representing 😀 and 💩.

### Step 1 — Identify the Code Points

$$
\begin{aligned}
cp[0] &= \text{U+1F600} = 😀 \\
cp[1] &= \text{U+1F4A9} = 💩 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Both exceed **U+FFFF** (the Basic Multilingual Plane limit), meaning they are **supplementary characters** requiring surrogate pairs in UTF-16.

### Step 2 — Apply Constructor

```java
int[] codePoints = {0x1F600, 0x1F4A9};
String emoji = new String(codePoints, 0, codePoints.length);
System.out.println(emoji);            // 😀💩
System.out.println(emoji.length());   // 4 (each emoji = 2 UTF-16 chars)
System.out.println(emoji.codePointCount(0, emoji.length())); // 2
```

### Step 3 — Length Discrepancy

$$
L_{char} = 2 \times \text{count} = 2 \times 2 = 4 \quad \text{(UTF-16 chars)}
$$

$$
L_{codepoint} = \text{count} = 2 \quad \text{(logical code points)}
$$

This is the **fundamental distinction** between `.length()` and `.codePointCount()` — and a guaranteed KTU "trick" question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Immutability Lifecycle of a String Object

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PoolCheck: Literal or new ?
    PoolCheck --> StringPool: Literal found in pool
    PoolCheck --> NewHeapObj: new keyword invoked

    StringPool --> SharedRef: Same reference returned
    NewHeapObj --> ConstructorCall: Selected constructor executes

    ConstructorCall --> Validation: Bounds and charset checks
    Validation --> DefensiveCopy: Copy source bytes or chars
    DefensiveCopy --> ImmutableObj: Final field assigned
    ImmutableObj --> InUse: Reference returned to caller

    InUse --> InUse: All operations return NEW String
    InUse --> GarbageCollected: Reference goes out of scope
```
