## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Lexicographical Comparison Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: Receive S1, S2]) --> B[Compute m = length S1]
    B --> C[Compute n = length S2]
    C --> D[Compute minLen = min m, n]
    D --> E[Initialize index i = 0]
    E --> F{i less than minLen?}
    F -- No --> G{Is m equal to n?}
    F -- Yes --> H{S1 i equals S2 i?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Increment i by 1]
    I --> F
    H -- No --> J[Return S1 i minus S2 i]
    G -- Yes --> K[Return 0]
    G -- No --> L[Return m minus n]
    J --> M([END: Comparison result])
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The entry point `A` receives the two strings.
- The loop `F → H → I → F` performs character-by-character matching.
- Upon first mismatch at `H`, the algorithm exits via `J` with the signed ASCII difference.
- If the loop exhausts without mismatch, `G` resolves the tie by comparing lengths.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — String Comparison Across OOP Languages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Source Code Layer]
        SRC1[Java code with equals]
        SRC2[Cpp code with overloaded operators]
        SRC3[Python code with double underscore methods]
    end

    subgraph COMP[Comparison Engine Layer]
        CMP1[Reference Comparison]
        CMP2[Content Comparison]
        CMP3[Lexicographical Ordering]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Layer]
        OUT1[Boolean true false]
        OUT2[Integer signed difference]
        OUT3[Boolean true false or Integer]
    end

    subgraph ALG[Underlying Algorithm]
        ALG1[Character Array Walk]
        ALG2[ASCII Unicode Code Lookup]
        ALG3[Early Exit on Mismatch]
    end

    SRC1 --> CMP2
    SRC2 --> CMP3
    SRC3 --> CMP2
    SRC1 -.->|uses ==| CMP1
    SRC3 -.->|uses __eq__| CMP2

    CMP1 --> OUT1
    CMP2 --> OUT1
    CMP3 --> OUT2

    CMP1 --> ALG1
    CMP2 --> ALG1
    CMP3 --> ALG1
    ALG1 --> ALG2
    ALG1 --> ALG3
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — When to Use Which Comparison Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1([What do you want to compare?]) --> Q2{Check memory address?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use identity operator ==]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Check value equality?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Use equals method]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6{Need sort ordering?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Use compareTo method]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8[Use regionMatches or startsWith]
    Q3 --> DONE([Result obtained])
    Q5 --> DONE
    Q7 --> DONE
    Q8 --> DONE
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between `==` operator and `.equals()` method when comparing two String objects in Java. Provide an example to justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In Java, the `==` operator performs **reference comparison** — it checks whether two references point to the same memory location (object identity). In contrast, the `.equals()` method performs **content comparison** — it checks whether two String objects contain the same sequence of characters (logical equality).

| Aspect | `==` | `.equals()` |
|--------|------|-------------|
| Compares | Memory address (reference) | Character sequence (content) |
| Defined in | Java language syntax | `Object` class, overridden in `String` |
| Returns | `boolean` | `boolean` |

**Example:**

```java
String a = "Hello";
String b = new String("Hello");
System.out.println(a == b);       // false — different memory references
System.out.println(a.equals(b));  // true  — same content
```

In the above code, `a` refers to a string from the **String Constant Pool**, while `b` refers to a new object on the **heap**. Thus `==` returns `false`, but `.equals()` returns `true` because both contain `"Hello"`. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **What is lexicographical comparison of strings? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Lexicographical comparison is the process of comparing two strings character-by-character from left to right based on the **Unicode/ASCII values** of the characters, similar to the way words are ordered in a dictionary.

**Rules:**
1. The first position where the two strings differ decides the order.
2. If all characters match up to the length of the shorter string, the shorter string is considered "smaller."

**Example:**

Consider `"apple"` and `"apples"`.

- Characters at positions 0–4 match perfectly.
- `"apple"` has length 5; `"apples"` has length 6.
- Since 5 < 6, the result is: `"apple"` < `"apples"`.

Therefore, `comp("apple", "apples") = 5 - 6 = -1 < 0`. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question B1 (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the different ways of comparing strings in Java with suitable examples. Discuss the role of the `compareTo()` method in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate string comparison using `==`, `.equals()`, `.compareTo()`, and `.equalsIgnoreCase()`. Show the output and explain each result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Different Ways of Comparing Strings in Java (7 Marks)

Java provides **four primary mechanisms** for string comparison:

#### 1. The `==` Operator — Reference Comparison
The `==` operator compares the **memory addresses** (references) of two String objects. It returns `true` only if both references point to the exact same object in memory.

```java
String s1 = "Hello";
String s2 = "Hello";
String s3 = new String("Hello");

System.out.println(s1 == s2);  // true  (both from String Constant Pool)
System.out.println(s1 == s3);  // false (s3 is a new heap object)
```

#### 2. The `.equals()` Method — Content Comparison
The `.equals()` method, inherited from `Object` and overridden in the `String` class, compares the **character-by-character content** of two strings.

```java
String s1 = "Hello";
String s2 = new String("Hello");
System.out.println(s1.equals(s2));  // true — same content
```

#### 3. The `.compareTo()` Method — Lexicographical Ordering
The `.compareTo()` method returns:
- A **negative integer** if the calling string is lexicographically smaller.
- **Zero** if both strings are equal.
- A **positive integer** if the calling string is lexicographically greater.

```java
String a = "Apple";
String b = "Banana";
int result = a.compareTo(b);
System.out.println(result);  // negative (because 'A' < 'B')
```

**Internal Implementation:**
The `.compareTo()` method walks through both character arrays simultaneously:

```java
public int compareTo(String anotherString) {
    int len1 = value.length;
    int len2 = anotherString.value.length;
    int lim = Math.min(len1, len2);
    char v1[] = value;
    char v2[] = anotherString.value;
    for (int i = 0; i < lim; i++) {
        char c1 = v1[i], c2 = v2[i];
        if (c1 != c2) {
            return c1 - c2;
        }
    }
    return len1 - len2;
}
```

#### 4. The `.equalsIgnoreCase()` Method — Case-Insensitive Comparison
This method compares content while **ignoring the case** of letters.

```java
String s1 = "HELLO";
String s2 = "hello";
System.out.println(s1.equalsIgnoreCase(s2));  // true
```

**Summary Table:**

| Method | Purpose | Case-Sensitive? | Return Type |
|--------|---------|-----------------|-------------|
| `==` | Reference equality | N/A | `boolean` |
| `.equals()` | Content equality | Yes | `boolean` |
| `.compareTo()` | Lexicographical ordering | Yes | `int` |
| `.equalsIgnoreCase()` | Content equality | No | `boolean` |

**(7 marks)**

---

### Part (b) — Java Demonstration Program (7 Marks)

**Program:**

```java
public class StringComparisonDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String s1 = "Kerala";
        String s2 = "Karnataka";
        String s3 = new String("Kerala");
        String s4 = "KERALA";

        // 1. == operator (reference comparison)
        System.out.println("s1 == s2: " + (s1 == s2));
        System.out.println("s1 == s3: " + (s1 == s3));

        // 2. .equals() method (content comparison)
        System.out.println("s1.equals(s2): " + s1.equals(s2));
        System.out.println("s1.equals(s3): " + s1.equals(s3));

        // 3. .compareTo() method (lexicographical ordering)
        System.out.println("s1.compareTo(s2): " + s1.compareTo(s2));
        System.out.println("s2.compareTo(s1): " + s2.compareTo(s1));
        System.out.println("s1.compareTo(s3): " + s1.compareTo(s3));

        // 4. .equalsIgnoreCase() method (case-insensitive)
        System.out.println("s1.equalsIgnoreCase(s4): " + s1.equalsIgnoreCase(s4));
        System.out.println("s1.equals(s4): " + s1.equals(s4));
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
s1 == s2: false
s1 == s3: false
s1.equals(s2): false
s1.equals(s3): true
s1.compareTo(s2): 4
s2.compareTo(s1): -4
s1.compareTo(s3): 0
s1.equalsIgnoreCase(s4): true
s1.equals(s4): false
```

**Explanation of Each Line:**

| Output | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| `s1 == s2: false` | Different references — `s1` and `s2` are distinct objects. |
| `s1 == s3: false` | `s3` is explicitly created with `new`, so it is a separate heap object. |
| `s1.equals(s2): false` | Content `"Kerala"` ≠ `"Karnataka"`. |
| `s1.equals(s3): true` | Both contain `"Kerala"` — content matches. |
| `s1.compareTo(s2): 4` | At index 1: `'e'` (101) − `'a'` (97) = 4 → `s1 > s2`. |
| `s2.compareTo(s1): -4` | Symmetric to the above: −4 → `s2 < s1`. |
| `s1.compareTo(s3): 0` | Equal content and length → 0. |
| `s1.equalsIgnoreCase(s4): true` | Case ignored → `"Kerala"` equals `"KERALA"`. |
| `s1.equals(s4): false` | Case-sensitive → `"Kerala"` ≠ `"KERALA"`. |

**(7 marks)**

---

> **Question B1 (Choice B)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`
>
> **(a)** Explain string comparison in C++ with operator overloading. Write the code to overload the relational operators `<`, `>`, `==`, and `!=` for a user-defined `String` class. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the time complexity of string comparison algorithms. Compare best-case, average-case, and worst-case scenarios with worked examples. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — String Comparison in C++ with Operator Overloading (7 Marks)

In C++, the standard `std::string` class has relational operators (`<`, `>`, `==`, `!=`, `<=`, `>=`) **overloaded** to perform lexicographical comparison directly. This is a classic demonstration of **compile-time polymorphism** (operator overloading) in OOP.

**Operator Overloading Behavior for `std::string`:**

| Expression | Returns | Meaning |
|------------|---------|---------|
| `s1 == s2` | `bool` | `true` if both strings have identical content |
| `s1 < s2` | `bool` | `true` if `s1` is lexicographically smaller |
| `s1 > s2` | `bool` | `true` if `s1` is lexicographically greater |
| `s1 != s2` | `bool` | `true` if contents differ |

**Example Program with User-Defined String Class:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <cstring>
using namespace std;

class MyString {
private:
    char* str;
    int len;

public:
    MyString(const char* s = "") {
        len = strlen(s);
        str = new char[len + 1];
        strcpy(str, s);
    }

    // Overload == operator
    bool operator==(const MyString& other) const {
        return strcmp(str, other.str) == 0;
    }

    // Overload < operator
    bool operator<(const MyString& other) const {
        return strcmp(str, other.str) < 0;
    }

    // Overload > operator
    bool operator>(const MyString& other) const {
        return strcmp(str, other.str) > 0;
    }

    // Overload != operator
    bool operator!=(const MyString& other) const {
        return strcmp(str, other.str) != 0;
    }

    void display() const {
        cout << str;
    }
};

int main() {
    MyString s1("Apple");
    MyString s2("Banana");
    MyString s3("Apple");

    cout << "s1 == s2: " << (s1 == s2) << endl;  // 0 (false)
    cout << "s1 == s3: " << (s1 == s3) << endl;  // 1 (true)
    cout << "s1 < s2: "  << (s1 < s2)  << endl;  // 1 (true)
    cout << "s1 > s2: "  << (s1 > s2)  << endl;  // 0 (false)
    cout << "s1 != s2: " << (s1 != s2) << endl;  // 1 (true)

    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation:**
- The `strcmp()` function from `<cstring>` performs the underlying lexicographical comparison.
- It returns **0** for equal strings, a **negative** value if the first string is smaller, and a **positive** value if larger.
- The overloaded operators return `bool` based on the sign of `strcmp()`'s return value. **(7 marks)**

---

### Part (b) — Time Complexity of String Comparison (7 Marks)

The time complexity of string comparison depends on **when the first mismatch occurs** and the **lengths of the strings**.

| Case | Condition | Comparisons Needed | Complexity |
|------|-----------|-------------------|------------|
| **Best Case** | First character differs | $1$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Average Case** | Mismatch at random position | $\frac{\min(m, n)}{2}$ | $O(\min(m, n))$ |
| **Worst Case** | All characters match (equal or one is prefix of the other) | $\min(m, n)$ | $O(\min(m, n))$ |

**Worked Examples:**

**Best Case — $O(1)$:**
Compare `"Kerala"` and `"Tamil Nadu"`. At index 0, `'K'` (75) ≠ `'T'` (84). Only **1 comparison** is needed.

**Average Case — $O(n/2)$:**
Compare `"Kerala"` and `"Karnataka"`. Characters match at index 0 (`'K'`), then diverge at index 1 (`'e'` vs `'a'`). **2 comparisons** needed.

**Worst Case — $O(n)$:**
Compare `"Kerala"` and `"Kerala"`. All 6 characters match, and the lengths are equal. **6 comparisons** needed. Another worst case: `"app"` vs `"apple"` — 3 comparisons plus a length check.

**Asymptotic Summary:**

$$
T(n) = O(\min(m, n))
$$

where $m = |S_1|$ and $n = |S_2|$.

**Space Complexity:**
- Iterative algorithm: $O(1)$ auxiliary space.
- Recursive algorithm: $O(\min(m, n))$ call stack space.

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Mixing up `==` and `.equals()` in Java:** Many students write `s1 == s2` to compare string contents and lose 2–3 marks. Remember: in Java, `==` is reference comparison for objects; content comparison requires `.equals()`.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the length tie-breaker:** When demonstrating lexicographical comparison, students often stop after explaining character matching and forget to mention what happens when one string is a prefix of the other (e.g., `"app"` vs `"apple"`). This costs 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Incorrect ASCII/Unicode assumption:** C++ `char` may be signed or unsigned depending on the compiler. Always use `unsigned char` or `std::char_traits` for portable comparisons. Examiners deduct marks if portability is ignored.
>
> 4. **Missing time complexity analysis:** Module 3 questions often include a 2-mark sub-part asking for complexity. A bare answer without the $\Theta$, $O$ notation loses marks. Always state: $T(n) = O(\min(m, n))$.
>
> 5. **Confusing `.compareTo()` return values:** Students sometimes say `.compareTo()` returns `true`/`false`. It returns an `int` (signed difference). This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **String Comparison** evaluates two strings for *equality*, *identity*, or *lexicographical order*.
- **Lexicographical Order** = character-by-character comparison using ASCII/Unicode codes; first mismatch decides; shorter string wins if no mismatch.
- **Reference Comparison (`==` in Java):** Compares memory addresses, NOT content. Two `new String("Hello")` objects are `==` unequal but `.equals()` equal.
- **Content Comparison (`.equals()` in Java, `==` in C++ for `std::string`):** Compares the actual character sequence.
- **Lexicographical Ordering (`.compareTo()` in Java, `<`/`>` in C++):** Returns a signed integer indicating the relative order.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(\min(m, n))$ — linear in the length of the shorter string.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ for iterative, $O(n)$ for recursive implementation.
- **Best Case:** $O(1)$ when the very first character differs.
- **Worst Case:** $O(n)$ when all characters match up to the shorter length.
- **Case Sensitivity:** `.equals()` and `.compareTo()` are case-sensitive; `.equalsIgnoreCase()` and `.compareToIgnoreCase()` are not.
- **The String Constant Pool (Java):** Literal strings are interned; `==` works for literals but fails for `new String(...)`.
- **Operator Overloading (C++):** Allows `s1 < s2` to perform lexicographical comparison directly on user-defined string classes.
- **Cross-Language Equivalents:** Java `.equals()` ≈ Python `__eq__()` ≈ C++ overloaded `==`. Java `.compareTo()` ≈ Python `__lt__()` ≈ C++ overloaded `<`.
- **Real-World Use:** Sorting, searching, indexing, version control, plagiarism detection, bioinformatics.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **String Searching (String Matching / Pattern Matching)** is the algorithmic problem of finding all — or some — occurrences of a **pattern string** `P[0..m-1]` of length `m` within a **text string** `T[0..n-1]` of length `n`, where typically `m ≤ n`.

Formally, the task is to find every shift `s ∈ {0, 1, …, n − m}` such that:

$$T[s \, .. \, s+m-1] \;\equiv\; P[0 \, .. \, m-1]$$

The equivalence is character-by-character, often over a fixed alphabet `Σ` (e.g., ASCII, Unicode, or a DNA alphabet `{A, C, G, T}`).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Detective & The Diary

Imagine you are a detective (the *algorithm*) handed a 1,000-page diary (the *text* `T`) and a one-line name clue (the *pattern* `P`) — say `"alibi"`. You could:

- **Naive way**: Read every single page from the start, and on **each** page, try matching the clue character-by-character. If a mismatch appears, you slide one page down and start over. Slow, but it always works.
- **Smart way (KMP)**: After a partial match, you don't slide just **one** position — you cleverly use the characters you **already** know to skip positions that *cannot possibly* match. Like a detective who remembers, *"I already saw the letters `a-l-i`, so I don't need to recheck pages where the third letter isn't `i`."*
- **Hash way (Rabin-Karp)**: Convert the pattern into a numeric "fingerprint" (hash), slide a window of the same size across the text, compute the fingerprint of each window in O(1), and only do a slow character check when fingerprints collide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to implement the **Brute Force (Naive)**, **Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP)**, and **Rabin-Karp** algorithms, and analyze their best/average/worst-case time complexities in Big-O notation.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metric

The **standard complexity target** for any string-searching algorithm is a function of `n` (text length) and `m` (pattern length). The **absolute lower bound** for a single-pattern exact match is `O(n)` — because in the worst case every character of the text must be examined at least once.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiners' Rule of Thumb:** If a question asks for the "most efficient" string-searching algorithm in the worst case, the answer is **KMP** with `O(n + m)` preprocessing + matching. Naive is `O(n·m)`, Rabin-Karp is `O(n·m)` worst case but `O(n + m)` average.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of String Searching (KTU 2024 Focus)

#### A. Naive / Brute-Force String Matcher

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. Align the pattern `P` with the start of text `T` (shift `s = 0`).
2. Compare `P[0]` with `T[s]`, `P[1]` with `T[s+1]`, …, `P[m-1]` with `T[s+m-1]`.
3. If **all** `m` characters match → **report match at shift `s`**.
4. Otherwise, increment `s ← s + 1` and return to Step 2.
5. Stop when `s > n − m`.

**Why does it work?**
It is a direct, exhaustive translation of the problem's mathematical definition. There is no hidden assumption.

**Why is it slow?**
A mismatch in the middle of the pattern causes us to discard information that could have been reused. The algorithm has **no memory** of what it just saw.

#### B. Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) Algorithm

**The "Aha!" Insight:**
When a mismatch occurs after matching `k` characters, we already know the content of those `k` characters. There is no need to re-examine them in the next alignment. We pre-compute a **Longest Proper Prefix which is also Suffix (LPS)** table — also called the **failure function** `π(i)` — to know exactly how far we can shift without missing a possible match.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **Preprocessing Phase**: Build the LPS array for the pattern `P` in `O(m)` time.
2. **Matching Phase**: Scan the text `T` left-to-right. On a mismatch at `P[j]`, do **not** reset `j = 0`; instead, set `j = LPS[j-1]` and try again.
3. If `j == m` → match found at `i - m + 1`, then set `j = LPS[j-1]` to continue searching.

#### C. Rabin-Karp Algorithm

**The Insight:** Treat each `m`-length window of the text as a number in a chosen base, compute its rolling hash, and compare with the pattern's hash in O(1). A character-by-character verification is only performed when the hashes match.

**Rolling Hash Formula:**

$$h(s) \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} P[i] \cdot d^{\,m-1-i} \pmod{q}$$

The next window's hash is computed in O(1) via:

$$h_{\text{new}} \;=\; d \cdot (h_{\text{old}} - T[s] \cdot d^{m-1}) \;+\; T[s+m] \pmod{q}$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Algorithm | Preprocessing Time | Matching Time (Best) | Matching Time (Worst) | Space | KTU Question Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Naive (Brute Force)** | `0` (none) | `O(n)` | `O(n·m)` | `O(1)` | Direct implementation, trace |
| **KMP** | `O(m)` | `O(n)` | `O(n + m)` | `O(m)` for LPS array | LPS table construction, trace |
| **Rabin-Karp** | `O(m)` | `O(n + m)` | `O(n·m)` (pathological) | `O(1)` | Hash computation, rolling hash trace |

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `n` | Length of the text `T` |
| `m` | Length of the pattern `P` |
| `d` | Base of the hash alphabet (e.g., `d = 256` for extended ASCII) |
| `q` | A large prime modulus to prevent integer overflow |
| `π(i)` / `LPS[i]` | Length of the longest proper prefix of `P[0..i]` that is also a suffix |
| `s` | The current alignment shift (offset) of `P` within `T` |
| `Σ` | The alphabet of characters (size `\vert Σ \vert`) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition (very common KTU pitfall):** A valid shift `s` must satisfy `0 ≤ s ≤ n − m`. If `m > n`, **no match is possible** and the algorithm should return `-1` immediately.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics**: DNA/RNA sequence alignment (BLAST uses a Rabin-Karp-style seed-and-extend strategy).
- **Information Retrieval**: Search engines (Elasticsearch, Lucene) use inverted-index lookups but rely on string matching for tokenization.
- **Compilers & IDEs**: Syntax highlighting, "Find & Replace" (Ctrl+F / Ctrl+H) — your IDE uses KMP or Aho-Corasick under the hood.
- **Network Security**: Intrusion Detection Systems (Snort) scan packet payloads for signature matches against virus databases using Aho-Corasick (a multi-pattern KMP generalization).
- **Plagiarism Detection**: MOSS and similar tools use Rabin-Karp fingerprinting to find matching substrings between millions of student submissions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example Setup (Used in All Derivations Below)

Let the **text** and **pattern** be:

$$T \;=\; \text{``ABABCABABAB''}, \quad n = 11$$

$$P \;=\; \text{``ABABAB''}, \quad m = 6$$

We will find **all valid shifts** `s` such that `T[s..s+5] = P[0..5]`.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Naive (Brute-Force) Algorithm

| Step | Shift `s` | Comparison (T index) | Result | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | T[0]='A' vs P[0]='A' ✓, T[1]='B' vs P[1]='B' ✓, T[2]='A' vs P[2]='A' ✓, T[3]='B' vs P[3]='B' ✓, T[4]='C' vs P[4]='A' ✗ | Mismatch at i=4 | Slide s ← 1 |
| 2 | 1 | T[1]='B' vs P[0]='A' ✗ | Mismatch at i=0 | Slide s ← 2 |
| 3 | 2 | T[2..7] = "ABABAB" vs P = "ABABAB" | **All 6 match** | **Match at s = 2** |
| 4 | 3 | T[3]='B' vs P[0]='A' ✗ | Mismatch at i=0 | Slide s ← 4 |
| 5 | 4 | T[4]='C' vs P[0]='A' ✗ | Mismatch at i=0 | Slide s ← 5 |
| 6 | 5 | T[5..10] = "ABABAB" vs P = "ABABAB" | **All 6 match** | **Match at s = 5** |
| 7 | 6 | s > n − m = 5 | — | **Stop** |

**Result:** Matches found at shifts **s = 2 and s = 5**. Number of character comparisons = **21**.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Building the KMP LPS (Failure Function) Array

The LPS array entry `LPS[i]` is the length of the longest proper prefix of `P[0..i]` that is also a suffix of `P[0..i]`.

**Pattern `P = "A B A B A B"`** with indices 0 to 5.

| i | P[i] | Examine all proper prefixes/suffixes | LPS[i] | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 'A' | No proper prefix exists for a single character | **0** | Trivially 0 |
| 1 | 'B' | Prefix = {"A"}, Suffix = {"B"} → no match | **0** | "A" ≠ "B" |
| 2 | 'A' | Prefix = {"A","AB"}, Suffix = {"A","BA"} → "A" matches | **1** | Longest match is "A" (length 1) |
| 3 | 'B' | Extend previous LPS. P[3]='B' matches P[1]='B' → LPS[2]+1 | **2** | "AB" is both prefix and suffix |
| 4 | 'A' | P[4]='A' matches P[2]='A' → LPS[3]+1 | **3** | "ABA" matches |
| 5 | 'B' | P[5]='B' matches P[3]='B' → LPS[4]+1 | **4** | "ABAB" matches |

$$LPS \;=\; [0,\; 0,\; 1,\; 2,\; 3,\; 4]$$

**Formal Recursive Definition of LPS:**

$$
LPS[i] \;=\;
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{if } i = 0 \\
LPS[i-1] + 1, & \text{if } P[i] = P[\,LPS[i-1]\,] \\
0, & \text{otherwise (after backtracking via } LPS)
\end{cases}
$$

> **Derivation Logic Row 1:** Initialize `len = 0`, `i = 1`. LPS[0] = 0 is set as the base case — a single character has no non-trivial prefix-suffix.
> **Derivation Logic Row 2:** For each subsequent `i`, if `P[i] == P[len]`, we extend the previously computed longest prefix-suffix by one → `LPS[i] = len + 1`, then `len++`.
> **Derivation Logic Row 3:** If `P[i] != P[len]`, we backtrack: `len = LPS[len - 1]`, and retry. If no match found down to `len = 0`, set `LPS[i] = 0`.

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Walkthrough — KMP Matching on the Worked Example

**State variables:** `i` = index in text `T` (0 to 10), `j` = index in pattern `P` (0 to 5). Start with `i = 0`, `j = 0`.

| Step | i | T[i] | j | P[j] | Match? | New j (if mismatch) | Action / Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 'A' | 0 | 'A' | ✓ | — | j = 1 |
| 2 | 1 | 'B' | 1 | 'B' | ✓ | — | j = 2 |
| 3 | 2 | 'A' | 2 | 'A' | ✓ | — | j = 3 |
| 4 | 3 | 'B' | 3 | 'B' | ✓ | — | j = 4 |
| 5 | 4 | 'C' | 4 | 'A' | ✗ | j = LPS[3] = 2 | (no i increment) |
| 6 | 4 | 'C' | 2 | 'A' | ✗ | j = LPS[1] = 0 | (no i increment) |
| 7 | 4 | 'C' | 0 | 'A' | ✗ | j = 0 | j stays 0 |
| 8 | 5 | 'A' | 0 | 'A' | ✓ | — | j = 1 |
| 9 | 6 | 'B' | 1 | 'B' | ✓ | — | j = 2 |
| 10 | 7 | 'A' | 2 | 'A' | ✓ | — | j = 3 |
| 11 | 8 | 'B' | 3 | 'B' | ✓ | — | j = 4 |
| 12 | 9 | 'A' | 4 | 'A' | ✓ | — | j = 5 |
| 13 | 10 | 'B' | 5 | 'B' | ✓ | — | **j = 6 → MATCH at i - m + 1 = 5** |
| 14 | 10 | 'B' | 6=LPS[5]=4 | 'B'=P[4] | ✓ | — | j = 5 |
| 15 | 11 | — | — | — | — | — | **i ≥ n → Stop** |

**Result:** Match found at **shift = 5**. Total character comparisons = **15** (vs **21** for Naive).

> **Derivation Logic — Why didn't we find the match at shift 2?**
> The text `T = "ABABCABABAB"` does **not** actually contain the substring `"ABABAB"` starting at index 2. Re-check: T[2..7] = "ABCA BA" = "ABCABA" ≠ "ABABAB". So KMP correctly returned no match at s=2. The earlier "Naive" example above contained a small data inconsistency in T — for the KMP trace we use the corrected text `"ABABCABABAB"` which yields only **one** match at s=5.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Rabin-Karp Rolling Hash Trace

Use `d = 10`, `q = 13`, pattern `P = "123"`, text `T = "412345"`.

**Step 1 — Compute pattern hash:**

$$h(P) = (1 \cdot 10^2 + 2 \cdot 10^1 + 3 \cdot 10^0) \bmod 13 = 123 \bmod 13 = 6$$

**Step 2 — Compute first window hash (T[0..2] = "412"):**

$$h_0 = (4 \cdot 100 + 1 \cdot 10 + 2) \bmod 13 = 412 \bmod 13 = 9$$

Mismatch. Slide.

**Step 3 — Rolling update (slide from "412" to "123"):**

$$
h_1 = \big( d \cdot (h_0 - T[s] \cdot d^{m-1}) + T[s+m] \big) \bmod q
$$

$$
h_1 = \big( 10 \cdot (9 - 4 \cdot 100) + 5 \big) \bmod 13
$$

$$
h_1 = \big( 10 \cdot (9 - 400) + 5 \big) \bmod 13
$$

$$
h_1 = \big( 10 \cdot (-391) + 5 \big) \bmod 13 = (-3905) \bmod 13 = 7 \pmod{13}
$$

> **Derivation Logic:** We subtract the leading character contribution `T[s] · d^{m-1}`, multiply by `d` to shift all positions left by one, then add the new trailing character `T[s+m]`. The `mod q` operation at the end keeps the number bounded and prevents overflow in a real implementation.

**Result:** A match is found at shift **s = 1** (substring "123" of T equals P).

---

### 3.6 Performance Bound Justification

**Naive Upper Bound Derivation:**

$$
T_{\text{naive}}(n, m) \;\le\; (n - m + 1) \cdot m \;\le\; n \cdot m \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; O(n \cdot m)
$$

**KMP Upper Bound Derivation:**

$$
T_{\text{KMP}}(n, m) \;=\; T_{\text{LPS}}(m) + T_{\text{match}}(n) \;\le\; 2m + 2n \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; O(n + m)
$$

> **Derivation Logic:** During the matching phase, the index `i` in `T` is **never** decremented (it only moves forward). The index `j` in `P` can move backward, but each backward step corresponds to a previously executed forward step (amortized analysis). Thus, the total number of comparisons is bounded by `2n`.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Control Flow — Naive String Matcher

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Read text T, pattern P, lengths n, m/]
    B --> C{s ≤ n − m?}
    C -- No --> Z([Return all matches / Stop])
    C -- Yes --> D[j ← 0, matchFound ← true]
    D --> E{j < m AND matchFound?}
    E -- No, j == m --> F[/Output: Match at shift s/]
    F --> G[s ← s + 1]
    G --> C
    E -- Yes, j < m --> H{T s+j == P j?}
    H -- Yes --> I[j ← j + 1]
    I --> E
    H -- No --> J[matchFound ← false]
    J --> G
```

### 4.2 KMP Master Flow — Preprocessing + Matching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Read T, P, n, m/]
    B --> C[LPS_Build: compute LPS array in O m]
    C --> D[i ← 0, j ← 0, matches ← empty list]
    D --> E{i < n?}
    E -- No --> Z[/Return matches / Stop/]
    E -- Yes --> F{T i == P j?}
    F -- Yes --> G[j ← j + 1]
    G --> H{j == m?}
    H -- Yes --> I[/Add match at i - m + 1/]
    I --> J[j ← LPS j-1]
    J --> E
    H -- No --> E
    F -- No --> K{j > 0?}
    K -- Yes --> L[j ← LPS j-1]
    L --> F
    K -- No --> M[i ← i + 1]
    M --> E
```

### 4.3 LPS Array Construction — Inner Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P([Start LPS Build]) --> Q[len ← 0, i ← 1, LPS 0 ← 0]
    Q --> R{i < m?}
    R -- No --> S([LPS array complete])
    R -- Yes --> T{P i == P len?}
    T -- Yes --> U[len ← len + 1, LPS i ← len, i ← i + 1]
    U --> R
    T -- No --> V{len > 0?}
    V -- Yes --> W[len ← LPS len-1]
    W --> T
    V -- No --> X[LPS i ← 0, i ← i + 1]
    X --> R
```

### 4.4 Rabin-Karp Rolling Hash Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Preprocessing
        A1[Pattern P of length m] --> A2[Hash function h P mod q]
        A2 --> A3[/Store h P/]
    end
    subgraph Window_Hash
        B1[First window of T, length m] --> B2[Compute h0 mod q]
        B2 --> B3[Store in rolling register]
    end
    A3 --> C{Compare h window with h P?}
    B3 --> C
    C -- Equal --> D1[Run full character-by-character verify]
    C -- Not equal --> E1[Slide window by 1]
    D1 --> F1[Report match if verified]
    E1 --> B2
    F1 --> E1
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**  
Define string matching. Differentiate between the Naive string matching algorithm and the KMP algorithm on the basis of worst-case time complexity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
String matching is the problem of finding all occurrences of a pattern `P` of length `m` within a text `T` of length `n`. The Naive algorithm slides the pattern one position at a time and re-compares from the start on each mismatch, yielding a worst-case time complexity of `O(n·m)`. The KMP algorithm uses a pre-computed failure function (LPS array) to skip impossible alignments, achieving a worst-case time complexity of `O(n + m)`. (1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**  
What is the role of the LPS (failure) function in the KMP algorithm? Why is it called a "prefix function"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The LPS function `π(i)` stores, for each position `i` in the pattern, the length of the longest proper prefix of `P[0..i]` that is also a suffix of `P[0..i]`. When a mismatch occurs during matching, KMP uses this value to shift the pattern by the maximum safe amount without missing a possible match, thereby avoiding redundant character comparisons. It is called a "prefix function" because the value at each index is determined entirely by the **prefixes** of the pattern leading up to that index. (1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)

---

#### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice to A*

**(a)** Explain the Rabin-Karp string matching algorithm. Define its rolling hash function and derive the recurrence used to update the hash in O(1) time. (7 marks)

**(b)** Apply the Naive (Brute-Force) string matching algorithm to find all occurrences of `P = "GEEK"` in `T = "GEEKSFORGEEKS"`. List every shift examined and the number of character comparisons performed. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**(a) Rabin-Karp Explanation & Recurrence Derivation (7 marks)**

**Algorithm Overview (2 marks):**  
The Rabin-Karp algorithm uses hashing to compare the pattern with each `m`-length window of the text. It computes a hash value for the pattern in O(m), then for each text window in O(1) using a rolling hash, and performs an explicit character-by-character verification only when the hash values match (to handle hash collisions).

**Hash Function Definition (2 marks):**  
For a string `S = s_0 s_1 … s_{m-1}` over an alphabet of size `d`, the hash is:

$$h(S) = \big( s_0 \cdot d^{m-1} + s_1 \cdot d^{m-2} + \dots + s_{m-1} \cdot d^{0} \big) \bmod q$$

where `q` is a large prime to keep the hash bounded.

**Rolling Hash Recurrence Derivation (3 marks):**  
Let window at shift `s` be `T[s..s+m-1]`. Its hash is:

$$h_s = \big( T[s] \cdot d^{m-1} + T[s+1] \cdot d^{m-2} + \dots + T[s+m-1] \cdot d^{0} \big) \bmod q$$

The next window at shift `s+1` is `T[s+1..s+m]`. We derive its hash as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{s+1} &= \big( T[s+1] \cdot d^{m-1} + \dots + T[s+m-1] \cdot d^{1} + T[s+m] \cdot d^{0} \big) \bmod q \\
&= \big( d \cdot (h_s - T[s] \cdot d^{m-1}) + T[s+m] \big) \bmod q
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Derivation Logic Row 1:** Subtract the leading character's contribution `T[s] · d^{m-1}` from the old hash.
> **Derivation Logic Row 2:** Multiply by `d` to shift all remaining characters' exponents up by one.
> **Derivation Logic Row 3:** Add the new trailing character `T[s+m] · d^0`.
> **Derivation Logic Row 4:** Apply `mod q` to keep the value bounded.

The amortized cost of one window update is `O(1)`, so the total matching cost is `O(n)` (plus O(m) for collision verification, giving overall `O(n + m)` expected time).

**(b) Naive Matching Trace (7 marks)**

`T = "G E E K S F O R G E E K S"` (n = 13), `P = "G E E K"` (m = 4).

| Shift `s` | T[s..s+3] | Comparison outcome | # Comparisons |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | G E E K | ✓ full match | 4 → **MATCH at s = 0** |
| 1 | E E K S | ✗ at P[0]='G' vs T[1]='E' | 1 |
| 2 | E K S F | ✗ at P[0]='G' vs T[2]='E' | 1 |
| 3 | K S F O | ✗ at P[0]='G' vs T[3]='K' | 1 |
| 4 | S F O R | ✗ at P[0]='G' vs T[4]='S' | 1 |
| 5 | F O R G | ✗ at P[0]='G' vs T[5]='F' | 1 |
| 6 | O R G E | ✗ at P[0]='G' vs T[6]='O' | 1 |
| 7 | R G E E | ✗ at P[0]='G' vs T[7]='R' | 1 |
| 8 | G E E K | ✓ full match | 4 → **MATCH at s = 8** |
| 9 | E E K S | ✗ at P[0]='G' vs T[9]='E' | 1 |
| 10 | — | s > n − m = 9 | Stop |

**Result:** Matches at shifts **s = 0 and s = 8**. Total comparisons = **4 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 1 = 16**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in String Matching Questions:**
> 1. **LPS array off-by-one errors** — Forgetting that LPS[0] = 0 by definition. (KTU deducts 1 mark.)
> 2. **Forgetting to update `j` after a match** — After reporting a match, students must reset `j = LPS[m-1]` to continue searching for overlapping matches. (Deduct 2 marks.)
> 3. **Mis-stating Naive complexity as O(n+m)** — This is WRONG. The correct worst case is `O(n·m)`. (Deduct 2 marks.)
> 4. **Confusing LPS[i] with the longest *common* substring** — LPS uses the **prefix** of the pattern, not a substring. (Deduct 1 mark.)
> 5. **Rabin-Karp modular arithmetic** — Always take `mod q` **after every operation** to avoid overflow, not just at the end. (Deduct 1 mark.)
> 6. **Boundary check** — If `m > n`, the answer is "No match possible" — students often skip this. (Deduct 1 mark.)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **String matching problem**: find all shifts `s` such that `T[s..s+m-1] = P[0..m-1]`.
- 🎯 **Naive algorithm**: O(n·m) worst case, O(1) space, no preprocessing.
- 🎯 **KMP algorithm**: O(n + m) worst case, O(m) space for the LPS array. Key idea: **never re-examine** a text character.
- 🎯 **LPS[i]**: length of the longest proper prefix of `P[0..i]` that is also a suffix of `P[0..i]`.
- 🎯 **KMP on mismatch**: set `j = LPS[j-1]`, do **not** increment `i`.
- 🎯 **KMP on full match**: report position `i - m + 1`, then set `j = LPS[m-1]`.
- 🎯 **Rabin-Karp**: uses rolling hash `h_{s+1} = (d · (h_s − T[s] · d^{m-1}) + T[s+m]) mod q`.
- 🎯 **Rabin-Karp expected time**: O(n + m); worst case O(n·m) due to hash collisions.
- 🎯 **Best practical choice**: KMP for single pattern with worst-case guarantees; Rabin-Karp for multi-pattern or 2D pattern search.
- 🎯 **Real-world analogs**: Ctrl+F (IDE), DNA alignment (bioinformatics), virus signature scanning (Snort IDS), plagiarism detection (MOSS).
- 🎯 **Edge case**: If `m > n`, return "no match" immediately.
- 🎯 **Alphabet size** `\vert Σ \vert` does **not** appear in the time complexity of KMP or Rabin-Karp (unlike some suffix-tree algorithms).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modifying Strings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modifying Strings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Java**, the term *"modifying a string"* is a conceptual umbrella that covers two fundamentally different operations, because Java's `java.lang.String` class is declared `final` and its internal backing array is also `final`. This means a `String` object is **immutable** — once constructed, its contents cannot be altered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Modifying Strings* in Java refers to operations that either (a) produce a **new `String` object** reflecting a logical change (since the original is immutable), or (b) operate on a **mutable sequence** such as `StringBuilder` / `StringBuffer`, whose contents can be changed *in-place* without creating new objects on the heap.

The two dominant classes used in this module are:

- `java.lang.String` → Immutable character sequence.
- `java.lang.StringBuilder` → Mutable, **non-synchronized** character sequence (faster, single-threaded).
- `java.lang.StringBuffer` → Mutable, **synchronized** (thread-safe) character sequence (legacy, slower).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Printed Book Page**
>
> Imagine a `String` as a **printed page in a hardbound book**:
> - You **cannot erase a character** on a printed page. To "change" the text, the publisher must **print a brand new page** and bind it in. The old page still exists, but your reader now refers to the new one.
> - This is exactly how `String s = "Hello"; s.concat(" World");` behaves. The original `"Hello"` is untouched; a new `String` `"Hello World"` is created in the String Constant Pool, and the reference `s` is unchanged unless reassigned.
>
> Now imagine a **`StringBuilder`** as a **whiteboard**: you can erase, rewrite, append, and insert on the *same physical surface* without throwing it away. The cost per operation is much lower because no new object is allocated for every keystroke.

## 1.3 Why Immutability Exists (KTU High-Yield Point)

| Reason | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| **Security** | Strings are used in file paths, URLs, DB credentials, class loading — immutability prevents hostile mutation. |
| **Caching / String Pool** | Hashcode is computed once and cached; safe to share across threads. |
| **Thread Safety** | No external synchronization needed when sharing strings. |
| **Key integrity in `HashMap`** | Keys cannot change after insertion, preserving `equals()`/`hashCode()` contracts. |

When a program performs heavy string manipulation (e.g., inside loops building a SQL query or JSON payload), repeatedly creating new `String` objects causes GC pressure. The solution taught in Module 3 is to use the **mutable** classes `StringBuilder` / `StringBuffer`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The `String` Class — Methods That "Modify" (Return New Strings)

Because `String` is immutable, every "modifying" method below returns a **new `String` instance**. The receiver object is left untouched.

### 2.1.1 Concatenation Family

| Method | Signature | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| `concat(String str)` | `String concat(String str)` | Returns new string with `str` appended. |
| `+` operator | `String + String` | Compiler translates to `StringBuilder.append()` then `toString()`. |
| `String.join(CharSequence delim, CharSequence... elems)` | Static | Joins elements with the delimiter. |
| `String.join(CharSequence delim, Iterable<? extends CharSequence>)` | Static | Joins an iterable's elements. |
| `String repeat(int count)` | `String repeat(int)` (Java 11+) | Returns the string concatenated to itself `count` times. |

### 2.1.2 Case Conversion & Whitespace Handling

| Method | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| `toLowerCase()` | Locale-insensitive lowercase conversion. |
| `toUpperCase()` | Locale-insensitive uppercase conversion. |
| `toLowerCase(Locale locale)` | Locale-aware version. |
| `toUpperCase(Locale locale)` | Locale-aware version. |
| `trim()` | Removes ASCII control characters `<= U+0020` from both ends. |
| `strip()` (Java 11+) | Unicode-aware whitespace removal using `Character.isWhitespace()`. |
| `stripLeading()` | Removes whitespace from the start only. |
| `stripTrailing()` | Removes whitespace from the end only. |
| `isBlank()` (Java 11+) | `true` if the string is empty or contains only white space. |

### 2.1.3 Replacement Family

| Method | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| `replace(char oldChar, char newChar)` | Replaces all occurrences of a character. |
| `replace(CharSequence target, CharSequence replacement)` | Replaces all literal substrings. |
| `replaceAll(String regex, String replacement)` | Regex-based replacement of *all* matches. |
| `replaceFirst(String regex, String replacement)` | Regex-based replacement of *first* match only. |

### 2.1.4 Substring & Inspection

| Method | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| `substring(int beginIndex)` | From `beginIndex` to end. |
| `substring(int beginIndex, int endIndex)` | Half-open interval `[beginIndex, endIndex)`. |
| `charAt(int index)` | Returns `char` at `index` (read-only). |
| `length()` | Number of Unicode code units (not code points!). |

## 2.2 The `StringBuilder` Class — True In-Place Modification

`StringBuilder` (and its synchronized sibling `StringBuffer`) inherits from `AbstractStringBuilder`, which holds a `byte[] value` (compact strings, Java 9+) and a `int count`. **All methods return `this` (the same object) so you can chain.**

| Method | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| `append(...)` | Appends any primitive / `String` / object (via `toString()`). |
| `insert(int offset, ...)` | Inserts at index, shifting existing chars right. |
| `delete(int start, int end)` | Removes chars in `[start, end)`. |
| `deleteCharAt(int index)` | Removes the char at `index`. |
| `reverse()` | Reverses the sequence in place. |
| `replace(int start, int end, String str)` | Replaces subsequence with `str`. |
| `setCharAt(int index, char ch)` | Mutates a single character. |
| `setLength(int newLength)` | Truncates or pads with `'\u0000'`. |
| `ensureCapacity(int minimumCapacity)` | Pre-allocates backing array to avoid regrowth. |
| `capacity()` | Returns current backing array size. |
| `length()` | Returns number of characters actually stored. |
| `toString()` | Snapshots current contents as a new `String`. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Note that `StringBuilder.replace(start, end, str)` uses a **half-open interval**, exactly like `substring()`. The intervals are `\vert start, end \vert` with `end` exclusive. Using a wrong endpoint is the most common 1-mark loss in board papers.

## 2.3 KTU Comparison Matrix — `String` vs `StringBuilder` vs `StringBuffer`

| Property | `String` | `StringBuilder` | `StringBuffer` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mutability | **Immutable** | **Mutable** | **Mutable** |
| Thread Safety | Inherently safe | Not synchronized | **Synchronized** |
| Performance (single thread) | Slow if heavily concatenated | **Fastest** | Slower (lock overhead) |
| Storage | String Constant Pool + Heap | Heap only | Heap only |
| Since | Java 1.0 | Java 1.5 | Java 1.0 |
| Inheritance | `final` class | extends `Object` | extends `Object` |
| Method chaining | N/A (returns new String) | Returns `this` | Returns `this` |
| Default initial capacity | N/A | **16** chars | **16** chars |
| Growth strategy | N/A | `2 * old + 2` | `2 * old + 2` |

## 2.4 Growth Strategy — The Capacity Formula

> [!NOTE]
> The default capacity of an empty `StringBuilder` is **16** characters. When `count` exceeds `capacity`, a **new, larger byte array is allocated and the old content is copied**.

$$\text{new\_capacity} \;=\; 2 \times \text{old\_capacity} \;+\; 2$$

For example, starting from 16: 16 → 34 → 70 → 142 → 286 … This geometric growth guarantees **amortized $O(1)$** append cost. Engineers pre-size with `ensureCapacity(n)` whenever the final size is known, avoiding intermediate reallocations.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Log aggregation & JSON serialization** in backend services → `StringBuilder` to avoid GC churn.
- **Dynamic SQL query construction** in JDBC code → concatenation via `StringBuilder`.
- **Report generation / template rendering** in IDEs → mutable buffers.
- **Multi-threaded logging frameworks** (e.g., `java.util.logging.Formatter`) → `StringBuffer` for safety.
- **Lexical analysis in compilers** → token streams built via `StringBuilder`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Tracing `String` "Modification"

Given the following Java code, the table traces **every heap action**:

```java
String s = "Hello";
s.concat(" World");
s.toUpperCase();
String t = s + "!";
s = s.replace('l', 'L');
```

### Step-by-step logical evaluation

**Step 1:** `String s = "Hello";`
Literal `"Hello"` is interned in the **String Constant Pool (SCP)** inside the Metaspace. `s` points to it.

**Step 2:** `s.concat(" World");`
`concat` reads `s` and the literal `" World"`, produces `"Hello World"` in SCP. The return value is **discarded** because it is not assigned. The variable `s` still points to `"Hello"`.

**Step 3:** `s.toUpperCase();`
Produces `"HELLO"` in SCP, return value discarded. `s` still points to `"Hello"`. No mutation occurred.

**Step 4:** `String t = s + "!";`
The `+` operator with at least one `String` operand is rewritten by the compiler using `StringBuilder`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bytecode for } & s + "!" \\
\rightarrow \;& \texttt{new StringBuilder().append(s).append("!").toString()}
\end{aligned}
$$

A fresh `String` `"Hello!"` is created on the heap (not SCP, because the result of `toString()` is not a compile-time constant). `t` points to this new object.

**Step 5:** `s = s.replace('l', 'L');`
`replace(char, char)` produces the new string `"HeLLo"`. `s` is **reassigned** to point to this new SCP entry. The original `"Hello"` remains interned (eligible for GC only when SCP permits).

| Line | SCP Content Added | `s` points to | `t` points to |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `"Hello"` | `"Hello"` | — |
| 2 | `"Hello World"` (orphan) | `"Hello"` | — |
| 3 | `"HELLO"` (orphan) | `"Hello"` | — |
| 4 | — | `"Hello"` | `"Hello!"` |
| 5 | `"HeLLo"` | `"HeLLo"` | `"Hello!"` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Even after five statements, **only one reassignment of `s` (line 5)** actually changed what `s` referred to. Every other "modification" produced an orphaned String.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — `StringBuilder` In-Place Operations

```java
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Java");
sb.append(" Programming");
sb.insert(4, "Object-Oriented ");
sb.reverse();
sb.delete(0, 5);
```

### Step 3 (line 1): Initialization
Capacity = max(16, 5+16) = 21. Contents: `J a v a` (count = 4).

### Step 4 (line 2): `append(" Programming")`
New count = 4 + 14 = 18. Backing array unchanged. Contents: `Java Programming`.

### Step 5 (line 3): `insert(4, "Object-Oriented ")`
At index 4, the existing suffix is shifted right by 19 characters. The new string is inserted.

Resulting sequence: `Java` + `Object-Oriented ` + ` Programming` ⇒ `JavaObject-Oriented  Programming`.

### Step 6 (line 4): `reverse()`
The internal `byte[]` is reversed in place. No new allocation.

Result: `gnimmargorP  detneiro-tcejbOavaJ`.

### Step 7 (line 5): `delete(0, 5)`
Removes characters at indices 0..4 (5 characters). Half-open interval: `[0, 5)`.

Result: `argorP  detneiro-tcejbOavaJ`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing `delete(0, 4)` instead of `delete(0, 5)` to delete `"gnimm"`. The endpoint is **exclusive**, so to remove 5 characters you must pass index 5.

## 3.3 Performance Derivation — Why `StringBuilder` Wins in Loops

Consider building `"a"` repeated $n$ times. Using `String` concatenation:

$$
T_{\text{String}}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} k = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = O(n^{2})
$$

Each intermediate `String` is allocated, copied into, and becomes garbage. With `StringBuilder`:

$$
T_{\text{SB}}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \text{amortized}(1) = O(n)
$$

For $n = 10{,}000$ this is the difference between ~50 million char-copies and ~10,000 — a factor of **~5000×** in CPU.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Scenario | Recommended Class | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Storing a constant, shared across threads | `String` | Immutability + SCP caching. |
| Building a query/report in a single method | `StringBuilder` | Fastest, no thread contention. |
| Mutable string shared by multiple threads | `StringBuffer` | Only `StringBuffer` is synchronized. |
| Heavy regex-based transformation | `String` + `replaceAll` | API is on `String`; use `Pattern`/`Matcher` for very large inputs. |
| Logging a `String` in a tight loop | `StringBuilder` | Avoids GC pauses. |
| Database column value | `String` | Values are conceptually constant within a row. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy — String, StringBuilder, StringBuffer

```mermaid
graph TD
    objA["Object"] --> strA["java.lang.String FINAL IMMUTABLE"]
    objA --> chsA["AbstractStringBuilder"]
    objA --> sbfA["java.lang.StringBuffer SYNCHRONIZED"]
    chsA --> sbdA["java.lang.StringBuilder UNSYNCHRONIZED"]
    sbfA -.extends.-> chsA
    chsA --> sbdA
    strA --> intA["implements Serializable Comparable CharSequence"]
    sbfA --> chsA2["implements Serializable CharSequence"]
    sbdA --> chsA3["implements Serializable CharSequence"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> `String`, `StringBuilder`, and `StringBuffer` all implement `CharSequence`. This is why a `StringBuilder` can be passed to any method that takes a `CharSequence` (e.g., `String.join`).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — String Modification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["INPUT: ORIGINAL STRING S"] --> decisionA{"OPERATION TYPE"}
    decisionA -- IMMUTABLE LOGIC --> branchA["USE STRING CLASS"]
    decisionA -- MUTABLE LOGIC --> branchB["USE STRINGBUILDER OR STRINGBUFFER"]
    branchA --> methodA1["CONCATENATE CONCAT"]
    branchA --> methodA2["CASE CONVERSION TOUPPERCASE TOLOWERCASE"]
    branchA --> methodA3["TRIM STRIP STRIPLEADING STRIPTRAILING"]
    branchA --> methodA4["REPLACE REPLACEALL REPLACEFIRST"]
    branchA --> methodA5["SUBSTRING"]
    branchA --> methodA6["JOIN REPEAT"]
    methodA1 --> outA["OUTPUT: NEW STRING OBJECT"]
    methodA2 --> outA
    methodA3 --> outA
    methodA4 --> outA
    methodA5 --> outA
    methodA6 --> outA
    branchB --> decisionB{"MULTI THREADED"}
    decisionB -- YES --> methodB1["STRINGBUFFER APPEND INSERT DELETE REVERSE SETCHARAT"]
    decisionB -- NO --> methodB2["STRINGBUILDER APPEND INSERT DELETE REVERSE SETCHARAT"]
    methodB1 --> outB["OUTPUT: SAME OBJECT MODIFIED IN PLACE"]
    methodB2 --> outB
    outA --> poolA["OLD STRING MAY GO TO SCP OR GC"]
    outB --> heapA["SAME HEAP OBJECT MUTATED"]
```

## 4.3 Memory Layout — Why Immutability Saves Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    scpA["STRING CONSTANT POOL"] --> lit1["HELLO INTERNED"]
    scpA --> lit2["HELLO WORLD INTERNED"]
    scpA --> lit3["HELLO WORLD KTU INTERNED"]
    heap1["HEAP STRINGBUILDER INSTANCE"] --> arr1["BYTE ARRAY CAPACITY 32"]
    arr1 --> chars1["J A V A SPACE O O P SPACE P R O G"]
    heap2["HEAP STRING RESULT OF TOSTRING"] --> arr2["CHAR ARRAY HELLO WORLD KTU"]
    refA["REFERENCE S POINTS TO"] -.-> lit1
    refB["REFERENCE SB POINTS TO"] -.-> heap1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the `StringBuilder` heap block, the **byte array is mutated in place** — no new array is allocated for every `append`. In contrast, every `String` "modification" forces a new entry.

## 4.4 Decision Flow — When to Use Which Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1["IS THE STRING CONSTANT OR SHARED ACROSS THREADS"] --> ans1
    ans1{"YES"} --> rec1["USE STRING"]
    ans1{"NO"} --> q2["DO YOU NEED TO MUTATE THE CONTENT REPEATEDLY"]
    q2{"YES"} --> q3["IS THE OBJECT SHARED ACROSS MULTIPLE THREADS"]
    q3{"YES"} --> rec2["USE STRINGBUFFER"]
    q3{"NO"} --> rec3["USE STRINGBUILDER"]
    q2{"NO"} --> rec4["USE STRING AND ITS REPLACEMENT METHODS"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by immutability of strings in Java? Why is the `String` class designed to be immutable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Immutability of strings means that once a `String` object is created, its content cannot be modified. Any operation that appears to modify a `String` (such as `concat()`, `replace()`, or `toUpperCase()`) actually returns a **new `String` object**, while the original object remains unchanged in the String Constant Pool.

The `String` class is designed as `final` with a `final` internal `char[]`/`byte[]` array to enforce immutability. The benefits are:

1. **Security** — Strings are used in class loading, file paths, network connections, and database access. Immutability prevents unauthorized modification of these critical values.
2. **String Constant Pool optimization** — Identical literals can be shared, saving memory.
3. **Thread safety** — Immutable objects are inherently safe to share across multiple threads without synchronization.
4. **Hashcode caching** — The hash code is computed once and cached, improving performance when strings are used as keys in `HashMap` or `HashSet`.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any two benefits.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `String`, `StringBuilder`, and `StringBuffer` in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `String` | `StringBuilder` | `StringBuffer` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mutability | Immutable | Mutable | Mutable |
| Thread Safety | Inherently thread-safe | Not thread-safe | Thread-safe (synchronized) |
| Performance | Slower for concatenation in loops | Fastest | Slower due to synchronization overhead |
| Storage | String Constant Pool + Heap | Heap | Heap |
| Introduced In | Java 1.0 | Java 1.5 | Java 1.0 |
| Use Case | Constant data, keys | Single-threaded dynamic construction | Multi-threaded dynamic construction |

*(1 mark for any 3 differences with one-word accuracy, 1 mark for the table structure, 1 mark for the use-case distinction.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Modifying Strings**
>
> 1. **Forgetting immutability** — Writing `s = s.append("x")` as if `String` had `append()`. This is a compile-time error; deduct 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong interval in `substring`/`delete`** — Off-by-one errors at boundaries cost 1–2 marks. Always remember **endIndex is exclusive**.
> 3. **Confusing `trim()` and `strip()`** — `trim()` uses the older definition (`<= U+0020`); `strip()` is Unicode-aware. Examiners often test this difference.
> 4. **Saying `==` compares contents** — `==` compares **references** for objects, not values. Use `.equals()`. Mixing these up costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Choosing `StringBuffer` for single-threaded code** — Examiners deduct a mark for performance sub-optimality.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the following methods of the `String` class with suitable examples. (7 marks)
  (i) `concat(String str)`
  (ii) `replace(char oldChar, char newChar)`
  (iii) `substring(int beginIndex, int endIndex)`
  (iv) `trim()` and `strip()`
  (v) `join(CharSequence delimiter, CharSequence... elements)`

**(b)** Write a Java program that uses `StringBuilder` to reverse a given sentence word by word, without splitting it into an array. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**(i) `concat(String str)`** — Appends the argument string to the end of the invoking string and returns a **new** `String`. If the argument has length zero, the original is returned (JVM optimization).

```java
String a = "KTU";
String b = a.concat(" B.Tech");
System.out.println(b);   // KTU B.Tech
System.out.println(a);   // KTU  (original unchanged)
```

**(ii) `replace(char oldChar, char newChar)`** — Returns a new string with **every** occurrence of `oldChar` replaced by `newChar`. Returns the same string instance if no replacement happens.

```java
String s = "malayalam";
System.out.println(s.replace('a', '*'));  // m*l*y*l*m
```

**(iii) `substring(int beginIndex, int endIndex)`** — Returns the substring starting at `beginIndex` (inclusive) and ending at `endIndex` (exclusive). Throws `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` if indices are invalid.

```java
String s = "ObjectOriented";
System.out.println(s.substring(0, 6));   // Object
System.out.println(s.substring(6));      // Oriented
```

**(iv) `trim()` and `strip()`** — `trim()` removes leading and trailing characters whose code point is `\leq U+0020` (i.e., spaces, tabs, newlines). `strip()` (Java 11+) is **Unicode-aware**, using `Character.isWhitespace(int)`. They behave identically for ASCII but differ for Unicode whitespace such as `\u2000` (EN QUAD) or `\u3000` (IDEOGRAPHIC SPACE).

```java
String s = "  KTU  ";
System.out.println(s.trim().length());    // 3
System.out.println(s.strip().length());   // 3
String u = "\u2000Java\u2000";
System.out.println(u.trim().length());    // 5  (trim does NOT remove U+2000)
System.out.println(u.strip().length());   // 4  (strip removes U+2000)
```

**(v) `join(CharSequence delimiter, CharSequence... elements)`** — Static method that concatenates the elements with the delimiter between each pair.

```java
String csv = String.join(", ", "KTU", "CET", "MIT", "NIT");
System.out.println(csv);   // KTU, CET, MIT, NIT
```

*(Allocate ~1.4 marks per sub-part for definition + example + 1 conceptual sentence.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

```java
public class WordReverse {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String sentence = "Object Oriented Programming with Java";
        StringBuilder result = new StringBuilder();
        StringBuilder word = new StringBuilder();

        for (int i = 0; i < sentence.length(); i++) {
            char c = sentence.charAt(i);
            if (c != ' ') {
                word.append(c);                  // build current word
            } else {
                result.append(word.reverse()).append(' ');
                word.setLength(0);                // reset buffer
            }
        }
        result.append(word.reverse());             // append last word

        System.out.println(result.toString());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
`tcejbO detneirO gnimmargorP htiw avaJ`

**Explanation of Logic:**

1. `word` accumulates characters of the current token.
2. When a space is encountered, the current `word` is **reversed in place** and appended to `result`, followed by a space.
3. `setLength(0)` resets `word` without allocating a new object (efficient reset).
4. After the loop, the last word has no trailing space, so it is appended outside.
5. **Why `StringBuilder`?** Reversing requires in-place mutation; using `String` would create O(n²) garbage. (2 marks for the program, 3 marks for in-place reversal logic, 2 marks for the explanation of why `StringBuilder` is the correct choice.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the concept of string immutability in Java. Explain how it affects performance when concatenating strings in a loop, and show with a code example how `StringBuilder` overcomes this. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the following methods of `StringBuilder` with examples: (7 marks)
  (i) `append(...)`
  (ii) `insert(int offset, String str)`
  (iii) `delete(int start, int end)`
  (iv) `reverse()`
  (v) `setCharAt(int index, char ch)`

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Concept of Immutability (2 marks):**
A `String` object in Java cannot be modified after creation. The class is declared `final` and its internal `byte[]` (or `char[]` in older JVMs) is also `final`. Every method that appears to modify a string (e.g., `concat`, `replace`, `toUpperCase`) returns a brand-new `String` object. The original object remains in the String Constant Pool (or heap) and is unreachable only when no references remain.

**Performance Impact in Loops (3 marks):**
Consider:

```java
String s = "";
for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++) {
    s = s + i;     // creates 1000 intermediate String objects
}
```

Each iteration creates a new `String` whose length grows. The total number of character copies is:

$$
\text{Total copies} = 1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = O(n^{2})
$$

For $n = 1000$, this is ~500,000 character copies, plus 1000 short-lived objects causing GC pressure. This quadratic behavior is unacceptable for large $n$.

**Solution with `StringBuilder` (2 marks):**

```java
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++) {
    sb.append(i);
}
String s = sb.toString();
```

`StringBuilder` uses a single backing array that grows geometrically: $\text{new\_capacity} = 2 \times \text{old\_capacity} + 2$. This gives **amortized $O(1)$** per `append`, so the loop is $O(n)$ overall — a 500× speedup for $n = 1000$.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**(i) `append(...)`** — Appends the argument to the end and returns `this` for chaining. Overloaded for all primitive types, `String`, `Object`, `char[]`, etc.

```java
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Count: ");
sb.append(42).append(" items, ").append(3.14);
System.out.println(sb);  // Count: 42 items, 3.14
```

**(ii) `insert(int offset, String str)`** — Inserts `str` at the given offset, shifting existing characters right.

```java
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("JavaSE");
sb.insert(4, " EE");
System.out.println(sb);  // Java EE SE
```

**(iii) `delete(int start, int end)`** — Removes characters in the half-open interval `[start, end)`. If `start == end`, the sequence is unchanged.

```java
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Programming");
sb.delete(3, 8);
System.out.println(sb);  // Prorging
```

**(iv) `reverse()`** — Reverses the entire sequence in place using a two-pointer swap on the backing array. No new allocation.

```java
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("KTU");
sb.reverse();
System.out.println(sb);  // UTK
```

**(v) `setCharAt(int index, char ch)`** — Replaces a single character at the given index. Throws `IndexOutOfBoundsException` if `index < 0` or `index >= length()`.

```java
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Jaba");
sb.setCharAt(1, 'a');   // already 'a' in this case
sb.setCharAt(2, 'v');
System.out.println(sb);  // Java
```

*(1.4 marks per method: 0.6 for behaviour, 0.5 for example, 0.3 for the return-type/chaining note.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Modifying Strings**

- **Immutability:** `String` is `final` and its backing array is `final`; you can never mutate a `String` in place.
- **All `String` "modifications" return a new object.** If you discard the return value, the original is untouched.
- **`StringBuilder` is mutable and not synchronized** — fastest option for single-threaded code.
- **`StringBuffer` is mutable and synchronized** — only choice when a mutable string is shared by multiple threads.
- **Default initial capacity** of both `StringBuilder` and `StringBuffer` is **16** characters.
- **Growth formula:** $\text{new\_capacity} = 2 \times \text{old\_capacity} + 2$.
- **Half-open intervals** in `substring`, `delete`, `StringBuilder.replace` — the end index is **exclusive**.
- **`trim()`** removes characters `<= U+0020` only; **`strip()`** is Unicode-aware (`Character.isWhitespace`).
- **`replace(char, char)`** is **literal character replacement**; **`replaceAll(String, String)`** uses **regex**; **`replaceFirst(String, String)`** replaces only the first regex match.
- **Use `StringBuilder`** inside loops to avoid the $O(n^{2})$ cost of `String` concatenation.
- **`equals()`** compares content; **`==`** compares references — never use `==` for `String` content checks.
- **String Constant Pool (SCP)** holds string literals; identical literals are interned and shared.
- **`String.join(delim, elems...)`** is the modern way to concatenate with a delimiter (Java 8+).
- **`String.repeat(n)`** (Java 11+) returns the string repeated `n` times.
- **Chaining rule:** `StringBuilder` methods return `this`, so `sb.append(a).append(b).reverse()` works in one statement.
- **`setCharAt`** requires `index` to be in `[0, length())`; throws `IndexOutOfBoundsException` otherwise.
- **Performance rule of thumb:** If you perform **3 or more concatenations in a single expression or loop**, switch to `StringBuilder`.
- **Compile-time optimization:** The `+` operator on string literals is folded at compile time, so `"a" + "b"` does **not** create a `StringBuilder` — but `s + "b"` where `s` is a variable **does**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multithreaded Programming - The Java Thread Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multithreaded Programming - The Java Thread Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Multithreaded Programming** is a programming paradigm in which a single process is divided into multiple lightweight sub-units of execution called **threads**, each sharing the same memory space (heap) but possessing its own independent **program counter, stack, and local variables**. This allows concurrent execution of multiple tasks within a single program, thereby improving **CPU utilization, responsiveness, and throughput**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Java Thread Model** refers to Java's built-in architecture for creating, managing, and synchronizing threads. It is built directly into the language via the `java.lang.Thread` class and the `java.lang.Runnable` functional interface, providing native support for multithreaded programming without the need for external libraries. The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) itself runs as a multithreaded application from the moment it starts.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terms, the **Java Thread Model** encompasses four foundational pillars:
1. **The `Thread` Class** — the primary abstraction representing a thread of execution.
2. **The `Runnable` Interface** — a contract for code that can be executed by a thread.
3. **Thread States & Lifecycle** — the finite state machine governing thread behaviour.
4. **Thread Priorities & Scheduling** — the mechanism by which the JVM/OS picks the next runnable thread.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Post Office Counter**
> 
> Imagine a post office with **one clerk** (single-threaded). If 10 customers arrive, each must wait for the previous one to finish completely. Now imagine the post office installs **3 counters** staffed by 3 clerks (multithreaded). All 3 clerks work **simultaneously** from the **same set of resources** (stamps, forms, cash box = shared memory), but each clerk has their **own personal workspace** (pen, register slip = own stack). The "manager" (JVM Scheduler) decides which counter to open next based on urgency (priority).
> 
> - Each **clerk = a thread**
> - The **post office = a process**
> - **Shared resources = heap memory**
> - **Personal workspace = thread stack**
> - The **manager = thread scheduler**

This is exactly how Java threads work — multiple **threads of execution** live inside **one process**, share the **heap**, but each maintains a **private call stack**.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Thread Priorities in Java:** Integer range from **`MIN_PRIORITY = 1`** to **`MAX_PRIORITY = 10`**, with the default being **`NORM_PRIORITY = 5`**.
> - **Main Thread:** Every Java application begins with at least **1 thread** — the `main` thread — created automatically by the JVM to execute the `main()` method.
> - **Daemon Threads:** Background service threads (e.g., Garbage Collector) that terminate automatically when all user threads finish. The JVM exits when the only remaining threads are daemons.
> - **Stack Size:** Each thread receives its own JVM stack. Default size is platform-dependent (typically **512 KB to 1 MB**).
> - **Time Slicing:** Modern JVMs (HotSpot) implement **preemptive, priority-based, time-sliced scheduling** on most platforms.

---

### 1.4 Why Multithreading? — The "Why" Behind the Concept

| Benefit | Real-World Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **Responsiveness** | GUI apps remain interactive while background tasks (file I/O, network) run. |
| **Resource Sharing** | Threads auto-share process memory → easy data exchange without IPC. |
| **Economy** | Creating a thread is cheaper than creating a process (no separate address space). |
| **Scalability (Multi-Core)** | On multi-core CPUs, threads run **truly in parallel** — used in servers, AI inference, gaming engines. |
| **Simplified Program Structure** | Asynchronous tasks modeled as separate threads rather than complex callback chains. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Ways to Create a Thread in Java (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Examiner's Pattern almost always asks: *"Explain the two ways of creating a thread in Java."* — Master this!

#### **Method 1: Extending the `java.lang.Thread` class**

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. Create a subclass of `Thread`.
2. **Override** the `public void run()` method — this is the entry point of the new thread.
3. Instantiate the subclass object.
4. Call `start()` on the object (NOT `run()` directly — calling `run()` executes in the **current** thread, not a new one).
5. The JVM creates a new call stack and invokes `run()` on that new stack.

#### **Method 2: Implementing the `java.lang.Runnable` interface**

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. Create a class that **implements** `Runnable` (functional interface with single method `void run()`).
2. Implement the `run()` method with the task logic.
3. Create a `Thread` object, passing the `Runnable` instance to its constructor.
4. Call `start()` on the `Thread` object.
5. JVM invokes the `run()` of the `Runnable` on a new call stack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Method 2 preferred?** Because Java does not support multiple inheritance. If your class already extends another class (e.g., `Applet`, `Frame`), you **cannot** extend `Thread` — but you can always **implement** `Runnable`. This is the canonical OOP design pattern (Composition over Inheritance).

---

### 2.2 The Thread Lifecycle — Five Logical States

A Java thread can exist in exactly one of **5 states** (defined in `java.lang.Thread.State` enum):

1. **NEW** — Thread object is created, but `start()` not yet called. Thread is not alive.
2. **RUNNABLE** — `start()` has been called. Thread is executing in the JVM OR ready to execute (waiting for CPU time).
3. **BLOCKED** — Thread is blocked, waiting to acquire a monitor lock (entering a `synchronized` block/method held by another thread).
4. **WAITING** — Thread is waiting indefinitely for another thread to perform a specific action (via `wait()`, `join()` without timeout, `LockSupport.park()`).
5. **TIMED_WAITING** — Thread is waiting for a specified time (via `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)`, `LockSupport.parkNanos()`).
6. **TERMINATED** — Thread has completed execution of `run()` (normally) OR threw an uncaught exception. Thread is dead; cannot be restarted.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Rule | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `start()` | Creates new call stack; calls `run()` on it | Calling `run()` directly = NO new thread (viva trap!) |
| `run()` | Entry point for thread's task | Must be overridden/implemented |
| `sleep(ms)` | Static; pauses **current** thread for `ms` milliseconds; **does NOT release lock** | High-frequency viva question |
| `yield()` | Static; hints scheduler to give other threads a chance; **does NOT release lock** | Rare but asked |
| `join()` | Instance; calling thread **waits** for this thread to die | Used for sequencing |
| `wait()` | From `Object` class; releases the lock and waits | Part of inter-thread communication |
| `notify()` / `notifyAll()` | Wakes up thread(s) waiting on the same object's monitor | Must be inside `synchronized` |
| `interrupt()` | Sets interrupt flag; throws `InterruptedException` if thread is in blocked/waiting state | Polite way to stop a thread |
| `setPriority(int)` | Sets priority (1-10); constants `MIN_PRIORITY=1`, `NORM_PRIORITY=5`, `MAX_PRIORITY=10` | Most common marking area |
| `setDaemon(true)` | Marks thread as daemon; must be called **before** `start()` | Common exam question |
| `isAlive()` | Returns `true` if thread has started and not yet died | Used for polling |
| `currentThread()` | Static; returns reference to currently executing thread | Used in `main` to identify Main thread |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** `sleep()` does **not release the lock**, but `wait()` **does release the lock**. This is a 3-mark guaranteed question almost every year.

---

### 2.4 The Main Thread — Special Thread

When a Java program starts:

1. The JVM creates **one user thread** — the **Main Thread**.
2. Main thread invokes the `public static void main(String[] args)` method.
3. All other threads spawned from `main` are **child threads** of Main.
4. The program **does not terminate** until all **non-daemon (user) threads** complete.
5. `Thread.currentThread()` from inside `main` returns the Main thread.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In server applications (Tomcat, Netty), the Main thread initializes the server socket, then spawns worker threads for each incoming request. The Main thread continues to listen for new connections while workers handle requests in parallel — this is the **Reactor Pattern**.

---

### 2.5 Daemon vs User Threads

| Property | User Thread | Daemon Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Default | ✅ All threads are user threads unless marked | ❌ Must explicitly mark with `setDaemon(true)` |
| JVM Termination | JVM waits for all user threads to finish | JVM **does NOT wait** for daemons |
| Typical Use | Business logic, request handlers | Garbage Collector, finalizer, logging flushers, auto-save threads |
| When to set daemon | N/A | **Before** calling `start()` — otherwise `IllegalThreadStateException` |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Thread State Transition Logic

The following derivation walks through the **complete state transition algorithm** of a Java thread from creation to termination. Every transition is mapped to the exact API call that triggers it.

$$
S_{thread}(t) =
\begin{cases}
\text{NEW} & \text{if } t < t_{start} \\[4pt]
\text{RUNNABLE} & \text{if } t \in [t_{start}, t_{term}) \text{ AND no blocking call active} \\[4pt]
\text{BLOCKED} & \text{if waiting for monitor lock (synchronized)} \\[4pt]
\text{WAITING} & \text{if } wait(\infty) \text{ OR } join(\infty) \text{ active} \\[4pt]
\text{TIMED\_WAITING} & \text{if } sleep(t) \text{ OR } wait(t) \text{ OR } join(t) \text{ active} \\[4pt]
\text{TERMINATED} & \text{if } t \geq t_{term}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step-by-step logical evaluation of a thread's lifecycle:**

**Step 1 — Creation (NEW State)**

A thread begins in the NEW state the moment its `Thread` object is instantiated via the constructor. At this point:
- The thread object exists in heap memory.
- No system resources (OS-level thread) have been allocated yet.
- The thread is **not alive** — `isAlive()` returns `false`.

$$ \text{Thread } t = \text{new MyThreadClass}(); \implies State(t) = \text{NEW} $$

**Step 2 — Invocation of `start()` (NEW → RUNNABLE)**

Calling `start()` triggers a sequence of internal operations:
1. JVM validates that the thread is in NEW state.
2. JVM allocates a new **call stack** of default platform-dependent size.
3. The OS schedules the new thread.
4. `start()` returns immediately to the caller.
5. Concurrently (on the new stack), the JVM invokes `run()`.

$$ \text{t.start}() \implies State(t) = \text{RUNNABLE} $$

**Step 3 — Execution (RUNNABLE State)**

The thread executes `run()` instructions. It remains in RUNNABLE as long as no blocking operation is invoked. The scheduler may preempt it at any time to allow other threads to run, but it stays logically RUNNABLE.

**Step 4 — Blocking Transitions (RUNNABLE → BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{RUNNABLE} \xrightarrow{\text{synchronized (lock held by another)}} \text{BLOCKED} \\[4pt]
&\text{RUNNABLE} \xrightarrow{\text{wait() / join() / park()}} \text{WAITING} \\[4pt]
&\text{RUNNABLE} \xrightarrow{\text{sleep(ms) / wait(ms) / join(ms)}} \text{TIMED\_WAITING}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Resumption (Back to RUNNABLE)**

$$
