\begin{aligned}
&\text{BLOCKED} \xrightarrow{\text{lock acquired}} \text{RUNNABLE} \\[4pt]
&\text{WAITING} \xrightarrow{\text{notify() / notifyAll() / unpark()}} \text{RUNNABLE} \\[4pt]
&\text{TIMED\_WAITING} \xrightarrow{\text{timeout expired OR notify()}} \text{RUNNABLE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Termination (RUNNABLE → TERMINATED)**

Termination occurs in exactly one of three ways:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Termination}_{cause} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{NORMAL} & \text{if } run() \text{ returns naturally} \\[3pt]
\text{UNCAUGHT\_EXCEPTION} & \text{if an exception escapes } run() \\[3pt]
\text{STOP\_API\_CALLED} & \text{deprecated; throws } ThreadDeath
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** Once a thread reaches TERMINATED, it **cannot** be restarted. A second call to `start()` throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. This is a one-way trip.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Sleep vs Wait vs Join vs Yield

The following matrix is the **definitive comparison** that KTU examiners test repeatedly. Each row represents one method, and each column a behavioural property.

| Property | `sleep(ms)` | `wait(ms)` | `join()` | `yield()` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Class** | `Thread` (static) | `Object` (instance) | `Thread` (instance) | `Thread` (static) |
| **Releases lock?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Throws `InterruptedException`?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Must be in `synchronized`?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Target** | Current thread | Calling thread (waits on object) | Calling thread (waits for target to die) | Current thread |
| **Duration** | Specified (ms) | Optional (ms) or infinite | Optional (ms) or infinite | None (immediate return) |
| **State Transition** | RUNNABLE → TIMED_WAITING | RUNNABLE → WAITING/TIMED_WAITING | RUNNABLE → WAITING/TIMED_WAITING | RUNNABLE → RUNNABLE (hint) |
| **Wake-up trigger** | Timeout | `notify()` / `notifyAll()` / timeout | Target thread death / timeout | Scheduler chooses |

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example: Priority Computation Logic

**Problem:** Given a system with 3 threads — Thread A (priority 3), Thread B (priority 7), Thread C (priority 5) — all in RUNNABLE state, and the scheduler uses **strict priority-based preemption**, determine the execution order in the first 3 scheduling rounds.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Identify the highest-priority RUNNABLE thread.

$$ P_{max} = \max(P_A, P_B, P_C) = \max(3, 7, 5) = 7 $$

The thread with the maximum priority is selected.

$$ \text{Selected} = \text{Thread B} $$

**Step 2:** Execute Thread B for one time quantum. Repeat the selection logic for the next round.

$$ \text{Round 1: } \max(3, 5) = 5 \implies \text{Thread C executes} $$

$$ \text{Round 2: } \max(3) = 3 \implies \text{Thread A executes} $$

**Step 3:** Cycle continues in **Round-Robin within same priority** if multiple threads share the same priority.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Execution Order (strict priority)} &= B \rightarrow C \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow A \rightarrow \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Real-World Caveat:** Modern JVMs (HotSpot) on most operating systems use priority as a **hint**, not a strict guarantee. The OS scheduler ultimately decides. A higher-priority thread does not necessarily preempt a lower-priority one. This nuance appears in KTU viva.

---

### 3.4 Worked Code Trace: Multi-Thread Output Ordering

**Problem:** Predict the output of the following Java program:

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public MyThread(String name) { super(name); }
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + ": " + i);
        }
    }
}
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread("A");
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread("B");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step logical derivation:**

**Step 1:** Main thread executes. Creates `t1` (NEW state) and `t2` (NEW state). Both are in NEW.

$$ State(t1) = State(t2) = \text{NEW} $$

**Step 2:** `t1.start()` is called. `t1` transitions to RUNNABLE. The OS schedules it on a core. Main thread returns immediately and proceeds.

$$ State(t1) = \text{RUNNABLE} $$

**Step 3:** `t2.start()` is called. Same process as Step 2.

$$ State(t2) = \text{RUNNABLE} $$

**Step 4:** Both threads are now RUNNABLE. The scheduler interleaves their output unpredictably. Possible output sequences include:

$$ \text{Output} \in \{ \text{ABC ordering}\} $$

For example: `A:1, B:1, A:2, B:2, A:3, B:3` OR `A:1, A:2, B:1, A:3, B:2, B:3` — the **order is non-deterministic**.

**Step 5:** After both `run()` methods complete, both threads reach TERMINATED.

$$ State(t1) = State(t2) = \text{TERMINATED} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** The KTU examiner will accept any interleaving as a valid output for this type of question, but will **deduct 1 mark** if the student says "the order is fixed" or "Thread A executes completely first". The keyword is **"non-deterministic interleaving"**.

---

### 3.5 Engineering Derivation: Daemon Thread Termination Logic

**Problem:** A Java application has 1 Main thread (user) and 2 Daemon threads (GC, Logger). The Main thread finishes its work. Will the JVM exit?

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** List all active threads and classify them.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{user} &= \{ \text{Main thread} \} \\
T_{daemon} &= \{ \text{GC thread}, \text{Logger thread} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the JVM termination rule:

$$ \text{JVM continues if } \vert T_{user} \vert > 0 $$

**Step 3:** After Main thread completes:

$$ \vert T_{user} \vert = 0 $$

**Step 4:** JVM now performs the daemon shutdown sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(a) All daemon threads receive an interrupt} \\
&\text{(b) Daemon threads terminate abruptly (no finally blocks guaranteed)} \\
&\text{(c) JVM halts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Yes, the JVM exits, and the daemon threads are killed without graceful shutdown.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Never use daemons for critical work like writing to a database or flushing a transaction log — they may be killed mid-execution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Java Thread Lifecycle — Mermaid State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : new Thread()

    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start()

    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : synchronized lock unavailable
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait() / join() / park()
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep(ms) / wait(ms) / join(ms)

    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify() / notifyAll() / unpark()
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout expired / notify()

    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run() returns normally
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : uncaught exception
    BLOCKED --> TERMINATED : stop() (deprecated)
    WAITING --> TERMINATED : interrupt + exit
    TIMED_WAITING --> TERMINATED : timeout + exit

    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows represent legal state transitions. There is **no arrow** from TERMINATED back to NEW — a dead thread cannot be reborn. The most common transitions in real code are: NEW → RUNNABLE → RUNNABLE (loops internally during execution) → TERMINATED.

---

### 4.2 Two Methods of Thread Creation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Logic in run method] --> B{Choose Creation Method}

    B -->|Method 1| C[Class extends Thread]
    C --> C1[Override public void run]
    C1 --> C2[Create object of subclass]
    C2 --> C3[Call start method on object]
    C3 --> C4[JVM creates new call stack]
    C4 --> C5[run executes on new stack]

    B -->|Method 2| D[Class implements Runnable]
    D --> D1[Implement public void run]
    D1 --> D2[Create object of implementing class]
    D2 --> D3[Pass to new Thread runnable]
    D3 --> D4[Call start on Thread object]
    D4 --> D5[JVM creates new call stack]
    D5 --> D6[Runnable run executes on new stack]

    C5 --> E[Thread running concurrently]
    D6 --> E

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style C fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#4527a0
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `C`, `C1`, etc.) with no reserved keywords. Labels contain only plain text — no bold, italics, or HTML tables — to ensure safe rendering.

---

### 4.3 Thread Memory Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Process[Java Process Memory]
        subgraph Heap[Shared Heap]
            OBJ1[Object A]
            OBJ2[Object B]
            OBJ3[Static Variables]
        end

        subgraph Stack1[Thread 1 Stack]
            F1[Frame: method1]
            F2[Frame: method2]
            LV1[Local Variables]
        end

        subgraph Stack2[Thread 2 Stack]
            F3[Frame: method1]
            F4[Frame: method2]
            LV2[Local Variables]
        end

        subgraph StackMain[Main Thread Stack]
            FM[Frame: main method]
            LVM[Local Variables]
        end
    end

    Process --> Heap
    Process --> Stack1
    Process --> Stack2
    Process --> StackMain

    Stack1 -.->|can access| Heap
    Stack2 -.->|can access| Heap
    StackMain -.->|can access| Heap

    style Heap fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style Stack1 fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style Stack2 fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style StackMain fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** All three threads **share the same heap** (objects, static fields), but each has its **own private stack** (local variables, method frames). This is why **synchronization** is needed when multiple threads access the **same object** — to prevent race conditions on shared heap data.

---

### 4.4 Thread Scheduling Priority Queue — Conceptual Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Scheduler[OS Thread Scheduler]
        Q[Runnable Queue]
    end

    subgraph PriorityLanes[Priority Lanes]
        L1[Lane 1 - Priority 10]
        L2[Lane 2 - Priority 7 to 9]
        L3[Lane 3 - Priority 4 to 6]
        L4[Lane 4 - Priority 1 to 3]
    end

    CPU[CPU Core - Time Slice]

    L1 --> Q
    L2 --> Q
    L3 --> Q
    L4 --> Q
    Q --> CPU

    style L1 fill:#ef5350,stroke:#b71c1c
    style L2 fill:#ff9800,stroke:#e65100
    style L3 fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17
    style L4 fill:#66bb6a,stroke:#1b5e20
    style CPU fill:#42a5f5,stroke:#0d47a1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read:** The scheduler maintains a runnable queue. Threads with higher priority (Lane 1) are picked first. If multiple threads share a priority, they are scheduled in a round-robin fashion. The CPU executes one thread per time slice (typically 10-100 ms), then preempts it and selects the next.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between `wait()` and `sleep()` in Java multithreading.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `wait()` | `sleep()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Class** | Defined in `java.lang.Object` | Defined in `java.lang.Thread` |
| **Lock Release** | **Releases** the monitor lock | **Does not release** the lock |
| **Synchronization Required** | Must be called from a `synchronized` context | No `synchronized` requirement |
| **Wake-up Mechanism** | Woken up by `notify()` / `notifyAll()` or timeout | Only wakes up after timeout expires |
| **Purpose** | Inter-thread communication | Pausing execution for a fixed duration |

> **Awarding Logic:** 1 mark for class distinction, 1 mark for lock release behaviour, 1 mark for wake-up mechanism.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**What is a daemon thread? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **daemon thread** is a low-priority background thread that provides services to user threads. The JVM does **not** wait for daemon threads to complete before shutting down — the JVM exits as soon as all user (non-daemon) threads terminate.
>
> Daemon threads are typically used for **service-oriented background tasks** such as:
> - **Garbage Collection** thread (provided by the JVM itself)
> - **Finalizer** thread
> - **Auto-save** threads in editors
> - **Logging flushers** in servers
>
> A thread can be marked as daemon using `setDaemon(true)` **before** calling `start()`. Example: `t.setDaemon(true);`

> **Awarding Logic:** 1 mark definition, 1 mark example (Garbage Collector preferred), 1 mark for the rule (must be before start).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Java Thread Model. Describe the different states in the life cycle of a thread with a neat state transition diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

**The Java Thread Model (3 Marks):**

The Java Thread Model is the architecture provided by the Java language and the JVM to support multithreaded programming natively. It is built upon the following foundations:

1. **A thread is the smallest unit of execution** scheduled by the OS within a process.
2. **Every Java thread has its own:** program counter, register set, and call stack.
3. **All threads in a process share:** the heap memory (objects) and method area (class metadata).
4. The model is exposed via the `java.lang.Thread` class and the `java.lang.Runnable` interface.
5. The JVM itself is multithreaded (e.g., the Garbage Collector runs in its own thread).

**Thread Lifecycle States (3 Marks):**

A thread exists in one of the following six states (defined in `Thread.State` enum):

- **NEW:** Object created, `start()` not called. Thread is not alive.
- **RUNNABLE:** `start()` has been called; thread is executing or ready to execute.
- **BLOCKED:** Waiting to acquire a monitor lock to enter a `synchronized` block.
- **WAITING:** Waiting indefinitely for another thread's action (`wait()`, `join()` without timeout).
- **TIMED_WAITING:** Waiting for a specified time (`sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)`).
- **TERMINATED:** `run()` has completed normally or an uncaught exception terminated it.

**State Transition Diagram (1 Mark):**

```
NEW ─── start() ──→ RUNNABLE
                      │
       ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
       │              │              │
       ↓              ↓              ↓
   BLOCKED        WAITING     TIMED_WAITING
       │              │              │
       └──── lock ────┴──── notify ──┘
                      │
                      ↓
                 TERMINATED
```

> **Awarding Logic:** 3 marks for model, 3 marks for states, 1 mark for diagram.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Write a Java program to create two threads. One thread should print "Hello" five times and the other should print "World" five times. Use the `Runnable` interface.**

**Model Solution:**

```java
// File: HelloWorldThreads.java
class HelloTask implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println("Hello");
            try {
                Thread.sleep(100); // Small delay for visible interleaving
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println("Hello thread interrupted.");
            }
        }
    }
}

class WorldTask implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println("World");
            try {
                Thread.sleep(100);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println("World thread interrupted.");
            }
        }
    }
}

public class HelloWorldThreads {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Create Runnable objects
        HelloTask helloTask = new HelloTask();
        WorldTask worldTask = new WorldTask();

        // Step 2: Wrap them in Thread objects
        Thread t1 = new Thread(helloTask, "Hello-Thread");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(worldTask, "World-Thread");

        // Step 3: Start both threads
        t1.start();
        t2.start();

        // Optional: Make Main wait for both
        try {
            t1.join();
            t2.join();
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            e.printStackTrace();
        }

        System.out.println("Main thread exiting.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation (for full marks):**

1. **Two classes implement `Runnable`** — `HelloTask` and `WorldTask`. This is Method 2 of thread creation (preferred over extending `Thread`).
2. **`run()` is overridden** in each class with the print loop.
3. **In `main`, two `Thread` objects** are created, each wrapping a `Runnable` instance.
4. **`start()` is called** on each thread — this invokes `run()` on a new call stack for each.
5. **`join()` calls** make the main thread wait until both child threads terminate — ensuring clean output before "Main thread exiting."
6. **Output is non-deterministic** — the 10 print statements appear in an interleaved order (e.g., `Hello, World, Hello, World, ...` OR `Hello, Hello, World, ...` — both are valid).

> **Awarding Logic:** 2 marks for class structure, 2 marks for `main` method with `start()` calls, 1 mark for proper `Runnable` usage, 1 mark for explanation of output non-determinism, 1 mark for `join()` usage.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the two ways of creating threads in Java with suitable examples. Compare them.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**

Java provides two standard mechanisms to create a thread. Both ultimately result in a new thread of execution with its own call stack, but they differ in design philosophy and flexibility.

---

**Method 1: Extending the `Thread` Class (3 Marks):**

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public MyThread(String name) {
        super(name); // Call Thread constructor to set name
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        // Task to be executed in this thread
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " - Count: " + i);
        }
    }
}

public class ThreadExtendsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Worker-1");
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Worker-2");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Steps:**
1. Define a subclass of `java.lang.Thread`.
2. Override the `run()` method with the task logic.
3. Create an instance and call `start()`.

---

**Method 2: Implementing the `Runnable` Interface (2 Marks):**

```java
class MyRunnable implements Runnable {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " - Count: " + i);
        }
    }
}

public class ThreadRunnableDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyRunnable task = new MyRunnable();
        Thread t1 = new Thread(task, "Worker-1");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task, "Worker-2");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Steps:**
1. Define a class that **implements** `Runnable`.
2. Implement the `run()` method.
3. Pass the `Runnable` instance to a `Thread` constructor.
4. Call `start()` on the `Thread` object.

---

**Comparison Table (1 Mark):**

| Aspect | Extending `Thread` | Implementing `Runnable` |
|---|---|---|
| **Inheritance** | Occupies the single inheritance slot | Class is free to extend another class |
| **Flexibility** | Less flexible | More flexible (Composition over Inheritance) |
| **Object Sharing** | Each thread needs its own subclass instance | Multiple threads can share **one** `Runnable` instance |
| **Design Pattern** | Inheritance-based | Composition-based (preferred) |
| **Best Practice** | Avoid in production code | Recommended approach |

> **Awarding Logic:** 1 mark intro, 3 marks for Method 1 + code, 2 marks for Method 2 + code, 1 mark for comparison.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the following thread methods with examples: `join()`, `yield()`, and `setPriority()`.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. `join()` Method (2.5 Marks):**

> The `join()` method allows one thread to **wait** until another thread completes its execution. It is an instance method called on a `Thread` object.

```java
public class JoinDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        Thread worker = new Thread(() -> {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
                System.out.println("Worker: " + i);
                try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
            }
        });

        worker.start();
        worker.join(); // Main thread waits here until worker dies
        System.out.println("Main resumes after worker finished.");
    }
}
```

- **Overloaded forms:** `join()` (waits indefinitely), `join(long ms)`, `join(long ms, int ns)`.
- **State transition:** Calling thread moves from RUNNABLE to WAITING (or TIMED_WAITING if a timeout is provided).
- **Use case:** Sequencing — ensuring a worker thread completes before the main program exits.

---

**2. `yield()` Method (2 Marks):**

> The `yield()` method is a **hint** to the thread scheduler that the current thread is willing to relinquish its current use of the CPU. The scheduler **may** ignore this hint.

```java
public class YieldDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Runnable task = () -> {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
                System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + ": " + i);
                Thread.yield(); // Hint to scheduler: give others a chance
            }
        };
        new Thread(task, "A").start();
        new Thread(task, "B").start();
    }
}
```

- It is a **static** method — affects the **currently executing** thread.
- The thread **remains in RUNNABLE state** — it does NOT go to BLOCKED or WAITING.
- It does **NOT release any lock** it may hold.
- In modern JVMs, `yield()` is rarely useful because the OS scheduler already time-slices aggressively.

---

**3. `setPriority()` Method (2.5 Marks):**

> The `setPriority(int priority)` method sets a thread's priority, which the scheduler **may** use to determine execution order.

```java
public class PriorityDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread low    = new Thread(() -> { for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) System.out.println("Low"); },    "Low");
        Thread normal = new Thread(() -> { for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) System.out.println("Normal"); }, "Normal");
        Thread high   = new Thread(() -> { for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) System.out.println("High"); },   "High");

        low.setPriority(Thread.MIN_PRIORITY);     // 1
        normal.setPriority(Thread.NORM_PRIORITY); // 5 (default)
        high.setPriority(Thread.MAX_PRIORITY);    // 10

        low.start();
        normal.start();
        high.start();
    }
}
```

- **Priority constants:** `MIN_PRIORITY = 1`, `NORM_PRIORITY = 5`, `MAX_PRIORITY = 10`.
- **Default priority of a new thread** = priority of the thread that created it.
- **Main thread's default priority** is `NORM_PRIORITY (5)`.
- If `setPriority()` is called with a value outside `[1, 10]`, it throws `IllegalArgumentException`.
- **Caveat:** Priority is a **hint**, not a guarantee. The OS scheduler has the final say.

> **Awarding Logic:** 2.5 + 2 + 2.5 marks distributed across the three methods with code examples.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Calling `run()` instead of `start()`** — This is the #1 mark-loss trap. If a student writes `t1.run();` instead of `t1.start();`, the program will run **sequentially** in the main thread, not concurrently. Examiners will deduct **2-3 marks** for this mistake.
> 2. **Confusing `wait()` with `sleep()`** — Always remember: `wait()` releases the lock, `sleep()` does NOT. Wrong = 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Forgetting to handle `InterruptedException`** — `sleep()`, `wait()`, and `join()` are checked exceptions. Missing try-catch = compilation error = 0 marks for that part.
> 4. **Setting daemon status after `start()`** — Calling `setDaemon(true)` after `start()` throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. Common 1-mark trap.
> 5. **Thinking priority = strict ordering** — Priority is a hint. Stating "Thread A will definitely run first because its priority is higher" will lose you 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 (Java Thread Model)**

- ✅ A **thread** is a lightweight sub-process. Java supports multithreading **natively**.
- ✅ Two creation methods: **(1) extend `Thread`**, **(2) implement `Runnable`** (preferred).
- ✅ Always call **`start()`**, never `run()` directly. `start()` allocates a new call stack.
- ✅ The **Main thread** is created by the JVM automatically. Use `Thread.currentThread()` to access it.
- ✅ **Six thread states** (in `Thread.State` enum): `NEW`, `RUNNABLE`, `BLOCKED`, `WAITING`, `TIMED_WAITING`, `TERMINATED`.
- ✅ `sleep()` **does not** release the lock; `wait()` **does** release the lock. (Memorize this table.)
- ✅ `sleep()`, `wait()`, `join()` all throw **`InterruptedException`** (checked).
- ✅ `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` must be called from a **`synchronized` context**.
- ✅ Priority range: **1 to 10** (constants: `MIN_PRIORITY=1`, `NORM_PRIORITY=5`, `MAX_PRIORITY=10`).
- ✅ Priority is a **hint**, not a guarantee — the OS scheduler has the final say.
- ✅ **Daemon threads** (e.g., Garbage Collector) terminate when only daemons remain. Must set `setDaemon(true)` **before** `start()`.
- ✅ **`isAlive()`** returns `true` if thread is between `start()` and termination.
- ✅ **`yield()`** is a static hint; the thread stays RUNNABLE and does NOT release locks.
- ✅ **`join()`** makes the calling thread wait for the target thread to die.
- ✅ Once a thread is **TERMINATED**, it **cannot be restarted** — second `start()` throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.
- ✅ All threads in a process **share the heap** but have **private stacks** — this is why synchronization is needed.
- ✅ **Non-deterministic output** is expected from multithreaded programs; the KTU examiner will accept any valid interleaving.
- ✅ **Main thread default priority** is `NORM_PRIORITY (5)`; child threads inherit their creator's priority.
- ✅ Default new thread **is a user (non-daemon) thread** unless explicitly marked.
- ✅ **Inter-thread communication** is achieved via `wait()` / `notify()` / `notifyAll()` on a shared object's monitor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Main Thread
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Main Thread

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **Java Concurrency Model**, the **Main Thread** is the *first non-daemon user thread* that the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** automatically creates and starts when a Java application's `main()` method is invoked. It serves as the *root execution context* of every standalone Java program, from which all additional user-defined child threads are typically spawned.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *Object Oriented Concepts (OEERT613)*, the main thread is treated as the *entry point of multithreading* — the controlling thread that orchestrates the program's lifecycle, holds the reference to the primary `Thread` object, and dictates the default thread priority, name, and group assignments for newly created threads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):**
> Every Java application implicitly possesses **at least one thread** — the *main thread* — responsible for executing the `main()` method. The JVM creates this thread automatically; the programmer does **not** instantiate it using `new Thread()`.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **theatre production**:

- The **Director** arrives on the set the moment the show begins → this is the **Main Thread**. They are *already there* before any actor walks on stage.
- The **Director** then calls actors, gives them scripts, and sends them onto the stage to perform in parallel → these are **child threads** spawned by the main thread.
- The **Director (main thread)** continues managing the overall show, but each **actor (child thread)** performs independently.
- The **show ends** only when the Director's job is done — the program terminates when `main()` returns and the main thread dies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** Consider an **operating system's bootstrap process**. The OS kernel (main thread) initializes hardware, then launches system services (child threads) in parallel. Similarly, the JVM's main thread initializes the runtime and launches user threads concurrently.

---

## 1.3 Key Properties of the Main Thread

| Property | Default Value for Main Thread |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Name** | `"main"` (literal string) |
| **Priority** | `5` (Thread.NORM_PRIORITY) |
| **Thread Group** | `main` (the main thread group) |
| **Daemon Status** | `false` (it is a **user thread**) |
| **Thread ID** | `1` (typically the first non-zero ID) |
| **Created By** | JVM (not by programmer) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** A program can have **only one main thread**, but it can spawn *multiple* child threads from it. The main thread's name is **immutable by default** unless explicitly changed using `setName()`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How the Main Thread Works

The lifecycle of the main thread follows a precise sequence executed by the JVM:

- **Step 1 — Class Loading:** The JVM's class loader locates the compiled `.class` file containing the `main()` method.
- **Step 2 — Main Thread Creation:** The JVM internally invokes `Thread()` and assigns the name `"main"`, priority `5`, and group `"main"`.
- **Step 3 — Method Invocation:** The main thread calls `public static void main(String[] args)` and begins executing statements sequentially.
- **Step 4 — Child Thread Spawning:** Any `new Thread()` or `new Thread(runnableObject).start()` invoked from `main()` creates a *child thread*; the main thread continues independently.
- **Step 5 — Concurrency Phase:** During execution, the main thread and child threads run **concurrently** on the OS's thread scheduler.
- **Step 6 — Termination:** When `main()` returns, the main thread enters the `TERMINATED` state. **However, the JVM does NOT exit** if any *user thread* (non-daemon) is still alive.

---

## 2.2 The `Thread.currentThread()` Mechanism

The **static method** `Thread.currentThread()` returns a reference to the *currently executing thread object*. This is the primary way the main thread refers to itself.

$$\text{Thread current} = \text{Thread.currentThread()}$$

- When called **inside `main()`**, it returns the main thread object.
- When called **inside a child thread's `run()` method**, it returns that child thread's reference.
- This is the **only** way to obtain a reference to the main thread from within a static context, because `main()` is `static` and cannot use `this`.

---

## 2.3 KTU Concept Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Description | Default Main Thread Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `currentThread()` | Static method returning current thread reference | Returns the **main** thread inside `main()` |
| `getName()` | Returns thread's name | Returns `"main"` |
| `setName(String)` | Modifies thread's name | Can rename main to any custom string |
| `getId()` | Returns unique thread identifier | Returns a long (e.g., `1`) |
| `getPriority()` | Returns priority 1–10 | Returns `5` (NORM_PRIORITY) |
| `setPriority(int)` | Sets priority 1–10 | Allowed range: `1` to `10` |
| `isAlive()` | Tests if thread has started and not died | `true` during execution |
| `isDaemon()` | Tests daemon status | Returns `false` for main thread |
| `setDaemon(boolean)` | Marks thread as daemon | Must be called **before** `start()` |
| `Thread.sleep(ms)` | Suspends current thread | Main thread can sleep too |
| `join()` | Waits for thread to die | Main can `join()` on child threads |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thread Priority Constants (defined in `java.lang.Thread`):**
> - `MIN_PRIORITY` = **1**
> - `NORM_PRIORITY` = **5**
> - `MAX_PRIORITY` = **10**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The main thread concept is foundational in:

- **GUI Applications (Swing/JavaFX):** The *Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)* is conceptually a specialized main thread handling UI events.
- **Server Applications (Tomcat, Netty):** The *main thread* accepts connections and spawns worker threads for request handling.
- **Android Development:** The *UI thread* (formerly called main thread) handles all user interface updates — touching it improperly causes `ANR` (Application Not Responding) errors.
- **Embedded Systems (Raspberry Pi / IoT):** The main loop thread coordinates sensor reading, communication, and actuator threads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough: Reference Acquisition and Property Extraction

Below is an exhaustive analytical breakdown of how the main thread is interrogated and manipulated in a typical Java program. Each step is logically derived from the JVM specification.

### Step A — Program Entry Point

When the JVM launches a Java application, it performs the following resolution:

$$
\text{JVM} \rightarrow \text{Locate class with } \texttt{public static void main(String[] args)}
$$

If found, the JVM instantiates the **main thread** object internally:

$$
\text{Thread}_{\text{main}} = \text{new Thread()} \quad \text{(implicit, by JVM)}
$$

The thread is configured with default attributes:

$$
\text{name} \leftarrow \texttt{"main"}, \quad \text{priority} \leftarrow 5, \quad \text{group} \leftarrow \texttt{"main"}, \quad \text{daemon} \leftarrow \text{false}
$$

### Step B — Acquiring Reference via `currentThread()`

The `currentThread()` method is a **static factory** defined in `java.lang.Thread`. Its signature is:

$$
\texttt{public static Thread currentThread()}
$$

Calling this method returns a reference to the **thread that is currently executing the call site**.

$$
\text{Reference}_{\text{retrieved}} = \text{Thread.currentThread()}
$$

- **Logic:** The JVM maintains an internal pointer to the currently scheduled thread in each call stack frame. `currentThread()` dereferences this pointer and returns the corresponding `Thread` object.
- **Result in `main()`:** The retrieved reference points to the main thread.

### Step C — Extracting Properties

Once the reference is obtained, the following calls yield observable values:

| Method Call | Logical Result | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `t.getName()` | `"main"` | Default name set by JVM |
| `t.getId()` | `1` (typically) | First thread allocated |
| `t.getPriority()` | `5` | `NORM_PRIORITY` constant |
| `t.isDaemon()` | `false` | Main thread is always user thread |
| `t.isAlive()` | `true` (during execution) | Thread has started, not yet died |
| `t.getThreadGroup()` | Reference to `"main"` group | Default thread group |

### Step D — Spawning a Child Thread

From within `main()`, a child thread is created using the `Thread` class:

$$
\text{Thread child} = \text{new Thread}(\texttt{new MyRunnable()})
$$

After invoking `start()`:

$$
\text{child.start()} \Rightarrow \text{JVM invokes } \texttt{child.run()} \text{ in a new call stack}
$$

The main thread and child thread now execute **concurrently** on the CPU's thread scheduler.

### Step E — Synchronization via `join()`

The main thread can *wait* for a child to complete:

$$
\text{mainThread.join()} \Rightarrow \text{Block main until target terminates}
$$

This is a **barrier synchronization** — main halts, child proceeds, main resumes only after child dies.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Thread State Transitions of the Main Thread

| State | Trigger to Enter | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `NEW` | (Transient — JVM creates it) | Allocated but not yet started |
| `RUNNABLE` | `main()` is called | Eligible for CPU scheduling |
| `BLOCKED` | Waiting for monitor lock | Trying to enter `synchronized` block held by another thread |
| `WAITING` | `Object.wait()`, `Thread.join()` (no timeout) | Indefinitely waiting for signal |
| `TIMED_WAITING` | `Thread.sleep(ms)`, `join(ms)` | Waiting for fixed duration |
| `TERMINATED` | `main()` returns | `run()` method has completed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The main thread transitions from `NEW` → `RUNNABLE` → (various waiting states) → `TERMINATED`. The JVM keeps the program alive as long as **any user thread is alive**, even after `main()` returns.

---

## 3.3 Common Pitfalls (Analytical)

- **Calling `setDaemon(true)` on the main thread throws `IllegalThreadStateException`** because the main thread is *already started*. Daemon status must be set **before** `start()`.
- **Renaming the main thread does not affect the program entry point** — the JVM still calls the method named `main`, not the renamed thread.
- **Setting priority to an out-of-range value (e.g., 0 or 11) throws `IllegalArgumentException`.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Main Thread Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateNew : JVM creates Thread object
    stateNew --> stateRunnable : main method invoked
    stateRunnable --> stateTimedWaiting : Thread sleep / join timeout
    stateTimedWaiting --> stateRunnable : Timeout expires
    stateRunnable --> stateWaiting : Object wait / join no timeout
    stateWaiting --> stateRunnable : notify / target terminates
    stateRunnable --> stateBlocked : Monitor lock contention
    stateBlocked --> stateRunnable : Lock acquired
    stateRunnable --> stateTerminated : main method returns
    stateTerminated --> [*] : JVM may exit if no user threads remain
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** This Mermaid state diagram illustrates the six legal states a thread (including main) can occupy. Transitions follow the rules defined in `java.lang.Thread.State`.

---

## 4.2 Main Thread & Child Thread Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[JVM Startup] --> B[Create Main Thread]
    B --> C[Name: main, Priority: 5, Daemon: false]
    C --> D[Invoke main method]
    D --> E{Program Logic}
    E --> F[Sequential Statement Execution]
    E --> G[Create Child Thread via new Thread and start]
    G --> H[Child Thread: RUNNABLE State]
    F --> I[Thread sleep or join call]
    I --> J[Main Thread: TIMED_WAITING or WAITING]
    H --> K[Child Thread terminates]
    J --> L[Main Thread resumes RUNNABLE]
    L --> M[main method returns]
    M --> N[Main Thread TERMINATED]
    K --> O{JVM Exit Check}
    N --> O
    O --> P[Exit: All user threads dead]
    O --> Q[Continue: User threads still alive]
```

---

## 4.3 Thread Class Hierarchy (Reference Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[java.lang.Object] --> B[java.lang.Thread]
    C[java.lang.Runnable] -.implements.- D[Custom Class]
    B -.extends.- D
    E[Main Thread Instance] -.is a.- B
    F[Child Thread Instance] -.is a.- B
    style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#bfb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `Thread` class extends `Object` and implements `Runnable`. A custom thread class can either extend `Thread` or implement `Runnable` — the latter is preferred for flexibility and Java's single-inheritance constraint.

---

## 4.4 Functional Flow: Daemon vs. User Thread Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Starts] --> B{Main Thread Type}
    B -->|User Thread default| C[JVM waits for main to die]
    C --> D{Any child user threads alive}
    D -->|Yes| E[JVM keeps running]
    D -->|No| F[JVM exits]
    G[Spawned child thread] --> H{isDaemon status}
    H -->|true Daemon| I[JVM exits even if daemon is alive]
    H -->|false User| J[JVM waits for it to die]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**What is the main thread in Java? Explain its default properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The main thread is the **first thread** automatically created by the JVM when a Java program begins execution. It is responsible for invoking the `main(String[] args)` method and serves as the parent for any child threads created during program execution.

**Default properties of the main thread:**

- **Name:** `"main"`
- **Priority:** `5` (equivalent to `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY`)
- **Daemon Status:** `false` (it is a user thread, so JVM waits for it to terminate)
- **Thread Group:** Belongs to the `"main"` thread group
- **Thread ID:** Typically `1` (assigned sequentially by the JVM)

The main thread is **not** instantiated by the programmer using the `new` operator — it is created *implicitly* by the JVM runtime environment.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]`
**How do you obtain a reference to the main thread inside the `main()` method? Why can't you use `this`?**

**Model Answer:**

A reference to the main thread can be obtained using the **static method** `Thread.currentThread()`. Since this method is `static` and returns the thread object that is currently executing the call, calling it from within `main()` returns the main thread reference.

**Why `this` cannot be used:**

The `main()` method is declared as `public static void`. Because it is `static`, it belongs to the **class**, not to any object instance. Therefore, the keyword `this` — which refers to the current object — is **unavailable** in a static context. `Thread.currentThread()` circumvents this limitation by relying on the JVM's call-stack inspection rather than object reference.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of the main thread in Java. Discuss its default name, priority, and daemon status. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a suitable example, demonstrate how a program can obtain a reference to the main thread and display its properties using `Thread.currentThread()`. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
The main thread is the **first non-daemon user thread** automatically created by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** when a Java application begins execution. It invokes the `public static void main(String[] args)` method and acts as the *root of the thread tree* for the application.

**2. Default Properties (3 marks):**

| Property | Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Name | `"main"` | Used for identification in debugging/logs |
| Priority | `5` (NORM_PRIORITY) | Default mid-range scheduling hint |
| Daemon | `false` | JVM waits for it to die before exiting |
| Group | `"main"` | Default thread group |
| ID | `1` (typically) | Unique identifier |

**3. Lifecycle & Significance (2 marks):**
The main thread's lifecycle is bound to the `main()` method's execution. When `main()` returns, the main thread enters the `TERMINATED` state, but the JVM continues running if other user threads are alive. It serves as the *parent thread* for all child threads created via `new Thread().start()`.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**1. Concept (2 marks):**
The `Thread.currentThread()` static method returns a reference to the thread currently executing the code. Inside `main()`, this method returns the main thread's `Thread` object, which can then be queried using methods like `getName()`, `getPriority()`, and `isDaemon()`.

**2. Example Program (5 marks):**

```java
class MainThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();  // Get main thread reference

        System.out.println("Current Thread: " + t);
        System.out.println("Name: " + t.getName());
        System.out.println("ID: " + t.getId());
        System.out.println("Priority: " + t.getPriority());
        System.out.println("Is Alive: " + t.isAlive());
        System.out.println("Is Daemon: " + t.isDaemon());

        t.setName("MyMainThread");
        System.out.println("Updated Name: " + t.getName());
    }
}
```

**3. Sample Output (1 mark):**

```
Current Thread: Thread[main,5,main]
Name: main
ID: 1
Priority: 5
Is Alive: true
Is Daemon: false
Updated Name: MyMainThread
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> Students often confuse the **string "main"** (the method name) with the **thread name "main"**. These are *independent* concepts. Renaming the thread via `setName()` does NOT affect the method name. Also, do NOT call `t.start()` on the main thread — it is already running.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]`

**(a)** Differentiate between user threads and daemon threads. Why is the main thread classified as a user thread? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain with an example how the main thread can spawn and control child threads using `join()`. Show the output sequence. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Definitions (3 marks):**

- **User Thread:** A high-priority thread that keeps the JVM alive. The JVM does not exit until all user threads have completed execution.
- **Daemon Thread:** A low-priority background service thread (e.g., garbage collector). The JVM exits when only daemon threads remain, *even if they are still running*.

**2. Comparative Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | User Thread | Daemon Thread |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Default for `main()` | Yes | No |
| JVM exit behavior | JVM waits for it | JVM does NOT wait |
| Typical use | Application logic | Background services |
| Created via | Normal `new Thread()` | `setDaemon(true)` before `start()` |
| Priority | Application-defined | Usually low |

**3. Why Main is a User Thread (1 mark):**
The main thread is the program's *primary execution thread*. If it were a daemon, the JVM would terminate the moment `main()` finishes, killing any running user threads prematurely. Hence, JVM designers designate the main thread as a **user thread** by default to ensure controlled program termination.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**1. Concept of `join()` (2 marks):**
The `join()` method causes the **calling thread to wait** until the target thread on which `join()` is invoked completes. If the main thread calls `child.join()`, then main is blocked until the child thread terminates.

**2. Example Program (4 marks):**

```java
class ChildThread extends Thread {
    ChildThread(String name) {
        super(name);
    }
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + ": " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(500); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " finished.");
    }
}

class JoinDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        System.out.println("Main thread starting...");
        ChildThread t1 = new ChildThread("Child-1");
        t1.start();
        t1.join();  // Main waits for Child-1
        System.out.println("Main thread resumed after Child-1 finished.");
    }
}
```

**3. Output Sequence (1 mark):**

```
Main thread starting...
Child-1: 1
Child-1: 2
Child-1: 3
Child-1 finished.
Main thread resumed after Child-1 finished.
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. Forgetting `throws InterruptedException` or `try-catch` around `join()` and `sleep()` — KTU deducts marks for unhandled checked exceptions.
> 2. Calling `t1.run()` instead of `t1.start()` — this executes sequentially in the main thread, **defeating the purpose of multithreading**.
> 3. Not explaining **why** `join()` causes blocking — examiners expect "main thread enters WAITING state until child dies."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "The Main Thread"**

- **Main Thread Definition:** The first non-daemon user thread created automatically by the JVM to execute `main(String[] args)`.
- **Default Name:** `"main"` (can be changed via `setName()`).
- **Default Priority:** `5` (Thread.NORM_PRIORITY), valid range 1–10.
- **Daemon Status:** `false` (user thread) — JVM waits for it to die.
- **Reference Acquisition:** Use `Thread.currentThread()` (static method).
- **Why Not `this`:** `main()` is `static`; no implicit object reference exists.
- **Termination:** When `main()` returns, main thread → `TERMINATED` state.
- **JVM Exit Rule:** JVM exits only when **all user threads** (including main) have terminated, regardless of any remaining daemons.
- **Child Thread Creation:** `new Thread(runnable).start()` — always call `start()`, never `run()` directly.
- **`join()` Semantics:** Causes calling thread to wait for target thread's death.
- **`setDaemon(true)`:** Must be called **before** `start()`; otherwise throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.
- **Priority Constants:** `MIN_PRIORITY=1`, `NORM_PRIORITY=5`, `MAX_PRIORITY=10`.
- **Thread States:** `NEW`, `RUNNABLE`, `BLOCKED`, `WAITING`, `TIMED_WAITING`, `TERMINATED`.
- **KTU Favorite Question Pattern:** "Explain the main thread with its default properties" OR "Differentiate user and daemon threads with main thread as example."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Thread
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Creating Thread # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Thread** in Java is the smallest unit of execution within a process. It is an independent path of execution that shares the process's memory space (heap) but maintains its own **program counter**, **stack**, and **local variables**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Object Oriented Concepts (OEERT613)*, a thread is an object of the `java.lang.Thread` class (or an object whose class implements the `java.lang.Runnable` interface) that can run concurrently with other threads within the same program, enabling **multithreading**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 - Multithreading):** The two officially prescribed methods of creating a thread in Java are: > 1. **By extending the `Thread` class** > 2. **By implementing the `Runnable` interface** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **single chef (single thread)** cooking an entire 5-course meal. Each dish has to wait for the previous one to finish, so guests wait a long time. Now imagine a **restaurant kitchen with 5 chefs (5 threads)** — one grills, one boils, one plates, one bakes, one garnishes. All five work **simultaneously** on the same kitchen (shared process memory), but each has their own cutting board, knife, and recipe (independent **stack**). The kitchen itself is the **process**, and each chef is a **thread**. Similarly, in a Java program: - The **JVM process** = the restaurant kitchen. - Each **thread** = an independent chef. - The **shared heap memory** = the common pantry (ingredients). - Each thread's **stack** = the chef's personal workstation. > [!NOTE] > **Why two ways to create a thread?** > Think of it like choosing how to equip a chef: > - **Extending `Thread`**: The chef *is* a Thread (you get pre-baked capabilities but Java forces single inheritance — your chef can't inherit from another class). > - **Implementing `Runnable`**: The chef *has* a "cookable" task that can be assigned to any Thread (more flexible — your chef can still inherit from another important class). --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Thread States A thread in Java can exist in **6 states** as defined in `java.lang.Thread.State` enum: | State | Meaning | |---|---| | **NEW** | Thread object is created but `start()` not yet called | | **RUNNABLE** | Ready to run / running in JVM | | **BLOCKED** | Waiting to acquire a monitor lock | | **WAITING** | Waiting indefinitely (via `wait()`, `join()`, `park()`) | | **TIMED_WAITING** | Waiting for specified time (`sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)`) | | **TERMINATED** | Execution completed | > [!TIP] > For KTU board examinations, remembering the **two creation methods** and the **Thread class constructor hierarchy** will fetch full marks in Part A questions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Method 1 — Creating a Thread by Extending the `Thread` Class ### 2.1.1 Operational Steps 1. **Declare a subclass** of `java.lang.Thread`. 2. **Override the `run()` method** — this is the entry point for the new thread's execution. 3. **Instantiate** an object of the subclass. 4. **Invoke `start()`** on the object — this internally calls the overridden `run()` in a **new call stack**, NOT in the calling thread. ### 2.1.2 Class Hierarchy of the Thread Class The `java.lang.Thread` class directly implements the `Runnable` interface. ```java public class Thread implements Runnable { ... } ``` Since `Thread` already implements `Runnable`, the `run()` method is declared inside `Runnable` and overridden by `Thread`. ### 2.1.3 Key Constructors of `Thread` Class (High-Yield for KTU) | Constructor | Description | |---|---| | `Thread()` | Allocates a new Thread object | | `Thread(String name)` | Creates a thread with the given name | | `Thread(Runnable target)` | Allocates a new Thread using a Runnable task | | `Thread(Runnable target, String name)` | Creates a named thread for the Runnable task | | `Thread(ThreadGroup group, Runnable target)` | Allocates a new thread belonging to `group` | | `Thread(ThreadGroup group, String name)` | Named thread in a group | | `Thread(ThreadGroup group, Runnable target, String name)` | Full control: group + task + name | ### 2.1.4 Key Methods (Examiner Frequently Tests) | Method | Signature | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `start()` | `void start()` | Spawns new call stack and invokes `run()` | | `run()` | `void run()` | Entry point of thread's task | | `sleep(long ms)` | `static void sleep(long)` | Pauses current thread for `ms` milliseconds | | `join()` | `void join()` | Caller waits for this thread to die | | `getName()` | `String getName()` | Returns thread's name | | `setName(String)` | `void setName(String)` | Sets thread's name | | `getId()` | `long getId()` | Returns thread's unique ID | | `getPriority()` | `int getPriority()` | Returns priority (1–10) | | `setPriority(int)` | `void setPriority(int)` | Sets priority | | `currentThread()` | `static Thread currentThread()` | Returns reference to currently executing thread | | `isAlive()` | `boolean isAlive()` | True if thread has started and not yet died | | `yield()` | `static void yield()` | Hints scheduler to give other threads a chance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **`start()` vs `run()` — Most Common KTU Pitfall:** > - Calling `run()` directly executes it in the **current thread** (no new thread is created). > - Calling `start()` creates a **new thread** and then invokes `run()` inside it. > - Calling `start()` **twice** on the same thread object throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. --- ## 2.2 Method 2 — Creating a Thread by Implementing the `Runnable` Interface ### 2.2.1 Operational Steps 1. **Implement the `Runnable` interface** — provide an implementation for `public void run()`. 2. **Instantiate the implementing class** as a `Runnable` object. 3. **Create a `Thread` object**, passing the `Runnable` instance to its constructor. 4. **Invoke `start()`** on the `Thread` object. ### 2.2.2 Why Use `Runnable` Instead of Extending `Thread`? | Criterion | Extends Thread | Implements Runnable | |---|---|---| | Inheritance Flexibility | ❌ Cannot extend another class | ✅ Can extend any other class | | Object-Oriented Design | ❌ Threading forced via inheritance | ✅ Task separated from threading mechanism | | Multiple Threads Sharing Task | ❌ Each thread is a separate object | ✅ One `Runnable` shared among many `Thread` objects | | Preferred in Industry | Rare | **Recommended Best Practice** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever a question gives a choice, the **Runnable approach** is almost always the expected "best answer" because Java does not support multiple class inheritance, but a class *can* implement multiple interfaces and still extend one class. --- ## 2.3 The `java.lang.Runnable` Interface The interface has exactly one abstract method: ```java @FunctionalInterface public interface Runnable { void run(); } ``` Since Java 8, `@FunctionalInterface` annotation is used — meaning it can be used with **lambda expressions**. --- ## 2.4 Thread Lifecycle Flow (Conceptual) A thread's life moves through: $$\text{NEW} \xrightarrow{\text{start()}} \text{RUNNABLE} \xrightarrow{\text{scheduler}} \{\text{RUNNING}\} \xrightarrow{\text{sleep/wait/join}} \text{WAITING / TIMED\_WAITING / BLOCKED} \xrightarrow{\text{resume / lock acquired}} \text{RUNNABLE} \xrightarrow{\text{run() returns}} \text{TERMINATED}$$ --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > Since this is a programming topic (no physics/math equations), the "formula sheet" is replaced by a **Code-Architecture Cheat Sheet** summarizing every high-yield construct. | Concept | Syntax Template | Exam Keyword | |---|---|---| | Class extending Thread | `class MyT extends Thread { public void run() { ... } }` | "extend Thread" | | Class implementing Runnable | `class MyR implements Runnable { public void run() { ... } }` | "implement Runnable" | | Create & Start (extends) | `MyT t = new MyT(); t.start();` | "call start()" | | Create & Start (implements) | `Thread t = new Thread(new MyR()); t.start();` | "wrap in Thread" | | Get current thread | `Thread.currentThread().getName()` | "current thread" | | Lambda Runnable (Java 8+) | `Runnable r = () -> { ... };` | "functional interface" | | Set/Get name | `t.setName("Worker-1"); t.getName();` | "name the thread" | | Pause execution | `Thread.sleep(1000);` (checked `InterruptedException`) | "sleep" | | Wait for completion | `t.join();` | "join" | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write `t.run()` instead of `t.start()`. This is **not multithreading** — it is a normal sequential method call. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this mistake. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering | Domain | Thread Use Case | |---|---| | **Web Servers (Tomcat, Spring)** | One thread per HTTP request for concurrent client handling | | **Android UI** | Background threads for network/database; UI thread for rendering | | **Real-Time Systems (Embedded)** | Concurrent sensor polling, actuator control | | **Big Data (Hadoop/Spark)** | Worker thread pools for parallel data processing | | **GUI Applications** | Separate thread for long computations to keep UI responsive | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > Since the topic is **programmatic** rather than algebraic, the "derivations" here are **complete code walk-throughs** showing how each thread creation method behaves at the JVM level, including the call-stack execution trace. ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Extending `Thread` ### 3.1.1 Source Code ```java class MyThread extends Thread { public MyThread(String name) { super(name); // Calls Thread(String name) constructor } @Override public void run() { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { System.out.println(getName() + " - Count: " + i); try { Thread.sleep(500); // Pause 500 ms } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted."); } } System.out.println(getName() + " exiting."); } } public class ThreadDemo1 { public static void main(String[] args) { MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Worker-A"); MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Worker-B"); System.out.println("t1 initial state: " + t1.getState()); // Expected: NEW t1.start(); t2.start(); System.out.println("t1 alive after start: " + t1.isAlive()); // Expected: true } } ``` ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Execution Analysis **Step 1:** JVM invokes `main(String[] args)`. The main thread is created by JVM. **Step 2:** `MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Worker-A");` — A `Thread` object is created in the **heap**, but its state is **NEW**. No OS-level thread exists yet. **Step 3:** `MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Worker-B");` — Same as Step 2. **Step 4:** `t1.start();` — The JVM does the following: - Allocates a **new call stack** in JVM memory. - Registers the new thread with the OS thread scheduler. - Transitions `t1` from **NEW → RUNNABLE**. - Invokes the overridden `run()` on the **new stack**. **Step 5:** `t2.start();` — Same mechanism as Step 4, but for `t2`. **Step 6:** `main` continues to print `t1.isAlive()`. Since `start()` is non-blocking, `main` may reach this line while `t1` is still running → returns `true`. **Step 7:** Each worker thread prints its name and counter, sleeping 500 ms between iterations. The output interleaving depends on the OS scheduler (non-deterministic order). ### 3.1.3 Probable Output (order may vary) ``` t1 initial state: NEW t1 alive after start: true Worker-A - Count: 1 Worker-B - Count: 1 Worker-A - Count: 2 Worker-B - Count: 2 Worker-A - Count: 3 Worker-B - Count: 3 Worker-A - Count: 4 Worker-B - Count: 4 Worker-A - Count: 5 Worker-B - Count: 5 Worker-A exiting. Worker-B exiting. ``` > [!NOTE] > The actual interleaving is **non-deterministic** because the JVM delegates to the underlying OS scheduler. For KTU exams, mention this explicitly in viva/practical answers. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Implementing `Runnable` ### 3.2.1 Source Code ```java class MyTask implements Runnable { private String taskName; public MyTask(String taskName) { this.taskName = taskName; } @Override public void run() { Thread current = Thread.currentThread(); for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) { System.out.println(current.getName() + " [" + taskName + "] step " + i); try { Thread.sleep(300); } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println("Interrupted: " + current.getName()); return; } } } } public class ThreadDemo2 { public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException { MyTask task1 = new MyTask("Database-Query"); MyTask task2 = new MyTask("File-Write"); Thread t1 = new Thread(task1, "Thread-DB"); Thread t2 = new Thread(task2, "Thread-FS"); t1.start(); t2.start(); t1.join(); // Main waits for t1 to finish t2.join(); // Main waits for t2 to finish System.out.println("Main: Both workers finished."); } } ``` ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Execution Analysis **Step 1:** `MyTask task1 = new MyTask("Database-Query");` — A `Runnable` POJO is created in the heap. No thread is spawned. **Step 2:** `MyTask task2 = new MyTask("File-Write");` — Same as Step 1. **Step 3:** `Thread t1 = new Thread(task1, "Thread-DB");` — This constructor (`Thread(Runnable target, String name)`) binds the `Runnable` object `task1` to the new thread `t1`. Internally: $$t1.\text{target} \leftarrow \text{task1}$$ $$t1.\text{name} \leftarrow \text{"Thread-DB"}$$ **Step 4:** `t1.start();` — JVM: - Creates new call stack. - Sets the target to `task1`. - When the new stack begins execution, it calls `task1.run()`. - Inside `run()`, `Thread.currentThread()` returns `t1`. **Step 5:** `t2.start();` — Same as Step 4, but for `t2`. **Step 6:** `t1.join();` — Main thread's execution is suspended. It waits in **WAITING** state until `t1` transitions to **TERMINATED**. **Step 7:** `t2.join();` — Main waits again, this time for `t2`. **Step 8:** Once both joins return, main prints the final message and the program exits. ### 3.2.3 Critical Code Line Explained | Code Line | What Happens | |---|---| | `new Thread(task1, "Thread-DB")` | Wraps the `Runnable` task inside a `Thread` object | | `t1.start()` | Creates new OS thread → invokes `task1.run()` | | `Thread.currentThread()` | Returns reference to **executing** thread (here, `t1`) | | `t1.join()` | Caller (`main`) enters `WAITING` until `t1` dies | --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU) | Parameter | Extends `Thread` | Implements `Runnable` | |---|---|---| | Keyword used | `extends Thread` | `implements Runnable` | | Method to override | `run()` from `Thread` | `run()` from `Runnable` | | Object created | `MyThread t = new MyThread()` | `Thread t = new Thread(new MyRunnable())` | | Inheritance slot consumed | Yes (Java single inheritance) | No | | Same task object used for multiple threads | ❌ Not idiomatic | ✅ `new Thread(r).start(); new Thread(r).start();` | | Lambda support (Java 8+) | ❌ Cannot | ✅ `Runnable r = () -> {...}` | | Industry preference | Rare | **Preferred** | | OOP purity | Mixes "task" with "thread" | Separates task from thread | --- ## 3.4 Exhaustive Lambda-Based Runnable (Modern Java — Bonus for KTU Viva) ```java public class LambdaThread { public static void main(String[] args) { // Java 8+ lambda replaces anonymous inner class Runnable task = () -> { for (int i = 1; i <= 4; i++) { System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " -> " + i); try { Thread.sleep(250); } catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); } } }; Thread t1 = new Thread(task, "Lambda-1"); Thread t2 = new Thread(task, "Lambda-2"); t1.start(); t2.start(); } } ``` ### 3.4.1 Step-by-Step Reasoning **Step 1:** `Runnable task = () -> {...};` — Compiler treats this as implementing `Runnable` with the lambda as the `run()` body. `task` is **not yet a thread**. **Step 2:** `new Thread(task, "Lambda-1")` — Thread constructor stores `task` reference internally. **Step 3:** `t1.start();` and `t2.start();` — Both threads share the **same** `task` object. Their `run()` invocations execute concurrently, both iterating the **same** lambda scope but with **separate call stacks** and local variables. **Step 4:** Inside `run()`, `Thread.currentThread().getName()` distinguishes the two threads. > [!IMPORTANT] > Although both threads share the `Runnable` object, **local variables inside the lambda are NOT shared** — each thread gets its own stack frame. Only **fields of the outer enclosing class** (if captured) would be shared. This distinction is crucial for thread-safety questions in KTU. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Thread Lifecycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NEW : new MyThread() NEW --> RUNNABLE : start() RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : synchronized lock contention RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait() / join() / park() RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep(ms) / wait(ms) / join(ms) BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify() / notifyAll() / join target dies TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout expires / lock acquired RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run() returns TERMINATED --> [*] ``` > **Figure description:** A thread enters the JVM as NEW, transitions to RUNNABLE after `start()`, can be moved to BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING depending on synchronization or sleeping, and finally ends in TERMINATED. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Two Methods of Creating a Thread ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Need a new thread in Java?] --> B{Choose Creation Method} B -- Option 1 --> C[Define class extending Thread] C --> C1[Override public void run] C1 --> C2[Instantiate: MyT obj = new MyT] C2 --> C3[Call obj.start] C3 --> C4[JVM spawns new call stack] C4 --> C5[Invokes obj.run on new stack] B -- Option 2 --> D[Define class implementing Runnable] D --> D1[Implement public void run] D1 --> D2[Instantiate: MyR r = new MyR] D2 --> D3[Wrap in Thread: Thread t = new Thread r] D3 --> D4[Call t.start] D4 --> D5[JVM spawns new call stack] D5 --> D6[Invokes r.run on new stack] C5 --> E[Thread is now executing concurrently] D6 --> E ``` > **Figure description:** Two parallel paths for creating a thread — extending `Thread` (Class → Override `run` → Instantiate → `start`) versus implementing `Runnable` (Class → Implement `run` → Instantiate → Wrap in `Thread` → `start`). Both paths converge into concurrent execution. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — JVM Memory Layout With Two Threads ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph JVM_Process["JVM Process Memory"] subgraph Heap["Shared Heap Memory"] ObjT1["WorkerA object
Thread instance"] ObjT2["WorkerB object
Thread instance"] Runnable1["Shared Runnable target
if using Runnable"] end subgraph MainStack["Main Thread Stack"] M1["main frame"] M2["reference to WorkerA"] M3["reference to WorkerB"] end subgraph Stack1["Thread-1 Stack"] S1A["run frame"] S1B["local i, temp vars"] end subgraph Stack2["Thread-2 Stack"] S2A["run frame"] S2B["local i, temp vars"] end end M2 -.points to.-> ObjT1 M3 -.points to.-> ObjT2 ObjT1 -.calls.-> S1A ObjT2 -.calls.-> S2A S1A -.uses.-> Runnable1 S2A -.uses.-> Runnable1 ``` > **Figure description:** Illustrates that all threads share the **heap** (objects), but each thread has its **own private stack** for local variables and method calls. When using `Runnable`, multiple `Thread` stacks can invoke the same `Runnable` target object on the heap. --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — `start()` Internal Call Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Main as Main Thread participant MyT as Worker Object participant JVM as JVM Scheduler participant NewStack as New Thread Stack participant OS as Operating System Main->>MyT: new MyThread() Note over MyT: State = NEW Main->>MyT: start() MyT->>JVM: register new thread JVM->>OS: create native thread OS-->>JVM: thread ID JVM->>NewStack: allocate call stack JVM->>NewStack: invoke run() NewStack->>MyT: run() executes Note over MyT: State = RUNNABLE MyT-->>JVM: run() returns JVM->>OS: terminate native thread Note over MyT: State = TERMINATED ``` > **Figure description:** When `start()` is called, the JVM registers the thread, the OS creates a native thread, a new call stack is allocated, and `run()` is invoked inside it. When `run()` returns, the OS thread is terminated and the Java object enters TERMINATED state. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between `start()` and `run()` methods of the `Thread` class.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | `start()` | `run()` | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Creates a **new thread** and invokes `run()` inside it | Defines the **task code** to be executed | | Thread creation | **Yes** — spawns a new call stack | **No** — executes in the calling thread | | Invocation count | Can be called **only once** per thread object | Can be called **multiple times** like a normal method | | JVM behavior | Registers with OS thread scheduler | Treated as a normal instance method call | | Exception on second call | Throws `IllegalThreadStateException` | No exception | > Calling `t.run()` directly **does not** create a new thread; it just executes `run()` sequentially in the current thread. To achieve multithreading, `t.start()` must be called. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List two ways of creating a thread in Java. Which is preferred and why?** **Model Answer:** The two ways of creating a thread in Java are: 1. **By extending the `java.lang.Thread` class** — Override its `run()` method and invoke `start()`. 2. **By implementing the `java.lang.Runnable` interface** — Define `run()`, then pass the object to a `Thread` constructor and invoke `start()`. **Preferred:** **Implementing the `Runnable` interface.** **Reasons:** - Java does not support multiple class inheritance. If a class extends `Thread`, it cannot extend any other class. - Implementing `Runnable` separates the **task** from the **threading mechanism**, adhering to OO design principles. - The same `Runnable` object can be passed to multiple `Thread` objects, enabling **task sharing**. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **a)** Explain the two ways of creating threads in Java with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **b)** Write a Java program to create three threads that print numbers 1 to 5, alphabets 'A' to 'E', and symbols '*', '#', '@' respectively. Use the `Runnable` interface. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question A** ### Part (a) — Two Ways of Creating Threads **Method 1: Extending the `Thread` Class** The `java.lang.Thread` class itself implements the `Runnable` interface. We can subclass `Thread` and override its `run()` method to define the task. ```java class MyThreadExt extends Thread { public MyThreadExt(String name) { super(name); } @Override public void run() { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { System.out.println(getName() + " => " + i); try { Thread.sleep(200); } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted"); } } } } public class ExtDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { MyThreadExt t1 = new MyThreadExt("Alpha"); MyThreadExt t2 = new MyThreadExt("Beta"); t1.start(); // Spawns new thread t2.start(); // Spawns another new thread } } ``` **Key points:** - `getName()` is inherited from `Thread`. - `start()` creates a new thread; calling `run()` directly would not. **Method 2: Implementing the `Runnable` Interface** ```java class MyTaskRun implements Runnable { private String label; public MyTaskRun(String label) { this.label = label; } @Override public void run() { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { System.out.println(label + " => " + i); try { Thread.sleep(200); } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println(label + " interrupted"); } } } } public class RunDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { MyTaskRun task1 = new MyTaskRun("Alpha"); MyTaskRun task2 = new MyTaskRun("Beta"); Thread t1 = new Thread(task1); Thread t2 = new Thread(task2); t1.start(); t2.start(); } } ``` **Key points:** - The `Runnable` object (`task1`, `task2`) holds the **task**. - The `Thread` object (`t1`, `t2`) holds the **thread mechanism**. - `Thread.currentThread()` can be used inside `run()` to identify which thread is executing. **(7 marks)** --- ### Part (b) — Program Using `Runnable` ```java class NumberPrinter implements Runnable { @Override public void run() { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) { System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " Number: " + i); try { Thread.sleep(150); } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println("Interrupted"); } } } } class AlphaPrinter implements Runnable { @Override public void run() { for (char c = 'A'; c <= 'E'; c++) { System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " Alpha: " + c); try { Thread.sleep(150); } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println("Interrupted"); } } } } class SymbolPrinter implements Runnable { private char[] symbols = {'*', '#', '@'}; @Override public void run() { for (char sym : symbols) { System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " Symbol: " + sym); try { Thread.sleep(150); } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println("Interrupted"); } } } } public class MultiPrinter { public static void main(String[] args) { Thread numThread = new Thread(new NumberPrinter(), "T1"); Thread alphaThread = new Thread(new AlphaPrinter(), "T2"); Thread symbolThread = new Thread(new SymbolPrinter(), "T3"); numThread.start(); alphaThread.start(); symbolThread.start(); } } ``` **Explanation:** - Three separate `Runnable` classes are defined, each encapsulating one task. - Each is wrapped inside a `Thread` object and started. - `Thread.currentThread().getName()` ensures each thread is identifiable in output. - Output order is **non-deterministic** due to scheduling. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **a)** Explain the lifecycle of a thread in Java with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)** **b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates the difference between invoking `run()` directly and invoking `start()` on a thread object. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B** ### Part (a) — Thread Lifecycle A thread in Java moves through the following states (defined in `java.lang.Thread.State`): 1. **NEW** — Thread object is created, but `start()` not yet invoked. 2. **RUNNABLE** — After `start()`, the thread is ready to run or currently running. 3. **BLOCKED** — Waiting to acquire a monitor lock (e.g., `synchronized` block). 4. **WAITING** — Waiting indefinitely for another thread to perform an action (`wait()`, `join()`, `park()`). 5. **TIMED_WAITING** — Waiting for a specified time (`sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)`). 6. **TERMINATED** — `run()` has completed execution. **Diagram (already covered in Section 4.1):** `NEW → RUNNABLE → {BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING} → RUNNABLE → TERMINATED` **Transitions:** - `NEW → RUNNABLE`: by calling `start()`. - `RUNNABLE → BLOCKED`: when entering a `synchronized` block whose monitor is held by another thread. - `RUNNABLE → WAITING`: by calling `wait()`, `join()`, or `LockSupport.park()`. - `RUNNABLE → TIMED_WAITING`: by calling `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)`, or `parkNanos()`. - `WAITING / TIMED_WAITING → RUNNABLE`: by `notify()` / `notifyAll()` / timeout expiry. - `RUNNABLE → TERMINATED`: when `run()` completes normally or via an uncaught exception. **(7 marks)** --- ### Part (b) — `run()` vs `start()` Demonstration ```java class DemoThread extends Thread { public DemoThread(String name) { super(name); } @Override public void run() { for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) { System.out.println(getName() + " running -> " + i); try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted"); } } } } public class RunVsStart { public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException { DemoThread t1 = new DemoThread("Thread-X"); DemoThread t2 = new DemoThread("Thread-Y"); System.out.println("--- Case 1: Calling run() directly ---"); t1.run(); // Executes in MAIN thread, NOT in a new thread System.out.println("After t1.run(), main thread name: " + Thread.currentThread().getName()); System.out.println("\n--- Case 2: Calling start() ---"); t2.start(); // Creates a NEW thread for execution System.out.println("After t2.start(), main thread name: " + Thread.currentThread().getName()); t2.join(); // Main waits for t2 to finish System.out.println("\nMain thread finished."); } } ``` **Probable Output:** ``` --- Case 1: Calling run() directly --- Thread-X running -> 1 Thread-X running -> 2 Thread-X running -> 3 After t1.run(), main thread name: main --- Case 2: Calling start() --- After t2.start(), main thread name: main Thread-Y running -> 1 Thread-Y running -> 2 Thread-Y running -> 3 Main thread finished. ``` **Explanation:** - **Case 1:** `t1.run()` executes the `run()` method **as a normal instance method** in the **`main` thread**. No new thread is created. The output is fully sequential. - **Case 2:** `t2.start()` creates a **new thread** called `Thread-Y`.
