Its `run()` executes **concurrently** with `main`, hence the interleaved output where `main` prints its message before `Thread-Y` prints its first iteration. - After `t2.join()`, `main` waits for `Thread-Y` to die, ensuring the "Main thread finished" message is printed **last**. **(7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **2 marks deducted** if `run()` is shown without `@Override` annotation in code. > - **1–2 marks deducted** if `InterruptedException` is missing in code that uses `sleep()` / `wait()` / `join()`. > - **2 marks deducted** if `t.run()` is written instead of `t.start()` in multithreading code (this makes the code **sequential**, not concurrent). > - **1 mark deducted** if the answer does not mention that `start()` can only be called **once** per thread object. > - **3 marks deducted** if the answer does not explain why `Runnable` is preferred over extending `Thread` (single inheritance limitation). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution; Java supports multithreading. - **Two official ways** to create a thread: 1. Extend `java.lang.Thread` and override `run()`. 2. Implement `java.lang.Runnable` and pass the object to a `Thread` constructor. - **`start()`** creates a new thread → invokes `run()` in that thread. - **`run()`** holds the task code; calling it directly is **not multithreading**. - `start()` can only be called **once**; otherwise `IllegalThreadStateException` is thrown. - **`Runnable` is preferred** because Java lacks multiple class inheritance. - A class implementing `Runnable` **can still extend another class**; a class extending `Thread` **cannot**. - The same `Runnable` object **can be shared** across multiple `Thread` objects. - Since Java 8, `Runnable` is a **functional interface** — usable with **lambda expressions**. - Thread states: **NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED**. - `Thread.currentThread()` returns a reference to the executing thread. - `Thread.sleep(ms)` pauses the current thread (checked `InterruptedException`). - `t.join()` makes the caller wait until thread `t` finishes. - `getName()` / `setName()` manage thread names for debugging. - **Constructors to remember for KTU:** `Thread()`, `Thread(String)`, `Thread(Runnable)`, `Thread(Runnable, String)`, `Thread(ThreadGroup, Runnable)`. - **Always handle `InterruptedException`** in code that calls `sleep()`, `wait()`, `join()`. - The JVM uses the OS thread scheduler — execution order is **non-deterministic**.

topics.title: Creating Multiple Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Creating Multiple Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language (and the broader Object-Oriented paradigm), a **Thread** is the smallest independent unit of execution within a process. Every Java program has at least one thread — the **main thread** — which is spawned automatically by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) when the program begins execution.

**Creating Multiple Threads** refers to the mechanism by which a single Java program launches **two or more concurrent execution paths** that operate in parallel (on multi-core CPUs) or in an interleaved fashion (on single-core CPUs via time-slicing), all coexisting within the same process and sharing the same memory heap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEERT613 - Module 3):**
> *"Multithreading is a Java feature that allows concurrent execution of two or more parts of a program for maximum utilization of CPU. Each part of such a program is called a thread."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **single chef cooking in a kitchen**. He chops vegetables, then boils water, then stirs the gravy, then serves the plate. All tasks are done **sequentially** (one-threaded program).

Now imagine **three chefs in the same kitchen**:
- Chef 1 chops vegetables
- Chef 2 boils water
- Chef 3 stirs the gravy

All three work **simultaneously**, sharing the same kitchen (memory), the same gas stove (CPU), the same spices (resources). This is **multithreading** — multiple independent workers (threads) cooperating inside the same kitchen (process) to finish the meal (program) faster.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just like chefs share a kitchen, threads in Java share the same **heap memory** but each thread has its own **stack memory** (for local variables and method calls).

### 1.3 Two Official Ways to Create Threads in Java

The Java API provides **two standard mechanisms** to create a new thread, both defined in the `java.lang` package:

| # | Method | Mechanism | Key Class/Interface |
|---|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Extending the Thread class** | Inheritance (subclassing) | `java.lang.Thread` |
| 2 | **Implementing the Runnable interface** | Composition (preferred, since Java does not support multiple class inheritance) | `java.lang.Runnable` |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thread Priority Constants (defined in `Thread` class):**
> - `Thread.MIN_PRIORITY` = **1**
> - `Thread.NORM_PRIORITY` = **5** (default)
> - `Thread.MAX_PRIORITY` = **10**

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention:** A thread is automatically assigned a default name in the format `Thread-N` (e.g., `Thread-0`, `Thread-1`), unless explicitly named using the `Thread(String name)` constructor or the `setName()` method.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1: Creating a Thread by Extending the `Thread` Class

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic Steps

1. **Declare a subclass** of `java.lang.Thread`.
2. **Override the `run()` method** — this method contains the code that constitutes the new thread's task. The JVM executes whatever is inside `run()` when the thread is started.
3. **Instantiate an object** of your subclass. This object represents a Thread instance, but the thread is **not yet running**.
4. **Invoke the `start()` method** on the instance. This performs two critical actions:
   - Allocates a new call stack for the new thread.
   - Invokes the `run()` method in that newly allocated stack, causing the thread to enter the **runnable state**.
5. The original (calling) thread continues immediately after `start()` returns — it does **not** wait for the new thread to finish.

### 2.1.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why override `run()`?** Because `Thread`'s default `run()` does nothing. The thread's "job" is whatever you place inside `run()`.
- **Why call `start()` and not `run()` directly?** Calling `run()` directly executes the code in the **current** thread (no new thread is created). Only `start()` triggers the JVM to create a new call stack and dispatch a new OS thread.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often call `run()` instead of `start()`. This results in **no multithreading** — the code runs sequentially. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 2.2 Method 2: Creating a Thread by Implementing the `Runnable` Interface

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic Steps

1. **Declare a class that implements `java.lang.Runnable`**. The `Runnable` interface contains a single abstract method signature: `public void run()`.
2. **Provide an implementation of the `run()` method** inside your class. This contains the task that the thread will execute.
3. **Instantiate an object** of your class implementing `Runnable` (this is **not yet a thread** — it is a "task").
4. **Pass the `Runnable` object as a constructor argument** to a new `Thread` object: `Thread t = new Thread(myRunnableObj);`
5. **Invoke `start()`** on the `Thread` object. The JVM creates a new thread that, when scheduled, calls the `run()` method of your `Runnable` object.

### 2.2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why prefer `Runnable` over extending `Thread`?** Java does not support multiple class inheritance. If your class extends `Thread`, it **cannot extend any other class**. By implementing `Runnable`, your class is free to extend another class — this preserves OOP flexibility.
- **Why separate the "task" (`Runnable`) from the "worker" (`Thread`)?** This is a classic OOP design pattern: **separation of concerns**. The same `Runnable` task object can be passed to multiple `Thread` objects, enabling the same job to be executed by several workers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Return / Unit | Notes |
|---------|------------------|---------------|-------|
| Thread class extension | `class MyThread extends Thread { public void run() { ... } }` | Class declaration | Single inheritance locked |
| Runnable implementation | `class MyTask implements Runnable { public void run() { ... } }` | Class declaration | Preferred OOP approach |
| Thread constructor (Runnable) | `Thread(Runnable target)` | Thread object | Most common constructor |
| Thread constructor (named) | `Thread(Runnable target, String name)` | Thread object | Sets thread name |
| Start thread | `t.start()` | void | Creates new call stack, calls `run()` |
| Run method | `t.run()` | void | Executes in current thread (NOT multithreaded) |
| Current thread reference | `Thread.currentThread()` | Thread | Returns calling thread |
| Get thread name | `t.getName()` | String | Default: `Thread-N` |
| Set thread name | `t.setName(String name)` | void | Custom naming |
| Get priority | `t.getPriority()` | int (1–10) | Default: 5 |
| Set priority | `t.setPriority(int p)` | void | Range: `MIN_PRIORITY` to `MAX_PRIORITY` |
| Sleep (static) | `Thread.sleep(long millis)` | void | Throws `InterruptedException` |
| Join | `t.join()` | void | Caller waits for `t` to die |
| Yield (static) | `Thread.yield()` | void | Hints scheduler to give up CPU |
| Check alive | `t.isAlive()` | boolean | True if started but not yet dead |
| Daemon flag | `t.setDaemon(boolean on)` | void | JVM exits when only daemons remain |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications of Multithreading:**
> - **Web Servers (Tomcat, Jetty):** Each incoming HTTP request is handled by a separate thread from a thread pool.
> - **GUI Applications (Swing/JavaFX):** The Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) handles UI events separately from background computations, preventing the interface from "freezing."
> - **Real-time Data Processing:** Financial trading systems use thousands of threads to monitor market feeds simultaneously.
> - **Game Development:** Rendering, physics simulation, and AI logic run as separate threads for smooth 60 FPS performance.
> - **Database Connection Pools:** Each query may be executed on its own thread for parallel throughput.

## 2.5 Important `Thread` Class Methods Reference

| Method | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `start()` | Instance | Causes this thread to begin execution; JVM calls `run()`. |
| `run()` | Instance | Entry point for the new thread's task. |
| `sleep(long ms)` | Static | Pauses the **current** thread for `ms` milliseconds. |
| `join()` | Instance | Calling thread waits until this thread terminates. |
| `join(long ms)` | Instance | Waits at most `ms` milliseconds for this thread to die. |
| `yield()` | Static | Hints the scheduler that the current thread is willing to yield. |
| `interrupt()` | Instance | Interrupts a sleeping/waiting thread. |
| `isAlive()` | Instance | Returns `true` if the thread has been started and not yet died. |
| `setDaemon(boolean)` | Instance | Marks thread as daemon (background service thread). |
| `currentThread()` | Static | Returns a reference to the currently executing thread. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Method 1: Extending `Thread` Class — Complete Java Program

Below is a fully working, syntactically verified Java program demonstrating thread creation by extending `Thread`.

```java
// File: ThreadByExtension.java
class MyThread extends Thread {

    // Constructor to set a custom thread name
    public MyThread(String name) {
        super(name);   // Calls Thread(String name) constructor
    }

    // Override the run() method - this is the thread's task
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " - Count: " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(500);   // Pause 500ms to simulate work
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " has finished execution.");
    }
}

public class ThreadByExtension {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        // Step 1: Create two thread objects
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Worker-A");
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Worker-B");

        // Step 2: Display default priorities
        System.out.println(t1.getName() + " priority: " + t1.getPriority());
        System.out.println(t2.getName() + " priority: " + t2.getPriority());

        // Step 3: Start the threads (NOT run())
        t1.start();
        t2.start();

        System.out.println("Main thread has finished launching workers.");
    }
}
```

### 3.1.1 Sample Output (Order May Vary — Nondeterministic!)

```
Worker-A priority: 5
Worker-B priority: 5
Main thread has finished launching workers.
Worker-A - Count: 1
Worker-B - Count: 1
Worker-A - Count: 2
Worker-B - Count: 2
Worker-A - Count: 3
Worker-B - Count: 3
Worker-A - Count: 4
Worker-B - Count: 4
Worker-A - Count: 5
Worker-B - Count: 5
Worker-A has finished execution.
Worker-B has finished execution.
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the output order unpredictable?**
> Once `start()` is called, the JVM's thread scheduler decides the order of execution. On multi-core CPUs, both threads may literally run at the same instant. On single-core CPUs, they are time-sliced. The Java specification does **not** guarantee any specific execution order between threads.

## 3.2 Method 2: Implementing `Runnable` Interface — Complete Java Program

```java
// File: ThreadByRunnable.java
class MyTask implements Runnable {

    private final String taskName;

    public MyTask(String taskName) {
        this.taskName = taskName;
    }

    // Must implement the single run() method of Runnable
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(taskName + " executing step " + i
                    + " on " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
            try {
                Thread.sleep(400);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(taskName + " was interrupted.");
            }
        }
    }
}

public class ThreadByRunnable {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        // Step 1: Create task objects (these are NOT threads yet)
        MyTask task1 = new MyTask("Download-Task");
        MyTask task2 = new MyTask("Upload-Task");
        MyTask task3 = new MyTask("Backup-Task");

        // Step 2: Wrap each task inside a Thread object
        Thread t1 = new Thread(task1, "Download-Worker");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task2, "Upload-Worker");
        Thread t3 = new Thread(task3, "Backup-Worker");

        // Step 3: Set custom priorities
        t1.setPriority(Thread.MAX_PRIORITY);    // 10
        t2.setPriority(Thread.NORM_PRIORITY);   // 5
        t3.setPriority(Thread.MIN_PRIORITY);    // 1

        // Step 4: Start all threads
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        t3.start();

        // Step 5: Main thread waits for t1 to finish before printing
        try {
            t1.join();
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("Main thread interrupted while waiting.");
        }

        System.out.println("Download task is confirmed complete. Main exiting.");
    }
}
```

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Analysis of the Above Code

**Step 1 — Task Creation:** `MyTask` is a plain class implementing `Runnable`. It encapsulates a unit of work (a "task") but is **not** a thread. It cannot call `start()` on itself.

**Step 2 — Thread Wrapping:** Each `MyTask` object is passed to the `Thread(Runnable target, String name)` constructor. At this moment, a Thread object exists, but the underlying OS thread has **not** been created yet — the thread is in the **NEW** state.

**Step 3 — Priority Configuration:** `setPriority()` accepts an integer in the range **[1, 10]**. The OS scheduler uses this as a **hint**, not a hard guarantee. Higher-priority threads *tend* to get more CPU time, but starvation can still occur.

**Step 4 — Thread Activation:** `t1.start()` triggers the JVM to allocate a new Java stack and call `t1`'s `run()` method on a new OS thread. The same happens for `t2` and `t3` almost instantaneously. All three threads are now in the **RUNNABLE** state.

**Step 5 — Synchronization via `join()`:** The main thread calls `t1.join()`, which blocks the main thread until `t1` terminates. This is a classic way to enforce ordering between threads.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Extending `Thread` | Implementing `Runnable` |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Inheritance | Consumes the single-inheritance slot | Leaves inheritance free |
| OOP Purity | Lower — inheritance misused for code reuse | Higher — composition used properly |
| Reusability of task | Task tied to a Thread subclass | Same `Runnable` can be passed to many Threads |
| Access to `Thread` methods | Direct (e.g., `this.start()`) | Indirect (via `Thread.currentThread()`) |
| Recommended by Java docs | Discouraged for new code | **Recommended** |
| Use case | Quick demos, simple cases | Production code, thread pools |

## 3.4 Thread Lifecycle States (Six States per Java Documentation)

The complete lifecycle of a Java thread is described by the following **six states** defined in `java.lang.Thread.State`:

$$
\text{NEW} \xrightarrow{\text{start()}} \text{RUNNABLE} \xrightarrow{\text{scheduler}} \begin{cases} \text{BLOCKED} \\ \text{WAITING} \\ \text{TIMED\_WAITING} \end{cases} \rightarrow \text{TERMINATED}
$$

| State | Description | How it Enters |
|-------|-------------|---------------|
| **NEW** | Thread object created but `start()` not yet called | `Thread t = new Thread(...)` |
| **RUNNABLE** | Thread is ready to run, waiting for CPU | `t.start()` |
| **BLOCKED** | Waiting to acquire a monitor lock (synchronized block) | Entering `synchronized` method |
| **WAITING** | Waiting indefinitely for another thread's action | `wait()`, `join()` (no timeout) |
| **TIMED_WAITING** | Waiting for a specified time period | `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)` |
| **TERMINATED** | Thread has completed execution | `run()` returns normally or throws |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula — Total Time Slice Approximation:**
> If a single-core CPU runs $N$ threads, each receiving an equal time slice of $T_{\text{slice}}$ milliseconds, then the average time between two consecutive executions of the same thread is approximately:
> $$\text{T}_{\text{context\_switch}} = (N - 1) \cdot T_{\text{slice}}$$
> This is a useful heuristic for understanding why multithreading does not always yield linear speedup.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thread Creation Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of creating multiple threads in Java, including both creation paths and the lifecycle transitions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Java Program Starts] --> B[JVM Creates Main Thread]
    B --> C{Choose Creation Method}

    C -->|Method 1| D1[Declare class extending Thread]
    D1 --> D2[Override public void run method]
    D2 --> D3[Instantiate subclass object]
    D3 --> D4[Invoke start method]
    D4 --> E1[NEW STATE]

    C -->|Method 2| F1[Declare class implementing Runnable]
    F1 --> F2[Implement public void run method]
    F2 --> F3[Instantiate task object]
    F3 --> F4[Pass to new Thread constructor]
    F4 --> F5[Invoke start method]
    F5 --> E1

    E1 --> G[RUNNABLE STATE]
    G --> H{Scheduler Decision}
    H -->|CPU available| I[RUNNING - executes run method]
    H -->|CPU not available| G

    I --> J{Suspend Condition}
    J -->|sleep ms| K[TIMED_WAITING]
    J -->|join or wait| L[WAITING]
    J -->|lock contention| M[BLOCKED]
    J -->|run completes| N[TERMINATED]

    K --> G
    L --> G
    M --> G

    I -.->|yield method| G
```

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram: Two Creation Paths

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph path1[Path 1 - Extend Thread]
        P1A[Your Class] -->|extends| P1B[java.lang.Thread]
        P1B --> P1C[Override run method]
        P1C --> P1D[Object.start method]
    end

    subgraph path2[Path 2 - Implement Runnable]
        P2A[Your Class] -->|implements| P2B[java.lang.Runnable]
        P2B --> P2C[Implement run method]
        P2C --> P2D[Wrap in new Thread obj]
        P2D --> P2E[Object.start method]
    end

    P1D --> final[Thread Begins Execution]
    P2E --> final

    final --> result[New Call Stack Allocated by JVM]
    result --> runcall[run method Invoked on New Thread]
```

## 4.3 Thread State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : new Thread obj
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start method
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : lock contention
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait or join
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify or thread dies
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep or wait ms
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout expires
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run returns
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows represent **state transitions triggered by method calls or scheduler decisions**. The `[*]` symbols denote the start and end of the lifecycle. Notice that a thread **cannot** return to `NEW` from any state — once started, it can never be restarted.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define a thread in Java. List any two ways of creating threads in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **thread** in Java is the smallest independent unit of execution within a program. It is a lightweight subprocess that shares the same memory (heap) with other threads of the same process. Every Java application has at least one thread — the **main thread** — created automatically by the JVM.

The two standard ways of creating threads in Java are:

1. **By extending the `java.lang.Thread` class** and overriding its `run()` method.
2. **By implementing the `java.lang.Runnable` interface** and providing the `run()` method, then passing the object to a `Thread` constructor.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two methods.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Differentiate between `start()` and `run()` methods of the `Thread` class.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | `start()` | `run()` |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Action** | Creates a new call stack and a new OS thread | Executes code in the **current** call stack |
| **Multithreading** | Yes — initiates a new thread | No — runs sequentially in the calling thread |
| **Invocation** | Called only **once** per thread object | Can be called any number of times |
| **Purpose** | Activates the thread for execution | Contains the task to be executed |

*(1.5 marks for each difference — cover at least two distinct points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the two ways of creating threads in Java with suitable code examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Way 1: Extending the `Thread` Class**

A new class is created that extends `java.lang.Thread`. The `run()` method of `Thread` is overridden to define the task.

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public MyThread(String name) { super(name); }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " - " + i);
        }
    }
}

public class Demo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Alpha");
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Beta");
        t1.start();   // Creates new thread
        t2.start();   // Creates another new thread
    }
}
```

**Way 2: Implementing the `Runnable` Interface**

A class implements `Runnable` and provides the `run()` method. The object is then wrapped in a `Thread` object.

```java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " - " + i);
        }
    }
}

public class Demo2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyTask task = new MyTask();
        Thread t1 = new Thread(task, "Worker-1");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task, "Worker-2");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Key Comparison:** Implementing `Runnable` is preferred because Java does not support multiple class inheritance; implementing `Runnable` leaves the class free to extend another class. *(7 marks — 3 for first method with code, 3 for second method with code, 1 for comparison.)*

---

**(b) Write a Java program to create three threads that print numbers 1 to 5 with a 300 ms delay between prints. Set different priorities for each thread and display the thread name in the output. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```java
class NumberThread extends Thread {

    public NumberThread(String name) {
        super(name);
    }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " prints: " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(300);   // 300 ms delay
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " has finished.");
    }
}

public class ThreeThreadsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        NumberThread t1 = new NumberThread("HighPriority");
        NumberThread t2 = new NumberThread("NormalPriority");
        NumberThread t3 = new NumberThread("LowPriority");

        t1.setPriority(Thread.MAX_PRIORITY);    // 10
        t2.setPriority(Thread.NORM_PRIORITY);   // 5
        t3.setPriority(Thread.MIN_PRIORITY);    // 1

        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        t3.start();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Points:**

- The class `NumberThread` extends `Thread` and overrides `run()`. *(1 mark)*
- Three thread objects are created with descriptive names. *(1 mark)*
- `setPriority()` is used to assign distinct priorities (10, 5, 1). *(2 marks)*
- `Thread.sleep(300)` introduces a 300 ms delay; the `InterruptedException` is caught. *(1 mark)*
- `start()` (not `run()`) is called to launch the threads concurrently. *(1 mark)*
- The output order is non-deterministic because thread scheduling is JVM/OS dependent. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) What is a daemon thread? Write a Java program that creates one user thread and one daemon thread, and explain the difference in their termination behavior. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A **daemon thread** is a low-priority background service thread that provides services to user threads. The JVM does not wait for daemon threads to complete before shutting down — when all user threads finish, the JVM terminates all daemon threads automatically and exits.

**Program:**

```java
class MyDaemon extends Thread {
    public MyDaemon(String name) { super(name); }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        if (isDaemon()) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " is a Daemon thread. Running...");
        } else {
            System.out.println(getName() + " is a User thread. Running...");
        }
        for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " - tick " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(400);
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " completed normally.");
    }
}

public class DaemonDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        MyDaemon userThread = new MyDaemon("UserThread");
        MyDaemon daemonThread = new MyDaemon("DaemonThread");

        daemonThread.setDaemon(true);   // Mark as daemon BEFORE start()

        userThread.start();
        daemonThread.start();

        System.out.println("Main thread ends here.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**

- `setDaemon(true)` must be called **before** `start()`; otherwise `IllegalThreadStateException` is thrown. *(2 marks)*
- The daemon thread is killed automatically by the JVM when `main` and `userThread` complete, even if its loop has not finished. *(2 marks)*
- The user thread is allowed to complete its full iteration loop before JVM exits. *(2 marks)*
- This difference demonstrates that daemon threads are intended for background services like garbage collection, auto-save, etc. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) Explain the six states of a thread lifecycle in Java with the methods that cause transitions between states. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | State | Description | Caused By |
|---|-------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | **NEW** | Thread object created; `start()` not yet called | `new Thread(...)` |
| 2 | **RUNNABLE** | Thread is ready; waiting for CPU time | `start()` |
| 3 | **BLOCKED** | Waiting to acquire an object's monitor lock | Entering `synchronized` block |
| 4 | **WAITING** | Waiting indefinitely until notified | `wait()`, `join()` without timeout |
| 5 | **TIMED_WAITING** | Waiting for a specified time period | `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)` |
| 6 | **TERMINATED** | Thread has finished execution | `run()` returns or throws uncaught exception |

**Key Transitions:**

- `NEW → RUNNABLE`: Calling the `start()` method. *(1 mark)*
- `RUNNABLE → BLOCKED`: Attempting to enter a `synchronized` method/block already held by another thread. *(1 mark)*
- `RUNNABLE → WAITING`: Calling `wait()` (no timeout) on a locked object or `join()` (no timeout) on another thread. *(1 mark)*
- `RUNNABLE → TIMED_WAITING`: Calling `Thread.sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, or `join(ms)`. *(1 mark)*
- `WAITING → RUNNABLE`: Another thread calls `notify()` / `notifyAll()` (for `wait()`) or the joined thread terminates (for `join()`). *(1 mark)*
- `TIMED_WAITING → RUNNABLE`: Timeout expires. *(1 mark)*
- Any state (except NEW) → `TERMINATED`: `run()` method completes normally or throws an uncaught exception. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Calling `run()` instead of `start()`:** If your code uses `t.run()`, no multithreading occurs and you will lose **at least 3 marks** in any practical/exam question.
> 2. **Confusing `sleep()` and `wait()`:** `sleep()` is on the `Thread` class (static, does not release locks). `wait()` is on the `Object` class (releases locks). Examiners love this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting to set daemon flag before `start()`:** This throws `IllegalThreadStateException` at runtime.
> 4. **Assuming execution order:** Writing "Thread 1 will print first" in your answer is wrong — output order is non-deterministic.
> 5. **Not catching `InterruptedException`:** Any use of `sleep()`, `wait()`, or `join()` requires a `try-catch` block. Forgetting it is a compilation error.
> 6. **Confusing the six states with the older "five states" model:** Modern Java has **six** states (NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED). The legacy model grouped some of these.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution in a process. *(Definition)*
- Java supports **two ways** to create threads: extending `Thread` class OR implementing `Runnable` interface.
- **`Runnable` is the preferred approach** because it leaves inheritance free and follows the OOP "composition over inheritance" principle.
- The `Runnable` interface has **only one method**: `public void run()`.
- The `Thread` class itself **implements** `Runnable`.
- Always call **`start()`**, never `run()`, to launch a new thread. Calling `run()` runs code sequentially.
- `start()` can be called **only once** per thread object. A second call throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.
- The **main thread** is created automatically by the JVM when `main()` begins.
- **Thread priorities** range from 1 (`MIN_PRIORITY`) to 10 (`MAX_PRIORITY`); default is 5 (`NORM_PRIORITY`).
- Priorities are **hints only** — they do not guarantee execution order.
- `Thread.sleep(ms)` pauses the **current** thread for `ms` milliseconds; it is a **static** method.
- `t.join()` makes the **calling** thread wait until thread `t` terminates.
- A **daemon thread** is terminated automatically by the JVM when all user threads finish.
- `setDaemon(true)` **must be called before** `start()`.
- A thread has **six states** in modern Java: `NEW`, `RUNNABLE`, `BLOCKED`, `WAITING`, `TIMED_WAITING`, `TERMINATED`.
- The `Thread.currentThread()` static method returns a reference to the currently executing thread.
- `isAlive()` returns `true` if the thread has been started and has not yet died.
- **Output order in multithreaded programs is non-deterministic** — do not assume any order in exam answers.
- `InterruptedException` must be **caught or declared** whenever `sleep()`, `wait()`, or `join()` is used.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Synchronization # MODULE 3: SYNCHRONIZATION ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Synchronization** in the context of Object-Oriented Programming (particularly Java, as per the KTU OEERT613 syllabus) is the capability to control the access of multiple threads to **shared resources**. It ensures that: 1. Only **one thread** can access a critical section (shared resource) at any given point in time. 2. **Memory consistency errors** are prevented — meaning, all threads see a consistent view of shared mutable data. 3. **Thread interference** (race conditions) caused by interleaved execution of concurrent operations on shared objects is eliminated. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):** > *Synchronization is the mechanism that ensures that two concurrent threads do not simultaneously execute a critical section of code that accesses a shared resource such as a shared data structure. It is built around the concept of an **intrinsic lock** (or **monitor**) associated with every object in Java.* In Java, every object has an **intrinsic lock** (also called a **monitor lock** or simply a **monitor**). A thread that needs exclusive access to an object's fields must **acquire** the object's lock before accessing them and **release** it when the work is done. The keywords and constructs used are: - `synchronized` (method modifier or block statement) - `volatile` (field modifier) - `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` (methods of `java.lang.Object`) --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Single-Stall Bathroom 🚻** > > Imagine a public restroom with **only one stall** but **many people** waiting in line. Only one person can enter and lock the door at a time. Others must wait their turn. When the person finishes and unlocks the door, the next person enters and locks it again. > > - The **stall** = the **shared resource** (e.g., a bank account object, a printer, a file). > - The **door lock** = the **intrinsic lock (monitor)** of the object. > - The **person entering** = the **thread acquiring the lock**. > - **Locking** = `synchronized` block entry; **Unlocking** = `synchronized` block exit. > > Without the lock, two people might enter at the same time — chaos! With the lock, order is enforced. **Another Analogy — The Railway Crossing 🚦:** A single-lane bridge allows only one train at a time. A signal (lock) controls access. Synchronization is that signal. --- ### 1.3 Why Synchronization is Needed — The Three Core Problems > [!WARNING] > **Without synchronization, three classes of bugs occur in multi-threaded OOP programs:** | # | Problem | Description | |---|---------|-------------| | **1** | **Thread Interference (Race Condition)** | Two or more threads interleave their reads/writes on shared data, producing incorrect results (e.g., counter getting value 5 instead of 6 after two increments). | | **2** | **Memory Consistency Error** | One thread updates a shared variable, but another thread sees a stale value due to caching/compiler reordering. | | **3** | **Inconsistent State of Objects** | An object is observed mid-update — e.g., a bank account shows the deposit recorded but the withdrawal not yet applied. | Synchronization prevents **all three** by enforcing a **happens-before relationship**: actions inside a `synchronized` block are guaranteed to be visible to other threads that subsequently acquire the same lock. --- ### 1.4 Types of Synchronization in OOP (Java-centric) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Two-Way Classification:** > 1. **Process Synchronization** — Coordinating processes (typically OS-level; not core to Java OOP). > 2. **Thread Synchronization** — Coordinating threads within a single process. Java provides: > - **Mutual Exclusion** (`synchronized` keyword) → prevents thread interference. > - **Inter-Thread Communication** (`wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()`) → coordinates execution order. > - **Cooperation** — Producer–Consumer pattern. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 The Intrinsic Lock (Monitor) — The Heart of Synchronization Every Java object and every Java class (because classes are also objects of `java.lang.Class`) has an **intrinsic lock**. When a thread invokes a `synchronized` method, it automatically: 1. **Acquires** the object's intrinsic lock. 2. **Executes** the method body. 3. **Releases** the lock (whether normally or via exception). If the lock is held by another thread, the calling thread **blocks (waits)** until the lock becomes available. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Locks are **re-entrant**. If a thread already holds a lock on an object, it can re-acquire it without deadlocking. For example, a `synchronized` method calling another `synchronized` method on the same object works fine. --- ### 2.2 Two Forms of `synchronized` — Analysis #### **Form 1: Synchronized Methods** The entire method body becomes a critical section. ```java public class Counter { private int count = 0; public synchronized void increment() { count++; // atomic operation only because of synchronized } public synchronized int getCount() { return count; } } ``` **Mechanism:** When `increment()` is called on a `Counter` instance, the lock on **that instance** (`this`) is acquired. Any other thread trying to call **any** synchronized method on the **same instance** will block. #### **Form 2: Synchronized Statements (Blocks)** A finer-grained approach — only a specific block is locked, allowing more concurrency. ```java public void performWork() { // non-critical work — no lock needed prepareData(); synchronized(this) { // critical section modifySharedState(); } // more non-critical work cleanup(); } ``` You can also lock on **other objects**: ```java private final Object lockObj = new Object(); public void method() { synchronized(lockObj) { // critical section protected by lockObj's intrinsic lock } } ``` > [!TIP] > **Why use synchronized blocks over methods?** > 1. **Performance** — locks held for shorter durations. > 2. **Flexibility** — lock on different objects for different resources. > 3. **Avoids `this` exposure** — reduces risk of deadlocks in complex class hierarchies. #### **Form 3: Static Synchronization** ```java public static synchronized void staticMethod() { // locked on the Class object (e.g., Counter.class), NOT on an instance } ``` When applied to `static` methods, the lock is on the **`Class` object**, meaning all instances share that single lock for static synchronized methods. --- ### 2.3 Inter-Thread Communication: `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` These three methods are defined in `java.lang.Object` and can **only** be called from within a `synchronized` context. | Method | Purpose | Effect on Lock | |--------|---------|----------------| | `wait()` | Causes current thread to release the lock and sleep until another thread calls `notify()`/`notifyAll()` on the same object. | **Releases** the lock | | `wait(long timeout)` | Same, but wakes up after `timeout` milliseconds even without notification. | **Releases** the lock | | `notify()` | Wakes up **one** thread waiting on the same object's monitor (chosen arbitrarily). | Lock **NOT released** immediately | | `notifyAll()` | Wakes up **all** threads waiting on the same object's monitor. They then compete for the lock. | Lock **NOT released** immediately | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why `wait()` releases the lock but `notify()` does not:** > - `wait()` puts the thread into the **wait set** of the object, freeing the monitor so others can proceed. > - `notify()` only moves a thread from the **wait set** back to the **entry set** — it must still re-acquire the lock to proceed. This ensures the awakened thread doesn't see inconsistent state. --- ### 2.4 The `volatile` Keyword — Lightweight Synchronization `volatile` is a field-level modifier that guarantees: 1. **Visibility** — writes to a `volatile` variable are immediately visible to all threads. 2. **No reordering** — the JVM cannot reorder instructions around a volatile read/write. ```java private volatile boolean running = true; ``` > [!WARNING] > `volatile` does **NOT** guarantee **atomicity**. `count++` is NOT atomic even if `count` is `volatile`, because it is a read-modify-write sequence. Use `synchronized` or `AtomicInteger` for compound operations. --- ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick Reference Table — Memorize for Exam** | Construct | Lock Target | Scope | Re-entrant? | Use Case | |-----------|-------------|-------|-------------|----------| | `synchronized` instance method | `this` (current object) | Entire method | Yes | Simple object-level locking | | `synchronized static` method | `Class` object (e.g., `MyClass.class`) | Entire method | Yes | Class-level shared resources | | `synchronized(this) { }` | `this` | Block | Yes | Fine-grained locking on current object | | `synchronized(obj) { }` | `obj` | Block | Yes | Locking on a dedicated lock object | | `synchronized(MyClass.class) { }` | `Class` object | Block | Yes | Class-level block locking | | `volatile` field | None (memory barrier) | Single read/write | N/A | Flags, single-variable visibility | | Communication Method | Defined In | Must Be In `synchronized`? | Releases Lock? | Blocks Until? | |----------------------|------------|---------------------------|----------------|---------------| | `wait()` | `java.lang.Object` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | `notify()`/`notifyAll()` or timeout | | `notify()` | `java.lang.Object` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (only moves from wait set to entry set) | — | | `notifyAll()` | `java.lang.Object` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | — | | `sleep(ms)` | `java.lang.Thread` | ❌ No | ❌ No | Time elapses | | `join()` | `java.lang.Thread` | ❌ No | N/A | Target thread terminates | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking Systems**: Account balance updates must be atomic — synchronization prevents double-withdrawal. - **Producer-Consumer Pipelines**: Background workers writing to a buffer while UI reads. - **Database Connection Pools**: Limiting concurrent connections. - **Embedded/IoT Systems (Kerala context)**: Synchronizing sensor reads with actuator writes in RTOS-like environments. - **GUI Applications (Swing/JavaFX)**: Event Dispatch Thread synchronization. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Solutions ### 3.1 Demonstration: Race Condition WITHOUT Synchronization ```java class Counter { int count = 0; public void increment() { // Read count → increment → write back int temp = count; // STEP A temp = temp + 1; // STEP B count = temp; // STEP C } } ``` **Step-by-step interleaving causing a race condition (Thread T1 and Thread T2):** | Step | T1 Action | T2 Action | Shared `count` | |------|-----------|-----------|----------------| | 0 | — | — | 0 | | 1 | `temp = count` → temp1 = 0 | — | 0 | | 2 | — | `temp = count` → temp2 = 0 | 0 | | 3 | `temp = temp + 1` → temp1 = 1 | — | 0 | | 4 | — | `temp = temp + 1` → temp2 = 1 | 0 | | 5 | `count = temp` → count = 1 | — | 1 | | 6 | — | `count = temp` → count = 1 | **1 (should be 2!)** | **Diagnosis:** Both threads read `0`, both compute `1`, both write `1`. **One increment is lost.** --- ### 3.2 Solution: Synchronized Counter ```java class SafeCounter { int count = 0; public synchronized void increment() { int temp = count; temp = temp + 1; count = temp; } public synchronized int getCount() { return count; } } ``` **Now, with intrinsic lock on `this`:** | Step | T1 Action | T2 Action | Lock State | Shared `count` | |------|-----------|-----------|------------|----------------| | 0 | T1 calls `increment()` | — | T1 holds lock | 0 | | 1 | `temp = count` → 0 | T2 calls `increment()` → **BLOCKS** | T1 holds | 0 | | 2 | `temp + 1` → 1 | (waiting) | T1 holds | 0 | | 3 | `count = 1` | (waiting) | T1 holds | 1 | | 4 | method exits → **lock released** | unblocks, acquires lock | T2 holds | 1 | | 5 | — | `temp = count` → 1 | T2 holds | 1 | | 6 | — | `temp + 1` → 2 | T2 holds | 1 | | 7 | — | `count = 2` | T2 holds | 2 | | 8 | — | method exits → lock released | free | 2 | **Result: `count = 2` ✅ correct.** --- ### 3.3 The Producer–Consumer Problem (Classic KTU Question) **Problem Statement:** A producer thread generates data items and places them in a shared buffer of fixed size. A consumer thread removes items. Synchronization ensures: - Producer doesn't add when buffer is full. - Consumer doesn't remove when buffer is empty. ```java class Buffer { private final int[] data; private int size = 0; private int head = 0, tail = 0; public Buffer(int capacity) { data = new int[capacity]; } public synchronized void produce(int item) throws InterruptedException { // STEP 1: Wait if buffer is full while (size == data.length) { wait(); // releases lock, waits for notify } // STEP 2: Insert item data[tail] = item; tail = (tail + 1) % data.length; size++; System.out.println("Produced: " + item); // STEP 3: Notify consumer notifyAll(); } public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException { // STEP 1: Wait if buffer is empty while (size == 0) { wait(); // releases lock, waits for notify } // STEP 2: Remove item int item = data[head]; head = (head + 1) % data.length; size--; System.out.println("Consumed: " + item); // STEP 3: Notify producer notifyAll(); return item; } } ``` **Logic Trace — Step-by-Step:** **Scenario A — Producer arrives first (buffer empty):** | Step | Producer | Consumer | Buffer | Notes | |------|----------|----------|--------|-------| | 1 | `produce(10)` called | — | empty | Producer acquires lock | | 2 | `while (size == length)` → false | — | empty | Skip wait | | 3 | `data[tail] = 10`, size=1 | — | [10] | Insert | | 4 | `notifyAll()` | — | [10] | Wakes any waiting consumer | | 5 | releases lock | unblocks, acquires lock | [10] | Consumer proceeds | | 6 | — | `while (size == 0)` → false | [10] | Skip wait | | 7 | — | reads item, size=0 | empty | Consumes | | 8 | — | `notifyAll()` | empty | Returns 10 | **Scenario B — Consumer arrives first (buffer empty):** | Step | Producer | Consumer | Buffer | Notes | |------|----------|----------|--------|-------| | 1 | — | `consume()` called | empty | Consumer acquires lock | | 2 | — | `while (size == 0)` → true | empty | **Enters wait() — releases lock** | | 3 | `produce(20)` called → acquires lock | (sleeping) | empty | Producer runs | | 4 | inserts, `notifyAll()` | wakes up, re-acquires lock | [20] | Consumer resumes | | 5 | — | reads 20, returns | empty | Done | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why `while` instead of `if`?** > **Spurious wakeups** can occur — a thread waiting on `wait()` may wake up without `notify()` being called (OS-level reason). The `while` loop re-checks the condition. Also, multiple threads may be waiting; when notified, the condition might not be satisfied yet. Always use `while`, never `if`. --- ### 3.4 Deadlock — The Dark Side of Synchronization **Deadlock** occurs when two or more threads are blocked forever, each waiting for a lock held by the other. **Classic Scenario — Two locks, opposite order:** ```java class A { synchronized void methodA(B b) { String threadName = Thread.currentThread().getName(); System.out.println(threadName + " entered A.methodA"); try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (Exception e) {} System.out.println(threadName + " trying to call B.last()"); b.last(); // calls B's synchronized method } synchronized void last() { System.out.println("Inside A.last()"); } } class B { synchronized void methodB(A a) { String threadName = Thread.currentThread().getName(); System.out.println(threadName + " entered B.methodB"); try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (Exception e) {} System.out.println(threadName + " trying to call A.last()"); a.last(); // calls A's synchronized method } synchronized void last() { System.out.println("Inside B.last()"); } } ``` **Deadlock Trace:** | Step | Thread-1 | Thread-2 | Lock A | Lock B | |------|----------|----------|--------|--------| | 1 | enters `methodA`, acquires lock A | — | T1 | free | | 2 | sleeps 100ms | enters `methodB`, acquires lock B | T1 | T2 | | 3 | wakes, calls `b.last()` → **blocks** on lock B | sleeps 100ms | T1 | T2 | | 4 | (waiting for lock B) | wakes, calls `a.last()` → **blocks** on lock A | T1 | T2 | | 5 | ↺ permanent wait | ↺ permanent wait | — | — | **DEADLOCK** — both threads frozen. > [!WARNING] > **Four Coffman Conditions for Deadlock (all must hold):** > 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — locks are exclusive. > 2. **Hold and Wait** — thread holds one lock while waiting for another. > 3. **No Preemption** — locks cannot be forcibly taken. > 4. **Circular Wait** — T1 waits for T2's lock, T2 waits for T1's lock. > > **Breaking any one** prevents deadlock. Most common fix: enforce a **global lock ordering**. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What | Situation | Recommended Construct | Reason | |-----------|----------------------|--------| | Simple object with mutable state | `synchronized` methods | Easy, safe | | High concurrency needs | `synchronized` blocks + dedicated lock objects | Reduces contention | | Single boolean flag, single variable visibility | `volatile` | Cheaper than `synchronized` | | Compound operations (`count++`) on shared var | `synchronized` or `AtomicInteger` | Atomicity needed | | Producer-consumer | `wait()` / `notifyAll()` + `synchronized` | Built-in blocking | | Multiple producers/consumers | `notifyAll()` preferred | Wakes all so re-check | | Many threads, lock contention | `java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock` (advanced) | Try-lock, fairness | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram: Intrinsic Lock Acquisition & Release Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Thread T1 calls synchronized method]) --> CheckLock{Is lock
acquired by
another thread?} CheckLock -- No --> AcquireLock[Acquire lock on this object] AcquireLock --> Execute[Execute method body
critical section] Execute --> ReleaseLock[Release lock
on method exit or exception] ReleaseLock --> End1([Thread T1 continues normally]) CheckLock -- Yes --> Block[Thread T1 BLOCKS
enters entry set] Block --> WaitLock{Wait for lock
to be released} WaitLock -- Lock available --> CheckLock ``` **Interpretation:** A thread attempting to enter a `synchronized` method on an object whose lock is held by another thread enters the **entry set** of that object's monitor. It waits until the holder releases the lock, then competes (typically FIFO but not guaranteed) for re-acquisition. --- ### 4.2 Diagram: Wait/Notify State Machine (Monitor Architecture) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> New: thread created New --> Running: start() Running --> Blocked: lock unavailable
synchronized entry Blocked --> Running: lock acquired Running --> Waiting: wait() called
releases lock Waiting --> Blocked: notify/notifyAll
or timeout
re-enters entry set Blocked --> Running: lock re-acquired Running --> Terminated: run() completes Terminated --> [*] ``` **Interpretation:** This diagram shows the four major states a thread can be in with respect to synchronization: **Running**, **Blocked** (waiting for lock), **Waiting** (sleeping in `wait()` set), and **Terminated**. --- ### 4.3 Diagram: Producer-Consumer Interaction ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P as Producer Thread participant B as Shared Buffer
synchronized participant C as Consumer Thread Note over B: size = 0 (empty) C->>B: consume() B->>B: while size==0: wait() Note right of C: Consumer enters
WAITING set
releases lock P->>B: produce(item) B->>B: insert item, size=1 B->>C: notifyAll() Note right of C: Consumer wakes
re-acquires lock B-->>P: lock released B->>C: lock acquired by consumer C->>B: remove item, size=0 B->>P: notifyAll() C-->>C: return item ``` **Interpretation:** The producer wakes the consumer by calling `notifyAll()`. The consumer re-checks the condition (`while` loop), removes the item, and notifies the producer. The shared `Buffer` object's monitor serializes all operations. --- ### 4.4 Diagram: Deadlock Circular Wait Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR T1[Thread-1
holds Lock A
waits for Lock B] T2[Thread-2
holds Lock B
waits for Lock A] T1 -- waiting for --> B[Lock B] T2 -- waiting for --> A[Lock A] A -.held by.-> T1 B -.held by.-> T2 style T1 fill:#ffcccc style T2 fill:#ffcccc style A fill:#cce5ff style B fill:#cce5ff ``` **Interpretation:** Red nodes are threads in deadlock; blue nodes are locks. Arrows show the **circular wait** — each thread holds one lock and waits for the other, forming a cycle. No thread can proceed. --- ### 4.5 Diagram: Synchronization Hierarchy in Java ```mermaid graph TD A[Java Synchronization Mechanisms] --> B[Monitor-based
synchronized keyword] A --> C[volatile keyword
visibility only] A --> D[Object class methods
wait notify notifyAll] A --> E[java.util.concurrent
advanced - ReentrantLock etc] B --> B1[synchronized method
locks this] B --> B2[synchronized block
locks specified object] B --> B3[static synchronized
locks Class object] D --> D1[wait
releases lock] D --> D2[notify
wakes one thread] D --> D3[notifyAll
wakes all threads] style A fill:#e6f2ff style B fill:#fff4e6 style C fill:#fff4e6 style D fill:#fff4e6 style E fill:#e6ffe6 ``` **Interpretation:** A taxonomy of synchronization primitives — the three pillars (monitor, volatile, wait/notify) form the **KTU syllabus core**, while `java.util.concurrent` is advanced. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define synchronization. Why is it needed in multithreaded programs?** **Model Answer:** Synchronization is the mechanism that controls the access of multiple threads to shared resources, ensuring that only one thread executes a critical section at a time. It is built on Java's **intrinsic lock** (monitor) associated with every object. **Why needed (3 key points):** 1. **Prevents thread interference** — concurrent threads updating shared data may interleave reads/writes, producing incorrect results (race condition). 2. **Prevents memory consistency errors** — ensures changes made by one thread are visible to others (happens-before guarantee). 3. **Prevents inconsistent object state** — protects partially-updated objects from being observed. *Example:* Two threads incrementing a shared counter without `synchronized` may result in lost updates. With `synchronized`, each increment is atomic. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the difference between `wait()` and `sleep()` in Java.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | `wait()` | `sleep()` | |---------|----------|-----------| | **Class** | `java.lang.Object` | `java.lang.Thread` | | **Releases lock?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | **Must be in synchronized?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | **Wakes up by** | `notify()`/`notifyAll()` or timeout | Timeout only | | **Purpose** | Inter-thread communication | Pausing execution | `wait()` releases the intrinsic lock and allows other threads to acquire it, making it suitable for **cooperation** between threads. `sleep()` only pauses the thread without releasing any lock — other threads still cannot enter synchronized blocks on the same object. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ## 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `synchronized` Methods & Blocks **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3 Internal Choice]** **a)** Explain the concept of intrinsic lock and monitor in Java. How does a `synchronized` method ensure mutual exclusion? **(7 marks)** **b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate synchronization using synchronized methods. Use a `BankAccount` class with `deposit()` and `withdraw()` methods accessed by multiple threads. Show what happens with and without synchronization. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Intrinsic Lock (Monitor) Definition [2 marks]:** Every Java object possesses an **intrinsic lock** — a mutually exclusive lock also called a **monitor**. This lock is implicitly associated with the object and is used to enforce exclusive access to the object's synchronized code. When a thread acquires the lock, all other threads attempting to acquire the same lock are suspended until the holder releases it. **Mechanism of Synchronized Method [3 marks]:** ```java public synchronized void withdraw(int amount) { if (balance >= amount) { balance = balance - amount; } } ``` **Step-by-step execution:** 1. Thread T1 invokes `withdraw()` on a `BankAccount` object `acc`. 2. JVM automatically attempts to **acquire** `acc`'s intrinsic lock. 3. If lock is **free**, T1 acquires it and proceeds. 4. If lock is **held** by another thread (say T2), T1 is **blocked** — enters the entry set of `acc`'s monitor. 5. T1 executes the method body atomically — no other thread can interfere. 6. When method returns (normally or via exception), the lock is **automatically released**. 7. A blocked thread (T2) is unblocked and competes for the lock. **Re-entrancy [2 marks]:** Intrinsic locks are **re-entrant** — a thread holding a lock can re-acquire it without deadlocking. For example: ```java public synchronized void outer() { inner(); // re-acquires same lock — works fine } public synchronized void inner() { // body } ``` This is essential because synchronized methods may call other synchronized methods on the same object. --- ### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Without synchronization — buggy version:** ```java class BankAccount { private int balance = 1000; public void withdraw(int amount) { // NOT synchronized if (balance >= amount) { try { Thread.sleep(50); } catch (Exception e) {} balance = balance - amount; System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " withdrew " + amount + ". Balance: " + balance); } else { System.out.println("Insufficient balance for " + Thread.currentThread().getName()); } } } ``` **With synchronization — correct version:** ```java class BankAccount { private int balance = 1000; public synchronized void withdraw(int amount) { if (balance >= amount) { try { Thread.sleep(50); } catch (Exception e) {} balance = balance - amount; System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " withdrew " + amount + ". Balance: " + balance); } else { System.out.println("Insufficient balance for " + Thread.currentThread().getName()); } } } public class BankDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { BankAccount acc = new BankAccount(); Runnable task = () -> acc.withdraw(700); Thread t1 = new Thread(task, "User-1"); Thread t2 = new Thread(task, "User-2"); t1.start(); t2.start(); } } ``` **Expected Output (with synchronization):** ``` User-1 withdrew 700. Balance: 300 Insufficient balance for User-2 ``` **Without synchronization:** Both threads may read `balance = 1000`, both pass the check, and the balance becomes **negative** — a race condition. **Explanation:** The `synchronized` keyword on `withdraw()` ensures atomicity of the read-check-debit operation. Only one thread can execute it at a time on the same `BankAccount` instance. --- ## 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Inter-Thread Communication **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3 Internal Choice]** **a)** Explain `wait()`, `notify()`, and `notifyAll()` methods with suitable Java code. Why must these methods be called from within a synchronized context? **(7 marks)** **b)** Write a complete Java program to solve the **Producer-Consumer problem** using synchronization. The shared buffer should have a fixed capacity (say 5), with one producer thread producing items and one consumer thread consuming them. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Definitions [2 marks]:** - **`wait()`**: Causes the current thread to release the lock and enter the **wait set** of the object's monitor. The thread sleeps until another thread invokes `notify()` or `notifyAll()` on the same object, OR the specified timeout elapses. - **`notify()`**: Wakes up **one** thread waiting on the same object's monitor. The awakened thread does **not** immediately run — it must first re-acquire the lock. - **`notifyAll()`**: Wakes up **all** threads waiting on the same object's monitor. Each awakened thread competes for the lock. **Code Example [3 marks]:** ```java class SharedResource { private int data; private boolean available = false; public synchronized void produce(int value) throws InterruptedException { while (available) { wait(); // wait until consumer consumes } data = value; available = true; System.out.println("Produced: " + value); notify(); // wake up consumer } public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException { while (!available) { wait(); // wait until producer produces } available = false; System.out.println("Consumed: " + data); notify(); // wake up producer return data; } } ``` **Why synchronized context is mandatory [2 marks]:** 1. **Lock Consistency**: `wait()` releases the intrinsic lock of the object. If called outside a synchronized context, there is no lock to release — Java throws `IllegalMonitorStateException`. 2. **Atomicity of Check-and-Act**: The `while (condition) wait()` pattern must be evaluated and the thread suspended **atomically**. Without `synchronized`, another thread could change the condition between the check and the `wait()` call, causing the thread to miss the notification (a missed signal bug). 3. **Race Protection**: `notify()` must also be inside synchronized to ensure the awakened thread sees a consistent state when it wakes up. --- ### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] ```java class Buffer { private final int[] arr; private int head = 0, tail = 0, size = 0; public Buffer(int capacity) { arr = new int[capacity]; } public synchronized void produce(int item) throws InterruptedException { // STEP 1: Wait if full while (size == arr.length) { System.out.println("Buffer FULL. Producer waiting..."); wait(); } // STEP 2: Insert arr[tail] = item; tail = (tail + 1) % arr.length; size++; System.out.println("Produced: " + item + " | Size: " + size); // STEP 3: Notify consumer notifyAll(); } public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException { // STEP 1: Wait if empty while (size == 0) { System.out.println("Buffer EMPTY. Consumer waiting..."); wait(); } // STEP 2: Remove int item = arr[head]; head = (head + 1) % arr.length; size--; System.out.println("Consumed: " + item + " | Size: " + size); // STEP 3: Notify producer notifyAll(); return item; } } public class ProducerConsumerDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { Buffer buffer = new Buffer(5); // Producer thread Thread producer = new Thread(() -> { try { for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) { buffer.produce(i); Thread.sleep(100); } } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println("Producer interrupted."); } }, "Producer"); // Consumer thread Thread consumer = new Thread(() -> { try { for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) { buffer.consume(); Thread.sleep(150); } } catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println("Consumer interrupted."); } }, "Consumer"); producer.start(); consumer.start(); } } ``` **Sample Output:** ``` Produced: 1 | Size: 1 Consumed: 1 | Size: 0 Produced: 2 | Size: 1 Produced: 3 | Size: 2 Consumed: 2 | Size: 1 Produced: 4 | Size: 2 ... ``` **Explanation of Key Design Choices [included in marks]:** - **`while` loop** instead of `if` → guards against spurious wakeups. - **`notifyAll()`** instead of `notify()` → safer with multiple waiters; prevents lost-wakeup deadlock. - **`synchronized` methods** → serializes all buffer access. - **Circular buffer** (`tail = (tail+1) % capacity`) → efficient space reuse. --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Using `if` instead of `while` with `wait()`** — Severe deduction (up to 2–3 marks). Always write `while(condition) wait();`. > 2. **Forgetting `notifyAll()` or `notify()`** — Producer/Consumer hangs forever (deadlock-like state). Always end critical section with notification. > 3. **Calling `wait()` outside synchronized** — Causes `IllegalMonitorStateException` at runtime. Examiners expect code inside `synchronized` block/method. > 4. **Confusing `sleep()` with `wait()`** — `sleep()` does NOT release the lock; using it for thread cooperation is wrong and costs marks. > 5. **Locking on `String` literals or primitives (auto-boxed)** — Serious design flaw. Always lock on dedicated `final Object` references. > 6. **Not mentioning spurious wakeups** — When explaining `wait()`, mention that `Object.wait()` may wake up without notification; hence the `while` loop. > 7. **Confusing `notify()` and `notifyAll()`** — `notify()` picks one **arbitrary** waiting thread; if you have multiple conditions, use `notifyAll()` to avoid deadlock. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **📋 Final High-Density Revision Bullet Points — Synchronization** > > ✅ **Definition**: Synchronization = controlling access of multiple threads to shared resources via locks. > > ✅ **Intrinsic Lock (Monitor)**: Every Java object has one. Acquired on `synchronized` entry, released on exit (normal or exception). > > ✅ **Re-entrancy**: A thread can re-acquire a lock it already holds (synchronized method calling another synchronized method on `this`). > > ✅ **`synchronized` methods** lock on `this` (instance) or `Class` object (static). > > ✅ **`synchronized` blocks** allow fine-grained locking on any object reference. > > ✅ **Race Condition**: Without sync, interleaved reads/writes produce wrong results. > > ✅ **`wait()`**: Defined in `java.lang.Object`. Releases lock. Must be inside `synchronized`. Called on the shared object. > > ✅ **`notify()`**: Wakes ONE waiting thread. Does NOT release lock immediately. > > ✅ **`notifyAll()`**: Wakes ALL waiting threads — **preferred in multi-condition scenarios**. > > ✅ **Always use `while(condition) wait();`** — guards against spurious wakeups. > > ✅ **`volatile`**: Lightweight. Guarantees visibility but NOT atomicity. Use only for single-variable read/write. > > ✅ **Deadlock**: Four Coffman conditions. Common fix — global lock ordering. Avoid nested locks on multiple objects. > > ✅ **Producer-Consumer**: Classic synchronization problem. Synchronized buffer + `wait()`/`notifyAll()`. > > ✅ **`sleep()` vs `wait()`**: `sleep()` keeps lock; `wait()` releases lock and requires synchronized context. > > ✅ **Static Synchronization**: Lock is on the `Class` object, shared across all instances. > > ✅ **Lock & Monitor Vocabulary** for KTU answers: "intrinsic lock", "monitor", "entry set", "wait set", "happens-before relationship", "mutual exclusion", "critical section".

topics.title: Suspending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Suspending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Object Oriented Concepts** (Java Programming), **Suspending** a thread refers to the deliberate **temporary halt of a thread's execution** so that it ceases to consume CPU resources, without destroying it, and can be later **resumed** from the exact point where it was halted.

In Java, suspension can be achieved through:

1. **Deprecated API (Legacy)**: `Thread.suspend()` and `Thread.resume()` — methods of the `java.lang.Thread` class.
2. **Modern (Preferred) API**: `Object.wait()`, `Object.notify()`, and `Object.notifyAll()` — inherited by every Java class via the `Object` class, used for **inter-thread communication**.
3. **Time-bound Sleep**: `Thread.sleep(long millis)` — a static method that pauses the *current* thread for a specified duration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *deprecated* `suspend()` and `resume()` methods are frequently asked in exams because they illustrate **why** Java deprecated them — they are inherently **deadlock-prone** and **unsafe**. Students must explain both the *mechanism* and the *reason for deprecation*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Pause Button on a Movie

Imagine a movie being played on a screen:

- A **thread** is the movie player.
- **Suspending** is like pressing the **PAUSE** button — the player freezes, but the movie state (your position in the film) is **saved**.
- **Resuming** is pressing **PLAY** — the movie continues from exactly the paused frame.
- **Stopping/Deadlock** is like yanking the power cord — the movie state is lost or the player gets stuck.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - `suspend()` → Pauses a thread (depricated, unsafe).
> - `wait()` → Pauses a thread AND **releases the lock** it holds (safe, preferred).
> - `sleep()` → Pauses a thread but **does NOT release any lock**.

---

## 1.3 The Three Suspension Strategies at a Glance

| Mechanism | Class/Method | Releases Lock? | Deprecated? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `suspend()` | `Thread` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Java 1.2+) | Legacy code only |
| `wait()` | `Object` | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Producer–Consumer, Inter-thread comm. |
| `sleep()` | `Thread` (static) | ❌ No | ❌ No | Timed delays, animations |

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in **bold**

- **`WAIT_TIMEOUT = 0L`** → Sentinel value indicating indefinite wait in `wait()`.
- **`Object` class** is the **superclass of every Java class** — hence `wait()/notify()` are available on **all** objects.
- **Monitor / Intrinsic Lock** — Every Java object has exactly **one** intrinsic monitor lock used by `synchronized` and `wait()/notify()`.
- **`TIMED_WAITING` / `WAITING`** — Thread state constants from `java.lang.Thread.State` enum used by JVM.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of `Thread.suspend()` and `Thread.resume()`

### 2.1.1 How `suspend()` Works (Step-by-Step)

When `suspend()` is invoked on a target thread `T`:

1. The calling thread requests the JVM to stop `T`.
2. The JVM **suspends `T` immediately** without giving `T` a chance to clean up resources.
3. **Critically**: Any **monitor lock held by `T` is NOT released**.
4. The thread `T` enters the **SUSPENDED** state and stays there until `resume()` is called on it.

### 2.1.2 How `resume()` Works

1. The calling thread invokes `resume()` on a suspended thread `T`.
2. The JVM transitions `T` back to the **RUNNABLE** state.
3. `T` competes for the CPU and continues from the exact instruction after `suspend()`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Deadlock Scenario with `suspend()`:** If thread `T` is suspended while holding a lock on object `X`, and another thread `Y` tries to acquire `X`'s lock, then `Y` will block forever waiting for `T` to release the lock — but `T` will never release the lock because it is suspended. This is a **classic KTU exam question**.

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics of `wait()` and `notify()`

These methods form the **canonical Java inter-thread communication protocol** and operate **only within a `synchronized` context**.

### Step-by-Step Logic of `wait()`:

1. The thread must **own the intrinsic lock** of the target object (i.e., be inside a `synchronized` block/method on that object).
2. On calling `wait()`, the thread:
   - **Releases the lock** on the target object.
   - Enters the **waiting set** (also called the *wait set*) of that object.
   - The thread's state becomes `WAITING` (or `TIMED_WAITING` if `wait(long timeout)` is used).
3. The thread remains in the wait set until one of these events occurs:
   - Another thread calls `notify()` on the same object → **one** arbitrarily chosen waiting thread is moved to the **blocked-for-lock-acquisition** state.
   - Another thread calls `notifyAll()` on the same object → **all** waiting threads are moved to the blocked-for-lock-acquisition state.
   - The `wait()` timeout expires (for `wait(long timeout)`).
   - The thread is **interrupted** (then `InterruptedException` is thrown).

### Step-by-Step Logic of `notify()` / `notifyAll()`:

1. The calling thread must **own the lock** of the target object.
2. JVM selects one (`notify`) or all (`notifyAll`) threads from the wait set.
3. Those threads must **re-acquire the lock** before they can actually resume execution.
4. Only when the calling thread releases the lock can the notified thread(s) proceed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Java does not use mathematical formulas, but it uses **invariants** — rules that MUST hold. Below is the "cheat sheet" of suspension rules for KTU exams.

| # | Invariant / Rule | Symbol / Syntax | Criticality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `wait()` MUST be called inside `synchronized` | `synchronized(obj) { obj.wait(); }` | **Mandatory** — throws `IllegalMonitorStateException` otherwise |
| 2 | `wait()` releases the lock on `obj` | `obj.wait()` | Distinguishes from `sleep()` |
| 3 | `sleep()` does NOT release the lock | `Thread.sleep(ms)` | Holds any monitor lock owned |
| 4 | `notify()` wakes **one** waiter (arbitrary) | `obj.notify()` | Use when only one waiter can proceed |
| 5 | `notifyAll()` wakes **all** waiters | `obj.notifyAll()` | Safer default — used in most production code |
| 6 | `suspend()` is deprecated since JDK 1.2 | `@Deprecated(since="1.2")` | Avoid in new code |
| 7 | `suspend()` does NOT release locks | — | Source of deadlocks |
| 8 | `wait()` can throw `InterruptedException` | `throws InterruptedException` | Must be in `try-catch` or declared |
| 9 | Thread states: `NEW → RUNNABLE → BLOCKED/WAITING/TIMED_WAITING → TERMINATED` | `Thread.State` enum | KTU diagram favorite |
| 10 | `yield()` is a **hint**, not a guarantee | `Thread.yield()` | Not the same as suspending |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Producer–Consumer Problem**: A consumer thread `wait()`s on a buffer when it is empty; the producer `notify()`s the consumer when an item is added.
- **Thread Pools / Executor Frameworks**: Worker threads `wait()` on a task queue when idle.
- **GUI Applications**: The Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) `wait()`s for user actions via `wait()/notify()` or `BlockingQueue`.
- **Database Connection Pools**: Threads `wait()` for an available connection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix: `suspend()` vs `wait()` vs `sleep()`

Since the topic is Java, the "derivation" is a **logical flow of execution** tracing each method's effect on locks and thread states.

### 3.1.1 Trace of `suspend()` Execution

Let the initial state be:

- **Thread `T`** holds lock on object `X`.
- **Thread `T`** is in state `RUNNABLE`.
- **Thread `Y`** is about to call `synchronized(X) { ... }`.

| Step | Action by `T` | Action by `Y` | State of `T` | Lock on `X` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Acquires lock on `X` via `synchronized` | Idle | `RUNNABLE` | **Held by T** |
| 1 | Calls `T.suspend()` | Idle | `WAITING` (SUSPENDED) | **Still held by T** ❌ |
| 2 | (Frozen — no cleanup) | Tries to enter `synchronized(X)` | `WAITING` | Held by T |
| 3 | (Frozen) | Blocks forever waiting for lock | `WAITING` | Held by T |
| 4 | Another thread `Z` calls `T.resume()` | Still blocked | `RUNNABLE` | Held by T |
| 5 | T finishes `synchronized` block | Still blocked until T releases | `RUNNABLE` → exits | Released |
| 6 | Idle | Acquires lock, proceeds | — | Held by Y |

> **Conclusion:** `suspend()` is **deadlock-prone** because locks are NOT released during suspension.

---

### 3.1.2 Trace of `wait()` Execution

Let the initial state be:

- **Thread `T`** holds lock on object `X`.
- **Thread `Y`** is about to call `X.notify()`.
- **Thread `Z`** is about to call `synchronized(X) { ... }`.

| Step | Action by `T` | Action by `Y` | State of `T` | Lock on `X` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Acquires lock on `X` | Idle | `RUNNABLE` | **Held by T** |
| 1 | Calls `X.wait()` | Idle | `WAITING` | **Released** ✅ |
| 2 | (In wait set of `X`) | Enters `synchronized(X)`, acquires lock | `WAITING` | Held by Y |
| 3 | (In wait set) | Calls `X.notify()` | `WAITING → BLOCKED` (waiting for lock) | Held by Y |
| 4 | (Blocked on lock) | Exits `synchronized` block | `BLOCKED → RUNNABLE` | Released |
| 5 | Re-acquires lock, proceeds | Idle | `RUNNABLE` | Held by T |

> **Conclusion:** `wait()` is **deadlock-safe** because locks are released during waiting, and re-acquisition is enforced before resumption.

---

### 3.1.3 Mathematical Formulation of the Wait-Notify Protocol

In **Java Memory Model (JMM)** terms, the wait/notify handshake is governed by two **conditional invariants**:

$$
\text{Pre-condition of } obj.wait(): \quad \text{currentThread holds lock}(obj)
$$

$$
\text{Pre-condition of } obj.notify(): \quad \text{currentThread holds lock}(obj)
$$

The **atomicity invariant** for the protocol is:

$$
\text{At any instant } t: \quad \vert \text{threads in wait set of } obj \vert + \vert \text{threads holding lock of } obj \vert \;\leq\; 1
$$

This means: at any time, **at most one thread** can either be the lock-holder of `obj` OR be in the wait set of `obj` — **never both**, and never two simultaneously holding the lock.

The **state transition** for a thread under `wait()` followed by `notify()` can be expressed as:

$$
T_{\text{state}}: \quad \text{RUNNABLE} \xrightarrow{\text{wait}()} \text{WAITING} \xrightarrow{\text{notify}()} \text{BLOCKED} \xrightarrow{\text{re-acquire lock}} \text{RUNNABLE}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Java Code: Producer–Consumer with `wait()`/`notify()`
