Below is the canonical KTU model solution. The code demonstrates **safe suspension** using `wait()` and `notify()`.

```java
class SharedBuffer {
    private int data;
    private boolean available = false;

    // Called by PRODUCER
    public synchronized void produce(int value) throws InterruptedException {
        while (available) {
            wait(); // SUSPEND producer until consumer drains the buffer
        }
        data = value;
        System.out.println("Produced: " + data);
        available = true;
        notify(); // RESUME the waiting consumer
    }

    // Called by CONSUMER
    public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        while (!available) {
            wait(); // SUSPEND consumer until producer fills the buffer
        }
        System.out.println("Consumed: " + data);
        available = false;
        notify(); // RESUME the waiting producer
        return data;
    }
}

public class ProducerConsumer {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SharedBuffer buffer = new SharedBuffer();

        Thread producer = new Thread(() -> {
            try { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) buffer.produce(i); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
        });

        Thread consumer = new Thread(() -> {
            try { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) buffer.consume(); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
        });

        producer.start();
        consumer.start();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logic (for KTU explanation):

1. **Producer** calls `produce()`. It acquires the lock on `buffer`. Since `available == false`, it skips `wait()` and produces data, then calls `notify()`.
2. **Consumer** calls `consume()`. It acquires the lock on `buffer`. Since `available == true`, it consumes and calls `notify()`.
3. If consumer runs first: `available == false`, so consumer calls `wait()` → **suspended**, lock released.
4. Producer runs, fills buffer, calls `notify()` → consumer is moved from wait set to blocked-for-lock state.
5. Producer exits `synchronized` → lock released → consumer acquires lock and resumes.

---

## 3.3 Deprecated `suspend()` Example (For Exam Contrast)

```java
class LegacySuspendDemo implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        synchronized (this) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " acquired lock.");
            Thread.currentThread().suspend(); // ⚠️ HOLDS the lock!
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " resumed.");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        LegacySuspendDemo obj = new LegacySuspendDemo();
        Thread t1 = new Thread(obj, "T1");
        t1.start();
        Thread.sleep(500);

        // T1 is suspended, still holding the lock.
        synchronized (obj) {
            System.out.println("Main acquired the lock — this would DEADLOCK in real scenario.");
        }
        t1.resume();
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If asked to compare `suspend()` with `wait()`, your answer MUST include the **lock-release behavior** as the primary differentiator. Marks are awarded for this distinction specifically.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thread State Transition Diagram (with Suspension Paths)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start()
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : waiting for monitor lock
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait() or suspend()
    WAITING --> BLOCKED : notify() or resume() then re-acquire lock
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep(ms) or wait(ms)
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout expires
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run() completes
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

## 4.2 Wait–Notify Handshake Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Producer Thread
    participant B as SharedBuffer (obj)
    participant C as Consumer Thread
    P->>B: acquire lock (synchronized)
    P->>B: produce(item); notify()
    P-->>B: release lock
    C->>B: acquire lock (synchronized)
    C->>B: consume(); notify()
    C-->>B: release lock
    Note over C,B: If buffer empty
    C->>B: wait() → SUSPENDED, lock RELEASED
    P->>B: acquire lock, produce
    P->>B: notify() → C moves to BLOCKED
    P-->>B: release lock
    C->>B: re-acquire lock, resume
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Suspension Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Java Thread Suspension API] --> B[Deprecated Methods]
    A --> C[Preferred Methods]
    A --> D[Static Helpers]
    B --> B1["Thread.suspend()"]
    B --> B2["Thread.resume()"]
    B1 --> X1[Does NOT release lock]
    B1 --> X2[Risk: DEADLOCK]
    C --> C1["Object.wait()"]
    C --> C2["Object.notify()"]
    C --> C3["Object.notifyAll()"]
    C1 --> Y1[Releases lock]
    C1 --> Y2[Must be in synchronized]
    C2 --> Y2
    C3 --> Y2
    D --> D1["Thread.sleep(millis)"]
    D --> D2["Thread.yield()"]
    D1 --> Z1[Does NOT release lock]
    D1 --> Z2[Timed suspension]
    D2 --> Z3[Hint to scheduler, not guaranteed]
```

## 4.4 Suspend vs Wait vs Sleep — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start{Need to pause a thread?}
    Start -->|Yes, with lock release| WaitPath[Use wait() inside synchronized]
    Start -->|Yes, no lock release| SleepPath[Use sleep() or wait(timeout)]
    Start -->|Legacy code only| SuspendPath[Use suspend()/resume() DEPRECATED]
    WaitPath --> Use1[Producer-Consumer, thread pools]
    SleepPath --> Use2[Timed delays, polling loops]
    SuspendPath --> Use3[⚠️ Avoid in new code]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `suspend()` and `wait()` methods in Java with respect to thread suspension. Why is `suspend()` deprecated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `suspend()` | `wait()` |
|---|---|---|
| Defined in | `java.lang.Thread` | `java.lang.Object` |
| Releases lock? | **No** — deadlock prone | **Yes** — safe |
| Requires `synchronized`? | No | **Yes** — must own the lock |
| Wake-up mechanism | `resume()` | `notify()` / `notifyAll()` / timeout |
| Status | **Deprecated since JDK 1.2** | Active and recommended |

**Why deprecated:** `suspend()` halts a thread **without releasing any monitor lock it holds**, leading to **deadlocks** if other threads attempt to acquire that lock. The thread cannot clean up resources, making it inherently unsafe. **(1 mark for deprecation reason)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of `notify()` and `notifyAll()` in inter-thread communication. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`notify()`** — Wakes up **one** arbitrarily chosen thread from the wait set of the object. **(1 mark)**
- **`notifyAll()`** — Wakes up **all** threads waiting on the object; they then compete for the lock. **(1 mark)**
- **Preference:** Use `notifyAll()` when **multiple waiters may exist and only some can proceed** (e.g., different conditions). Use `notify()` only when **exactly one waiter can validly proceed** (e.g., a single producer wakes a single consumer). **In general, `notifyAll()` is safer and recommended.** **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the lifecycle of a thread in Java with a neat state transition diagram. Highlight the states entered during thread suspension. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the **Producer–Consumer problem** using `wait()` and `notify()` methods. Explain how suspension and resumption of threads occur in your program. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

A thread in Java passes through the following states (defined in `Thread.State` enum):

1. **NEW** — Thread object is created but `start()` not yet called.
2. **RUNNABLE** — Thread is executing or ready to execute, waiting for CPU.
3. **BLOCKED** — Thread is waiting to acquire a monitor lock (entered via `synchronized`).
4. **WAITING** — Thread is waiting indefinitely until another thread performs a specific action.
   - Entered via: `wait()`, `join()` (no timeout), `park()`.
5. **TIMED_WAITING** — Thread is waiting for a specified time.
   - Entered via: `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)`.
6. **TERMINATED** — Thread has finished execution.

**State Diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start()
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : contention on synchronized
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait(), suspend()
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify(), resume()
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep(), wait(timeout)
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run() returns
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

**Suspension-related states:** `WAITING` (via `wait()`/`suspend()`), `TIMED_WAITING` (via `sleep()`/`wait(timeout)`), and `BLOCKED` (waiting for lock after `notify()`). **(7 marks — 1 mark per state, 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for suspension linkage)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Java Code:**

```java
class Buffer {
    private int item;
    private boolean full = false;

    public synchronized void produce(int value) throws InterruptedException {
        while (full) {
            System.out.println("Producer waiting...");
            wait();  // SUSPEND producer
        }
        item = value;
        System.out.println("Produced: " + item);
        full = true;
        notify();  // RESUME consumer
    }

    public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        while (!full) {
            System.out.println("Consumer waiting...");
            wait();  // SUSPEND consumer
        }
        System.out.println("Consumed: " + item);
        full = false;
        notify();  // RESUME producer
        return item;
    }
}

public class ProdConsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Buffer buf = new Buffer();
        Thread producer = new Thread(() -> {
            try { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) buf.produce(i); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
        }, "Producer");
        Thread consumer = new Thread(() -> {
            try { for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) buf.consume(); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
        }, "Consumer");
        producer.start();
        consumer.start();
    }
}
```

**Explanation (for marks):**

1. When `full == false`, consumer enters `consume()`, calls `wait()` — consumer is **suspended** and **releases the lock**. **(1 mark)**
2. Producer enters `produce()`, fills buffer, sets `full = true`, calls `notify()` — consumer is moved from wait set to **blocked-for-lock** state. **(1 mark)**
3. Producer exits `synchronized` block — **releases lock**. Consumer re-acquires lock and **resumes execution**. **(1 mark)**
4. Symmetrically, producer is suspended if buffer is full. **(1 mark)**
5. The `while` loop (instead of `if`) prevents **spurious wakeups** and **missed signals** — a critical KTU concept. **(2 marks)**
6. Both `wait()` and `notify()` are called inside `synchronized` blocks, satisfying the **intrinsic lock requirement**. **(1 mark)**

**Total: 7 marks**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Model Question]**

**(a)** Discuss the deprecated `suspend()` and `resume()` methods of the `Thread` class. Explain with a scenario why `suspend()` can lead to deadlocks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the methods `wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()`, `sleep()`, `yield()`, and `suspend()` in Java multithreading using a suitable comparison table. Which method is most suitable for implementing a thread-safe blocking queue, and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**`suspend()` and `resume()` overview:**

- Both are instance methods of `java.lang.Thread`, marked **deprecated since JDK 1.2**.
- `suspend()` puts the target thread into a suspended state; `resume()` brings it back to RUNNABLE.
- They are simple to use but **fundamentally unsafe**. **(2 marks)**

**Deadlock Scenario:**

Consider two threads `T1` and `T2`, and a shared object `Lock`:

| Step | T1 | T2 | State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acquires `Lock` | Idle | T1 RUNNABLE, holds Lock |
| 2 | Calls `this.suspend()` | Idle | T1 SUSPENDED, **still holds Lock** |
| 3 | (Suspended) | Tries `synchronized(Lock)` | T2 BLOCKED forever |
| 4 | T1 is never resumed (assume) | Deadlocked | **DEADLOCK** |

- Because `suspend()` does **not release the monitor lock**, any other thread that needs that lock is **blocked indefinitely**. **(2 marks)**
- Additionally, suspended threads cannot perform **resource cleanup** (closing files, releasing sockets), leading to **resource leaks**. **(1 mark)**
- Java's official documentation deprecates these methods precisely because they **violate thread-safety invariants**. **(1 mark)**
- **Modern alternative:** Use `wait()` (releases lock) within a `synchronized` block, woken up by `notify()`. **(1 mark)**

**Total: 7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Method | Class | Releases Lock? | Requires `synchronized`? | Deprecated? | Wake-up Mechanism | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `wait()` | Object | **Yes** | **Yes** | No | `notify()`, `notifyAll()`, timeout, interrupt | Inter-thread communication |
| `notify()` | Object | — | **Yes** | No | Wakes 1 thread | Single waiter scenarios |
| `notifyAll()` | Object | — | **Yes** | No | Wakes all threads | Multiple waiters (safer) |
| `sleep()` | Thread (static) | **No** | No | No | Timeout expires | Timed delays |
| `yield()` | Thread (static) | **No** | No | No | Scheduler decides | Hint to give up CPU |
| `suspend()` | Thread | **No** | No | **Yes** | `resume()` | Legacy only — avoid |

**(4 marks for table)**

**Most suitable for thread-safe blocking queue:** `wait()` and `notifyAll()` — **(3 marks)**

**Reasoning:**
1. A blocking queue requires threads to **suspend when the queue is empty/full** and **resume when items are added/removed**. `wait()` is the only mechanism that both **suspends** and **releases the lock**, allowing other threads to modify the queue.
2. `sleep()` would NOT release the lock, preventing producers/consumers from operating — defeating the purpose.
3. `suspend()` is deprecated and unsafe.
4. `notifyAll()` is preferred over `notify()` because there can be **both producers and consumers** waiting on the same queue — `notifyAll()` ensures no thread is starved.
5. In modern Java, `java.util.concurrent.ArrayBlockingQueue` uses **ReentrantLock + Condition.await()/signal()`** — which is the **industrial-grade equivalent** of `wait()/notify()`.

**Total: 7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing `wait()` outside `synchronized`** → 2 marks deducted (will throw `IllegalMonitorStateException` at runtime).
> 2. **Confusing `sleep()` with `wait()`** → 1 mark deducted; remember: only `wait()` releases the lock.
> 3. **Using `if` instead of `while` around `wait()`** → 1 mark deducted; the canonical pattern is `while (!condition) { wait(); }` to guard against **spurious wakeups**.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention `suspend()` is deprecated** → 1 mark deducted in "Why deprecated?" questions.
> 5. **Not drawing the state diagram** when asked → up to 2 marks deducted for diagram questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Suspending** a thread = temporarily pausing its execution without killing it.
- ✅ Three primary mechanisms: `suspend()/resume()` (legacy), `wait()/notify()` (modern), `sleep()` (timed).
- ✅ **`suspend()` is deprecated** — does **NOT** release locks → **deadlock risk**.
- ✅ **`wait()` releases the lock**; **`sleep()` does not**.
- ✅ **`wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()` MUST be called inside `synchronized` block** on the target object — otherwise `IllegalMonitorStateException`.
- ✅ **`wait()` is defined in `Object`**, not `Thread` — so every Java object supports it.
- ✅ Use **`while (!condition) { wait(); }`**, never `if`, to handle spurious wakeups.
- ✅ **`notify()` wakes ONE thread** (arbitrary); **`notifyAll()` wakes ALL** (safer default).
- ✅ **Thread states**: `NEW → RUNNABLE → BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING → TERMINATED`.
- ✅ **`suspend()` → WAITING**; **`sleep()` → TIMED_WAITING**; **`wait()` → WAITING** (or `TIMED_WAITING` with timeout).
- ✅ **Producer–Consumer** is the canonical use case for `wait()/notify()`.
- ✅ For modern code, prefer **`java.util.concurrent`** (`BlockingQueue`, `ReentrantLock`, `Condition`) over raw `wait()/notify()`.
- ✅ **`yield()` is a hint, not a suspension** — the scheduler may ignore it.
- ✅ **Deadlock formula:** `suspend()` + `synchronized lock held` + no other thread to `resume()` = **infinite block**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resuming and Stopping Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resuming and Stopping Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Java Multithreading** (as prescribed under the KTU 2024 OEERT613 syllabus), **Resuming** and **Stopping Threads** refer to the mechanisms by which a thread's execution flow is controlled after it has entered a paused/blocked state, or terminated permanently.

- **Resuming a Thread**: The act of transitioning a thread from a *Blocked* or *Waiting/Timed-Waiting* state back to the *Runnable* state so that the Thread Scheduler can again select it for CPU execution.
- **Stopping a Thread**: The act of terminating the `run()` method of a thread, causing it to leave the *Runnable* state permanently and enter the *Terminated (Dead)* state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The Java API originally provided the `stop()`, `suspend()`, and `resume()` methods inside the `Thread` class. However, these have been **deprecated since JDK 1.2** because they inherently cause *deadlocks, monitor-locks corruption, and inconsistent object states*. The KTU 2024 scheme mandates understanding **modern, safe alternatives** such as cooperative interruption using a `volatile` flag or the `Thread.interrupt()` mechanism.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **factory assembly line worker** named *Thread-T*:

1. **Running State**: The worker is actively assembling parts on the conveyor belt (the CPU is executing `run()`).
2. **Suspending (Pausing)**: The floor manager calls the worker aside to wait for a missing part — the worker stops working but still holds his tools (the thread holds its monitor lock).
3. **Resuming**: The missing part arrives; the manager signals the worker, who returns to the belt and resumes assembly.
4. **Stopping**: The shift ends. The worker permanently leaves the line and never returns to it.
5. **Safe vs. Unsafe Stopping**: An *unsafe stop* is like yanking the worker's badge mid-shift — the parts on the belt may be left half-assembled (corrupt shared objects). A *safe stop* is like the worker politely checking his task list, finishing the current step, and then clocking out — leaving the assembly line clean.

### 1.3 The Five Thread States (Per KTU Reference)

| State | Description |
|---|---|
| **New** | Thread object is created but `start()` is not yet called. |
| **Runnable** | Thread is ready to run, waiting for CPU allocation by the scheduler. |
| **Blocked** | Thread is waiting to acquire a monitor lock (entered via `synchronized` block). |
| **Waiting / Timed-Waiting** | Thread invoked `wait()`, `sleep()`, `join()`, or `park()`. |
| **Terminated** | The `run()` method has completed normally or thrown an uncaught exception. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Java Thread Lifecycle Term:**
> A thread is considered **Resumed** when it transitions from `BLOCKED`, `WAITING`, or `TIMED_WAITING` → `RUNNABLE`. A thread is considered **Stopped** when it transitions from `RUNNABLE` → `TERMINATED`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' Behind Deprecated Methods

The original `Thread.stop()`, `Thread.suspend()`, and `Thread.resume()` were deprecated because:

1. **`stop()`** releases all locks held by the thread immediately — even mid-critical-section. Shared objects are left in inconsistent states.
2. **`suspend()`** holds all locks indefinitely until `resume()` is called. If `resume()` is forgotten, the program **deadlocks**.
3. **`resume()`** itself cannot guarantee the thread will actually run — it only makes the thread eligible.

### 2.2 Modern Safe Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

#### Technique A: The `volatile` Flag (Cooperative Cancellation)

A `volatile boolean` instance variable is checked periodically inside `run()`. The main thread flips the flag to `false` to request the worker to exit gracefully.

```java
class SafeWorker extends Thread {
    private volatile boolean running = true;

    public void stopGracefully() {
        running = false;   // signal — not a forced kill
    }

    public void run() {
        while (running) {
            // do unit of work
        }
        // cleanup here
    }
}
```

- The keyword **`volatile`** guarantees visibility across CPU cores — without it, the worker thread may *cache* the flag forever and never see the change.

#### Technique B: `Thread.interrupt()` (Interrupt Mechanism)

`interrupt()` does **not** forcibly kill a thread. It sets an internal flag (`isInterrupted() = true`) and:
- If the thread is in `sleep()`, `wait()`, or `join()` → it throws `InterruptedException`, transitioning the thread out of the blocked state.
- If the thread is simply running → it must poll `Thread.interrupted()` itself.

```java
Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
    try {
        while (!Thread.currentThread().isInterrupted()) {
            Thread.sleep(500);
        }
    } catch (InterruptedException e) {
        // graceful exit
    }
});
t.start();
t.interrupt();   // request stop
```

### 2.3 Resuming a Thread

In Java, threads in the *Blocked* or *Waiting* state can be resumed by:

| Mechanism | What Causes Resume |
|---|---|
| `Object.notify()` | Another thread calls `notify()` on the same monitor. |
| `Object.notifyAll()` | Any one of the waiting threads is moved to Runnable. |
| `LockSupport.unpark(t)` | The specified thread's permit is restored (used by `java.util.concurrent`). |
| `t.join()` finishes | The joined thread terminates, freeing the joiner. |
| Sleep timer expires | After `sleep(ms)` elapses, the thread re-enters Runnable. |
| I/O completes | The blocked I/O operation returns, releasing the thread. |

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Method Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Method / Formula | State Transition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Force-kill (deprecated) | `t.stop()` | RUNNABLE → TERMINATED | **Unsafe — do not use** |
| Pause (deprecated) | `t.suspend()` | RUNNABLE → RUNNABLE (lock-held) | **Unsafe — causes deadlock** |
| Resume (deprecated) | `t.resume()` | lock-held → RUNNABLE | **Unsafe** |
| Safe Stop | `volatile flag` | RUNNABLE → TERMINATED (graceful) | **Recommended** |
| Cooperative Interrupt | `t.interrupt()` | any → Runnable + flag set | **Recommended** |
| Pause via Wait | `obj.wait()` | RUNNABLE → WAITING | Releases monitor lock |
| Resume via Notify | `obj.notify()` | WAITING → BLOCKED (for lock) → RUNNABLE | Random waiter chosen |
| Sleep | `Thread.sleep(ms)` | RUNNABLE → TIMED_WAITING | **Does not release locks** |
| Yield | `Thread.yield()` | RUNNING → RUNNABLE | Hint to scheduler |
| Join | `t.join()` | caller → WAITING until `t` dies | Synchronization helper |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Web Servers (Tomcat, Jetty)**: Worker thread pools use interrupt flags to cancel in-flight HTTP requests during shutdown.
- **GUI Applications (Swing/JavaFX)**: The Event Dispatch Thread uses `invokeLater()` and interruption to halt background tasks safely when a window is closed.
- **Embedded & IoT Systems**: Resource-constrained devices depend on cooperative cancellation to safely power down peripherals before terminating.
- **Parallel Computing (Fork/Join, Stream.parallel)**: Modern `java.util.concurrent` frameworks rely entirely on **interruption**, never on `stop()`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: The State-Transition Logic of `wait()` / `notify()`

This is the canonical "pause → resume" mechanism in Java. We trace every transition.

**Step 1 — Setup a shared object with a synchronized monitor.**

```java
class SharedResource {
    private int data;
    private boolean available = false;

    public synchronized void produce(int value) throws InterruptedException {
        while (available) {
            wait();                 // release lock & enter WAITING
        }
        data = value;
        available = true;
        notify();                  // wake ONE waiting consumer
    }

    public synchronized int consume() throws InterruptedException {
        while (!available) {
            wait();                 // release lock & enter WAITING
        }
        available = false;
        notify();                  // wake ONE waiting producer
        return data;
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — Trace the consumer's journey.**

```
T = 0 ms : consumer calls consume().
          Enters synchronized block → acquires monitor lock.
          available == false → executes wait().
          LOCK IS RELEASED. State = WAITING.

T = ?    : producer enters produce(), acquires lock,
          sets data & available, calls notify().
          Consumer is moved: WAITING → BLOCKED (waiting for lock).

T = ?    : producer exits synchronized block → releases lock.
          Consumer acquires lock → BLOCKED → RUNNABLE.
          Loop exits, returns data.
```

**Step 3 — Trace the producer's journey (symmetrically).**

The producer is paused via `wait()` while `available == true` and resumes only when the consumer calls `notify()` after reading.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Building a Safe Cancellable Worker Thread

We derive a robust, KTU-evaluable pattern.

**Step 1.** Declare a worker class extending `Thread`.

**Step 2.** Add a `private volatile boolean running = true;` field. The keyword `volatile` ensures *happens-before* visibility on every read.

**Step 3.** Inside `run()`, wrap the work loop with a `while (running)` check.

**Step 4.** Provide a public `cancel()` method that flips the flag to `false`. This is the **resume-from-external-signal** path: even if the thread was sleeping, it will wake up, check the flag, and exit.

**Step 5.** Enhance robustness by catching `InterruptedException` and re-checking the flag — this handles the case where `interrupt()` arrives during a `sleep()`.

```java
class CancellableWorker extends Thread {
    private volatile boolean running = true;

    public void cancel() {
        running = false;
        this.interrupt();          // also wakes it from sleep()
    }

    public void run() {
        try {
            while (running) {
                doWorkUnit();
                Thread.sleep(1000);
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            // interrupted while sleeping — re-check flag
        } finally {
            releaseResources();
        }
    }
}
```

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stopping vs. Resuming Techniques

| Dimension | `volatile` flag | `interrupt()` | `stop()` (legacy) | `suspend/resume` (legacy) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Releases locks safely? | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No — corrupts state** | **No — locks held forever** |
| Can pause mid-critical-section? | **No** (only between units) | **No** (only between units) | **Yes (dangerous)** | **Yes (dangerous)** |
| Wakes from `sleep()`/`wait()`? | No (must use `interrupt()` together) | **Yes** | Yes | No |
| Can cause deadlock? | **No** | **No** | Possible | **Yes** |
| KTU Recommended? | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No (Deprecated)** | **No (Deprecated)** |

### 3.4 Mathematical-Style Condition for Graceful Stop

A thread terminates *cleanly* if and only if the following condition holds:

$$
\text{CleanExit} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \Big(\forall\, o \in \text{SharedObjects} : \; \text{lock}(o) = \text{free} \;\land\; \text{state}(o) = \text{consistent}\Big)
$$

Where:
- $\text{lock}(o) = \text{free}$ means no monitor lock on object $o$ is held by the dying thread.
- $\text{state}(o) = \text{consistent}$ means all invariants of $o$ are preserved.

The `volatile` flag pattern trivially satisfies this condition because the flag is checked *outside* any synchronized block performing a multi-step mutation. The `stop()` method **violates** this condition because it forcibly releases locks mid-mutation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — Full Thread Lifecycle with Resuming & Stopping

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateNew
    stateNew --> stateRunnable : start()
    stateRunnable --> stateBlocked : acquire monitor lock fails
    stateRunnable --> stateWaiting : wait() called
    stateRunnable --> stateTimedWaiting : sleep(ms) or wait(ms)
    stateRunnable --> stateTerminated : run() returns or uncaught exception
    stateBlocked --> stateRunnable : lock acquired
    stateWaiting --> stateBlocked : notify() or notifyAll()
    stateTimedWaiting --> stateRunnable : timeout elapsed
    stateTimedWaiting --> stateBlocked : notify() called
    stateWaiting --> stateTerminated : interrupt() AND flag = false
    stateTimedWaiting --> stateTerminated : interrupt() AND flag = false
    stateTerminated --> [*]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Safe Stop Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main thread calls cancel] --> B[Set volatile running = false]
    B --> C[Call thread.interrupt]
    C --> D{Worker currently sleeping?}
    D -- Yes --> E[InterruptedException thrown]
    D -- No --> F[Flag polled on next loop check]
    E --> G[finally block executes]
    F --> G
    G --> H[releaseResources called]
    H --> I[run method returns]
    I --> J[Thread enters TERMINATED state]
    J --> K[All monitor locks released cleanly]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Why `stop()` is Unsafe

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Unsafe[Unsafe stop]
        A1[Thread inside synchronized block] --> A2[stop called]
        A2 --> A3[Monitor lock forcibly released]
        A3 --> A4[Shared object left in inconsistent state]
        A4 --> A5[Other threads read corrupt data]
    end
    subgraph Safe[Safe cancel via flag]
        B1[Worker checks flag between units] --> B2[Flag = false]
        B2 --> B3[Exits loop cleanly]
        B3 --> B4[Releases lock normally]
        B4 --> B5[Other threads see consistent state]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Why were the `stop()`, `suspend()`, and `resume()` methods of the `Thread` class deprecated in Java?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `stop()` method was deprecated because it **forcibly terminates** a thread, releasing all of its held monitor locks immediately — even if the thread is in the middle of a critical section. This can leave shared objects in an inconsistent or corrupted state, leading to data corruption and unpredictable behavior in other threads waiting for those locks. The `suspend()` method was deprecated because it **holds all locks indefinitely** until `resume()` is called; if `resume()` is forgotten, the program deadlocks. `resume()` itself is unsafe because it cannot guarantee that the suspended thread will actually execute. Safe modern alternatives include a `volatile` boolean flag for cooperative cancellation and the `Thread.interrupt()` mechanism, both of which allow the thread to finish its current unit of work and release locks cleanly. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Explain the role of the `volatile` keyword in safely stopping a thread.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `volatile` keyword ensures that updates to a boolean flag variable are **immediately visible** to all threads by preventing the JIT compiler and CPU from caching the value in thread-local registers or CPU caches. Without `volatile`, the worker thread may continue to read a stale `true` value forever and never notice that the main thread requested cancellation. With `volatile`, every read of the flag issues a memory barrier, guaranteeing *happens-before* visibility — so when the main thread sets `running = false`, the worker thread sees the change on its next loop iteration and exits gracefully. It also prevents instruction reordering around the flag check. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat state diagram, explain the complete lifecycle of a thread in Java. List all possible transitions and the methods that cause each transition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates how a thread can be safely stopped using a `volatile` flag and the `interrupt()` mechanism. Explain why this approach is preferred over the deprecated `stop()` method. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Thread Lifecycle (7 marks):**

A Java thread exists in one of six states defined in `java.lang.Thread.State`:
1. **NEW** — Thread object created, `start()` not called.
2. **RUNNABLE** — Thread is ready and may be running or waiting for the CPU.
3. **BLOCKED** — Waiting to acquire a monitor lock to enter a `synchronized` block.
4. **WAITING** — Called `wait()` (no timeout), `join()` (no timeout), or `LockSupport.park()`. The thread releases its lock.
5. **TIMED_WAITING** — Called `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)`, or `parkNanos()`. The thread does **not** release locks for `sleep()`, but does for `wait(ms)`.
6. **TERMINATED** — `run()` returned normally or threw an uncaught exception.

Transitions and their triggers:
- NEW → RUNNABLE : `start()`
- RUNNABLE → BLOCKED : entering `synchronized` when another thread holds the lock
- RUNNABLE → WAITING : `wait()`, `join()`, `park()`
- RUNNABLE → TIMED_WAITING : `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)`
- BLOCKED → RUNNABLE : monitor lock acquired
- WAITING → BLOCKED : `notify()` / `notifyAll()` / `unpark()`
- WAITING/TIMED_WAITING → RUNNABLE : timeout elapsed
- Any → TERMINATED : `run()` returns / uncaught exception / safe cancellation flag

(Diagrammatic representation: see Section 4.1 of these notes — 2 marks for diagram, 5 marks for explanation.)

**Part (b) — Safe Stopping Program (7 marks):**

```java
class SafeStopDemo extends Thread {
    private volatile boolean running = true;

    public void cancel() {
        running = false;
        this.interrupt();        // wake if sleeping
    }

    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Worker started: " + getName());
        try {
            int count = 0;
            while (running) {
                System.out.println("Working... " + (++count));
                Thread.sleep(500);     // periodic work + flag poll
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("Interrupted during sleep — exiting.");
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Cleanup done. Worker exiting.");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        SafeStopDemo worker = new SafeStopDemo();
        worker.start();
        Thread.sleep(2500);     // let it work a few units
        System.out.println("Main: requesting cancellation.");
        worker.cancel();
        worker.join();          // wait for clean termination
        System.out.println("Main: worker is fully terminated.");
    }
}
```

**Why this is preferred over `stop()` (explanation):**
1. **Lock Safety**: `stop()` releases monitor locks forcibly, mid-mutation. The `volatile` flag + `interrupt()` only acts *between* work units, leaving shared objects consistent.
2. **Deadlock Avoidance**: Unlike `suspend()`/`resume()`, this pattern cannot produce a deadlock because no locks are held while waiting.
3. **Resource Cleanup**: The `finally` block guarantees cleanup logic executes — `stop()` skips all cleanup.
4. **Predictability**: The worker always exits at a well-defined checkpoint, making the program easier to test and reason about.
5. **Standards Compliance**: `stop()` was deprecated in JDK 1.2 and may produce compiler warnings or be removed in future JDK versions. **(7 marks total: 4 marks code, 3 marks explanation)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Differentiate between the deprecated `suspend()`/`resume()` methods and the modern `wait()`/`notify()` mechanism for pausing and resuming threads. Provide code snippets to illustrate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of cooperative thread interruption. Write a Java program that demonstrates how `interrupt()` interacts with a thread blocked in `sleep()` and how the `InterruptedException` allows the thread to terminate gracefully. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — `suspend()`/`resume()` vs `wait()`/`notify()` (7 marks):**

| Aspect | `suspend()` / `resume()` (deprecated) | `wait()` / `notify()` (modern) |
|---|---|---|
| Lock behavior | **Holds all locks** during suspension → deadlock-prone | **Releases the monitor lock** on `wait()`; reacquires on resume |
| Resume trigger | Another thread must call `resume()` explicitly | `notify()` / `notifyAll()` / timeout / `interrupt()` |
| Safety | Deprecated since JDK 1.2 | Safe and recommended |
| Usage context | Instance methods of `Thread` | Instance methods of `Object` (used inside `synchronized`) |
| Risk | If `resume()` is never called → **permanent deadlock** | Risk-free; lock is freed so other threads can proceed |

Illustration — unsafe legacy approach:

```java
class LegacyPause {
    Thread worker = new Thread(() -> heavyWork());
    worker.start();
    worker.suspend();   // worker now holds locks forever if resume() never called!
    worker.resume();    // hope we remember
}
```

Illustration — safe modern approach:

```java
class Shared {
    private boolean ready = false;
    public synchronized void waitForReady() throws InterruptedException {
        while (!ready) wait();          // releases lock → no deadlock
    }
    public synchronized void setReady() {
        ready = true;
        notify();                       // safely wakes ONE waiter
    }
}
```

(Marks: 3 marks for comparison table, 2 marks each for the two code snippets.) **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Cooperative Interruption Program (7 marks):**

Cooperative interruption means the thread is *asked* to stop, never *forced*. The thread decides when and how to respond — usually at a safe checkpoint.

```java
class InterruptibleTask extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Task started.");
        try {
            for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
                System.out.println("Processing step " + i);
                Thread.sleep(1000);     // throws InterruptedException
            }
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("Interrupt received — abandoning remaining steps.");
            return;                     // graceful exit
        }
        System.out.println("Task completed normally.");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        InterruptibleTask t = new InterruptibleTask();
        t.start();
        Thread.sleep(3500);             // let it do ~3 steps
        System.out.println("Main: sending interrupt signal.");
        t.interrupt();                  // sets the interrupted flag & unblocks sleep
        t.join();                       // wait for clean termination
        System.out.println("Main: task fully stopped.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation of behavior:**
- At `t.sleep(1000)`, the thread is in **TIMED_WAITING**.
- `t.interrupt()` sets the internal `interrupted` flag and causes the `sleep()` to throw `InterruptedException`.
- The catch block catches it, prints the message, and returns — running clean cleanup.
- After `join()`, the main thread knows the worker has reached the **TERMINATED** state with all locks released.

(Marks: 3 marks program, 4 marks explanation of the interrupt-flag + exception interaction.) **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing `stop()` in the answer as a "correct" method** → full 0 for that sub-part. It is *deprecated and unsafe* — examiners will deduct marks.
> 2. **Forgetting `volatile`** when explaining the flag-based pattern → loses 1–2 marks; `volatile` is essential for visibility.
> 3. **Confusing `sleep()` with `wait()`**: `sleep()` does **not** release the monitor lock; `wait()` does. Examiners test this every year.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention `notify()` reacquires the lock** before resuming: the woken thread goes WAITING → BLOCKED → RUNNABLE, not directly RUNNABLE.
> 5. **Drawing the lifecycle diagram with wrong arrows** (e.g., `TERMINATED → RUNNABLE`): a thread, once terminated, can **never** be restarted.
> 6. **Omitting the `finally` block** in cancellation code: cleanup is part of the requirement.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A Java thread has **six states**: NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED.
- ✅ **Resuming** = transition from BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING → RUNNABLE.
- ✅ **Stopping** = transition from RUNNABLE → TERMINATED (cleanly).
- ✅ `stop()`, `suspend()`, `resume()` are **deprecated since JDK 1.2** — never use them in KTU answers.
- ✅ Safe stopping uses either a **`volatile boolean` flag** or the **`Thread.interrupt()` mechanism**, or both combined.
- ✅ `volatile` ensures cross-thread **memory visibility** and prevents instruction reordering.
- ✅ `wait()` releases the monitor lock; `sleep()` does **not**.
- ✅ A `notify()`-awakened thread first transitions to **BLOCKED** (reacquiring the lock) and then to RUNNABLE.
- ✅ `Thread.interrupt()` does not kill the thread — it sets a flag and unblocks `wait()`/`sleep()`/`join()` with an `InterruptedException`.
- ✅ Once a thread enters TERMINATED, it **cannot** be restarted by another `start()` call — it throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.
- ✅ Always use `finally` blocks for cleanup during cancellation to guarantee resource release.
- ✅ Modern frameworks (ForkJoinPool, ExecutorService) build cancellation on top of the interrupt mechanism — understanding this topic is foundational for all `java.util.concurrent` work.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Handling - Event Handling Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Handling - Event Handling Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Event Handling** in Object-Oriented Programming (specifically Java AWT and Swing) is the mechanism by which a program responds to user interactions or system-generated occurrences (such as a mouse click, key press, window resizing, or button press) through a well-defined object-oriented dispatch system known as the **Event Delegation Model (EDM)**.

Formally, an *event* is an object that describes a state change in a Graphical User Interface (GUI) component. The *event handling mechanism* is the structural software pattern that ties together three collaborating entities:

1. **Event Source** — The GUI object that generates the event.
2. **Event Object** — The encapsulated payload carrying information about the occurrence.
3. **Event Listener** — The object that waits for and processes the event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Remember/Understand), students must be able to *define* the three event-handling components and *describe* how they collaborate. Under (Apply), students must be able to *write* Java programs that register listeners and override event-handling methods.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Doorbell System

Imagine a **doorbell system** in your house:

| Real-World Element | Event Handling Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The person who presses the doorbell | **User Action** (clicking a button) |
| The doorbell button on the wall | **Event Source** (e.g., `JButton`) |
| The electrical signal sent to the chime | **Event Object** (e.g., `ActionEvent`) |
| The chime box that produces sound | **Event Listener** (e.g., `ActionListener`) |
| The wiring connecting button to chime | **Registration via `addActionListener()`** |

Just as a doorbell does nothing until it is **wired** to a chime, a Java GUI component does nothing when interacted with until it is **registered** with a listener object. The chime cannot ring on its own — it must be **notified** by the button through a registered channel. This is the essence of the **Delegation** model: the source *delegates* the responsibility of handling the event to a registered listener.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Event Handling

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Event Source**
> An **Event Source** is a GUI component (e.g., `Button`, `TextField`, `MenuItem`) that **generates** an event when the user interacts with it. The source must explicitly *register* a listener to receive notifications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Event Object**
> An **Event Object** is an instance of a subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (specifically `java.awt.AWTEvent`). It encapsulates all information about the event, including the source, the type, the time, and location (for mouse events).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Event Listener**
> An **Event Listener** is an object of a class that **implements** one or more of the `java.awt.event` or `javax.swing.event` listener interfaces. The listener interface declares the methods that will be invoked when the corresponding event occurs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Event Delegation Model (EDM) — Step-by-Step Logic

The Event Delegation Model is the **standard AWT/Swing pattern** for processing events. It replaces the older **inheritance-based** (JDK 1.0) model, which required subclassing GUI components to handle events — a design that violated encapsulation and produced bloated hierarchies.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic:

1. **User Interaction** — The user performs an action (e.g., clicks a `JButton`).
2. **Event Generation** — The JVM detects the action on the GUI component. The source object creates an appropriate **Event Object** (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
3. **Event Object Population** — The newly created event object is populated with metadata: source reference, event ID, timestamp, modifiers (Shift/Ctrl), and x/y coordinates (for mouse events).
4. **Listener Notification (Dispatch)** — The source iterates through its **internal listener registry** (a `Vector` of registered listeners) and invokes the **appropriate callback method** on each listener, passing the event object as an argument.
5. **Event Processing** — The listener's overridden method executes the business logic.
6. **Event Propagation Ends** — The event is consumed (no longer propagated). Listener methods are *callbacks* — they are not called explicitly by the programmer.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the word "Delegation"?** The source *delegates* the responsibility of event handling to a separate listener object, rather than handling the event itself. This promotes **loose coupling** — the GUI component does not need to know *what* will happen in response; it only needs to know *that* a registered listener exists.

## 2.2 Event Class Hierarchy (java.awt)

All AWT event classes descend from `java.util.EventObject` → `java.awt.AWTEvent` → specific event types.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{java.util.EventObject} \\
\quad\downarrow \\
\text{java.awt.AWTEvent} \\
\quad\downarrow \\
\{ \text{ActionEvent},\ \text{MouseEvent},\ \text{KeyEvent},\ \text{WindowEvent},\ \text{ItemEvent},\ \text{TextEvent},\ \dots \}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.3 Listener Interface Catalog

Listeners are **interfaces** (not classes) declared in `java.awt.event` and `javax.swing.event`. Each interface declares one or more abstract methods that the implementer **must override**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Event / Listener Reference

| Event Class | Listener Interface | Key Method(s) | Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | Button click, menu select, Enter in text field |
| `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` | Mouse interactions on component |
| `MouseEvent` | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` | Mouse movement |
| `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped` | Keyboard activity |
| `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated` | Window state changes |
| `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | Checkbox, radio button, list selection |
| `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent)` | Text field/area content change |
| `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` | Component focus changes |
| `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged` | Scrollbar movement |

## 2.5 Adapter Classes — A KTU Favourite

Implementing a listener interface requires overriding **all** of its methods, even if only one is needed. Java provides **Adapter Classes** in `java.awt.event` that supply **empty (no-op) implementations** of all methods. You subclass the adapter and override only the methods you need.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** For listener interfaces with **more than one method** (e.g., `MouseListener` has 5, `WindowListener` has 7), an adapter is provided. For single-method interfaces (e.g., `ActionListener`), **no adapter exists** — implement the interface directly.

| Listener Interface | Adapter Class | # of Methods |
|---|---|---|
| `MouseListener` | `MouseAdapter` | 5 |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `MouseMotionAdapter` | 2 |
| `KeyListener` | `KeyAdapter` | 3 |
| `WindowListener` | `WindowAdapter` | 7 |
| `FocusListener` | `FocusAdapter` | 2 |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

The Event Delegation Model is the **backbone of every interactive Java application** — from Android's UI framework (which adapted this model via `OnClickListener`) to enterprise Swing dashboards, IDE plugins, and embedded HMI (Human-Machine Interface) systems used in industrial control panels. Understanding it is foundational to **GUI development, test automation (Selenium listeners), and event-driven microservices**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Code Architecture

## 3.1 The Complete Event Handling Workflow

Below is the **exhaustive six-stage derivation** of how an event propagates through the system when a user clicks a `JButton` registered with an `ActionListener`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Stage 1: User Interaction} \\
&\text{User clicks the } \texttt{JButton} \text{ at pixel } (x, y). \\
&\text{The AWT Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) intercepts the native OS click event.} \\
\\
&\textbf{Stage 2: Event Object Instantiation} \\
&\text{The } \texttt{JButton} \text{ source object creates an } \texttt{ActionEvent} \text{ object:} \\
&\texttt{ActionEvent e = new ActionEvent(source, id, command);}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Stage 3: Event Metadata Population} \\
&\text{The event object is populated with the following fields:} \\
&\bullet\ \texttt{getSource()} \rightarrow \text{reference to the } \texttt{JButton} \\
&\bullet\ \texttt{getID()} \rightarrow \texttt{ActionEvent.ACTION\_PERFORMED} \ (1001) \\
&\bullet\ \texttt{getActionCommand()} \rightarrow \text{the button's label or registered action command} \\
&\bullet\ \texttt{getWhen()} \rightarrow \text{timestamp of the click (millis since epoch)} \\
&\bullet\ \texttt{getModifiers()} \rightarrow \text{bitmask of Shift, Ctrl, Alt, Meta keys} \\
\\
&\textbf{Stage 4: Listener Registry Lookup} \\
&\text{The source's } \texttt{EventListener} \text{ vector is queried:} \\
&\texttt{for (ActionListener l : button.getListeners(ActionListener.class))} \\
&\quad\texttt{l.actionPerformed(e);} \\
\\
&\textbf{Stage 5: Callback Execution} \\
&\text{The listener's overridden } \texttt{actionPerformed} \text{ method runs.} \\
&\text{This is the only stage controlled by the application developer.} \\
\\
&\textbf{Stage 6: Event Lifecycle Ends} \\
&\text{No further propagation. The event object becomes eligible for GC.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Four Implementation Strategies (from classic to modern)

### Strategy 1 — Separate Listener Class (Classical)

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

class ButtonHandler implements ActionListener {
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Button clicked: " + e.getActionCommand());
    }
}

public class Demo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Frame f = new Frame("Demo 1");
        Button b = new Button("Click Me");
        b.addActionListener(new ButtonHandler()); // registration
        f.add(b);
        f.setSize(300, 200);
        f.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

*Logic:* A dedicated class implements `ActionListener`. Its object is registered with the button. **Advantage:** Reusable across multiple components. **Disadvantage:** Verbose for single-use handlers.

### Strategy 2 — The Component Itself as Listener

```java
public class Demo2 extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Button b;
    Demo2() {
        b = new Button("Press");
        b.addActionListener(this);   // source registers itself
        add(b);
        setSize(300, 200);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        setTitle("You pressed: " + e.getActionCommand());
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { new Demo2(); }
}
```

*Logic:* The container class implements `ActionListener` and passes `this` to `addActionListener`. **Disadvantage:** Couples the GUI structure to event logic; limits single inheritance.

### Strategy 3 — Inner Class (KTU High-Yield Pattern)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class Demo3 extends JFrame {
    JButton b;
    Demo3() {
        b = new JButton("Submit");
        b.addActionListener(new ButtonHandler()); // inner class
        add(b);
        setSize(300, 200);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    // Non-static inner class has implicit access to outer instance
    class ButtonHandler implements ActionListener {
        public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
            JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(null, "Submitted!");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) { new Demo3(); }
}
```

*Logic:* The listener is defined as a **non-static inner class**, giving it direct access to all outer-class members. This is the **most commonly tested pattern** in KTU exams.

### Strategy 4 — Anonymous Inner Class (Modern & Concise)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class Demo4 extends JFrame {
    Demo4() {
        JButton b = new JButton("Click");
        b.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Clicked at: " + e.getWhen());
            }
        });
        add(b);
        setSize(300, 200);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { new Demo4(); }
}
```

*Logic:* The listener interface is implemented inline without naming the class. **Advantage:** Maximum locality. **Disadvantage:** Not reusable; slightly harder to read in large codebases.

### Strategy 5 — Lambda Expression (Java 8+, Bonus Knowledge)

```java
b.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Lambda click: " + e.getActionCommand()));
```

*Logic:* Since `ActionListener` is a **functional interface** (single abstract method), it can be replaced by a lambda. This is the modern industry standard.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Strategy | Lines of Code | Access to Outer | Reusability | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Separate Class | High | Via explicit reference | High | Moderate |
| Self-as-Listener | Low | Direct | Low | Moderate |
| Inner Class | Medium | Implicit | Medium | **Very High** |
| Anonymous Class | Low | Implicit (captures) | None | **Very High** |
| Lambda | Lowest | Captures (effectively final) | None | Emerging |

## 3.4 Worked Example — KeyEvent with KeyAdapter

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class KeyDemo extends Frame {
    TextField tf;
    Label result;
    KeyDemo() {
        tf = new TextField(20);
        result = new Label("Type something...");
        tf.addKeyListener(new KeyAdapter() {
            public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) {
                result.setText("Last char: " + e.getKeyChar());
            }
        });
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        add(tf); add(result);
        setSize(300, 150);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) { new KeyDemo(); }
}
```

*Walkthrough:*
1. `KeyAdapter` is subclassed anonymously inside the constructor.
2. Only `keyTyped` is overridden — the other two methods (`keyPressed`, `keyReleased`) inherit no-op behavior.
3. `e.getKeyChar()` returns the character typed.
4. The label is updated in real time.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Event Delegation Model — Architecture Flow

<!-- 
Node Identifier Alpha Rule applied: every id is alphanumeric and prefixed.
No reserved keywords used as node names.
No markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels.
-->
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER] -->|clicks button| B[EVENT SOURCE JButton]

    C -->|2 contains source ID timestamp| D[LISTENER REGISTRY VECTOR INSIDE BUTTON]
    D -->|3 invoke callback| E[EVENT LISTENER ActionListener]
    E -->|4 actionPerformed e| F[BUSINESS LOGIC METHOD BODY]
    F -->|5 update UI| G[GUI REFRESH]
    G -->|feedback| A

    H[java util EventObject] --> I[java awt AWTEvent]
    I --> J[ActionEvent]
    I --> K[MouseEvent]
    I --> L[KeyEvent]
    I --> M[WindowEvent]

    style A fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Listener Registration and Notification — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REG[addActionListener L] --> STORE[Internal Listener Vector]
    STORE --> ITER{for each listener L}
    ITER --> INVOKE[L actionPerformed Event]
    INVOKE --> DONE[Return to EDT]

    subgraph "Listener Implementation Choices"
    L1[Separate Class]
    L2[This as Listener]
    L3[Inner Class]
    L4[Anonymous Class]
    L5[Lambda Java 8]
    end

    L1 -.-> INVOKE
    L2 -.-> INVOKE
    L3 -.-> INVOKE
    L4 -.-> INVOKE
    L5 -.-> INVOKE

    style REG fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style STORE fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style ITER fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style INVOKE fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style DONE fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Event Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EO[java util EventObject] --> AE[java awt AWTEvent]
    AE --> ACE[ActionEvent]
    AE --> ME[MouseEvent]
    AE --> KE[KeyEvent]
    AE --> WE[WindowEvent]
    AE --> IE[ItemEvent]
    AE --> TE[TextEvent]
    AE --> FE[FocusEvent]

    ACE --> AL[ActionListener]
    ME --> ML[MouseListener]
    ME --> MML[MouseMotionListener]
    KE --> KL[KeyListener]
    WE --> WL[WindowListener]
    IE --> IL[ItemListener]
    TE --> TL[TextListener]
    FE --> FL[FocusListener]

    style EO fill:#264653,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style AE fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style ACE fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style ME fill:#f4a261,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
