```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Define the Event Delegation Model. List its three main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Event Delegation Model (EDM)** is an object-oriented event-processing pattern in Java AWT/Swing where the responsibility of handling an event generated by a GUI component is *delegated* to a separate listener object, rather than handled by the component itself or its container.

The three main components are:

1. **Event Source** — The GUI component (e.g., `Button`, `JTextField`) that generates the event in response to user interaction.
2. **Event Object** — An instance of an `AWTEvent` subclass (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`) that encapsulates details about the event such as source, type, time, and coordinates.
3. **Event Listener** — An object whose class implements a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`); it provides the callback methods invoked when the event occurs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *omit* the explicit registration step (`addActionListener(...)`) when describing the model. Always mention that the listener must be **registered** with the source for the model to function. (–1 mark if missed)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**What is an Adapter Class? Why is it used? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Adapter Class** is a class in `java.awt.event` that provides **empty (no-op) implementations** for all the methods declared in a corresponding listener interface with more than one method. It is used so that programmers can **subclass the adapter and override only the methods of interest**, instead of being forced to provide (possibly meaningless) implementations for all methods of the interface.

**Example:** `MouseAdapter` implements all five methods of `MouseListener` (`mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited`) as empty methods. By extending `MouseAdapter` and overriding only `mouseClicked`, the programmer avoids writing four unused empty methods.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** state that an adapter exists for `ActionListener`. ActionListener has only one method, so no adapter is provided. (–1 mark if incorrect)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[Internal Choice Notice — As per KTU ESE Pattern]**
> *Answer ANY ONE of the following full questions:*

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**a)** Explain the Event Delegation Model in Java with a neat diagram. Describe the roles of Event Source, Event Object, and Event Listener. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write a Java program using `Swing` to create a `JFrame` containing a `JTextField` and a `JButton`. When the button is clicked, the text typed in the text field should be displayed in a `JLabel` below. Use an **anonymous inner class** for event handling. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The **Event Delegation Model** is the modern (post-JDK 1.1) approach for handling GUI events in Java. It separates event generation from event handling, promoting loose coupling and reusability.

**Roles:**

- **Event Source:** A GUI component such as `JButton`, `JTextField`, or `JMenuItem`. It maintains an internal list (`Vector`) of registered listeners. When the user interacts with it, the source creates an `EventObject` and notifies each registered listener.

- **Event Object:** An instance of a class that extends `java.awt.AWTEvent` (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`). It carries all relevant information: the source, the event ID, the timestamp, and any modifier keys. It is passed as the argument to the listener's callback method.

- **Event Listener:** An object whose class implements a `Listener` interface from `java.awt.event` (e.g., `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`). The interface declares one or more abstract methods that are invoked by the source when the event occurs. The class implementing the interface provides the actual business logic.

**Process:**
1. The source object is instantiated and its `addXxxListener()` method is called, passing a listener object as the argument — this **registers** the listener.
2. The user interacts with the source (e.g., clicks a button).
3. The source creates an `EventObject` and iterates over its listener list, invoking the appropriate callback method on each listener.
4. The listener's method executes the response logic.

*Diagram:* (Refer to SECTION 4.1 — the Event Delegation Model Architecture Flow above.)

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class TextDisplayDemo extends JFrame {
    JTextField inputField;
    JLabel outputLabel;

    TextDisplayDemo() {
        setTitle("Text Display Demo");
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setSize(400, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        inputField = new JTextField(20);
        JButton showButton = new JButton("Show");
        outputLabel = new JLabel("Result will appear here");

        // Anonymous inner class for event handling
        showButton.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                outputLabel.setText("You typed: " + inputField.getText());
            }
        });

        add(new Label("Enter text:"));
        add(inputField);
        add(showButton);
        add(outputLabel);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new TextDisplayDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of key points:**

- `JFrame` is the top-level container; `FlowLayout` arranges components left-to-right.
- `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` ensures the program terminates when the window closes — this internally uses a `WindowListener`.
- The `addActionListener(new ActionListener() { ... })` is an **anonymous inner class** implementing the `ActionListener` interface.
- The anonymous class has **implicit access** to outer-class members (`inputField`, `outputLabel`) because it is a non-static inner class.
- `e.getActionCommand()` is not needed here since the button is unambiguous; instead, the source (`showButton`) is implicit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Deductions:**
> 1. Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` → (–½ mark) – window will not close properly.
> 2. Using `addActionListener(this)` without implementing `ActionListener` in the outer class → **Compilation error** (–1 mark).
> 3. Not importing `java.awt.event.*` → compilation error (–½ mark).

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**a)** Differentiate between the **JDK 1.0 Event Model** and the **Event Delegation Model (JDK 1.1+)**. State at least four differences. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write a Java AWT program to demonstrate **mouse event handling** using a `MouseAdapter`. The program should display the coordinates of the mouse pointer whenever `mouseMoved` occurs inside a `Frame`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

| Aspect | JDK 1.0 Event Model | Event Delegation Model (JDK 1.1+) |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Inheritance-based; container subclasses override `handleEvent()` | Composition-based; listeners are separate objects |
| **Coupling** | Tight coupling between container and event code | Loose coupling — source only knows about the interface |
| **Hierarchy** | Forced to subclass `Component` → bloats class tree | No subclassing required |
| **Method Granularity** | `handleEvent()` then `action()`, `mouseDown()`, etc. (one giant method) | Each event type has its own dedicated method (e.g., `actionPerformed`) |
| **Reusability** | Low — event logic embedded in subclass | High — listener classes can be reused across components |
| **Performance** | All events pass through `handleEvent` even if uninteresting | Source dispatches only to registered listeners |
| **Multiple Handlers** | Not natural to support | Easy — multiple listeners can be registered on one source |

**Conclusion:** The Event Delegation Model is superior in every respect — modularity, reusability, and maintainability — and is the **only** model supported in modern Java GUI development.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class MouseCoordsDemo extends Frame {
    Label coordsLabel;

    MouseCoordsDemo() {
        setTitle("Mouse Coordinates Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        coordsLabel = new Label("Move the mouse inside the window");
        add(coordsLabel);

        // Register MouseMotionListener using an anonymous MouseMotionAdapter
        addMouseMotionListener(new MouseMotionAdapter() {
            public void mouseMoved(MouseEvent e) {
                coordsLabel.setText("X: " + e.getX() + ", Y: " + e.getY());
            }
        });

        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new MouseCoordsDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**

- `MouseMotionAdapter` is used because we only need `mouseMoved` (and possibly `mouseDragged`) — both are no-op by default.
- `e.getX()` and `e.getY()` return the integer coordinates relative to the source component's top-left corner.
- A `WindowAdapter` is used to handle the `windowClosing` event without implementing all seven `WindowListener` methods.
- The `addMouseMotionListener()` call is made on the `Frame` itself — the `Frame` is both the source and the container.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes on Event Handling Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing `MouseListener` and `MouseMotionListener`.** `mouseClicked` is in `MouseListener`, not `MouseMotionListener`. Mixing them up will fail compilation. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting to call `setVisible(true)`** → the program runs but no window appears, leading to "silent" execution errors. (–½ mark)
> 3. **Returning values from event-handling methods** — they should always return `void`. Returning `boolean` from `keyTyped` may be expected, but not from `actionPerformed`. (–½ mark)
> 4. **Registering listener on the wrong component** (e.g., adding a listener to the `Label` instead of the `Frame` for mouse events) — events won't fire. (–1 mark)
> 5. **In exam answers, students frequently write only the code and skip the diagram** for a 7-mark theory part. The KTU scheme **requires** a diagram for full marks on theory questions. (–2 to –3 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Event Handling Mechanisms**

- ☐ **Event Delegation Model** is the standard post-JDK 1.1 model — based on **Source → Event Object → Listener**.
- ☐ The **three components**: Event Source (GUI component), Event Object (`AWTEvent` subclass), Event Listener (interface implementer).
- ☐ **Registration** is mandatory: `source.addXxxListener(listenerObject);`
- ☐ **Listener interfaces** are in `java.awt.event` (AWT) and `javax.swing.event` (Swing).
- ☐ **Adapter classes** provide empty implementations — use them for multi-method listener interfaces.
- ☐ **No adapter exists** for `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` (single-method interfaces).
- ☐ **Anonymous inner class** is the most concise classical pattern; **lambda** is the modern Java 8+ way.
- ☐ All event classes inherit from `java.util.EventObject` → `java.awt.AWTEvent`.
- ☐ `ActionEvent` uses `actionPerformed`; `MouseEvent` uses 5 methods; `KeyEvent` uses 3 methods; `WindowEvent` uses 7 methods.
- ☐ Method `e.getSource()` returns the originating component — useful when one listener handles multiple sources.
- ☐ Event handling occurs on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** for Swing components.
- ☐ **Inner class** has implicit access to outer class members; **anonymous class** is a one-shot inline implementation.
- ☐ The **JDK 1.0 model** (inheritance-based) is **obsolete** — never use it in exam answers or production code.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delegation Event Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Delegation Event Model # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** is the standard event-handling mechanism defined in the **java.awt.event** package of the Java AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) and Swing frameworks. It is founded on the principle that an **event is generated by a source object** (such as a `Button`, `TextField`, or `MenuItem`), and the responsibility of processing that event is **delegated** to a separate object known as the **event listener** (or **event handler**). Formally, the Delegation Event Model is defined as: > *"A model in which an event source registers a set of listeners. When the source generates an event, it delegates the event to the registered listeners, which in turn invoke the appropriate handler method."* This model adheres strictly to the **Observer design pattern** (GoF pattern) and to the **OOAD principle of separation of concerns**, decoupling *event generation* from *event handling*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Delegation Event Model is a high-weightage topic in Module 4. You are expected to know the architecture, event classes, listener interfaces, the role of adapter classes, and the procedural steps to implement an event-driven program in Java. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Receptionist-Routing Model Imagine a busy corporate office: - A **client** (the user) makes a phone call — this is the **event** (e.g., a button click). - The **switchboard / receptionist** is the **event source** (e.g., the `Button` component). The receptionist does *not* solve the problem. - The **specialist department** that picks up the call is the **listener** (e.g., an object implementing `ActionListener`). - The receptionist must have a **phone number** for the specialist — this is **registration** via a method like `addActionListener()`. > The receptionist (source) **does not know** how the specialist (listener) handles the call — it just routes the call. This decoupling is the *essence of delegation*. ## 1.3 The Four Core Components of DEM | # | Component | Technical Role | Plain-English Role | |---|-----------|---------------|--------------------| | 1 | **Event Source** | The GUI component that generates events | The *trigger* (e.g., a button) | | 2 | **Event Object** | Encapsulates information about the event | The *envelope* carrying event details | | 3 | **Event Listener** | The interface implemented by the handler class | The *recipient* of the event | | 4 | **Event Handler** | The actual method that processes the event | The *action* taken in response | > [!NOTE] > **Key Fact:** A single event source can be registered with **multiple listeners**, and a single listener can handle events from **multiple sources** — this is one of DEM's principal advantages over the older JDK 1.0 inheritance-based model. ## 1.4 Why DEM Replaced the JDK 1.0 Event Model In the **JDK 1.0 model**, a container had to *override* the methods like `action()`, `mouseDown()`, `keyDown()` to handle events. The drawbacks were: - Promoted **tight coupling** between UI and business logic. - Forced the programmer to subclass container classes. - Made code **hard to maintain** and **non-reusable**. The Delegation Event Model solves these issues by allowing any class (not just subclasses) to become an event handler simply by **implementing a listener interface**. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture Breakdown ### A. Event Source - Any object that can **register** listeners and **fire** events. - Examples: `Button`, `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `List`, `MenuItem`, `Scrollbar`, `TextField`, `Window`. - The source exposes an `addListener()` method for registration and a `removeListener()` method for de-registration. - When the user interacts, the source **invokes** the listener's method automatically — this is the *delegation*. ### B. Event Object - A subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (the root class in the AWT event hierarchy). - Carries data: source reference, ID, modifiers, coordinates, character typed, etc. - Examples: `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `WindowEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `TextEvent`, `AdjustmentEvent`, `ComponentEvent`, `ContainerEvent`, `FocusEvent`, `InputEvent`, `PaintEvent`, `MouseWheelEvent`. ### C. Event Listener Interface - Declared in `java.awt.event`. - A listener interface contains one or more abstract methods (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`). - The class that wants to handle events must **implement** the appropriate interface. ### D. Event Handler - The concrete method (e.g., `actionPerformed`) inside the listener class that contains the response logic. ## 2.2 Event Class Hierarchy (Java AWT) ``` java.util.EventObject (root — has getSource()) | +-- java.awt.AWTEvent | +-- ActionEvent +-- AdjustmentEvent +-- ItemEvent +-- TextEvent +-- ComponentEvent | +-- ContainerEvent +-- FocusEvent +-- InputEvent | +-- KeyEvent +-- MouseEvent | +-- MouseWheelEvent +-- PaintEvent +-- WindowEvent ``` > [!NOTE] > All event classes inherit the method `Object getSource()` from `EventObject`, allowing the listener to identify which component fired the event. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Listener Interfaces & Methods | Event Class | Listener Interface | Abstract Method(s) | Adapter Class | |-------------|-------------------|--------------------|---------------| | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | — | | `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged(AdjustmentEvent)` | — | | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | — | | `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent)` | — | | `ComponentEvent` | `ComponentListener` | `componentResized`, `componentMoved`, `componentShown`, `componentHidden` | `ComponentAdapter` | | `ContainerEvent` | `ContainerListener` | `componentAdded`, `componentRemoved` | `ContainerAdapter` | | `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` | `FocusAdapter` | | `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` | `KeyAdapter` | | `MouseEvent` (clicks) | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` | `MouseAdapter` | | `MouseEvent` (motion) | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` | `MouseMotionAdapter` | | `MouseWheelEvent` | `MouseWheelListener` | `mouseWheelMoved(MouseWheelEvent)` | — | | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified` | `WindowAdapter` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Table-Without-Pipes Rule (KTU Answer-Writing Tip):** When you reproduce this table in the exam, avoid drawing a vertical `|` inside cells. If you must write absolute value or set notation, use $\vert x \vert$ or $A \mid B$ in math mode — never bare `|x|`. This prevents the markdown table from breaking and keeps your answer sheet clean. ## 2.4 Adapter Classes — The Convenience Wrappers - An **adapter class** provides **empty (no-op) implementations** of all methods in a multi-method listener interface. - They allow you to override **only the methods you care about**, instead of writing stubs for every abstract method. - Located in the `java.awt.event` package. - Examples: `WindowAdapter`, `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `FocusAdapter`, `MouseMotionAdapter`, `ComponentAdapter`, `ContainerAdapter`. - Adapter classes are abstract in concept but are written as concrete classes with empty bodies. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Trap:** There is **no `ActionAdapter`** or `ItemAdapter` because the corresponding interfaces have only **one** abstract method each — there is nothing to "stub out". You can simply implement the interface directly. ## 2.5 Procedural Steps to Use DEM (Five Canonical Steps) 1. **Import** the required AWT packages: `java.awt.*` and `java.awt.event.*`. 2. **Implement** the appropriate listener interface in the handler class. 3. **Override** the relevant event-handling method(s). 4. **Create** the GUI component (event source) inside the constructor or `init()`. 5. **Register** the listener with the source using the `addListener()` method. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **GUI Development:** All Java desktop applications (Swing/JavaFX) and Android (which uses a similar model internally) rely on delegation. - **Embedded Systems:** Event-driven firmware uses the same source–listener decoupling. - **Network Programming:** Asynchronous callbacks (e.g., REST webhooks, MQTT) are direct descendants of the DEM philosophy. - **IoT & Robotics:** Sensor interrupts in robotics middleware (e.g., ROS) mirror the listener-registration pattern. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Process Flow & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The End-to-End Event Flow Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step logical flow** of the Delegation Event Model — from a user click to the final handler execution. This is the kind of answer that earns full marks in a 14-mark question. > **Step 1 — User Interaction:** The user performs a meaningful action (e.g., presses a mouse button while the cursor is over a `Button`). > > **Step 2 — AWT Event Queue Detection:** The AWT subsystem on the user's JVM detects the interaction and constructs an appropriate `EventObject` subclass (e.g., `ActionEvent` for a button activation). > > **Step 3 — Source Identification:** The newly created event object has its `source` field set to the GUI component that originated it. This is done via `EventObject.setSource()` (called by the constructor). > > **Step 4 — Source Forwards Event:** The source component maintains an internal list of registered listeners (one per event type). It iterates through this list and calls the relevant interface method on each listener, **passing the event object as an argument**. > > **Step 5 — Listener Method Invocation:** The listener's implemented method (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`) executes the response logic. > > **Step 6 — Handler Returns Control:** After the handler finishes, control returns to the AWT event-dispatch thread, which becomes ready to process the next event in the queue. > > **Step 7 — Optional Re-drawing:** If the handler changed the UI state, `repaint()` is invoked, which causes the AWT to call `update()` and then `paint()` to redraw the component. ## 3.2 Mathematical Formulation of Delegation Although DEM is a software model, the *flow* can be expressed symbolically for analytical clarity: $$ L = \{ l_1, l_2, l_3, \dots, l_n \} $$ where $L$ is the set of listeners registered with a given source $S$. When an event $E$ of type $T$ is generated by $S$: $$ \forall l_i \in L_T \;\;,\;\; l_i.\text{handleEvent}(E) $$ where $L_T$ is the subset of $L$ registered for event type $T$, and $L_T \subseteq L$. > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Every listener registered for a particular event type receives **exactly one** invocation per event. There is no broadcast to unrelated listeners — this is the **selective, type-safe nature of DEM**. ## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: DEM vs. JDK 1.0 Model | Evaluation Criterion | JDK 1.0 Inheritance Model | Delegation Event Model (DEM) | |----------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------| | Coupling | Tight (must subclass container) | Loose (any class can be a listener) | | Reusability | Poor (handler is buried in container class) | Excellent (handler is a separate class) | | Class Hierarchy Inflation | Yes — every container is a subclass | No — no need to extend container classes | | Multi-handler for one event | Not possible (single `action()` override) | Possible — many listeners per source | | Adapter Support | None | Full (e.g., `WindowAdapter`, `MouseAdapter`) | | Event Type Discrimination | Manual (ID-checking via `event.id`) | Automatic (compiler enforces via interface) | | KTU 2024 Recommended | ✗ (legacy) | ✓ (current standard) | ## 3.4 Detailed Java Implementation Walkthrough Below is a fully commented, line-by-line explanation of a button-click handler — exactly the type of program KTU expects in 7-mark sub-questions. ```java import java.awt.*; // Step 1a — for Frame, Button, Label, etc. import java.awt.event.*; // Step 1b — for ActionEvent, ActionListener // Step 2: The handler class implements the ActionListener interface public class ButtonDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener { Label status; Button b1, b2; ButtonDemo() { // Step 3: Set up the GUI components setLayout(new FlowLayout()); status = new Label("Click a button..."); b1 = new Button("Yes"); b2 = new Button("No"); // Step 4: Register the listener with both sources b1.addActionListener(this); b2.addActionListener(this); add(status); add(b1); add(b2); setSize(300, 150); setVisible(true); } // Step 5: Override the handler method public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { String cmd = e.getActionCommand(); if (cmd.equals("Yes")) { status.setText("You clicked YES"); } else if (cmd.equals("No")) { status.setText("You clicked NO"); } } public static void main(String[] args) { new ButtonDemo(); } } ``` ### Line-by-Line Logic - **Lines 1–2:** The AWT and event packages are imported. Without `java.awt.event.*`, the `ActionListener` interface and `ActionEvent` class are not visible. - **Line 5:** The class `ButtonDemo` extends `Frame` (a top-level window) and **implements** `ActionListener`. Implementing the interface is a contract to provide the `actionPerformed` method. - **Lines 9–10:** Two `Button` components are instantiated. Buttons are *event sources*. - **Lines 14–15:** `addActionListener(this)` is the **registration step**. The current object (`this`) is registered as a listener for both buttons. From this moment, AWT knows whom to call when either button is clicked. - **Lines 22–28:** The `actionPerformed` method receives the `ActionEvent e`. Calling `e.getActionCommand()` returns the button's label, allowing the handler to differentiate between the two buttons. - **Line 32:** `main` instantiates the GUI, triggering the entire event-handling infrastructure. ## 3.5 The Window-Closing Case — A Common Exam Topic A frequent KTU 14-mark question asks: *"Write a Java program to close a window when the close button is clicked."* The expected solution is: ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class CloseDemo extends Frame { CloseDemo() { setSize(300, 200); setVisible(true); // Register a WindowAdapter (an adapter class) addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { System.exit(0); // Terminates the JVM } }); } public static void main(String[] args) { new CloseDemo(); } } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why an Adapter is used here:** `WindowListener` has **seven** abstract methods. Implementing all seven just to close the window is wasteful — `WindowAdapter` supplies the other six as no-ops, so we override only `windowClosing`. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Delegation Event Model — High-Level Architecture The diagram below illustrates the four-actor interaction of DEM: the **User**, the **Event Source**, the **Event Object**, and the **Event Listener**. ```mermaid flowchart LR user[USER] -->|interacts with| source[EVENT SOURCE
Button TextField MenuItem] source -->|creates| eventobj[EVENT OBJECT
ActionEvent MouseEvent KeyEvent] source -.->|1 register| listener[EVENT LISTENER
implements ActionListener etc] eventobj -->|2 passed as argument| listener listener -->|3 invokes| handler[EVENT HANDLER
actionPerformed etc] handler -->|4 response| ui[GUI UPDATE] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Dotted arrows indicate *registration* (one-time setup). Solid arrows indicate the *runtime event flow* (one-shot per event). ## 4.2 Event Class Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD eventobj[java util EventObject] awtevent[java awt AWTEvent] eventobj --> awtevent awtevent --> action[ActionEvent] awtevent --> adjust[AdjustmentEvent] awtevent --> item[ItemEvent] awtevent --> text[TextEvent] awtevent --> comp[ComponentEvent] comp --> cont[ContainerEvent] comp --> focus[FocusEvent] comp --> input[InputEvent] input --> key[KeyEvent] input --> mouse[MouseEvent] mouse --> wheel[MouseWheelEvent] awtevent --> paint[PaintEvent] awtevent --> window[WindowEvent] ``` ## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — From Click to Handler Execution ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Event Source participant AWT as AWT Subsystem participant L as Event Listener participant H as Event Handler U->>S: 1 Clicks button S->>AWT: 2 Detects interaction AWT->>AWT: 3 Creates ActionEvent object AWT->>S: 4 Forwards event S->>L: 5 Invokes actionPerformed L->>H: 6 Executes handler logic H-->>U: 7 Updated UI visible ``` ## 4.4 Listener-to-Source Registration Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR btn[Button] -->|addActionListener| alistener[ActionListener] txt[TextField] -->|addActionListener| alistener txt2[TextField] -->|addTextListener| tlistener[TextListener] win[Window] -->|addWindowListener| wlistener[WindowListener] mouse[Component] -->|addMouseListener| mlistener[MouseListener] mouse2[Component] -->|addKeyListener| klistener[KeyListener] ckbox[Checkbox] -->|addItemListener| ilistener[ItemListener] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow represents the registration call. Note that **TextField** participates in *two* registrations (text changes and Enter-key action events) — a common point of confusion in exams. ## 4.5 DEM vs JDK 1.0 — Visual Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph JDK1[JDK 1.0 Inheritance Model] j1[MyFrame] --> j2[overrides action] j2 --> j3[overrides mouseDown] j2 --> j4[overrides keyDown] end subgraph DEM[Delegation Event Model] d1[MyFrame] --> d2[Button] d2 -->|addActionListener| d3[HandlerA] d2 -->|addMouseListener| d4[HandlerB] d2 -->|addKeyListener| d5[HandlerC] end ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** > > **Q1.** *What is the Delegation Event Model? List its key components.* > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Delegation Event Model is an event-handling approach in Java AWT where the responsibility of handling an event generated by a source is *delegated* to a separate listener object. The four key components are: > 1. **Event Source** — the GUI component that generates the event (e.g., Button). > 2. **Event Object** — encapsulates event data; a subclass of `java.util.EventObject`. > 3. **Event Listener** — an interface implemented by the handler class. > 4. **Event Handler** — the actual method that responds to the event. > > *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four components.)* > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** > > **Q2.** *Differentiate between an Event Listener interface and an Adapter class in Java AWT.* > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > | Aspect | Listener Interface | Adapter Class | > |--------|--------------------|---------------| > | Nature | Pure abstract interface with all methods abstract | Concrete class with all methods implemented as empty | > | Usage | Must implement *all* methods | Override *only* the methods you need | > | Example | `WindowListener` (7 methods) | `WindowAdapter` (provides 6 no-ops) | > | Existence of `ActionAdapter`? | N/A | None — single-method interfaces do not need adapters | > > *(Award 1 mark per row of valid comparison, 3 rows for 3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > **INSTRUCTIONS TO STUDENT:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split across two sub-parts (7 + 7). --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(ETwo full choices provided — pick one)* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** > **A(a).** Explain the Delegation Event Model in Java with a neat diagram. List the major event classes and listener interfaces. *(7 marks)* > > **A(b).** Write a Java AWT program to create a `Frame` containing two buttons labelled "Red" and "Blue". Clicking a button should change the background colour of the `Frame` accordingly. Use the Delegation Event Model. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — A(a) (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** The Delegation Event Model is the AWT mechanism in which an event generated by a *source* component is delegated to a *listener* object that implements a specific event-listener interface. **Architecture (2 marks):** A neat diagram showing User → Event Source → Event Object → Event Listener → Event Handler, with registration arrows from source to listener. **Event Classes (2 marks):** `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `WindowEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `TextEvent`, `AdjustmentEvent`, `FocusEvent`, `ComponentEvent`, `ContainerEvent`. **Listener Interfaces (2 marks):** `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`, `ItemListener`, `TextListener`, `AdjustmentListener`, `FocusListener`, `ComponentListener`, `ContainerListener` — each matching its respective event. #### Model Solution — A(b) (7 marks) ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class ColorChangeDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener { Button bRed, bBlue; ColorChangeDemo() { setLayout(new FlowLayout()); bRed = new Button("Red"); bBlue = new Button("Blue"); bRed.addActionListener(this); // registration bBlue.addActionListener(this); add(bRed); add(bBlue); setSize(300, 200); setVisible(true); } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { String c = e.getActionCommand(); if (c.equals("Red")) setBackground(Color.RED); else if (c.equals("Blue")) setBackground(Color.BLUE); } public static void main(String[] args) { new ColorChangeDemo(); } } ``` **Mark Distribution:** - 1 mark — correct package imports. - 2 marks — proper class declaration implementing `ActionListener`. - 2 marks — registration via `addActionListener`. - 2 marks — correct override of `actionPerformed` with colour logic. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative choice)* **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]** > **B(a).** Explain the role of **Adapter classes** in the Delegation Event Model. List any four adapter classes with their corresponding listener interfaces. *(7 marks)* > > **B(b).** Write a Java AWT program to display a `Frame` that closes when the user clicks the window's close button. Use a `WindowAdapter` class. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — B(a) (7 marks) **Role of Adapter Classes (3 marks):** Adapter classes provide default (empty) implementations of *all* methods in a multi-method listener interface. They eliminate the need to write empty stubs for unused methods, making the code cleaner and more focused. They follow the **Null Object design pattern** at a method level. **Four Adapter Classes (4 marks — 1 each):** | Adapter Class | Corresponding Listener Interface | Listener Methods (Count) | |---------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------| | `WindowAdapter` | `WindowListener` | 7 | | `MouseAdapter` | `MouseListener` | 5 | | `KeyAdapter` | `KeyListener` | 3 | | `MouseMotionAdapter` | `MouseMotionListener` | 2 | | `FocusAdapter` | `FocusListener` | 2 | | `ComponentAdapter` | `ComponentListener` | 4 | | `ContainerAdapter` | `ContainerListener` | 2 | #### Model Solution — B(b) (7 marks) ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class CloseWindowDemo extends Frame { CloseWindowDemo() { setSize(400, 300); setVisible(true); addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { System.exit(0); } }); } public static void main(String[] args) { new CloseWindowDemo(); } } ``` **Mark Distribution:** - 1 mark — correct imports of `java.awt.*` and `java.awt.event.*`. - 2 marks — anonymous inner class extending `WindowAdapter`. - 2 marks — proper override of `windowClosing` with `System.exit(0)`. - 2 marks — correct registration via `addWindowListener`. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Confusing `actionPerformed` parameter name:** Use `ActionEvent e` consistently; some students write `ActionEvent ae` but the convention expected is `e`. > 2. **Forgetting to register the listener:** Writing `addActionListener(this)` is worth 2 marks alone. Many students create the button but forget this critical line — and the program does nothing. > 3. **Writing `action` instead of `actionPerformed`:** This is the **JDK 1.0** style. In DEM, the method **must** be named `actionPerformed` — case and spelling matter. > 4. **Using `System.exit(0)` in the wrong context:** Acceptable in `windowClosing`. In `windowClosed`, it causes an infinite recursion. > 5. **Trying to import `ActionAdapter`:** No such class exists — students confuse themselves between `ActionListener` and an adapter. The correct rule: **adapters exist only for interfaces with 2+ methods**. > 6. **Single event-source single listener misconception:** DEM permits *many* listeners per source and *many* sources per listener. Examiners often test this in 2-mark sub-questions. > 7. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`:** The frame won't appear — and the student panics thinking their code is wrong. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > **Important Things to Remember (Module 4 — Delegation Event Model)** - **DEM = Source + Listener + Event Object + Handler** (four pillars). - **Package locations:** Components in `java.awt`, events and listeners in `java.awt.event`. - **Root event class:** `java.util.EventObject` — provides `getSource()`. - **AWT-event root:** `java.awt.AWTEvent`. - **Registration call pattern:** `source.addListener(listenerObject)`. - **De-registration call pattern:** `source.removeListener(listenerObject)`. - **Single-method interfaces** (no adapter): `ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, `TextListener`, `AdjustmentListener`, `MouseWheelListener`. - **Multi-method interfaces** (have adapters): `WindowListener` (7), `MouseListener` (5), `KeyListener` (3), `ComponentListener` (4), `FocusListener` (2), `MouseMotionListener` (2), `ContainerListener` (2). - **Key methods to remember by heart:** - `ActionListener` → `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` - `MouseListener` → `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` - `KeyListener` → `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` - `WindowListener` → `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified` - **Closable window pattern:** Use `addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { System.exit(0); } })`. - **Advantages over JDK 1.0:** Loose coupling, reusability, multiple listeners per source, type-safety, no class-hierarchy inflation. - **Observer pattern mapping:** Source = Subject; Listener = Observer; Event = Notification message. - **Exam mantra:** *Register first, override second, call `setVisible(true)` last.* > [!NOTE] > **Final Study Tip:** In the KTU 2024 exam, the 14-mark question is almost always a *combination* — one sub-part tests theory (definitions, list, or comparison) and the other tests a *practical Java program*. Memorize one program template per major event type (Action, Window, Mouse, Key) and you can adapt to any 14-mark question asked.

topics.title: Event Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Event Class** in Java is a concrete subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (or its descendant `java.awt.AWTEvent`) that encapsulates the *semantic information* and *source context* of a user or system action occurring on a Graphical User Interface (GUI) component. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Object Oriented Concepts, event classes are the *payload carriers* of the **Delegation Event Model (DEM)** — they are instantiated by the JVM/AWT event dispatch thread and passed to registered listener objects via the `EventObject` superclass's `getSource()` and `getID()` mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 Focus:**
> Event classes reside primarily in the package `java.awt.event` (and `javax.swing.event` for Swing extensions). They form the **M in MVC (Model-View-Controller)** when listeners consume them, and the **Event Object** role in the canonical **GoF Observer Pattern**, which is the architectural foundation of the Delegation Event Model.

The canonical class signature at the root is:

```java
public class EventObject implements java.io.Serializable {
    protected Object source;
    public EventObject(Object source) { this.source = source; }
    public Object getSource() { return source; }
    public int getID() { return 0; } // overridden in AWTEvent
    public String toString() { ... }
}
```

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are in a **post office (the JVM)**. A **customer (the user)** drops a **letter (the event)** into the **mailbox (the GUI component like a button)**. The post office needs to know:

1. **What happened?** — Did the customer click? Press a key? Resize the window?
2. **Where did it happen?** — Which exact button/window/checkbox?
3. **Extra details** — Which key? Which mouse coordinates? What modifier keys (Shift/Ctrl) were held?

An **Event Class is the standardized envelope containing all this information**. Just as a registered postal letter has a tracking number, sender address, and content — an `EventObject` has a `source`, an `ID`, and class-specific fields (like `MouseEvent`'s `(x, y)` coordinates or `KeyEvent`'s `keyCode`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> A `JButton` doesn't *decide* what to do when clicked. It simply *creates and broadcasts* an `ActionEvent`. Any object that has registered interest (a `ActionListener`) receives this event-object and decides what action to take. The event class is the **language** in which the component speaks to its listeners.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants

- **Total core AWT event classes (KTU-mandated count):** **11** direct subclasses of `AWTEvent`.
- **Root superclass:** `java.util.EventObject`.
- **Standard event IDs:** Integer constants (e.g., `ACTION_PERFORMED = 1001`, `MOUSE_PRESSED = 501`, `KEY_PRESSED = 401`).
- **Source object type:** Always `Object` (polymorphic — could be any `Component`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Event Class Hierarchy — The "Why" Behind It

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes a **structured understanding of inheritance-based event categorization**. The hierarchy is not arbitrary — it reflects *semantic grouping* of user interactions:

- **Component-level events** (resize, move, show, hide) → `ComponentEvent`
- **Input device events** (mouse, keyboard) → `InputEvent` (abstract intermediate class)
- **Semantic action events** (button click, menu selection) → `ActionEvent`
- **State-change events** (checkbox toggle, list selection) → `ItemEvent`
- **Container structural events** (component added/removed) → `ContainerEvent`
- **Window lifecycle events** (open, close, iconify) → `WindowEvent`
- **Focus events** (gained/lost keyboard focus) → `FocusEvent`
- **Text content events** (text changed) → `TextEvent`
- **Scrollbar events** → `AdjustmentEvent`
- **Paint/Repaint events** → `PaintEvent`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `InputEvent` is abstract:**
> `InputEvent` exists to share common fields (`modifiers`, `when` timestamp) between `KeyEvent` and `MouseEvent`. This is a textbook example of **code reuse through inheritance** — a core OO principle tested in KTU exams.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — How an Event Class Functions

1. **Event Generation (by Source Component):**
   The GUI component (e.g., `JButton`) detects an internal state change (mouse release inside bounds). It calls `EventQueue.dispatchEvent()` with a *freshly constructed* event object.

2. **Event ID Assignment:**
   Each event class has a constructor that accepts a `source` and an `id` (an integer from a defined constant set, e.g., `ActionEvent.ACTION_PERFORMED`).

3. **Event Queuing:**
   The AWT creates a dedicated **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** that dequeues events one at a time, ensuring **thread-safety** of GUI operations.

4. **Event Dispatching (Listener Notification):**
   For each registered listener, the toolkit invokes the appropriate callback method (e.g., `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`) and **passes the event object as the parameter**.

5. **Listener Consumption & Disposal:**
   The listener reads `e.getSource()`, `e.getID()`, and class-specific accessors. The event object becomes eligible for **garbage collection** after the call returns.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Event Class Reference Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Memorize for ESE**

| Event Class | Package | Trigger Condition | Key Accessor Methods (besides getSource, getID) | Constructors (Most Used) |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|-----------------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| `ActionEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Button click, menu item select, Enter in text field | `getActionCommand()`, `getModifiers()` | `ActionEvent(src, id, cmd)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Mouse press, release, click, enter, exit, move, drag | `getX()`, `getY()`, `getPoint()`, `getButton()`, `getClickCount()`, `isPopupTrigger()` | `MouseEvent(src, id, when, mods, x, y, clicks, pop, button)` |
| `KeyEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Key pressed, released, typed (character) | `getKeyChar()`, `getKeyCode()`, `getKeyLocation()`, `isActionKey()` | `KeyEvent(src, id, when, mods, keyCode, keyChar, loc)` |
| `WindowEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Window opened, closing, closed, iconified, deactivated, activated | `getWindow()`, `getOppositeWindow()` | `WindowEvent(src, id, opposite, oldState, newState)` |
| `ItemEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Checkbox toggle, choice/list selection | `getItem()`, `getItemSelectable()`, `getStateChange()` | `ItemEvent(src, id, item, stateChange)` |
| `TextEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Text value changed | *(inherits only getSource)* | `TextEvent(src, id)` |
| `AdjustmentEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Scrollbar value changed | `getAdjustable()`, `getAdjustmentType()`, `getValue()` | `AdjustmentEvent(src, id, type, value)` |
| `ComponentEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Component moved, resized, shown, hidden | `getComponent()` | `ComponentEvent(src, id)` |
| `FocusEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Focus gained or lost | `isTemporary()`, `getOppositeComponent()` | `FocusEvent(src, id, temporary, opposite)` |
| `ContainerEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Component added to/removed from container | `getChild()`, `getContainer()` | `ContainerEvent(src, id, child)` |
| `PaintEvent` | `java.awt.event` | Component needs repainting | `getUpdateRect()` | `PaintEvent(src, id, updateRect)` |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Event classes are the **backbone of every interactive Java application** — from IDEs (IntelliJ, Eclipse) to enterprise dashboards (Bloomberg Terminal) to mobile (Android's `MotionEvent`, `KeyEvent` are direct conceptual descendants). The pattern they enable — **decoupling event producers from event consumers** — is the same pattern used in:

- **Node.js `EventEmitter`** (JavaScript)
- **C# delegates and `EventArgs`**
- **Kafka message brokers** (event objects as payloads)
- **IoT sensor data pipelines** (sensor readings as event objects)

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:**
> Mastery of the Java event class hierarchy is a prerequisite for Swing/JavaFX GUI development, Android mobile development, and reactive programming (RxJava). It is a foundational skill tested not just in KTU exams but in campus placements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive Complexity Note:**
> Event Classes is a *conceptual and architectural* topic — not a numerical derivation. Hence, "derivations" here are the **logical deduction of how class hierarchies, method contracts, and event flow are constructed** from first principles of OO design.

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: From User Action to Event Object

Let us **derive the complete event-handling chain** from first principles, in exhaustive step-by-step detail.

**Step 1 — The User's Physical Action:**
The user presses the left mouse button while the cursor is over a `JButton` instance `b1` located at screen coordinates `(150, 200)`.

**Step 2 — The AWT Internal Detection (OS-Level Hook):**
The underlying Operating System dispatches a native mouse-press message to the Java application's window. The AWT native peer (a platform-specific implementation object) receives this.

**Step 3 — Construction of the Event Object:**
The peer calls the `JButton`'s internal `processMouseEvent` method, which constructs a `MouseEvent` object. The construction is logically:

$$
\text{event} = \text{MouseEvent}(b_1, \text{id}, t_{\text{now}}, \text{mods}, x, y, \text{clicks}, \text{pop}, \text{button})
$$

where each parameter is filled as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{source} \;&=\; b_1 \quad \text{(the originating component)} \\
\text{id} \;&=\; \text{MouseEvent.MOUSE\_PRESSED} \;=\; 501 \\
t_{\text{now}} \;&=\; \text{System.currentTimeMillis()} \quad \text{(timestamp)} \\
\text{mods} \;&=\; \text{InputEvent.SHIFT\_DOWN\_MASK} \mid \text{InputEvent.CTRL\_DOWN\_MASK} \quad \text{(bitwise OR of held keys)} \\
x \;&=\; 150 \quad \text{(local x-coordinate relative to } b_1) \\
y \;&=\; 200 \quad \text{(local y-coordinate relative to } b_1) \\
\text{clicks} \;&=\; 1 \quad \text{(single click count)} \\
\text{pop} \;&=\; \text{false} \quad \text{(not a popup trigger on this OS)} \\
\text{button} \;&=\; \text{MouseEvent.BUTTON1} \;=\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:**
- The `id` is an integer constant because Java events use a compact integer encoding to minimize memory in the event queue (a performance optimization since 1996).
- The `mods` field is a `bitmask` — each modifier key occupies one bit, so `OR`-ing produces a single integer representing all held modifiers. This is the **standard bitwise encoding pattern** for KTU-level questions on `InputEvent`.
- The `(x, y)` are *translated* by the peer from screen coordinates to coordinates relative to the source component's origin.

**Step 4 — Event Queue Insertion:**
The constructed `MouseEvent` is placed onto the **system event queue**:

$$
\text{EventQueue} \leftarrow \text{enqueue}(\text{event})
$$

**Step 5 — Event Dispatch Thread (EDT) Picks Up Event:**
The EDT (a dedicated `Thread` named `"AWT-EventQueue-0"`) loops:

$$
\text{while} \; (\text{true}) \; \{ \; \text{event} = \text{EventQueue.nextEvent()}; \; \text{event.dispatch();} \; \}
$$

**Step 6 — `dispatch()` Method Invocation:**
Each event class overrides `AWTEvent.dispatch()`:

$$
\text{MouseEvent.dispatch()} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Component.dispatchEventImpl(event)}
$$

Inside `Component.dispatchEventImpl`, the toolkit checks the **listener registry** for `MouseListener` and `MouseMotionListener` instances.

**Step 7 — Listener Notification:**
For each registered `MouseListener ml_i`, the appropriate callback is invoked:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{if} \; (\text{id} == \text{MOUSE\_PRESSED}) &\;\rightarrow\; \text{ml}_i.\text{mousePressed}(\text{event}) \\
\text{if} \; (\text{id} == \text{MOUSE\_RELEASED}) &\;\rightarrow\; \text{ml}_i.\text{mouseReleased}(\text{event}) \\
\text{if} \; (\text{id} == \text{MOUSE\_CLICKED}) &\;\rightarrow\; \text{ml}_i.\text{mouseClicked}(\text{event}) \\
\text{if} \; (\text{id} == \text{MOUSE\_ENTERED}) &\;\rightarrow\; \text{ml}_i.\text{mouseEntered}(\text{event}) \\
\text{if} \; (\text{id} == \text{MOUSE\_EXITED}) &\;\rightarrow\; \text{ml}_i.\text{mouseExited}(\text{event})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Listener Reads Event Data (Example Logic):**
The listener's `mousePressed(MouseEvent e)` method executes, e.g.:

```java
public void mousePressed(MouseEvent e) {
    if (e.getButton() == MouseEvent.BUTTON1 && e.getClickCount() == 2) {
        System.out.println("Double-click at: " + e.getPoint());
    }
}
$$

The `e.getPoint()` method internally returns:

$$
\text{Point } p = \text{new Point}(e.x, e.y)
$$

**Step 9 — Event Object Lifecycle Ends:**
After all listeners are notified, the `MouseEvent` reference is dropped, and the **garbage collector** reclaims its memory.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Abstract `InputEvent` Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does `InputEvent` exist as an abstract class?** This is a frequently asked KTU question.

**Logical Deduction:**
- Both `KeyEvent` and `MouseEvent` share two common data fields: a **timestamp** (`when`) and **modifier keys held** (`modifiers`).
- Without a common parent, these fields would be duplicated in both subclasses, violating the **DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)** principle.
- Java's designers created `InputEvent` as an abstract intermediate node to centralize this shared state.

$$
\text{EventObject} \;\rightarrow\; \text{AWTEvent} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{InputEvent}}_{\text{abstract}} \;\rightarrow\; 
\begin{cases}
\text{KeyEvent} \\
\text{MouseEvent} \rightarrow \text{MouseWheelEvent}
\end{cases}
$$

The `modifiers` field uses **bitwise encoding** for performance:

$$
\text{modifiers} = \bigvee_{k \in \text{pressed keys}} \; 2^{b(k)}
$$

where $b(k)$ is the bit position assigned to modifier key $k$ (e.g., `SHIFT_DOWN_MASK = 1 << 6 = 64`).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Event ID Constants (Exam Critical)

| Event Class | ID Constant | Integer Value | Listener Method Called |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|------------------------|
| `ActionEvent` | `ACTION_PERFORMED` | **1001** | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_PRESSED` | **501** | `mousePressed(MouseEvent)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_RELEASED` | **502** | `mouseReleased(MouseEvent)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_CLICKED` | **500** | `mouseClicked(MouseEvent)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_ENTERED` | **504** | `mouseEntered(MouseEvent)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_EXITED` | **505** | `mouseExited(MouseEvent)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_MOVED` | **503** | `mouseMoved(MouseEvent)` |
| `MouseEvent` | `MOUSE_DRAGGED` | **506** | `mouseDragged(MouseEvent)` |
| `KeyEvent` | `KEY_PRESSED` | **401** | `keyPressed(KeyEvent)` |
| `KeyEvent` | `KEY_RELEASED` | **402** | `keyReleased(KeyEvent)` |
| `KeyEvent` | `KEY_TYPED` | **400** | `keyTyped(KeyEvent)` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WINDOW_OPENED` | **200** | `windowOpened(WindowEvent)` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WINDOW_CLOSING` | **201** | `windowClosing(WindowEvent)` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WINDOW_CLOSED` | **202** | `windowClosed(WindowEvent)` |
| `WindowEvent` | `WINDOW_ICONIFIED` | **203** | `windowIconified(WindowEvent)` |
| `ItemEvent` | `ITEM_STATE_CHANGED` | **701** | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Event Class Hierarchy (Full Inheritance Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    EO["java util EventObject ROOT CLASS"]
    AWTE["java awt AWTEvent abstract"]

    MWE["MouseWheelEvent"]

    EO --> AWTE
    AWTE --> ACT
    AWTE --> ITM
    AWTE --> TXT
    AWTE --> ADJ
    AWTE --> PAINT

    CE --> WE
    CE --> CNTE
    IE --> KE
    IE --> ME
    ME --> MWE

    style EO fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

**Legend:**
- 🟡 **Gold** = Root superclass
- 🔴 **Red** = Abstract intermediate classes
- 🔵 **Blue** = Abstract intermediate (Component-level grouping)
- 🟢 **Green** = Concrete event classes you instantiate

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Event Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    USER["USER ACTION Click Key Press Window Resize"]
    OS["Operating System Native Event"]
    PEER["AWT Peer Native Component"]
    GEN["Event Class Constructor MouseEvent KeyEvent ActionEvent etc"]

    EDT["Event Dispatch Thread EDT"]
    DISP["dispatch Method on Event Object"]
    REG["Component Listener Registry"]
    L1["Listener 1 Object"]
    L2["Listener 2 Object"]
    L3["Listener N Object"]
    ACTN["Listener Callback Method e g actionPerformed"]
    GC["Garbage Collector Reclaims Event Object"]

    USER --> OS
    OS --> PEER
    PEER --> GEN
    GEN --> Q
    Q --> EDT
    EDT --> DISP
    DISP --> REG
    REG --> L1
    REG --> L2
    REG --> L3
    L1 --> ACTN
    L2 --> ACTN
    L3 --> ACTN
    ACTN --> GC

    style USER fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OS fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PEER fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style Q fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style EDT fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style DISP fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style REG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style GC fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Listener-to-EventClass Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph EVENT_CLASSES ["EVENT CLASSES"]
        AE1["ActionEvent"]
        ME1["MouseEvent"]
        KE1["KeyEvent"]
        WE1["WindowEvent"]
        IE1["ItemEvent"]
        TE1["TextEvent"]
        ADE1["AdjustmentEvent"]
    end

    subgraph LISTENERS ["LISTENER INTERFACES"]
        AL["ActionListener"]
        ML["MouseListener"]
        MML["MouseMotionListener"]
        MWhL["MouseWheelListener"]
        KL["KeyListener"]
        WL["WindowListener"]
        IL["ItemListener"]
        TL["TextListener"]
        ADL["AdjustmentListener"]
    end

    AE1 --> AL
    ME1 --> ML
    ME1 --> MML
    ME1 --> MWhL
    KE1 --> KL
    WE1 --> WL
    IE1 --> IL
    TE1 --> TL
    ADE1 --> ADL

    style EVENT_CLASSES fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style LISTENERS fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> Each event class is *consumed* by exactly one (or more) listener interface. For example, a single `MouseEvent` can trigger callbacks in `MouseListener` (press/release/click/enter/exit) AND `MouseMotionListener` (moved/dragged) — a single event, multiple listener categories.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define an Event Class. List any four common event classes in Java AWT and state the condition that triggers each.**

**Model Answer:**

An **event class** is a subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (or `java.awt.AWTEvent`) that encapsulates information about a user or system action occurring on a GUI component.

Four common event classes and their trigger conditions:

| Event Class | Trigger Condition |
|-------------|-------------------|
| `ActionEvent` | Clicking a button, selecting a menu item, pressing Enter in a text field |
| `MouseEvent` | Any mouse interaction — press, release, click, enter, exit, move, or drag |
| `KeyEvent` | Pressing, releasing, or typing a character on the keyboard |
| `WindowEvent` | Opening, closing, iconifying, deactivating, or activating a window |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for the table)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of `getSource()` and `getID()` methods inherited from `EventObject` and `AWTEvent` respectively.**

**Model Answer:**

- **`getSource()`** — Inherited from `java.util.EventObject`. Returns a reference to the `Object` (typically the GUI component) that originally generated the event. The listener uses this to identify *which* component fired the event, especially when a single listener handles multiple components.

- **`getID()`** — Inherited from `java.awt.AWTEvent`. Returns an integer constant identifying the *type* of the event (e.g., `MOUSE_PRESSED = 501`, `ACTION_PERFORMED = 1001`). The listener uses this to determine *what kind* of sub-event occurred, allowing a single callback method to handle multiple event types polymorphically.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for each method explanation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the complete event class hierarchy starting from `java.util.EventObject` to all concrete event classes in `java.awt.event`. Explain the purpose of the abstract classes `AWTEvent` and `InputEvent` in this hierarchy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable Java code example, demonstrate how an `ActionEvent` is generated, dispatched, and handled using the Delegation Event Model. Identify and explain each component of the event flow. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

The complete hierarchy (as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes) is:

```
java.util.EventObject
        │
        └── java.awt.AWTEvent (abstract)
                ├── ActionEvent
                ├── ItemEvent
                ├── TextEvent
                ├── AdjustmentEvent
                ├── PaintEvent
                ├── ComponentEvent
                │       ├── FocusEvent
                │       ├── WindowEvent
                │       └── ContainerEvent
                └── InputEvent (abstract)
                        ├── KeyEvent
                        └── MouseEvent
                                └── MouseWheelEvent
```

**Purpose of `AWTEvent` (abstract class):**
- Acts as the **common root** for all AWT-related events.
- Introduces the `id` field and `getID()` method, providing a **standard mechanism to identify event subtypes** (using integer constants like `ACTION_PERFORMED`).
- Implements the `dispatch()` method template that all event classes inherit.
- Provides protected `consumed` field for event consumption control.

**Purpose of `InputEvent` (abstract class):**
- Provides a **common parent for input device events** (`KeyEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `MouseWheelEvent`).
- Centralizes **shared fields**: `when` (long timestamp) and `modifiers` (int bitmask of held modifier keys like Shift, Ctrl, Alt).
- Avoids code duplication (DRY principle) — without it, `KeyEvent` and `MouseEvent` would each redundantly store these fields.
- Defines common constants: `SHIFT_MASK`, `CTRL_MASK`, `ALT_MASK`, `BUTTON1_MASK`, `BUTTON2_MASK`, `BUTTON3_MASK`, and the modern `SHIFT_DOWN_MASK`, `CTRL_DOWN_MASK`, etc.

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram + 2 for AWTEvent + 2 for InputEvent)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class EventDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    Label resultLabel;
    Button clickButton;
    TextField nameField;

    public EventDemo() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        nameField = new TextField(15);
        clickButton = new Button("Click Me");
        resultLabel = new Label("Result will appear here");

        add(nameField);
        add(clickButton);
        add(resultLabel);

        // Registration of listener — Delegation
        clickButton.addActionListener(this);
        nameField.addActionListener(this);

        setSize(300, 200);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        // Step 1: Identify source using getSource()
        Object src = e.getSource();

        // Step 2: Identify action command
        String cmd = e.getActionCommand();

        if (src == clickButton) {
            resultLabel.setText("Button clicked! Command: " + cmd);
        } else if (src == nameField) {
            resultLabel.setText("Enter pressed in text field. Input: " + nameField.getText());
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new EventDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Event Flow Components:**

1. **Event Source** — `clickButton` (the `Button` object) and `nameField` (the `TextField` object). These are the components that *generate* events.

2. **Event Class** — `ActionEvent` — instantiated by the AWT peer when the button is clicked or Enter is pressed in the text field.

3. **Event Listener Interface** — `ActionListener` — declares the `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` method contract.

4. **Event Listener (Concrete)** — `EventDemo` class itself, which `implements ActionListener` and provides the `actionPerformed` implementation.

5. **Registration** — `clickButton.addActionListener(this)` — binds the listener to the source. This is the *delegation* step.

6. **Event Dispatch** — When clicked, AWT constructs an `ActionEvent(source=clickButton, id=ACTION_PERFORMED, command="Click Me")` and dispatches it to the EDT.

7. **Event Handling** — `actionPerformed` is invoked, and `getSource()` distinguishes between the two registered sources.

*(7 marks — 3 for code + 4 for component explanation with proper event flow)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the `MouseEvent` class in detail. List all event IDs associated with it and the corresponding `MouseListener` and `MouseMotionListener` methods. Write a Java program to detect a double-click on a button and print the coordinates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent`, and `WindowEvent` with respect to their trigger conditions, listener interfaces, and key accessor methods. Give one real-world scenario for each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

`MouseEvent` is a concrete subclass of `InputEvent` that represents all mouse-related interactions: press, release, click, enter, exit, move, and drag.

**Event IDs and Corresponding Listener Methods:**

| Event ID Constant | Integer Value | Listener Interface | Method Called |
|-------------------|---------------|-------------------|---------------|
| `MOUSE_CLICKED` | **500** | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked(MouseEvent)` |
| `MOUSE_PRESSED` | **501** | `MouseListener` | `mousePressed(MouseEvent)` |
| `MOUSE_RELEASED` | **502** | `MouseListener` | `mouseReleased(MouseEvent)` |
| `MOUSE_MOVED` | **503** | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseMoved(MouseEvent)` |
| `MOUSE_ENTERED` | **504** | `MouseListener` | `mouseEntered(MouseEvent)` |
| `MOUSE_EXITED` | **505** | `MouseListener` | `mouseExited(MouseEvent)` |
| `MOUSE_DRAGGED` | **506** | `MouseMotionListener` | `mouseDragged(MouseEvent)` |
| `MOUSE_WHEEL` | **507** | `MouseWheelListener` | `mouseWheelMoved(MouseWheelEvent)` |

**Key Accessor Methods of `MouseEvent`:**
- `getX()` — returns x-coordinate
- `getY()` — returns y-coordinate
- `getPoint()` — returns `Point(x, y)`
- `getButton()` — returns `BUTTON1`, `BUTTON2`, or `BUTTON3`
- `getClickCount()` — distinguishes single vs. double clicks
- `getModifiers()` — returns held modifier keys as bitmask
- `isPopupTrigger()` — checks if event should trigger a popup menu
- `isAltDown()`, `isShiftDown()`, `isControlDown()` — convenience methods

**Java Program — Double-Click Detection:**

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class DoubleClickDemo extends Frame implements MouseListener {
    Label info;

    public DoubleClickDemo() {
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        Button btn = new Button("Double Click Here");
        info = new Label("Awaiting input...");
        add(btn);
        add(info);
        btn.addMouseListener(this);
        setSize(350, 150);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
        if (e.getClickCount() == 2 && e.getButton() == MouseEvent.BUTTON1) {
            info.setText("Double-click detected at (" + e.getX() + ", " + e.getY() + ")");
        }
    }

    // Other required methods (empty implementations)
    public void mousePressed(MouseEvent e) {}
    public void mouseReleased(MouseEvent e) {}
    public void mouseEntered(MouseEvent e) {}
    public void mouseExited(MouseEvent e) {}

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new DoubleClickDemo();
    }
}
```

*(7 marks — 2 for IDs table + 2 for accessors + 3 for code)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

| Feature | `ActionEvent` | `ItemEvent` | `WindowEvent` |
|---------|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Trigger Condition** | Button click, menu item selection, Enter in text field, list double-click | Checkbox toggle, choice selection, list item selection | Window opened, closing, closed, iconified, activated, deactivated |
| **Listener Interface** | `ActionListener` | `ItemListener` | `WindowListener` |
| **Listener Method** | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | `windowOpened(WindowEvent)`, `windowClosing(WindowEvent)`, etc. (7 methods) |
| **Key Accessor Method** | `getActionCommand()`, `getModifiers()` | `getItem()`, `getStateChange()`, `getItemSelectable()` | `getWindow()`, `getOppositeWindow()`, `getNewState()`, `getOldState()` |
| **ID Constant Value** | `ACTION_PERFORMED = 1001` | `ITEM_STATE_CHANGED = 701` | `WINDOW_OPENED = 200`, `WINDOW_CLOSING = 201`, etc. |
| **Real-World Scenario** | "Submit" button on a login form | Toggling a "Remember Me" checkbox on login | Clicking the close (X) button of an application window, prompting "Save before exit?" dialog |

**Real-World Scenarios Expanded:**

- **ActionEvent:** When a user clicks the "Place Order" button in an e-commerce checkout page, an `ActionEvent` is fired. The handler invokes the order-placement service.

- **ItemEvent:** When a user toggles a "Dark Mode" checkbox in a settings panel, an `ItemEvent` is fired. The handler reads `getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED` and switches the UI theme.

- **WindowEvent:** When a user clicks the close (X) button of a document editor, a `WINDOW_CLOSING` event is fired. The handler displays a "Save changes?" confirmation dialog and either saves or discards the document before exiting.

*(7 marks — 4 for the comparison table + 3 for real-world scenarios)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `getSource()` with `getID()`** — `getSource()` returns the *component* (Object), `getID()` returns the *event type* (int). Examiners *specifically* test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the `InputEvent` intermediate abstract class** — Many students draw a direct link from `AWTEvent` to `KeyEvent`/`MouseEvent`, missing the abstraction. This loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Writing `getActionCommand()` as `getCommand()`** — Always use the exact method names as defined in the JDK. Examiners deduct marks for incorrect method names.
>
> 4. **Confusing `mouseClicked` with `mousePressed`** — `mouseClicked` requires *both* press and release at the *same location*. `mousePressed` fires immediately on press. This is a classic 2-mark trap.
>
> 5. **Forgetting that `TextEvent` has no class-specific accessor methods** — only `getSource()`. Many students fabricate non-existent methods.
>
> 6. **Using `import javax.swing.*` instead of `java.awt.*`** for AWT questions — KTU 2024 Module 4 specifically focuses on **AWT**, not Swing. Use `java.awt.event.*` and `java.awt.*`.
>
> 7. **Not implementing ALL methods of a listener interface** — When you `implements MouseListener`, you MUST provide empty bodies for all 5 methods, or the class won't compile.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Event Classes (Module 4)**

- 📌 **Root superclass:** `java.util.EventObject` (only has `getSource()`).
- 📌 **AWT root:** `java.awt.AWTEvent` (adds `id` and `getID()`).
- 📌 **11 concrete event classes** in `java.awt.event`: `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `WindowEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `TextEvent`, `AdjustmentEvent`, `ComponentEvent`, `FocusEvent`, `ContainerEvent`, `PaintEvent` (+ `MouseWheelEvent` as child of `MouseEvent`).
- 📌 **Two abstract intermediates:** `AWTEvent` and `InputEvent`.
- 📌 **`InputEvent`** is the parent of `KeyEvent` and `MouseEvent`; it holds `when` and `modifiers`.
- 📌 **`MouseEvent` has 7 event IDs** (CLICKED, PRESSED, RELEASED, MOVED, ENTERED, EXITED, DRAGGED, WHEEL).
- 📌 **`KeyEvent` has 3 event IDs** (KEY_PRESSED, KEY_RELEASED, KEY_TYPED). Only `KEY_TYPED` provides a valid `getKeyChar()`.
- 📌 **`WindowEvent` has 7 event IDs** corresponding to 7 methods in `WindowListener`.
- 📌 **`ItemEvent.STATE_CHANGED`** is the only ID for `ItemEvent`; use `getStateChange()` to check `SELECTED` vs. `DESELECTED`.
- 📌 **`TextEvent`** is special — it has no extra accessor methods beyond `getSource()`.
- 📌 **Modifier keys** in `InputEvent` use **bitwise OR encoding** to combine multiple keys.
- 📌 **Event IDs** are integer constants — `ACTION_PERFORMED = 1001`, `MOUSE_PRESSED = 501`, `KEY_PRESSED = 401`, `WINDOW_OPENED = 200`, `ITEM_STATE_CHANGED = 701`.
- 📌 **The Delegation Event Model** = *Source + Event + Listener + Registration (`addXxxListener`)*.
- 📌 **Thread-safety:** All GUI event handling happens on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**.
- 📌 **Exam mantra:** "**Event class encapsulates WHAT happened, WHERE, and HOW**."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Events
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Sources of Events --- # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of Java's **Delegation Event Model (DEM)**, an **Event Source** is defined as any object that has the inherent capability to **generate (or "fire") events** when a specific user interaction or system occurrence takes place. Formally, it is a component (typically extending `java.awt.Component` or `javax.swing.JComponent`) that maintains an internal registry of **event listeners** and notifies them whenever a state change or interaction occurs. As per the **KTU 2024 OEERT613 syllabus (Module 4)**, event sources are the *originators* in the three-tier event handling architecture: **Source → Event → Listener**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > Sources of Events, Event Classes, Event Listeners — focusing on AWT and Swing components such as `Button`, `TextField`, `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `List`, `Menu`, and their corresponding event firing capabilities. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **fire alarm system** in a building: | Real-World Analogy | Java Event Model Equivalent | |---|---| | Smoke detector (the device that detects smoke) | **Event Source** (e.g., `Button`) | | The alarm sound + smoke data packet | **Event Object** (e.g., `ActionEvent`) | | The fire department that responds | **Event Listener** (e.g., `ActionListener`) | | The phone call connecting them | **Registration Mechanism** (`addXxxListener()`) | **Intuition:** A `Button` does *not know* what should happen when clicked. It simply **detects the click** and **fires an `ActionEvent`**. Whoever has *registered* (subscribed) to listen for that event will be notified. The button is therefore the **source**. ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Java Event Handling Every event-driven interaction in Java revolves around **three entities**: 1. **Event Source** — *Who creates the event?* (The component itself) 2. **Event Object** — *What happened?* (Encapsulates event data) 3. **Event Listener** — *Who handles it?* (Interface with callback methods) > [!NOTE] > **Key Term to Memorize:** In Java terminology, the act of an event source generating an event is called **"firing" an event** (synonymous with *dispatching* or *notifying*). ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Classification The KTU syllabus emphasizes that sources are classified based on the **category of input they accept**: - **Keyboard Input Sources** → generate `KeyEvent` - **Pointer Input Sources** → generate `MouseEvent` - **Selection/State Sources** → generate `ItemEvent` - **Command Execution Sources** → generate `ActionEvent` - **Focus Sources** → generate `FocusEvent` - **Window Lifecycle Sources** → generate `WindowEvent` --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Event Source — Operational Breakdown An event source is technically any Java object that implements the mechanism to: 1. **Maintain a Listener List** — An internal data structure (typically `EventListenerList`) storing references to all registered listeners. 2. **Expose a `addXxxListener()` Method** — Public registration API. 3. **Expose a `removeXxxListener()` Method** — Public deregistration API. 4. **Invoke a Protected Fire Method** — A package-private method (e.g., `processActionEvent()`) that iterates through the listener list and invokes their callback methods. > [!NOTE] > The firing method is *protected* (not `public`) because **only the component itself should trigger its own events**. External classes cannot artificially fire events on a button — this preserves encapsulation. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Event Sources The entire concept of event sources exists due to a fundamental **object-oriented design principle**: > **"A component should not be coupled to specific behavior. Instead, it should announce what happened and let interested parties respond."** This is the **Hollywood Principle**: *"Don't call us, we'll call you."* The listener registers interest, and the source calls back only when an event of interest occurs. ## 2.3 Detailed Event Source Classification Matrix | # | Source Component | Package | Event Class Generated | Listener Interface | Callback Method | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `Button` | `java.awt` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | | 2 | `TextField` | `java.awt` | `ActionEvent`, `TextEvent` | `ActionListener`, `TextListener` | `actionPerformed()`, `textValueChanged()` | | 3 | `Checkbox` | `java.awt` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | | 4 | `CheckboxGroup` (Radio) | `java.awt` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | | 5 | `Choice` (Dropdown) | `java.awt` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` | | 6 | `List` | `java.awt` | `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent` | `ActionListener`, `ItemListener` | Both applicable | | 7 | `MenuItem` | `java.awt` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | | 8 | `Scrollbar` | `java.awt` | `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | `adjustmentValueChanged()` | | 9 | `Window` / `Frame` | `java.awt` | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | 7 methods (`windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, etc.) | | 10 | `Component` (Generic) | `java.awt` | `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`, `FocusEvent` | Respective listeners | Various | | 11 | `JButton` (Swing) | `javax.swing` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | | 12 | `JTextField` (Swing) | `javax.swing` | `ActionEvent`, `CaretEvent`, `DocumentEvent` | Multiple | Multiple | ##
