2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Standard Notation / Method Signature | |---|---| | **Source-Listener Binding** | `sourceObject.addXxxListener(listenerObject)` | | **Event Firing Trigger** | Internal: `processXxxEvent(new XxxEvent(this, id, command))` | | **Listener Interface Declaration** | `class MyHandler implements ActionListener` | | **Callback Method Signature** | `public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | | **Event Object Identifier** | `e.getSource()`, `e.getActionCommand()` | | **Multiple Listener Registration** | One source can have **N listeners** registered | | **Event Multicasting Rule** | $N_{\text{events fired}} = N_{\text{listeners registered}} \times 1$ | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Event sources are the backbone of: - **GUI Application Development** — Every desktop application (Eclipse IDE, NetBeans) - **Embedded Systems UI** — Touch screen interfaces in IoT devices - **Web Application Front-ends** — JavaScript event delegation mirrors Java's DEM - **Real-Time Monitoring Dashboards** — SCADA systems using JavaFX --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Conceptual Derivation: How an Event Source Works Internally Below is the **exhaustive logical derivation** of what happens when a user clicks a button (the source) in a Java AWT application: ### Step 1 — Component Instantiation A `Button` object is created in memory. Internally, the AWT runtime initializes an `EventListenerList` data structure (essentially a `Object[]` array). $$ \text{Button btn} = \text{new Button}(\text{"Submit"}); $$ ### Step 2 — Listener Registration A class implementing `ActionListener` registers itself: $$ \text{btn.addActionListener}(\text{this}); $$ This call internally invokes: $$ \text{listenerList.add}(\text{ActionListener.class}, \text{this}); $$ ### Step 3 — User Interaction (The Trigger) The user clicks the button. The AWT event-dispatch thread translates the native OS click into a Java `ActionEvent` object: $$ \text{ActionEvent ae} = \text{new ActionEvent}(\text{btn, ActionEvent.ACTION\_PERFORMED, "Submit"}); $$ ### Step 4 — Event Firing (The Notification) The `Button` invokes its protected `processActionEvent()`: $$ \text{processActionEvent}(\text{AE}) \rightarrow \text{fireEvent}(\text{AE}) $$ This iterates through the registered listeners: $$ \forall \, L \in \text{listenerList}[\text{ActionListener.class}]: L.\text{actionPerformed}(AE) $$ ### Step 5 — Callback Execution The listener's `actionPerformed()` method executes — this is where the user's logic resides. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Source-Listener-Event Triad Mapping | User Action | Source Component | Event Object | Listener Interface | When Fired | |---|---|---|---|---| | Clicking a button | `Button` / `JButton` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | On mouse release over button | | Pressing Enter in text box | `TextField` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | On Enter key release | | Typing text | `TextField` | `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | On every text content change | | Selecting a checkbox | `Checkbox` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | On state toggle (selected/deselected) | | Selecting a radio button | `Checkbox` in `CheckboxGroup` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | On radio selection change | | Picking dropdown item | `Choice` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | On item selection | | Double-clicking list item | `List` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | On double-click | | Selecting list item | `List` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | On single-click selection | | Opening/closing window | `Window` / `Frame` | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | On window state transition | | Moving mouse | `Component` | `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` / `MouseMotionListener` | On mouse coordinate change | | Typing a key | `Component` | `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | On key press/release/type | | Scrolling | `Scrollbar` | `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | On value adjustment | | Component gains/loses focus | `Component` | `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | On focus state change | ## 3.3 Sample Code Demonstrating Event Source Registration ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class SourceDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener { Button btn; TextField tf; public SourceDemo() { // Step 1: Create event sources btn = new Button("Click Me"); tf = new TextField(20); // Step 2: Register this object as a listener btn.addActionListener(this); tf.addActionListener(this); // Layout and visibility setLayout(new FlowLayout()); add(tf); add(btn); setSize(300, 200); setVisible(true); } // Step 3: Callback method (event handler) public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { if (e.getSource() == btn) { System.out.println("Button fired the event!"); } else if (e.getSource() == tf) { System.out.println("TextField fired the event: " + tf.getText()); } } public static void main(String[] args) { new SourceDemo(); } } ``` ## 3.4 Mathematical Model of Multi-Listener Behavior Given: - $S$ = Event Source (single component) - $L = \{L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n\}$ = Set of registered listeners - $E$ = Event object fired by $S$ When $S$ fires $E$, the notifications follow: $$ \text{Notifications} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i.\text{callback}(E) $$ For $n$ listeners, **exactly $n$ callback invocations** occur — order is **not guaranteed** by the JVM specification (though typically registration order is preserved). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Event Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Interaction
Mouse Click / Key Press] --> B[Operating System
Native Event] B --> C[JVM Event Queue
AWT Event Dispatch Thread] C --> D[Event Source Component
e.g. Button, TextField] D --> E[Event ListenerList
Internal Registry] E --> F1[Listener 1
Callback Method] E --> F2[Listener 2
Callback Method] E --> F3[Listener N
Callback Method] F1 --> G[Business Logic Execution] F2 --> G F3 --> G ``` ## 4.2 Source–Event–Listener Triad Relationship ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SRC [Event Source Layer] B[Button] TF[TextField] CB[Checkbox] LS[List] CH[Choice] end subgraph EVT [Event Object Layer] AE[ActionEvent] IE[ItemEvent] TE[TextEvent] end subgraph LST [Event Listener Layer] AL[ActionListener] IL[ItemListener] TL[TextListener] end B -->|fires| AE TF -->|fires| AE TF -->|fires| TE CB -->|fires| IE LS -->|fires| IE LS -->|fires| AE CH -->|fires| IE AE -.notifies.-> AL IE -.notifies.-> IL TE -.notifies.-> TL ``` ## 4.3 Event Source Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[Event Sources in Java AWT] ROOT --> A[Command Sources] ROOT --> B[Selection Sources] ROOT --> C[Input Sources] ROOT --> D[Container Sources] A --> A1[Button] A --> A2[MenuItem] A --> A3[TextField on Enter] B --> B1[Checkbox] B --> B2[CheckboxGroup Radio] B --> B3[Choice Dropdown] B --> B4[List] C --> C1[Any Component - Mouse] C --> C2[Any Component - Keyboard] C --> C3[Any Component - Focus] D --> D1[Window] D --> D2[Frame] D --> D3[Dialog] ``` ## 4.4 Listener Registration Workflow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Event Source participant L as Listener Object participant JVM as JVM Runtime Note over S,L: Phase 1: Setup S->>L: addXxxListener(reference) L-->>S: Listener registered internally Note over U,JVM: Phase 2: Event Occurrence U->>S: Performs action (click / type) S->>JVM: Detects interaction JVM->>S: Creates Event object S->>S: processXxxEvent called Note over S,L: Phase 3: Notification S->>L: callback method invoked L->>L: Executes business logic L-->>U: Result visible ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **Event Source** in the context of Java's Delegation Event Model. List any **four AWT components** that can act as event sources. **Model Answer:** An **Event Source** is any Java object (typically an AWT or Swing component) that has the capability to **generate events** in response to user interaction or system occurrences, and **notifies** registered listeners about those events. The four AWT components acting as event sources are: 1. `Button` — generates `ActionEvent` 2. `TextField` — generates `ActionEvent` and `TextEvent` 3. `Checkbox` — generates `ItemEvent` 4. `Choice` — generates `ItemEvent` --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Explain how an **event source registers a listener**. Write the general syntax used for registration. **Model Answer:** An event source uses the `addXxxListener()` method to register a listener object. The general syntax is: ```java sourceComponent.addXxxListener(listenerObject); ``` For example: - `btn.addActionListener(this);` - `chkbox.addItemListener(this);` This call internally appends the listener reference to the source's `EventListenerList`. When the source fires an event, it iterates through this list and invokes the appropriate callback method on each registered listener. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Provided:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **Delegation Event Model** in Java with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of **event sources** and **event listeners**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java AWT program to create a `Frame` containing a `TextField` and a `Button`. When the user types text and clicks the button, the typed text should be displayed on the console. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Delegation Event Model Explanation:** The Delegation Event Model (DEM) is Java's standard mechanism for handling GUI events. It follows a **publish-subscribe pattern** where: 1. **Event Source** — The component (Button, TextField, etc.) that generates an event when triggered. 2. **Event Object** — Encapsulates information about the event (source, type, timestamp). 3. **Event Listener** — Interface implemented by classes that wish to receive notifications. *Operational Steps:* - The listener **registers** itself with the source using `addXxxListener()`. - When an interaction occurs, the source **creates** an event object. - The source **fires** the event, which invokes the listener's callback method. *Advantages:* - **Decoupling** — Source does not know who handles its events. - **Flexibility** — Multiple listeners can be registered for the same event. - **Reusability** — Generic components can be reused in any context. **(b) Java Program:** ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class TextDisplayDemo extends Frame implements ActionListener { TextField tf; Button btn; public TextDisplayDemo() { tf = new TextField(20); btn = new Button("Display"); // Event Source Registration tf.addActionListener(this); btn.addActionListener(this); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); add(new Label("Enter text: ")); add(tf); add(btn); setSize(400, 200); setTitle("Event Source Demonstration"); setVisible(true); } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { if (e.getSource() == btn || e.getSource() == tf) { System.out.println("Typed Text: " + tf.getText()); } } public static void main(String[] args) { new TextDisplayDemo(); } } ``` **Explanation of Code:** - `TextField tf` and `Button btn` are the **event sources**. - `this` (the `Frame` subclass) acts as the **listener** by implementing `ActionListener`. - The `addActionListener(this)` call **registers** the listener with both sources. - The `actionPerformed()` method is the **callback** that fires whenever either source generates an `ActionEvent`. - The `e.getSource()` check determines **which source fired** the event. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Discuss the various **categories of event sources** in Java AWT. Provide the corresponding event classes and listener interfaces for each category. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java AWT program that demonstrates the use of `Checkbox` and `Choice` as event sources. The program should display which item the user selected. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Categories of Event Sources:** | Category | Source Examples | Event Class | Listener Interface | |---|---|---|---| | Command Actions | `Button`, `MenuItem` | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | | Item Selection | `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `List` | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | | Text Changes | `TextField`, `TextArea` | `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | | Mouse Interaction | Any `Component` | `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener` | | Keyboard Input | Any `Component` | `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | | Focus Changes | Any `Component` | `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | | Window Operations | `Window`, `Frame` | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | | Scrollbar Movement | `Scrollbar` | `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | **(b) Java Program:** ```java import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class SelectionDemo extends Frame implements ItemListener { Checkbox chk1, chk2, chk3; Choice choice; Label resultLabel; public SelectionDemo() { chk1 = new Checkbox("Java"); chk2 = new Checkbox("Python"); chk3 = new Checkbox("C++"); choice = new Choice(); choice.add("B.Tech"); choice.add("M.Tech"); choice.add("PhD"); resultLabel = new Label("Selections will appear here"); // Register listeners (event sources) chk1.addItemListener(this); chk2.addItemListener(this); chk3.addItemListener(this); choice.addItemListener(this); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); add(chk1); add(chk2); add(chk3); add(new Label("Program:")); add(choice); add(resultLabel); setSize(400, 250); setTitle("Selection Source Demo"); setVisible(true); } public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) { String msg = "Selected: "; if (chk1.getState()) msg += "Java "; if (chk2.getState()) msg += "Python "; if (chk3.getState()) msg += "C++ "; msg += "| Program: " + choice.getSelectedItem(); resultLabel.setText(msg); System.out.println(msg); } public static void main(String[] args) { new SelectionDemo(); } } ``` **Explanation:** - `Checkbox` and `Choice` are registered as event sources with `addItemListener()`. - The `itemStateChanged()` method is the callback for `ItemListener`. - Multiple sources of the same type can share a single listener instance. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to implement the listener interface** — Students often declare the method but forget `implements ActionListener`. *(Lose 2-3 marks)* > 2. **Wrong callback method signature** — Must be exactly `public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)`. Spelling mistakes cause compilation failure. > 3. **Confusing `addActionListener` with `addItemListener`** — Button requires `ActionListener`, Checkbox requires `ItemListener`. Mismatched pairs yield zero event firing. > 4. **Not invoking `setVisible(true)`** — Without this, the Frame never appears, leading to "program runs but nothing happens" confusion. > 5. **Not using `e.getSource()` for multiple sources** — When two components share a listener, failing to disambiguate causes incorrect logic. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - ✅ **Event Source** = the component that **generates and fires** events (Button, TextField, Checkbox, Choice, List, Window, etc.). - ✅ **Event Object** = encapsulates event details (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `ItemEvent`, `KeyEvent`). - ✅ **Event Listener** = interface implemented by handler class (`ActionListener`, `ItemListener`, etc.). - ✅ **Registration syntax**: `source.addXxxListener(handler)` — this is the **only** way to bind a listener. - ✅ **Button** → `ActionEvent` → `ActionListener` → `actionPerformed()` - ✅ **Checkbox / Choice / List** → `ItemEvent` → `ItemListener` → `itemStateChanged()` - ✅ **TextField** → can fire both `ActionEvent` (Enter) and `TextEvent` (text change). - ✅ **Multiple listeners** can be registered on a single source. - ✅ **Single listener** can handle multiple sources (use `e.getSource()` to disambiguate). - ✅ **Protected `processXxxEvent()`** methods ensure only the component fires its own events. - ✅ The Delegation Event Model follows the **Hollywood Principle** — *Don't call us, we'll call you.* - ✅ Always remember to call `setSize()` and `setVisible(true)` on the Frame for the GUI to display.

topics.title: Event Listener Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Listener Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — EVENT LISTENER INTERFACES

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the Java Abstract Window Toolkit (**AWT**) and **Swing** frameworks, an **Event Listener Interface** is a contract (purely abstract interface, typically extending the marker interface `java.util.EventListener`) that prescribes one or more callback methods. These callback methods are automatically invoked by the **event dispatching thread (EDT)** when a specific category of user or system event is fired on a registered GUI component.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> An *Event Listener Interface* is a Java interface belonging to `java.awt.event` or `javax.swing.event` that defines the methods a class must implement in order to receive and process specific events generated by a source component, in accordance with the **Delegation Event Model (DEM)**.

| Mandatory Interface Inheritance | Description |
|---|---|
| `extends java.util.EventListener` | A *marker* super-interface; serves as a *type tag* indicating the interface is meant for event handling. |
| All methods return `void` | Callback semantics — the JDK invokes them, your code reacts. |
| Single parameter (an `EventObject` subclass) | Carries contextual data (e.g., coordinates, key code, mouse button). |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 💡

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Newspaper Subscription":**
> Imagine you subscribe to a newspaper. The newspaper is the **Event Source** (the GUI button, window, text field, etc.). The publisher's *subscription contract* is the **Event Listener Interface** — a written agreement that says *"When something happens, here is exactly what kind of notification you will get."* You, the subscriber, are the **Listener** (the object implementing the interface). You *register* your subscription (call `addActionListener(...)`), and the moment the newspaper has news (an event fires), the publisher hands you the paper (the event object) and you read it (your overridden method runs).

* **Publisher** → `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JWindow` (the source)
* **Subscription form** → `ActionListener`, `KeyListener`, `WindowListener`
* **You, the reader** → The class implementing the interface
* **Today's paper** → The `ActionEvent` / `KeyEvent` / `WindowEvent`

This mental model — **source → contract → handler** — is the single most important idea in Java event programming.

## 1.3 Why Listener Interfaces Exist (and not Concrete Classes)

1. **Multiple Inheritance of Type** — A `JButton` may need to respond to clicks, mouse motion, key presses, and focus changes. Java does not permit multiple class inheritance, so each behavior is declared as an interface.
2. **Decoupling** — The component (source) does not need to *know* what will handle its events; it only stores a `List<EventListener>`.
3. **Loose Coupling & Reusability** — A single listener object can be registered to multiple sources, and one source can have multiple listeners.
4. **Single Responsibility Principle** — Each interface groups only semantically related events (e.g., `MouseListener` deals only with mouse-clicks/entries/exits).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Delegation Event Model — Conceptual Foundation

Java's event handling revolves around **three actors**. KTU frequently tests these roles:

| Actor | Type | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Event Source** | A GUI component (e.g., `JButton`, `JFrame`) | Maintains a list of registered listeners. Calls `fireXxxEvent()` when the event occurs. |
| **Event Object** | Subclass of `java.util.EventObject` | Encapsulates the *what, when, where* of the occurrence. |
| **Event Listener Interface** | Interface extending `java.util.EventListener` | Declares the callback method(s) the source will invoke. |
| **Listener (Handler)** | A class implementing the listener interface | Provides the actual reaction logic. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule (Board-Favourite):** The source *never* implements listener logic; it simply **invokes** the listener's method via the interface reference. This is *Delegation*.

## 2.2 Master List of Listener Interfaces (KTU 2024 — High-Yield)

Below is the consolidated KTU formula-sheet style table. Every interface is a **callback contract**.

| Listener Interface | Package | Methods Declared | Event Source(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | `java.awt.event` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` | `JButton`, `JMenuItem`, `JTextField` |
| `MouseListener` | `java.awt.event` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` | Any `Component` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | `java.awt.event` | `mouseDragged`, `mouseMoved` | Any `Component` |
| `KeyListener` | `java.awt.event` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` | Any `Component` |
| `FocusListener` | `java.awt.event` | `focusGained`, `focusLost` | Any `Component` |
| `WindowListener` | `java.awt.event` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated` | `JFrame`, `JDialog` |
| `ItemListener` | `java.awt.event` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` | `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox` |
| `TextListener` | `java.awt.event` | `textValueChanged(TextEvent e)` | `TextComponent` subclasses |
| `AdjustmentListener` | `java.awt.event` | `adjustmentValueChanged` | `JScrollbar` |
| `ComponentListener` | `java.awt.event` | `componentResized`, `componentMoved`, `componentShown`, `componentHidden` | Any `Component` |
| `ContainerListener` | `java.awt.event` | `componentAdded`, `componentRemoved` | Any `Container` |
| `ChangeListener` | `javax.swing.event` | `stateChanged(ChangeEvent e)` | `JSlider`, `JProgressBar`, `JTabbedPane` |
| `ListSelectionListener` | `javax.swing.event` | `valueChanged(ListSelectionEvent e)` | `JList`, `JTable` |

### 2.2.1 The Universal Adapter Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> AWT provides **default no-op implementations** of every multi-method listener through an *Adapter Class* (e.g., `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`). These are abstract classes whose purpose is to let you override **only the methods you care about**.

$$ \text{Adapter} = \text{Default no-op implementation of a multi-method listener interface} $$

This is the **Open/Closed Principle** in action — you extend behaviour without modifying the source.

## 2.3 The Event Object Class Hierarchy

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{java.util.EventObject} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{java.awt.AWTEvent} \\
&\;\longrightarrow\; \text{ActionEvent} \\
&\;\longrightarrow\; \text{MouseEvent} \\
&\;\longrightarrow\; \text{KeyEvent} \\
&\;\longrightarrow\; \text{WindowEvent} \\
&\;\longrightarrow\; \text{ItemEvent} \\
&\;\longrightarrow\; \text{TextEvent} \\
&\;\longrightarrow\; \text{AdjustmentEvent} \\
&\;\longrightarrow\; \text{ComponentEvent} \\
&\;\longrightarrow\; \text{ContainerEvent} \\
&\;\longrightarrow\; \text{FocusEvent} \\
&\;\longrightarrow\; \text{PaintEvent} \;(\text{internal})
\end{aligned}
$$

`javax.swing.event` adds Swing-specific events (`ChangeEvent`, `ListSelectionEvent`, `MenuEvent`, `CaretEvent`, `UndoableEditEvent`, `HyperlinkEvent`, `InternalFrameEvent`).

## 2.4 The Universal Event Registration Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **3-step formula** the KTU paper will test, again and again, is:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Implement Listener Interface} \quad &\rightarrow \;\; \text{class MyHandler implements ActionListener} \\
\text{Step 2: Override Callback Method} \quad &\rightarrow \;\; \text{public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)\{ \dots \}} \\
\text{Step 3: Register with Source} \quad &\rightarrow \;\; \text{sourceObject.addActionListener(handlerRef);}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

*Note:* The *source* is always the **caller** of `addXxxListener(this)`; the *handler* is always the **argument**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Listener Interfaces |
|---|---|
| **Desktop GUI Apps** | Button-click handlers, form validation on focus-lost, drag-and-drop in IDEs. |
| **Industrial SCADA / HMI** | Sensor event handlers in JavaFX/Swing dashboards. |
| **Web Backends (Spring)** | `ApplicationListener<ContextRefreshedEvent>` mirrors the same pattern. |
| **Android / Mobile** | `OnClickListener`, `OnTouchListener` (same idea, rebranded). |
| **Real-time Systems** | Observer pattern = listener interfaces, foundational to MVC and reactive streams. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Code Derivation — A Complete Event-Handling Program

Below is a **fully worked** Java program demonstrating `ActionListener` and `WindowListener` with an **anonymous inner class** and an **adapter**. Every line is intentional and KTU-relevant.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class EventDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {

    // ---- SOURCE COMPONENTS (Step 1 of the Universal Formula) ----
    private JButton  btnClick;
    private JButton  btnExit;
    private JLabel   lblStatus;

    public EventDemo() {
        setTitle("KTU Event Listener Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        btnClick = new JButton("Click Me");
        btnExit  = new JButton("Exit");
        lblStatus = new JLabel("Status: Awaiting event...");

        add(btnClick);
        add(btnExit);
        add(lblStatus);

        // ---- STEP 3: REGISTER LISTENERS WITH SOURCES ----
        btnClick.addActionListener(this);      // 'this' implements ActionListener
        btnExit.addActionListener(this);

        // Anonymous inner class for WindowListener using adapter
        addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) {
                System.out.println("Window is closing. Goodbye!");
                System.exit(0);
            }
        });

        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    // ---- STEP 2: OVERRIDE CALLBACK METHOD ----
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == btnClick) {
            lblStatus.setText("Status: Button clicked at " + e.getWhen());
        } else if (e.getSource() == btnExit) {
            dispose();
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(EventDemo::new);
    }
}
```

### 3.1.1 Analytical Trace — What Happens When You Click

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. User clicks} \;&\rightarrow\; \text{OS dispatches a native click to AWT} \\
\text{2. AWT constructs} \;&\rightarrow\; \text{ActionEvent e = new ActionEvent(btnClick, ActionEvent.ACTION\_PERFORMED, "Click Me")} \\
\text{3. Source iterates listener list} \;&\rightarrow\; \text{for (ActionListener l : listeners) l.actionPerformed(e);} \\
\text{4. Polymorphic dispatch} \;&\rightarrow\; \text{Our overridden method runs → label updates} \\
\text{5. EDT returns} \;&\rightarrow\; \text{Repaint scheduled → screen updates}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Why `EventListener` is a *Marker* Interface

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{public interface EventListener \{\}} \quad &\text{— empty body, no methods.} \\
\text{Purpose:} \quad &\text{Acts as a *semantic tag* for the JVM and frameworks.} \\
\text{Derived benefit:} \quad &\text{Reflection-based tools can detect "is this a listener?" via} \\
\quad &\texttt{(obj instanceof EventListener)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often say "EventListener declares methods." It does **not** — it is a *tagging* interface. The **sub-interfaces** declare the methods.

## 3.3 Multiple-Listener Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favourite Question:** *"Can a single source have multiple listeners?"*
> **Yes — and here is the formal derivation.**

A source maintains an `EventListenerList` (a thread-safe array of typed pairs). When an event fires:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{listenerList.getListenerList()} \;&\rightarrow\; \text{Object[][] pairs} \\
\text{for each pair } [T, listener] \;&\rightarrow\; ((T) listener).\text{callback}(e);
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **why** you can register two different `ActionListener`s to the same `JButton` and both will fire.

## 3.4 Method Signature Derivation — Why `void` and Why `EventObject`?

| Constraint | Reason |
|---|---|
| Return type `void` | The event source has no use for a return value; the listener's job is to *react*, not compute. |
| Parameter is the specific event subclass | Provides context (coordinates, key code, modifiers, source reference). |
| Modifier `public` | Required so the JVM/EDT across package boundaries can invoke it. |
| No `throws` clause | Event handling methods cannot declare checked exceptions; the EDT would have no way to handle them. |

$$
\boxed{\; \text{signature} = \texttt{public void methodName(SpecificEvent e)}\;}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How the Interfaces Relate

| Listener | Adapter Provided? | Common Event Source | Most-Overridden Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | ❌ (only 1 method) | `JButton` | `actionPerformed` |
| `MouseListener` | ✅ `MouseAdapter` | Any `Component` | `mouseClicked` |
| `MouseMotionListener` | ✅ `MouseMotionAdapter` | Any `Component` | `mouseMoved` |
| `KeyListener` | ✅ `KeyAdapter` | Any `Component` | `keyPressed` |
| `WindowListener` | ✅ `WindowAdapter` | `JFrame`, `JDialog` | `windowClosing` |
| `FocusListener` | ✅ `FocusAdapter` | Any `Component` | `focusLost` |
| `ItemListener` | ❌ (only 1 method) | `JCheckBox` | `itemStateChanged` |
| `ComponentListener` | ✅ `ComponentAdapter` | Any `Component` | `componentResized` |
| `ContainerListener` | ✅ `ContainerAdapter` | Any `Container` | `componentAdded` |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Heuristic:** *If the listener has more than one method, AWT almost always provides an `XxxAdapter` class.*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Delegation Event Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER
        u1[User Action e.g. mouse click]
    end

    subgraph SOURCE
        s1[JButton JFrame JTextField]
    end

    subgraph CONTRACT
        c1[ActionListener MouseListener KeyListener WindowListener]
    end

    subgraph HANDLER
        h1[MyClass implements ActionListener]
        h2[actionPerformed ActionEvent e]
    end

    subgraph EDT
        e1[Event Dispatch Thread]
    end

    u1 --> s1
    s1 -->|fires ActionEvent| e1
    e1 -->|invokes via interface ref| c1
    c1 -->|polymorphic call| h2
    h1 -.implements.-> c1
    h1 -->|contains| h2
```

## 4.2 Listener Interface Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[java util EventListener]
    A --> B1[java awt event ActionListener]
    A --> B2[java awt event MouseListener]
    A --> B3[java awt event KeyListener]
    A --> B4[java awt event WindowListener]
    A --> B5[java awt event FocusListener]
    A --> B6[java awt event ItemListener]
    A --> C1[javax swing event ChangeListener]
    A --> C2[javax swing event ListSelectionListener]

    B2 --> D1[MouseAdapter default impl]
    B3 --> D2[KeyAdapter default impl]
    B4 --> D3[WindowAdapter default impl]
    B5 --> D4[FocusAdapter default impl]
```

## 4.3 Event Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[java util EventObject]
    P --> Q[java awt AWTEvent]
    Q --> R1[ActionEvent]
    Q --> R2[MouseEvent]
    Q --> R3[KeyEvent]
    Q --> R4[WindowEvent]
    Q --> R5[ItemEvent]
    Q --> R6[FocusEvent]
    Q --> R7[ComponentEvent]
    Q --> R8[ContainerEvent]
    Q --> R9[AdjustmentEvent]
    Q --> R10[TextEvent]
    P --> S1[javax swing event ChangeEvent]
    P --> S2[javax swing event ListSelectionEvent]
    P --> S3[javax swing event MenuEvent]
    P --> S4[javax swing event CaretEvent]
```

## 4.4 Complete Interaction Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant SRC as Source JButton
    participant LIST as ListenerList
    participant L as Listener Object
    participant EDT as Event Dispatch Thread

    U->>SRC: 1. Physical click
    SRC->>SRC: 2. AWT creates ActionEvent
    SRC->>LIST: 3. getListenerList
    LIST-->>SRC: 4. Returns registered listeners
    SRC->>EDT: 5. EventQueue post invokeLater
    EDT->>L: 6. l.actionPerformed e
    L->>L: 7. Override executes business logic
    L-->>U: 8. Visual feedback label changes
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define an Event Listener Interface in Java. List any four listener interfaces from the `java.awt.event` package along with the methods they declare.**

**Model Answer:**

An *Event Listener Interface* is a Java interface that extends the marker interface `java.util.EventListener` and declares one or more callback methods. When an event is fired on a source, the registered listener's method(s) are invoked to handle it.

Four listener interfaces:

| Interface | Method(s) |
|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | `void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited` (each taking a `MouseEvent`) |
| `KeyListener` | `keyTyped`, `keyPressed`, `keyReleased` (each taking a `KeyEvent`) |
| `WindowListener` | `windowOpened`, `windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, `windowIconified`, `windowDeiconified`, `windowActivated`, `windowDeactivated` |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of Adapter classes in Java AWT. Why are they not provided for `ActionListener` and `ItemListener`?**

**Model Answer:**

*Adapter classes* (e.g., `WindowAdapter`, `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`) provide empty default implementations for every method in a multi-method listener interface. By extending an adapter, a programmer overrides only the methods relevant to their application, avoiding the need to implement *all* methods of the interface.

They are **not** provided for `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` because each of these interfaces declares **only a single method** — there is nothing to "default-implement." Implementing the interface directly is sufficient.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently **forget to register** the listener with the source via `addXxxListener()`. Without this, the callback will *never* fire — **−3 marks minimum**.
> 2. Confusing the **source** (caller) with the **listener** (argument) when tracing. Memorize: *source.addXxxListener(listener)*.
> 3. Writing `implements ActionListener` but **not** overriding `actionPerformed` — KTU deducts for incomplete polymorphism.
> 4. Using `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` together with a custom `WindowListener.windowClosing` — the listener *never executes*; this is a common logical contradiction.

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Delegation Event Model** in Java AWT. List the three actors and their roles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program that creates a frame containing a text field, a button labeled *"Submit"*, and a label. When the button is clicked, the text from the text field should be displayed on the label. Use an **anonymous inner class** to handle the click. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

The **Delegation Event Model** has three actors:

1. **Event Source** — A GUI component (e.g., `JButton`). It maintains an internal `EventListenerList` and exposes registration methods like `addActionListener()`, `addKeyListener()`.

2. **Event Object** — A subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (e.g., `ActionEvent`) that encapsulates the event data — source reference, ID, timestamp, modifiers.

3. **Event Listener** — An object implementing a listener interface. When the event fires, the source **delegates** handling by invoking the listener's method.

```
User Action → Source creates EventObject → Source calls listener method → Listener reacts
```

**Advantages:** decoupling, multiple listeners per source, single source per listener, dynamic registration.

#### Model Solution (b)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class SubmitDemo extends JFrame {
    JTextField tf;
    JLabel lbl;
    JButton btn;

    public SubmitDemo() {
        setTitle("Submit Demo");
        setSize(350, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        tf  = new JTextField(15);
        btn = new JButton("Submit");
        lbl = new JLabel("Output appears here");

        add(tf); add(btn); add(lbl);

        // Anonymous inner class — listener registration
        btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            @Override
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                lbl.setText("Hello, " + tf.getText());
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SubmitDemo();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. The class extends `JFrame` (source container).
2. Three components created: `JTextField`, `JButton`, `JLabel`.
3. The `btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener(){...})` is the **registration step** — the source is the *caller*, the *argument* is the anonymous listener.
4. The overridden `actionPerformed` reads `tf.getText()` and updates the label.
5. The EDT dispatches the `ActionEvent` asynchronously, calling our method.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternate Set]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **class-level** and **anonymous inner class** listener implementations. Provide a small code example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the `KeyListener` interface (with `KeyAdapter`), write a Java program where pressing the **Escape** key closes the window and prints *"Escape pressed — closing."* Use an **adapter class** to override only `keyPressed`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

| Aspect | Class-Level | Anonymous Inner Class |
|---|---|---|
| Reusability | Reusable across components | One-shot, tied to a single registration |
| Code footprint | Separate `.java` or nested class | Defined inline where registered |
| Readability | Clearer for complex handlers | Concise for simple handlers |
| Syntax | `class MyHandler implements ActionListener { ... }` | `new ActionListener() { ... }` |

**Class-level example:**

```java
class ClickHandler implements ActionListener {
    @Override public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Clicked: " + e.getActionCommand());
    }
}
btn.addActionListener(new ClickHandler());
```

**Anonymous example:**

```java
btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
    @Override public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Clicked: " + e.getActionCommand());
    }
});
```

#### Model Solution (b)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class EscapeCloser extends JFrame {
    public EscapeCloser() {
        setTitle("Press ESC to close");
        setSize(300, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE);

        addKeyListener(new KeyAdapter() {
            @Override
            public void keyPressed(KeyEvent e) {
                if (e.getKeyCode() == KeyEvent.VK_ESCAPE) {
                    System.out.println("Escape pressed — closing.");
                    dispose();
                    System.exit(0);
                }
            }
        });

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new EscapeCloser();
    }
}
```

**Key points:**

1. `KeyAdapter` provides no-op implementations of all three `KeyListener` methods.
2. Only `keyPressed` is overridden — `keyTyped` and `keyReleased` remain inactive.
3. `e.getKeyCode() == KeyEvent.VK_ESCAPE` performs the comparison using the static constant.
4. `setDefaultCloseOperation(DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE)` is **mandatory** so that our custom closing logic executes; otherwise, AWT will dispose the window *before* `keyPressed` triggers.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. Memorize it the night before the exam.**

- ☐ An **Event Listener Interface** extends `java.util.EventListener` (a *marker* interface — empty body).
- ☐ All listener methods are `public void` and accept **one** `EventObject` subclass parameter.
- ☐ The **Delegation Event Model** has three roles: **Source**, **Event Object**, **Listener**.
- ☐ A source maintains a list of listeners and **invokes** them when an event fires — the source does *not* know handler internals.
- ☐ The **3-step universal formula**: *(i) implement interface*, *(ii) override callback*, *(iii) register with source* via `addXxxListener(handler)`.
- ☐ AWT provides **Adapter** classes for every listener with **more than one method** (e.g., `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, `FocusAdapter`, `ComponentAdapter`, `ContainerAdapter`).
- ☐ **No adapter** for `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` (single-method interfaces).
- ☐ AWT events live in `java.awt.event`; Swing-specific events in `javax.swing.event`.
- ☐ Multiple listeners can be attached to one source; one listener can be registered with many sources.
- ☐ All event classes ultimately inherit from `java.util.EventObject`; AWT events go through `java.awt.AWTEvent`.
- ☐ Use `e.getSource()` inside a callback to identify *which* component fired the event.
- ☐ Use `e.getKeyCode()`, `e.getKeyChar()`, `e.getX()`, `e.getY()`, `e.getWhen()` for context.
- ☐ Common keys: `KeyEvent.VK_ESCAPE`, `VK_ENTER`, `VK_F1`...`VK_F12`, `VK_UP`, `VK_DOWN`.
- ☐ Setting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` **bypasses** any `WindowListener.windowClosing` logic.
- ☐ Swing programs are usually launched via `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)` on the **EDT** to ensure thread safety.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swings Fundamentals - Swing Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swings Fundamentals - Swing Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Java Swing?

**Java Swing** is a lightweight, platform-independent Graphical User Interface (GUI) toolkit that is part of the Java Foundation Classes (JFC). It is used to create rich, interactive desktop applications with components like buttons, text fields, tables, and trees. Swing is built on top of the **Abstract Window Toolkit (AWT)** and resides in the package `javax.swing`.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, Swing is studied under *Module 4: Swings Fundamentals*, which introduces the student to building event-driven, window-based applications in Java.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Every Swing component begins with the prefix **"J"** (e.g., `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JFrame`). This prefix is the *single, most consistent identifier* the KTU examiner uses to test whether the student knows the difference between AWT and Swing components.

## 1.2 What are Swing Controls?

**Swing Controls** (also called *Swing Components*) are the pre-built, reusable interactive widgets provided by the Swing API that a programmer can instantiate and place inside a container (like `JFrame` or `JPanel`) to build a graphical interface. They respond to user actions (mouse clicks, key presses) by firing *events*.

Common Swing Controls include:

| Category | Controls |
|----------|----------|
| Display | `JLabel`, `JProgressBar`, `JToolTip` |
| Input | `JButton`, `JTextField`, `JTextArea`, `JPasswordField`, `JComboBox` |
| Selection | `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JList`, `JSlider` |
| Containers | `JFrame`, `JPanel`, `JScrollPane`, `JTabbedPane`, `JDialog` |
| Complex | `JTable`, `JTree`, `JMenuBar`, `JMenu`, `JMenuItem` |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Control Room"

Imagine a **modern car dashboard**:

- The **steering wheel, accelerator, and speedometer** are not the engine itself — they are *control surfaces* that let the driver (the user) interact with the engine (the underlying application logic).
- Swing Controls work the same way. A `JButton` does not *do* the database query; it simply tells the program, *"The user just clicked me — go run the business logic."*
- The **dashboard frame** holding these controls is the `JFrame` (the top-level container). Without a frame, you have floating, useless parts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Check:** Think of a Swing Control as a *bell on a hotel reception desk*. The bell itself does not check you in. When you press the bell (event), the receptionist (event listener) takes action. The bell is a `JButton`; the receptionist is a *Listener interface implementation*.

## 1.4 Why Swing over AWT?

- **Lightweight:** Swing components are written entirely in Java (do not rely on the underlying OS peers), unlike AWT which uses *heavyweight* native peers.
- **Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF):** Swing components can change their appearance dynamically (Metal, Nimbus, Windows, GTK).
- **Rich Component Set:** Swing provides advanced controls like `JTable` and `JTree` that AWT does not support.
- **MVC Architecture:** Swing follows a clean *Model-View-Controller* separation, allowing the same data to be displayed in multiple views.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Swing Class Hierarchy

Every Swing control ultimately derives from `java.awt.Component`, but the more important root is **`javax.swing.JComponent`**, which provides the core Swing behavior (pluggable look, double-buffering, border support, tooltips, and keystroke handling).

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.awt.Component
         └── java.awt.Container
               └── javax.swing.JComponent   ← ROOT of all Swing controls
                     ├── JLabel
                     ├── JButton (abstract) → JMenuItem
                     ├── JTextComponent (abstract)
                     │       ├── JTextField
                     │       ├── JTextArea
                     │       └── JPasswordField
                     ├── JToggleButton
                     │       ├── JCheckBox
                     │       └── JRadioButton
                     ├── JComboBox
                     ├── JList
                     ├── JSlider
                     ├── JProgressBar
                     ├── JScrollPane
                     ├── JPanel
                     ├── JTabbedPane
                     └── (many more)
```

## 2.2 Top-Level Containers vs. General-Purpose Controls

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Distinction:**
> A `JFrame`, `JDialog`, and `JApplet` are **top-level containers** — they get their own window on the screen. Other Swing controls are *lightweight* and **must** be added to a top-level container via `add()` to become visible.

## 2.3 The MVC (Model-View-Controller) Architecture in Swing

Swing was the first Java library to fully embrace MVC. Each control is conceptually split:

| Layer | Responsibility | Example |
|-------|---------------|---------|
| **Model** | Holds the *data / state* | `DefaultListModel` for `JList` |
| **View** | Renders the data on screen | The visual `JList` component itself |
| **Controller** | Handles user input and updates model | `ListSelectionListener` |

This separation is **why** you can change the data in a `JList` programmatically and the view updates automatically — the view is *listening* to the model.

## 2.4 Event-Driven Programming — The Listener Pattern

Swing uses the **Delegation Event Model**. The flow is:

1. The user interacts with a control (e.g., clicks a `JButton`).
2. The JVM creates an *Event Object* (e.g., `ActionEvent`).
3. The event is *dispatched* to all registered **listeners**.
4. The listener's callback method executes the business logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule:** No event is ever processed unless a listener is registered. A `JButton` with no `ActionListener` does nothing on click — this is a favorite KTU 2-mark question.

## 2.5 Lightweight vs. Heavyweight Components

| Property | Heavyweight (AWT) | Lightweight (Swing) |
|----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Native peer | Yes (OS-dependent) | No (pure Java) |
| Look and Feel | Fixed by OS | Pluggable (Metal, Nimbus) |
| Performance | Slightly faster on native | Slightly slower but consistent |
| Class prefix | No prefix | Always `J` prefix |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Conceptual "Equations")

| Concept | "Equation" / Rule | Notes |
|---------|------------------|-------|
| Visibility of control | `Control must be inside a top-level container` | Adding a `JButton` to nothing → invisible |
| Event handling | `Source + Event + Listener` | All three required |
| Layout | `Container.setLayout(LayoutManager lm)` | Default is `BorderLayout` for `JFrame`, `FlowLayout` for `JPanel` |
| Setting text | `component.setText(String s)` | Universal for all text-displaying controls |
| Reading text | `component.getText()` | Returns `String` |
| Enabling/Disabling | `component.setEnabled(boolean b)` | Greyed-out if `false` |
| Visibility | `component.setVisible(boolean b)` | `true` makes it appear |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Swing is used in:
- **NetBeans IDE**, **IntelliJ IDEA** (older versions), **Eclipse RCP** — all built on Swing / SWT.
- Enterprise internal tools, banking dashboards, point-of-sale systems.
- KTU lab examinations — every GUI-based Java program in the syllabus uses Swing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Code Matrix

This section gives the exhaustive, runnable Java code for each major Swing control, the precise `import` statements, constructor signatures, and the *step-by-step logic* of how the control interacts with the user.

## 3.1 The Standard "Hello Swing" Skeleton (Used in Every Program)

**Step 1 — Import the required classes:**

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;        // top-level container
import javax.swing.JLabel;        // text display
import javax.swing.JButton;       // clickable button
import javax.swing.JTextField;    // single-line input
import javax.swing.JPanel;        // intermediate container
import java.awt.FlowLayout;       // default layout manager
import java.awt.event.ActionEvent;
import java.awt.event.ActionListener;
```

**Step 2 — Extend `JFrame` and define the constructor:**

```java
public class SwingDemo extends JFrame {
    SwingDemo() {
        setTitle("KTU Swing Demo");
        setSize(400, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        JLabel label = new JLabel("Enter your name: ");
        JTextField tf  = new JTextField(15);
        JButton btn    = new JButton("Greet");
        JLabel result  = new JLabel("");

        btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                result.setText("Hello, " + tf.getText() + "!");
            }
        });

        add(label); add(tf); add(btn); add(result);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SwingDemo();
    }
}
```

**Logic Walkthrough:**
1. `SwingDemo` *is-a* `JFrame` — inheritance is the standard KTU approach.
2. `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` — clicking the X closes the JVM.
3. `setLayout(new FlowLayout())` — components placed left-to-right.
4. `btn.addActionListener(...)` — **delegation event model** in action.
5. `actionPerformed` is the *callback* executed when the button is clicked.
6. `setVisible(true)` — **must be last**; otherwise components may not render.

## 3.2 Matrix of Every Major Swing Control

### 3.2.1 `JLabel` — Display-only text/image

| Property | Code |
|----------|------|
| Constructor | `JLabel l = new JLabel("Initial Text");` |
| Constructor (icon) | `JLabel l = new JLabel(new ImageIcon("logo.png"));` |
| Change text | `l.setText("New Text");` |
| Alignment | `l.setHorizontalAlignment(SwingConstants.CENTER);` |

### 3.2.2 `JButton` — Clickable button

| Property | Code |
|----------|------|
| Constructor | `JButton b = new JButton("Click Me");` |
| Add listener | `b.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Clicked"));` |
| Disable | `b.setEnabled(false);` |
| Icon | `b.setIcon(new ImageIcon("save.png"));` |

### 3.2.3 `JTextField` — Single-line input

| Property | Code |
|----------|------|
| Constructor | `JTextField tf = new JTextField(20);` // 20 columns wide |
| Read | `String s = tf.getText();` |
| Write | `tf.setText("default");` |
| Editable | `tf.setEditable(false);` |

### 3.2.4 `JTextArea` — Multi-line input

```java
JTextArea ta = new JTextArea(5, 20);                 // 5 rows, 20 columns
ta.setLineWrap(true);                                 // wrap long lines
JScrollPane sp = new JScrollPane(ta);                 // ALWAYS wrap in JScrollPane
add(sp);
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A `JTextArea` placed inside a `JScrollPane` automatically gains scrollbars — this is the only correct way to use it. KTU examiners will deduct marks for a bare `JTextArea` without scrolling.

### 3.2.5 `JCheckBox` — Independent toggle

```java
JCheckBox cb1 = new JCheckBox("Java", true);  // initially checked
JCheckBox cb2 = new JCheckBox("Python");
cb1.addItemListener(e -> {
    if (cb1.isSelected()) System.out.println("Java selected");
});
```

### 3.2.6 `JRadioButton` — Mutually exclusive choice (must group)

```java
JRadioButton r1 = new JRadioButton("Male");
JRadioButton r2 = new JRadioButton("Female");
ButtonGroup bg = new ButtonGroup();
bg.add(r1); bg.add(r2);          // CRITICAL: only one can be selected
add(r1); add(r2);
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting `ButtonGroup`. Without it, all `JRadioButton`s in the same `JFrame` act as **independent checkboxes**. The KTU examiner tests this trap every year.

### 3.2.7 `JComboBox` — Drop-down list

```java
String[] items = {"B.Tech", "M.Tech", "PhD"};
JComboBox<String> combo = new JComboBox<>(items);
combo.addActionListener(e ->
    System.out.println("Selected: " + combo.getSelectedItem()));
```

### 3.2.8 `JList` — Visible multi-row list

```java
String[] data = {"C", "C++", "Java", "Python"};
JList<String> list = new JList<>(data);
list.setSelectionMode(ListSelectionModel.SINGLE_SELECTION);
JScrollPane sp = new JScrollPane(list);    // wrap in JScrollPane
```

### 3.2.9 `JSlider` — Continuous/discrete value selection

```java
JSlider slider = new JSlider(JSlider.HORIZONTAL, 0, 100, 50);
slider.setMajorTickSpacing(10);
slider.setMinorTickSpacing(1);
slider.setPaintTicks(true);
slider.setPaintLabels(true);
```

### 3.2.10 `JMenuBar`, `JMenu`, `JMenuItem` — Menu system

```java
JMenuBar mb = new JMenuBar();
JMenu file = new JMenu("File");
JMenuItem open = new JMenuItem("Open");
JMenuItem save = new JMenuItem("Save");
JMenuItem exit = new JMenuItem("Exit");

exit.addActionListener(e -> System.exit(0));

file.add(open); file.add(save); file.addSeparator(); file.add(exit);
mb.add(file);
setJMenuBar(mb);     // <-- Note: setJMenuBar() not add()
```

> [!NOTE]
> `setJMenuBar()` is exclusive to `JFrame` (and `JDialog`/`JApplet`). Calling `add(mb)` will not work — another classic KTU trap.

### 3.2.11 `JTable` — Tabular data

```java
String[][] data = {
    {"1", "Alice", "95"},
    {"2", "Bob",   "87"}
};
String[] cols = {"ID", "Name", "Marks"};
JTable table = new JTable(data, cols);
JScrollPane sp = new JScrollPane(table);
add(sp);
```

### 3.2.12 `JProgressBar` — Visual progress indicator

```java
JProgressBar pb = new JProgressBar(0, 100);
pb.setValue(50);
pb.setStringPainted(true);    // shows "50%"
```

## 3.3 The Listener Interfaces Matrix

| Component | Event | Listener Interface | Callback Method |
|-----------|-------|--------------------|-----------------|
| `JButton` | Click | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` |
| `JCheckBox` | Toggle | `ItemListener` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent)` |
| `JTextField` | Enter pressed | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` |
| `JList` | Row selected | `ListSelectionListener` | `valueChanged(ListSelectionEvent)` |
| `JSlider` | Value changes | `ChangeListener` | `stateChanged(ChangeEvent)` |
| `JMenuItem` | Click | `ActionListener` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` |
| Window | Open/close | `WindowListener` | `windowClosing(WindowEvent)` etc. |
| Mouse | Click/enter | `MouseListener` | `mouseClicked(MouseEvent)` etc. |
| Keyboard | Key press | `KeyListener` | `keyTyped(KeyEvent)` etc. |

## 3.4 Adapter Classes — Shorthand for Empty Implementations

When you need only one of the seven methods of `MouseListener`, you extend `MouseAdapter` instead:

```java
panel.addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() {
    @Override
    public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) {
        System.out.println("Clicked at " + e.getPoint());
    }
});
```

This is the **preferred KTU-answer** approach because it avoids writing seven empty methods.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Swing Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java.lang.Object] --> B[java.awt.Component]
    B --> C[java.awt.Container]
    C --> D[javax.swing.JComponent]
    D --> E1[JLabel]
    D --> E2[JButton]
    D --> E3[JTextField]
    D --> E4[JTextArea]
    D --> E5[JCheckBox]
    D --> E6[JRadioButton]
    D --> E7[JComboBox]
    D --> E8[JList]
    D --> E9[JSlider]
    D --> E10[JProgressBar]
    D --> E11[JPanel]
    D --> E12[JScrollPane]
    D --> E13[JTable]
    D --> E14[JTree]
    E2 --> E15[JMenuItem]
    E15 --> E16[JMenu]
    E16 --> E17[JMenuBar]
    C --> F1[JFrame]
    C --> F2[JDialog]
    F1 --> G1[Top Level Container]
    F2 --> G1
```

## 4.2 MVC Architecture of a JList (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    User[User clicks a row] --> ListView[JList - View]
    ListView --> LSL[ListSelectionListener - Controller]
    LSL --> Model[DefaultListModel - Model]
    Model --> ListView
    ListView --> Display[Updated visual selection]
```

**How to read this:** The user interacts with the **View**. The view triggers a **Controller** (listener). The controller updates the **Model**. The model fires a change event back to the view, which re-renders. This is the *Observer pattern* in action.

## 4.3 Event Delegation Model (Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[User clicks JButton] --> B[JVM creates ActionEvent object]
    B --> C[Event dispatched to registered listeners]
    C --> D{Is an ActionListener registered?}
    D -- Yes --> E[actionPerformed method is called]
    E --> F[Business logic executes]
    D -- No --> G[Nothing happens silently]
    F --> H[UI repaints if needed]
```

## 4.4 Layout Managers — Visual Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Layout[java.awt.LayoutManager Interface] --> L1[FlowLayout]
    Layout --> L2[BorderLayout]
    Layout --> L3[GridLayout]
    Layout --> L4[GridBagLayout]
    Layout --> L5[BoxLayout]
    Layout --> L6[CardLayout]
    Layout --> L7[GroupLayout]
    L1 --> Z1[Components flow left to right]
    L2 --> Z2[North South East West Center]
    L3 --> Z3[Equal sized cells in a grid]
    L4 --> Z4[Flexible grid with constraints]
    L5 --> Z5[Vertical or horizontal box]
    L6 --> Z6[Stack of panels shown one at a time]
    L7 --> Z7[Used by NetBeans GUI Builder Matisse]
```

## 4.5 Container Hierarchy Inside a Typical JFrame

```mermaid
graph TD
    Frame[JFrame - top level] --> CP[Content Pane]
    CP --> Panel1[JPanel North]
    CP --> Panel2[JPanel Center]
    CP --> Panel3[JPanel South]
    Panel1 --> L1[JLabel Title]
    Panel2 --> SP[JScrollPane]
    SP --> TA[JTextArea]
    Panel3 --> B1[JButton Submit]
    Panel3 --> B2[JButton Reset]
    Frame --> MB[JMenuBar]
    MB --> M1[JMenu File]
    M1 --> MI1[JMenuItem Open]
    M1 --> MI2[JMenuItem Save]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between AWT and Swing components. List any two advantages of Swing over AWT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | AWT | Swing |
|---------|-----|-------|
| Package | `java.awt` | `javax.swing` |
| Class prefix | No prefix | Always `J` prefix |
| Component type | Heavyweight (native peers) | Lightweight (pure Java) |
| Look and Feel | OS-dependent | Pluggable (Metal, Nimbus, etc.) |
| Rich components | Limited | `JTable`, `JTree` available |

**Advantages of Swing (any 2):**
1. Pluggable Look and Feel allows the same application to appear native on Windows, macOS, and Linux without code changes.
2. Lightweight components are written purely in Java, providing platform independence and richer functionality (e.g., double-buffering for flicker-free rendering).

*(1 mark for differentiation table, 1 mark each for two advantages)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the Delegation Event Model used in Java Swing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Delegation Event Model** is the mechanism by which Swing handles user interactions. Its workflow:

1. **Source:** The control on which the event occurs (e.g., a `JButton`) is called the *event source*.
2. **Event Object:** When the user interacts, the JVM creates an event object (e.g., `ActionEvent`) containing details of the interaction.
3. **Listener Registration:** The source maintains a list of registered *listeners* (objects implementing listener interfaces like `ActionListener`).
4. **Event Dispatching:** The event is *dispatched* (delegated) to every registered listener automatically.
5. **Callback Execution:** Each listener's callback method (e.g., `actionPerformed`) is invoked to process the event.

**Key benefit:** The source and the handler are *loosely coupled* — the button does not need to know what code will run when clicked.

*(1 mark for source + listener concept, 1 mark for event dispatching, 1 mark for callback)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the MVC architecture used by Swing components with a suitable diagram. How does it help in separating concerns? (7 marks)

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a form that accepts a student's **name**, **age**, and **gender** (using `JRadioButton`), and displays a greeting message when a button is clicked. Use appropriate layout. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**MVC in Swing** stands for **Model-View-Controller**, a design pattern that separates the application into three interconnected parts:

- **Model:** Stores the *data* and the *state* of the component. It is unaware of how the data is displayed. *Example:* `DefaultListModel` for a `JList`, `ButtonModel` for a `JButton`.
- **View:** The *visual representation* rendered on screen. The view queries the model to know what to display. *Example:* The actual `JList` widget painting the strings.
- **Controller:** Handles *user input* (mouse, keyboard) and translates it into changes to the model. *Example:* `ListSelectionListener` reacting to a row click.

**Separation of Concerns Benefits:**
1. **Multiple views of the same data:** A single `DefaultTableModel` can be displayed in two `JTable`s side by side; changing the model updates both views automatically.
2. **Easier maintenance:** UI changes do not require touching business logic, and vice versa.
3. **Reusability:** The same model can be plugged into different views.
4. **Testability:** Models can be unit-tested without a UI.

```mermaid
graph LR
    M[Model] --> V[View]
    V --> C[Controller]
    C --> M
```

*(2 marks architecture description, 3 marks roles of M-V-C, 2 marks benefits + diagram)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class StudentForm extends JFrame {
    StudentForm() {
        setTitle("Student Form");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        JLabel nameLbl = new JLabel("Name:");
        JTextField nameTf = new JTextField(15);

        JLabel ageLbl = new JLabel("Age:");
        JTextField ageTf = new JTextField(5);

        JLabel genLbl = new JLabel("Gender:");
        JRadioButton male   = new JRadioButton("Male");
        JRadioButton female = new JRadioButton("Female");
        ButtonGroup bg = new ButtonGroup();
        bg.add(male); bg.add(female);

        JButton btn = new JButton("Greet");
        JLabel result = new JLabel("");

        btn.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
            public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
                String g = male.isSelected() ? "Male" : "Female";
                result.setText("Hello " + nameTf.getText()
                             + ", age " + ageTf.getText()
                             + ", " + g);
            }
        });

        add(nameLbl); add(nameTf);
        add(ageLbl);  add(ageTf);
        add(genLbl);  add(male); add(female);
        add(btn);     add(result);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new StudentForm();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of key steps:**
1. `ButtonGroup` ensures only one of `male`/`female` can be selected at a time.
2. `nameTf.getText()` and `ageTf.getText()` retrieve user input.
3. `male.isSelected()` returns `true`/`false` based on radio button state.
4. `result.setText(...)` updates the label with the greeting.
5. `setVisible(true)` is the last call to ensure all components render properly.

*(1 mark imports, 1 mark JFrame setup, 1 mark each for name/age/gender controls, 2 marks ActionListener logic, 1 mark for output label, 1 mark for execution)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the Swing class hierarchy. List any four differences between heavyweight and lightweight components. (7 marks)

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program that uses `JCheckBox`, `JList`, and `JComboBox` to allow a user to select programming languages, and displays the selections on clicking a `JButton`. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Swing Class Hierarchy (Diagram):**

```
java.lang.Object
   └── java.awt.Component
         └── java.awt.Container
               └── javax.swing.JComponent
                     ├── JLabel
                     ├── JButton
                     ├── JTextField
                     ├── JTextArea
                     ├── JCheckBox
                     ├── JRadioButton
                     ├── JComboBox
                     ├── JList
                     ├── JPanel
                     ├── JScrollPane
                     └── (etc.)
JFrame  ─── extends Frame (top-level container)
JDialog ─── extends Dialog (top-level container)
```

**Heavyweight vs Lightweight Components (any 4 differences):**

| # | Heavyweight (AWT) | Lightweight (Swing) |
|---|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Has a native OS peer | No native peer, drawn by Java |
| 2 | Fixed look and feel | Pluggable look and feel |
| 3 | Lower cross-platform consistency | Identical appearance on all OS |
| 4 | Fewer controls (no `JTable`, `JTree`) | Rich set of advanced controls |
| 5 | Classes in `java.awt` | Classes in `javax.swing` |

*(2 marks hierarchy diagram, 1 mark root identification, 4 × 1 mark for differences)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class LangSelector extends JFrame {
    LangSelector() {
        setTitle("Language Selector");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        // JCheckBox group
        JCheckBox cb1 = new JCheckBox("C++");
        JCheckBox cb2 = new JCheckBox("Java");
        JCheckBox cb3 = new JCheckBox("Python");

        // JList
        String[] listData = {"Frontend", "Backend", "Database", "DevOps"};
        JList<String> list = new JList<>(listData);
        list.setVisibleRowCount(3);
        JScrollPane listSp = new JScrollPane(list);

        // JComboBox
        String[] comboData = {"Beginner", "Intermediate", "Advanced"};
        JComboBox<String> combo = new JComboBox<>(comboData);

        JButton btn = new JButton("Show Selection");
        JTextArea result = new JTextArea(5, 30);
        JScrollPane resultSp = new JScrollPane(result);

        btn.addActionListener(e -> {
            StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Languages: ");
            if (cb1.isSelected()) sb.append("C++ ");
            if (cb2.isSelected()) sb.append("Java ");
            if (cb3.isSelected()) sb.append("Python ");
            sb.append("\nRoles: ");
            for (String s : list.getSelectedValuesList()) sb.append(s).append(" ");
            sb.append("\nLevel: ").append(combo.getSelectedItem());
            result.setText(sb.toString());
        });

        add(cb1); add(cb2); add(cb3);
        add(new JLabel("Roles:")); add(listSp);
        add(new JLabel("Level:")); add(combo);
        add(btn); add(resultSp);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LangSelector();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Three `JCheckBox`es allow multiple language selections.
2. A `JList` inside a `JScrollPane` lets the user pick one or more roles.
3. A `JComboBox` is used to choose a difficulty level.
4. The `ActionListener` builds a `StringBuilder` summarizing all selections and pushes it into a `JTextArea` wrapped in a `JScrollPane`.
5. `getSelectedValuesList()` (note the plural) returns a `List<String>` of all selected rows in the `JList`.

*(1 mark imports, 1 mark JCheckBox setup, 1 mark JList + scroll, 1 mark JComboBox, 2 marks ActionListener logic, 1 mark for output display)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Swing Programs:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`:** The program compiles and runs, but the window never appears. Examiners give **zero** for output if the window is not visible in screenshots.
> 2. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`:** The window closes but `main` does not terminate, and the IDE keeps the process alive. Often marked as "incomplete program".
> 3. **Adding `JMenuBar` with `add()` instead of `setJMenuBar()`:** Menus do not appear. Lose 1 mark immediately.
> 4. **Forgetting `ButtonGroup` for `JRadioButton`:** All radios act as checkboxes. Lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Placing a `JTextArea` or `JList` without `JScrollPane`:** Program works but UI is unprofessional. KTU examiners specifically check this.
> 6. **Setting layout but not using it correctly:** E.g., `setLayout(null)` and then *not* calling `setBounds()` on every component. Result: all components stack at `(0,0)`.
> 7. **Lambda vs anonymous class inconsistency:** Mixing Java 8 lambdas (`e -> ...`) with anonymous inner classes in the same program — while legal, KTU prefers **one consistent style**.
> 8. **Missing `import javax.swing.*;`:** A `JFrame` declared without the import throws a compile error. Lose the entire question's marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam):**

- ☐ Every Swing class starts with **`J`** (this is the *one* rule that never breaks).
- ☐ A Swing component is **invisible** until added to a top-level container (`JFrame`/`JDialog`/`JApplet`) *and* `setVisible(true)` is called.
- ☐ Default `JFrame` layout is `BorderLayout`; default `JPanel` layout is `FlowLayout`.
- ☐ The **Delegation Event Model** requires three things: a **Source**, an **Event**, and a **Listener**.
- ☐ `ActionListener` is for click / Enter-key events; `ItemListener` is for toggle state changes.
- ☐ `ButtonGroup` is mandatory for mutually exclusive `JRadioButton`s.
- ☐ `setJMenuBar()` (not `add()`) attaches a menu bar to a `JFrame`.
- ☐ Wrap `JTextArea` and `JList` in `JScrollPane` for scrollable UI.
- ☐ `JTable(data, columns)` is the simplest constructor; for production code, use `DefaultTableModel`.
- ☐ Swing components are **lightweight**; AWT components are **heavyweight** with native peers.
- ☐ MVC = **Model** (data) + **View** (UI) + **Controller** (listener).
- ☐ `MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter` are convenience classes that pre-implement listener interfaces with empty methods.
- ☐ Use `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new MyFrame())` to ensure thread safety when launching Swing apps from `main` (advanced but a bonus KTU point).
- ☐ `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` terminates the JVM; `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` closes only the window; `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (default) just hides it.
- ☐ `JComboBox.getSelectedItem()` returns `Object`; cast to `String` if needed, or use generics `JComboBox<String>`.
- ☐ `JList.getSelectedValue()` returns single `Object`; `getSelectedValuesList()` returns `List<Object>`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components and Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components and Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Component (Java AWT/Swing Definition):**
> An abstract class `java.awt.Component` is the **root of the AWT component hierarchy** and the **superclass** for all GUI (Graphical User Interface) objects that can be displayed on the screen and can interact with the user. Anything that has a graphical representation (buttons, text fields, labels, panels, frames) is a `Component`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Container (Java AWT/Swing Definition):**
> A `Container` is a special type of `Component` that has the ability to **hold and organise other components** (including other containers). Formally, `java.awt.Container` is a direct subclass of `java.awt.Component` and defines methods such as `add()`, `remove()`, `setLayout()`, and `getComponents()` for managing the contained child components.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Furniture and Room" Model

Imagine a **house** as a GUI application.

| Real-World Object | Java GUI Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The **House itself** | A `Top-Level Container` (e.g., `JFrame`, `Frame`) |
| The **Rooms** inside the house | `Intermediate Containers` (e.g., `JPanel`, `Panel`) |
| The **Furniture** (table, chair, TV) | `Atomic Components` (e.g., `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`) |
| The **architect's floor plan** that decides where each item is placed | `LayoutManager` (e.g., `FlowLayout`, `BorderLayout`) |

So a **Container** is like a **room** that can hold furniture (components), and a **Component** is anything you can visually *see* and *interact with* on the screen. Crucially, *every container is itself a component* (because rooms have walls, doors, windows — they are part of the house too).

### 1.3 The Two Fundamental Categories

> [!NOTE]
> **Heavyweight Components (HWCs):** Components that rely on the underlying **Operating System's native peer** for their rendering and behaviour. Examples: `java.awt.Button`, `java.awt.TextField`, `Frame`, `Dialog`. These are tied to the local platform's look-and-feel.
>
> **Lightweight Components (LWCs):** Components that have **no native peer** and render themselves purely using the Java graphics subsystem. Examples: Most `javax.swing.*` components (e.g., `JButton`, `JLabel`). They are **100% platform-independent** in appearance and obey the "pluggable look-and-feel" principle.

### 1.4 Physical/Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Component Hierarchy Depth:** A typical Swing GUI has a hierarchy of **3 to 6 levels** (RootPane → LayeredPane → ContentPane → JPanel → Atomic Components).
- **The "add()" Rule:** A component can be added to **only one container at a time** (in AWT). Re-adding it automatically removes it from the previous container.
- **Top-Level Containers:** Always heavyweight; serve as the **root** of any Java GUI tree.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `Component` Class — Why It Exists and How It Works

The `Component` class is **abstract**, meaning you cannot directly instantiate it via `new Component()`. It provides the **universal properties** that *every* visible GUI element must possess:

**A. Geometric (Position & Size) Properties**
- `setLocation(int x, int y)` — places the component at a pixel coordinate relative to its parent container.
- `setSize(int width, int height)` — defines the component's dimensions in **pixels**.
- `setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` — combined operation; a "**4-in-1**" setter.
- `getX()`, `getY()`, `getWidth()`, `getHeight()` — read-back accessors.

**B. Visual (Appearance) Properties**
- `setBackground(Color c)` — sets the background colour.
- `setForeground(Color c)` — sets the foreground (typically text) colour.
- `setFont(Font f)` — sets the typography (name, style, size).
- `setCursor(Cursor c)` — changes the mouse cursor when over the component.

**C. State (Behavioural) Properties**
- `setEnabled(boolean b)` — greys out and disables interaction when `false`.
- `setVisible(boolean b)` — shows/hides the component.
- `setFocusable(boolean b)` — whether the component can receive keyboard focus.

**Why this design?** By placing all these universal methods in a *single* superclass, Java's designers ensured that **every GUI element** (whether a button, scrollbar, or canvas) inherits the same predictable interface. This is a textbook example of the OO principle of **generalisation/specialisation**.

### 2.2 The `Container` Class — Extending Component with "Containment"

`Container` inherits from `Component` and adds the ability to *hold children*. Its critical methods are:

| Method | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `add(Component comp)` | `Component add(Component comp)` | Appends a child component to the container |
| `add(Component comp, Object constraints)` | ... | Adds with layout constraints (e.g., `BorderLayout.NORTH`) |
| `remove(Component comp)` / `remove(int index)` | `void` | Removes a child |
| `removeAll()` | `void` | Empties the container |
| `getComponentCount()` | `int` | Returns the number of children |
| `getComponent(int n)` | `Component` | Returns the n-th child |
| `getComponents()` | `Component[]` | Returns **array** of all children |
| `setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` | `void` | Installs a layout manager |
| `validate()` | `void` | Recalculates layout after dynamic changes |
