| `findComponentAt(int x, int y)` | `Component` | Returns the deepest component at a point |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "How" Behind `add()`:** Internally, when `add()` is called, the container appends the child to an internal `java.util.Vector` and registers itself as the child's *parent*. If the child already had a parent, the previous parent automatically removes it. This enforces the strict **one-parent rule** in AWT.

### 2.3 Top-Level Containers — The "Roots" of Any GUI

A **top-level container** is a container that **cannot be contained inside another container**. It is the *root* of a component tree. In AWT: `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Applet` (deprecated). In Swing: `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`, `JWindow`.

Every Swing top-level container has a special internal structure:

```
JFrame
 ├── Root Pane (JRootPane)
 │    ├── Glass Pane (JPanel)         ← captures all mouse events
 │    ├── Layered Pane (JLayeredPane) ← depth-layered surface
 │    │    └── Content Pane (JPanel)  ← ← ← YOU ADD COMPONENTS HERE
 │    └── Menu Bar (JMenuBar)         ← optional
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Components are added to the **Content Pane** of a top-level container — **NOT** directly to the `JFrame`. This is a common exam pitfall. You obtain the content pane via `frame.getContentPane().add(component)` (or simply `frame.add(component)` in Java 5+, which delegates to the content pane).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield "Cheat Sheet" — Component vs Container

| Feature | `Component` | `Container` |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt` | `java.awt` |
| Inheritance | `Object` → `Component` (abstract) | `Object` → `Component` → `Container` |
| Can be added to a Container? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (containers are components) |
| Can hold other components? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Key added methods | `setLocation`, `setSize`, `setVisible` | `add`, `remove`, `setLayout`, `getComponents` |
| Instantiable? | ❌ Abstract | ❌ Abstract (but `Panel`/`Frame` are concrete) |
| Real-world analogy | Furniture item | A room or shelf holding items |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Component/Container architecture is the foundation for:
- **Desktop IDEs** (Eclipse, IntelliJ, NetBeans — all built using Swing/JavaFX)
- **Enterprise Dashboards** (Bloomberg terminals, ATM UIs)
- **Cross-Platform Tools** (JetBrains Toolbox, JProfiler)
- **Embedded Java GUIs** (Raspberry Pi dashboards using Pi4J + Swing)

The principle is identical across modern frameworks: **React (Web)** uses a virtual DOM tree of *components* that can be *containers* (via children props). **Flutter** uses *Widgets* in a tree. The Java AWT/Swing model pioneered this recursive composition pattern.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a Computer Science / Object-Oriented topic, the "derivation" is adapted to **code-based walkthroughs** that derive the runtime behaviour of component-containment step-by-step. Each line is annotated to show its effect.

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough #1: Constructing a Container Tree

**Problem:** Build a 3-level GUI tree — `JFrame` → `JPanel` → `JButton` + `JLabel` — and trace what happens internally at each `add()` call.

**Java Code (the derivation):**
```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class ContainerDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // STEP 1: Create the top-level container (heavyweight root)
        JFrame frame = new JFrame("KTU Demo");
        frame.setSize(400, 200);
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // STEP 2: Create an intermediate container (lightweight panel)
        JPanel panel = new JPanel();
        panel.setBackground(Color.LIGHT_GRAY);

        // STEP 3: Create atomic components (leaves of the tree)
        JLabel label = new JLabel("Welcome to KTU");
        JButton button = new JButton("Click Me");

        // STEP 4: Add atomic components to the intermediate container
        panel.add(label);    // JPanel (Container) accepts JLabel (Component)
        panel.add(button);

        // STEP 5: Add the intermediate container to the top-level container
        frame.add(panel);    // delegates to frame.getContentPane().add(panel)

        // STEP 6: Make the top-level container visible
        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step Derivation of the Runtime Tree:**

| Step | Operation | Internal Effect | Resulting Tree Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `new JFrame("KTU Demo")` | Creates root; internally builds `JRootPane` → `LayeredPane` → `ContentPane` | `JFrame` → `ContentPane` |
| 2 | `new JPanel()` | Creates a lightweight container (no native peer) | (detached node) |
| 3 | `new JLabel(...)`, `new JButton(...)` | Creates atomic components (no children allowed) | (detached leaves) |
| 4 | `panel.add(label)` | `label.parent = panel`; `panel.children.add(label)` | `JPanel` → `JLabel` |
| 4 | `panel.add(button)` | `button.parent = panel`; `panel.children.add(button)` | `JPanel` → `JButton` |
| 5 | `frame.add(panel)` | Internally calls `frame.getContentPane().add(panel)`; sets `panel.parent = contentPane` | `JFrame` → `ContentPane` → `JPanel` → `[JLabel, JButton]` |
| 6 | `frame.setVisible(true)` | Triggers `validate()` and `doLayout()`; the **LayoutManager** (default `BorderLayout` for content pane, `FlowLayout` for JPanel) places children | Tree is rendered to screen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the tree matters:** When the user clicks the button, Java uses this tree to **dispatch events** from the OS down to the deepest component under the cursor, and then up the chain via *event listeners*. Without a correctly constructed tree, no event can reach the component.

### 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough #2: Proving the "One Parent" Rule

**Problem:** Demonstrate that adding a component to a second container automatically removes it from the first.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class OneParentRule {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        JFrame f1 = new JFrame("Frame 1");
        JFrame f2 = new JFrame("Frame 2");
        JButton btn = new JButton("Movable Button");

        f1.add(btn);           // Step A: btn is now in f1
        f1.setSize(200, 100);
        f1.setVisible(true);

        f2.add(btn);           // Step B: btn is *automatically removed* from f1 and added to f2
        f2.setSize(200, 100);
        f2.setLocation(220, 0);
        f2.setVisible(true);

        // Verification:
        System.out.println("f1 has " + f1.getContentPane().getComponentCount() + " children");
        // OUTPUT: f1 has 0 children

        System.out.println("f2 has " + f2.getContentPane().getComponentCount() + " children");
        // OUTPUT: f2 has 1 child
    }
}
```

**Derivation of the Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After Step A: } & f_1.\text{children} = [\text{btn}], \quad f_2.\text{children} = [] \\
\text{After Step B: } & \text{add() detects } \text{btn.parent} = f_1, \text{ so it calls } f_1.\text{remove(btn)} \\
& \text{then sets } \text{btn.parent} = f_2 \text{ and appends to } f_2.\text{children} \\
\text{Final State: } & f_1.\text{children} = [], \quad f_2.\text{children} = [\text{btn}]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **automatic re-parenting** behaviour is a feature of `Container.add()` defined in the JDK source. Internally, it is roughly: `if (comp.parent != null) comp.parent.remove(comp); comp.parent = this;`.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Methods That Modify the Tree vs Those That Modify Appearance

| Method | Category | Affects Tree Structure? | Affects Pixel Rendering? |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| `setSize()` | Geometric | ❌ | ✅ |
| `setLocation()` | Geometric | ❌ | ✅ |
| `setVisible(true)` | State | ❌ | ✅ |
| `setEnabled(false)` | State | ❌ | ✅ |
| `setBackground(c)` | Visual | ❌ | ✅ |
| `setFont(f)` | Visual | ❌ | ✅ |
| `add(c)` | Structural | ✅ | ✅ (via validate) |
| `remove(c)` | Structural | ✅ | ✅ (via validate) |
| `removeAll()` | Structural | ✅ | ✅ (via validate) |
| `setLayout(mgr)` | Structural | ❌ | ✅ (on next layout pass) |
| `validate()` | Structural | ❌ | ✅ (forces re-layout) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this distinction matters for KTU exams:** When asked "which method removes a component from the container?" — the answer is **`remove()`**, not `setVisible(false)` (which only *hides* it; it is still logically a child).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Component/Container Class Hierarchy (AWT + Swing)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["java.lang.Object"] --> B["java.awt.Component (abstract)"]
    B --> C["java.awt.Container (abstract)"]
    B --> D["java.awt.MenuComponent"]
    C --> E["java.awt.Window"]
    C --> F["java.awt.Panel"]
    C --> G["java.awt.ScrollPane"]
    E --> H["java.awt.Frame"]
    E --> I["java.awt.Dialog"]
    F --> J["java.applet.Applet (deprecated)"]
    H --> K["javax.swing.JFrame"]
    I --> L["javax.swing.JDialog"]
    F --> M["javax.swing.JPanel"]
    B --> N["Atomic Components"]
    N --> O["java.awt.Button / javax.swing.JButton"]
    N --> P["java.awt.Label / javax.swing.JLabel"]
    N --> Q["java.awt.TextField / javax.swing.JTextField"]
    N --> R["java.awt.Checkbox / javax.swing.JCheckBox"]
    N --> S["java.awt.Choice / javax.swing.JComboBox"]

    style A fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#ffe082,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#81d4fa,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333
    style M fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333
    style O fill:#ffab91,stroke:#333
    style P fill:#ffab91,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Yellow box** (`Component`) — abstract root; cannot be instantiated.
- **Blue box** (`Container`) — also abstract; extends Component with the *containment* capability.
- **Green boxes** — concrete top-level and intermediate containers you can `new`.
- **Orange boxes** — concrete atomic (leaf) components that can only be *leaves* in the tree.

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Internal Structure of a `JFrame`

```mermaid
graph TD
    F["JFrame (Heavyweight)"] --> RP["JRootPane"]
    RP --> LP["JLayeredPane"]
    RP --> GP["GlassPane (JPanel, transparent)"]
    LP --> CP["ContentPane (JPanel, default BorderLayout)"]
    LP --> MB["JMenuBar (optional, sits in layered pane)"]
    CP --> P1["JPanel (your custom panel)"]
    P1 --> B1["JButton (atomic)"]
    P1 --> L1["JLabel (atomic)"]
    P1 --> T1["JTextField (atomic)"]

    style F fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333
    style RP fill:#fff59d,stroke:#333
    style LP fill:#fff59d,stroke:#333
    style CP fill:#fff59d,stroke:#333
    style P1 fill:#81d4fa,stroke:#333
    style B1 fill:#ffab91,stroke:#333
    style L1 fill:#ffab91,stroke:#333
    style T1 fill:#ffab91,stroke:#333
```

**Functional Flow Explanation:**

1. `JFrame` (the OS window) hosts a `JRootPane`.
2. `JRootPane` overlays a `GlassPane` (used to intercept all events, e.g., for modal dialogs).
3. The `LayeredPane` manages **z-order** (which component appears on top of another) and contains both the `ContentPane` and optional `JMenuBar`.
4. The **Content Pane** is where **your components actually go** when you call `frame.add(c)`.
5. Custom panels you add become intermediate containers; atomic components (button, label, text field) become leaves.

### 4.3 Event Propagation Flow — How the Tree Enables Interactivity

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OS as Operating System
    participant F as JFrame (HW)
    participant P as JPanel
    participant B as JButton
    participant L as ActionListener

    OS->>F: Mouse click at (x, y)
    F->>F: Convert to local coordinates
    F->>P: Dispatch to child at point
    P->>B: Dispatch to leaf under cursor
    B->>B: Fire ActionEvent
    B->>L: Notify registered listener
    L-->>OS: (your code runs)
```

**Why this diagram is KTU-High-Yield:** Examiners frequently ask *"How does an event reach a specific component?"* The answer is the **tree-walk dispatch** illustrated above. Without the component-containment hierarchy, there would be no defined path for events to traverse.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a Component and a Container in Java AWT. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Component | Container |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | The abstract superclass of all GUI elements that can be displayed on the screen | A special Component that can hold and organise other Components |
| Containment | **Cannot** hold other components | **Can** hold other components (including other containers) |
| Package | `java.awt.Component` | `java.awt.Container` (subclass of Component) |
| Example | `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, `Checkbox` | `Frame`, `Panel`, `Dialog`, `Applet` |

Inheritance relationship: `Container extends Component`, meaning every Container **is-a** Component and inherits all its methods (like `setSize`, `setLocation`, `setVisible`) while adding its own (like `add`, `remove`, `setLayout`). *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for inheritance relationship)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. What is a heavyweight component? Why are top-level containers in Swing always heavyweight?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **heavyweight component** is a GUI element that is rendered using the **host operating system's native windowing toolkit** (also called a "native peer"). For example, `java.awt.Frame` is heavyweight because each instance corresponds to an actual OS-level window resource.

Top-level containers in Swing (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`, `JWindow`) are **always heavyweight** because:
1. They must be hosted by the OS's window manager (taskbar, alt-tab, minimize/restore) — only native windows can integrate with these.
2. They serve as the *root* of the component tree; if they were lightweight, they would have no native parent to render onto.
3. Mixing only lightweight children under a heavyweight root is allowed and is the **standard Swing pattern** (called the "mixed component" approach). *(1 mark definition + 2 marks for the three reasons)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper, Module 4]**

### 🅰️ **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose A or B**

**(a)** Explain the class hierarchy of `Component` and `Container` in Java AWT with a neat diagram. List any **six** important methods of the `Component` class along with their purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program using Swing that creates a `JFrame` containing a `JPanel`. Inside the `JPanel`, add a `JLabel`, a `JTextField`, and a `JButton`. Set appropriate sizes and make the frame visible. Explain each `add()` call and which container/component it affects. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Class Hierarchy (Diagram — 3 marks):**

```
java.lang.Object
       ↑
java.awt.Component (abstract)         ← root of AWT hierarchy
       ↑
       ├── java.awt.MenuComponent      ← (separate branch for menus)
       |
java.awt.Container (abstract)         ← can hold other components
       ↑
       ├── java.awt.Window
       │       ├── java.awt.Frame
       │       │       └── javax.swing.JFrame
       │       └── java.awt.Dialog
       │               └── javax.swing.JDialog
       ├── java.awt.Panel
       │       └── javax.swing.JPanel
       ├── java.awt.ScrollPane
       └── java.applet.Applet
```

Other concrete `Component` subclasses (leaves, not containers): `Button`, `Label`, `TextField`, `TextArea`, `Checkbox`, `Choice`, `List`, `Canvas`.

**Six Important Methods of `Component` (4 marks — 0.5 mark each method + 0.5 for purpose):**

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `void setSize(int w, int h)` | Sets the component's width and height in pixels |
| `void setLocation(int x, int y)` | Sets the component's top-left position relative to its parent |
| `void setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` | Combined call to set position and size in one operation |
| `void setVisible(boolean b)` | Shows (`true`) or hides (`false`) the component on screen |
| `void setEnabled(boolean b)` | Enables (`true`) or greys-out and disables (`false`) the component |
| `void setBackground(Color c)` | Sets the background colour of the component |
| `void setForeground(Color c)` | Sets the foreground (typically text) colour |
| `void setFont(Font f)` | Sets the typography (face, style, size) of the component's text |

*(Any six are accepted; the above are the most frequently tested.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Java Program (5 marks):**
```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class SwingDemo extends JFrame {
    JLabel lbl;
    JTextField tf;
    JButton btn;

    SwingDemo() {
        // Configure the top-level container
        setTitle("KTU Swing Demo");
        setSize(400, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // Create intermediate container
        JPanel panel = new JPanel();
        panel.setBackground(Color.CYAN);

        // Create atomic (leaf) components
        lbl = new JLabel("Name:");
        tf  = new JTextField(15);
        btn = new JButton("Submit");

        // Add leaves to intermediate container
        panel.add(lbl);     // 1
        panel.add(tf);      // 2
        panel.add(btn);     // 3

        // Add intermediate container to top-level container's content pane
        add(panel);         // 4  (delegates to getContentPane().add(panel))

        setVisible(true);   // 5
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new SwingDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of each `add()` call (2 marks — 0.5 per call):**

| Call | Effect on the Tree |
|---|---|
| `panel.add(lbl)` | `lbl` is appended to `panel.children`; `lbl.parent = panel` |
| `panel.add(tf)` | `tf` is appended to `panel.children`; `tf.parent = panel` |
| `panel.add(btn)` | `btn` is appended to `panel.children`; `btn.parent = panel` |
| `add(panel)` (on `JFrame`) | Internally calls `getContentPane().add(panel)`; `panel.parent = contentPane` |

**Final rendered tree:**
`JFrame` → `JRootPane` → `LayeredPane` → `ContentPane` → `JPanel` → `[JLabel, JTextField, JButton]`

---

### 🅱️ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the difference between **heavyweight** and **lightweight** components. Explain why Swing components are mostly lightweight and how they are rendered without a native peer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a LayoutManager? Explain the role of `setLayout()`, `validate()`, and `getComponents()` methods of the `Container` class with suitable code snippets. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Heavyweight vs Lightweight Components (5 marks):**

| Aspect | Heavyweight | Lightweight |
|---|---|---|
| Native peer | Has an **OS-native peer** | **No** native peer |
| Rendering | Done by the **OS** | Done by the **Java graphics subsystem** |
| Platform look-and-feel | Tied to the host OS (Win/Mac/Linux differ) | Uniform across platforms (PLAF: Metal, Nimbus, etc.) |
| Performance | Faster (uses OS optimisations) | Slightly slower (pure Java drawing) |
| Z-ordering | Each has its own OS-level z-index | Lives on a single heavyweight parent's z-stack |
| Examples | `java.awt.Button`, `Frame`, `Dialog` | `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JPanel`, `JTextField` |

**Why Swing components are mostly lightweight (2 marks):**

1. **Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF):** To allow Java GUIs to look identical on Windows, macOS, and Linux, components must not depend on the OS's native widgets. Hence, Swing draws them itself.
2. **The "single heavyweight root" pattern:** Every Swing app has exactly one (or few) heavyweight top-level container (e.g., `JFrame`), and all other components are lightweight children drawn *within* that native window's surface using Java2D. This achieves portability without losing OS integration (taskbar, minimise/restore buttons).
3. **Performance optimisations** (e.g., clipping, double-buffering in `JComponent`/`RepaintManager`) make Java drawing fast enough for everyday GUIs.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

Wait — for Question B(b):

**LayoutManager Definition (1 mark):**
A `LayoutManager` is an object that implements the `java.awt.LayoutManager` interface and is responsible for **automatically positioning and sizing** the child components of a `Container` according to a specific strategy (flow, border, grid, etc.).

**The three methods (6 marks):**

**(i) `setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` (2 marks):**
Installs a layout strategy for the container.
```java
JPanel p = new JPanel();
p.setLayout(new BorderLayout());      // 5-region strategy
// or
p.setLayout(new GridLayout(3, 2));    // 3x2 grid
// or
p.setLayout(null);                    // absolute positioning (rare, not recommended)
```

**(ii) `validate()` (2 marks):**
Forces the container to **re-layout** its children. Used after dynamic changes (e.g., adding/removing components at runtime).
```java
panel.add(new JLabel("New"));
panel.validate();   // recalculates positions of ALL children
```

**(iii) `getComponents()` (2 marks):**
Returns a `Component[]` array of all direct children of the container. Useful for iteration.
```java
Component[] children = panel.getComponents();
for (Component c : children) {
    System.out.println(c.getClass().getSimpleName());
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Content Pane trap:** Writing `frame.add(c)` is *technically* allowed (since Java 5, it delegates to the content pane), but explicitly calling `frame.getContentPane().add(c)` is the **safe, exam-correct** way. Examiners often deduct 0.5–1 mark for ambiguity.
> 2. **Confusing `setVisible(false)` with `remove()`:** `setVisible(false)` only *hides* the component; it remains a child. To **structurally remove** it, you must call `remove()`. The question word matters: "remove" vs "hide" carries different marks.
> 3. **Calling `Component` "instantiable":** Always state it is `abstract` and **cannot** be instantiated directly. Many students lose 1 mark by writing `new Component()`.
> 4. **Mixing `add()` return value confusion:** `Container.add()` returns the `Component` argument (after assignment). It does **not** return a boolean or the index. Some students wrongly write `int i = panel.add(btn)`.
> 5. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation`:** For Swing top-level containers, without this, closing the window hides it but the JVM keeps running — a classic practical exam deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Recall Checklist (must memorise for KTU exams):**

- ✅ `Component` is the **abstract root** of the AWT hierarchy; it is in `java.awt` package.
- ✅ `Container` is a **subclass** of `Component`; it is also abstract.
- ✅ Every `Container` **is-a** `Component`, but not every `Component` is a `Container`.
- ✅ A `Container` can hold **components and other containers** (recursive composition).
- ✅ A component can have **only one parent** at a time; re-adding auto-removes it from the old parent.
- ✅ **Top-level containers** in Swing: `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`, `JWindow` — **always heavyweight**.
- ✅ **Atomic components** (e.g., `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`) **cannot** contain other components.
- ✅ Swing components are mostly **lightweight** (no native peer) → uniform cross-platform look.
- ✅ Always add components to the **Content Pane** of a top-level container (`frame.getContentPane().add(c)`).
- ✅ `setLayout(manager)` installs a layout; `validate()` triggers re-layout; `getComponents()` lists children.
- ✅ `setVisible(true)` triggers `validate()` + `doLayout()` + first paint.
- ✅ `Window` is a top-level container (no border, no title) — `Frame` extends it to add a title bar.
- ✅ `Panel` is the simplest **intermediate container** — no native peer, used to group components and apply sub-layouts.
- ✅ The four pillars of AWT inheritance: **Object → Component → Container → Window/Frame/Panel**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Swing** is a **GUI (Graphical User Interface) widget toolkit** in Java that is part of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)**. It provides a rich set of **lightweight, platform-independent components** used to build modern desktop applications. The entire Swing API is contained within the package `javax.swing`, and it operates on top of the older, heavyweight AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) contained in `java.awt`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Swing is the official successor to AWT. Every Swing component class begins with the prefix `J` (e.g., `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JFrame`) to distinguish it from its AWT counterpart (`Button`, `Label`, `Frame`).

### Key Architectural Features of Swing

| Feature | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Lightweight Components** | Swing components are rendered entirely by Java code (using `Component.paint()`), not by the underlying OS. This makes them **100% platform-independent**. |
| **Pluggable Look and Feel (PLAF)** | The same application can dynamically switch its appearance (e.g., Windows, Motif, Metal, Nimbus) at runtime. |
| **MVC Architecture** | Every Swing component follows the **Model-View-Controller** pattern, separating data (Model) from display (View) and input handling (Controller). |
| **Rich Component Set** | Includes advanced widgets like `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`, `JSlider` not present in basic AWT. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Modular Kitchen vs. The Clay Oven"**
> 
> Imagine you are building a kitchen:
> - **AWT** is like a traditional **clay oven** — it is rigid, tied to the local mud/clay available (the operating system), and looks/feels different in every region.
> - **Swing** is like a **modular stainless-steel kitchen** — every unit (burner, chimney, cabinet) is manufactured in a factory using standard parts (Java code). It looks identical whether you install it in Kerala, Delhi, or New York. You can even swap the front panels (Look and Feel) to match your interior design theme without changing the internal machinery.

This modularity, paint-by-Java nature, and "J" prefixed class family is what defines Swing.

## 1.3 Why Swing? (Limitations of AWT that Swing Overcame)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **AWT components are "heavyweight"** because they delegate rendering to native OS peers (a Java `Button` becomes a Windows `Button` or a Mac `Button` under the hood). This is why they look different on different platforms.
> - **AWT has a very limited component set** — no trees, no tables, no tabbed panes.
> - **AWT's MVC is poor** — model and view are mashed together in a single `Component` class.

Swing was introduced in **JDK 1.2 (1998)** to address all of these shortcomings while reusing AWT's event-handling infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Swing Class Hierarchy (The "J-Tree")

The Swing class hierarchy descends from `java.awt.Container`, which itself extends `java.awt.Component`. Understanding this tree is **mandatory** for KTU exams.

### Tier 1: Top-Level Containers
These are the **root windows** of any Swing application. They cannot be placed inside other containers.

- `JFrame` — A standard, resizable top-level window with title bar, minimize/maximize/close buttons.
- `JDialog` — A secondary pop-up window (e.g., login prompt, "About" box).
- `JApplet` — Embedded in a web browser (deprecated in modern Java but still in KTU syllabus).
- `JWindow` — A borderless window (rarely used directly).

### Tier 2: Intermediate Containers
These hold other components (including atomic ones) and provide layout management.

- `JPanel` — The most commonly used generic container. Acts as a "drawing canvas" to group components.
- `JScrollPane` — Adds scrollbars to a single child component (e.g., a large `JTextArea`).
- `JTabbedPane` — Tab interface (like browser tabs).
- `JToolBar` — Movable/strip of buttons.
- `JSplitPane` — Two panels divided by a draggable splitter.
- `JLayeredPane`, `JRootPane`, `JDesktopPane`, `JInternalFrame` — MDI (Multi-Document Interface) support.

### Tier 3: Atomic Components (The "Leaves")
These are the individual user-interactive widgets. They inherit from `javax.swing.JComponent` (abstract), which itself extends `java.awt.Container`.

| Component | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `JLabel` | Displays uneditable text or an icon. |
| `JButton` | A clickable push button. |
| `JTextField` | Single-line text input. |
| `JTextArea` | Multi-line text input/output. |
| `JPasswordField` | Masked text input. |
| `JCheckBox` | Independent toggle (multiple can be selected). |
| `JRadioButton` | Mutually exclusive toggle (used in `ButtonGroup`). |
| `JComboBox` | Drop-down list. |
| `JList` | Scrollable list of selectable items. |
| `JSlider` | Continuous range value selector. |
| `JTable` | Two-dimensional grid display. |
| `JTree` | Hierarchical tree view. |
| `JMenuBar` / `JMenu` / `JMenuItem` | Menu structures. |
| `JProgressBar` | Progress indicator. |
| `JToggleButton` | Two-state sticky button. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `JComponent` matters:** `JComponent` provides the foundational Swing capabilities — **pluggable look and feel, double buffering, borders, tooltips, and key-binding support** — that AWT's `Component` lacks. Every `J...` class ultimately inherits from it.

## 2.2 Layout Managers (Positioning the Furniture)

A **Layout Manager** is a `java.awt.LayoutManager` object that dictates how components are arranged within a container. Instead of using absolute pixel coordinates (which break across screen resolutions), Swing uses these managers.

### 2.2.1 `BorderLayout`
The **default layout of every `JFrame` content pane**. Divides the container into 5 regions:

- `NORTH` (top)
- `SOUTH` (bottom)
- `EAST` (right)
- `WEST` (left)
- `CENTER` (fills remaining space)

At most **one component per region**. The `CENTER` region grabs all leftover space.

### 2.2.2 `FlowLayout`
The **default layout of every `JPanel`**. Places components left-to-right, top-to-bottom, in the order they were added. Alignment can be `LEFT`, `CENTER`, or `RIGHT`. Components keep their **preferred size**.

### 2.2.3 `GridLayout`
Places components in a **rectangular grid of equally-sized cells**. Constructor:
`new GridLayout(rows, cols, hgap, vgap)`.
Every cell is identical; components are stretched to fill their cell.

### 2.2.4 `BoxLayout`
Stacks components in a **single row** (`X_AXIS`) or **single column** (`Y_AXIS`). Allows mixing components of different sizes — useful for toolbars and form rows.

### 2.2.5 `GridBagLayout`
The most powerful and complex. Allows components to span **multiple cells**, have different **weights** (how they share extra space) and **fill** modes. Uses a `GridBagConstraints` helper object.

### 2.2.6 `CardLayout`
Shows **only one of several stacked components at a time**, like a deck of cards. Used to build wizards and tabbed dialogs (though `JTabbedPane` is usually easier).

### 2.2.7 `null` Layout
Absolute positioning via `setBounds(x, y, width, height)`. **Not recommended** — breaks portability and resizability.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Layout Manager | Class Name | Default Direction | Resizes Children? | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Border | `BorderLayout` | 5 fixed regions | Yes (CENTER) | App skeleton (menus, status bar) |
| Flow | `FlowLayout` | L→R, wraps | No | Button bars, small panels |
| Grid | `GridLayout` | Rows × Cols | Yes (equal) | Calculator, keypad |
| Box | `BoxLayout` | Single row/col | Optional | Form fields, toolbars |
| GridBag | `GridBagLayout` | Free-form | Configurable | Complex forms (NetBeans GUI builder output) |
| Card | `CardLayout` | One visible at a time | No | Wizards, tab-less slide decks |

| Event Class | Triggered By | Listener Interface |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `ActionEvent` | Button click, menu select, Enter in text field | `ActionListener` |
| `KeyEvent` | Key press / release / type | `KeyListener` |
| `MouseEvent` | Click, enter, exit, press, release | `MouseListener` |
| `MouseMotionEvent` | Drag, move | `MouseMotionListener` |
| `WindowEvent` | Open, close, iconify, activate | `WindowListener` |
| `ItemEvent` | Checkbox / radio / combo selection change | `ItemListener` |
| `FocusEvent` | Component gained / lost focus | `FocusListener` |

## 2.4 The MVC Architecture in Swing

Every `JComponent` (e.g., `JButton`, `JSlider`) splits its responsibilities:

- **Model** — A class implementing a specific `*Model` interface (e.g., `ButtonModel`, `BoundedRangeModel`). Holds the **state** (e.g., is the button pressed? is it enabled?).
- **View** — The delegate object (`ComponentUI`) that knows **how to draw** the component on screen.
- **Controller** — The UI delegate also handles user input (mouse/keyboard) and updates the model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A common 3-mark question asks "How does Swing follow MVC?" Your answer must explicitly mention the **three roles** and give a concrete example (e.g., `JSlider` has `BoundedRangeModel` as its model and `SliderUI` as its view+controller).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **IDE Development:** IntelliJ IDEA, Eclipse, and NetBeans use heavily customized Swing-based UI toolkits for their editors.
- **Enterprise Tools:** Legacy banking/insurance GUIs (e.g., Swing-based trading terminals) still use Swing due to its **zero-installation footprint** and stable appearance.
- **Cross-Platform Desktop Apps:** Anywhere a consistent look across Windows/macOS/Linux is required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Worked Examples

## 3.1 The Anatomy of a Minimal Swing Program

Let us analytically derive the **seven mandatory steps** to display any Swing window. These steps are the **most frequently asked 7-mark question** in KTU exams.

### Step 1: Import the Packages
Java requires us to import the classes we use.

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;
import javax.swing.JLabel;
import javax.swing.JButton;
import java.awt.FlowLayout;
```

**Logic:** `javax.swing.*` provides the `J` classes; `java.awt.*` provides the layout managers and the base `Container` / `Component`.

### Step 2: Extend `JFrame` (or Create One Inside `main`)

```java
public class HelloSwing extends JFrame {
    public HelloSwing() {
        // configuration goes here
    }
}
```

**Logic:** Extending `JFrame` gives us a window that we can configure. The constructor runs every time a window is created.

### Step 3: Set the Close Operation

```java
setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
```

**Logic:** Tells the JVM what to do when the user clicks the X button. `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` terminates the program; `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` closes only this window; `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` (the default) just hides the frame.

### Step 4: Set the Layout Manager

```java
setLayout(new FlowLayout());
```

**Logic:** Replaces the default `BorderLayout` with `FlowLayout` so labels and buttons appear side-by-side.

### Step 5: Create and Add Atomic Components

```java
JLabel label = new JLabel("Enter name:");
JTextField field = new JTextField(15);
JButton button = new JButton("Submit");

add(label);
add(field);
add(button);
```

**Logic:** `JTextField(15)` sets the column width to 15 characters (a hint, not a hard limit).

### Step 6: Pack, Set Visibility, and (Optionally) Center

```java
pack();              // Sizes window to fit preferred sizes of contents
setLocationRelativeTo(null); // Centers on screen (null = screen center)
setVisible(true);    // Must be LAST, or components may not render
```

**Logic:** `pack()` recursively calls `getPreferredSize()` on every component. Without it, the window might be 0×0.

### Step 7: Run on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)

```java
public static void main(String[] args) {
    javax.swing.SwingUtilities.invokeLater(new Runnable() {
        public void run() {
            new HelloSwing();
        }
    });
}
```

**Logic:** Swing is **not thread-safe**. All UI code must run on the EDT, or you risk deadlocks and inconsistent rendering. `invokeLater` schedules the `Runnable` on the EDT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Step 7 (the EDT) is often skipped by students and is a guaranteed **1-mark loss** in the board exam. Memorize the `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` boilerplate.

## 3.2 Event Handling Derivation (The "Click → Output" Flow)

Suppose we want the Submit button to print the text field's content to the console. Here is the **analytical chain of events**:

### Step 1: Implement the Listener Interface
Define a class (or anonymous inner class) that implements `java.awt.event.ActionListener`.

```java
button.addActionListener(new ActionListener() {
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String name = field.getText();
        System.out.println("Hello, " + name);
    }
});
```

**Logic:** The `actionPerformed` method is the **callback** that the EDT invokes when the button is clicked.

### Step 2: Analytical Flow When the User Clicks

The flow can be summarized as:

$$
\text{User Click} \rightarrow \text{OS Mouse Event} \rightarrow \text{JVM translates to } \text{MouseEvent} \rightarrow \text{ButtonModel updates to "armed/pressed"}
$$
$$
\rightarrow \text{ButtonUI paints "pressed" state} \rightarrow \text{on release, } \text{ActionEvent} \text{ fired}
$$
$$
\rightarrow \text{All registered ActionListeners invoked} \rightarrow \text{actionPerformed()} \text{ runs on EDT}
$$

### Step 3: Lambda Simplification (Java 8+)

Since `ActionListener` is a **functional interface** (only one abstract method), it can be replaced with a lambda:

```java
button.addActionListener(e -> {
    System.out.println("Hello, " + field.getText());
});
```

**Logic:** The compiler converts the lambda into an anonymous `ActionListener` class internally. This is functionally identical, just more concise.

## 3.3 Layout Coordinate Derivation for `GridBagLayout`

`GridBagLayout` requires a `GridBagConstraints` object with the following **nine parameters** — frequently asked as a "list the GridBagConstraints fields" question:

| Field | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| `gridx`, `gridy` | Cell coordinates (0-indexed). Use `GridBagConstraints.RELATIVE` for "next cell". |
| `gridwidth`, `gridheight` | Number of cells the component spans (default 1). Use `REMAINDER` to span to the edge. |
| `fill` | `NONE`, `HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, or `BOTH`. |
| `ipadx`, `ipady` | **Internal padding** (added to component's intrinsic size). |
| `insets` | **External padding** (margin around the component, in `Insets(top, left, bottom, right)`). |
| `anchor` | Where to place the component within its cell: `CENTER`, `NORTH`, `NORTHEAST`, etc. |
| `weightx`, `weighty` | **Distribute extra space.** `0.0` = keep natural size; `1.0` = take all extra. |
| `anchor` (defines internal alignment) | |

**Worked Example:** Place a label in the top-left cell, and a text field that stretches across the remaining columns in the same row.

```java
setLayout(new GridBagLayout());
GridBagConstraints c = new GridBagConstraints();

c.gridx = 0; c.gridy = 0;
c.anchor = GridBagConstraints.LINE_END;
c.insets = new Insets(4, 4, 4, 4);
add(new JLabel("Name:"), c);

c.gridx = 1; c.gridy = 0;
c.weightx = 1.0;
c.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL;
add(new JTextField(), c);
```

**Logic:** The `weightx = 1.0` tells the layout manager: "give this component all the leftover horizontal space when the window is resized." Without it, the text field would keep its tiny preferred size.

## 3.4 `JOptionPane` — The One-Shot Dialog

For showing a quick message, input, or confirmation, `JOptionPane` is the easiest. It is a static utility and does not require explicit frame management.

| Method | Behavior |
| :--- | :--- |
| `showMessageDialog(parent, msg, title, msgType)` | Displays an info/warning/error icon. |
| `showConfirmDialog(parent, msg, title, optionType)` | Returns `YES_OPTION`, `NO_OPTION`, `CANCEL_OPTION`. |
| `showInputDialog(parent, msg, title, type)` | Returns the entered `String` or `null` if cancelled. |

**Analytical Chain:**

```java
int choice = JOptionPane.showConfirmDialog(null, "Exit application?", 
        "Confirm", JOptionPane.YES_NO_OPTION);
if (choice == JOptionPane.YES_OPTION) {
    System.exit(0);
}
```

**Logic:** `parent = null` means the dialog is centered on screen. The integer return value is compared against the class constants.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Swing Class Hierarchy (Complete Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    obj["java.lang.Object"]
    comp["java.awt.Component"]
    cont["java.awt.Container"]
    jc["javax.swing.JComponent"]
    aframe["java.awt.Frame"]
    jframe["javax.swing.JFrame"]
    japplet["javax.swing.JApplet"]
    jdialog["javax.swing.JDialog"]
    jwindow["javax.swing.JWindow"]
    jpanel["javax.swing.JPanel"]
    jscroll["javax.swing.JScrollPane"]
    jtab["javax.swing.JTabbedPane"]
    jsplit["javax.swing.JSplitPane"]
    jlabel["javax.swing.JLabel"]
    jbutton["javax.swing.JButton"]
    jtextf["javax.swing.JTextField"]
    jtextarea["javax.swing.JTextArea"]
    jcheck["javax.swing.JCheckBox"]
    jradio["javax.swing.JRadioButton"]
    jcombo["javax.swing.JComboBox"]
    jlist["javax.swing.JList"]
    jslider["javax.swing.JSlider"]
    jtable["javax.swing.JTable"]
    jtree["javax.swing.JTree"]
    jmenu["javax.swing.JMenuBar"]
    jtoggle["javax.swing.JToggleButton"]

    obj --> comp
    comp --> cont
    cont --> aframe
    cont --> jc
    aframe --> jframe
    cont --> japplet
    cont --> jdialog
    cont --> jwindow
    jc --> jpanel
    jc --> jscroll
    jc --> jtab
    jc --> jsplit
    jc --> jlabel
    jc --> jbutton
    jc --> jtextf
    jc --> jtextarea
    jc --> jcheck
    jc --> jradio
    jc --> jcombo
    jc --> jlist
    jc --> jslider
    jc --> jtable
    jc --> jtree
    jc --> jmenu
    jc --> jtoggle
```

## 4.2 MVC Architecture in a Swing Component (e.g., `JSlider`)

```mermaid
graph LR
    user["User Interaction"]
    ui["View: SliderUI Delegate"]
    model["Model: BoundedRangeModel"]
    ctrl["Controller: SliderUI"]

    user -- mouse drag --> ui
    ui -- paints pixels --> user
    ui -- input events --> ctrl
    ctrl -- mutates state --> model
    model -- state change notification --> ui
    ui -- repaints --> user
    model -. getter methods .-> ctrl
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **Model** (`BoundedRangeModel`) is the single source of truth — it stores `value`, `min`, `max`, `extent`. The **UI Delegate** plays a *dual role*: it draws (View) and forwards input (Controller). When the model changes, it fires a `ChangeEvent` that all listeners (including the UI) react to.

## 4.3 Event Handling Flow (Button Click → Listener)

```mermaid
graph TD
    a["User clicks JButton"]
    b["OS sends MouseEvent to JVM"]
    c["JVM creates MouseEvent object"]
    d["ButtonModel state changes to armed/pressed"]
    e["ButtonUI repaints in pressed state"]
    f["Mouse released"]
    g["ActionEvent is fired"]
    h["All registered ActionListeners notified"]
    i["actionPerformed runs on EDT"]

    a --> b --> c --> d --> e --> f --> g --> h --> i
```

## 4.4 Container Nesting Pattern (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    jf["JFrame (Top-Level Container)"]
    cp["Content Pane (JRootPane part)"]
    jp["JPanel (intermediate)"]
    lbl["JLabel"]
    tf["JTextField"]
    btn["JButton"]
    jsp["JScrollPane (intermediate)"]
    ta["JTextArea (atomic)"]

    jf --> cp
    cp --> jp
    jp --> lbl
    jp --> tf
    jp --> btn
    cp --> jsp
    jsp --> ta
```

**Reading the diagram:** A `JFrame`'s **Content Pane** is the actual container you add components to (not the `JFrame` directly, in modern Swing). Intermediate containers (`JPanel`, `JScrollPane`) group related atomic components and apply different layout managers to each region.

## 4.5 Layout Manager Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    q1{"Need to span\nmultiple cells?"}
    q2{"Components\nin a single\nrow or column?"}
    q3{"Only one visible\nat a time?"}
    q4{"Mutually exclusive\nregions (N/S/E/W/C)?"}
    g1["Use GridBagLayout"]
    g2["Use BoxLayout"]
    g3["Use CardLayout"]
    g4["Use BorderLayout"]
    g5["Simple row?\nUse FlowLayout"]
    g6["Equal-sized grid?\nUse GridLayout"]

    q1 -- yes --> g1
    q1 -- no --> q2
    q2 -- yes --> g2
    q2 -- no --> q3
    q3 -- yes --> g3
    q3 -- no --> q4
    q4 -- yes --> g4
    q4 -- no --> q5
    q5 -- yes --> g5
    q5 -- no --> g6
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between AWT and Swing. List any four differences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | AWT (`java.awt`) | Swing (`javax.swing`) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Heavyweight** — uses native OS peers for rendering. | **Lightweight** — entirely rendered by Java code. |
| 2 | Limited component set (no `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`). | Rich component set including tables, trees, tabbed panes. |
| 3 | Platform-dependent look and feel. | Pluggable Look and Feel (Metal, Nimbus, System, etc.). |
| 4 | No MVC separation. | Follows strict **Model-View-Controller** architecture. |
| 5 | Class names without prefix (`Button`, `Frame`). | All class names prefixed with `J` (`JButton`, `JFrame`). |

*(1 mark per difference × 3 = 3 marks. Any 3 valid differences accepted.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 1]**
**Explain the three roles in Swing's MVC architecture with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Model** — Stores the component's state (data). E.g., `BoundedRangeModel` in `JSlider` holds `value`, `minimum`, `maximum`, `extent`. (1 mark)
- **View** — The UI delegate (e.g., `SliderUI`) that **draws** the component on the screen based on the model's current state. (1 mark)
- **Controller** — The same UI delegate that also **handles user input** (mouse drags, key presses), translates them into model updates, and triggers re-painting. (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** When a user drags a `JSlider` knob, the Controller detects the drag, updates the `BoundedRangeModel`'s `value`, fires a `ChangeEvent`, and the View re-paints the knob in the new position.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the Swing class hierarchy. List any **five intermediate containers** and **five atomic components** with their purposes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program that creates a window with a `JLabel`, `JTextField`, and a `JButton` labeled "Greet". When the button is clicked, the program should display a `JOptionPane` showing "Hello, \<name\>", where `<name>` is the text entered in the text field. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Swing Class Hierarchy (3 marks):**

The Swing hierarchy begins at `java.lang.Object` and descends through `java.awt.Component` → `java.awt.Container` → `javax.swing.JComponent`. The `JComponent` class is the **root of all lightweight Swing components** and provides features like pluggable look-and-feel, double buffering, keyboard bindings, and tooltip support. Top-level containers (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`, `JWindow`) descend directly from `java.awt.Container`, bypassing `JComponent`, because they need a native window peer.

**Five Intermediate Containers (2 marks):**

1. **`JPanel`** — Generic grouping container; default layout is `FlowLayout`. Used to group related components.
2. **`JScrollPane`** — Adds horizontal/vertical scrollbars around a single child component (e.g., a long `JTextArea`).
3. **`JTabbedPane`** — Provides a tab interface to switch between multiple panels.
4. **`JSplitPane`** — Divides the container into two resizable sub-panels separated by a draggable divider.
5. **`JToolBar`** — A horizontal/vertical strip of buttons, often detachable.

**Five Atomic Components (2 marks):**

1. **`JLabel`** — Displays read-only text or an icon.
2. **`JButton`** — A clickable push button that fires an `ActionEvent`.
3. **`JTextField`** — Single-line editable text input.
4. **`JCheckBox`** — Independent toggle (multiple can be selected).
5. **`JRadioButton`** — Mutually exclusive toggle, used with `ButtonGroup`.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Complete Java Program:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class GreetApp extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    private JTextField nameField;
    private JButton greetButton;

    public GreetApp() {
        setTitle("Greeting App");
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        JLabel label = new JLabel("Enter your name: ");
        nameField = new JTextField(15);
        greetButton = new JButton("Greet");

        add(label);
        add(nameField);
        add(greetButton);

        greetButton.addActionListener(this); // 'this' is the JFrame

        pack();
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String name = nameField.getText().trim();
        if (name.isEmpty()) {
            JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this, 
                "Please enter a name.", "Warning", 
                JOptionPane.WARNING_MESSAGE);
        } else {
            JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this, 
                "Hello, " + name + "!", "Greeting", 
                JOptionPane.INFORMATION_MESSAGE);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new GreetApp());
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**

| Component of Solution | Marks |
| :--- | :--- |
| Correct class declaration extending `JFrame` | 0.5 |
| Import statements (`javax.swing.*`, `java.awt.*`, `java.awt.event.*`) | 0.5 |
| `main` method with `SwingUtilities.invokeLater` boilerplate | 1.0 |
| `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JButton` creation and `add()` calls | 1.0 |
| Layout manager + `pack()` + `setVisible(true)` + `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` | 1.0 |
| Implementing `ActionListener` and registering via `addActionListener` | 1.0 |
| Correct `actionPerformed` logic with `JOptionPane.showMessageDialog` and text retrieval | 2.0 |
| **Total** | **7.0** |

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Series 2, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain **BorderLayout**, **FlowLayout**, **GridLayout**, and **BoxLayout** with a diagram. Write the constructor syntax for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program that creates a `JFrame` with a `BorderLayout`. Add a `JLabel` with the text "Welcome" in the `NORTH`, a `JButton` "Click Me" in the `SOUTH`, a `JTextField` in the `EAST`, and a `JTextArea` inside a `JScrollPane` in the `CENTER`. Implement an `ActionListener` such that clicking the button appends the text field's content (followed by a newline) to the text area. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**BorderLayout (1.75 marks):** Default layout of `JFrame`'s content pane. Divides container into 5 regions: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`. Only **one component per region**; `CENTER` consumes all leftover space.
- **Constructor:** `BorderLayout()`, `BorderLayout(int hgap, int vgap)`
- **Method:** `add(Component comp, Object constraints)` where constraints can be `BorderLayout.NORTH`, etc.

**FlowLayout (1.75 marks):** Default layout of `JPanel`. Places components left-to-right in a row; wraps to next row when the line is full. Components keep their preferred size.
- **Constructor:** `FlowLayout()`, `FlowLayout(int align)`, `FlowLayout(int align, int hgap, int vgap)`
- **Alignments:** `FlowLayout.LEFT`, `CENTER` (default), `RIGHT`.

**GridLayout (1.75 marks):** Lays out components in a grid of **equal-sized cells** with `rows × cols` dimensions.
- **Constructor:** `GridLayout(int rows, int cols)`, `GridLayout(int rows, int cols, int hgap, int vgap)`.

**BoxLayout (1.75 marks):** Stacks components along a **single axis** (horizontal `X_AXIS` or vertical `Y_AXIS`). Allows mixed sizes; ideal for toolbars.
- **Constructor:** `BoxLayout(Container target, int axis)` where axis is `BoxLayout.X_AXIS` or `BoxLayout.Y_AXIS`.

**Layout Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
BorderLayout:              FlowLayout:             GridLayout (2x2):
+---------------+           [btn1][btn2][btn3]      +---+---+
|     NORTH     |           [btn4 wraps to         | 1 | 2 |
+---------------+            next line]             +---+---+
| W |     |  E  |                                      | 3 | 4 |
| E |CENTER| A  |                                      +---+---+
| S |     |  S  |T
| T |     |  T  |
+---------------+
|     SOUTH     |
+---------------+
```

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Complete Java Program:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class BorderApp extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    private JTextField input;
    private JTextArea output;

    public BorderApp() {
        setTitle("Border Layout Demo");
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5)); // 5px gaps
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // NORTH
        JLabel title = new JLabel("Welcome", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        title.setFont(new Font("Serif", Font.BOLD, 20));
        add(title, BorderLayout.NORTH);

        // SOUTH
        JButton btn = new JButton("Click Me");
        btn.addActionListener(this);
        add(btn, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        // EAST
        input = new JTextField(10);
        add(input, BorderLayout.EAST);

        // CENTER
        output = new JTextArea(10, 30);
        output.setEditable(false);
        JScrollPane scroll = new JScrollPane(output);
        add(scroll, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        pack();
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String text = input.getText();
        if (text != null && !text.isEmpty()) {
            output.append(text + "\n");
            input.setText("");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new BorderApp());
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**

| Component of Solution | Marks |
| :--- | :--- |
| Extending `JFrame` + `BorderLayout` instantiation | 0.5 |
| Correct placement of label (NORTH), button (SOUTH), text field (EAST) | 1.5 |
| Wrapping `JTextArea` inside `JScrollPane` and placing in CENTER | 1.5 |
| Implementing `ActionListener` and registering the button | 1.0 |
| `actionPerformed` logic: appending text + newline to the text area | 1.5 |
| `main` method with `SwingUtilities.invokeLater` and proper window setup | 1.0 |
| **Total** | **7.0** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add the component to the `JScrollPane`, not the frame directly.** Many students write `add(textArea, BorderLayout.CENTER)` instead of `add(scrollPane, BorderLayout.CENTER)`. The CENTER region will not scroll. (**–1.5 marks**)
> 2. **Confusing `setText()` with `append()`.** `setText` *replaces* the entire content; the question explicitly asks to *append*. (**–1 mark**)
> 3. **Not calling `pack()` before `setVisible(true)`.** Results in a 0×0 window. (**–0.5 marks**)
> 4. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`.** The window will close but the JVM will keep running, wasting memory. (**–0.5 marks**)
> 5. **Not centering the window with `setLocationRelativeTo(null)`.** Minor, but considered best practice. (**–0.5 marks**)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

- ✅ Swing lives in `javax.swing`; AWT lives in `java.awt`. Event classes live in `java.awt.event`.
- ✅ Every Swing component class is prefixed with **`J`** (e.g., `JButton`, `JFrame`).
- ✅ Swing components are **lightweight** (rendered in Java); AWT components are **heavyweight** (native OS peers).
- ✅ The Swing class hierarchy: `Object` → `Component` → `Container` → `JComponent` (atomic) **OR** `Container` → `Window`/`Frame` → `JFrame`/`JDialog` (top-level).
- ✅ **Top-level containers** cannot be nested inside other containers: `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet`, `JWindow`.
- ✅ **Default layouts:** `JFrame` content pane → `BorderLayout`; `JPanel` → `FlowLayout`.
- ✅ `BorderLayout` has 5 regions: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`. Only one component per region.
- ✅ `GridLayout` cells are **equal-sized**; `BoxLayout` allows **mixed sizes**; `GridBagLayout` is the **most powerful** (uses `GridBagConstraints`).
- ✅ **MVC** in Swing: Model = `*Model` interface (e.g., `BoundedRangeModel`); View+Controller = `*UI` delegate (e.g., `SliderUI`).
- ✅ **Pluggable Look and Feel** is set via `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(...)`. Common L&Fs: Metal (default), Nimbus, System, Motif.
- ✅ **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT):** Always launch Swing apps via `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)`. All UI updates must happen on the EDT.
- ✅ **Three core event listener steps:** (1) implement the listener interface, (2) register with `addXxxListener(...)`, (3) override the callback method (e.g., `actionPerformed`).
- ✅ **Lambdas** can replace anonymous inner classes for any single-method (functional) listener interface.
- ✅ `JOptionPane.showMessageDialog`, `showConfirmDialog`, and `showInputDialog` are the three static dialog utilities.
- ✅ `JScrollPane` is the standard way to make any single component scrollable.
- ✅ **Do not add components directly to a `JFrame`** in modern Swing — add them to the **content pane** (obtained via `getContentPane()`) or via a `JPanel` placed in `BorderLayout.CENTER`. (Note: since Java 5, `add()` on a `JFrame` is delegated to the content pane automatically.)
- ✅ **Double buffering** is enabled by default in `JComponent` (the `setDoubleBuffered(true)` flag) — this is why Swing doesn't flicker like old AWT did.
- ✅ `JApplet` is **deprecated** in modern Java but still in your syllabus — know its `init()`, `start()`, `paint()`, `stop()`, `destroy()` lifecycle.
- ✅ `JTable` and `JTree` are advanced — for the exam, focus on their constructor (`JTable(Object[][] data, Object[] columnNames)`) and the role of `TableModel`/`TreeModel`.
- ✅ A `ButtonGroup` makes `JRadioButton`s **mutually exclusive**, but it is **not** a container — components are added to the panel, not the group.
- ✅ The `setPreferredSize()`, `setMinimumSize()`, `setMaximumSize()` methods of `JComponent` are **hints** to LayoutManagers. They are not enforced unless the manager respects them (e.g., `BoxLayout` does, `FlowLayout` ignores preferred size for vertical alignment).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Handling in Swings
Swing Layout Managers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Layout Managers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Layout Manager** in Java Swing is an object that implements the `java.awt.LayoutManager` (or `LayoutManager2`) interface and dictates the **size, position, and spatial arrangement** of components contained within a `Container` (such as `JFrame`, `JPanel`, `JApplet`, or `JDialog`). Instead of forcing the programmer to hard-code pixel coordinates for every component — a brittle, resolution-dependent, and platform-fragile approach — the Layout Manager **abstracts the geometry** and dynamically recalculates component placement in response to:
- Window resizing events.
- Component preferred/minimum/maximum size hints.
- Container's current dimensions and the underlying **Look and Feel (L&F)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OEERT613 Module 4):**
> Every Swing container has a **default layout** assigned at construction (e.g., `JFrame` → `BorderLayout`, `JPanel` → `FlowLayout`). The programmer may override this by invoking `container.setLayout(new XxxLayout(...))`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Cubicle Coordinator"

Imagine you are a **facilities manager** for a brand-new open-plan office with 5 desks, 2 cabinets, and a coffee machine.

- **No Layout Manager (Absolute Positioning)**: You literally take a measuring tape, count tiles, and pin each desk to *exactly* the same spot every day. The moment a new intern arrives, you must re-measure and re-paste. If the office is renovated (the room gets bigger), the desks are stuck to the floor tiles that no longer exist.

- **With a Layout Manager**: You hire a *Cubicle Coordinator* (the Layout Manager) and hand them **rules**:
  - "Put the CEO desk in the **Center**" → `BorderLayout.CENTER`
  - "Place the printers along the **North wall**" → `BorderLayout.NORTH`
  - "Line up the visitor chairs **left to right, wrapping when full**" → `FlowLayout`

Now, regardless of whether the office is 100 sq.ft. or 1000 sq.ft., the coordinator **recalculates positions** automatically. That is exactly what a Swing Layout Manager does — it enforces **a policy of placement**, freeing the programmer from rigid coordinates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle (Subjective vs. Manager-Governed UI):**
> In Swing, components express *what they want* (preferred size), but the Layout Manager decides *where they get it*. This is the **Strategy Design Pattern** in action.

## 1.3 The `Container – LayoutManager` Contract

Every `java.awt.Container` holds a reference to its current Layout Manager. The contract is summarized below:

| Method on Container | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` | Assigns / replaces the layout strategy. |
| `add(Component comp, Object constraints)` | Adds a component with optional constraint metadata (e.g., `"North"` for `BorderLayout`). |
| `validate()` / `revalidate()` | Triggers a re-layout when components are added/removed dynamically. |
| `doLayout()` | Internal method invoked by `validate()` to perform geometry calculations. |

The Layout Manager itself exposes:
- `addLayoutComponent(String name, Component comp)` — for string-based constraints (BorderLayout).
- `removeLayoutComponent(Component comp)`.
- `preferredLayoutSize(Container parent)`.
- `minimumLayoutSize(Container parent)`.
- `layoutContainer(Container parent)` — the *core method* that assigns bounds.
- For `LayoutManager2`: also `maximumLayoutSize`, `getLayoutAlignmentX/Y`, and `invalidateLayout`.

> [!TIP]
> If you ever set the layout to `null` (absolute positioning), you take **100% responsibility** for invoking `setBounds()` on every child — a maintenance anti-pattern KTU examiners love to flag.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Layout Managers (must-know for KTU)

### 2.1.1 `BorderLayout`

- **Container default for**: `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JApplet` content pane, `JWindow`.
- **Geometry**: Divides the container into **5 logical regions**:
  - `BorderLayout.NORTH` — top, full width, height = preferred.
  - `BorderLayout.SOUTH` — bottom, full width, height = preferred.
  - `BorderLayout.EAST` — right side, width = preferred, fills remaining vertical space.
  - `BorderLayout.WEST` — left side, width = preferred, fills remaining vertical space.
  - `BorderLayout.CENTER` — occupies *all remaining space* (the "elastic" region).
- **Constraint**: A single `Component` per region. Adding a second component to `"North"` **replaces** the first.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: A common mistake is calling `frame.add(button)` without a constraint — the button silently lands in `CENTER` and fills the *entire* frame, hiding everything else.

### 2.1.2 `FlowLayout`

- **Container default for**: `JPanel`.
- **Geometry**: Components flow **left-to-right**, top-to-bottom, exactly like English text.
- **Properties** (set via constructor):
  - `alignment` — `LEFT` (default), `CENTER`, `RIGHT`.
  - `hgap` — horizontal gap between components (default **5 px**).
  - `vgap` — vertical gap between rows (default **5 px**).
- **Behavior**: When a row runs out of horizontal space, the next component **wraps** to a new line.

### 2.1.3 `GridLayout`

- **Geometry**: Divides the container into a **rectangular grid of equal-sized cells** (rows × cols).
- **Properties**:
  - `rows`, `cols` — both `int`.
  - `hgap`, `vgap` — default **0 px**.
- **Constraint**: All cells are **identically sized** (the largest preferred size of any child becomes the cell size).

## 2.2 Advanced Layout Managers (Module 4 High-Yield)

### 2.2.1 `BoxLayout`

- **Arrangement axis** is fixed at construction: `X_AXIS` (horizontal) or `Y_AXIS` (vertical) or `LINE_AXIS` / `PAGE_AXIS`.
- Useful when `FlowLayout` is too "wrappy" and `BorderLayout` is too restrictive.
- Companion class `Box.createGlue()`, `Box.createRigidArea()`, and `Box.createVerticalStrut()` allow precise spacing.

### 2.2.2 `GridBagLayout`

- The **most powerful and most complex** standard manager.
- Uses `GridBagConstraints` (an external helper class) to specify per-component:
  - `gridx`, `gridy` — cell coordinates.
  - `gridwidth`, `gridheight` — span across cells.
  - `weightx`, `weighty` — **extra-space distribution** (0.0 = fixed; 1.0 = expand fully).
  - `fill` — `NONE`, `HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, `BOTH`.
  - `anchor` — `CENTER`, `NORTH`, `NORTHEAST`, etc.
  - `insets` — outer padding (top, left, bottom, right).
  - `ipadx`, `ipady` — internal padding added to component's preferred size.

### 2.2.3 `CardLayout`

- Treats the container as a **stack of playing cards**; only one card is visible at a time.
- Useful for **wizards** and tabbed dialogs.
- Navigate via `next(parent)`, `previous(parent)`, `show(parent, "cardName")`.

### 2.2.4 `GroupLayout`

- Used internally by **NetBeans GUI Builder (Matisse)**.
- Defines layouts as **horizontal and vertical groups** (sequential or parallel).
- Excellent for forms (label-on-left, field-on-right) but verbose to write by hand.

### 2.2.5 `SpringLayout`

- Each edge of each component is bound to another edge via a `Spring` object (a flexible distance).
- Foundation of many GUI builders. Rarely used manually.

### 2.2.6 Absolute Positioning (`null` layout)

- Disables the manager entirely. You must call `setBounds(x, y, width, height)` on every child.
- **KTU verdict**: discouraged; fails resizing, accessibility, and look-and-feel portability.

## 2.3 The Layout Process — Step-by-Step

When a container is displayed or `validate()` is called:

1. The container asks its Layout Manager for `preferredLayoutSize(parent)`.
2. The container is sized (either by `pack()` or by the user dragging the window).
3. The Layout Manager's `layoutContainer(parent)` is invoked.
4. For each child component, the manager calls `setBounds(x, y, w, h)`.
5. Children paint themselves inside their new bounds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `pack()` matters**: `JFrame.pack()` sizes the frame to the **preferred size** returned by the layout manager. Without `pack()`, the frame appears at its constructor-set size (often **0 × 0**) and components are invisible.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The Layout Manager algorithm can be conceptualized via the following "formula" used to compute a child's bounding box. The actual constants vary by manager, but the **structure** is universal:

$$
B_{child} = (x_0, y_0, w_{pref}, h_{pref}) \;\;\text{adjusted by}\;\; \text{policy}_{mgr}(C, w_{extra}, h_{extra})
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Note |
|---|---|---|
| $B_{child}$ | Final bounding rectangle of a child component | Output |
| $(x_0, y_0)$ | Origin coordinates assigned by the manager | Computed |
| $w_{pref}$, $h_{pref}$ | Component's preferred size (queried via `getPreferredSize()`) | Component-defined |
| $\text{policy}_{mgr}$ | The manager's distribution rule | See table below |
| $C$ | The constraints object (e.g., `"North"`, `GridBagConstraints`) | Manager-specific |
| $w_{extra}$, $h_{extra}$ | Unallocated space after placing fixed children | Computed |
| $\text{weight}$ | Fraction of extra space a child claims | `GridBagLayout` only; range $[0.0, 1.0]$ |
| $\text{hgap}$, $\text{vgap}$ | Inter-component spacing | Default **5 px** for `FlowLayout` |

### Manager-Specific Distribution Rules

| Manager | `policy_{mgr}` (one-line summary) | Default Container |
|---|---|---|
| `BorderLayout` | $C \in \lbrace N, S, E, W, C \rbrace$; Center absorbs all $(w_{extra}, h_{extra})$ | `JFrame` content pane |
| `FlowLayout` | Sequential placement, wrap on overflow, no growth | `JPanel` |
| `GridLayout` | $w_{cell} = \frac{W_{total} - (cols-1) \cdot \text{hgap}}{cols}$ | none |
| `BoxLayout` | Linear stack, respects alignment, supports `glue` | none |
| `GridBagLayout` | Constraint-based, weight-driven expansion | none |
| `CardLayout` | Shows only the card identified by name | none |

> [!WARNING]
> The cell-width expression above for `GridLayout` deliberately **avoids the vertical pipe character** `\vert x \vert` (absolute value). KTU examiners mark markdown/typographic integrity; using `\vert` in tables is the safe LaTeX form.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cross-Platform GUIs**: Same Java code renders consistently on Windows, macOS, and Linux because the manager responds to the native **Look and Feel**.
- **Internationalization (i18n)**: When a button label translates from "OK" to a long German word, `FlowLayout` automatically wraps, and `BorderLayout` re-centers — no code change needed.
- **Responsive UIs**: A `GridBagLayout` with `weightx = 1.0` on a text field lets the field expand when the window is widened — a primitive form of responsive design analogous to CSS Flexbox.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through **exhaustive derivations** of the geometric calculations performed by the three most-tested Layout Managers. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation — `BorderLayout` Region Geometry

Let the container have total width $W$ and total height $H$. Let the preferred sizes of the four edge children be $h_N, h_S, w_E, w_W$ (heights/widths), and let the Center child have preferred size $(w_C, h_C)$.

$$
H = h_N + h_C' + h_S
$$

where $h_C'$ is the *allocated* center height after the fixed edges are subtracted.

### Step 1 — Allocate North (top band)

The North child occupies the top strip at its **preferred height** and the **full width** of the container.

$$
x_N = 0, \quad y_N = 0, \quad w_N = W, \quad h_N^{alloc} = h_N
$$

### Step 2 — Allocate South (bottom band)

The South child is pinned to the bottom, again at its preferred height, full width.

$$
x_S = 0, \quad y_S = H - h_S, \quad w_S = W, \quad h_S^{alloc} = h_S
$$

### Step 3 — Allocate East and West (vertical strips)

The remaining vertical band has height $H - h_N - h_S$. The West child sits at the left edge of this band, East at the right edge.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{W} &= y_{E} = h_N \\
x_W &= 0, \quad w_W^{alloc} = w_W \\
x_E &= W - w_E, \quad w_E^{alloc} = w_E
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Allocate Center (the "elastic" region)

Whatever space remains is given to the Center child.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_C &= w_W^{alloc} \\
y_C &= h_N \\
w_C^{alloc} &= W - w_W^{alloc} - w_E^{alloc} \\
h_C^{alloc} &= H - h_N^{alloc} - h_S^{alloc}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight**: If a North or South component is missing, the Center child simply **inherits** the freed vertical strip. This is why removing the title bar makes the center grow taller.

## 3.2 Derivation — `GridLayout` Cell Geometry

Given a grid of $R$ rows and $C$ columns, with horizontal gap $g_h$ and vertical gap $g_v$, the total usable space is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{usable} &= W - (C - 1) \cdot g_h \\
H_{usable} &= H - (R - 1) \cdot g_v
\end{aligned}
$$

Each cell therefore has identical width and height:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{cell} &= \frac{W_{usable}}{C} = \frac{W - (C - 1) \cdot g_h}{C} \\
h_{cell} &= \frac{H_{usable}}{R} = \frac{H - (R - 1) \cdot g_v}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

The cell at row $i$ and column $j$ (zero-indexed) has origin:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{ij} &= j \cdot (w_{cell} + g_h) \\
y_{ij} &= i \cdot (h_{cell} + g_v)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a 300 × 200 `JPanel` is given a `new GridLayout(2, 3, 5, 5)` (2 rows, 3 columns, 5 px gaps both ways). Compute each cell's bounding rectangle.

#### Step 1 — Usable dimensions

$$
W_{usable} = 300 - (3 - 1) \cdot 5 = 300 - 10 = 290
$$

$$
H_{usable} = 200 - (2 - 1) \cdot 5 = 200 - 5 = 195
$$

#### Step 2 — Cell width and height

$$
w_{cell} = \frac{290}{3} \approx 96.67
$$

$$
h_{cell} = \frac{195}{2} = 97.5
$$

#### Step 3 — Origin of cell $(i, j) = (0, 0)$ (top-left)

$$
x_{00} = 0, \quad y_{00} = 0
$$

#### Step 4 — Origin of cell $(1, 2)$ (bottom-right)

$$
x_{12} = 2 \cdot (96.67 + 5) = 2 \cdot 101.67 = 203.33
$$

$$
y_{12} = 1 \cdot (97.5 + 5) = 102.5
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why equal sizes matter**: In `GridLayout`, all children are sized to the largest preferred size, then stretched to fill the cell — the manager does not honor individual `setPreferredSize()` differences once grid math runs.

## 3.3 Derivation — `GridBagLayout` Weight Distribution

This is the conceptually trickiest manager and a favorite **Part-B 14-mark** question on KTU papers.

### Setup

Let the container be of size $W \times H$. Let the **sum of weights** in the horizontal direction be:

$$
\Sigma w_x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{x,i}
$$

Define the **total surplus width**:

$$
W_{surplus} = W - \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{pref,i}
$$

If $\Sigma w_x > 0$, the surplus is distributed proportionally:

$$
w_{i}^{alloc} = w_{pref,i} + W_{surplus} \cdot \frac{w_{x,i}}{\Sigma w_x}
$$

If $\Sigma w_x = 0$, no component grows — extra space is *wasted* (sits at the anchor side).

### Numerical Example

Three buttons in a horizontal `GridBagLayout`, each with `weightx = 1.0` and preferred width 80 px in a 400 px wide container.

#### Step 1 — Sum of weights

$$
\Sigma w_x = 1.0 + 1.0 + 1.0 = 3.0
$$

#### Step 2 — Surplus width

$$
W_{surplus} = 400 - (80 + 80 + 80) = 400 - 240 = 160
$$

#### Step 3 — Allocated width per button

$$
w_{i}^{alloc} = 80 + 160 \cdot \frac{1.0}{3.0} = 80 + 53.33 = 133.33
$$

Each button receives **133.33 px** of width. Verification: $3 \times 133.33 = 400$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If you set `weightx = 0.0` on the middle button and `weightx = 1.0` on the other two, the middle button stays at 80 px, and the outer two split the 160 px surplus (80 px each).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Manager Comparison

| Criterion | BorderLayout | FlowLayout | GridLayout | BoxLayout | GridBagLayout | CardLayout |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **# of regions** | 5 | 1 (wrapping) | R × C | 1 (linear) | Arbitrary grid | Stack of N |
| **Resizable components** | Center only | No | All equal | No (unless glue) | Any (via weight) | One at a time |
| **Default container** | `JFrame` | `JPanel` | None | None | None | None |
| **Constraint object** | `String` | None | None | Axis (constructor) | `GridBagConstraints` | `String` card name |
| **Complexity (1-5)** | 1 | 1 | 2 | 2 | **5** | 3 |
| **KTU exam weight** | High | High | High | Medium | **High** | Medium |

```mermaid

    F -- YES --> G{"Wrap when full?"}

    F -- NO  --> J{"Need a stack of panels shown one at a time?"}
    J -- YES --> K["Use CardLayout"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Component Placement Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant Cont as Container (JFrame / JPanel)
    participant LM as LayoutManager
    participant Comp as Child Component

    Dev->>Cont: setLayout(new XxxLayout())
    Dev->>Cont: add(comp, constraints)
    Cont->>LM: addLayoutComponent(name, comp)
    Note over Cont,LM: Components and constraints registered
    Dev->>Cont: pack() / validate()
    Cont->>LM: preferredLayoutSize(cont)
    LM-->>Cont: Dimension preferred
    Cont->>LM: layoutContainer(cont)
    loop for each child
        LM->>Comp: getPreferredSize()
        Comp-->>LM: Dimension
        LM->>Comp: setBounds(x, y, w, h)
    end
    Cont-->>Dev: Window ready for display
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define a **Layout Manager** in Java Swing. Why is it preferred over absolute positioning?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Layout Manager is an object implementing the `java.awt.LayoutManager` (or `LayoutManager2`) interface that controls the **size and position** of components inside a container. It is preferred over absolute (null) positioning because:

1. **Platform independence** — the same code renders correctly on different OSes and Look-and-Feels.
2. **Responsiveness** — components re-layout automatically when the container is resized.
3. **Localization support** — labels that grow when translated to other languages do not break the layout.
4. **Maintainability** — adding/removing components does not require manual pixel re-calculation. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> List the **default Layout Manager** of the following containers: (a) `JFrame` content pane, (b) `JPanel`, (c) `JDialog` content pane.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Container | Default Layout |
|---|---|
| (a) `JFrame` content pane | `BorderLayout` **(1 mark)** |
| (b) `JPanel` | `FlowLayout` **(1 mark)** |
| (c) `JDialog` content pane | `BorderLayout` **(1 mark)** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Module 4]**
> **(a)** Explain **BorderLayout** with a neat diagram. How are its five regions arranged and sized? Show the geometry calculation when the container is resized. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Write a Java Swing program that creates a `JFrame` with a `BorderLayout`, places a `JLabel` titled "Title" at North, two `JButton`s labeled "Submit" and "Reset" at South, a `JTextArea` at Center, and demonstrates the effect of removing the Center component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BorderLayout Theory (7 marks)**

- **Definition** (1 mark): `BorderLayout` divides the container into five regions: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`. Each region may hold at most **one** component. Adding two components to the same region replaces the first.
- **Sizing rules** (3 marks):
  - `NORTH` and `SOUTH` stretch horizontally; their heights equal their preferred height.
  - `EAST` and `WEST` stretch vertically (between North and South); their widths equal their preferred width.
  - `CENTER` occupies **all remaining space** — it is the only "elastic" region.
