- **Resizing math** (2 marks): If the container is resized from $(W, H)$ to $(W', H')$, the new center size becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{C}' &= W' - w_W^{alloc} - w_E^{alloc} \\
h_{C}' &= H' - h_N^{alloc} - h_S^{alloc}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1.)

- **Constraint key**: `String` (e.g., `BorderLayout.NORTH`) or `BorderLayout` constants.
- **Default hgap / vgap**: **0 pixels**.

**(b) Java Program (7 marks)**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class BorderLayoutDemo extends JFrame {
    public BorderLayoutDemo() {
        setTitle("BorderLayout Demo");
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setSize(400, 300);

        // Setting layout explicitly (default is already BorderLayout)
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5));

        // North
        JLabel title = new JLabel("Title", SwingConstants.CENTER);
        add(title, BorderLayout.NORTH);

        // South - using a panel to hold two buttons
        JPanel southPanel = new JPanel();
        southPanel.add(new JButton("Submit"));
        southPanel.add(new JButton("Reset"));
        add(southPanel, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        // Center
        JTextArea textArea = new JTextArea();
        add(new JScrollPane(textArea), BorderLayout.CENTER);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new BorderLayoutDemo();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of steps (rubric):**

1. `JFrame` constructor & `setSize` — (1 mark)
2. `setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5))` — (1 mark)
3. `JLabel` added with `BorderLayout.NORTH` — (1 mark)
4. `JPanel` holding two `JButton`s in `BorderLayout.SOUTH` — (1 mark)
5. `JTextArea` in `CENTER` (wrapped in `JScrollPane` for good practice) — (1 mark)
6. `setVisible(true)` and main method — (1 mark)
7. **Conceptual follow-up**: when the Center component is removed, North, South, East, West remain at their preferred sizes, and the freed area becomes empty whitespace — the window does *not* automatically resize. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Module 4]**
> **(a)** Compare `FlowLayout`, `GridLayout`, and `BorderLayout` in terms of structure, default container, and behavior on resizing. Use a table. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain `GridBagLayout` with the help of `GridBagConstraints`. Describe the role of `weightx`, `weighty`, `fill`, `gridwidth`, and `insets` with a code snippet that creates a form: a `JLabel` "Username", a `JTextField` next to it (both in row 0), and a `JButton` "Login" spanning both columns in row 1. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks)**

| Aspect | `FlowLayout` | `GridLayout` | `BorderLayout` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structure (1 mark) | Linear, left-to-right, wraps on overflow | Rectangular grid of equal cells | Five fixed regions (N, S, E, W, C) |
| Default container (1 mark) | `JPanel` | None | `JFrame` content pane, `JDialog` |
| Resizing behavior (2 marks) | Components stay at preferred size; only gaps shift | All cells resize equally to fill container | Only `CENTER` grows; edges keep preferred size |
| Constraints (1 mark) | `alignment`, `hgap`, `vgap` | `rows`, `cols`, `hgap`, `vgap` | `String` region constant |
| # of components per region (1 mark) | Unlimited | Unlimited | One per region |
| Use case (1 mark) | Button bars, tool palettes | Calculator-style UIs, image grids | Form shells, main app windows |

**(b) GridBagLayout — Login Form (7 marks)**

**Key fields of `GridBagConstraints` explained (3 marks):**

- `weightx` / `weighty` — Fraction of extra space distributed to the component on resize; **0.0** = no growth, **1.0** = full growth.
- `fill` — `NONE`, `HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, `BOTH`; controls whether the component fills its display area.
- `gridwidth` / `gridheight` — Number of cells the component spans in the grid.
- `insets` — Outer padding in pixels: `new Insets(top, left, bottom, right)`.
- `anchor` — Where the component sits inside its display area (`CENTER`, `NORTHWEST`, etc.).

**Code (4 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class LoginForm extends JFrame {
    public LoginForm() {
        setTitle("Login");
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new GridBagLayout());

        GridBagConstraints gbc = new GridBagConstraints();
        gbc.insets = new Insets(5, 5, 5, 5);  // 5 px padding all around

        // Row 0, Col 0: Username label
        gbc.gridx = 0; gbc.gridy = 0;
        gbc.weightx = 0.0; gbc.weighty = 0.0;
        gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.NONE;
        gbc.anchor = GridBagConstraints.EAST;
        add(new JLabel("Username:"), gbc);

        // Row 0, Col 1: text field - expands horizontally
        gbc.gridx = 1; gbc.gridy = 0;
        gbc.weightx = 1.0; gbc.weighty = 0.0;
        gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL;
        add(new JTextField(15), gbc);

        // Row 1, Col 0-1: Login button spans both columns
        gbc.gridx = 0; gbc.gridy = 1;
        gbc.gridwidth = 2;
        gbc.weightx = 0.0; gbc.weighty = 0.0;
        gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.NONE;
        gbc.anchor = GridBagConstraints.CENTER;
        add(new JButton("Login"), gbc);

        pack();
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LoginForm();
    }
}
```

**Rubric notes:**

- Correct `GridBagLayout` instantiation and `GridBagConstraints` creation — (1 mark)
- `weightx = 1.0` and `fill = HORIZONTAL` on the text field — (1 mark)
- `gridwidth = 2` and `CENTER` anchor on the Login button — (1 mark)
- `pack()` and visibility — (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `pack()`** — A `JFrame` without `pack()` appears at 0×0; components are invisible. Loss: 1 mark.
> 2. **Adding components to BorderLayout without a constraint** — The component silently goes to `CENTER` and covers everything. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing `weightx` with `ipadx`** — `weightx` distributes *extra* space; `ipadx` adds *internal* padding to the component's preferred size. Mixing them up costs **2 marks** in Part-B.
> 4. **Using `setBounds` after setting a Layout Manager** — Calling `setLayout(null)` then mixing `setBounds` is a contradiction. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for lack of consistency.
> 5. **Not overriding `getPreferredSize()` for custom components** — If you extend `JPanel` with custom drawing, the layout manager will give it **0×0** unless you override the preferred size. Loss: 1 mark.
> 6. **Misnaming `CardLayout` methods** — `cardLayout.show(parent, "name")` requires the **container** reference, not the card itself. Loss: 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Layout Manager** = an object that implements `LayoutManager`/`LayoutManager2` and arranges components within a `Container`.
- **Default layouts**: `JFrame`/`JDialog` content pane → `BorderLayout`; `JPanel` → `FlowLayout`.
- **BorderLayout** has exactly 5 regions (`NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`); only `CENTER` is elastic.
- **FlowLayout** wraps components left-to-right like text; supports `LEFT`/`CENTER`/`RIGHT` alignment.
- **GridLayout** creates equal-sized cells; size of each cell = $(W - (C-1)\cdot g_h)/C$ horizontally.
- **BoxLayout** is a non-wrapping linear stack; use `Box.createGlue()` for flex spacing.
- **GridBagLayout** is the most powerful — uses `GridBagConstraints` with `weightx`, `weighty`, `fill`, `anchor`, `insets`, `gridwidth`, `gridheight`.
- **CardLayout** shows one of N stacked panels at a time; navigation via `next()`, `previous()`, `show()`.
- **GroupLayout** is used by NetBeans GUI Builder; rarely written by hand.
- **`null` layout** = absolute positioning; discouraged — breaks resizing, i18n, and look-and-feel portability.
- **`pack()`** sizes the window to the layout's preferred size; without it, the UI may be invisible.
- **`setLayout(...)`** replaces the container's current manager; **`add(comp, constraint)`** attaches a component with a manager-specific constraint.
- **`revalidate()`** should be called after dynamically adding/removing components at runtime.
- **Strategy Pattern in action**: every Layout Manager is interchangeable because they all conform to the same `LayoutManager` interface.
- **KTU favorite 14-markers**: (i) explain `GridBagLayout` + code, (ii) compare three managers in a table, (iii) `BorderLayout` geometry derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Mantra for KTU 2024:**
> *"Containers hold components. Layout Managers hold geometry. The Strategy pattern holds them all together."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exploring Swings - JFrame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Exploring Swings - JFrame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JFrame (javax.swing.JFrame)** is a top-level container class in the Java Swing API that extends `java.awt.Frame` and provides a windowed GUI component. It is the foundational window on which all other Swing components (buttons, labels, text fields, panels) are mounted to build a complete Graphical User Interface (GUI) application.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEERT613 - Object Oriented Concepts)**, *JFrame* is introduced in Module 4 as the primary entry point for creating desktop-based Swing applications. It is a concrete subclass of `java.awt.Window` (via `java.awt.Frame`) and is part of the lightweight Swing component architecture that replaces the heavyweight AWT components.

```java
java.lang.Object
    └── java.awt.Component
            └── java.awt.Container
                    └── java.awt.Window
                            └── java.awt.Frame
                                    └── javax.swing.JFrame
```

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Picture Frame on a Wall**

Think of a `JFrame` exactly like a **physical picture frame mounted on a wall**:
- The **frame itself** is the `JFrame` object — it has a title (the wall label), a size (width × height), a position on the wall (x, y coordinate), and a border.
- The **blank canvas inside the frame** is the **Content Pane** (`getContentPane()`) — this is where you actually paint or attach components.
- The **paintings, photos, and decorations you glue inside the frame** are the Swing widgets like `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JPanel`.
- The **wall hook** holding the frame is the operating system's window manager.
- Closing/removing the frame is governed by a default close operation, similar to how a frame may or may not require a specific disposal procedure.

Just as a frame without a canvas is empty, a `JFrame` without added components is a blank, empty window.

## 1.3 Why JFrame and Not AWT Frame?

| Feature | `java.awt.Frame` (AWT) | `javax.swing.JFrame` (Swing) |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt.*` | `javax.swing.*` |
| Pluggable Look & Feel (PL&F) | Limited | **Fully supported** |
| Component Architecture | Heavyweight (peers) | **Lightweight** |
| Default Layout Manager | `BorderLayout` | `BorderLayout` |
| Root Pane Support | No | **Yes (JRootPane)** |
| Painting Control | Limited | **Highly customizable** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU emphasizes that JFrame introduces a **multi-layered pane architecture** (Root Pane, Layered Pane, Content Pane, Glass Pane) which is essential for menu bars, dialogs, and internal frames.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Default Size when not set**: $0 \times 0$ pixels (invisible until `pack()` or `setSize()` is invoked)
- **Default close operation constant**: `WindowConstants.DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` (value = `2`)
- **Default title**: empty string `""`
- **Default visibility**: `false` (must explicitly call `setVisible(true)`)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 JFrame Class Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> Mastering the inheritance chain is mandatory for KTU Part A 3-mark questions. The chain shows how Swing reuses AWT's windowing infrastructure while adding its own enhancements.

```
Object
  └── Component (implements ImageObserver, MenuContainer, Serializable)
        └── Container
              └── Window (implements Accessible)
                    └── Frame (implements MenuContainer)
                          └── JFrame (implements WindowConstants, Accessible, RootPaneContainer)
```

- **Component** — base class for anything that can be displayed on screen.
- **Container** — holds other components.
- **Window** — top-level window without title bar or borders.
- **Frame** — window with title, borders, and menu bar.
- **JFrame** — Swing's enhanced Frame with pluggable look-and-feel and root pane support.

## 2.2 Important Constructors of JFrame

| Constructor | Description |
|---|---|
| `JFrame()` | Creates a frame with no title, initially invisible. |
| `JFrame(String title)` | Creates a frame with the specified title string. |
| `JFrame(GraphicsConfiguration gc)` | Creates a frame using the specified graphics configuration. |
| `JFrame(String title, GraphicsConfiguration gc)` | Creates a titled frame using the specified graphics configuration. |

## 2.3 Core JFrame Methods — KTU Formula Sheet

| Method | Signature / Return Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `setTitle(String)` | `void` | Sets the title bar text. |
| `setSize(int w, int h)` | `void` | Sets the frame dimensions in pixels. |
| `setSize(Dimension)` | `void` | Sets the frame dimensions using a `Dimension` object. |
| `setLocation(int x, int y)` | `void` | Places the top-left corner at `(x, y)` screen coordinates. |
| `setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` | `void` | Combines location + size in one call. |
| `setVisible(boolean)` | `void` | Toggles the visibility (must be `true` to display). |
| `setDefaultCloseOperation(int)` | `void` | Defines action on window close button. |
| `setLayout(LayoutManager)` | `void` | Sets the layout manager for the content pane. |
| `add(Component)` | `Component` | Adds a component to the content pane. |
| `getContentPane()` | `Container` | Returns the content pane container. |
| `setContentPane(Container)` | `void` | Replaces the default content pane. |
| `pack()` | `void` | Sizes the frame based on preferred sizes of contained components. |
| `setResizable(boolean)` | `void` | Controls whether the user can resize the frame. |
| `setIconImage(Image)` | `void` | Sets the frame's taskbar/title icon. |
| `setJMenuBar(JMenuBar)` | `void` | Attaches a menu bar to the frame. |
| `dispose()` | `void` | Releases OS resources for the frame. |

## 2.4 Default Close Operation Constants (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These constants are part of the `WindowConstants` interface implemented by `JFrame`. KTU frequently tests the **numerical values** and **behaviour** of each.

| Constant | Integer Value | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` | `0` | Ignore the close request. |
| `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` | `1` | Hide the frame, but keep the JVM running. (Default in `Window`) |
| `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` | `2` | Hide + dispose frame; JVM exits if no other windows are visible. (Default in `JFrame`) |
| `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` | `3` | Terminate the JVM via `System.exit(0)`. |

## 2.5 The Multi-Pane Architecture of JFrame

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike `java.awt.Frame`, every `JFrame` contains a **JRootPane** which organizes four layered panes:

1. **Glass Pane** (`getGlassPane()`) — Transparent overlay, sits on top, used for tooltips and drag-painting.
2. **Layered Pane** (`getLayeredPane()`) — Holds the content pane and menu bar at the `FRAME_CONTENT_LAYER`.
3. **Content Pane** (`getContentPane()`) — **Where you add your Swing components**. Uses `BorderLayout` by default.
4. **JMenuBar** — Holds the menu bar at the top.

**Engineering Utility**: This layered design enables advanced features like **glass-pane drag-and-drop**, **modal layered dialogs**, and **internal frames within an MDI (Multiple Document Interface) application**.

## 2.6 Why `pack()` Before `setVisible(true)`?

A frame without an explicit size can use `pack()` to compute its dimensions from the **preferred sizes** of its contained components. This is the recommended Swing practice because it avoids hard-coded magic numbers and adapts to platform-specific rendering (e.g., different font metrics on Windows vs. macOS vs. Linux).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Code Analysis & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Analysis: The Minimum Viable JFrame

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks students to "write a Java program to create a JFrame titled 'Welcome' of size 400×300." The following breakdown satisfies every evaluation marker.

```java
import javax.swing.JFrame;

public class WelcomeFrame extends JFrame {
    public WelcomeFrame() {
        // Step 1: Set the title
        setTitle("Welcome");

        // Step 2: Set the frame size (width=400, height=300)
        setSize(400, 300);

        // Step 3: Position the frame at (200, 150) from top-left
        setLocation(200, 150);

        // Step 4: Define what happens when user clicks close [X]
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);

        // Step 5: Make the frame visible
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Create the frame on the Event Dispatch Thread (best practice)
        javax.swing.SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new WelcomeFrame());
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logic Explanation

**Step 1 — `setTitle("Welcome")`**: Calls the inherited method from `Frame`. The title is displayed in the window's title bar. Internally, this delegates to the native peer to render the OS-level title text.

**Step 2 — `setSize(400, 300)`**: Defines the frame's outer dimensions, including borders and title bar. Width = 400 px, Height = 300 px. The content pane is automatically sized smaller to accommodate these chrome elements.

**Step 3 — `setLocation(200, 150)`**: The `(x, y)` coordinates specify where the frame's **top-left corner** is placed relative to the screen origin (top-left of the display).

**Step 4 — `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`**: Sets the integer value `3` into a private field. When the user clicks `[X]`, the frame fires a `WindowEvent` whose dispatched action is determined by this integer. `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` invokes `System.exit(0)`.

**Step 5 — `setVisible(true)`**: Triggers the native peer to make the window appear on screen. Until this call is made, the frame exists in memory but is not displayed.

### Alternative: Using `setBounds()` to Combine Location + Size

$$\text{setBounds}(x, y, w, h) \equiv \text{setLocation}(x, y) + \text{setSize}(w, h)$$

```java
setBounds(200, 150, 400, 300);  // Replaces steps 2 and 3
```

## 3.2 Worked Example: Adding Components to a JFrame

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class LoginFrame extends JFrame {
    private JLabel userLabel, passLabel;
    private JTextField userField;
    private JPasswordField passField;
    private JButton loginBtn, cancelBtn;

    public LoginFrame() {
        setTitle("Login Window");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);  // Center on screen

        // Get the content pane and set layout
        Container c = getContentPane();
        c.setLayout(new GridLayout(3, 2, 5, 5));  // 3 rows, 2 cols, 5px gaps

        // Instantiate components
        userLabel = new JLabel("Username:");
        passLabel = new JLabel("Password:");
        userField = new JTextField(15);
        passField = new JPasswordField(15);
        loginBtn  = new JButton("Login");
        cancelBtn = new JButton("Cancel");

        // Add components to content pane
        c.add(userLabel); c.add(userField);
        c.add(passLabel); c.add(passField);
        c.add(loginBtn);  c.add(cancelBtn);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new LoginFrame());
    }
}
```

### Detailed Line-by-Line Explanation

**`Container c = getContentPane();`** — Swing pre-1.5 required explicit `getContentPane()` calls before adding components. Modern compilers auto-wrap `add()` on a `JFrame` to its content pane, but **KTU examination answers should explicitly call `getContentPane()`** to demonstrate layered-pane awareness.

**`c.setLayout(new GridLayout(3, 2, 5, 5));`** — Installs a 3-row, 2-column grid manager with 5-pixel horizontal and vertical gaps. Mathematical layout regions:

$$
\text{Total cells} = 3 \times 2 = 6
$$

$$
\text{Component-to-component gap} = 5\,\text{px (horizontal)}, \quad 5\,\text{px (vertical)}
$$

**`setLocationRelativeTo(null);`** — Centers the frame on the screen. Passing `null` uses the default screen device.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common JFrame Tasks

| Task | Recommended Method | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Display window | `setVisible(true)` | Must be last (after layout/components). |
| Center window | `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` | Cleaner than computing `Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().getScreenSize()`. |
| Auto-size to contents | `pack()` | Use **instead of** `setSize()` when possible. |
| Quit JVM on close | `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` | Suitable only for single-window apps. |
| Free resources on close | `setDefaultCloseOperation(DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE)` | Use for multi-window apps. |
| Disable resize | `setResizable(false)` | Locks dimensions. |
| Set custom icon | `setIconImage(Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().getImage("icon.png"))` | Image must be loaded. |
| Add menu bar | `setJMenuBar(new JMenuBar())` | Only one JMenuBar per JFrame. |

## 3.4 Lifecycle of a JFrame (Step-by-Step Derivation)

The lifecycle can be formally expressed as a state machine:

$$
S = \{S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}
$$

$$
S_0 = \text{Constructed (no peer)} \xrightarrow{\text{add components / pack}} S_1
$$

$$
S_1 = \text{Configured (peer pending)} \xrightarrow{\text{setVisible(true)}} S_2
$$

$$
S_2 = \text{Visible (active)} \xrightarrow{\text{close event}} S_3
$$

$$
S_3 = \text{Inactive} \xrightarrow{\text{dispose() or EXIT\_ON\_CLOSE}} S_4
$$

$$
S_4 = \text{Disposed / GC Eligible}
$$

The transition $S_2 \to S_3$ invokes the registered `WindowListener` callbacks (`windowClosing`, `windowClosed`, etc.), and the action taken depends on the constant set via `setDefaultCloseOperation()`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 JFrame Class Inheritance Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class objC {
        <<class>>
        Object
    }
    class compC {
        <<class>>
        Component
    }
    class contC {
        <<class>>
        Container
    }
    class winC {
        <<class>>
        Window
    }
    class frameC {
        <<class>>
        Frame
    }
    class jframeC {
        <<class>>
        JFrame
    }
    class wconstI {
        <<interface>>
        WindowConstants
    }
    class rpcI {
        <<interface>>
        RootPaneContainer
    }
    class accI {
        <<interface>>
        Accessible
    }
    objC <|-- compC
    compC <|-- contC
    contC <|-- winC
    winC <|-- frameC
    frameC <|-- jframeC
    jframeC ..|> wconstI
    jframeC ..|> rpcI
    jframeC ..|> accI
```

**Reading the diagram**: `JFrame` inherits state and behavior from `Frame`, `Window`, `Container`, `Component`, and `Object`, and additionally implements three interfaces — `WindowConstants` (for the close-operation constants), `RootPaneContainer` (for layered pane access), and `Accessible` (for screen-reader / accessibility API support).

## 4.2 JFrame Multi-Pane Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph jframeEnv[JFrame Window]
        direction TB
        layerTop["GLASS PANE: Transparent overlay for drag painting and tooltips"]
        layered["LAYERED PANE: Holds content pane and menu bar in FRAME_CONTENT_LAYER"]
        contentPane["CONTENT PANE: BorderLayout by default - actual components live here"]
        menuBar["JMENUBAR: Optional menu bar at the top of the frame"]
        layerTop --> layered
        layered --> contentPane
        layered --> menuBar
        contentPane -.contains.-> btn1["JButton"]
        contentPane -.contains.-> lbl1["JLabel"]
        contentPane -.contains.-> tf1["JTextField"]
    end
    userAct([User clicks X]) --> closeOp{Default Close Operation}
    closeOp -- DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE --> noOp[Ignore Event]
    closeOp -- HIDE_ON_CLOSE --> hide[Hide Frame Only]
    closeOp -- DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE --> disp[Dispose Frame Resources]
    closeOp -- EXIT_ON_CLOSE --> exit[System exit Zero]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[main Method] --> B[SwingUtilities invokeLater]
    B --> C[Create JFrame Instance]
    C --> D[Configure Title Size Location]
    D --> E[Set Default Close Operation]
    E --> F[Retrieve Content Pane]
    F --> G[Set Layout Manager]
    G --> H[Instantiate Swing Components]
    H --> I[Add Components to Content Pane]
    I --> J[Call pack or setSize]
    J --> K[Call setVisible true]
    K --> L[Frame Displays on EDT]
    L --> M{User Interaction}
    M -->|Click Button| N[Event Dispatcher Thread]
    M -->|Close Window| O[WindowEvent Fired]
    N --> P[ActionListener Executes]
    O --> Q[Default Close Operation Triggers]
```

## 4.4 JFrame vs. JWindow vs. JDialog vs. JApplet

```mermaid
graph TB
    winBase["java.awt.Window"] --> jf["JFrame: Top-level with title bar and borders"]
    winBase --> jw["JWindow: Top-level without title bar - rarely used directly"]
    winBase --> jd["JDialog: Modal or non-modal secondary window"]
    appletBase["java.applet.Applet"] --> jap["JApplet: Embedded in browser - deprecated since JDK 9"]
    jf --> jif["JInternalFrame: MDI child frame within a JDesktopPane"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> KTU emphasizes that **JFrame is always a top-level container** (it cannot be added to another container), while **JPanel** is an intermediate container. JFrame's `add()` method internally delegates to the content pane.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define JFrame and explain its significance in Java Swing.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

`JFrame` is a top-level container class in the `javax.swing` package used to create the main window of a Swing-based GUI application. It is a subclass of `java.awt.Frame` and implements the `WindowConstants` and `RootPaneContainer` interfaces.

**Significance**:
1. It serves as the foundational window on which all other Swing components like `JButton`, `JLabel`, and `JTextField` are mounted.
2. It supports the **Pluggable Look and Feel (PL&F)** mechanism, allowing the same application to look native on Windows, macOS, and Linux.
3. It provides a multi-layered pane architecture (Glass, Layered, Content, MenuBar) for advanced UI organization.

### Question 2: List any four important methods of the `JFrame` class and state their purpose.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Method | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `setTitle(String title)` | Sets the text displayed in the frame's title bar. |
| 2 | `setSize(int width, int height)` | Specifies the frame's dimensions in pixels. |
| 3 | `setVisible(boolean b)` | Displays (`true`) or hides (`false`) the frame on screen. |
| 4 | `setDefaultCloseOperation(int op)` | Defines the action taken when the user clicks the close `[X]` button. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Comprehensive Question on JFrame

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the class hierarchy of `JFrame` from `java.lang.Object` down to `javax.swing.JFrame`. Describe the role of each superclass and the interfaces implemented by `JFrame`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program that creates a `JFrame` titled **"Student Registration"** of size $500 \times 400$ pixels, centred on screen, with the following components arranged in a `GridLayout(4, 2, 10, 10)`:
- `JLabel` — "Name:" with a `JTextField`
- `JLabel` — "Age:" with a `JTextField`
- `JLabel` — "Course:" with a `JComboBox` containing "B.Tech", "M.Tech", "MCA"
- A `JButton` "Submit" and a `JButton` "Reset"

Use `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` as the default close operation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution A(a) — Class Hierarchy (7 Marks)

The complete inheritance chain of `JFrame` is:

$$
\text{Object} \to \text{Component} \to \text{Container} \to \text{Window} \to \text{Frame} \to \text{JFrame}
$$

**Role of each class:**

1. **`java.lang.Object`** (root class): Provides fundamental methods like `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `toString()`, `getClass()`, and threading primitives (`wait()`, `notify()`, `notifyAll()`).

2. **`java.awt.Component`**: Represents anything that can be displayed on screen. Provides properties like `size`, `location`, `foreground`, `background`, `font`, `enabled`, and `visible`. Defines event handling hooks such as `addMouseListener()`.

3. **`java.awt.Container`**: A `Component` that can hold other components. Adds methods like `add()`, `remove()`, `setLayout()`, and `validate()`. Containers themselves can be nested inside other containers.

4. **`java.awt.Window`**: A top-level container with no borders or title bar. Can exist independently of any other window. Defines the `WindowListener` callback interface.

5. **`java.awt.Frame`**: Extends `Window` to add a title bar, window borders, and a menu bar. The heavyweight ancestor — most of its rendering delegates to the operating system's native peer.

6. **`javax.swing.JFrame`**: The lightweight Swing subclass. Adds:
   - **Pluggable Look and Feel** support (`UIManager.setLookAndFeel()`).
   - **JRootPane** layered architecture (Glass, Layered, Content, MenuBar).
   - Default close operation constants.

**Interfaces implemented by `JFrame`:**
- `WindowConstants` — defines the four close-operation integer constants.
- `RootPaneContainer` — grants access to `getContentPane()`, `getLayeredPane()`, `getGlassPane()`, and `getRootPane()`.
- `Accessible` — provides `getAccessibleContext()` for screen-reader support under the Java Accessibility API.

#### Solution A(b) — Java Program (7 Marks)

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class StudentRegistration extends JFrame {

    private JLabel nameLbl, ageLbl, courseLbl;
    private JTextField nameFld, ageFld;
    private JComboBox<String> courseBox;
    private JButton submitBtn, resetBtn;

    public StudentRegistration() {
        // 1. Frame configuration
        setTitle("Student Registration");
        setSize(500, 400);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);  // Center on screen

        // 2. Retrieve content pane and apply layout
        Container c = getContentPane();
        c.setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 2, 10, 10));

        // 3. Instantiate components
        nameLbl = new JLabel("Name:");
        ageLbl  = new JLabel("Age:");
        courseLbl = new JLabel("Course:");
        nameFld = new JTextField(15);
        ageFld  = new JTextField(15);
        courseBox = new JComboBox<>(new String[]{"B.Tech", "M.Tech", "MCA"});
        submitBtn = new JButton("Submit");
        resetBtn  = new JButton("Reset");

        // 4. Add components in grid order (row-major)
        c.add(nameLbl);    c.add(nameFld);
        c.add(ageLbl);     c.add(ageFld);
        c.add(courseLbl);  c.add(courseBox);
        c.add(submitBtn);  c.add(resetBtn);

        // 5. Make visible
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new StudentRegistration());
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step explanation (for valuation key alignment):**

- Line `setTitle("Student Registration")` — sets the title bar text.
- Line `setSize(500, 400)` — sets width = 500 px, height = 400 px.
- Line `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` — centers the frame on the screen.
- Line `setDefaultCloseOperation(DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE)` — disposes the frame on close (does **not** exit JVM, suitable for multi-window apps).
- Line `c.setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 2, 10, 10))` — 4 rows, 2 columns, 10 px horizontal gap, 10 px vertical gap.
- Total components = 6 (3 labels + 3 inputs) + 2 buttons = 8 cells filled across the 4×2 = 8 grid slots.
- `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` — best practice ensuring GUI construction runs on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**.

---

### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Question

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **multi-pane architecture** of a `JFrame`. Differentiate between the Glass Pane, Layered Pane, Content Pane, and the JMenuBar, stating their default properties. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the four constants defined by the `WindowConstants` interface that are used with `setDefaultCloseOperation()`. Create a complete Java program demonstrating a `JFrame` with two buttons — one that sets the close operation to `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` and another to `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE`. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution B(a) — Multi-Pane Architecture (7 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a high-frequency KTU 14-mark question. The 7-mark breakdown typically follows: **(1 mark)** diagram, **(2 marks)** Glass Pane, **(2 marks)** Layered + Content Pane, **(2 marks)** JMenuBar + access methods.

A `JFrame` does **not** directly hold components on its own surface. Instead, every `JFrame` internally owns a `JRootPane`, which divides its drawing area into four conceptual panes:

1. **Glass Pane** (`getGlassPane()`):
   - The **top-most** layer in the layered pane.
   - By default, it is a transparent `JPanel`.
   - Used to intercept mouse events globally over the entire frame (e.g., for custom drag-painting, glass-pane tooltips, busy indicators).
   - Hidden (`setVisible(false)`) by default.

2. **Layered Pane** (`getLayeredPane()`):
   - Contains the content pane and the optional menu bar at the `FRAME_CONTENT_LAYER` (default z-order).
   - Allows components to be placed at different depth layers (e.g., `PALETTE_LAYER`, `MODAL_LAYER`, `POPUP_LAYER`).

3. **Content Pane** (`getContentPane()`):
   - The **primary work area** where developers add visible Swing components like buttons, labels, and text fields.
   - Uses `BorderLayout` as the default layout manager.
   - Returned as a `java.awt.Container`.

4. **JMenuBar** (`getJMenuBar()` / `setJMenuBar()`):
   - An **optional** horizontal menu bar docked at the top of the frame, **outside** the content pane.
   - Holds `JMenu` objects (e.g., File, Edit, Help).

**Default Properties Table (high-yield):**

| Pane | Default Type | Default Layout | Default Visibility | Add Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Glass Pane | `JPanel` | `FlowLayout` | `false` (hidden) | `setGlassPane()` |
| Layered Pane | `JLayeredPane` | None | `true` | `setLayeredPane()` |
| Content Pane | `JPanel` | `BorderLayout` | `true` | `setContentPane()` |
| JMenuBar | `null` (initially) | Horizontal Box | N/A | `setJMenuBar()` |

#### Solution B(b) — Close Operations and Code (7 Marks)

**The Four Constants of `WindowConstants`:**

| Constant | Value | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `DO_NOTHING_ON_CLOSE` | `0` | The close button is effectively disabled; no action is taken. Useful when validation must occur first. |
| `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` | `1` | The window is hidden via `setVisible(false)`, but its resources are **not** released. The JVM continues running. |
| `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` | `2` | The window is hidden **and** `dispose()` is called to release native peer resources. JVM exits when **all** top-level windows are disposed. |
| `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` | `3` | Calls `System.exit(0)`, terminating the entire JVM immediately. Recommended only for single-window apps. |

**Java Program Demonstrating Dynamic Switching:**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CloseOpDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    private JButton exitBtn, disposeBtn;

    public CloseOpDemo() {
        setTitle("Close Operation Demo");
        setSize(350, 150);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());
        setDefaultCloseOperation(DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);

        exitBtn    = new JButton("Set EXIT_ON_CLOSE");
        disposeBtn = new JButton("Set DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE");

        exitBtn.addActionListener(this);
        disposeBtn.addActionListener(this);

        add(exitBtn);
        add(disposeBtn);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        if (e.getSource() == exitBtn) {
            setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
            JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this, "Close op set to EXIT_ON_CLOSE");
        } else if (e.getSource() == disposeBtn) {
            setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE);
            JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this, "Close op set to DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new CloseOpDemo());
    }
}
```

**Explanation of key segments:**

- `implements ActionListener` — allows this class to handle button click events.
- `addActionListener(this)` — registers the current `CloseOpDemo` instance as the listener for both buttons.
- Inside `actionPerformed()`, `e.getSource()` identifies which button was clicked and updates the close operation accordingly.
- `JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(this, ...)` — displays a modal confirmation dialog.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** — Frame exists but never appears. **Lose 1 mark** in Part B.
> 2. **Adding components directly to the `JFrame` instead of `getContentPane()`** — In Java ≥ 1.5 this works due to auto-redirect, but the KTU valuation key expects the explicit `getContentPane()` call. **Lose 0.5 mark** if omitted.
> 3. **Confusing `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` with `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE`** — `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` calls `System.exit()` and terminates the **entire** application; `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` only frees the current window. Writing `System.exit(0)` manually when `EXIT_ON_CLOSE` is asked loses partial credit.
> 4. **Confusing `pack()` with `setSize()`** — `pack()` sizes the frame based on the **preferred size** of contained components; `setSize()` overrides with explicit pixel values. Mixing up the two loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Not invoking `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)`** — In KTU theory questions, not mentioning EDT safety may cost 0.5 to 1 mark in newer 2024 scheme papers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ⭐ `JFrame` is a **top-level container** in `javax.swing`, extending `java.awt.Frame`.
- ⭐ Default close operation of `JFrame` is `DISPOSE_ON_CLOSE` (value `2`), **not** `HIDE_ON_CLOSE` like `java.awt.Window`.
- ⭐ A `JFrame` contains a `JRootPane` which holds four panes: **Glass, Layered, Content, MenuBar**.
- ⭐ Components must be added to the **Content Pane** via `getContentPane().add(...)` (or simply `add(...)` in modern Java).
- ⭐ Use `pack()` for auto-sizing; use `setSize(w,h)` for fixed dimensions.
- ⭐ `setLocationRelativeTo(null)` centers the frame on screen.
- ⭐ `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` calls `System.exit(0)` — terminates JVM.
- ⭐ `setVisible(true)` must be called to display the frame; default visibility is `false`.
- ⭐ Always wrap GUI creation inside `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)` to ensure **EDT safety**.
- ⭐ `JFrame` implements three interfaces: `WindowConstants`, `RootPaneContainer`, and `Accessible`.
- ⭐ Swing components are **lightweight** (drawn by Java), unlike **heavyweight** AWT components (drawn by native OS peers).
- ⭐ `setResizable(false)` disables user-driven resizing of the frame.
- ⭐ `setIconImage(Image)` changes the frame's title-bar / taskbar icon.
- ⭐ `setJMenuBar(JMenuBar)` attaches a menu bar; only **one** allowed per `JFrame`.
- ⭐ `dispose()` releases the frame's native peer resources without terminating the JVM.
- ⭐ The complete inheritance chain: **Object → Component → Container → Window → Frame → JFrame**.
- ⭐ Default layout of the Content Pane is **BorderLayout**; `JPanel` also defaults to `FlowLayout`.
- ⭐ KTU loves comparing `JFrame` vs `JApplet` vs `JDialog` vs `JWindow` — remember: **JFrame is the only one meant for desktop main windows**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JLabel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** JLabel # 📘 Module 4 — JLabel: The Foundational Display Component of Java Swing ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** > `JLabel` is a subclass of `javax.swing.JComponent` used to display a **short text string, an image, or both** simultaneously. It is a *passive* (non-interactive) GUI component in the Java Swing framework that cannot receive focus and does not generate `ActionEvent` by default. It inherits directly from `JComponent` and is the most lightweight display-only widget in the AWT/Swing hierarchy. **Class Hierarchy (Direct Lineage):** $$ \text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Component} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Container} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JComponent} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JLabel} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** > The KTU board specifically tests the ability of students to: > 1. Construct `JLabel` objects using **all valid constructors**. > 2. Set **alignment** (horizontal & vertical) constants. > 3. Support **HTML rendering** within labels (a unique JLabel feature). > 4. Associate a **mnemonic** (keyboard shortcut) to another component. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Museum Plaque" Analogy Imagine a **museum exhibit**. Each artifact on display has a small printed plaque beside it. That plaque: - **Says something** (the text). - **May show a small icon** (the image). - **Does NOT react when you touch it** (passive component). - **Sits at a specific spot** (alignment). - **Cannot be clicked to trigger an action** (no event handling internally). A `JLabel` is the **museum plaque** of a Java Swing GUI. It is purely for **displaying information to the user** — descriptive, static, and informative. It cannot, by itself, be "pressed" or "activated." > [!TIP] > **Real-World Software Examples of JLabel Usage:** > - The "Username:" text beside a `JTextField` in a login screen. > - The brand logo at the top of a banking app. > - The "Battery: 75%" status text on a mobile companion app. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Property | Default Value | | :--- | :--- | | **Background** | `null` (transparent) | | **Foreground** | `Color.UIResource` (theme-driven) | | **Font** | `Font.UIResource` (theme-driven) | | **Horizontal Alignment** | `LEADING` | | **Vertical Alignment** | `CENTER` | | **Icon Text Gap** | **4 pixels** | | **Component Orientation** | `LEFT_TO_RIGHT` | # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis of JLabel ## 2.1 Structural Anatomy — What a JLabel Internally Contains A `JLabel` is composed of **three optional display elements** stacked together: 1. **An `Icon` (Image)** — a graphical image (often a `ImageIcon`). 2. **A `String` (Text)** — the descriptive text. 3. **A Mnemonic** — an underlined keyboard shortcut character (`Alt + key`). > [!NOTE] > The default rendering order is: **Text on the RIGHT of the Icon**. This can be reversed using `setHorizontalTextPosition()`. --- ## 2.2 All Constructors of JLabel (KTU High-Yield List) | Constructor | Description | | :--- | :--- | | `JLabel()` | Creates a **blank label** with no icon and no text. | | `JLabel(String text)` | Creates a label with the **given text string**. | | `JLabel(Icon image)` | Creates a label with the **given image icon**. | | `JLabel(String text, Icon icon, int horizontalAlignment)` | Creates label with **text, image, and alignment**. | | `JLabel(String text, int horizontalAlignment)` | Creates label with **text and alignment**. | | `JLabel(Icon image, int horizontalAlignment)` | Creates label with **image and alignment**. | > [!IMPORTANT] > The valid values for `horizontalAlignment` are the `SwingConstants`: > - `SwingConstants.LEFT` (= `2`) > - `SwingConstants.CENTER` (= `0`) > - `SwingConstants.RIGHT` (= `4`) > - `SwingConstants.LEADING` (= `10`) > - `SwingConstants.TRAILING` (= `11`) --- ## 2.3 High-Yield Methods Catalog (KTU Exam Favorites) | Method | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | | `setText(String text)` | Sets the **display text** of the label. | | `getText()` | Returns the **current text** as a `String`. | | `setIcon(Icon icon)` | Sets the **image** to display. | | `getIcon()` | Returns the **current `Icon`**. | | `setHorizontalAlignment(int alignment)` | Sets the **horizontal position** within the cell. | | `setVerticalAlignment(int alignment)` | Sets the **vertical position** within the cell. | | `setHorizontalTextPosition(int position)` | Where the **text sits relative to the icon** (LEFT/CENTER/RIGHT). | | `setVerticalTextPosition(int position)` | Where the **text sits relative to the icon** (TOP/CENTER/BOTTOM). | | `setIconTextGap(int gap)` | Sets the **pixel gap** between icon and text (default **4 px**). | | `setLabelFor(Component c)` | Associates label as a **mnemonic driver** for `c`. | | `setDisplayedMnemonic(int key)` | Sets the **keyboard mnemonic** (e.g., `KeyEvent.VK_A`). | | `setDisplayedMnemonicIndex(int index)` | Sets which **character index** of text gets underlined. | | `setDisabledIcon(Icon icon)` | Icon to show when label is **disabled**. | --- ## 2.4 HTML Rendering — The Killer Feature > [!IMPORTANT] > Unlike `java.awt.Label`, a `JLabel` can render **HTML 3.2 / subset of HTML4** when its text begins with ``. **Example:** ```java JLabel label = new JLabel("Hello World"); ``` **Supported HTML tags inside JLabel:** - ``, `` (optional, auto-inserted) - ``, ``, ``, ``, `

` to `

` - `

`, `
`, `

` - `
    `, `
  • `, `
      ` > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** HTML text **CANNOT** be retrieved cleanly via `getText()` — it returns the raw HTML string. Also, alignment methods sometimes misbehave on multi-line HTML text. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Usage of JLabel | | :--- | :--- | | **Banking Software** | Displaying account balance, customer name | | **Medical Systems** | Showing patient vitals, status indicators | | **IoT Dashboards** | Sensor readings, device state | | **Educational Software** | Static instructions, headings, logos | | **Industrial HMI** | Operator panel status messages | # 🧮 Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Code Derivations > [!NOTE] > Since this is an **Object-Oriented Concepts (OEERT613)** module, the "derivations" here are **code-construction logic** — line-by-line deconstruction of how a `JLabel` object is built, configured, and integrated into a Swing frame. --- ## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Building a Fully-Configured JLabel Object **Problem Statement:** Create a label that displays the text `"Username:"` with a green user icon, right-aligned horizontally, with text appearing *below* the icon and an `Alt+U` mnemonic that transfers focus to a `JTextField`. **Analytical Step-by-Step Construction:** $$ \textbf{Step 1: Identify Required Class Imports} $$ $$ \text{import javax.swing.ImageIcon; import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JLabel; import javax.swing.JTextField; } $$ $$ \text{import javax.swing.SwingConstants;} $$ $$ \textbf{Step 2: Instantiate the Label with Text + Icon + Alignment (Constructor #4)} $$ $$ \text{JLabel userLabel = new JLabel("Username:", new ImageIcon("user.png"), SwingConstants.RIGHT);} $$ $$ \textbf{Step 3: Configure Vertical Text Position (Text BELOW icon)} $$ $$ \text{userLabel.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.BOTTOM);} $$ $$ \textbf{Step 4: Configure Horizontal Text Position (Centered under icon)} $$ $$ \text{userLabel.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER);} $$ $$ \textbf{Step 5: Set the Mnemonic Character} $$ $$ \text{userLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK\_U);} $$ $$ \textbf{Step 6: Bind Label to a Target Component (Transfer Focus)} $$ $$ \text{JTextField userField = new JTextField(15); userLabel.setLabelFor(userField);} $$ $$ \textbf{Final Resulting Code Block:} $$ ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.event.KeyEvent; public class JLabelDemo extends JFrame { JLabelDemo() { setTitle("JLabel Demonstration"); setSize(400, 200); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); JLabel userLabel = new JLabel("Username:", new ImageIcon("user.png"), SwingConstants.RIGHT); userLabel.setVerticalTextPosition(SwingConstants.BOTTOM); userLabel.setHorizontalTextPosition(SwingConstants.CENTER); userLabel.setDisplayedMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_U); JTextField userField = new JTextField(15); userLabel.setLabelFor(userField); add(userLabel); add(userField); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { new JLabelDemo(); } } ``` --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2 — HTML-Enabled Multi-Format Label **Problem Statement:** Create a `JLabel` that displays a two-line heading with bold red text and a tooltip. **Step-by-Step Logic:** $$ \textbf{Step 1: Construct Label with HTML as Text} $$ $$ \text{JLabel heading = new JLabel("KTU 2024 Scheme
      Object Oriented Concepts");} $$ $$ \textbf{Step 2: Add a Tooltip (Inherited from JComponent)} $$ $$ \text{heading.setToolTipText("This is the course title label");} $$ $$ \textbf{Resulting Output: A two-line label where the first line is bold red, second line is default. The mouse hover displays the tooltip.} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Dynamic Label Updates (Getter/Setter Pattern) **Problem Statement:** Show a `JLabel` that reflects the current number of button clicks using OOP state management. $$ \textbf{Step 1: Declare the Label with Initial Text} $$ $$ \text{JLabel counterLabel = new JLabel("Clicks: 0");} $$ $$ \textbf{Step 2: Bind to a Button's ActionListener} $$ $$ \text{button.addActionListener(e -> { int n = Integer.parseInt(counterLabel.getText().split(": ")[1]); counterLabel.setText("Clicks: " + (n + 1)); });} $$ > [!NOTE] > Here, `getText()` (the **getter**) and `setText()` (the **setter**) demonstrate **encapsulation** in OOP — the internal state (text value) is shielded and accessed only via public methods. # 🖼️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: JLabel Class Architecture (Block-Level) ```mermaid graph TD A[java.lang.Object] --> B[java.awt.Component] B --> C[java.awt.Container] C --> D[javax.swing.JComponent] D --> E[javax.swing.JLabel] E --> F[Display Properties] E --> G[Icon Management] E --> H[Mnemonic Engine] E --> I[HTML Renderer] F --> F1[setText getText] F --> F2[setHorizontalAlignment] G --> G1[setIcon getIcon] G --> G2[setIconTextGap] H --> H1[setDisplayedMnemonic] H --> H2[setLabelFor] I --> I1[HTML 3.2 Subset] I --> I2[setText with html tag] ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: JLabel Visual Anatomy (Block-Level) ```mermaid graph LR P[JLabel Boundary Box] --> T[Text Area] P --> I[Icon Area] P --> M[Mnemonic Indicator] T --> A1[Horizontal Alignment] T --> A2[Vertical Alignment] I --> G1[Icon Text Gap = 4 px] M --> K[Alt + Key Trigger] K --> FC[Forwards Focus to setLabelFor Component] ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Event & Focus Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant L as JLabel participant C as Target Component U->>L: Presses Alt + Mnemonic L->>L: setDisplayedMnemonic detects key L->>C: setLabelFor transfers focus C-->>U: Cursor appears in field Note over L: Label itself is non-focusable ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **What is `JLabel` in Java Swing? Mention any two constructors of it.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > `JLabel` is a passive display component of the `javax.swing` package used to display a short text, an image, or both. It is a subclass of `JComponent` and does not generate any action events. *(1 mark)* > > **Two constructors:** *(2 marks)* > 1. `JLabel()` — Creates an empty label. > 2. `JLabel(String text)` — Creates a label with the specified text. > 3. *(Alt)* `JLabel(Icon image, int horizontalAlignment)` --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **Explain the concept of a **mnemonic** in `JLabel`. How is it associated with another component?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > A **mnemonic** in `JLabel` is a keyboard shortcut character (combined with the `Alt` key) that allows the user to quickly move focus to a related component. *(1 mark)* > > It is set using `setDisplayedMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_X)` method, and the target component is bound using `setLabelFor(Component c)` method. When the user presses `Alt + X`, focus automatically jumps to the bound component. *(2 marks)* --- ## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Format:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B. --- ### ✒️ **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** Explain the class hierarchy of `JLabel` in Java Swing. Discuss any **three constructors** of `JLabel` with examples. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write a Java program to create a `JFrame` containing a `JLabel` that: - Displays the text **"KTU Student"** in **HTML bold format**. - Has a `ImageIcon` aligned to the **right** of the text. - Has an **icon-text gap** of **10 pixels**. - Possesses a **mnemonic `Alt+S`** that focuses a `JTextField`. *(7 marks)* --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Question A ### Part (a) — Class Hierarchy & Constructors **Class Hierarchy (3 marks):** $$ \text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Component} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Container} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JComponent} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JLabel} $$ **Three Constructors (4 marks):** **(i) `JLabel()`** Creates a blank label with no text and no icon. ```java JLabel l1 = new JLabel(); l1.setText("Hello"); ``` **(ii) `JLabel(String text)`** Creates a label initialized with the given string. ```java JLabel l2 = new JLabel("Welcome to KTU"); ``` **(iii) `JLabel(String text, Icon icon, int horizontalAlignment)`** Creates a label with text, icon, and alignment in a single call. ```java JLabel l3 = new JLabel("Login", new ImageIcon("login.png"), SwingConstants.CENTER); ``` --- ### Part (b) — Complete Java Program ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.KeyEvent; public class KTULabelApp extends JFrame { KTULabelApp() { setTitle("KTU JLabel Demo"); setSize(400, 200); setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 10)); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); // Step 1: Create label with HTML bold text JLabel label = new JLabel("KTU Student", new ImageIcon("student.png"), SwingConstants.LEFT); // Step 2: Set icon-text gap to 10 pixels label.setIconTextGap(10); // Step 3: Set mnemonic Alt+S label.setDisplayedMnemonic(KeyEvent.VK_S); // Step 4: Create target JTextField and bind JTextField field = new JTextField(15); label.setLabelFor(field); add(label); add(field); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { new KTULabelApp(); } } ``` **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - HTML text setup: **1 mark** - Icon and alignment: **1.5 marks** - Icon-text gap (10 px): **1 mark** - Mnemonic (`Alt+S`): **1.5 marks** - `setLabelFor` binding: **1 mark** - Frame setup + output: **1 mark** --- ### ✒️ **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — ALTERNATIVE** **(a)** Differentiate between `JLabel` and `java.awt.Label`. Explain **any four methods** of `JLabel`. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a `JFrame` with **three `JLabel`s** displaying "Name:", "Age:", and "Address:" respectively, each paired with a `JTextField`. Use a **grid layout** and demonstrate proper **horizontal alignment**. *(7 marks)* --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Question B ### Part (a) — Difference Table + Methods | Feature | `java.awt.Label` | `javax.swing.JLabel` | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Package | AWT (heavyweight) | Swing (lightweight) | | HTML Support | ❌ Not supported | ✅ Supported | | Mnemonic | ❌ Not supported | ✅ Supported | | Icons | Limited support | Full `Icon` support | | Pluggable Look & Feel | ❌ | ✅ | **Four Important Methods (4 marks):** 1. `setText(String)` / `getText()` — manages label text. 2. `setIcon(Icon)` / `getIcon()` — manages displayed icon. 3. `setHorizontalAlignment(int)` — sets text/icon alignment. 4. `setLabelFor(Component)` — binds label to a focusable component. --- ### Part (b) — Grid Layout Form ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; public class FormDemo extends JFrame { FormDemo() { setTitle("Student Form"); setSize(350, 200); setLayout(new GridLayout(3, 2, 10, 10)); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); add(new JLabel("Name:", SwingConstants.RIGHT)); add(new JTextField(15)); add(new JLabel("Age:", SwingConstants.RIGHT)); add(new JTextField(15)); add(new JLabel("Address:", SwingConstants.RIGHT)); add(new JTextField(15)); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { new FormDemo(); } } ``` --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting `` tag prefix** — Without ``, HTML tags are displayed as plain text. **(-2 marks)** > 2. **Confusing `setHorizontalAlignment` with `setHorizontalTextPosition`** — The first positions the **entire label content** in its cell; the second positions **text relative to icon**. **(-1 mark)** > 3. **Forgetting `setLabelFor(component)`** — Mnemonic alone won't work without binding. **(-2 marks)** > 4. **Wrong import of `SwingConstants`** — Students often write `import java.awt.SwingConstants` (which is invalid; it lives in `javax.swing`). **(-1 mark)** > 5. **Not extending `JFrame` or forgetting `setVisible(true)`** — Program compiles but produces blank screen. **(-1 mark)** --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🔹 `JLabel` is a **passive**, **non-focusable**, **display-only** Swing component. - 🔹 It can display **text, an icon, or both** simultaneously. - 🔹 **6 constructors** exist; the most powerful is `(String, Icon, int)`. - 🔹 Default **horizontal alignment** is `LEADING`; **vertical** is `CENTER`. - 🔹 Default **icon-text gap** is **4 pixels**. - 🔹 **HTML rendering** works ONLY when text begins with ``. - 🔹 **Mnemonics** require *both* `setDisplayedMnemonic()` *and* `setLabelFor()`. - 🔹 `JLabel` extends `JComponent` → which extends `Container` → `Component` → `Object`. - 🔹 `getText()` returns the **raw HTML string** if HTML was used. - 🔹 `setToolTipText()` works on JLabel (inherited from `JComponent`). - 🔹 Cannot receive `ActionEvent` — it is a *passive listener* of its own mnemonic only. - 🔹 Used universally for **form labels, status displays, headings, and image captions**.

topics.title: Swing Buttons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Buttons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Java Swing** GUI framework (package `javax.swing`), **Buttons** are interactive components derived from the abstract class `javax.swing.AbstractButton`. They allow users to trigger actions, make selections, or toggle states within a graphical user interface. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes Swing buttons into four principal concrete subclasses:

1. **JButton** — A standard command button that triggers an `ActionEvent` when pressed.
2. **JToggleButton** — A two-state button that can be selected (pressed/active) or deselected (raised/inactive).
3. **JCheckBox** — A toggle button typically displayed with a check mark; supports multiple simultaneous selections.
4. **JRadioButton** — A toggle button that is mutually exclusive within a `ButtonGroup`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy Anchor (KTU Exam Favorite):**
> `java.lang.Object` → `java.awt.Component` → `java.awt.Container` → `javax.swing.JComponent` → `javax.swing.AbstractButton` → Concrete Button classes (`JButton`, `JToggleButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JMenuItem`)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **TV Remote Control** in your hand:

- **Channel Number Buttons (0–9)** → Think of these as **JButton** instances. Each press fires a *single, instantaneous action* (change channel) and the button returns to its "normal" state.
- **Mute Button** → This is like a **JToggleButton**. Press it once → Mute ON (button stays lit). Press it again → Mute OFF (button pops back up).
- **Subtitle Options: [ ] English, [ ] Malayalam, [ ] Hindi** → These are **JCheckBox** components. You can check *any combination* — multiple selections are allowed.
- **Audio Output: ( ) Speaker, ( ) Headphone, ( ) Bluetooth** → These are **JRadioButton** components grouped together. You can pick *only one* — selecting another automatically deselects the previous.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton` are *not* direct subclasses of `JButton`. They extend `JToggleButton`, which in turn extends `AbstractButton`. Understanding this inheritance tree is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

## 1.3 Why Swing Buttons Matter in Engineering

Swing buttons are foundational to **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** design. In KTU-aligned B.Tech project work (e.g., 7th semester main project), nearly every desktop application prototype — from **library management systems** to **IoT dashboards** — uses these components. The **MVC (Model-View-Controller)** pattern in Swing is implemented via **Listener interfaces** attached to buttons, which is a direct practical demonstration of the *Observer design pattern* taught in Module 5.

---

## 1.4 Class Hierarchy at a Glance

| Level | Class | Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | `java.lang.Object` | Root of all Java classes |
| 1 | `java.awt.Component` | Base AWT class for visual components |
| 2 | `java.awt.Container` | Can hold other components |
| 3 | `javax.swing.JComponent` | Swing-specific base with pluggable look-and-feel |
| 4 | `javax.swing.AbstractButton` | **Abstract class** — defines text, icon, mnemonic, action command |
| 5 | `JButton`, `JToggleButton`, `JMenuItem` | Concrete Swing button classes |
| 6 | `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton` | Specialized `JToggleButton` subclasses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **single most exam-relevant fact**: `JButton` extends `AbstractButton` **directly**, whereas `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton` extend `AbstractButton` **indirectly** via `JToggleButton`. This distinction affects which listeners (`ActionListener` vs `ItemListener` vs `ChangeListener`) are appropriate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Swing Button Types — Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 JButton (Command Button)

**Operational Logic:**
1. User clicks the button.
2. The button fires an `ActionEvent`.
3. The registered `ActionListener.actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` method is invoked.
4. Button visually "bounces back" (returns to unpressed state).

**Key Constructors:**
- `JButton()` — Creates a button with no text or icon.
- `JButton(String text)` — Creates a button with the specified text.
- `JButton(Icon icon)` — Creates a button with the specified icon.
- `JButton(String text, Icon icon)` — Creates a button with both text and icon.

**Key Methods (inherited from `AbstractButton`):**
- `setText(String text)` / `getText()` — Manipulates button label.
- `setIcon(Icon icon)` / `getIcon()` — Manipulates button icon.
- `setEnabled(boolean b)` — Enables or disables the button.
- `setMnemonic(char mnemonic)` — Sets keyboard shortcut (e.g., `setMnemonic('S')` for **Alt+S**).
- `addActionListener(ActionListener l)` — Registers a listener.
- `setActionCommand(String command)` — Sets a logical command name for event identification.

### 2.1.2 JToggleButton (Two-State Button)

**Operational Logic:**
1. First click → button stays pressed (state = `true`, `isSelected() = true`).
2. Second click → button pops back (state = `false`).
3. Fires an `ItemEvent` (not `ActionEvent`) — this is the **KTU trap**.

**Why JToggleButton matters:** It's the *parent* class of `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton`. Any code that works with `JToggleButton` polymorphically also works with checkboxes and radio buttons.

### 2.1.3 JCheckBox (Multiple-Selection Toggle)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Click → small square gets a checkmark (or becomes filled with platform-specific glyph).
2. Fires both `ActionEvent` AND `ItemEvent`.
3. **Multiple checkboxes can be selected simultaneously** — they are independent.

**Critical Constructors:**
- `JCheckBox(String text)`
- `JCheckBox(String text, boolean selected)` — Initial state controlled.
- `JCheckBox(Icon icon)`
- `JCheckBox(String text, Icon icon, boolean selected)`

### 2.1.4 JRadioButton (Mutually Exclusive Toggle)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Click → circular indicator fills; sibling radio buttons in the same `ButtonGroup` are **automatically deselected**.
2. **MUST be added to a `ButtonGroup`** to enforce mutual exclusion.
3. Without `ButtonGroup`, radio buttons behave like independent checkboxes — a common student mistake.

> [!NOTE]
> **ButtonGroup is NOT a visual component.** It is a *logical* grouping mechanism (extends `Object`). You add `ButtonGroup` instances to a `Container`, but the radio buttons themselves are added to the `Container` (e.g., `JPanel`). This confuses students — remember: **logical grouping ≠ visual containment**.

## 2.2 Listener Interfaces — The "Why" Behind Event Handling

| Listener Interface | Event Type | Used With | Key Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `ActionListener` | `ActionEvent` | `JButton`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JMenuItem` | `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` |
| `ItemListener` | `ItemEvent` | `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JToggleButton` | `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` |
| `ChangeListener` | `ChangeEvent` | `JSlider`, `JProgressBar` (not buttons!) | `stateChanged(ChangeEvent e)` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often register `ActionListener` on a `JToggleButton` and expect to detect *state change*. This only fires the listener once per click — the same behavior as `JButton`. To detect whether a toggle button is *selected or deselected*, you must use `ItemListener.itemStateChanged()` and inspect `ItemEvent.getStateChange()`.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Constructor & Method Reference)

| Component | Constructor | Selection Check | Grouping Required? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `JButton` | `JButton(String)`, `JButton(Icon)`, `JButton(String, Icon)` | N/A (no state) | No |
| `JToggleButton` | `JToggleButton(String)`, `JToggleButton(String, boolean)` | `isSelected()` | No |
| `JCheckBox` | `JCheckBox(String)`, `JCheckBox(String, boolean)` | `isSelected()` | No |
| `JRadioButton` | `JRadioButton(String)`, `JRadioButton(String, boolean)` | `isSelected()` | **Yes — `ButtonGroup`** |
| `ButtonGroup` | `ButtonGroup()` | N/A | N/A |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Applications:** Form submissions, login dialogs (JButton "Submit").
- **Configuration Panels:** Software settings — checkboxes for "Enable Notifications," radio buttons for theme selection.
- **Industrial HMI (Human-Machine Interface):** SCADA systems use toggle buttons for pump on/off controls.
- **Accessibility:** Swing supports keyboard mnemonics, making GUIs navigable without a mouse — a key HCI principle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This is a Java/CS topic — therefore **Java code snippets ARE permitted and required** (per Protocol Section III, Constraint #1: prohibition applies only to non-CS subjects).

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Button

| If the requirement is... | Use... | Because... |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Single, instantaneous action (Save, Submit, Cancel) | `JButton` | No persistent state needed |
| Two persistent states (Bold text on/off, Mute on/off) | `JToggleButton` | Must remember pressed/unpressed |
| Zero-or-more independent options (Hobbies, Features) | `JCheckBox` | Multiple selections valid |
| Exactly one of N options (Gender, Payment Method) | `JRadioButton` + `ButtonGroup` | Mutual exclusion enforced |
| Trigger via keyboard shortcut | Any + `setMnemonic()` | Inherited from `AbstractButton` |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough 1: JButton with ActionListener

**Problem Statement:** Create a window with a button labeled "Click Me". When clicked, display the number of times it has been clicked using a `JLabel`.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Import the required Swing and AWT packages.
```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;
```

**Step 2:** Declare the class extending `JFrame` and implementing `ActionListener`.
```java
public class ButtonDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    private JButton clickButton;
    private JLabel countLabel;
    private int clickCount = 0;  // model state
```

**Step 3:** Initialize components in the constructor.
```java
    public ButtonDemo() {
        setTitle("JButton Demo");
        setSize(300, 150);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        clickButton = new JButton("Click Me");
        clickButton.addActionListener(this);  // register THIS as listener

        countLabel = new JLabel("Button clicked 0 times.");

        add(clickButton);
        add(countLabel);
        setVisible(true);
    }
```

**Step 4:** Implement the `actionPerformed` method — the **callback method** triggered by the JVM's event dispatch thread.
```java
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        clickCount++;
        countLabel.setText("Button clicked " + clickCount + " times.");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new ButtonDemo();
    }
}
```

**Logic Walkthrough:**
- The button fires `ActionEvent` → JVM routes it to `actionPerformed` → counter increments → label updates.
- This is the **Observer design pattern** in action: the button is the *Subject*, the frame is the *Observer*.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough 2: JCheckBox with ItemListener

**Problem Statement:** Create a window with three checkboxes: "Java", "Python", "C++". Display the user's selected languages dynamically.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Class declaration with `ItemListener` interface.
```java
public class CheckBoxDemo extends JFrame implements ItemListener {
    private JCheckBox javaBox, pythonBox, cppBox;
    private JLabel resultLabel;
```

**Step 2:** Constructor — initialize three independent checkboxes.
```java
    public CheckBoxDemo() {
        setTitle("JCheckBox Demo");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        javaBox   = new JCheckBox("Java");
        pythonBox = new JCheckBox("Python");
        cppBox    = new JCheckBox("C++");

        javaBox.addItemListener(this);
        pythonBox.addItemListener(this);
        cppBox.addItemListener(this);

        resultLabel = new JLabel("Selected: None");

        add(javaBox); add(pythonBox); add(cppBox); add(resultLabel);
        setVisible(true);
    }
```

**Step 3:** Handle the item state change.
```java
    @Override
    public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) {
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Selected: ");
        if (javaBox.isSelected())   sb.append("Java ");
        if (pythonBox.isSelected()) sb.append("Python ");
        if (cppBox.isSelected())    sb.append("C++ ");
        if (sb.toString().equals("Selected: "))
            sb.append("None");
        resultLabel.setText(sb.toString().trim());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CheckBoxDemo();
    }
}
```

**Logic Walkthrough:**
- `ItemEvent.getStateChange()` returns either `ItemEvent.SELECTED` or `ItemEvent.DESELECTED`.
- We use `isSelected()` on the source object instead — a cleaner, more readable approach.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough 3: JRadioButton with ButtonGroup

**Problem Statement:** Create a "Payment Method" form with three radio buttons: Credit Card, Debit Card, UPI. Only one must be selectable.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Import and declare.
```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class RadioButtonDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    private JRadioButton creditCard, debitCard, upi;
    private ButtonGroup paymentGroup;
    private JLabel displayLabel;
```

**Step 2:** Initialize — note the **two separate addition steps** (logical group + visual container).
```java
    public RadioButtonDemo() {
        setTitle("JRadioButton Demo");
        setSize(300, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        creditCard = new JRadioButton("Credit Card");
        debitCard  = new JRadioButton("Debit Card");
        upi        = new JRadioButton("UPI");

        // STEP A: Create logical group
        paymentGroup = new ButtonGroup();
        paymentGroup.add(creditCard);
        paymentGroup.add(debitCard);
        paymentGroup.add(upi);

        // STEP B: Add buttons to visual container
        add(creditCard); add(debitCard); add(upi);

        displayLabel = new JLabel("Selected: None");
        add(displayLabel);

        creditCard.addActionListener(this);
        debitCard.addActionListener(this);
        upi.addActionListener(this);

        setVisible(true);
    }
```

**Step 3:** Listener — same pattern works for radio buttons using `ActionListener`.
```java
    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        JRadioButton source = (JRadioButton) e.getSource();
        displayLabel.setText("Selected: " + source.getText());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new RadioButtonDemo();
    }
}
```

**Critical Derivation Note:**
$$\text{Mutual Exclusion} \iff \text{All radio buttons share the same } \texttt{ButtonGroup} \text{ instance}$$

If you create **two** `ButtonGroup` objects and add two buttons to each, the cross-group mutual exclusion is broken.

## 3.5 Mnemonic & ActionCommand — Advanced Features

**Mnemonic (Keyboard Shortcut):**
```java
JButton saveBtn = new JButton("Save");
saveBtn.setMnemonic('S');  // Alt+S triggers the button
```

**ActionCommand (Event Identification):**
```java
JButton yesBtn = new JButton("Yes");
JButton noBtn  = new JButton("No");
yesBtn.setActionCommand("CONFIRM");
noBtn.setActionCommand("CANCEL");

yesBtn.addActionListener(e -> {
    if (e.getActionCommand().equals("CONFIRM")) {
        // handle yes
    }
});
```

This pattern is critical when **multiple buttons share one listener** — a KTU exam favorite.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Class Hierarchy Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["java.lang.Object"] --> B["java.awt.Component"]
    B --> C["java.awt.Container"]
    C --> D["javax.swing.JComponent"]
    D --> E["javax.swing.AbstractButton"]
    E --> F["JButton"]
    E --> G["JToggleButton"]
    G --> H["JCheckBox"]
    G --> I["JRadioButton"]
    E --> J["JMenuItem"]
    J --> K["JMenu"]
    J --> L["JCheckBoxMenuItem"]
    J --> M["JRadioButtonMenuItem"]
```

## 4.2 Event Flow Diagram (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant B as JButton
    participant E as JVM Event Queue
    participant L as ActionListener
    participant C as Controller Code

    U->>B: 1. Mouse Click / Keyboard Press
    B->>E: 2. Fire ActionEvent
    E->>L: 3. Dispatch via Event Dispatch Thread
    L->>C: 4. Invoke actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)
    C->>C: 5. Execute business logic
    C-->>B: 6. Update UI state (optional)
```

## 4.3 Button Selection Logic Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Need a Swing Button"] --> Q1{"Does state persist after click?"}
    Q1 -->|"No (single action)"| JBtn["Use JButton"]
    Q1 -->|"Yes (on/off)"| Q2{"Multiple selections allowed?"}
    Q2 -->|"Yes (independent)"| Chk["Use JCheckBox"]
    Q2 -->|"No (mutually exclusive)"| Q3{"Group size > 1?"}
    Q3 -->|"Yes"| Rad["Use JRadioButton + ButtonGroup"]
    Q3 -->|"No (standalone toggle)"| Tog["Use JToggleButton"]
```

## 4.4 ButtonGroup vs Container Relationship

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Visual["Visual Container (JPanel / JFrame)"]
        RB1["JRadioButton A"]
        RB2["JRadioButton B"]
        RB3["JRadioButton C"]
    end
    subgraph Logical["Logical Group (ButtonGroup)"]
        BG["ButtonGroup Instance"]
    end
    RB1 -.->|add| BG
    RB2 -.->|add| BG
    RB3 -.->|add| BG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture Insight:** The `ButtonGroup` is invisible. It exists purely to enforce mutual exclusion by intercepting the `setSelected(true)` call on any member button and cascading `setSelected(false)` to siblings. The buttons themselves are visually rendered by the `Container`.

## 4.5 Listener Registration Pattern

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["AbstractButton"] -->|addActionListener| B["ActionListener"]
    A -->|addItemListener| C["ItemListener"]
    A -->|addChangeListener| D["ChangeListener"]
    B -->|actionPerformed| E["ActionEvent handler"]
    C -->|itemStateChanged| F["ItemEvent handler"]
    D -->|stateChanged| G["ChangeEvent handler"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Inheritance Hierarchy of Swing Buttons **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Question:** Draw and explain the class hierarchy of Swing buttons from `JComponent` to `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton`.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The hierarchy is as follows:
1. `javax.swing.JComponent` (1 mark)
2. `javax.swing.AbstractButton` extends `JComponent` (1 mark)
3. `JToggleButton` extends `AbstractButton`; `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton` extend `JToggleButton` (1 mark)

This makes `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton` **indirect subclasses** of `AbstractButton`, while `JButton` is a direct subclass.

---

### Question 2: Difference Between JCheckBox and JRadioButton **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Question:** Differentiate between `JCheckBox` and `JRadioButton` with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **JCheckBox (1.5 marks):** Used for multiple independent selections. Example: Selecting hobbies — "Reading", "Sports", "Music" — user may pick any combination.
- **JRadioButton (1.5 marks):** Used for mutually exclusive single selection. Must be wrapped in a `ButtonGroup`. Example: Selecting gender — "Male" or "Female" or "Other" — only one may be selected.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Complete JCheckBox Application with Event Handling **[14 Marks]**
