**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the constructors and important methods of `JCheckBox` class with a neat diagram of its hierarchy.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Java Swing program that creates a window containing three checkboxes: "DSA", "OS", "DBMS". When the user selects/deselects any checkbox, the total fee (DSA=5000, OS=4000, DBMS=4500) must be displayed in a `JLabel` in real time.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Constructors and Methods of JCheckBox (7 marks):**

**Constructors (3 marks):**
- `JCheckBox()` — empty checkbox
- `JCheckBox(String text)` — with label text
- `JCheckBox(String text, boolean selected)` — with initial state
- `JCheckBox(Icon icon)` — with image
- `JCheckBox(String text, Icon icon)` — with both
- `JCheckBox(String text, Icon icon, boolean selected)` — full constructor

**Important Methods (3 marks):**
- `boolean isSelected()` — returns true if checked
- `void setSelected(boolean b)` — programmatically select/deselect
- `void setText(String text)` — set label
- `void addItemListener(ItemListener l)` — register state change listener
- `void addActionListener(ActionListener l)` — register action listener

**Hierarchy Diagram (1 mark):**
```
Object → Component → Container → JComponent → AbstractButton
                                              → JToggleButton
                                                  → JCheckBox
```

**(b) Java Program (7 marks):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class FeeCalculator extends JFrame implements ItemListener {
    private JCheckBox dsaBox, osBox, dbmsBox;
    private JLabel totalLabel;
    private static final int DSA_FEE = 5000;
    private static final int OS_FEE  = 4000;
    private static final int DBMS_FEE = 4500;

    public FeeCalculator() {
        setTitle("Course Fee Calculator");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        dsaBox  = new JCheckBox("DSA - Rs." + DSA_FEE);
        osBox   = new JCheckBox("OS  - Rs." + OS_FEE);
        dbmsBox = new JCheckBox("DBMS- Rs." + DBMS_FEE);

        dsaBox.addItemListener(this);
        osBox.addItemListener(this);
        dbmsBox.addItemListener(this);

        totalLabel = new JLabel("Total Fee: Rs. 0");

        add(dsaBox); add(osBox); add(dbmsBox); add(totalLabel);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e) {
        int total = 0;
        if (dsaBox.isSelected())  total += DSA_FEE;
        if (osBox.isSelected())   total += OS_FEE;
        if (dbmsBox.isSelected()) total += DBMS_FEE;
        totalLabel.setText("Total Fee: Rs. " + total);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new FeeCalculator();
    }
}
```

**Marking Scheme (7 marks):**
- Correct class declaration and imports — 1 mark
- Three checkboxes created with proper text — 1 mark
- ItemListener implemented and registered — 1 mark
- Fee calculation logic using `isSelected()` — 2 marks
- JLabel updated dynamically — 1 mark
- Correct main method and compilation-ready code — 1 mark

---

### Question B: ButtonGroup and JRadioButton Application **[14 Marks]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is `ButtonGroup` in Swing? Explain why it is essential for `JRadioButton`. Write a Java program to demonstrate three radio buttons — "BCA", "B.Tech", "M.Tech" — where selecting one displays the chosen course in a label.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of `ActionListener` and `ItemListener` interfaces in Swing with examples. Why can't `ChangeListener` be used with buttons?

#### Model Solution:

**(a) ButtonGroup Explanation and Program (7 marks):**

**Theory (3 marks):** `ButtonGroup` is a logical grouping class (extends `Object`, **not** a component) that enforces mutual exclusion among toggle buttons. Without it, `JRadioButton` instances behave like independent `JCheckBox`es. The group is added to logically, while the buttons themselves are added to the visual `Container`.

**Program (4 marks):**
```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CourseSelector extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    private JRadioButton bcaBtn, btechBtn, mtechBtn;
    private ButtonGroup courseGroup;
    private JLabel displayLabel;

    public CourseSelector() {
        setTitle("Course Selector");
        setSize(300, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        bcaBtn   = new JRadioButton("BCA");
        btechBtn = new JRadioButton("B.Tech");
        mtechBtn = new JRadioButton("M.Tech");

        courseGroup = new ButtonGroup();
        courseGroup.add(bcaBtn);
        courseGroup.add(btechBtn);
        courseGroup.add(mtechBtn);

        add(bcaBtn); add(btechBtn); add(mtechBtn);
        displayLabel = new JLabel("Selected: None");
        add(displayLabel);

        bcaBtn.addActionListener(this);
        btechBtn.addActionListener(this);
        mtechBtn.addActionListener(this);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    @Override
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        JRadioButton src = (JRadioButton) e.getSource();
        displayLabel.setText("Selected: " + src.getText());
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CourseSelector();
    }
}
```

**(b) Listener Interfaces Explanation (7 marks):**

**ActionListener (2.5 marks):** Triggered when a button is activated (clicked or Enter pressed). Fires an `ActionEvent`. Used with `JButton`, `JMenuItem`, and also works with toggle buttons. The `actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)` method receives the event. The event's `getSource()` returns the originating component.

**ItemListener (2.5 marks):** Triggered when a toggle button's *selection state* changes. Fires an `ItemEvent`. Used with `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, and `JToggleButton`. The `itemStateChanged(ItemEvent e)` method receives the event. We can call `e.getStateChange()` to get `ItemEvent.SELECTED` or `ItemEvent.DESELECTED`.

**Why not ChangeListener? (2 marks):** `ChangeListener` is meant for components whose value changes *continuously* (like `JSlider`, `JSpinner`, `JProgressBar`). Buttons fire discrete, instantaneous events. The Swing architecture routes button events through `ActionEvent` and `ItemEvent` channels, not `ChangeEvent`. Using `ChangeListener` on a button will compile but never fire.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing inheritance:** Writing "`JCheckBox` extends `JButton`" — this is **WRONG**. `JCheckBox` extends `JToggleButton`. (Lose 1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Adding `ButtonGroup` to a container:** Many students write `add(buttonGroup)` — this causes a compile error since `ButtonGroup` is not a `Component`. Add buttons to the container; add the same buttons to the `ButtonGroup` separately. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 3. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`:** The window won't close on the "X" click without this. (Lose 0.5 mark — minor deduction but reflects real-world testing awareness.)
> 4. **Using `==` instead of `.equals()` for string comparison** in `getActionCommand()` checks. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 5. **Not implementing the listener interface** — writing `addActionListener(null)` or passing a wrong type causes runtime NullPointerException. (Lose 2 marks.)

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ All Swing buttons inherit from `javax.swing.AbstractButton`.
- ✅ `JButton` extends `AbstractButton` **directly**; `JCheckBox` & `JRadioButton` extend via `JToggleButton`.
- ✅ `JButton` → use **ActionListener**.
- ✅ `JCheckBox` / `JRadioButton` → use **ItemListener** to detect state, **ActionListener** to detect click.
- ✅ `ButtonGroup` enforces **mutual exclusion** but is **not a visual component**.
- ✅ `setMnemonic(char)` enables **Alt+key** shortcuts (inherited from `AbstractButton`).
- ✅ `setActionCommand(String)` distinguishes between multiple buttons sharing one listener.
- ✅ `ChangeListener` is for **continuous-value** components (sliders, spinners) — **NOT** for buttons.
- ✅ Event handling is the practical realization of the **Observer Design Pattern**.
- ✅ `JToggleButton` is the conceptual "parent" for two-state buttons — understand it well; it appears in viva questions.
- ✅ Always close the JFrame explicitly: `setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`.
- ✅ Java is case-sensitive — `Jbutton` ≠ `JButton`. Compilation errors are unforgiving.

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Swing Buttons**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: JText Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** JText Field # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **`JTextField`** is a lightweight Swing component defined in the `javax.swing` package that allows the user to enter and edit a **single line of unformatted text**. It is a direct subclass of `javax.swing.text.JTextComponent` and inherits the foundational model-view architecture of editable text components, while extending `javax.swing.JComponent` for look-and-feel rendering. Formally, its inheritance hierarchy is: $$ \text{java.lang.Object} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Component} \rightarrow \text{java.awt.Container} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JComponent} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.text.JTextComponent} \rightarrow \text{javax.swing.JTextField} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** `JTextField` is part of the **Swing (JFC – Java Foundation Classes)** API. It replaced the legacy AWT `TextField` class and is a **lightweight** component (drawn entirely by Java, not relying on native OS peers). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine a **single-line entry box on a paper form** — like the field where you write your *name* or *phone number* in a bank withdrawal slip. You can type, backspace, select text, and edit — but you **cannot press Enter and start a new line inside the same box**. That is exactly what a `JTextField` represents in a GUI: | Real-World Form Element | GUI Equivalent | |---|---| | Single blank line for "Name" | `JTextField` | | Multi-line paragraph (Address) | `JTextArea` | | Masked password field | `JPasswordField` (subclass of `JTextField`) | | Dropdown choices | `JComboBox` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **`JPasswordField` is a direct subclass of `JTextField`** — this is a frequently asked concept in KTU exams. It overrides the `echoChar` to mask input. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants - **Default Columns (width)**: **0** (zero width is allowed; layout manager determines actual size based on preferred size). - **Default Horizontal Alignment**: `JTextField.LEFT` (which is `2`). - **Default Echo Character**: `\0` (null character, meaning characters are displayed as typed). - **Default Font**: Inherits from `JComponent` — typically **Sans Serif, Plain, 12 pt**. > [!TIP] > A column in `JTextField` is **NOT** the number of characters that can be typed. It is a width hint used by the layout manager, approximating the width of the platform's standard font's `m` character (or `0` for proportional fonts in modern JDKs). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a `JTextField` Works Internally The `JTextField` operates on the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** architecture, with the following key actors: 1. **Model (`Document` / `PlainDocument`)** - Stores the actual text content. - Tracks changes, insertions, and deletions. - Default model size limit: **unlimited** (constrained only by available memory). 2. **View (`FieldView` / `BasicTextFieldUI`)** - Responsible for rendering the text on screen. - Handles caret blinking, selection highlighting, and cursor positioning. 3. **Controller (`TextController` — internal)** - Manages keyboard and mouse events. - Translates user input (keystrokes, paste, cut) into model mutations. 4. **Pluggable LookAndFeel (`UIResource`)** - Allows the same `JTextField` to appear native on Windows, Metal on cross-platform, Nimbus on Linux, etc. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Key Point:** The `Document` model is the **single source of truth**. The `JTextField` itself acts as a façade — a thin wrapper over the `Document`. This is why `getText()` returns a snapshot from the model, not a stored variable. ## 2.2 Constructor Catalog (High-Yield for KTU Exams) | Constructor | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | `JTextField()` | Creates a new empty `TextField` with default columns (0). | Dynamic layouts. | | `JTextField(String text)` | Creates a `TextField` initialized with given text. | Pre-filled forms. | | `JTextField(int columns)` | Creates an empty `TextField` with specified column width. | Fixed-width forms. | | `JTextField(String text, int columns)` | Creates a `TextField` with both text and width. | Pre-filled + sized. | | `JTextField(Document doc, String text, int columns)` | Creates a `TextField` using a custom `Document` model. | Advanced — custom validators. | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Method Cheat Sheet | Method Signature | Return Type | Purpose | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | `getText()` | `String` | Returns the current text content. | ★★★★★ | | `setText(String t)` | `void` | Replaces current text with `t`. | ★★★★★ | | `getColumns()` | `int` | Returns the number of columns. | ★★★★ | | `setColumns(int c)` | `void` | Sets preferred width. | ★★★★ | | `setEditable(boolean b)` | `void` | Enables/disables user editing. | ★★★★ | | `isEditable()` | `boolean` | Checks if field is editable. | ★★★ | | `setHorizontalAlignment(int a)` | `void` | Aligns text (LEFT/CENTER/RIGHT/LEADING/TRAILING). | ★★★★ | | `getHorizontalAlignment()` | `int` | Returns alignment constant. | ★★★ | | `addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | `void` | Registers listener for **Enter key** press. | ★★★★★ | | `getActionListeners()` | `ActionListener[]` | Returns registered listeners. | ★★ | | `setActionCommand(String cmd)` | `void` | Sets command string for the action event. | ★★★ | | `getActionCommand()` | `String` | Returns the action command. | ★★ | | `selectAll()` | `void` | Selects all text in the field. | ★★★ | | `setSelectionStart(int)`, `setSelectionEnd(int)` | `void` | Programmatic selection. | ★★ | | `setFont(Font f)` | `void` (inherited from `JComponent`) | Sets display font. | ★★★ | | `setBackground(Color c)` | `void` (inherited) | Sets background color. | ★★★ | | `setForeground(Color c)` | `void` (inherited) | Sets text color. | ★★★ | | `requestFocusInWindow()` | `void` (inherited) | Gives keyboard focus to the field. | ★★★ | ## 2.4 Alignment Constants (Statics — Must Memorize) | Constant | Value | Visual Result | |---|---|---| | `JTextField.LEFT` | `2` | Text aligned to left edge. | | `JTextField.CENTER` | `0` | Text centered. | | `JTextField.RIGHT` | `1` | Text aligned to right edge. | | `JTextField.LEADING` | `10` | LTR: Left, RTL: Right (locale-aware). | | `JTextField.TRAILING` | `11` | LTR: Right, RTL: Left (locale-aware). | > [!WARNING] > **Do NOT confuse** `RIGHT` (`1`) with `TRAILING` (`11`) or `LEADING` (`10`). They are NOT the same — `LEADING/TRAILING` are **locale-aware** and change behavior based on component orientation. ## 2.5 Event Handling — The Critical Link `JTextField` fires **two distinct event types** that KTU students must distinguish: 1. **`ActionEvent`** — fired **only when the user presses the Enter key** inside the field. Captured via `addActionListener()`. 2. **`DocumentEvent` / `CaretEvent`** — fired on **every text change** or caret movement. Captured via `Document.addDocumentListener()`. > [!NOTE] > This distinction is **the most commonly tested concept** in KTU Module 4 viva and written exams. "Pressing Enter in a JTextField triggers an ActionEvent" is a one-line answer worth 3 marks. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Login Forms**: Username and password fields (with `JPasswordField` subclass). - **Data Entry Systems**: Numeric input for calculators, billing software, inventory systems. - **Search Bars**: Live search with `DocumentListener` firing on every keystroke (debounced in production). - **Configuration Panels**: Settings dialogs in IDEs and developer tools. - **IoT & Embedded GUIs**: Sensor readouts, threshold-entry controls. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Code Analysis Since this is a CS curriculum topic (Object Oriented Concepts with Java), this section provides **exhaustive, step-by-step code construction and analysis** — equivalent in rigor to a mathematical derivation. ## 3.1 Example 1: Basic `JTextField` with ActionListener (Login Scenario) **Problem Statement:** Build a Swing window with a `JTextField` for entering a username. When the user types a name and presses **Enter**, the program should display a greeting in the console. ### Step 1: Identify Required Classes We need: - `JFrame` — the main window. - `JTextField` — the input field. - `JLabel` — to display the field's purpose. - `ActionListener` (functional interface) — to handle Enter key. ### Step 2: Identify Required Imports ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JTextField; import javax.swing.JLabel; import javax.swing.SwingConstants; import java.awt.FlowLayout; import java.awt.event.ActionEvent; import java.awt.event.ActionListener; ``` ### Step 3: Construct the Class Skeleton ```java public class LoginDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener { JLabel label; JTextField nameField; LoginDemo() { // Constructor logic } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { // Event handler logic } public static void main(String[] args) { new LoginDemo(); } } ``` ### Step 4: Initialize the Frame Properties ```java LoginDemo() { setTitle("Login Window"); setSize(400, 200); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); ``` ### Step 5: Create and Configure the JTextField ```java label = new JLabel("Enter Username: "); nameField = new JTextField(15); // 15 columns wide nameField.setHorizontalAlignment(JTextField.CENTER); nameField.setActionCommand("SUBMIT"); ``` ### Step 6: Register the ActionListener ```java nameField.addActionListener(this); // 'this' is the JFrame implementing ActionListener ``` ### Step 7: Add Components to the Frame ```java add(label); add(nameField); setVisible(true); } ``` ### Step 8: Implement the Event Handler ```java public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { String cmd = e.getActionCommand(); if (cmd.equals("SUBMIT")) { String username = nameField.getText(); System.out.println("Welcome, " + username + "!"); } } ``` ### Step 9: Complete Program (Verified & Executable) ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JTextField; import javax.swing.JLabel; import java.awt.FlowLayout; import java.awt.event.ActionEvent; import java.awt.event.ActionListener; public class LoginDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener { JLabel label; JTextField nameField; LoginDemo() { setTitle("Login Window"); setSize(400, 150); setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 20)); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); label = new JLabel("Enter Username: "); nameField = new JTextField(15); nameField.setHorizontalAlignment(JTextField.CENTER); nameField.setActionCommand("SUBMIT"); nameField.addActionListener(this); add(label); add(nameField); setVisible(true); } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { if (e.getActionCommand().equals("SUBMIT")) { System.out.println("Welcome, " + nameField.getText() + "!"); } } public static void main(String[] args) { new LoginDemo(); } } ``` ### Step 10: Execution Flow Trace 1. **Program start** → `main()` calls `new LoginDemo()`. 2. **Constructor executes** → Frame, label, and `JTextField` are built and added. 3. **Window is displayed** → User sees the label and an empty 15-column field. 4. **User types** "Alice" → Each keystroke updates the `Document` model, but **no `ActionEvent` fires** (only the caret moves). 5. **User presses Enter** → `ActionEvent` is dispatched; `actionPerformed()` runs. 6. **Output** → "Welcome, Alice!" is printed to the console. ## 3.2 Example 2: Live Character Counter Using `DocumentListener` **Problem Statement:** Create a `JTextField` of maximum 20 characters, with a label that shows the live count of characters typed. ```java import javax.swing.JFrame; import javax.swing.JTextField; import javax.swing.JLabel; import javax.swing.event.DocumentEvent; import javax.swing.event.DocumentListener; import java.awt.FlowLayout; public class CharCounter extends JFrame { JTextField inputField; JLabel counterLabel; CharCounter() { setTitle("Live Character Counter"); setSize(400, 120); setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 20)); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); inputField = new JTextField(20); counterLabel = new JLabel("Characters: 0 / 20"); // Step A: Attach DocumentListener to the field's Document inputField.getDocument().addDocumentListener(new DocumentListener() { public void insertUpdate(DocumentEvent e) { updateCount(); } public void removeUpdate(DocumentEvent e) { updateCount(); } public void changedUpdate(DocumentEvent e) { updateCount(); } // for attribute changes }); add(inputField); add(counterLabel); setVisible(true); } private void updateCount() { int len = inputField.getText().length(); counterLabel.setText("Characters: " + len + " / 20"); } public static void main(String[] args) { new CharCounter(); } } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Insight:** `DocumentListener` fires on **every** text mutation, while `ActionListener` fires **only on Enter**. This is the foundation of all real-time validation (e.g., password strength meters, search-as-you-type). ## 3.3 Example 3: Using Lambda Expressions (Java 8+ Style) Since `ActionListener` is a **functional interface** (single abstract method), KTU 2024 may expect the lambda form: ```java nameField.addActionListener(e -> { String text = nameField.getText(); System.out.println("Submitted: " + text); }); ``` This **reduces 6+ lines to 2 lines** and is fully equivalent to the anonymous inner class form. ## 3.4 Method-by-Method Logical Mapping Table | KTU Question Type | Method/Concept to Use | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | "Read input from a field" | `getText()` | Retrieves current model content as a `String`. | | "Pre-populate a field" | `setText("value")` or constructor with `String` arg. | Initializes the model with given data. | | "Make the field non-editable" | `setEditable(false)` | Disables user input; data is still readable. | | "Detect Enter key press" | `addActionListener(...)` | Only `ActionEvent` is fired on Enter in a `JTextField`. | | "Detect every text change" | `getDocument().addDocumentListener(...)` | Fires on every model mutation. | | "Display text in red" | `setForeground(Color.RED)` | Visual cue, inherited from `JComponent`. | | "Align text to the right" | `setHorizontalAlignment(JTextField.RIGHT)` | Visual alignment. | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Inheritance Hierarchy of `JTextField` ```mermaid graph TD A["java.lang.Object"] --> B["java.awt.Component"] B --> C["java.awt.Container"] C --> D["javax.swing.JComponent"] D --> E["javax.swing.text.JTextComponent"] E --> F["javax.swing.JTextField"] F --> G["javax.swing.JPasswordField"] style A fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#333 style B fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#2874a6 style C fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#2874a6 style D fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72 style E fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#154360 style F fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608 style G fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f ``` **Reading Guide:** - **Gray node** = Java base class. - **Light blue** = AWT classes. - **Medium blue** = Swing root. - **Deeper blue** = Text component root. - **Yellow** = `JTextField` (the focus of this module). - **Red** = Subclass `JPasswordField`. ## 4.2 MVC Architecture of `JTextField` ```mermaid graph LR U[User Input] -->|Keyboard/Mouse| V[View
BasicTextFieldUI] V -->|Update Request| M[Model
PlainDocument] M -->|Notify Change| V V -->|Render| D[Display Screen] M -.->|Fires DocumentEvent| L[DocumentListener] M -.->|Fires ActionEvent on Enter| A[ActionListener] style U fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608 style V fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1b4f72 style M fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d style D fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f style L fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21 style A fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21 ``` **Key Insight:** The **Model** (`PlainDocument`) is the single source of truth. The View queries it for rendering, and both the `DocumentListener` and `ActionListener` are downstream observers of the same model. ## 4.3 Event Flow Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant TF as JTextField participant M as Document participant AL as ActionListener participant DL as DocumentListener U->>TF: Type character 'A' TF->>M: insertString(offset, "A", attrs) M->>DL: fire insertUpdate(event) DL-->>U: counterLabel updates U->>TF: Press Enter key TF->>AL: fire actionPerformed(event) AL-->>U: Console prints "Welcome, Alice!" U->>TF: Type 'B' (after Enter) TF->>M: insertString(...) M->>DL: fire insertUpdate(event) Note over AL: ActionListener NOT triggered ``` **Critical Note:** Notice that the `ActionListener` is **only** invoked on Enter — typing characters does **NOT** trigger it, only the `DocumentListener`. ## 4.4 Component Layout Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TB subgraph JF["JFrame: LoginDemo"] direction TB L1["JLabel: 'Enter Username:'"] TF1["JTextField: 15 columns
Alignment: CENTER
ActionCommand: SUBMIT"] L2["JLabel: 'Password:'"] PF1["JPasswordField: 15 columns
EchoChar: *"] BTN["JButton: 'Login'"] end TF1 -->|addActionListener| EL["Event Dispatch Thread"] PF1 -->|addActionListener| EL BTN -->|addActionListener| EL EL -->|actionPerformed| H["Handler: validateUser()"] style JF fill:#ebf5fb,stroke:#2874a6 style L1 fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608 style TF1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608 style L2 fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608 style PF1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f style BTN fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72 style EL fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d style H fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21 ``` **Reading Guide:** This represents a complete login form architecture. The `JPasswordField` is a `JTextField` subclass with `echoChar` set to `'*'`. All three components route their events to the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)** which invokes the `Handler`. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]** **Q:** What is the difference between `ActionEvent` and `DocumentEvent` in the context of a `JTextField`? Which listener interface is used to handle each? **Model Answer (Step-by-Step):** | Aspect | `ActionEvent` | `DocumentEvent` | |---|---|---| | **When fired** | Only when user presses **Enter key** inside the `JTextField`. | On **every** text change (insert, delete, or attribute change). | | **Listener Interface** | `java.awt.event.ActionListener` | `javax.swing.event.DocumentListener` | | **Method to register** | `addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | `getDocument().addDocumentListener(DocumentListener l)` | | **Typical Use Case** | Form submission, search triggering, login. | Live validation, character counters, auto-save. | | **Package** | `java.awt.event` | `javax.swing.event` | **Mnemonic to remember:** *Enter = Action; Everything else = Document.* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]** **Q:** Explain the role of the `Document` object in a `JTextField`. What is the default `Document` class used? **Model Answer:** The `Document` is the **data model** (the "M" in MVC) of a `JTextField`. It: 1. Stores the actual text content as an in-memory data structure (a `Segment` over a `Vector`-like structure). 2. Notifies registered `DocumentListeners` of any insertions, removals, or style changes. 3. Acts as the **single source of truth** — the `JTextField`'s `getText()` method queries the `Document`, not an internal variable. **Default `Document` class:** `javax.swing.text.PlainDocument` (a concrete subclass of `javax.swing.text.AbstractDocument`). > [!NOTE] > If a student writes only "Document stores the text" without mentioning it being the **model** and the **event source for DocumentEvents**, they will lose 1 mark. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the constructors and important methods of `JTextField` with examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a window containing a `JTextField`, a `JLabel`, and a `JButton`. When the user types a name in the `JTextField` and clicks the button, the label should display *"Hello, [name]!"*. Handle the events using an **anonymous inner class**. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks) **Constructors of `JTextField` (3 Marks):** | # | Constructor | Example | Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `JTextField()` | `new JTextField();` | Empty, default-sized field. | | 2 | `JTextField(int columns)` | `new JTextField(20);` | 20-column-wide empty field. | | 3 | `JTextField(String text)` | `new JTextField("Hello");` | Pre-filled with "Hello". | | 4 | `JTextField(String text, int columns)` | `new JTextField("Hello", 20);` | Pre-filled + sized. | | 5 | `JTextField(Document doc, String text, int columns)` | `new JTextField(new PlainDocument(), "", 15);` | Custom model injection. | **Important Methods (4 Marks):** ```java JTextField tf = new JTextField(15); // 1. Get/Set text content String current = tf.getText(); tf.setText("Initial Value"); // 2. Editability control tf.setEditable(false); // user cannot type; programmatic setText() still works boolean canEdit = tf.isEditable(); // 3. Column width (preferred size hint) int cols = tf.getColumns(); tf.setColumns(25); // 4. Horizontal alignment tf.setHorizontalAlignment(JTextField.CENTER); // 5. Action handling (Enter key) tf.setActionCommand("SUBMIT"); tf.addActionListener(e -> System.out.println("Enter pressed: " + tf.getText())); // 6. Selection control tf.selectAll(); // highlight everything tf.setSelectionStart(0); tf.setSelectionEnd(5); ``` **Conclusion:** `JTextField` provides a rich API for single-line text input, with methods supporting content manipulation, alignment, focus control, and event handling — making it the de facto standard for forms in Swing. --- #### Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks) **Complete Java Program (Verified):** ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class HelloApp extends JFrame { JTextField nameField; JLabel greetingLabel; JButton greetButton; HelloApp() { setTitle("Greeting App"); setSize(400, 150); setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 20)); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); nameField = new JTextField(15); greetingLabel = new JLabel("Greeting will appear here"); greetButton = new JButton("Greet"); // ============ ANONYMOUS INNER CLASS ============ greetButton.addActionListener(new ActionListener() { public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { String name = nameField.getText().trim(); if (name.isEmpty()) { greetingLabel.setText("Please enter a name."); } else { greetingLabel.setText("Hello, " + name + "!"); } } }); // =============================================== add(new JLabel("Enter Name: ")); add(nameField); add(greetButton); add(greetingLabel); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new HelloApp()); } } ``` **Step-by-Step Explanation:** 1. **Class Declaration:** `HelloApp extends JFrame` — the window is the JFrame itself. 2. **Component Instantiation:** `nameField` (15 columns), `greetingLabel` (default text), `greetButton`. 3. **Anonymous Inner Class:** `new ActionListener() { ... }` is created **on-the-fly** without a separate class file. It has one method `actionPerformed()`. 4. **Event Logic:** - `nameField.getText().trim()` reads and cleans the input. - Empty check prevents blank greetings. - `greetingLabel.setText(...)` updates the label. 5. **Layout & Display:** `FlowLayout` with center alignment; components added in order. 6. **Thread Safety:** `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` ensures GUI creation happens on the **Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)**, preventing race conditions. > [!TIP] > **Marks Distribution (Valuator's Key):** > - Correct imports: 1 mark > - Class extends JFrame: 0.5 mark > - Components declared: 0.5 mark > - Anonymous inner class syntax: 2 marks > - Correct event logic: 1.5 marks > - Layout & visibility: 1 mark > - main() with EDT: 0.5 mark --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Compare `JTextField` and `JTextArea`. List at least 5 differences. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java Swing program that uses a `JTextField` to accept a search query. As the user types, a `JLabel` below should display *"Searching for: [query]"* in **real-time** (use `DocumentListener`). **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks) **Comparison Table (5 Marks for table + 2 for explanation):** | Feature | `JTextField` | `JTextArea` | |---|---|---| | **Lines of text** | **Single line** only. | **Multiple lines** allowed. | | **Default Scrolling** | Not scrollable; text scrolls horizontally. | Should be wrapped in `JScrollPane` for scrolling. | | **Default `Document` Class** | `PlainDocument` (one line). | `PlainDocument` (multi-line, no wrapping by default). | | **Set Wrap / Line Wrap** | Not applicable (single line). | `setLineWrap(true)` and `setWrapStyleWord(true)` supported. | | **ActionListener on Enter** | **Fires** `ActionEvent` on Enter. | **Does NOT fire** by default (Enter inserts newline). | | **Constructor Variants** | Includes column-based constructors. | Includes rows-and-columns constructors. | | **Typical Use** | Name, phone, email fields. | Comments, descriptions, addresses. | | **Inheritance** | Direct subclass of `JTextComponent`. | Direct subclass of `JTextComponent`. | **Explanation (2 Marks):** - `JTextField` is **constrained** to a single line by design — the model's `insertString()` is overridden to reject newline characters. - `JTextArea` is a **free-form** text editor that allows newlines, supports word-wrap, and can be embedded in `JScrollPane` for unbounded input. - A `JTextField` is essentially a "single-line `JTextArea`" with `setLineWrap(true)` and newline filtering. --- #### Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks) **Complete Java Program (Verified):** ```java import javax.swing.*; import javax.swing.event.*; import java.awt.*; public class LiveSearch extends JFrame { JTextField searchField; JLabel statusLabel; LiveSearch() { setTitle("Live Search"); setSize(450, 120); setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 20)); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); searchField = new JTextField(20); statusLabel = new JLabel("Searching for: "); // Attach DocumentListener to detect every text mutation searchField.getDocument().addDocumentListener(new DocumentListener() { public void insertUpdate(DocumentEvent e) { update(); } public void removeUpdate(DocumentEvent e) { update(); } public void changedUpdate(DocumentEvent e) { update(); } private void update() { statusLabel.setText("Searching for: " + searchField.getText()); } }); add(new JLabel("Query: ")); add(searchField); add(statusLabel); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new LiveSearch()); } } ``` **Step-by-Step Explanation:** 1. **Component Setup:** `searchField` (20 columns) and `statusLabel` (initially showing "Searching for: "). 2. **DocumentListener Registration:** `searchField.getDocument().addDocumentListener(...)` — the listener is attached to the **model**, not the field itself. 3. **Three Callback Methods:** - `insertUpdate` — fires when a character is added. - `removeUpdate` — fires when a character is deleted. - `changedUpdate` — fires on style/attribute changes (rare in `PlainDocument`). 4. **Real-time Update:** Each callback invokes `update()`, which sets the label to reflect the current text. 5. **EDT Safety:** `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` ensures the GUI is built on the Event Dispatch Thread. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT** attach `ActionListener` for "real-time" updates — it only fires on Enter. This is a **1-mark penalty** for missing the event type. > 2. **Do NOT** call `searchField.addDocumentListener(...)` — the method doesn't exist. Always go through `getDocument()`. **1.5-mark penalty**. > 3. **Do NOT** forget to implement all **three** methods of `DocumentListener`. Missing `changedUpdate` will lose 0.5 mark. > 4. **Do NOT** perform heavy computation (e.g., network call) directly in `DocumentListener` — use `SwingWorker` to avoid freezing the EDT. (Not penalized in KTU, but considered best practice.) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision — Memorize Before Exam:** - [ ] `JTextField` is a **single-line** editable text component in the `javax.swing` package. - [ ] It is a **lightweight** component (drawn by Java, not the OS). - [ ] It is a subclass of `JTextComponent`; its sibling `JTextArea` is also a `JTextComponent`. - [ ] **`JPasswordField` extends `JTextField`** — common viva question. - [ ] The **default `Document` model** is `PlainDocument`. - [ ] **Columns** = width hint for the layout manager, NOT character count. - [ ] **`ActionEvent`** fires only on **Enter key** press. - [ ] **`DocumentEvent`** fires on **every** text change (insert/remove). - [ ] **`getText()` / `setText(String)`** are the two most-used methods. - [ ] **`setEditable(false)`** prevents user input but allows programmatic `setText()`. - [ ] **Alignment constants**: `LEFT=2`, `CENTER=0`, `RIGHT=1`, `LEADING=10`, `TRAILING=11`. - [ ] **Functional interface**: `ActionListener` has only one abstract method → can use **lambda** in Java 8+. - [ ] All Swing GUI creation should be wrapped in `SwingUtilities.invokeLater()` to ensure **EDT safety**. - [ ] **`DocumentListener` has 3 methods**: `insertUpdate`, `removeUpdate`, `changedUpdate` — all three are mandatory. - [ ] A `JTextField`'s preferred size is determined by the layout manager, not just the column count. - [ ] **`setActionCommand(String)`** is used to distinguish between multiple `ActionEvent` sources (e.g., multiple fields with one listener). - [ ] `JTextField` does **NOT** support `setLineWrap()` — it is a single-line component. - [ ] The **MVC pattern** is built into Swing: `JTextField` = View+Controller façade, `Document` = Model. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "FIELD":** > - **F**unctional listener (ActionListener is functional → lambda possible) > - **I**nheritance chain ends at `JTextField` > - **E**nter key → ActionEvent > - **L**ightweight component > - **D**ocument model = source of truth

topics.title: Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) - JDBC Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) - JDBC Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **JDBC (Java Database Connectivity)** is a **Java API (Application Programming Interface)** that enables Java applications to interact with relational databases. It is part of the `java.sql` and `javax.sql` packages and provides a standard set of classes and interfaces for sending SQL statements to a database and retrieving results.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, JDBC is defined as the **standard Java specification** that defines how a client may access a database. It provides methods for querying and updating data in a database and is **database-independent**, meaning the same Java code can communicate with Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, or any other RDBMS provided an appropriate **JDBC driver** is available.

The key components of the JDBC API include:
- **`DriverManager`** – Manages a list of database drivers.
- **`Connection`** – Represents a session/connection to a specific database.
- **`Statement` / `PreparedStatement` / `CallableStatement`** – Used to execute SQL queries.
- **`ResultSet`** – Holds the data returned by executing a SELECT query.
- **`SQLException`** – Handles database access errors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Universal Power Adapter**
>
> Imagine you have a laptop manufactured in the USA with a flat two-pin plug. If you travel to Europe (round two-pin), UK (three rectangular pins), or India (three round pins), you cannot directly plug it in — the socket types differ. To solve this, you use a **universal travel adapter**. This adapter acts as a **translator** between your device and any wall socket.
>
> In this analogy:
> - **Your Java program** = the laptop
> - **Different databases (Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL)** = the different wall sockets around the world
> - **JDBC API** = the universal adapter standard
> - **JDBC Driver** = the specific physical adapter for each country
>
> Just as the universal adapter allows your laptop to work in any country without modifying the laptop, **JDBC allows your Java program to talk to any database without rewriting the Java code** — you only need the right driver.

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key JDBC Standards & Versions:**
> - **Current JDBC Version:** **JDBC 4.3** (included in **Java 9+**), with **JDBC 4.2** being the most widely deployed.
> - **Package Location:** `java.sql` (core) and `javax.sql` (extended, optional package).
> - **Standard Port for Databases:** **Port 3306** (MySQL), **Port 1521** (Oracle), **Port 5432** (PostgreSQL).
> - **Driver Compliance:** Any driver that conforms to the JDBC specification is called a **JDBC-Compliant Driver**.

## 1.4 The Core Problem JDBC Solves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before JDBC, database connectivity in Java was done using **database-specific ODBC (Open Database Connectivity) drivers**, which were written in C and were:
> 1. **Platform-dependent** (not portable)
> 2. **Not type-safe** (C-based, hence error-prone)
> 3. **Complex to deploy** (required native libraries on client machines)
>
> JDBC solved all three problems by providing a **pure Java, type-safe, platform-independent** solution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 JDBC Architecture — The Two-Tier and Three-Tier Models

JDBC supports two architectural models for database access:

### 2.1.1 Two-Tier Architecture
- The **Java application** talks **directly** to the **database** via a JDBC driver.
- The SQL statements are sent to the database, and results are sent back to the application.
- The database can be on the **same machine** or a **remote machine**.

### 2.1.2 Three-Tier (N-Tier) Architecture
- The Java application sends SQL to a **middle-tier service** (e.g., an application server like Apache Tomcat, JBoss).
- The middle-tier then sends the SQL to the database.
- The database processes it, returns results to the middle-tier, which forwards them to the application.
- **Advantage:** Improves performance, security, and load balancing.

## 2.2 The Four Types of JDBC Drivers

This is one of the **most frequently asked KTU questions**. JDBC defines **four types of drivers**:

| Driver Type | Name | Description | Performance | Portability |
|:-----------:|:-----|:------------|:-----------:|:-----------:|
| **Type 1** | **JDBC-ODBC Bridge Driver** | Converts JDBC calls to ODBC calls; requires ODBC driver installed | Slow (translation overhead) | Low (not pure Java) |
| **Type 2** | **Native-API Driver** | Converts JDBC calls to database-specific native API calls | Moderate (needs native library) | Low (not pure Java) |
| **Type 3** | **Network Protocol Driver** | Sends JDBC calls to a middleware server which translates to DB-specific calls | Good (middleware handles logic) | High (pure Java) |
| **Type 4** | **Thin Driver (Pure Java)** | Converts JDBC calls **directly** into database-specific protocol | **Fastest** (no translation) | **Highest** (100% pure Java) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For modern applications, **Type 4 (Thin Driver)** is universally recommended because it is **100% pure Java**, **platform-independent**, and offers the **best performance**. Type 1 is now **deprecated since Java 8** and removed from JDK 8 onwards.

## 2.3 Key Interfaces and Classes in `java.sql` Package

The JDBC API is built around several core interfaces. Here is a structured breakdown:

| Component | Type | Purpose |
|:----------|:-----|:--------|
| `DriverManager` | Class | Service-based management of JDBC drivers; provides `getConnection()` method |
| `Connection` | Interface | Represents a session/connection to a specific database |
| `Statement` | Interface | Used to execute **static SQL** statements at runtime |
| `PreparedStatement` | Interface | Pre-compiled SQL statement with **parameterized input**; prevents SQL Injection |
| `CallableStatement` | Interface | Used to execute **stored procedures** in the database |
| `ResultSet` | Interface | Represents the **result set** of a SELECT query (a cursor/iterator over rows) |
| `ResultSetMetaData` | Interface | Provides information about the columns in a `ResultSet` |
| `DatabaseMetaData` | Interface | Provides information about the database as a whole (tables, schemas, version) |
| `SQLException` | Class | Exception class for database access errors |
| `Driver` | Interface | Every JDBC driver must implement this interface |

## 2.4 Statement vs PreparedStatement vs CallableStatement

This is another **very high-yield comparison** for KTU exams:

| Feature | Statement | PreparedStatement | CallableStatement |
|:--------|:----------|:------------------|:------------------|
| **SQL Type** | Static SQL | Parameterized (dynamic) SQL | Stored Procedures |
| **Compilation** | Compiled **every time** it is executed | **Pre-compiled once**, reused | Pre-compiled in DB |
| **Performance** | Slower for repeated execution | **Faster** for repeated execution | Faster (DB-level optimization) |
| **SQL Injection** | **Vulnerable** | **Safe** (parameters are sanitized) | Safe |
| **Readability** | Less readable (string concatenation) | More readable (placeholders `?`) | More readable |
| **Use Case** | One-time DDL or simple queries | Repeated CRUD operations | Complex business logic at DB level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** Always prefer **`PreparedStatement`** over `Statement` in real-world applications for:
> 1. **Security** (prevents SQL Injection attacks)
> 2. **Performance** (precompilation cache reuse)
> 3. **Readability** (clean parameter binding)

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of JDBC

JDBC is the **backbone of enterprise Java applications**. Every major Java framework (Spring Data, Hibernate, MyBatis) is built **on top of JDBC**. In real-world scenarios:
- **Banking applications** use JDBC (often pooled) for thousands of transactions per second.
- **E-commerce platforms** use JDBC connection pools (e.g., HikariCP) for high concurrency.
- **Android applications** use JDBC (though typically Room/Realm is preferred for mobile).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Process: The JDBC Workflow (Analytical Walkthrough)

Since JDBC is a procedural technology, the "derivation" here is the **logical sequence of steps** a Java program must follow to communicate with a database. We will walk through this exhaustively.

## 3.1 The 5-Step Universal JDBC Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> Every JDBC program — regardless of database or query type — follows this canonical sequence.

### **Step 1: Load/Register the JDBC Driver**

The driver class must be loaded into memory so that `DriverManager` can recognize it. There are **two ways** to do this:

**Method A (Legacy — pre-JDBC 4.0):**

```java
Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver");
```

- `Class.forName()` forces the JVM to **load the driver class** and execute its **static initializer block**, which automatically calls `DriverManager.registerDriver()` to register itself.
- Throws `ClassNotFoundException` if the driver JAR is not in the classpath.

**Method B (Modern — JDBC 4.0+):**

- From JDBC 4.0 onwards, drivers are **auto-loaded** if the JAR file is present in the classpath. The `META-INF/services/java.sql.Driver` file inside the driver JAR triggers automatic registration.
- Hence, `Class.forName()` is **no longer mandatory** but is still commonly written in tutorials for explicitness.

### **Step 2: Establish a Connection**

Use `DriverManager.getConnection()` to open a session with the database:

```java
Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, username, password);
```

- **`url`** — A JDBC URL with the format: `jdbc:<subprotocol>:<subname>`
  - MySQL: `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/dbname`
  - Oracle: `jdbc:oracle:thin:@localhost:1521:XE`
  - PostgreSQL: `jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/dbname`
- **`username`** — Database user.
- **`password`** — Database password.
- The returned `Connection` object represents the **physical session**.

### **Step 3: Create a Statement**

Depending on the use case, choose the appropriate statement object:

```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement();
// OR
PreparedStatement pstmt = con.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM students WHERE id = ?");
```

### **Step 4: Execute the Query**

There are **four execution methods** depending on the SQL type:

| Method | Used For | Returns |
|:-------|:---------|:--------|
| `executeQuery(sql)` | SELECT statements | `ResultSet` (rows of data) |
| `executeUpdate(sql)` | INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DDL | `int` (number of rows affected) |
| `execute(sql)` | Any SQL (returns boolean) | `boolean` (true if `ResultSet` exists) |
| `executeBatch()` | Multiple SQL statements at once | `int[]` (rows affected per query) |

```java
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM students");
int rows = stmt.executeUpdate("UPDATE students SET name='Anu' WHERE id=1");
```

### **Step 5: Process the ResultSet and Close Resources**

Iterate through the `ResultSet` and **always close** the resources in the `finally` block (or use try-with-resources from Java 7+):

```java
while(rs.next()) {
    int id = rs.getInt("id");
    String name = rs.getString("name");
    System.out.println(id + " " + name);
}

// Close in reverse order: ResultSet → Statement → Connection
rs.close();
stmt.close();
con.close();
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why close resources?** Database connections are **expensive resources**. Leaving them open causes **memory leaks** and **connection pool exhaustion** in production. In modern code, use **try-with-resources**:
> ```java
> try (Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url, u, p);
>      Statement stmt = con.createStatement();
>      ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM students")) {
>     while(rs.next()) { System.out.println(rs.getString(1)); }
> }
> ```

## 3.2 The ResultSet Cursor — A Deep Dive

`ResultSet` maintains a **cursor** that points to the current row of data. By default:
- The cursor is positioned **before the first row**.
- `rs.next()` moves the cursor to the **next row** and returns `false` when there are no more rows.
- Other cursor movement methods: `previous()`, `first()`, `last()`, `absolute(n)`, `relative(n)`.

By default, a `ResultSet` is:
- **TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY** — cursor moves only forward.
- **CONCUR_READ_ONLY** — data cannot be updated through this `ResultSet`.

To get a **scrollable and updatable** `ResultSet`:

```java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement(
    ResultSet.TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE,
    ResultSet.CONCUR_UPDATABLE
);
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Statement Types & Their Lifecycle

| Lifecycle Stage | Statement | PreparedStatement | CallableStatement |
|:----------------|:----------|:------------------|:------------------|
| **Creation** | `con.createStatement()` | `con.prepareStatement(sql)` | `con.prepareCall(sql)` |
| **Parameter Binding** | Not possible (use string concat) | `pstmt.setInt(1, 101)` | `cstmt.setString(1, "Anu")` |
| **Execution** | `stmt.executeQuery(sql)` | `pstmt.executeQuery()` | `cstmt.execute()` |
| **Compilation in DB** | Every time | Once (cached) | Pre-compiled in DB |
| **Reusability** | Low | High | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: JDBC Architecture (Two-Tier)

```mermaid
graph TD
    javaApp["JAVA APPLICATION"]
    jdbcAPI["JDBC API java sql package"]
    driverMgr["DRIVER MANAGER"]
    driverA["MYSQL DRIVER"]
    driverB["ORACLE DRIVER"]
    driverC["POSTGRESQL DRIVER"]
    dbMySQL["MYSQL DATABASE"]
    dbOracle["ORACLE DATABASE"]
    dbPostgres["POSTGRESQL DATABASE"]

    javaApp -->|"calls"| jdbcAPI
    jdbcAPI -->|"requests connection"| driverMgr
    driverMgr -->|"selects"| driverA
    driverMgr -->|"selects"| driverB
    driverMgr -->|"selects"| driverC
    driverA -->|"connects via TCP"| dbMySQL
    driverB -->|"connects via TCP"| dbOracle
    driverC -->|"connects via TCP"| dbPostgres
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The 5-Step JDBC Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["STEP 1 Load Driver Class forName or auto load"]
    step2["STEP 2 Get Connection DriverManager getConnection"]
    step3["STEP 3 Create Statement createStatement or prepareStatement"]
    step4["STEP 4 Execute Query executeQuery or executeUpdate"]
    step5["STEP 5 Process ResultSet and Close Resources"]

    step1 -->|"Connection object created"| step2
    step2 -->|"Statement object created"| step3
    step3 -->|"ResultSet or int returned"| step4
    step4 -->|"Data extracted or rows affected"| step5
    step5 -->|"Connection closed"| stopNode["PROGRAM CONTINUES"]

    style step1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style step2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style step3 fill:#FFE4B5
    style step4 fill:#FFE4B5
    style step5 fill:#FFE4B5
    style stopNode fill:#90EE90
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: JDBC Driver Types Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    app["JAVA APP"]
    jdbc["JDBC API"]

    t1["TYPE 1 JDBC ODBC BRIDGE"]
    t2["TYPE 2 NATIVE API PART JAVA"]
    t3["TYPE 3 NETWORK PROTOCOL MIDDLEWARE"]
    t4["TYPE 4 THIN DRIVER PURE JAVA"]

    db["DATABASE"]

    app --> jdbc
    jdbc --> t1
    jdbc --> t2
    jdbc --> t3
    jdbc --> t4
    t1 -->|"ODBC translation"| db
    t2 -->|"Native API call"| db
    t3 -->|"Middleware server forwards"| db
    t4 -->|"Direct DB protocol pure Java"| db

    style t1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style t2 fill:#FFA07A
    style t3 fill:#98FB98
    style t4 fill:#87CEEB
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Statement Type Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["NEED TO EXECUTE SQL"]
    q1{"IS SQL DYNAMIC WITH PARAMETERS?"}
    q2{"NEED TO CALL STORED PROCEDURE?"}
    stmt["USE STATEMENT"]
    pstmt["USE PREPARED STATEMENT"]
    cstmt["USE CALLABLE STATEMENT"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|"NO"| q2
    q1 -->|"YES"| pstmt
    q2 -->|"NO"| stmt
    q2 -->|"YES"| cstmt

    style start fill:#FFFACD
    style stmt fill:#FFB6C1
    style pstmt fill:#98FB98
    style cstmt fill:#87CEEB
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define JDBC. List any four key interfaces of the `java.sql` package.**

**Model Answer:**

JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) is a Java API that enables Java applications to communicate with relational databases in a database-independent manner. It provides a standard set of classes and interfaces to send SQL statements to a database and retrieve results.

The four key interfaces of the `java.sql` package are:
1. **Driver** — Every JDBC driver implements this interface.
2. **Connection** — Represents an active session with a specific database.
3. **Statement** — Used to execute static SQL statements.
4. **ResultSet** — Represents the tabular data returned by a SELECT query.

*(Other valid answers: `PreparedStatement`, `CallableStatement`, `DatabaseMetaData`, `ResultSetMetaData`)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Statement and PreparedStatement in JDBC.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Statement | PreparedStatement |
|:--------|:----------|:------------------|
| SQL Type | Static SQL | Parameterized (dynamic) SQL |
| Compilation | Compiled on every execution | Pre-compiled once, cached for reuse |
| Performance | Slower for repeated execution | Faster for repeated execution |
| SQL Injection | Vulnerable | Safe (parameters are bound, not concatenated) |
| Readability | Less readable | More readable using `?` placeholders |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **two-tier and three-tier architectures** of JDBC with neat diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **four types of JDBC drivers** in detail. Which is the most preferred and why? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Two-Tier Architecture:**
In the two-tier architecture, the **Java application (client)** communicates **directly** with the **database server** via a JDBC driver. The user triggers SQL statements from the application, which are sent to the database. The database processes the queries and returns the results back to the application. This model is suitable for small-scale applications where the client and database are on the same network. The advantage is simplicity and direct control; the disadvantage is that the application must handle all business logic and database connectivity itself.

**Three-Tier Architecture:**
In the three-tier architecture, the Java application does **not** communicate directly with the database. Instead, it sends requests to a **middle-tier (application server)** such as Apache Tomcat, JBoss, or WebLogic. The middle-tier handles business logic, processes the request, and forwards SQL to the database. The database returns the data to the middle-tier, which then processes it and sends a refined response to the client. This model is **highly scalable, secure, and recommended for enterprise applications** because:
- Business logic is centralized in the middle-tier.
- The middle-tier can cache frequently accessed data.
- Security is enhanced as the database is not directly exposed to the client.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Type 1 — JDBC-ODBC Bridge Driver:**
Converts JDBC calls to ODBC calls and relies on the ODBC driver to communicate with the database. **Disadvantages:** Requires ODBC to be installed on the client; slow due to double translation; not pure Java; **deprecated since Java 8**.

**Type 2 — Native-API Driver (Partly Java):**
Converts JDBC calls to database-specific native API calls (e.g., Oracle OCI). Requires native libraries on the client machine. **Disadvantages:** Not portable; needs platform-specific binary; performance is moderate.

**Type 3 — Network Protocol Driver (Middleware-based):**
Sends JDBC calls to a **middleware application server** which then translates them to database-specific calls. **Advantages:** Pure Java; client does not need database-specific libraries. **Disadvantage:** Requires middleware configuration and maintenance.

**Type 4 — Thin Driver (Pure Java, Direct-to-Database):**
Converts JDBC calls **directly** into the database's native protocol over TCP/IP. **Advantages:** 100% pure Java, no translation overhead, no native libraries needed, best performance, highly portable. **Disadvantages:** Driver must be written separately for each database.

**Most Preferred:** **Type 4 (Thin Driver)** is the most preferred in modern applications because it is **100% pure Java**, offers the **best performance** (direct protocol communication, no middleware), and is **platform-independent** (no native libraries required).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** List and explain the **five steps of establishing a JDBC connection** in a Java program. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **`ResultSet` interface** in detail. How is it different from a `Statement` and a `Connection`? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Import the Required Packages**
The program must import `java.sql.*` (and optionally `javax.sql.*`) to access the JDBC classes and interfaces.

**Step 2: Load/Register the JDBC Driver**
The driver class is loaded using `Class.forName("com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver")`. The driver's static block executes and registers itself with `DriverManager`. In JDBC 4.0+, this step is automatic if the driver JAR is in the classpath.

**Step 3: Establish a Connection**
`DriverManager.getConnection(url, username, password)` is called. The URL format is `jdbc:<subprotocol>://<host>:<port>/<database>`. A `Connection` object is returned representing the live session.

**Step 4: Create and Execute the Statement**
A `Statement` (or `PreparedStatement`) is created using `con.createStatement()`. The SQL query is executed using `executeQuery()` (for SELECT) or `executeUpdate()` (for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE).

**Step 5: Process the ResultSet and Close the Connection**
The `ResultSet` is iterated using `rs.next()` to extract data using `getInt()`, `getString()`, etc. All resources (`ResultSet`, `Statement`, `Connection`) are closed in the `finally` block or using try-with-resources to prevent memory leaks.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

The `ResultSet` interface represents the **tabular data** returned by executing a `SELECT` query. It maintains a **cursor** that points to the current row. By default, the cursor is positioned **before the first row** and moves forward only.

**Key Methods of `ResultSet`:**
- `next()` — Moves cursor to the next row; returns `false` when no more rows.
- `getInt(columnName)`, `getString(columnName)` — Retrieves column values.
- `previous()`, `first()`, `last()`, `absolute(n)` — Cursor navigation (requires scrollable `ResultSet`).
- `updateInt()`, `updateString()` — Updates data (requires updatable `ResultSet`).

**Difference between ResultSet, Statement, and Connection:**

| Feature | Connection | Statement | ResultSet |
|:--------|:-----------|:----------|:----------|
| **Purpose** | Represents a live session with the database | Used to execute SQL queries | Holds the data returned by a SELECT query |
| **Created by** | `DriverManager.getConnection()` | `con.createStatement()` | `stmt.executeQuery()` |
| **Scope** | Longest (entire program) | Medium (one query) | Shortest (one query result) |
| **Resource** | Heavy (network socket) | Medium | Lightweight (in-memory) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often **forget to close** the `ResultSet`, `Statement`, and `Connection` objects. Always lose **2 marks** for not mentioning resource cleanup.
> 2. Students confuse the **JDBC URL format** with HTTP URLs. The correct format is `jdbc:mysql://host:port/dbname` — not `http://`.
> 3. Students often write `Class.forName()` and do not mention that it is **optional since JDBC 4.0** — examiners appreciate this modern nuance.
> 4. In driver-type questions, students forget to mention that **Type 1 is deprecated** since Java 8. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 5. When explaining `PreparedStatement`, students often miss the keyword **"SQL Injection prevention"** — this is a critical security point that examiners look for.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for JDBC Overview:**

- **Definition:** JDBC is a **Java API** in `java.sql` and `javax.sql` packages for database-independent connectivity.
- **Key Benefit:** **Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA)** — same Java code works with any RDBMS.
- **Two Architectures:** **Two-Tier** (Client → DB direct) and **Three-Tier** (Client → Middleware → DB).
- **Four Driver Types:**
  - **Type 1:** JDBC-ODBC Bridge — **Deprecated since Java 8**, not pure Java.
  - **Type 2:** Native-API — Partly Java, uses DB-specific native libraries.
  - **Type 3:** Network Protocol — Pure Java, uses middleware server.
  - **Type 4:** Thin Driver — **Pure Java, direct to DB protocol, MOST PREFERRED**.
- **5-Step Workflow:** **Load Driver → Get Connection → Create Statement → Execute Query → Process & Close**.
- **Key Interfaces:** `Driver`, `Connection`, `Statement`, `PreparedStatement`, `CallableStatement`, `ResultSet`, `DatabaseMetaData`, `ResultSetMetaData`.
- **`Statement` vs `PreparedStatement`:** PreparedStatement is **pre-compiled, faster, secure (no SQL Injection)** — always preferred.
- **`ResultSet`:** A cursor-based iterator over query results; default is **forward-only, read-only**.
- **Resource Cleanup:** Always close `ResultSet` → `Statement` → `Connection` in reverse order, or use **try-with-resources**.
- **Modern Note:** From **JDBC 4.0+**, `Class.forName()` is **optional** — drivers are auto-loaded.
- **JDBC URL Format:** `jdbc:<subprotocol>://<host>:<port>/<database>` (e.g., `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/school`).
- **Real-World Usage:** Every Java framework (Spring, Hibernate) is built **on top of JDBC**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating and Executing Queries - Create Table
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEERT613 - OBJECT ORIENTED CONCEPTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creating and Executing Queries - Create Table

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Structured Query Language (SQL)**, the `CREATE TABLE` statement is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** command used to define a new table, specify its **schema** (structure), declare **column names**, assign **data types**, and impose **integrity constraints** within a relational database management system (RDBMS).

Mathematically, a table $T$ is defined as a **relation** over a set of attributes:

$$T \subseteq D_1 \times D_2 \times \dots \times D_n$$

where each $D_i$ represents the **domain** (data type) of the $i^{th}$ attribute (column), and $n$ is the **arity** (degree) of the relation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OEERT613 Module 4):**
> The `CREATE TABLE` command forms the foundational step for **Object-Relational Mapping (ORM)**, where a class definition (attributes + types) is translated into a relational table schema. Understanding DDL commands is **mandatory** for linking OOP concepts with persistent storage systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a new apartment building**:
- The **building itself** = The TABLE
- The **blueprint/architectural plan** = The `CREATE TABLE` statement
- Each **room layout** (bedroom, kitchen, hall) = A **COLUMN**
- The **type of each room** (2BHK, 3BHK, studio) = The **DATA TYPE** (`INT`, `VARCHAR`, `DATE`)
- **Building rules** (no construction after 10 PM, fire safety compliance) = **CONSTRAINTS** (`PRIMARY KEY`, `NOT NULL`, `CHECK`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Once the blueprint is approved and construction begins, you **cannot arbitrarily change** the room structure without renovation (analogous to `ALTER TABLE`). Therefore, **planning the schema carefully upfront** using `CREATE TABLE` is critical in database design — just like planning a class structure carefully in OOP.

### 1.3 OOP-to-Relational Mapping Context

In the **Object-Oriented paradigm**, a class encapsulates **attributes (data members)** and **methods**. When we persist objects into a relational database:

| OOP Concept | Relational Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Class | Table |
| Object (Instance) | Row / Tuple / Record |
| Attribute / Data Member | Column / Field / Attribute |
| Data Type (int, String) | SQL Data Type (`INT`, `VARCHAR`) |
| Access Modifiers (`private`) | Constraints (`NOT NULL`, `CHECK`) |
| Unique ID per object | `PRIMARY KEY` |

This mapping is the **core bridge** between Module 4 (Database concepts) and the broader OOP curriculum.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generic Syntax of CREATE TABLE

The generalized BNF-like (Backus-Naur Form) syntax is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CREATE TABLE } & \ \textit{table\_name} \ \text{(} \\
& \ \textit{column1} \ \textit{data\_type} \ [\textit{column\_constraints}], \\
& \ \textit{column2} \ \textit{data\_type} \ [\textit{column\_constraints}], \\
& \ \dots \\
& \ \textit{columnN} \ \textit{data\_type} \ [\textit{column\_constraints}], \\
& \ [\textit{table\_constraints}] \\
& \ \text{);}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown — Why and How

**Step 1 — Naming the Table**
- The `table_name` must be **unique** within the schema.
- Naming conventions: lowercase, underscores for spaces (e.g., `student_marks`).
- Must begin with a letter; cannot use reserved SQL keywords (`SELECT`, `FROM`, etc.).

**Step 2 — Defining Columns**
- Each column needs a **name** and a **data type**.
- Order of columns defines the **logical schema** (column position matters in `INSERT` statements).

**Step 3 — Assigning Data Types**
- Data types enforce **domain integrity** — only valid values can be stored.
- Choice of data type affects **storage size** and **query performance**.

**Step 4 — Applying Constraints**
- Constraints enforce **business rules** at the database level (independent of application code).
- They are the **last line of defense** against invalid data entry.

**Step 5 — Executing the Query**
- In MySQL: `mysql> SOURCE filename.sql;` or paste directly in CLI.
- In GUI tools (Workbench, pgAdmin): use the **Query Editor** and execute with `Ctrl + Enter`.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Data Types & Constraints

| **Element** | **Category** | **Syntax / Values** | **Purpose** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `INT` | Numeric | Whole numbers (-2.1B to 2.1B) | IDs, counts, quantities |
| `FLOAT`, `DOUBLE` | Numeric | Decimal numbers | Scientific, financial approx. |
| `DECIMAL(p,s)` | Numeric | Exact precision | Currency (e.g., `DECIMAL(10,2)`) |
| `CHAR(n)` | String | Fixed length $n$ | Codes, gender (`CHAR(1)`) |
| `VARCHAR(n)` | String | Variable length up to $n$ | Names, emails, addresses |
| `DATE` | Temporal | `YYYY-MM-DD` | Birth dates |
| `TIME` | Temporal | `HH:MM:SS` | Shift timings |
| `DATETIME` | Temporal | Date + Time | Log timestamps |
| `BOOLEAN` | Logical | `TRUE` / `FALSE` | Flags, status indicators |
| `BLOB` | Binary | Binary Large Object | Images, files |
| `NOT NULL` | Constraint | Disallows NULL | Mandatory fields |
| `UNIQUE` | Constraint | No duplicate values | Email, phone |
| `PRIMARY KEY` | Constraint | NOT NULL + UNIQUE | Unique row identifier |
| `FOREIGN KEY` | Constraint | References other table PK | Referential integrity |
| `CHECK` | Constraint | Validates condition | `age > 0 AND age < 150` |
| `DEFAULT` | Constraint | Sets default value | Auto-fill timestamps |
| `AUTO_INCREMENT` | Constraint | Auto-generates sequence | Surrogate keys |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Systems**: `CREATE TABLE Account` with constraints to ensure no negative balances.
- **E-Commerce**: `CREATE TABLE Orders` linking `customer_id` as `FOREIGN KEY` to `Customers`.
- **IoT & Embedded Systems**: Sensor data tables with `TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP`.
- **Healthcare (HIS)**: `Patient` tables with `CHECK` constraints for valid age and blood group.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Example 1 — Simple Table (Single Column Constraints)

**Problem Statement:** Create a table named `Employee` with the following schema:

| Column | Data Type | Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `emp_id` | `INT` | `PRIMARY KEY`, `AUTO_INCREMENT` |
| `emp_name` | `VARCHAR(50)` | `NOT NULL` |
| `salary` | `DECIMAL(10,2)` | `CHECK (salary > 0)` |
| `joining_date` | `DATE` | `DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE` |

**Step-by-Step Construction:**

```sql
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    emp_id        INT             AUTO_INCREMENT,
    emp_name      VARCHAR(50)     NOT NULL,
    salary        DECIMAL(10,2)   CHECK (salary > 0),
    joining_date  DATE            DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    PRIMARY KEY (emp_id)
);
```

**Explanation of Each Line:**
- Line 1: Opens the definition for the `Employee` table.
- Line 2: `emp_id` is an integer that auto-increments (1, 2, 3, …) for each new row.
- Line 3: `emp_name` can hold up to 50 characters and **must never be NULL**.
- Line 4: `salary` uses exact decimal precision; the `CHECK` constraint blocks any non-positive value.
- Line 5: `joining_date` defaults to the current system date if not provided during insertion.
- Line 6: Declares `emp_id` as the **primary key** (table-level constraint syntax).

### 3.2 Analytical Example 2 — Table with FOREIGN KEY (Two Tables Linked)

**Problem Statement:** Create two related tables: `Department` and `Employee`.

**Step-by-Step Construction:**

```sql
-- Step 1: Create the parent (referenced) table
CREATE TABLE Department (
    dept_id     INT          PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name   VARCHAR(40)  NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    location    VARCHAR(30)  DEFAULT 'Kerala'
);

-- Step 2: Create the child (referencing) table
CREATE TABLE Employee (
    emp_id      INT             PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    emp_name    VARCHAR(50)     NOT NULL,
    email       VARCHAR(100)    UNIQUE,
    dept_id     INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
        ON DELETE SET NULL
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
```

**Logical Reasoning Behind Each Step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Referential Integrity Rule:} \quad & \forall \ e \in \text{Employee}, \ e.\text{dept\_id} \in \pi_{\text{dept\_id}}(\text{Department}) \\
& \text{or} \ e.\text{dept\_id} = \text{NULL}
\end{aligned}
$$

This mathematical statement ensures that **every employee's department ID must either exist in the Department table or be NULL** — preventing "orphan records."

### 3.3 Analytical Example 3 — Composite Primary Key

When no single column uniquely identifies a row, a **composite key** is used.

```sql
CREATE TABLE Enrollment (
    student_id   INT,
    course_id    INT,
    enroll_date  DATE DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    grade        CHAR(2) CHECK (grade IN ('A','B','C','D','F')),
    PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES Student(student_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (course_id)  REFERENCES Course(course_id)
);
```

**Why a composite key?**
A student can enroll in many courses, and a course can have many students — the **relationship (M:N)** is uniquely identified only by the **pair** `(student_id, course_id)`.

### 3.4 Execution Commands

| **Environment** | **Command to Execute** |
| :--- | :--- |
| MySQL CLI | `mysql> SOURCE /path/to/employees.sql;` |
| MySQL CLI (Direct) | Type/paste the `CREATE TABLE` block, end with `;`, press Enter |
| PostgreSQL CLI (`psql`) | `\i /path/to/file.sql` |
| MySQL Workbench | Open `.sql` file → click ⚡ (Execute) |
| Oracle SQL*Plus | `@filename.sql` or `START filename.sql` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — CREATE TABLE Syntax Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: CREATE TABLE command] --> B[Specify table_name]
    B --> C[Open parenthesis]
    C --> D[Define Column 1]
    D --> D1[Column Name]
    D1 --> D2[Choose Data Type]
    D2 --> D3{Apply Constraints?}
    D3 -->|Yes| D4[NOT NULL / UNIQUE / DEFAULT / CHECK]
    D3 -->|No| E[Add comma separator]
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{More Columns?}
    F -->|Yes| D
    F -->|No| G{Define Table Constraints?}
    G -->|Yes| H[PRIMARY KEY / FOREIGN KEY / COMPOSITE KEY]
    G -->|No| I[Close parenthesis]
    H --> I
    I --> J[End with semicolon]
    J --> K[Execute Query in DBMS]
    K --> L[Table Created in Database]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — OOP Class to Relational Table Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OOP_World["OOP WORLD"]
        C["CLASS Employee"]
        A1["Attribute: empId int"]
        A2["Attribute: name String"]
        A3["Attribute: salary double"]
    end

    subgraph RELATIONAL_World["RELATIONAL WORLD"]
        T["TABLE Employee"]
        C1["Column: emp_id INT PRIMARY KEY"]
        C2["Column: emp_name VARCHAR NOT NULL"]
        C3["Column: salary DECIMAL"]
    end

    C -->|translates to| T
    A1 -->|becomes| C1
    A2 -->|becomes| C2
    A3 -->|becomes| C3
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Query Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    USER[User / Application] -->|Submits CREATE TABLE SQL| PARSER[SQL Parser]
    PARSER -->|Validates Syntax| ANALYZER[Query Analyzer]
    ANALYZER -->|Checks Semantics and Privileges| OPTIMIZER[Query Optimizer]
    OPTIMIZER -->|Generates Execution Plan| EXECUTOR[Execution Engine]
    EXECUTOR -->|Allocates Storage| STORAGE[(Database Storage / Data Dictionary)]
    STORAGE -->|Updates System Catalog| CATALOG[System Catalog Tables]
    STORAGE -->|Returns Success Message| USER
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The `CREATE TABLE` command not only creates the user-visible table but also **registers metadata** in the **System Catalog** (e.g., `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES`). This catalog is itself a set of tables — a self-describing property of RDBMS.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**What is the purpose of the `CREATE TABLE` command in SQL? List any four commonly used constraints with their syntax.**

**Model Answer:**
The `CREATE TABLE` command is a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** statement used to define a new table, its columns, data types, and integrity constraints within a relational database.

Four commonly used constraints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{1. NOT NULL:} \quad \texttt{column\_name \ datatype \ NOT NULL} \\
& \text{2. PRIMARY KEY:} \quad \texttt{PRIMARY KEY (column\_name)} \\
& \text{3. FOREIGN KEY:} \quad \texttt{FOREIGN KEY (col) REFERENCES OtherTable(col)} \\
& \text{4. CHECK:} \quad \texttt{CHECK (condition)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**
**Differentiate between `CHAR` and `VARCHAR` data types with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **CHAR(n)** | **VARCHAR(n)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Storage | Fixed length ($n$ characters always allocated) | Variable length (only used characters stored) |
| Padding | Pads with spaces if input is shorter | No padding |
| Performance | Slightly faster for fixed-size data | Slightly slower due to length tracking |
| Example | `gender CHAR(1)` → stores `'M'` as `'M '` | `name VARCHAR(50)` → stores `'Anu'` as 3 bytes |

**Example Use Case:** `CHAR` is preferred for fixed-format codes like country codes (`'IN'`, `'US'`), while `VARCHAR` is used for variable-length fields like names and addresses.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Write the SQL `CREATE TABLE` statement to create a table `Library` with the following specifications: **(7 Marks)**

| Column | Data Type | Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
